steps.detail: Slice haloumi into 1cm slabs. Pat dry — surface moisture prevents browning. Dry pan over medium-high, no oil. Lay slabs in and don't touch for 2 min for a gold crust. Flip once. Haloumi squeaks on the tooth while warm — eat fast.
tips: No colour after 2 minutes means the pan wasn't hot enough — nudge the heat up rather than cooking longer.

steps.title: Soften the wrap
steps.content: Warm wrap.
steps.detail: 10 sec on a dry pan per side, or 15 sec direct on a gas flame for a few char marks, or 10 sec in the microwave wrapped in a damp paper towel. Warm makes it pliable; cold wraps crack when you roll them.
warnings: Going over the gas flame? Use tongs and keep the wrap moving — it scorches in seconds.

steps.title: Build the layers
steps.content: Layer with avo, haloumi, cucumber, rocket, feta.
steps.detail: Lay the wrap flat. Smash half an avocado down the middle third, leaving a 2cm gap at top and bottom. Haloumi on top, cucumber batons, a handful of rocket, crumbled feta. Don't overload — one layer each, or you won't be able to roll it.
tips: Season the avocado with a pinch of salt as you smash it — it lifts the whole wrap.

steps.title: Roll and slice
steps.content: Roll tight, slice on the bias.
steps.detail: Fold the top and bottom edges in over the filling, then roll tightly from one side, tucking as you go. Press gently on the seam. Slice diagonally with a sharp knife — bias cut looks better and exposes the colourful cross-section. Eat with both hands.
tips: Taking it to work? Roll the bottom half in baking paper — it holds the shape and catches drips.

title: Chilli Con Carne
description: Smoky, spicy, crowd-feeding.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge and freezes brilliantly for up to 3 months. Reheat until piping hot right through.
make_ahead: Chilli is better the next day — make the whole pot up to 2 days ahead and reheat gently with a splash of water.
leftovers: Pile over corn chips with cheese and grill for nachos, or spoon into a jacket potato.

substitutions.swap: pork mince, or swap in a third tin of beans to go meat-free
halve it for kids, or use cayenne sparingly — it's hotter
green capsicum, or a grated carrot and zucchini

steps.title: Brown mince, bloom spices
steps.content: Brown mince with spices.
steps.detail: Hot heavy pot, splash of oil. Crumble 700g mince in in large chunks and don't touch for 3 min — let a deep brown crust form on the bottom. Then break up and cook till no pink remains, 5 min. Tip in 1 tsp cumin, 1 tbsp paprika, 1 tsp chilli powder. Stir 60 sec till spices bloom and the kitchen smells like a taqueria.
tips: If a lot of liquid comes out, keep going — browning only starts once it's evaporated.
warnings: Wash hands, board and spoon after handling raw mince — keep it clear of anything served raw.

steps.title: Soften the vegetables
steps.content: Add diced onion and capsicum.
steps.detail: Push mince to the sides. In the clearing, add one finely diced brown onion and one finely diced red capsicum. Stir 6 min in the beef fat till soft. A big pinch of salt helps draw moisture out.

steps.title: Add the tins
steps.content: Tip in tomatoes and beans.
steps.detail: Tip in two tins of tomatoes (crush whole ones by hand), two tins of beans (kidney and black bean is a great mix), drained and rinsed. Splash water from the tins in too. Stir, scraping the bottom for any stuck fond. Season, a square of dark chocolate or 1 tsp cocoa adds depth if you have it.
tips: Rinse the beans until the water runs clear — the tin liquid can make the chilli gluey.

steps.title: Simmer till thick
steps.content: Simmer 40 min.
steps.detail: Bring to a bubble, drop heat, lid half-on. 40 min gentle simmer, stirring every 10 min so it doesn't catch. It should thicken considerably — spoon dragged through leaves a trail. Taste for salt, a squeeze of lime at the end brightens everything.
tips: If it thickens too far before time's up, splash in water — you can always reduce it again.

steps.title: Load it up
steps.content: Serve with rice or corn chips.
steps.detail: Ladle over fluffy rice with a dollop of sour cream, grated cheese, sliced avocado, chopped coriander, lime wedge. Or build loaded corn chips — pile chilli over chips, blanket with cheese, grill till bubbly. Either way, the toppings matter.
tips: No sour cream? A dollop of plain yoghurt does the same cooling job.

title: Banana Bread
description: Speckled bananas, soft butter, the kindest loaf.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container at room temperature — the fridge dries it out. Freeze individual slices for up to 3 months and toast them straight from frozen.
leftovers: Toast thick slices in a buttered pan till golden — day-three banana bread's best form.

substitutions.swap: brown sugar gives a deeper caramel note
mixed spice, or a good grating of nutmeg
swap up to half for wholemeal — add a splash of milk if the batter turns stiff

steps.title: Mash till chunky
steps.content: Mash bananas.
steps.detail: Three very ripe bananas — brown-spotted or fully black is perfect; they're sweeter and softer. Peel and mash in a bowl with a fork till broken down but still a little chunky. Totally smooth loses the banana pockets in the finished loaf.
tips: Bananas not ripe enough? Bake them whole in their skins at 180°C for 15 minutes till black.

steps.title: Whip butter and sugar
steps.content: Cream butter and sugar, add eggs.
steps.detail: In a stand mixer or with beaters: 100g soft butter and 3/4 cup sugar beaten 3 min till pale and fluffy — aeration is what gives the loaf a tender crumb. Add 2 eggs one at a time, beating between each. If it looks curdled, a tablespoon of the flour fixes it.
tips: Soft butter dents easily under a finger — short microwave bursts rescue a fridge-cold block.

steps.title: Fold it together
steps.content: Fold in bananas, flour, cinnamon.
steps.detail: Fold in the mashed banana with a spatula. Sift in 2 cups of plain flour, the 2 tsp baking powder and 1 tsp cinnamon. Fold in big sweeping strokes till JUST combined — overmixing develops gluten and gives you a tough, rubbery loaf. Streaks of flour are fine to stop at.
tips: Fold in a figure-eight, turning the bowl as you go — fewer strokes, better crumb.

steps.title: Bake till skewer-clean
steps.content: Bake 55 min at 170°C.
steps.detail: Line a loaf tin with baking paper (overhang the sides so you can lift it out later). Pour in the batter, smooth the top. 170°C oven, middle shelf, 55 min. Done when a skewer in the centre comes out with a few moist crumbs but no raw batter. Cover with foil for the last 15 if the top is browning too fast.
warnings: Don't open the oven before the 40-minute mark — the rise can collapse.

steps.title: Cool before slicing
steps.content: Cool in tin 15 min.
steps.detail: Rest in the tin 15 min — critical. Hot banana bread is fragile and will break if you tip it out too early. Then lift out with the paper onto a rack to cool fully before slicing. Butter on thick slices, or toasted the next day with more butter.

title: Poached Eggs
description: Vinegar, simmer, spoon, done. Eggs-ellence.
storage: Best eaten fresh. Poached eggs keep 1 day submerged in cold water in the fridge; make the toast fresh every time.
make_ahead: Poach up to a day ahead: slip the cooked eggs straight into iced water, refrigerate, then re-warm for 30–60 seconds in hot (not boiling) water.

substitutions.swap: any clear vinegar — apple cider works
English muffins or crumpets hold a runny yolk beautifully

steps.title: Bring water to a shiver
steps.content: Simmer water with a splash of vinegar.
steps.detail: Medium saucepan, 8cm of water, a dash of white vinegar (the acid helps the whites set fast). Bring to a bare simmer — tiny bubbles breaking the surface, NOT a rolling boil which will shred the eggs. A thermometer reads about 85°C.
tips: Poaching more than two? Use your widest saucepan — crowding drops the water temperature.

steps.title: Poach the eggs
steps.content: Swirl, slip in egg, 3 min.
steps.detail: Crack each egg into a small ramekin first — lets you see if a yolk breaks before committing. Stir the simmering water with a spoon to create a gentle whirlpool. Slide the egg into the centre of the swirl — the motion wraps the white around the yolk. 3 min for runny yolk and set whites. Don't poke.
tips: The freshest eggs hold together best — older whites spread. Save old eggs for scrambling.
Poach one at a time until you're confident — the whirlpool only shapes one egg well.

steps.title: Lift and drain
steps.content: Lift, rest on tea towel.
steps.detail: Lift out with a slotted spoon, letting water drain. Rest briefly on a clean tea towel — catches the water that would otherwise make your toast soggy. Trim any raggedy white edges with scissors if you're plating fancy; nobody's watching at home though.

steps.title: Assemble on toast
steps.content: Pile on buttered toast.
steps.detail: Thick-cut toast, buttered generously (butter goes on while toast is hot so it melts in). Egg perched on top, crack of sea salt, crack of pepper. Break the yolk with the edge of a knife and watch it flood over the butter. That's breakfast.
tips: Drop the toast when the eggs go in — both land hot at the same moment.

title: Greek Salad
description: Tomato, cucumber, olives, feta, oregano. Nothing hiding.
storage: Once dressed and salted it won't keep — the tomato weeps and the cucumber goes limp within hours. Eat it the day it's made.
make_ahead: Chop the vegetables a few hours ahead if you must, but only salt and dress just before serving.

substitutions.swap: red wine vinegar is the traditional choice
spring onions — milder, no soaking needed
capers in a pinch — use fewer

steps.title: Chop the vegetables
steps.content: Chunk tomato, cucumber, red onion.
steps.detail: Cut ripe tomatoes into irregular chunks (don't cube them — rustic is the point), cucumber into half-moons (halve lengthways, then slice), half a red onion thinly sliced (then soak in cold water 5 min if raw onion is too harsh for you, drain, pat dry).
tips: Room-temperature tomatoes taste twice as good — never straight from the fridge.

steps.title: Dress and rest
steps.content: Toss with oil and lemon.
steps.detail: Big bowl. Toss the tomato, cucumber, onion with a generous glug of good olive oil and the juice of half a lemon. Pinch of salt, a scatter of dried oregano. The salt pulls juice from the tomato — that juice IS the dressing. Let it sit 5 min.

steps.title: Crown with feta
steps.content: Top with feta slab.
steps.detail: A proper Greek salad doesn't crumble the feta — it sits ON TOP as a whole slab. Lay a thick block of feta across the top of the bowl, drizzle with more oil, sprinkle of oregano. Presentation matters; it's a simple salad dressing up.
tips: Feta from a tub of brine at the deli is creamier than the vacuum-packed blocks.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.content: Crack of pepper.
steps.detail: Generous crack of pepper over the feta and salad. A few kalamata olives if you have them. Eat with warm pita bread to mop up the juice at the bottom of the bowl — that's the best part. No cheese should ever touch a tomato without being present at a greek salad.
tips: Warm pita in a dry pan while the salad sits — 30 seconds a side.

title: Hummus & Flatbread
description: Creamy chickpea dip. Snack-dinner energy.
storage: Keeps 4 days airtight in the fridge with a thin film of oil over the top, or freeze for up to 3 months — thaw overnight and re-whip with a splash of water.
make_ahead: Hummus improves after a few hours in the fridge as the garlic mellows — make it up to 3 days ahead and warm the wraps just before eating.
leftovers: Spread it in sandwiches, dollop on roast veg, or thin with lemon juice for a salad dressing.

substitutions.swap: pita, Turkish bread, or corn chips
smoked paprika, or leave it out
roast the cloves first for a mellower dip

steps.title: Blitz the base
steps.content: Blitz chickpeas, garlic, lemon, cumin.
steps.detail: Drain and rinse a tin of chickpeas — don't skip the rinse, the brine tastes tinny. For extra-creamy hummus, pinch the skins off each chickpea (tedious but transformative). Into a food processor with 2 garlic cloves, juice of a whole lemon, 1 tsp cumin, pinch of salt, 2 tbsp tahini if you have it.
tips: Microwave the drained chickpeas for 60 seconds first — warm chickpeas blitz noticeably smoother.

steps.title: Emulsify with oil
steps.content: Stream in oil till silky.
steps.detail: Blitz on high for 60 sec to break everything down. Then WITH THE MOTOR RUNNING, stream in 1/3 cup of good olive oil slowly through the feed tube. This emulsifies the oil into the chickpea base for silky texture. 2 min total blending. Add a splash of iced water if it's stiff — cold water makes it whip up lighter.
tips: Stop and scrape down the sides once — whole chickpeas hide under the blades.
Taste at the end — hummus usually wants more lemon or salt than you think.

steps.title: Char the wraps
steps.content: Warm wraps, tear, dip.
steps.detail: Warm wraps one at a time on a dry pan or direct gas flame for 10 sec a side till flexible with some char spots. Tear into tearable pieces and pile next to the hummus. Drizzle the hummus with more oil, a dust of paprika, a few chickpeas reserved for texture. Dip, inhale.
warnings: Going over the gas flame? Use tongs and keep the wrap moving — it scorches in seconds.

title: Carbonara
description: Eggs, parmesan, bacon, pepper. No cream. Fight me.
storage: Keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge, though the sauce never comes back quite as silky — reheat gently in a pan with a splash of water, never blasted in the microwave. Don't freeze.
leftovers: Press leftovers into a hot oiled pan and fry into a crisp golden pasta cake.

substitutions.swap: pancetta or guanciale if the deli has it — the traditional choice
pecorino romano for a sharper, more Roman sauce
any long pasta — linguine or fettuccine

steps.title: Render the bacon
steps.content: Crisp bacon in a dry pan.
steps.detail: No oil needed — bacon renders its own fat. Lay strips in a cold pan, turn to medium. As the fat melts, the bacon shrinks and crisps. 5 min till deeply gold. Lift out with tongs. Leave the rendered fat in the pan — that's the sauce base.
tips: Snip the bacon smaller with scissors first if you want crisp bits through every bite.

steps.title: Boil the spaghetti
steps.content: Cook spaghetti, reserve water.
steps.detail: Heavily salted water, rolling boil. Cook 1 min less than packet. Scoop out a full cup of pasta water before draining — carbonara needs more than most.
tips: Set a timer for packet time minus one minute the moment the pasta hits the water.
A mug dunked straight into the pot is the easiest way to grab pasta water before draining.

steps.title: Make the egg paste
steps.content: Whisk eggs, parmesan, pepper.
steps.detail: In a bowl, whisk 2 whole eggs with finely grated parmesan (fine, microplane if you have it — smoother sauce) and a LOT of cracked black pepper. It should look like a thick paste. No cream, never. Set aside.
tips: Use room-temperature eggs — fridge-cold ones cool the pasta and the sauce won't loosen.

steps.title: Marry pasta and sauce
steps.content: OFF heat, toss pasta with egg mix.
steps.detail: This is the critical step. The pan must be off the heat and only warm — too hot and the eggs scramble. Drag drained pasta into the pan with tongs, toss to warm through 20 sec in the bacon fat. Then pour in the egg mix, toss vigorously. Splash in pasta water as you go — the starch emulsifies with the egg into a silky sauce that clings. Scrambled eggs = too hot. Silky = right.
tips: Too tight? More pasta water. Too soupy? Keep tossing — it thickens as it cools.
warnings: The eggs are only gently set by residual heat — use fresh, clean, uncracked eggs.

steps.title: Plate it fast
steps.content: Fold in bacon, serve.
steps.detail: Tear bacon and fold through. More pepper on top, extra parmesan if you like. Eat the second it's plated — carbonara waits for no one.
tips: Warm the bowls under the hot tap and dry them — carbonara cools fast on cold plates.

title: Salmon Teriyaki
description: Sticky, glossy, rice underneath.
equipment: small saucepan with lid (for the rice)
storage: Cooked salmon keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge; the rice cools fast and keeps only 1 day. Reheat gently — flaking it through fried rice beats microwaving a fillet. Don't freeze.
make_ahead: The sauce keeps a week in a jar in the fridge — mix a double batch and teriyaki anything later in the week.
leftovers: Flake leftover salmon through fried rice with peas and the last of the spring onion.

substitutions.swap: brown sugar or maple syrup, same amount
chicken thigh fillets — cook 6–8 minutes a side, to 74°C at the thickest point
basmati or medium-grain, whatever's in the cupboard

steps.title: Crisp the skin
steps.content: Sear salmon skin-side down.
steps.detail: Pat salmon fillets dry, particularly the skin — wet skin NEVER crisps. Season both sides with salt. Hot pan, splash of oil, medium-high. Lay fillets in SKIN-SIDE DOWN and press gently with a spatula for 10 sec to stop them curling. Don't touch for 4 min — let the skin crisp to shatter-crunch. Flip for 90 sec on the flesh side. Remove to a plate.
tips: Get the rice on first: 1 cup rice, 1.5 cups water, boil, then lid on lowest heat 12 minutes and rest 10 off the heat.
Watch the side of the fillet — the flesh turns opaque from the bottom up, telling you when to flip.
warnings: Wash hands and board after handling raw salmon — keep it clear of anything served raw.
Salmon skin can spit in hot oil — lower the fillets in away from you.

steps.title: Mix the teriyaki
steps.content: Mix soy, honey, grated ginger.
steps.detail: Small bowl: 3 tbsp soy sauce, 2 tbsp honey, a thumb of freshly grated ginger. Whisk till the honey dissolves into the soy. Grating (not chopping) the ginger releases its juice — more flavour, no chewy fibres.
tips: No need to peel young ginger — scrape papery skin off with a teaspoon.

steps.title: Reduce the glaze
steps.content: Add sauce, reduce until sticky.
steps.detail: Same pan, drop heat to medium. Pour in the teriyaki mix — it'll hiss and bubble. Let it reduce 2 min till glossy and syrupy; it should coat the back of a spoon. Don't walk away; honey goes from glossy to burnt in seconds.
tips: Pull it off the heat a touch early — it keeps thickening as it cools.

steps.title: Glaze and serve
steps.content: Serve over rice with spring onion.
steps.detail: Salmon back in skin-side UP this time (preserves the crispy skin). Spoon the sticky sauce over the flesh a few times to glaze. 30 sec to warm through. Over a mound of jasmine rice, sliced spring onion scattered, sesame seeds if you have them. Steamed greens on the side. Check the thickest fillet before plating: the flesh should flake under gentle pressure from a fork, with at most a blush of deeper pink in the very centre.

title: Potato Salad
description: BBQ side dish of the gods.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge — the yoghurt dressing holds up well. Don't freeze; potato goes grainy. Serve cold or at room temperature, never reheated.
make_ahead: Even better made a few hours ahead — the flavours meld in the fridge. Hold back the parsley until serving.
leftovers: Stir through a drained tin of tuna and some capers for an instant lunch.

substitutions.swap: mayonnaise, sour cream, or half-and-half with the yoghurt
dill is the classic; chives work too
finely diced red onion, soaked 5 minutes in cold water

steps.title: Cook the potatoes
steps.content: Boil potatoes till tender.
steps.detail: Cut potatoes into 3cm chunks (skin on — nutrition and texture). Into a pot of cold salted water — starting cold cooks them evenly. Bring to a boil, simmer 12 min till a knife slides through with no resistance. Drain and let the steam rise off for 2 min — dry potatoes soak up dressing better than wet ones.
tips: Salt the water like pasta water — it's your only chance to season inside the potato.

steps.title: Boil and chop the eggs
steps.content: Boil eggs 8 min, cool, chop.
steps.detail: Lower eggs gently into a separate pot of simmering water. Timer for EXACTLY 8 min — just-set yolk, no grey ring. Straight into iced water to stop the cook and loosen the shell. Peel under a gentle stream, halve, then roughly chop.
tips: Older eggs peel far more easily than fresh ones — use the back of the carton first.

steps.title: Dress it warm
steps.content: Toss warm potato with yoghurt, eggs, spring onion.
steps.detail: While potatoes are still WARM (they absorb dressing better warm than cold), tip into a big bowl with half a cup of yoghurt (or yoghurt + mayo half-half for richer), the chopped eggs, thinly sliced spring onions, 1 tsp dijon if you like a little kick. Fold gently — don't mash the potato or eggs.

steps.title: Season and rest
steps.content: Season well, scatter parsley.
steps.detail: Big pinch of salt, crack of pepper, taste and adjust — potato is bland without enough salt. Tear parsley over the top. Rest 15 min before serving so the flavours meld; rest a full hour in the fridge and it's even better. A squeeze of lemon or splash of vinegar lifts the whole thing if it tastes flat.

title: Minestrone
description: Vegetables, beans, pasta. A ladle of summer in winter.
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge, though the pasta swells as it sits. Freeze the base without pasta for up to 3 months; reheat till piping hot.
make_ahead: Make the soup base up to 2 days ahead and stop before the pasta — add and cook it fresh in the reheated soup.
leftovers: Day-two minestrone is nearly a stew — pile it on toast with extra parmesan.

substitutions.swap: any tin works — cannellini, borlotti or chickpeas
green beans, silverbeet or whatever veg is going soft in the crisper
any hard grating cheese — tasty at a pinch

steps.title: Dice all the veg
steps.content: Dice vegetables.
steps.detail: Small, even dice on everything — 1cm cubes on two carrots, two stalks of celery, one onion; grate a couple of garlic cloves. Small dice means a spoonful catches every vegetable and they cook at the same speed. This is the step that takes the longest; do it all before any heat goes on.
tips: Halve the carrot lengthways first so it sits flat — flat side down makes safe, even dice.

steps.title: Build the soffritto
steps.content: Soften onion, carrot.
steps.detail: Glug of olive oil in a heavy pot over medium. Onion and carrot with a pinch of salt — soffritto. 8 min till onion is translucent and carrot is softening. This is the flavour foundation; don't rush. Add garlic the last minute.
tips: The pinch of salt draws water out of the onion so it softens without browning — you want sweet, not charred.

steps.title: Simmer the soup
steps.content: Add tomato, beans, stock, simmer 15 min.
steps.detail: Tip in a tin of tomatoes (crush whole ones with your hand), a tin of drained beans, and a litre of vegetable stock. Chunks of zucchini too if you fancy. Bring to a simmer. 15 min uncovered for the veg to tenderise and the flavours to marry.
tips: Scrape the base as the stock goes in to lift any stuck-on browned bits — that's flavour.

steps.title: Cook pasta in the soup
steps.content: Add pasta, cook 8 min.
steps.detail: A cup of small pasta (ditalini, broken spaghetti, whatever) straight into the simmering soup. 8 min till al dente. Pasta will keep soaking up broth as it sits, so serve within 20 min or make it a thick soup next day.
tips: Taste a piece at 7 min — al dente means a slight bite at the centre, not chalky.

steps.title: Ladle and finish
steps.content: Top with parmesan.
steps.detail: Ladle into bowls. Generous shower of grated parmesan — it melts into the soup and adds salty depth. Drizzle of good olive oil, crack of pepper, torn basil if you have it. Crusty bread alongside is mandatory.
tips: Save parmesan rinds in the freezer — next batch, simmer one in the soup for extra depth.

title: Chicken Noodle Soup
description: Broth for the soul. Sick day cure.
storage: Broth and chicken keep 3 days airtight in the fridge; the noodles swell, so store them separately if you can. Freeze broth and chicken (no noodles) up to 3 months and reheat till piping hot.
make_ahead: Poach and shred the chicken a day ahead and keep it in the broth; cook the noodles fresh when you reheat.
leftovers: Thin day-two soup with a splash of stock, or eat it thick — it's basically chicken noodle stew.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast — poach 15 min and never let it boil or it turns stringy
egg noodles, risoni or any small pasta
skip it for a plainer broth, or use a strip of lemon zest instead

steps.title: Poach the chicken
steps.content: Poach chicken in stock with ginger, 20 min.
steps.detail: Chicken thighs (skin off, bone out — easier), a thumb of ginger sliced into coins, 1.5L chicken stock in a pot. Bring to a bare simmer — little bubbles, never a rolling boil or the chicken gets tough. 20 min and it's poached through. The broth becomes gingery and chickeny. Check the thickest thigh before shredding: cut in — no pink, juices running clear, or 74°C on a thermometer. If in doubt, give it a few more minutes at a simmer.
tips: Skim off any grey foam that rises in the first few minutes — it keeps the broth clear.
warnings: Raw chicken rules: hands, board and tongs get hot soapy water before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Shred the meat
steps.content: Shred.
steps.detail: Lift chicken out with tongs onto a plate. When cool enough to handle, use two forks to pull it into strips along the grain. Or your hands if the meat's cool enough — rougher shreds soak up broth better than knife cuts.
tips: Fish out the ginger coins now — biting into one later is a surprise.

steps.title: Simmer the noodles
steps.content: Cook noodles in broth.
steps.detail: Bring broth back to a simmer. Break spaghetti into thirds and drop straight in — cooking in stock not water means every strand soaks up flavour. Cook till just al dente per packet timing (usually 7 min).
tips: Stir for the first minute so the strands don't weld into a clump.

steps.title: Warm chicken and veg through
steps.content: Return chicken, add carrot and spring onion.
steps.detail: Chicken back into the pot with thinly sliced carrot and sliced spring onions. 2 min to warm the chicken and barely soften the veg — you want a little bite in the carrot, not mush.
tips: Keep some spring onion greens back to scatter raw over the bowls.

steps.title: Taste and season
steps.content: Season well.
steps.detail: Taste and season well — soup always needs more salt than you think, and stock varies. Big pinch of salt, plenty of cracked pepper. A squeeze of lemon lifts it. Ladle into bowls piping hot; this is medicine.
tips: Season at the end, not the start — broth concentrates as it simmers and gets saltier on its own.

title: Pork Larb
description: Thai mince salad. Herby, punchy, easy.
storage: The pork keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge; store lettuce separately and add fresh herbs when serving. Not one for the freezer.
leftovers: Cold larb over steamed jasmine rice with an extra squeeze of lime is a legitimate lunch.

substitutions.swap: chicken mince — add a splash of oil to the pan, it's leaner
light soy (tamari to stay gluten-free) plus a pinch of salt gets close, though you lose the funk
lemon at a pinch — you want sharp, fresh acid
iceberg, baby cos or butter lettuce all cup well

steps.title: Crisp the mince
steps.content: Brown mince in dry pan till crisp.
steps.detail: Heat a dry pan (no oil) over medium-high. Crumble 500g pork mince in in big chunks. Cook without touching for 3 min for brown crust on the bottom, then break up with a wooden spoon and stir-fry another 4 min till it's deeply browned and some edges are crisp. Dry-frying concentrates the flavour — this is the core of larb.
tips: If liquid pools, raise the heat and let it evaporate — browning only starts once the pan is dry again.
warnings: Wash hands and spoon after handling raw pork mince, and keep it well clear of the herbs and lettuce.

steps.title: Dress while hot
steps.content: Off heat, toss with fish sauce, lime, herbs, chilli.
steps.detail: Off heat. Into a big bowl with the hot pork: 2 tbsp fish sauce, juice of two limes, three thinly sliced spring onions, a big handful each of mint and coriander (torn not chopped — bruising turns herbs black), one sliced fresh chilli. Toss. Taste — should hit salty, sour, herby, hot. Adjust in tiny increments.
tips: Roll the limes hard under your palm before juicing — you'll get half as much juice again.
warnings: Wash your hands after slicing the chilli and keep them away from your eyes.

steps.title: Build the lettuce cups
steps.content: Spoon into lettuce cups.
steps.detail: Peel off whole leaves of iceberg lettuce for cups — cup them in your palm, spoon in a heaped tablespoon of warm larb, top with more herbs if you like. Two-bite snacks. Eat with your hands; sticky rice or jasmine rice on the side is traditional.
tips: Inner leaves make the sturdiest cups — save the floppy outer ones for a salad.

title: Eggplant Parma
description: The vego pub hero. Crumbed, tomato, bubbly cheese.
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge. Reheat in a 180°C oven, not the microwave, to bring the crumb back. Freezes in portions up to 2 months.
make_ahead: Fry the planks and make the napoli up to a day ahead; assemble and bake when you're ready to eat.
leftovers: Cold eggplant parma in a crusty roll with rocket is a first-rate sandwich.

substitutions.swap: panko for extra crunch, or stale bread blitzed fine
any good melting cheese — a pizza blend or young cheddar
flat-leaf parsley, or leave it out
passata — use about 400 ml

steps.title: Salt the eggplant
steps.content: Slice eggplant lengthways.
steps.detail: Slice a eggplant lengthways into 1cm-thick planks. Salt both sides lightly and rest on paper towel 10 min — pulls out bitter moisture. Pat dry. Even thickness matters; wobbly slices cook unevenly.
tips: A serrated knife saws through the glossy skin more easily than a straight blade.

steps.title: Crumb and fry
steps.content: Flour, egg, crumb, fry golden.
steps.detail: Three shallow plates: seasoned flour, 2 beaten eggs, breadcrumbs. Dredge each plank flour (shake off), egg (let drip), crumbs (press firmly both sides). Shallow fry in 5mm of hot oil over medium-high, 2 min a side till deep gold. Drain on paper.
tips: Test the oil with a pinch of crumbs — an instant lively sizzle means go.
warnings: Hot oil spits — pat the planks dry, lower them in away from you, and never add water to the pan.

steps.title: Layer the parma
steps.content: Layer in dish with tomato and mozzarella.
steps.detail: Baking dish — spread a ladle of napoli (gently simmered crushed tomato with garlic, salt, pepper) across the base. Lay crumbed eggplant planks on top in one layer. Spoon more sauce over each, then tear mozzarella into chunks and dot over.
tips: Pat the mozzarella dry if it's the wet ball kind — extra whey makes a soggy top.

steps.title: Bake till bubbling
steps.content: Bake 25 min at 200°C.
steps.detail: 200°C oven, middle rack, 25 min till the cheese is deeply bubbling and blistered with gold patches. If it's pale, flash under the grill for 2 min — watch it; cheese goes from gold to burnt fast.
tips: Bubbling at the centre, not just the edges, is the sign it's properly hot through.

steps.title: Finish with basil
steps.content: Basil to finish.
steps.detail: Torn basil leaves scattered just before serving — adding them before the oven turns them black. Crack of pepper, a final drizzle of good olive oil. Crusty bread and a green salad on the side.

title: Breakfast Burrito
description: Eggs, beans, cheese, all rolled up.
storage: Best eaten straight away. A seared burrito wrapped in foil keeps 1 day in the fridge — reheat in a dry pan or sandwich press, and add the avocado fresh.
make_ahead: The bean mash keeps 3 days in the fridge — make a double batch and mornings get faster.

substitutions.swap: any melting cheese you've got
chives, or leave it out if coriander tastes like soap to you
a tin of refried beans, warmed straight up

steps.title: Soft-scramble the eggs
steps.content: Scramble eggs.
steps.detail: Crack 4 eggs into a bowl and beat with salt, pepper, a splash of milk for lightness. Warm a knob of butter in a non-stick pan over low heat — low is the secret to silky scramble. Pour eggs in. As they start to set at the edges, fold gently with a spatula in big slow strokes. Off heat while still glossy and slightly underdone — residual heat finishes them.
tips: Non-stick holds a lot of heat — lift the pan right off between folds if things are moving fast.

steps.title: Make the bean mash
steps.content: Warm beans, mash lightly.
steps.detail: Drain a tin of beans (black or pinto), tip into a small pan with a splash of water, pinch of cumin, pinch of salt. Warm 3 min. Mash about half with a fork for texture — some whole, some mashed binds the filling so it doesn't spill from the tortilla.
tips: Keep it loose enough to spread — add water a splash at a time until it just slumps off the fork.

steps.title: Load the tortillas
steps.content: Pile onto warm tortillas with cheese, avo.
steps.detail: Warm tortillas one at a time on a dry pan for 15 sec a side till pliable. Down the middle third: eggs, beans, grated cheese, slices of avocado, torn coriander, hot sauce. Don't overload — overstuffed burritos explode.
tips: Grate the cheese first so it goes on while the eggs and beans are hot enough to melt it.

steps.title: Roll and seal
steps.content: Roll tight, sear seam-side down.
steps.detail: Fold the top and bottom edges in over the filling, then roll tightly from one side — tuck the filling in with your fingers as you go. Place seam-side-down in a hot dry pan for 60 sec — seals the roll and toasts the outside. Flip once. Slice diagonally.
tips: Taking it to go? Wrap in foil instead of slicing — it steams a little and holds together.

title: Stir-Fry Beef
description: Fridge-emptying weeknight. Hot wok, fast hands.
equipment: medium saucepan with lid for rice
storage: Cool leftovers fast; the rice keeps just 1 day in the fridge, the beef and veg 2 days airtight. Reheat in a hot pan or microwave till piping hot.
make_ahead: Slice and marinate the beef and chop all the veg in the morning — the cooking itself is a 10-minute sprint.
leftovers: Day-old rice plus the leftover beef and veg makes a very good fried rice — crack an egg in.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh strips (cook through, no pink), or firm tofu pressed dry
hoisin for sweeter, or 2 extra tbsp soy with a pinch of sugar
green beans, snow peas or whatever crunchy veg needs using

steps.title: Slice and marinate the beef
steps.content: Slice beef thin, marinate in soy and ginger.
steps.detail: Freeze the beef 15 min first — much easier to slice thin. Cut ACROSS the grain (perpendicular to muscle fibres) into 3mm strips. In a bowl, toss with 1 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tsp grated ginger, 1 tsp cornflour if you have it. Rest 10 min while you prep everything else. Cornflour velvetises the meat.
tips: Get the rice on before you slice: 2 cups rinsed, 3 cups water, lid on, 12 min on low then 10 min off the heat.
warnings: Keep the raw beef and its board away from the veg, and wash up before touching anything else.

steps.title: Sear the beef hard
steps.content: Blast in hot wok, 1 min, remove.
steps.detail: Wok smoking-hot — a drop of water should vaporise on contact. 1 tbsp oil, swirl to coat. Tip the beef in in a single layer. Don't stir for 60 sec — let it sear. Then toss fast for 60 sec more till edges are deep brown but centres still pink. Out to a plate. Crowding = steam; you might need to work in batches.
tips: No wok? Your widest frying pan, screaming hot, and work in two batches.
warnings: A smoking wok spits when the beef lands — stand back and add it with tongs, not fingers.

steps.title: Char the veg
steps.content: Stir-fry veg, add garlic.
steps.detail: Same wok, more oil. Drop heat slightly. Broccoli florets, sliced capsicum go in first — they take longer. 2 min tossing. Then 3 grated garlic cloves and a thumb of grated ginger. 30 sec. Veg should be bright and slightly charred, still with crunch.
tips: A tablespoon of water and a lid for 30 sec steams the broccoli stems tender without losing the char.
warnings: Garlic scorches in seconds at wok heat — keep everything moving once it's in.

steps.title: Bring it together
steps.content: Return beef, splash in oyster sauce.
steps.detail: Beef back in with any resting juices. Splash in 3 tbsp oyster sauce. Toss 60 sec till everything's glossy and coated. Off heat, drizzle sesame oil.
tips: Sauce looking thick and claggy? A splash of water loosens it to a gloss that coats.

steps.title: Serve over rice
steps.content: Over rice.
steps.detail: Pile over hot jasmine rice. Sliced spring onion scattered on top. Chilli flakes or fresh chilli if you want heat. Eat immediately while the wok heat is still in the dish.

title: Greek Lamb Souva
description: Grilled lamb cutlets, tzatziki, warm bread. Backyard Friday.
storage: Tzatziki keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge; cooked cutlets 3 days too. Don't freeze the tzatziki — yoghurt splits. Assemble the wraps fresh.
make_ahead: Tzatziki improves overnight — make it a day ahead. The lamb can sit in its marinade a few hours, but no longer with the lemon in it.
leftovers: Cold lamb and tzatziki over chopped tomato, cucumber and torn bread makes a next-day salad worth having.

substitutions.swap: lamb shoulder steaks, or chicken thighs — cook thighs right through, no pink
pita or Lebanese bread
dill, for a more traditional tzatziki
any thick plain yoghurt — just not sweetened

steps.title: Marinate the cutlets
steps.content: Marinate the lamb in oil, lemon and oregano.
steps.detail: Lamb cutlets are quick-cooking chops, so skip the cubing — just trim any heavy fat and toss them in a bowl with a glug of oil, the juice of half a lemon, the oregano, salt and pepper. Let them sit while you make the tzatziki; even 15 min of marinating lifts them.
tips: Let the cutlets come to room temperature while they marinate — cold centres stay raw while the outside chars.
warnings: Wash hands and anything the raw lamb touched before you start on the tzatziki.

steps.title: Make the tzatziki
steps.content: Grate cucumber into yoghurt with garlic and mint.
steps.detail: Grate half a cucumber on the coarse side, then squeeze the shreds hard in a tea towel to get rid of the water — watery tzatziki is sad tzatziki. Tip squeezed cucumber into a bowl with 1 cup yoghurt, 2 grated garlic cloves, big handful chopped mint, juice of half a lemon, pinch salt. Stir. Rest 10 min for flavours to meld.
tips: Squeeze the cucumber in two lots — smaller handfuls wring out drier.

steps.title: Char the lamb
steps.content: Grill the cutlets 6 min.
steps.detail: BBQ or griddle pan blazing hot, oil the grills. Lay the cutlets on and don’t move them for 2–3 min so a proper char forms. Flip once and give another 2–3 min for blushing pink in the middle. Rest a couple of minutes before serving — they keep cooking off the heat.
tips: Pink and juicy is right for cutlets — pull them when springy with a little give, or 60°C at the thickest part.
warnings: Lamb fat flares on the BBQ — keep a cooler zone free and shift the cutlets over if flames jump.

steps.title: Roll the souvas
steps.content: Serve in warm flatbread with tzatziki.
steps.detail: Warm flatbreads 15 sec a side on the BBQ. Smear tzatziki down the middle, lay the grilled cutlets on top. Sliced tomato, red onion, extra oregano, squeeze of lemon. Roll tight, eat standing up.
tips: Slice the red onion paper-thin and soak it in cold water for 5 min if raw onion bites too hard.

title: Fried Egg Sandwich
description: Yolk, butter, sauce of your choosing.
equipment: non-stick frying pan with a lid
storage: Not a keeper — assembled, it goes soggy within the hour. Eat it the moment it's built.

substitutions.swap: tomato or BBQ sauce if vegemite's not your thing
a soft roll or an English muffin, split and toasted

steps.title: Dress the bread
steps.content: Butter bread, scrape one side with vegemite.
steps.detail: Two slices of soft white bread (sourdough works too if you want grown-up). Butter generously on ONE side — butter side goes inside. On the other slice, scrape the thinnest layer of vegemite — it's powerful, a smear not a blanket. Balance is everything.
tips: Room-temp butter spreads without tearing the bread — 10 sec in the microwave if it's fridge-hard.

steps.title: Cook the eggs your way
steps.content: Fry eggs to your liking.
steps.detail: Hot non-stick pan, knob of butter. Crack 2 eggs straight in. For runny yolks: cover with a lid for 90 sec — steam cooks the tops while bottoms crisp. For over-easy: flip once carefully, 10 sec. For well-done: 3 min uncovered. Your call.
tips: Whites fully set and opaque right up to the yolk's edge is your visual for done.

steps.title: Stack and press
steps.content: Sandwich. Mess is expected.
steps.detail: Eggs onto the vegemite slice, butter slice on top (buttered side DOWN, touching egg). Press gently. Yolk breaks and floods the bread — that's the whole point. Eat over a plate. Mess is expected.

title: Mac & Cheese
description: Béchamel, cheddar, pasta, bubbly top. Comfort.
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge and freezes in portions up to 2 months. Reheat covered in a 180°C oven, or microwave with a splash of milk to loosen the sauce.
make_ahead: Assemble the whole dish (minus baking) up to a day ahead and fridge it; bake straight from cold, adding 10 minutes.
leftovers: Cold mac fries into crisp golden squares in a buttered pan — arguably better than round one.

substitutions.swap: tasty is fine; go half gruyère if you're feeling fancy
panko, or stale bread blitzed to crumbs
macaroni is classic; penne, shells or spirals all work

steps.title: Undercook the pasta
steps.content: Cook pasta.
steps.detail: Big pot of heavily salted water, rolling boil. Cook macaroni 2 min LESS than packet — it'll finish in the oven and you don't want mush. Drain. Don't rinse — the starch helps sauce cling.
tips: Salt the water like the sea — about a tablespoon; it's your only chance to season the pasta itself.

steps.title: Build the white sauce
steps.content: Make béchamel: melt butter, whisk in flour, milk.
steps.detail: Same pot, medium heat. Melt 60g butter. Whisk in 60g flour (equal weights = classic roux). Cook 2 min stirring constantly — the paste turns blonde and smells nutty. Raw flour tastes chalky; cook it out. Slowly pour in 750ml warmed milk in a steady stream, whisking constantly. It'll thicken as it heats. Bring to a bare simmer; cook 5 min till it coats the back of a spoon.
tips: Lumps happen — beat hard with the whisk, or worst case blitz smooth with a stick blender and carry on.

steps.title: Melt in the cheddar
steps.content: Off heat, melt in cheese.
steps.detail: OFF THE HEAT. Handful at a time, whisk in 250g grated cheddar till melted and smooth. If the pan's too hot when cheese goes in, the sauce splits and goes grainy. A grating of nutmeg, a teaspoon of dijon, salt, pepper.
tips: Grate the block yourself — pre-grated bags are dusted with starch and can melt grainy.

steps.title: Coat the pasta
steps.content: Fold in pasta.
steps.detail: Tip drained pasta into the cheese sauce, fold through till every macaroni is coated in cheese silk. Looks loose? Good — it firms up in the oven.

steps.title: Crumb and bake
steps.content: Top with crumbs, bake 15 min at 200°C.
steps.detail: Transfer to a baking dish. Scatter breadcrumbs (buttered if you want extra crunch — toss crumbs with 1 tbsp melted butter first) across the top, a little more cheese. 200°C, 15 min till the top is deeply gold and the edges are bubbling. Rest 5 min — molten cheese burns tongues.
tips: Set the dish on a tray — a bubbling-over mac is a sad oven to clean.

title: Lentil Bolognese
description: All the depth of a slow ragù. Zero meat.
storage: The sauce keeps 4 days airtight in the fridge and freezes up to 3 months. Reheat with a splash of water — it thickens as it sits. Store pasta separately.
make_ahead: Like any ragu it's better the next day — make the sauce up to 3 days ahead and reheat gently while the pasta cooks.
leftovers: The ragu doubles as a jacket potato topping, a lasagne filling or a base for baked eggs.

substitutions.swap: brown or green lentils — simmer 10 min longer and expect a chunkier ragu
water plus a stock cube, or plain water and season harder at the end
any pasta shape you've got

steps.title: Sweat the soffritto
steps.content: Soften diced carrot, onion, garlic.
steps.detail: Finely dice 2 carrots, 1 onion, 3 garlic cloves. Small dice = every spoonful catches all three. Glug of olive oil in a heavy pot over medium. Veg in with a pinch of salt, 10 min till onion is translucent and carrot is softening. This soffritto is the depth; don't rush.
tips: If the veg starts catching before it's soft, a splash of water and a lower flame resets things.

steps.title: Load the pot
steps.content: Add lentils, stock, tomatoes.
steps.detail: Tip in 1.5 cups of red lentils (rinsed till the water runs clearer), 500ml vegetable stock, 2 tins of tomatoes (crush with your hand). Splash water into the empty tins to rinse out the rest and tip that in too. Big pinch of salt, pepper, bay leaf if you have one.
tips: No bay leaf? A pinch of dried oregano or a parmesan rind does the same background work.

steps.title: Simmer low and slow
steps.content: Simmer 35 min till thick.
steps.detail: Bring to a bubble, drop to a simmer, lid slightly ajar. 35 min stirring every 10 min so lentils don't catch on the bottom. They break down into a thick, meaty-textured ragu. A splash of milk or a knob of butter at the end (plant milk or olive oil to keep it vegan) rounds the edges off the tomato acid.
tips: Done when a spoon dragged across the base leaves a clear trail that holds for a second.

steps.title: Marry sauce and spaghetti
steps.content: Toss with cooked spaghetti.
steps.detail: Cook spaghetti 1 min less than the packet in heavily salted water. Reserve half a cup of pasta water. Toss pasta into the sauce with a splash of water — creates the emulsion that makes the sauce cling. Plate up, generous parmesan (use nutritional yeast for vegan), crack of pepper. Torn basil.
tips: Tongs beat a spoon here — lift and drop the pasta through the sauce till every strand is coated.

title: Roast Veg Couscous
description: Meal-prep friendly, pretty, bright.
equipment: large heatproof bowl with a plate or foil to cover
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge; eat it cold, at room temperature, or microwave 30 seconds. Don't freeze — the veg turns watery.
make_ahead: The whole salad holds well — make it a day ahead, keep the feta separate and crumble it on when serving.
leftovers: Fold leftovers through baby spinach with a spoonful of yoghurt for a quick lunch bowl.

substitutions.swap: goat's cheese, or shaved parmesan
pumpkin, cut to the same size
pearl couscous or quinoa — cook per packet instead

steps.title: Roast the veg
steps.content: Chop and roast veg 30 min, 200°C.
steps.detail: Cube sweet potato into 2cm chunks, red onion into wedges, zucchini into half-moons, capsicum into rough 3cm pieces. Spread on a tray in a single layer — crowded veg steam instead of caramelise. Toss with a good glug of oil, salt, pepper, any dried herbs you have. 200°C, 30 min, shaking the tray halfway.
tips: Cut the sweet potato a touch smaller than everything else — it's the slowest to cook.

steps.title: Soak the couscous
steps.content: Make couscous per packet.
steps.detail: Per packet usually: equal parts couscous and boiling water (or stock for more flavour), salt, splash of oil. Cover with a plate or foil, rest 10 min. Fluff vigorously with a fork to separate grains. Cous-cous loves a fluff — pressed-together grains become a lump.
tips: Boiling water straight from the kettle, not hot-tap water — couscous needs the full heat to steam through.

steps.title: Dress it warm
steps.content: Toss veg through couscous with lemon.
steps.detail: Tip the hot roasted veg straight onto the couscous while everything is still warm — warm absorbs dressing better. Juice and zest of a lemon, extra glug of olive oil, toss with a big spoon. Taste — more salt if flat, more lemon if rich.
tips: Zest the lemon before you juice it — it's near impossible after.

steps.title: Finish with feta
steps.content: Crumble feta on top.
steps.detail: Crumble feta over the top in rough chunks — don't stir it through or it disappears. Pine nuts or almonds toasted dry 3 min in a pan add crunch. Mint or parsley torn over. Eats warm or fridge-cold tomorrow for lunchboxes.

title: Chicken Parma
description: Schnitty, napoli, ham, cheese. The classic.
equipment: meat mallet or rolling pin and baking paper
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge; reheat in a 180°C oven so the crumb revives. Freeze crumbed, uncooked schnitties up to 2 months rather than finished parmas.
make_ahead: Crumb the schnitties up to a day ahead (on a tray in the fridge, loosely covered — the crumb sets) and make the napoli 2 days out.
leftovers: Cold parma, shredded lettuce and mayo in a crusty roll — the parma sanga is its own reward.

substitutions.swap: thigh fillets bashed flat — juicier, a touch longer in the pan
leave it off entirely and you've got a cheese schnitty — still great
a pizza-blend bag or young cheddar melts fine
panko for a shaggier, crunchier crust

steps.title: Flatten, crumb and fry
steps.content: Crumb and shallow-fry schnitties.
steps.detail: Butterfly breasts (slice horizontally to open like a book, don't cut all the way), bash between baking paper till 1cm thick. Flour-egg-crumb each piece, pressing firmly. Shallow-fry in 5mm of butter-oil mix over medium, 3 min a side till deep gold. Drain on paper. You want cooked-through but crumb only just set — it finishes in the oven. Check one before saucing: cut into the thickest part — no pink and juices running clear, or 74°C on a thermometer.
tips: One hand for dry, one for wet when crumbing — flour and crumbs with one, egg with the other.
warnings: Hot soapy water for hands, board and mallet after the raw chicken — before you touch anything else.
Lower the schnitties in away from you — 5mm of hot fat spits.

steps.title: Cook down the napoli
steps.content: Reduce tomato with garlic, salt.
steps.detail: Same pan, drop heat to medium. Tip in a tin of tomatoes (crush with your hand) and 2 grated garlic cloves. Big pinch of salt, teaspoon of sugar if it's sharp. Simmer 10 min till thickened to a spoonable sauce. Taste — should be punchy; it stands up to cheese.
tips: It's reduced enough when a spoon dragged through leaves a gap that fills back slowly.

steps.title: Stack the parmas
steps.content: Top each schnitty with napoli, ham, mozzarella.
steps.detail: Schnitties on a baking tray. Ladle napoli over each (generous but not drowning — edges should still peek). Lay a slice of ham on each. Then tear mozzarella into chunks and dot over, letting some hang over the edges where it'll crisp. Optional: handful of grated parmesan for extra sharp.
tips: Sauce mostly where the ham will sit — bare crumb at the edges stays crisp under the grill.

steps.title: Blister the cheese
steps.content: Grill till melted.
steps.detail: Under a hot grill (top heat) for 4–6 min till the cheese is bubbling and deeply blistered with gold spots — some char is the whole point of parma. Watch it — cheese goes from gold to burnt in seconds. Rest 2 min. Chippies and salad alongside is pub-law.
tips: Set the tray about 10 cm below the element — closer scorches the top before the cheese melts through.

title: Overnight Oats
description: Tomorrow-you will thank today-you.
equipment: 500 ml jar or container with a tight lid
storage: Keeps 2 days sealed in the fridge; it thickens as it sits, so loosen with a splash of milk before eating. Don't freeze.
make_ahead: This is make-ahead by design — assemble two or three jars on Sunday night and breakfast is sorted till midweek.

substitutions.swap: any milk you drink — oat and soy both work well
maple syrup, or half a mashed banana for no added sugar
grated apple with a pinch of cinnamon, or chopped fresh fruit
coconut yoghurt to keep it dairy-free (use a plant milk too)

steps.title: Mix the base
steps.content: Mix oats, milk, yoghurt, honey in a jar.
steps.detail: Into a jar or a container with a tight lid: 1/2 cup rolled oats (not instant — instant goes gluey), 1/2 cup milk (dairy or oat or whatever you drink), 1/3 cup yoghurt, 1 tsp honey. Stir thoroughly so no dry pockets remain. The oats will swell and thicken overnight; if it looks too liquidy now, that's normal.
tips: Run a butter knife around the inside of the jar after stirring — dry oats love to hide against the glass.

steps.title: Add the berries
steps.content: Top with berries.
steps.detail: Scatter 1/2 cup berries over the top — frozen is fine, they'll half-thaw by morning and bleed colour into the oats. Don't stir them through yet; keep them visible on top for when you open the jar tomorrow. A sprinkle of chia seeds adds a pudding-like texture if you have some.
tips: Leave a couple of centimetres of headroom in the jar — the oats swell overnight.

steps.title: Chill overnight
steps.content: Lid on, fridge overnight.
steps.detail: Lid on tight. Fridge at least 4 hours, ideally overnight. Morning-you opens it, gives it a stir (oats will be thick and creamy), maybe adds more honey or a splash of milk if it's too thick. Extra toppings in the morning: sliced banana, chopped nuts, a spoon of peanut butter. Eats cold or warmed 30 sec in the microwave if you're a warm-breakfast person.

title: Pancakes
description: Stack, butter, syrup. Weekend mandate.
equipment: oven on low with a heatproof plate for stacking
storage: Cooked pancakes keep 2 days airtight in the fridge, or freeze up to 2 months with baking paper between layers. Reheat in the toaster or a dry pan.
make_ahead: Whisk the dry ingredients into a jar up to a week ahead — morning-you only has to add milk, egg and melted butter.
leftovers: Cold pancakes spread with butter and jam and rolled up make a decent lunchbox surprise.

substitutions.swap: self-raising flour — skip the baking powder if you do
buttermilk for extra-fluffy pancakes, same amount
3 tbsp neutral oil in the batter, though butter still wins in the pan

steps.title: Make the batter
steps.content: Whisk dry, then wet ingredients.
steps.detail: In one bowl: 1.5 cups plain flour, 2 tsp baking powder, 2 tbsp sugar, pinch of salt. Whisk to break up lumps. In a separate jug: 1.5 cups milk, 1 egg, 50g melted cooled butter. Beat with a fork. Tip wet into dry and whisk just till combined — lumps are FINE. Overmixed batter makes tough, flat pancakes. Gluten is the enemy here.
tips: Melt the butter first thing so it cools while you measure — hot butter cooks the egg on contact.

steps.title: Rest the batter
steps.content: Rest 10 min.
steps.detail: Cover the bowl with a tea towel and leave on the bench 10 min. The flour hydrates, the baking powder activates, the gluten relaxes. Skipping this gives you chewy pancakes. While it rests, get a warm plate ready in the oven (lowest setting) for stacking finished ones.
tips: It thickens as it sits — that's the flour drinking, not a mistake. Don't thin it out.

steps.title: Cook in batches
steps.content: Cook 2 min a side in buttered pan.
steps.detail: Non-stick pan over medium — not high, high burns outside before inside cooks. Swirl a knob of butter. Ladle in 1/4 cup batter per pancake. Wait till bubbles form and pop across the surface, 2 min. Flip once — second side cooks faster, about 90 sec. Don't press down with the spatula; squashes the air out.
tips: The first pancake is a tester — it tells you if the pan is too hot. Adjust and carry on.
Wipe the pan with paper towel between batches so the butter doesn't burn and speckle them.

steps.title: Stack and serve
steps.content: Stack unreasonably high.
steps.detail: Stack on that warm plate — five or six high, impressive is the whole point. Pat of butter on top of the stack so it melts down between the layers. Maple syrup poured generously. Berries or sliced banana if you're feeling wholesome. Eat fast; nobody likes cold pancakes.

title: Fish & Chips
description: Beer batter, crispy chips, fresh lemon.
equipment: oven and large tray (if baking the chips)
storage: Best eaten straight away, and don't freeze once fried. Leftover fish keeps 1–2 days airtight in the fridge; re-crisp in a 200°C oven for 10 min, never the microwave.
make_ahead: The chips' first fry can be done a few hours ahead — leave them on a rack, then give them the hot second fry just before serving. The batter must be made fresh and cold.
leftovers: Flake leftover fish into soft tacos with slaw and a squeeze of lime.

substitutions.swap: any firm white fish — flathead, snapper or ling all batter beautifully
cold soda water — same bubbles and crunch, no alcohol

steps.title: Start the chips
steps.content: Cut chips, twice-fry or oven-bake.
steps.detail: Cut potatoes into 1cm-thick chips, skin on. For twice-fry: first fry in 160°C oil for 6 min till soft but pale, drain, then blast at 190°C for 3 min till deeply gold and crisp. For oven: toss with 3 tbsp oil, salt, spread on a tray, 220°C for 30 min flipping once. Start the chips first — they take longest.
tips: Soak the cut chips in cold water 10 min and dry well — rinsing off starch makes them crisper.
Floury potatoes like sebago give the fluffiest middles.
warnings: Dry the chips thoroughly before they go anywhere near the oil — water in hot oil spits violently.

steps.title: Mix the beer batter
steps.content: Make batter with flour, beer, salt.
steps.detail: In a bowl, whisk 1 cup plain flour, 1 tsp baking powder, pinch of salt with 1 cup of cold beer. Cold is key — the cold batter hits hot oil for maximum crisp. Whisk just till smooth; a few lumps are fine. Should be the consistency of pouring cream. Rest 5 min.
tips: Make the batter only once the oil is nearly at temperature — it loses its fizz if it waits around.

steps.title: Fry the fish
steps.content: Dip fish, fry 4 min till crisp.
steps.detail: Heat 5cm of neutral oil in a heavy pot to 180°C (a cube of bread should turn gold in 30 sec). Pat fish fillets dry, dust lightly in flour first (helps batter cling). Dip in batter, let excess drip, lower gently into oil away from you. 4 min till deep gold and crisp. Lift onto paper towel. The fish is done when it flakes easily — open the thickest fillet gently with a fork to check.
tips: Fry two fillets at a time at most — each addition drops the oil temperature.
warnings: Never leave hot oil unattended, and keep a lid nearby — smother an oil fire, never water it.
Lower each fillet in slowly, away from you, and let go at the last moment.

steps.title: Salt and serve
steps.content: Serve with lemon.
steps.detail: Lemon wedges alongside — squeezed over JUST before eating. Malt vinegar and tartare sauce if you've got them. Eat on newspaper (or a plate if you're civilised) with the chips piled next to. Salt hot chips immediately — hot surface absorbs salt better than cool. The whole deal eaten within 10 min.

title: Thai Beef Salad
description: Rare beef, herbs, chilli, lime. Summer dinner.
storage: Once dressed it doesn't keep — the herbs blacken and the veg weeps. Undressed parts keep separately overnight, and cooked beef keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge.
make_ahead: Mix the dressing and chop the veg a few hours ahead, but cook and slice the beef at the last minute and don't dress the salad until you're ready to serve.
leftovers: Leftover beef slices are excellent in a crusty roll with the herbs and a smear of the dressing.

substitutions.swap: rump is the budget pick; sirloin or scotch fillet if you're celebrating
soy sauce at a pinch, though you lose the salty funk that makes it Thai
lemons work — add a small splash of rice vinegar to sharpen them up

steps.title: Sear and rest the steak
steps.content: Sear steak rare, rest, slice.
steps.detail: Pat steak dry, season heavily with salt and pepper. Hot pan, splash of oil. Sear 90 sec a side — rare is traditional, the warm beef cooks slightly in the dressing. 2 min a side for medium-rare if rare isn't your thing. Rest 5 min on a board (this is critical — slicing straight away loses all the juice). Slice very thin ACROSS the grain.
tips: The pan is ready when the oil shimmers and just starts to smoke — the steak should roar when it lands.
warnings: Rest and slice on a clean board, not the one that held the raw steak.

steps.title: Dress the salad
steps.content: Toss veg and herbs with fish sauce, lime, chilli.
steps.detail: In the biggest bowl you have: half a cucumber cut into chunks, 200g cherry tomatoes halved, big handful each of mint and coriander torn (not chopped), sliced red onion. Pour over the dressing: 2 tbsp fish sauce, juice of 2 limes, 1 tsp sugar, 1 thinly sliced fresh chilli. Toss gently. Taste — salty, sour, a touch sweet, hot. Balance is everything.
tips: Roll the limes hard under your palm before juicing — you'll get noticeably more out of them.
Fix one element at a time: too salty means more lime, too sour means a pinch more sugar.
warnings: Wash your hands after slicing the chilli — chilli fingers and eyes are a bad mix.

steps.title: Finish the platter
steps.content: Fan beef over the top.
steps.detail: Fan beef slices over the top of the dressed salad on a big platter — DON'T toss through. The beef wants to sit on top for presentation and so the juices mingle with the dressing. Pour any resting juices from the board over the top. Extra herbs, extra chilli if you love heat, crushed peanuts for crunch.

title: Butter Chicken
description: Melting tandoori-spiced chicken in a silky butter-tomato sauce.
equipment: large heavy frying pan with deep sides
medium saucepan with a lid for rice
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge and freezes up to 3 months — thaw overnight and reheat gently to piping hot, stirring so the cream doesn't split. Cook rice fresh, or cool it fast and use within a day.
make_ahead: Marinate the chicken the night before — overnight in the yoghurt is transformative. The finished curry also tastes better the next day, so it's a great cook-ahead dinner.
leftovers: Spoon leftovers over a jacket potato, or roll into a wrap with rice and a dollop of yoghurt for lunch.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast works but dries out faster — simmer gently and check early
coconut cream holds up fine and keeps it lighter on dairy
garam masala for a warmer, sweeter spice profile
any long-grain rice, or warmed naan on its own

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.content: Marinate chicken in yoghurt, turmeric, curry powder, 30 min.
steps.detail: Cube chicken thigh into 3cm pieces. In a bowl: yoghurt, 1 tsp turmeric, 2 tbsp curry powder, salt, a squeeze of lemon if you have it. Mix with the chicken till every piece is coated. 30 min fridge is good; overnight is transformative — yoghurt's lactic acid tenderises the meat while the spices penetrate.
tips: Turmeric stains plastic and benchtops — mix in a glass or stainless bowl and wipe spills fast.
warnings: Wash hands, board and knife with hot soapy water straight after cubing the raw chicken.

steps.title: Sear the chicken
steps.content: Sear chicken in butter, set aside.
steps.detail: Knob of butter in a heavy pan over medium-high till foaming. Shake excess marinade off each piece of chicken and sear in batches — overcrowding steams instead of sears. 2 min a side for deep gold char patches. Chicken isn't fully cooked yet — it finishes in the sauce. Remove to a plate.
tips: Leave each piece alone till it releases from the pan freely — if it's sticking, it isn't ready to turn.
warnings: Yoghurt-marinated chicken spits in hot butter — shake off the excess and lay pieces down away from you.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.content: Soften onion, garlic, ginger.
steps.detail: Same pan, don't wash. Drop heat to medium. More butter if needed. One finely diced onion, 3 grated garlic cloves, a thumb of grated ginger. 6 min till soft and golden at edges. The fond from the chicken lifts into this base.
tips: Keep ginger in the freezer and it grates like a dream — no stringy bits.

steps.title: Simmer the sauce
steps.content: Blend tomato smooth, add with cream, simmer 10 min.
steps.detail: Tip a tin of tomatoes into a blender and blitz smooth (or push through a sieve for silky texture). Pour into the pan with the onion mix. 1/2 cup cream stirred through. Bring to a bare simmer — don't boil hard once cream's in or it can split. 10 min till thickened and the raw tomato edge is gone.
tips: No blender? Buy crushed tomatoes instead of whole and they'll smooth out with a good simmer.

steps.title: Finish the chicken
steps.content: Return chicken, simmer 10 min.
steps.detail: Chicken back in with any resting juices. Simmer very gently for 10 min till the chicken is cooked through (check by cutting one in half — no pink). Taste for salt and a pinch of sugar if the tomato is sharp. A knob of extra butter at the end rounds it to silk.
tips: Get the basmati on now — it'll be ready right as the curry is.

steps.title: Serve it up
steps.content: Serve over basmati with coriander.
steps.detail: Fluff basmati. Ladle butter chicken into shallow bowls, rice alongside (not under — it goes mushy). Tear a big handful of coriander over the top. Naan if you have it, for mopping the sauce. A dollop of extra yoghurt cools the spice for anyone who wants it.

title: Lamb Kofta with Tzatziki
description: Spiced lamb skewers, cool yoghurt, warm flatbread. Friday night on the grill.
storage: Cooked kofta keep 3 days airtight in the fridge or freeze up to 3 months; reheat until piping hot. Tzatziki keeps 2 days in the fridge but won't survive the freezer.
make_ahead: Shape the kofta up to a day ahead and keep covered in the fridge — a long chill helps them hold on the grill. The tzatziki is happy made a few hours ahead too.
leftovers: Crumble leftover kofta through a tomato pasta sauce, or reheat and stuff into a pita with salad.

substitutions.swap: beef mince, or half lamb half beef for a milder kofta
pita or Lebanese bread — anything that rolls or pockets
flat-leaf parsley, or half mint half parsley

steps.title: Squeeze the onion dry
steps.content: Grate onion, squeeze dry.
steps.detail: Grate a red onion on the coarse side of a box grater into a sieve or tea towel. Squeeze HARD to remove the juice — wet onion makes kofta fall apart on the grill. Liquid out, oniony pulp left behind. Counterintuitive but essential.
tips: A clean tea towel wrings out more juice than hands alone — twist both ends like a bonbon.

steps.title: Mix and knead the lamb
steps.content: Mix lamb, onion, garlic, spices, half the mint.
steps.detail: Big bowl: 700g lamb mince, the squeezed grated onion, 3 grated garlic cloves, 2 tsp cumin, 1 tsp paprika, salt, pepper, half a handful of finely chopped mint. Mix with your hands for 60 sec — till it tightens up and becomes sticky. That stickiness is the protein binding, which keeps kofta on the skewer.
tips: Fry a teaspoon of the mix and taste it — you can't check raw lamb for seasoning.
warnings: Raw lamb: wash your hands and the bowl before touching the tzatziki ingredients.

steps.title: Shape and chill
steps.content: Shape onto skewers, chill 10 min.
steps.detail: Divide into 8 portions. Shape each around a skewer (soak wooden skewers 30 min first, or use metal) into a long 2cm-thick sausage. Press firmly so it clings. Chill 10 min — firms them up so they don't slide off on the grill. Skipping this step means half your kofta in the coals.
tips: Wet hands stop the mince sticking to you while you shape.

steps.title: Make the tzatziki
steps.content: Grate cucumber into yoghurt with remaining mint and garlic for tzatziki.
steps.detail: Grate half a cucumber on the coarse side, squeeze HARD to remove water. Tip into a bowl with 1 cup yoghurt, 2 grated garlic cloves, big handful chopped mint, juice of half a lemon, pinch of salt. Stir. This is tzatziki — rest 10 min so flavours meld.
tips: Full-fat Greek yoghurt only — the light stuff turns watery within the hour.

steps.title: Grill the kofta
steps.content: Grill skewers 4 min a side.
steps.detail: BBQ or griddle pan blazing hot, oil the grills. Kofta on, don't move for 2 min — let char marks form. Turn once and cook about 5 min a side, until firm and no longer pink in the centre — ground lamb should be cooked through, not left rare. Rest 3 min on a board.
tips: If a kofta won't release from the grill, give it another 30 seconds — they let go once properly charred.
warnings: Lamb fat drips and flares on a bbq — move the skewers away from any flame-ups.

steps.title: Wrap and serve
steps.content: Wrap with tzatziki and lemon.
steps.detail: Warm flatbreads 15 sec a side on the hot grill. Smear tzatziki down the middle, slide two kofta off the skewer on top. Squeeze of lemon, sliced tomato, extra mint. Roll tight, eat standing up. Mess is expected.

title: Pork Banh Mi
description: Crackly roll, spicy pork, pickled veg, coriander. Vietnamese sandwich royalty.
storage: Assembled rolls don't keep — the bread goes soggy fast. Cooked pork keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge and pickles a week in their brine; assemble fresh each time.
make_ahead: The pickles genuinely improve — make them up to a week ahead and keep them in their brine in the fridge. The pork can marinate overnight.
leftovers: Leftover pork and pickles over steamed rice with a fried egg makes a very good bowl.

substitutions.swap: pork shoulder steaks or chicken thigh — anything that takes a hard sear
Kewpie mayo if you have it — closer to the Vietnamese original
any chilli sauce, or a pinch of chilli flakes stirred through the soy

steps.title: Marinate the pork
steps.content: Slice pork thin, marinate in soy, sriracha, lime.
steps.detail: Freeze pork belly 15 min for easier slicing. Cut into 5mm slices across the grain. In a bowl, toss with 2 tbsp soy, 1 tbsp sriracha, juice of half a lime, a teaspoon of sugar. 15 min marinade while you prep everything else. Don't skip the sugar — it helps caramelisation.
tips: Set a timer for the 15-minute freeze — forgotten pork belly turns into a brick.
warnings: Raw pork: wash the board, knife and your hands before you start on the vegetables.

steps.title: Pickle the veg
steps.content: Pickle julienned carrot and cucumber in vinegar and sugar, 15 min.
steps.detail: Julienne one carrot and half a cucumber (thin matchsticks, 5cm long). Into a bowl with 1/3 cup vinegar, 2 tbsp sugar, pinch salt, splash of water. Submerge the veg. 15 min minimum — the crunch stays but the sharpness mellows and the acid cuts through rich pork.
tips: A vegetable peeler gets you started — take ribbons, then stack and slice into matchsticks.

steps.title: Caramelise the pork
steps.content: Sear pork in hot pan till caramelised.
steps.detail: Hot pan, splash of oil. Pork in a single layer, don't crowd — it'll stew instead of sear if piled up. 90 sec one side till deep caramelised, flip, 60 sec the other. Sugar in the marinade will hit the pan and catch easily — medium-high not max. Slices this thin cook through in the searing time — they're done when no pink remains and the edges are lacquered dark.
tips: Cook in two batches if your pan is small — colour matters more than speed here.
warnings: The sugary marinade spits when it hits the hot pan — lay each slice down away from you.

steps.title: Build the rolls
steps.content: Split baguettes, spread mayo, stack pork, pickles, coriander, chilli.
steps.detail: Split a crusty baguette lengthways — don't cut all the way through, hinge it open. Smear mayo on one cut side, sriracha on the other if you want extra heat. Stack pork, then drained pickles, a fistful of coriander, sliced fresh chilli. Close tightly. Crackly bread, cool pickles, rich pork, herby fresh — that's banh mi.
tips: Pull a little of the soft crumb out of each baguette — more room for filling, less jaw work.

title: Rainbow Quinoa Salad
description: Fluffy quinoa, roast pumpkin, beetroot, feta, seeds. Meal-prep gold.
equipment: small frying pan for toasting the almonds
storage: Dressed, it keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge — the rocket wilts but stays pleasant. Add the feta and almonds fresh each day if you can, and don't freeze.
make_ahead: Roast the veg, cook the quinoa and shake the dressing up to 2 days ahead — store them separately and toss with the rocket when you're ready to eat.
leftovers: Stuff leftovers into a wrap with hummus for a lunch that travels well.

substitutions.swap: couscous or brown rice (couscous means it's no longer gluten-free)
goat's cheese, or Danish feta for something creamier
pepitas, walnuts or cashews — whatever's in the cupboard
vacuum-packed cooked beets — skip the roasting and add them at the toss

steps.title: Roast the veg
steps.content: Roast diced pumpkin and beetroot 30 min at 200°C.
steps.detail: Cube pumpkin into 2cm chunks, beetroot into 1.5cm (smaller — beets take longer). Line a tray so beet juice doesn't stain. Toss with 2 tbsp oil, salt, pepper. Spread in a single layer. 200°C oven, 30 min till edges are caramelised and a knife slides through easily. The pumpkin will be golden, beets deep purple.
tips: Keep the pumpkin and beetroot at opposite ends of the tray so everything doesn't turn pink.
Beetroot stains hands — a splash of lemon juice scrubs it off.

steps.title: Cook the quinoa
steps.content: Rinse and cook quinoa in 3 cups water, 15 min.
steps.detail: Rinse 1.5 cups quinoa in a fine sieve under cold water for 30 sec — washes off a natural bitter coating called saponin. Skip it and the salad tastes soapy. Into a pot with 3 cups water, pinch salt, bring to a boil, lid on, drop to low. 15 min till you see little curled tails on each grain. Off heat 5 min covered, then fluff.
tips: Cook it in stock instead of water if you have some — quinoa drinks up flavour.

steps.title: Shake the dressing
steps.content: Whisk lemon juice with olive oil for dressing.
steps.detail: In a jar: juice and zest of a lemon, 3 tbsp olive oil, pinch of salt, pinch of sugar if you want to round off the lemon. Shake till emulsified (creamy-thick, not separated). Taste — should be bright. Too sharp? More oil or a drizzle of honey.
tips: Zest the lemon before you juice it — near impossible the other way around.

steps.title: Toss it together
steps.content: Toss quinoa, roast veg, rocket, mint with dressing.
steps.detail: Biggest bowl you have. Cooked quinoa (room temp is fine), roasted veg (slightly warm is great — absorbs dressing better), three handfuls of rocket, torn mint. Pour over the dressing and toss gently with tongs — don't mash the beets into everything or it all turns pink.

steps.title: Finish with feta
steps.content: Crumble feta, scatter almonds.
steps.detail: Crumble feta generously over the top — don't stir it through or it disappears. Scatter toasted almonds for crunch (toast almonds dry in a pan 3 min till fragrant). Eats warm, room temp, or fridge-cold tomorrow for lunch. Better the next day as the flavours meld.
tips: Almonds go from toasted to burnt in seconds — tip them out the moment they smell nutty.

title: Sticky Date Pudding
description: The pub classic. Dark, toffee-soaked sponge. Serve with cream, obviously.
equipment: 20 cm square cake tin and baking paper
storage: The pudding keeps 3 days airtight (in the fridge in warm weather) and freezes up to 3 months. The sauce has cream in it, so refrigerate it — it sets like toffee when cold, but loosens again over low heat with a splash of cream.
make_ahead: Bake the pudding a day ahead — it reheats beautifully (20 sec a square in the microwave). Make the sauce fresh; it only takes 5 minutes.
leftovers: Crumble a leftover square over vanilla ice cream and call it a sundae.

substitutions.swap: pitted prunes — surprisingly good, with a slightly darker flavour
self-raising flour — drop the 2 tsp baking powder (keep the bicarb for the dates)

ingredients.amount: 300 ml thickened (for sauce), plus extra to serve

steps.title: Soak the dates
steps.content: Soak dates in 1 cup boiling water, 10 min.
steps.detail: Pit the dates if they're not already (you'll know — if you bite one, you'll know fast). Chop roughly. Cover with 1 cup boiling water and 1 tsp bicarb (the bicarb breaks down the dates and neutralises their acidity). Rest 10 min — they swell and soften into a paste. Mash gently with a fork.
tips: The water will fizz when the bicarb goes in — that's it working on the dates.

steps.title: Cream butter, add eggs
steps.content: Cream butter and sugar, beat in eggs.
steps.detail: In a stand mixer or with electric beaters: 125g soft butter and 1 cup brown sugar, beat 3 min till pale and fluffy. Pale = aerated = tender crumb. Add 2 eggs one at a time, beating between. If it looks curdled, a tablespoon of flour fixes it.
tips: The butter should dent easily under a finger but not look oily — 20 sec microwave bursts if it's fridge-hard.

steps.title: Fold the batter
steps.content: Fold in flour, baking powder, date mix, vanilla.
steps.detail: Sift in 1.5 cups plain flour and 2 tsp baking powder. Fold with a spatula till just combined — don't beat. Add the date mix (with liquid) and 1 tsp vanilla. Fold gently. Batter should be thick and pourable.
tips: Scrape right to the bottom of the bowl once — flour pockets hide down there.

steps.title: Bake the pudding
steps.content: Bake 30 min at 180°C.
steps.detail: Line a square 20cm tin with baking paper (overhang for easy lifting). Pour in batter, smooth top. 180°C oven, middle rack, 30 min. Skewer comes out with a few moist crumbs, not raw batter. It'll sink slightly in the middle — that's perfect for catching sauce.
warnings: Don't open the oven before the 25-minute mark — a rush of cold air can sink the middle.

steps.title: Make the toffee sauce
steps.content: Simmer brown sugar, butter, cream for sauce.
steps.detail: Same pot, no wash needed. Melt 120g butter with 1 cup brown sugar and 300ml thickened cream. Whisk till the sugar dissolves. Bring to a bare simmer for 3 min till glossy and thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. Rich, toffee-like, dangerous.
warnings: Hot sugar-and-cream sauce burns far worse than boiling water — don't taste it straight from the pot.

steps.title: Soak and serve
steps.content: Pour over warm pudding, serve with thickened cream.
steps.detail: Poke holes in the warm pudding with a skewer. Pour half the hot sauce over — it soaks in and disappears. Let it drink. Slice into squares, plate warm with the rest of the sauce poured on top and a dollop of thickened cream or ice cream. Don't refrigerate the sauce — it sets like toffee.
tips: Warm the plates — the sauce stays glossy instead of setting on contact.

title: Pumpkin & Sage Risotto
description: Sweet roast pumpkin folded through creamy rice, crispy sage on top.
equipment: wide heavy pan for the risotto
saucepan to keep the stock warm
storage: Cool leftovers quickly and get them into the fridge within the hour — cooked rice shouldn't sit out. Eat within a day, reheated piping hot with a splash of stock to loosen; freezing wrecks the texture.
make_ahead: Roast the pumpkin and crisp the sage earlier in the day. The risotto itself wants to be stirred, finished and eaten in one sitting.
leftovers: Roll cold risotto into balls, crumb and shallow-fry them — arancini is the whole reason to cook extra.

substitutions.swap: extra stock with a squeeze of lemon — you lose a little depth, not the dish
