steps.title: Gloss and serve
steps.content: Pour in the sauce, toss to gloss, serve over rice.
steps.detail: Pour the sauce down the side of the hot wok so it sizzles, then toss for a minute until it thickens slightly and coats everything in a shine. If it looks dry, a splash of water loosens it; oyster sauce is already salty so taste before adding more soy. Scatter the green spring-onion tops over and serve straight away on steamed jasmine rice. Before plating, cut the biggest piece of chicken open — it should be white right through with clear juices.

title: Beef Rendang
description: Indonesia's slow-cooked dry curry — beef braised in spiced coconut milk until the sauce caramelises into a dark, clinging glaze.
equipment: saucepan with lid for the rice
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge and freezes brilliantly for up to 3 months. Reheat gently with a splash of water until piping hot, and cool any leftover rice fast — eat it within a day.
make_ahead: Rendang is famously better the next day — make it fully up to 3 days ahead and reheat gently with a splash of water.
leftovers: Shred leftover rendang into toasted rolls with cucumber, or fold it through fried rice.

substitutions.swap: chuck or gravy beef — any cut with connective tissue that loves a long braise
1–2 tsp chilli flakes, added with the turmeric

steps.title: Blitz the spice paste
steps.content: Blitz a spice paste from onion, garlic, ginger and chilli.
steps.detail: Roughly chop the onions, garlic, ginger and chilli, then blend to a rough paste with a splash of water (a stick blender in a jug works fine). This paste is the backbone of the curry — pounding or blending it releases far more flavour than just chopping. Leave the seeds in the chilli for more heat or scrape them out for a milder, family-friendly version.
tips: A rough, slightly coarse paste fries better than a fine watery purée.
warnings: Wash your hands after handling the chilli — the burn lingers and finds its way to eyes.

steps.title: Fry paste until fragrant
steps.content: Fry the paste with turmeric until fragrant, 8 min.
steps.detail: Heat a glug of oil in a heavy pot and cook the paste with the turmeric over medium heat, stirring often, until it darkens and smells deeply aromatic rather than raw and sharp. This is the most important step — undercooked paste tastes harsh. It'll spit a little; keep the heat moderate so it doesn't catch and turn bitter.
tips: Watch the colour — it shifts from bright and raw to a deeper rusty orange as it comes good.

steps.title: Add beef and coconut milk
steps.content: Add beef, coconut milk and whole spices.
steps.detail: Cut the brisket into large 4cm chunks — it shrinks a lot over the long cook, so don't go small. Add it to the pot with the coconut milk, cinnamon stick and star anise, plus a good pinch of salt. Stir so every piece is submerged. Brisket is ideal because its connective tissue melts to gelatine over hours, giving that silky texture leaner cuts never reach.
tips: Cut across the grain of the brisket — the chunks end up more tender for it.
warnings: Wash your hands, board and knife after cutting the raw beef, before you touch anything else.

steps.title: Simmer low, 90 minutes
steps.content: Simmer uncovered and low for 1.5 hours.
steps.detail: Bring to a gentle simmer, then keep it on the lowest steady bubble, uncovered, stirring every 15 min or so. The liquid slowly reduces and the beef turns fork-tender. Don't rush with high heat — that boils the coconut milk into a greasy split mess instead of letting it gradually thicken into sauce. Top up with a little water only if it dries before the beef is tender.
tips: The beef is ready for the next stage when a fork twists into a chunk with almost no resistance.

steps.title: Reduce to a glaze
steps.content: Reduce hard at the end until the sauce caramelises, 30–45 min.
steps.detail: Once the beef is tender, let the sauce keep reducing. This is what makes a rendang rather than a curry — the coconut oil eventually separates out and starts to fry the meat in its own spiced paste, going from pale to a glossy dark brown. Stir constantly now so the bottom doesn't scorch. It's done when the sauce clings to the beef and there's almost no liquid left.
tips: Drop the heat if the gentle crackle turns to sharp spitting — you want frying, not scorching.
Put the jasmine rice on now — it'll be ready as the rendang comes off.

steps.title: Settle, then serve
steps.content: Rest, then serve with jasmine rice.
steps.detail: Fish out the cinnamon stick and star anise, then let the rendang sit off the heat for 10 min so the flavours settle. Like most braises it tastes even better the next day once the spices mellow and deepen. Serve over steamed jasmine rice to soak up every bit of the dark, intense sauce.

title: Tofu Japchae
description: Korean stir-fried noodles tossed in a sweet-savoury soy-sesame sauce with crisp veg, golden tofu and a hum of gochujang.
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge; don't freeze — glass noodles turn brittle, then mushy. Eat it cold, at room temperature, or flash it through a hot pan.
make_ahead: Japchae holds beautifully — make it a few hours ahead and serve at room temperature, tossing it again with a small splash of sesame oil first.
leftovers: Cold japchae is a legitimate lunch straight from the container — add a fried egg on top for dinner, round two.

substitutions.swap: mung-bean vermicelli — cook it more briefly and watch it closely
1 tsp sriracha plus a pinch more sugar — different, but the warmth is there
shredded silverbeet or kale, given an extra minute in the pan

steps.title: Crisp the tofu
steps.content: Press and pan-fry the tofu until golden.
steps.detail: Pat the tofu very dry with a tea towel and press it under a plate for 10 min while you prep — wet tofu steams and falls apart instead of crisping. Cut into 2cm cubes and fry in a little oil over medium-high, turning, until golden and firm on several sides. A crust forms a skin that holds the cubes together when you toss them through the noodles later.
tips: Turn the cubes with chopsticks or two forks — a spatula scrapes the golden crust off.

steps.title: Make sauce, boil noodles
steps.content: Mix the sauce and cook the noodles.
steps.detail: Stir the soy sauce, sesame oil, gochujang and sugar together until the sugar dissolves — this single sauce both seasons and glosses the whole dish. Boil the glass noodles to just tender, then drain and rinse briefly under cold water. Rinsing stops the cooking and washes off surface starch so they stay springy and slip apart instead of clumping into a sticky ball.
tips: Snip the drained noodles once or twice with scissors — full-length strands are a wrestle to toss and eat.

steps.title: Flash-fry the veg
steps.content: Stir-fry the vegetables separately, briefly.
steps.detail: Julienne the carrot, slice the mushrooms and cut the spring onions into batons. Stir-fry them in a hot pan with a touch of oil for just 2–3 min so they keep colour and crunch — soft, grey veg makes a sad japchae. Add the spinach at the very end and toss only until it wilts, which takes seconds; then squeeze out excess water so it doesn't dilute the sauce.
tips: Cut the carrot into matchsticks as thin as you can — after 2 minutes they should flex, not snap.

steps.title: Toss everything together
steps.content: Toss noodles, veg, tofu and sauce together.
steps.detail: Return everything to the big pan off the heat, pour over the sauce and toss with tongs until every strand is evenly coated and glistening. Doing this off the heat means the sauce coats rather than catches and burns. Taste and adjust — a little more soy for salt, a pinch more sugar to round it out, or extra gochujang if you want more warmth.
tips: Tongs plus a lifting-and-dropping motion coats the strands more evenly than stirring.

steps.title: Drizzle and serve
steps.content: Finish with sesame oil and serve warm or cool.
steps.detail: Give it a final drizzle of sesame oil and a good toss — added at the end its nutty aroma stays bright rather than cooking off. Japchae is one of the few noodle dishes that's just as good at room temperature, so it's perfect for lunchboxes or making ahead. Scatter any reserved spring-onion green over the top to serve.

title: Char Siu Pork
description: Cantonese sticky barbecue pork — lacquered in honey, soy and miso, roasted until the edges char and the inside stays juicy.
equipment: wire rack over a lined baking tray
saucepan with lid for the rice
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge, or freeze slices for up to 2 months. Reheat gently, covered, so the sugary glaze warms through without catching.
make_ahead: Marinate up to 24 hours ahead — longer is genuinely better here. You can also roast earlier in the day and flash it under the grill to re-glaze before serving.
leftovers: Char siu is the start of great fried rice, a ramen topping or a bao filling — slice it thin and go.

substitutions.swap: pork scotch fillet (neck) — same cut family and ideal for char siu
hoisin sauce, for a sweeter, more classic takeaway flavour
brown sugar dissolved in a splash of hot water

steps.title: Cut strips, mix marinade
steps.content: Cut pork into long strips and make the marinade.
steps.detail: Slice the shoulder lengthways into strips about 4cm thick — long thin pieces give you maximum charred, caramelised surface, which is the whole point of char siu. Whisk the honey, soy, miso, grated garlic, grated ginger and sesame oil into a glossy marinade. The miso stands in beautifully for the usual fermented bean paste, bringing deep savoury umami and helping the pork brown.
tips: Follow the muscle seams as you slice — the strips hold together better.
warnings: Keep one board for the raw pork and wash up before touching anything else.

steps.title: Marinate the pork
steps.content: Marinate at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.
steps.detail: Reserve a couple of spoons of marinade in the fridge for glazing, then coat the pork in the rest and leave it covered in the fridge — two hours will do, but overnight is far better. The salt and miso slowly penetrate and season the meat right through, while the sugars set it up to lacquer. Bring it back to room temperature before roasting so it cooks evenly.
tips: A snap-lock bag beats a bowl — flip it whenever you open the fridge and every side gets its turn.
warnings: The reserved marinade must never touch the raw pork — it goes on later as the glaze.

steps.title: Roast on the rack
steps.content: Roast at high heat on a rack, 25 min.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C and sit the pork on a rack over a lined tray — the rack lets heat circulate underneath and stops the strips stewing in their juices. Roast 25 min, turning once halfway. A rack also catches the drips so the sugary marinade doesn't burn against the meat and turn acrid. By the end of the 25 minutes the pork should be cooked through — firm to the touch with clear juices — because the grill step that follows only colours it.
tips: Line the tray with foil and add a splash of water — the drips won't smoke and cleanup is nothing.

steps.title: Lacquer under the grill
steps.content: Glaze with honey and blast to caramelise.
steps.detail: Brush the pork generously with the reserved marinade plus an extra drizzle of honey, then crank the oven to its highest grill setting for 5–8 min. Watch it like a hawk — the honey takes the strips from glossy to charred fast, and a minute too long means bitter black edges. You're after sticky, blistered, mahogany patches, not uniform brown.
tips: Charred spots on the high points are correct — that faint bitter edge against the sweet is the char siu flavour.
Get the jasmine rice on now — it'll be ready as the pork finishes resting.
warnings: Hot honey glaze sticks and burns skin — use tongs, not fingers, to turn the strips.

steps.title: Rest and slice thin
steps.content: Rest, then slice across the grain.
steps.detail: Let the pork rest on a board for 10 min before cutting — resting lets the juices redistribute so they stay in the meat rather than running out onto the board. Slice across the grain into thin pieces for tenderness, and serve over jasmine rice with any sticky pan juices spooned over the top.
tips: A long sharp knife drawn through in one stroke gives cleaner slices than sawing.

title: Roast Grape & Ricotta Toast
description: Grapes blistered jammy in the oven, piled on creamy ricotta toast with honey and thyme. Brunch that looks fancy, ready in about 25 minutes, mostly hands-off.
equipment: baking tray lined with baking paper
storage: The parts keep separately: roast grapes 3 days airtight in the fridge, whipped ricotta 2 days. Assembled toast doesn't keep — build it fresh each time.
make_ahead: Roast the grapes up to 2 days ahead and keep them, with their syrup, in the fridge — bring back to room temperature or rewarm briefly before serving.
leftovers: Leftover roast grapes are brilliant on porridge, thick yoghurt or a cheese board.

substitutions.swap: halved figs or quartered plums roast the same way
fresh goat's cheese or labne, whipped the same way
pecans or slivered almonds

steps.title: Roast the grapes
steps.content: Toss grapes with oil and thyme, roast 18 min at 200°C.
steps.detail: Pull the grapes off their stems and scatter on a lined tray — leave them whole so they keep their juice until they burst. Toss with 1 tbsp olive oil, a pinch of salt and the thyme sprigs, then roast at 200°C for about 18 min until the skins split and they look shrunken and jammy. Roasting drives off water and concentrates the sugar, turning ordinary supermarket grapes into something sticky and intense.
tips: Give the tray a shake halfway so the grapes colour evenly and don't stick.

steps.title: Whip the lemon ricotta
steps.content: Whip the ricotta smooth with lemon.
steps.detail: Tip the ricotta into a bowl with a squeeze of lemon, a small drizzle of olive oil and a pinch of salt, then beat with a fork or whisk for a minute until it loosens and goes light. Shop ricotta is grainy straight from the tub — a quick whip turns it spreadable and almost like a soft cheese spread. The lemon lifts it so it doesn't taste flat under the sweet grapes.
tips: Full-fat deli ricotta whips creamier than the smooth tub kind.

steps.title: Crisp the bread
steps.content: Toast the bread well.
steps.detail: Toast thick slices of a good sourdough or country loaf until properly golden and crisp — soft bread goes soggy the moment the warm grapes land. A sturdy crust gives you something to bite against the soft ricotta and bursting fruit. Drizzle each slice with a little olive oil while still hot if you like.
tips: If thick slices won't fit the toaster, a hot dry frying pan or the oven grill does the job.

steps.title: Build the toasts
steps.content: Spread ricotta, pile on grapes, drizzle honey.
steps.detail: Spread the whipped ricotta thickly over each toast, then spoon over the roast grapes along with the syrupy juices left on the tray — that sticky liquid is liquid gold, don't waste it. Drizzle with honey, scatter the optional toasted walnuts for crunch, and finish with a few thyme leaves and a crack of pepper. Eat straight away while the grapes are warm against the cool ricotta.
tips: Tilt the tray and scrape the grape syrup off with a spatula — it comes away easily while warm.

title: Kiwi-Mango Salsa over Pan-Fried Fish
description: Crisp-skinned barramundi under a fresh kiwi and mango salsa with lime and chilli. Bright, summery and on the table in twenty minutes.
storage: Cooked fish keeps 1–2 days airtight in the fridge, though the skin won't stay crisp. The salsa is best the day it's made, and neither component freezes well.
make_ahead: Dice the fruit an hour or two ahead if you like, but add the lime, salt and coriander no more than 30 minutes before eating so the salsa stays fresh, not soupy.
leftovers: Flake leftover fish through the remaining salsa with rice, or fold it into a taco with a little mayo.

substitutions.swap: any firm white fish — snapper, flathead or ling
pineapple, diced just as small
mint, if you're in the no-coriander camp

steps.title: Cube kiwi and mango
steps.content: Dice the kiwi and mango for the salsa.
steps.detail: Peel and dice the kiwi and mango into small, even 5mm cubes so every forkful has a bit of each. Choose kiwi that gives just slightly to a gentle squeeze — rock-hard ones are sour and mushy ones turn to slush. Tip into a bowl with the diced chilli (deseed it first if you want less heat) and a pinch of salt.
tips: Cut the mango cheeks off the stone, score a grid in the flesh and turn the skin inside out — the cubes pop right off.
warnings: Wash your hands after dicing the chilli — the burn transfers to eyes and lips hours later.

steps.title: Dress and rest salsa
steps.content: Dress the salsa with lime and coriander.
steps.detail: Squeeze in the juice of half the lime, add roughly chopped coriander and stir gently. Let it sit while you cook the fish — 10 min of resting lets the fruit release a little juice and the flavours marry into a proper salsa rather than just chopped fruit. Taste and add more lime or salt; it should be sharp and lively.
tips: Stir with a spoon, not a fork — the fruit bruises easily once dressed.

steps.title: Pat the fillets dry
steps.content: Dry the fish and season the skin.
steps.detail: Pat the barramundi fillets bone-dry with paper towel, especially the skin — moisture is the enemy of crisp skin, it just steams. Season both sides with salt right before cooking. Salting too early draws water out and you're back to a wet fillet.
tips: Run your fingers over the flesh and pull any pin bones now, not at the table.

steps.title: Fry skin-side down
steps.content: Pan-fry skin-side down until crisp, then flip.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a non-stick pan over medium-high until it shimmers. Lay the fillets in skin-side down and press flat with a spatula for the first 10 seconds so the skin doesn't curl away from the heat. Leave them untouched for 4–5 min until the skin is crisp and golden and the flesh has turned opaque most of the way up, then flip for just 1–2 min to finish. Resist the urge to move them — fish releases itself from the pan when the crust is ready. After the flip, check the thickest part — the flesh should be opaque right through and flake apart under gentle pressure.
warnings: Lay the fillets down away from you so any hot oil spits away from your wrist.

steps.title: Serve with salsa
steps.content: Plate fish skin-up and spoon over the salsa.
steps.detail: Sit each fillet skin-side up to keep that hard-won crisp, then spoon the kiwi-mango salsa alongside or over the flesh — not over the skin, or it softens. Squeeze the last of the lime over the top. The cool, sharp fruit against the hot, savoury fish is the whole point of the dish.

title: Grape, Walnut & Rocket Salad
description: Peppery rocket, sweet grapes, toasted walnuts and shaved parmesan in a sharp balsamic dressing. The five-minute salad that lifts any plate.
storage: Dressed leaves don't keep — the rocket wilts to nothing, so only dress what you'll eat. Undressed components keep separately in the fridge for a day or two.
make_ahead: Toast the walnuts and whisk the dressing in the serving bowl hours ahead — just keep the rocket out of it until the moment you serve.

substitutions.swap: baby spinach or a mixed-leaf blend — milder, less peppery
pecans, toasted the same way
any hard grating cheese shaved thin — grana padano or a sharp aged cheddar
red wine vinegar plus an extra pinch of honey

steps.title: Toast and chop walnuts
steps.content: Toast the walnuts in a dry pan.
steps.detail: Toast the walnuts in a dry pan over medium heat, shaking often, for 3–4 min until fragrant and a shade darker. Watch them like a hawk — nuts go from golden to burnt in seconds, and burnt walnuts taste acrid. Tip them straight out of the hot pan onto a plate so they stop cooking, then roughly chop once cool.

steps.title: Emulsify the dressing
steps.content: Whisk the dressing in the bottom of the bowl.
steps.detail: In your serving bowl, whisk the balsamic, dijon, honey and a pinch of salt together first, then drizzle in the olive oil while whisking so it emulsifies into a thick, glossy dressing rather than splitting. The dijon is the trick — it acts as an emulsifier that holds the oil and vinegar together. Building it in the bowl saves washing up.
tips: Taste it on a rocket leaf rather than a spoon — the leaf's pepper changes how much acid you want.

steps.title: Split the grapes
steps.content: Halve the grapes.
steps.detail: Cut the grapes in half lengthways. Halving does two things: it stops them rolling off the fork, and it lets their sweet juice mingle into the dressing. If you've got fat seedless grapes they're perfect; otherwise flick out any pips.
tips: Sandwich a handful between two plates and run the knife through the gap — a whole row halves in one stroke.

steps.title: Toss and shave parmesan
steps.content: Toss the rocket through the dressing and plate.
steps.detail: Add the rocket to the bowl and toss gently with your hands until just coated — dress it only at the last second, because rocket wilts fast once the acid hits it. Scatter over the grapes and walnuts, then use a peeler to shave parmesan over the top. Serve immediately while the leaves are still lively and the walnuts crunchy.

title: Tea-Poached Pears
description: Whole pears gently poached in spiced black tea until tender and amber, served with toasted hazelnuts and a drizzle of honey syrup. A lighter, fragrant take on poached pears.
equipment: medium pot just wide enough to fit the pears
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge with the pears submerged in their syrup in an airtight container. Serve cold or rewarm gently in the syrup; freezing turns the pears woolly, so don't.
make_ahead: These are better made a day ahead — poach the pears, cool them in the syrup and let them steep overnight for a deeper amber colour and more spice.
leftovers: Slice leftover pears over porridge or yoghurt, and save the syrup for drizzling on ice cream or stirring into soda water.

substitutions.swap: earl grey or chai bags — both play nicely with the spices
almonds or walnuts, toasted the same way
1/2 cup caster sugar or brown sugar dissolved into the hot tea
thick cream or vanilla ice cream

steps.title: Brew the spiced tea
steps.content: Brew the spiced tea poaching liquid.
steps.detail: Bring about 1 litre of water to the boil in a pot just wide enough to hold the pears, then take it off the heat and drop in the tea bags, cinnamon, star anise and a few strips of lemon peel. Let the tea steep 4 min then lift the bags out — leave them longer and the tannins turn the liquid bitter and astringent. Stir in the honey until it dissolves.
tips: Take the lemon peel off with the vegetable peeler in wide strips and avoid the white pith — it's the bitter part.

steps.title: Peel the pears
steps.content: Peel the pears, leaving the stalks on.
steps.detail: Choose firm, slightly underripe pears such as Beurre Bosc or Packham — ripe ones collapse into mush in the hot liquid. Peel them smoothly with a vegetable peeler but keep the stalk on as a handle and for looks. Rub each peeled pear with the cut lemon to stop it browning while you work, and slice a sliver off the base so they sit upright.
tips: Peel in long strokes from stalk to base so the surface stays smooth — choppy peeling leaves ridges that show after poaching.

steps.title: Poach at a bare simmer
steps.content: Poach gently for 25–30 min, turning.
steps.detail: Lower the pears into the tea and keep it at the barest simmer — just a few bubbles trembling at the surface. A hard boil cooks the outside to mush before the centre softens. Lay a scrunched square of baking paper on top to keep them submerged, and turn every 10 min so they colour evenly to a warm amber. They're done when a skewer slides into the thickest part with gentle resistance.
tips: If the liquid doesn't quite cover the pears, top it up with a little boiling water — the paper does the rest.

steps.title: Reduce the syrup
steps.content: Lift out the pears and reduce the syrup.
steps.detail: Set the pears aside and turn the heat up, boiling the tea liquid hard until it reduces by about half to a light, pourable syrup that just coats a spoon — usually 8–10 min. Remember it keeps thickening as it cools, so stop while it still looks a touch loose or you'll end up with toffee.
tips: Test on a cold saucer: a drop should hold a slow trail rather than run like water.
warnings: Reducing syrup is far hotter than boiling water and can spit near the end — stir with a long-handled spoon.

steps.title: Toast the hazelnuts
steps.content: Toast and chop the hazelnuts.
steps.detail: Toast the hazelnuts in a dry pan over medium heat, shaking often, until fragrant and the skins darken, about 4 min. Tip onto a clean tea towel and rub firmly to slough off the bitter papery skins, then chop roughly. Dry-toasting wakes up their oils — raw hazelnuts taste flat next to the spiced pears.
tips: Stay by the pan — nuts go from toasted to burnt in under a minute, and they keep cooking a little off the heat.

steps.title: Plate and drizzle
steps.content: Serve with syrup, hazelnuts and yoghurt.
steps.detail: Stand a pear on each plate, spoon over the warm tea syrup and scatter the hazelnuts. A dollop of cold thick yoghurt alongside cuts the sweetness beautifully. You can poach these a day ahead and let them steep in the cooled syrup overnight — they take on a deeper amber colour and even more spice.
tips: Warm pears with cold yoghurt is the point — pull the yoghurt from the fridge at the last minute.

title: Masala Chai Porridge
description: Creamy oats simmered in spiced chai with banana and toasted hazelnuts. A warming, fragrant bowl that beats plain porridge on a cold morning.
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge. It sets firm as it chills — reheat gently with a good splash of milk, stirring, until loose and creamy again. Not worth freezing.

substitutions.swap: 2 black tea bags plus a pinch each of ground cardamom, ginger and extra cinnamon
oat or soy milk both work — oat milk makes it extra creamy
almonds, walnuts or pepitas, toasted the same way
chopped dates or a handful of sultanas

steps.title: Steep the chai
steps.content: Steep the chai in warm milk.
steps.detail: Gently warm the milk in a saucepan until steaming but not boiling, then drop in the chai bags and let them steep off the heat for 4–5 min. Steeping in milk rather than water pulls the cardamom, ginger and clove deep into the dish — this is where the whole bowl gets its perfume. Squeeze and lift the bags out, pressing to get every drop of spice.
tips: Steaming looks like small bubbles at the pan's edge and wisps rising — pull it off before a full boil or the milk can catch and skin.

steps.title: Simmer the oats
steps.content: Add the oats and simmer, stirring.
steps.detail: Stir the rolled oats and cinnamon into the spiced milk and bring to a gentle simmer over medium-low heat. Stir often, scraping the base so the milk doesn't catch and scorch — thin pans burn milk fast and that taste spreads through everything. Cook 5–7 min until the oats are soft and the porridge is thick and creamy. Use rolled oats, not instant, which turn to glue.
tips: It's ready when a spoon dragged across the base leaves a trail that slowly fills back in.

steps.title: Toast the hazelnuts
steps.content: Toast the hazelnuts while it cooks.
steps.detail: In a separate dry pan, toast the hazelnuts over medium heat for 3–4 min, shaking often, until fragrant and golden under the skins. Rub off the loose skins in a tea towel and chop roughly. Toasting is worth the extra pan — it brings out a deep, buttery crunch that raw nuts just can't give.
tips: Don't wander off — they turn from golden to burnt fast, so tip them out of the hot pan the moment they smell nutty.

steps.title: Loosen and top
steps.content: Loosen, sweeten and serve topped.
steps.detail: The porridge thickens as it sits, so stir in a splash more milk to bring it back to a loose, spoonable consistency, then sweeten to taste with honey. Spoon into bowls and top with sliced banana, the toasted hazelnuts and quartered figs if you have them. A final drizzle of honey and a dusting of cinnamon finishes it off.
tips: Slice the banana at the last moment so it doesn't brown while the porridge finishes.

title: Patatas Bravas
description: Crispy fried potatoes under a smoky paprika tomato sauce — the tapas bar staple, with a little chorizo crisped through.
storage: Best eaten straight away. The sauce keeps 4 days airtight in the fridge or freezes for 3 months; leftover potatoes keep 2 days and re-crisp on a tray in a 200°C oven for about 10 min.
make_ahead: The brava sauce keeps well — make it up to 3 days ahead and reheat while the potatoes fry. You can parboil and dry the potatoes a few hours ahead too.
leftovers: Fold leftover potatoes and sauce through beaten eggs for a quick Spanish-ish omelette, or top with a fried egg for lunch.

substitutions.swap: leave it out for a vegetarian plate — add an extra 1/2 tsp smoked paprika to the sauce instead
400 ml passata for a smoother sauce with no blitzing
a little chopped chive, or skip it

steps.title: Parboil the potatoes
steps.content: Peel and cube potatoes, then parboil 6 min.
steps.detail: Cut the potatoes into roughly 3 cm chunks so they cook evenly. Start them in cold, well-salted water and bring to the boil — starting cold means the outside doesn't turn to mush before the centre softens. Simmer just until a knife slides in with a little resistance, about 6 min. Drain and let them steam-dry in the colander for 5 min; dry surfaces crisp, wet ones steam and go soggy.
tips: Give the drained chunks a rough shake in the colander — scuffed, fluffy edges fry up crispier.

steps.title: Simmer the brava sauce
steps.content: Make the brava sauce.
steps.detail: Warm a glug of olive oil over medium heat, add the minced garlic and smoked paprika, and stir for 30 seconds only — paprika scorches fast and turns bitter, so keep it moving and don't let it smoke. Tip in the tinned tomato, a pinch of salt, and simmer 10 min until thick and glossy. Blitz or mash smooth if you like it silky. Taste — it should be smoky and gently warming, not fiery.
tips: If your tomatoes taste sharp, a small pinch of sugar rounds the sauce out.

steps.title: Fry the chorizo coins
steps.content: Crisp the chorizo.
steps.detail: Slice the chorizo into coins. Fry in a dry pan over medium heat — chorizo renders its own deep-red, paprika-stained oil, so you need no extra fat. Cook until the edges crisp and curl, about 4 min, then lift out, leaving that ruby oil behind. Don't toss it — that oil is pure flavour for frying the potatoes.
tips: Slice the coins about 5 mm thick — thinner ones crisp right through, thicker ones stay chewy in the middle.

steps.title: Fry until golden
steps.content: Fry the potatoes until golden.
steps.detail: Add more olive oil to the chorizo oil to a depth of about 1 cm and bring to a shimmer over medium-high. Fry the dried potato chunks in a single uncrowded layer — crowding drops the temperature and steams them. Turn only once they've formed a crust and release easily from the pan, about 10–12 min total, until deep golden and crunchy. Drain on paper and salt straight away.
tips: Test with one chunk first — it should sizzle steadily the moment it hits the oil before the rest go in.
warnings: Hot oil spits when food goes in — make sure the potatoes are properly dry and lower them in gently, away from you.

steps.title: Sauce and serve
steps.content: Plate and serve.
steps.detail: Pile the hot potatoes on a plate, spoon the warm brava sauce over and around — not tossed through, or they soften — and scatter the crisp chorizo on top. Finish with chopped parsley if using. Serve immediately while the potatoes still crackle; bravas wait for no one.

title: Gambas al Ajillo
description: Spanish sizzling garlic prawns in chilli-spiked olive oil — ten minutes, one pan, bread for mopping.
equipment: heatproof board or trivet for the table
storage: Seafood is a 1–2 day proposition: keep leftovers airtight in the fridge and reheat very gently in a pan with the oil — the microwave turns prawns rubbery. Don't freeze once cooked.
leftovers: Toss leftover prawns and a spoonful of the garlic oil through hot spaghetti with extra parsley.

substitutions.swap: frozen peeled prawns work well — thaw fully and dry them extra hard
1/2 tsp dried chilli flakes, added with the garlic
coriander isn't traditional but works if it's what you've got

steps.title: Dry and season prawns
steps.content: Pat the prawns dry and season.
steps.detail: Peeled prawns straight from the bag are wet, and wet prawns steam grey instead of searing. Press them between paper towel until properly dry, then season with a pinch of salt. Bringing them to room temperature for 10 min also helps them cook evenly in the brief time they're in the pan.
warnings: Keep raw prawns cold until you cook them, and wash hands, board and knife after handling — don't let them touch the bread.

steps.title: Infuse the oil
steps.content: Slowly infuse the oil with garlic and chilli.
steps.detail: Thinly slice the garlic and chilli. Add them to the cold olive oil in a wide frying pan, then turn the heat to medium-low. Starting cold lets the garlic perfume the oil gently as it warms — the goal is soft, pale gold, sweet garlic. The moment it turns past gold it's bitter, so pull the pan off the heat if it's colouring too fast.
tips: Tilt the pan now and then so the oil pools and the garlic slices stay submerged and cook evenly.

steps.title: Flash-sear the prawns
steps.content: Sear the prawns.
steps.detail: Turn the heat up to medium-high and slide in the prawns in one layer. They cook fast — about 1 minute per side. Watch the colour: a prawn is done the instant it turns pink and curls into a loose C. A tight O means overcooked and rubbery. Stir in the smoked paprika off the heat so it blooms in the warm oil without scorching.
tips: Lay them in clockwise from the handle so you can flip them in the same order — the first one in is the first one turned.

steps.title: Serve straight from the pan
steps.content: Finish and serve sizzling.
steps.detail: Squeeze in the lemon, scatter over chopped parsley, and check the salt. Bring the whole pan to the table still spitting — that sizzle is the dish. Tear the crusty bread and mop up the garlicky, paprika-stained oil; in Spain the oil is the best part, never wasted.
warnings: The pan stays scorching at the table — set it on a board or trivet and point the handle where no one will knock it.

title: Tortilla Espanola
description: The classic Spanish potato omelette — soft, custardy centre, served warm or at room temperature in thick wedges.
equipment: flat plate wider than the pan
heatproof strainer and bowl for the oil
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge. Eat at room temperature or warm gently in a low oven; don't freeze — the potato and egg turn watery and grainy.
make_ahead: Tortilla is made for sitting around — cook it up to a day ahead, refrigerate, and serve at room temperature in wedges.
leftovers: A cold wedge in a crusty roll — a bocadillo de tortilla — is the classic Spanish lunch.

substitutions.swap: leave it out — Spaniards argue both ways — or use a finely sliced leek
light olive oil is fine for the poaching; save your good oil for salads

steps.title: Slice spuds and onion
steps.content: Thinly slice potatoes and onion.
steps.detail: Peel the potatoes and slice them about 3 mm thick — thin enough to go meltingly soft. A traditional tortilla isn't fried crisp; the potatoes are gently poached in oil until tender. Slice the onion finely too. Don't rinse the potatoes — the starch helps the tortilla hold together.
tips: A mandoline makes quick, even 3 mm slices if you have one — even thickness means everything softens at the same time.

steps.title: Confit in olive oil
steps.content: Poach the potatoes and onion in oil.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a non-stick pan over medium-low. Add the potatoes and onion with the salt — the oil should just cover them. Cook gently for about 15 min, stirring now and then, until a slice crushes easily between your fingers. You're confit-poaching, not frying, so keep it at a lazy bubble; brown bits mean the heat is too high.
tips: Stir from the bottom up with a spatula so the slices swap places without breaking.

steps.title: Soak potato in egg
steps.content: Drain, then fold into beaten eggs.
steps.detail: Strain the potatoes (keep that lovely oil for next time) and let them cool for a couple of minutes so they don't scramble the eggs. Beat the eggs with a pinch of salt, then fold in the warm potatoes and onion. Let it sit 5 min so the potato soaks up the egg — this is what gives a tortilla its set, sliceable body.
warnings: Strain the hot oil into a metal or heatproof bowl — never a plastic jug, and never down the sink.

steps.title: Set the first side
steps.content: Cook the first side.
steps.detail: Heat 1 tbsp of the reserved oil in the pan over medium. Pour in the mix and shape the edges into a neat round with a spatula. Cook 4–5 min, shaking the pan occasionally so it doesn't stick, until the base is set and golden but the top is still loose and wobbly. The centre stays soft on purpose.
tips: Before you flip, run the spatula around the edge and give the pan a shake — the tortilla should slide freely.

steps.title: Flip onto a plate
steps.content: Flip and finish.
steps.detail: The classic move: lay a flat plate over the pan, hold it tight, and confidently invert the whole thing — commit, or it folds. Slide the tortilla back in raw-side down and tuck the edges under for a domed shape. Cook 3 more min. Rest 5 min before cutting; it firms as it cools and is best warm, not hot, in fat wedges.
warnings: Loose egg and hot oil can dribble during the flip — hold the plate down firmly and do it over the bench, not over your feet.

title: Chilli Black Bean Soup
description: A thick, smoky black bean soup with cumin and lime — cheap, filling, and ready in half an hour from the pantry.
storage: Keeps 4 days airtight in the fridge or freezes for 3 months. It thickens as it sits — reheat until piping hot, loosening with a splash of water or stock.
make_ahead: Like most bean soups it's better the next day — make it up to 3 days ahead and squeeze in the lime just before serving so it stays bright.
leftovers: Simmer leftovers down until thick and use as a burrito, taco or nacho filling.

substitutions.swap: tinned kidney beans, or one tin of each
any colour capsicum, or a finely diced carrot
plain Greek yoghurt, or sliced avocado
chicken stock if you're not keeping it vegetarian

steps.title: Sweat the base
steps.content: Dice and soften the vegetables.
steps.detail: Finely dice the onion and capsicum, mince the garlic. Sweat the onion and capsicum in a glug of oil over medium heat for 6–8 min until soft and just starting to colour — this base of softened veg is the soup's backbone, so don't rush it. Add the garlic only in the last minute, as it burns and goes acrid faster than the rest.
tips: Soft with lightly golden edges is the goal — if the veg is browning fast, drop the heat rather than stirring harder.

steps.title: Bloom the spices
steps.content: Toast the spices.
steps.detail: Stir in the cumin and chilli powder and cook for 30 seconds until fragrant. Toasting ground spices in the hot oil before any liquid goes in is what wakes them up — it releases their oils and deepens the flavour. Keep it moving; dry spices catch and turn bitter in seconds.
tips: Have the drained beans and stock within reach before the spices hit the pan — 30 seconds goes quickly.

steps.title: Simmer the beans
steps.content: Add beans and stock, then simmer.
steps.detail: Drain and rinse the black beans (the tin liquid is starchy and can taste tinny), then tip them in with the stock. Bring to a simmer and cook 15 min. A gentle simmer lets the beans break down a little and thicken the broth naturally — no flour needed.
tips: Crush a few beans against the side of the pot with your spoon partway through to help the broth thicken.

steps.title: Blend for body
steps.content: Blend part of the soup.
steps.detail: Scoop out about a third of the soup and blitz it smooth, then stir it back in — or pulse a stick blender in the pot a few times. This half-blended trick gives you a creamy body while keeping whole beans for texture. For a thinner soup, loosen with a splash more stock.
warnings: Hot soup expands in a jug blender — fill it no more than half, hold the lid down with a tea towel, or use a stick blender in the pot.

steps.title: Brighten with lime
steps.content: Finish with lime and serve.
steps.detail: Squeeze in the lime juice off the heat — its acidity lifts the whole pot and brightens the earthy beans, so don't skip it. Taste and adjust salt. Ladle into bowls with a swirl of sour cream if you like. A bit of chilli powder bloomed in oil on top makes it look the part.
tips: Roll the lime firmly under your palm before cutting — it gives up noticeably more juice.

title: Arroz con Pollo
description: Latin one-pot chicken and rice — golden with saffron, studded with chorizo and red capsicum. Comfort in a single pan.
equipment: large deep ovenproof pan or casserole with a lid
small bowl for steeping the saffron
storage: Cool leftovers quickly and get them into the fridge within the hour — with rice in the dish, eat within a day, reheated until piping hot all the way through. Not one for the freezer; the rice goes mushy.
leftovers: Fry day-after rice and shredded chicken hard in a little oil until crusty underneath — brilliant next-day fried rice.

substitutions.swap: 1/2 tsp ground turmeric gives the colour; the flavour's different but still good
medium-grain rice is even more traditional and holds its shape well
drumsticks work too — give them an extra 5 min in the covered simmer
leave it out and add an extra 1/2 tsp smoked paprika

steps.title: Bloom saffron, brown chicken
steps.content: Bloom the saffron and brown the chicken.
steps.detail: Crumble the saffron threads into a few tablespoons of the warm stock and leave them to steep — saffron is fat- and water-soluble, and soaking releases far more colour and honeyed aroma than tossing the dry threads in. Meanwhile, season the chicken thighs and brown them skin-side down in an ovenproof pan over medium-high until deeply golden, about 5 min a side. Lift out; that fond left in the pan is flavour.
tips: The chicken will tell you when to turn — it releases from the pan easily once the skin is properly browned.
warnings: Wash hands, board and tongs after handling raw chicken, and don't let it touch anything that won't be cooked.

steps.title: Build the sofrito
steps.content: Render the chorizo and soften the veg.
steps.detail: Slice and fry the chorizo in the chicken fat until it releases its red oil and crisps at the edges. Add the diced onion, capsicum and garlic and cook 6 min until soft, scraping up the browned bits from the chicken — they dissolve into the base and carry deep, savoury flavour through the whole dish.
tips: If the fond is stubborn, a tablespoon of water loosens it — it evaporates again in seconds.

steps.title: Coat and toast the rice
steps.content: Toast the rice.
steps.detail: Stir in the rice and paprika and cook for 2 min until the grains turn glossy and smell nutty. Toasting the rice in the fat before liquid goes in coats each grain so it cooks up separate rather than gluey — the same trick as a risotto or pilaf.
tips: Every grain should look glossy and coated before any liquid goes in — keep stirring until none look chalky.

steps.title: Simmer covered
steps.content: Add stock and nestle in the chicken.
steps.detail: Pour in the saffron stock and the rest of the chicken stock, stir once to settle the rice in an even layer, then nestle the chicken thighs back on top, skin proud of the liquid so it stays crisp. Bring to a simmer, then cover and cook on low (or in a 180C oven) for 18–20 min. Resist stirring once the rice is in — stirring releases starch and makes it claggy.
tips: If you can hear fierce bubbling through the lid, nudge the heat down — the rice should absorb, not boil dry.

steps.title: Add peas and rest
steps.content: Add peas, rest, and fluff.
steps.detail: Scatter the frozen peas over the top in the last 5 min — they only need to warm through, and adding them early dulls their colour. When the liquid is absorbed and the rice is tender, take it off the heat and let it sit covered for 5 min. Fluff gently with a fork and serve from the pan. Before serving, check the biggest thigh at its thickest point — the juices should run clear and the meat should reach 74°C, with no pink at the bone.

title: Bangers & Mash with Onion Gravy
description: Snags on a cloud of buttery mash, drowned in slow, sweet onion gravy. The ultimate pub comfort plate.
equipment: wide frying pan for the onions
second frying pan for the snags
storage: Keeps 3 days in airtight containers in the fridge, components stored separately. The gravy freezes for 3 months; reheat everything until piping hot, loosening the gravy with a little water.
make_ahead: The onion gravy is the slow part — make it up to 3 days ahead and reheat with a splash of water while the snags cook.
leftovers: Bubble and squeak: fry leftover mash into cakes until crusty and top with a fried egg — or slice the snags into the gravy for a quick pie filling.

substitutions.swap: chicken stock for a lighter gravy — add an extra dash of Worcestershire for depth
a splash of soy sauce plus a pinch of sugar
cream, warmed, for a richer mash

steps.title: Boil the spuds
steps.content: Boil the potatoes for mash.
steps.detail: Peel and cut potatoes into even 4cm chunks so they cook at the same rate. Start them in cold, well-salted water and bring to the boil — starting cold means the outside doesn't turn to mush before the middle is done. Simmer 15–18 min until a knife slides in with no resistance. Drain and let them steam-dry in the colander for a minute; dry potato makes fluffier mash.
tips: Salt the water like you mean it — it's your only chance to season the inside of the potato.

steps.title: Caramelise the onions
steps.content: Slowly caramelise the onions.
steps.detail: Halve and finely slice the onions. Melt a knob of the butter in a wide pan over medium-low and add the onions with a pinch of salt. This is the heart of the gravy — go slow, 15–18 min, stirring now and then, until they're deeply golden and jammy. Rushing on high heat just burns the edges and leaves them raw inside. The salt draws out water and helps them soften.
tips: If they stick and catch, a splash of water lifts the sticky brown layer back into the onions — that's flavour, not a mistake.

steps.title: Brown snags all over
steps.content: Brown the snags.
steps.detail: While the onions go, heat a separate pan over medium and add the snags with a tiny splash of oil. Turn them every few minutes for 12–15 min so they colour all over, not just two sides. Resist cranking the heat — sausages split and dry out if seared too hard. They're done when firm with no pink at the centre; let them rest while you finish the gravy.
tips: Don't prick them — the fat inside keeps them juicy, and pricked snags leak and dry out.
warnings: Snags are raw sausage mince — wash your hands and tongs after handling them, and cook through with no pink at the centre.

steps.title: Turn onions into gravy
steps.content: Build the onion gravy.
steps.detail: Sprinkle the flour over the caramelised onions and stir for a minute to cook out the raw taste — it should look like wet sand. Pour in the beef stock a splash at a time, stirring constantly so no lumps form, then add the Worcestershire. Simmer 5 min until it coats the back of a spoon. Taste and season; if it's too thick, loosen with a splash of water.
tips: Any juices from the resting snags belong in the gravy — tip them straight in.

steps.title: Mash it silky
steps.content: Mash with butter and warm milk.
steps.detail: Mash the potatoes well, then beat in the rest of the butter and the milk. Warm the milk first (a quick zap in the microwave) — cold milk seizes the starch and makes gluey mash. For a silky finish, push the spuds through a ricer or sieve. Season generously; under-salted mash tastes flat.
tips: Mash, don't blend — a food processor overworks the starch and turns mash to wallpaper paste.

steps.title: Assemble the plates
steps.content: Plate it up.
steps.detail: Spoon a big swirl of mash onto each plate, lean the snags against it, and ladle the onion gravy over the lot so it pools. Scatter chopped parsley if you like a bit of fresh colour. Serve straight away while everything's piping hot — mash cools fast.
tips: Warm the plates for a minute in a low oven — mash goes cold and claggy fast on a cold plate.

title: Full English Fry-Up
description: The proper weekend blowout: snags, bacon, mushrooms, eggs and grilled tomato, all on one plate.
equipment: second frying pan for the eggs
storage: Best eaten the moment it's plated. Leftover snags, bacon and mushrooms keep 2 days airtight in the fridge and reheat well in a hot pan; fried eggs and toast don't keep — cook those fresh.
leftovers: Leftover snags and bacon make a very good breakfast roll the next morning.

substitutions.swap: any tinned baked beans — a shake of Worcestershire lifts the budget ones
flat field mushrooms have the most flavour if you can get them
sourdough or English muffins — both fry beautifully in the pan fat

steps.title: Start the snags
steps.content: Start the snags first.
steps.detail: A fry-up is all about timing — start with the things that take longest. Get the snags into a cold pan with a little oil and bring the heat up to medium. They need 12–15 min, turning often, so they're well ahead of everything else. Starting in a cold pan lets the fat render gently instead of the skin tightening and bursting. They're cooked when firm to a squeeze with no pink at the centre — sacrifice one and cut it open if you're not sure.
warnings: Wash your hands after laying in the raw snags — sausage meat is raw mince and needs the same care.

steps.title: Add bacon and mushrooms
steps.content: Add the bacon and mushrooms.
steps.detail: Push the snags to one side and lay in the bacon. Halve or thickly slice the mushrooms and add them too — they'll soak up the bacon fat. Don't crowd or stir the mushrooms too soon; let them sit so they brown and go meaty rather than stewing in their own water. The bacon wants 3–4 min a side to crisp.
tips: Bacon curls less if you snip the fatty edge in a couple of places before it goes in.

steps.title: Tomatoes and beans on
steps.content: Grill the tomatoes and warm the beans.
steps.detail: Halve the tomatoes, season the cut faces, and put them under a hot grill (or in the pan, cut-side down) until softened and a little charred — about 5 min. Tip the beans into a small pot and warm through over low heat, stirring so they don't catch on the bottom. Keep them barely simmering; boiling hard turns them to paste.
tips: A tiny pinch of sugar on the tomato faces helps them char and tames their acidity.

steps.title: Fry eggs low and slow
steps.content: Fry the eggs.
steps.detail: Wipe out a clean pan, add a knob of butter or a little of the bacon fat over medium-low, and crack in the eggs. For a runny yolk and set white, baste the tops by spooning hot fat over them, or pop a lid on for a minute. Low and slow keeps the whites tender — high heat gives you rubbery, lacy edges.
tips: The white is set when it's gone from glassy to opaque right up to the yolk — that's your cue to plate.

steps.title: Toast and butter the bread
steps.content: Toast the bread.
steps.detail: Toast the bread and butter it while warm so it melts in. If you want the full diner experience, fry the bread instead in the leftover pan fat until golden — it soaks up all that flavour. Either way, do this last so the toast lands hot on the plate.
warnings: If you fry the bread, the pan fat is very hot and shallow — lay slices down away from you so it doesn't splash.

steps.title: Load the warm plates
steps.content: Build the plate.
steps.detail: Pile everything onto warmed plates — snags, bacon, mushrooms, tomato, a little heap of beans, eggs on top and toast on the side. Warming the plates first (a minute in a low oven) stops the whole lot going cold the second it's served. Brown sauce or tomato sauce is non-negotiable.
tips: Plate the eggs last so the yolks are still trembling when the plate lands.

title: Cheddar Cheese Scones
description: Tall, fluffy savoury scones loaded with sharp cheddar. Best eaten warm with a slab of butter.
equipment: baking tray lined with baking paper
6 cm round cutter (or a floured knife)
storage: Best the day they're baked. Store airtight for up to 2 days and revive in a warm oven for 5 min, or freeze baked scones for 2 months and thaw before warming through.
make_ahead: Rub the butter into the flour and stir through the cheese hours ahead — keep the bowl covered in the fridge and add the liquid just before baking. Cut, unbaked scones also freeze well: bake from frozen, adding 3–4 min.
leftovers: Split day-old scones and toast them under the grill with a little extra cheese on top.

substitutions.swap: any sharp hard cheese — swapping 30 g for parmesan deepens the flavour
1/2 tsp mustard powder or a pinch of cayenne in the flour
self-raising flour — drop the baking powder to 1 tsp

steps.title: Rub in the butter
steps.content: Rub butter into the flour.
steps.detail: Sift the flour and baking powder into a big bowl with a good pinch of salt. Cut the cold butter into cubes and rub it into the flour with your fingertips, lifting and letting it fall, until it looks like breadcrumbs. Cold butter is key — those little flecks melt in the oven and create steam pockets, which is what makes scones rise tall and flaky.
tips: If your hands run warm, chill the cubed butter for 10 min first and work quickly with just your fingertips.

steps.title: Stir through the cheddar
steps.content: Stir through the cheese.
steps.detail: Grate the cheddar and stir most of it through the flour mixture, holding a handful back for the tops. Use a sharp, mature cheddar — mild cheese gets lost once baked. Mixing it through now means cheese in every bite, not just on top.
tips: Grate on the coarse side of the box grater — fine shreds vanish into the dough, coarse ones leave molten pockets.

steps.title: Mix a shaggy dough
steps.content: Bring the dough together.
steps.detail: Beat the egg into the milk (and the dijon if using, for a savoury kick) and pour most of it in, keeping a little back for glazing. Stir with a knife just until it clumps into a shaggy dough. The golden rule of scones: mix as little as possible. Overworking develops gluten and gives you tough, dense hockey pucks instead of light scones.
tips: Stop while it still looks rough and patchy — it comes together when you pat it out.

steps.title: Cut without twisting
steps.content: Pat out and cut.
steps.detail: Tip onto a floured bench and gently pat — don't roll — into a slab about 3cm thick. Keeping them tall helps them rise. Cut straight down with a floured cutter and lift away; never twist the cutter, as twisting seals the edges and the scones rise lopsided. Press the offcuts together gently for the last couple.
tips: Dip the cutter in flour between every cut so it punches through cleanly.

steps.title: Glaze and bake hot
steps.content: Glaze and bake.
steps.detail: Sit the scones close together on a lined tray (touching helps them push each other up), brush the tops with the reserved milk and scatter on the rest of the cheese. Bake at 220°C for 12–15 min until risen and golden. A hot oven gives them an immediate blast of lift; too cool and they spread instead of rising.
tips: Keep the glaze off the cut sides — dribbles down the edges glue the layers and stunt the rise.
warnings: Don't open the oven for the first 10 min — the rush of cold air can stall the rise.

steps.title: Cool briefly, serve warm
steps.content: Cool a little, then serve warm.
steps.detail: Let them cool on a rack for a few minutes so the insides set — straight out of the oven they're still a bit doughy in the middle. Split and load with cold butter while they're still warm. They're best the day they're made; revive any leftovers with 5 min in a warm oven.

title: Beer-Braised Brisket
description: Brisket slow-cooked in dark beer until it's spoon-tender, with a deep malty onion gravy.
equipment: large heavy ovenproof pot with a lid (cast iron is ideal)
large spoon or ladle for skimming
storage: Keeps 4 days in the fridge stored in its gravy in an airtight container, or freezes for 3 months (slice it first). Reheat gently, covered, until piping hot right through.
make_ahead: Braises love a day's head start — cook it fully, cool and refrigerate the brisket in its liquid overnight, then lift off the set fat, slice the meat cold (much neater) and reheat gently in the gravy.
leftovers: Shred leftovers into the gravy for brilliant sandwiches, tacos or a pie filling.

substitutions.swap: stout for a darker, maltier gravy — or extra stock with 1 tbsp brown sugar to skip the beer
chuck or oyster blade braise just as happily
parsnips, or half and half

steps.title: Sear a deep crust
steps.content: Sear the brisket hard.
steps.detail: Pat the brisket really dry and season all over with salt and pepper. Heat a splash of oil in a heavy oven-proof pot over high until it's just smoking, then sear each face for 3–4 min until deeply browned. Don't rush or move it — that dark crust is pure flavour and is what gives the finished gravy its richness. Lift the brisket out onto a plate.
tips: If the brisket is too big to lie flat, cut it in two and sear the pieces separately — better contact beats one awkward slab.
warnings: The oil is at smoking point — lower the meat in away from you with tongs and expect it to spit.
Wash your hands after seasoning the raw beef, and keep its juices away from anything you'll eat raw.

steps.title: Sweat the veg
steps.content: Soften the vegetables.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to medium and add roughly chopped onion and carrot to the same pot. Scrape up the browned bits stuck to the base as they release moisture — that stuck-on 'fond' is concentrated flavour you don't want to waste. Cook 8 min until softened, adding the smashed garlic for the final minute so it doesn't burn and turn bitter.
tips: The moisture from the onions does the deglazing for you — just keep scraping as they soften.

steps.title: Cook out paste and flour
steps.content: Add tomato paste and flour.
steps.detail: Stir in the tomato paste and cook for a minute until it darkens to brick-red — this cooks off the raw, tinny taste. Sprinkle over the flour and stir for another minute; the flour will thicken the braising liquid into a proper gravy later. It should look like a thick, rusty paste coating the veg.
tips: Keep the paste moving across the base — it darkens to brick-red fast and burns just as fast if it sits.

steps.title: Deglaze with beer
steps.content: Pour in the beer and stock.
steps.detail: Tip in the beer and let it bubble hard for 2 min — this burns off the raw alcohol and mellows any bitterness, leaving the malty depth behind. Add the stock and bay leaves, then nestle the brisket back in. The liquid should come about halfway up the meat, not cover it; a braise isn't a boil.
tips: Pour the beer in off the boil zone and stir the base clean before adding the stock — nothing stuck, nothing burnt.

steps.title: Braise until fork-tender
steps.content: Braise low and slow.
steps.detail: Bring to a gentle simmer, put the lid on, and slide into a 150°C oven for 3 to 3.5 hours. Leave it alone for the first 2 hours. It's ready when a fork slides in and twists with no resistance — brisket is done when it's tender, not when the timer says so. A tough cut just needs more time, never more heat.
tips: If the liquid is boiling hard when you check at 2 hours, drop the oven 10 degrees — you want lazy, occasional bubbles.

steps.title: Rest, then make gravy
steps.content: Rest, then thicken the gravy.
steps.detail: Lift the brisket out and rest it under foil for 20 min — resting lets the juices settle back into the meat instead of running out the moment you cut. Skim the fat off the surface of the liquid and simmer it on the stovetop until glossy and thickened. Slice the brisket across the grain into thick slabs and spoon the gravy over.
tips: Let the liquid stand a few minutes before skimming — the fat pools at the edges and lifts off much more easily.

title: Welsh Rarebit
description: Posh cheese on toast: a molten, savoury cheddar-and-beer sauce grilled until blistered and golden.
equipment: spatula or table knife for spreading
storage: The unspread topping keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge. Once grilled it doesn't keep — assemble and eat straight away.
make_ahead: The cheese mixture keeps for up to 3 days in the fridge — spread it cold onto the toast and grill straight from there, adding a minute.
leftovers: Stir a spoonful of leftover topping through hot mash, or melt it over a baked potato.

substitutions.swap: milk for a gentler, kid-friendly sauce
swap up to half for gruyère — nuttier and just as melty
1/2 tsp mustard powder whisked in with the flour

steps.title: Make the roux
steps.content: Make a quick roux.
steps.detail: Melt the butter in a small saucepan over medium heat, then stir in the flour and cook for a minute until it smells nutty and looks like a smooth paste. This roux is what stops the sauce splitting later — cooking the flour now means no raw, floury taste in the finished rarebit.
tips: Grate the cheddar before you start — the sauce moves quickly once the roux is on.

steps.title: Loosen with beer
steps.content: Whisk in the beer.
steps.detail: Pour the beer in slowly, whisking the whole time so it stays lump-free, until you have a thick, smooth base. Let it bubble gently for a minute to cook off the raw alcohol. The beer gives rarebit its signature savoury, slightly bitter edge that plain cheese on toast doesn't have.
tips: Add the first splash and whisk it completely smooth before the next — lumps formed early never whisk out.

steps.title: Melt in the cheddar
steps.content: Melt in the cheese.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to low and add the grated cheddar a handful at a time, stirring until each lot melts before adding more. Low heat is crucial — boil cheese and it turns greasy and stringy as the fat splits out. You're after a thick, glossy, spoonable sauce.
tips: If it starts looking oily at the edges, pull the pan off the heat and stir — you've caught it just in time.

steps.title: Enrich off the heat
steps.content: Season and enrich.
steps.detail: Take the pan off the heat and stir in the dijon, Worcestershire and the egg yolk. The yolk thickens the sauce and helps it grill to a glorious golden brown. Adding it off the heat stops it scrambling. Taste and add a crack of pepper — it shouldn't need salt with all that cheese.
tips: Save the spare egg white for tomorrow's omelette — only the yolk goes in.

steps.title: Toast the bases
steps.content: Toast the bread.
steps.detail: Lightly toast the bread on both sides under the grill first. Toasting before topping means the base stays crisp and doesn't go soggy under the wet sauce — soft bread collapses into a mess. You want sturdy, golden slices ready to hold the topping.
tips: Day-old bread toasts sturdier than fresh — a good use for the end of the loaf.

steps.title: Grill until blistered
steps.content: Spread thick and grill.
steps.detail: Spread the cheese mixture right to the edges and quite thick — a thin layer just disappears. Whack it under a hot grill for 2–3 min until bubbling, blistered and brown in patches. Watch it like a hawk; it goes from golden to burnt in seconds. Eat immediately while molten.
tips: Set the tray on the highest shelf and leave the grill door ajar so you can see every second of colour.

title: Tuna Mornay Bake
description: The tinned-tuna classic every Australian grew up on. Silky homemade white sauce, peas, plenty of cheddar and a crunchy golden top.
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge; reheat covered in a 180°C fan-forced oven until piping hot in the middle. Freezes for up to 2 months, though the sauce is a little less silky after thawing.
make_ahead: Assemble the whole bake up to a day ahead and keep it covered in the fridge — add 10 extra minutes in the oven from cold.
leftovers: A scoop reheated and pressed into a jaffle or toastie is the best kind of trashy-good lunch.

substitutions.swap: a tin of salmon, or shredded leftover cooked chicken
frozen corn, or a mix of both
any good melting cheese — tasty, colby or mozzarella
any short shape — macaroni, penne and spirals all work

steps.title: Get everything ready
steps.content: Preheat oven to 180°C fan-forced; grate the cheese, drain the tuna, get the pasta water on.
steps.detail: Preheat the oven to 180°C fan-forced. Put a large pot of water on to boil with a big pinch of salt. While it heats, grate the cheddar, drain the tuna well — press it in the tin so it isn't watery — and measure out the butter, flour and milk. The white sauce moves quickly once it starts, so you want everything within arm's reach before you begin.

steps.title: Boil the pasta
steps.content: Cook pasta 2 minutes less than the packet says; add peas for the last minute, then drain.
steps.detail: Tip the pasta into the boiling water and give it one good stir so nothing sticks. Cook it 2 minutes short of the packet time — it keeps cooking in the oven, and slightly firm pasta is what stops the bake going mushy. Throw the peas in for the final minute, then drain everything together in a colander. No need to rinse.
tips: Salt the water until it tastes like the sea — it's your only chance to season the pasta itself.

steps.title: Make the roux
steps.content: Melt the butter, stir in the flour, cook 1 minute until it smells like biscuit.
steps.detail: Melt the butter in a medium saucepan over medium heat until it foams. Add the flour all at once and stir constantly with a wooden spoon — it will come together into a thick, sandy paste called a roux. Cook it for about a minute, stirring the whole time, until it smells faintly of shortbread. That minute cooks out the raw flour taste; if it starts to brown, pull the pan off the heat.
tips: The roux thickens the whole sauce — roughly equal weights of butter and flour is a ratio worth memorising.

steps.title: Whisk in the milk
steps.content: Add milk a splash at a time, whisking until smooth, then simmer until it coats a spoon.
steps.detail: Take the pan off the heat and add a splash of milk, whisking hard until smooth — it will look gluey at first, which is normal. Keep adding the milk a little at a time, whisking each addition in fully before the next; go slowly early on and you'll never get lumps. Return to medium heat and whisk until it comes to a gentle bubble and thickens enough to coat the back of a spoon, about 3-4 minutes. If lumps sneak in anyway, whisk like mad or push the sauce through a sieve — nobody will ever know.
tips: Cold milk into hot roux is fine as long as it goes in gradually — dumping it all at once is what makes lumps.

steps.title: Turn it into mornay
steps.content: Off the heat, stir in most of the cheddar and the mustard; season it properly.
steps.detail: Pull the pan off the heat and stir in about three-quarters of the cheddar until it melts through — off the heat, so the cheese stays silky instead of splitting. Stir in the dijon if you're using it, then season with salt and a good grind of pepper. Taste it: the sauce should be confidently savoury on its own, because the pasta will mute it once everything is combined.

steps.title: Combine and top
steps.content: Fold tuna, pasta and peas through the sauce; into the dish, top with cheese and crumbs.
steps.detail: Fold the drained tuna through the sauce in big flakes — don't mash it to a paste; the chunks are the good bit. Add the pasta and peas and stir gently until everything is coated. Scrape it all into the baking dish, level the top, and scatter over the remaining cheddar and then the breadcrumbs — that's your golden crust.

steps.title: Bake until golden
steps.content: Bake 20 minutes until bubbling at the edges and golden on top; rest 5 minutes before serving.
steps.detail: Bake for 20 minutes, until the top is deep golden and the sauce is bubbling up around the edges — that bubble is how you know it's hot in the middle. If the top browns before it bubbles, cover loosely with foil and keep going. Let it sit for 5 minutes out of the oven so the sauce settles from molten back to creamy, then serve straight from the dish.

title: Curried Sausages
description: The retro weeknight hero: browned snags in a golden curry gravy with peas and carrot, piled over buttery mash.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge, and the flavour improves overnight. Freeze the sausages and gravy (without the mash) for up to 3 months.
make_ahead: The curry is even better the next day — make it fully ahead and reheat gently while you mash fresh potatoes.
leftovers: Spoon leftovers into a pie dish, top with puff pastry and bake — an instant curried-snag pie.

substitutions.swap: chicken or pork sausages both work — anything thick and fairly plain
vegetable or beef stock, or water with an extra pinch of salt
frozen corn, or green beans cut into short lengths

steps.title: Prep the vegetables
steps.content: Peel and chunk the potatoes; slice the onion and cut the carrots into coins.
steps.detail: Peel the potatoes and cut them into even chunks about the size of a golf ball — even sizes cook at the same rate, so no hard middles. Halve, peel and slice the onion into thin half-moons, and cut the carrots into coins about 5 mm thick. Get all the knife work done now; the rest of this cook is mostly stirring and waiting.

steps.title: Boil the potatoes
steps.content: Start potatoes in cold salted water; boil about 15 minutes until a knife slides in easily.
steps.detail: Put the potato chunks in a large pot, cover with cold water by a few centimetres and add a big pinch of salt — starting in cold water cooks them evenly right to the middle. Bring to the boil and keep them at a steady bubble for about 15 minutes. They're done when a knife slides in with no resistance. Drain and leave them in the colander to steam-dry while you finish the curry.

steps.title: Brown the snags
steps.content: Brown the sausages all over in a little oil, about 10 minutes, then set them aside.
steps.detail: While the potatoes boil, heat the oil in a large deep frying pan over medium heat. Add the sausages and cook, turning every couple of minutes, until browned on all sides — about 10 minutes. They don't need to be cooked through yet; they'll finish in the gravy. Lift them out onto a plate and leave the fat behind in the pan — that's the flavour base for the gravy.
tips: Medium heat, not high — sausages that brown too fast split their skins and dry out.
warnings: Sausages spit as they brown — stand back a little when turning and keep the heat at medium.
Wash your hands and anything the raw sausages touched before you move on to other food.

steps.title: Build the curry gravy
steps.content: Soften onion and carrot in the pan, add curry powder and flour, then stir in stock gradually.
steps.detail: Add the onion and carrot to the sausage fat and cook for about 5 minutes, until the onion is soft and golden, scraping up the browned bits on the pan base as you stir. Sprinkle over the curry powder and flour and stir for a minute — it will look dry and clumpy, which is exactly right; you're cooking the raw taste out of both. Now add the stock a splash at a time, stirring each addition in before the next, until you have a smooth, gravy-thick sauce.
tips: Adding stock gradually is the same trick as any white sauce — slow at the start means no lumps.

steps.title: Slice, simmer, add peas
steps.content: Slice sausages into thick coins, return to the gravy with the peas, simmer 10 minutes.
steps.detail: Slice the sausages into coins about 2 cm thick — a little pink inside is fine at this point. Return them and any juices from the plate to the pan, add the peas, and simmer gently for 10 minutes, until the carrot is tender and the sausage is cooked through with no pink remaining. The gravy should coat the back of a spoon; if it tightens up too much, loosen it with a splash of water.
warnings: The sliced sausages aren't fully cooked yet — wash the board and knife straight after slicing, before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Mash and serve
steps.content: Mash the potatoes with butter and warm milk; pile the curried sausages over the top.
steps.detail: Tip the drained potatoes back into their pot and mash until smooth. Add the butter and warm milk and stir through — warm milk keeps mash fluffy, where cold milk turns it gluey. Season well with salt and pepper and taste it; under-seasoned mash lets the whole plate down. Serve big spoonfuls of mash with the curried sausages and plenty of gravy over the top.

title: Rissoles with Onion Gravy
description: Proper Aussie rissoles — soft, savoury beef patties with a glossy onion gravy built from the pan scrapings. Mash or peas alongside and dinner's sorted.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge, rissoles and gravy together. Freeze for up to 3 months and reheat gently in a pan with a splash of water.
make_ahead: Shape the rissoles up to a day ahead and keep them covered in the fridge — they hold together even better after a long chill.
leftovers: A cold rissole on white bread with tomato sauce is a genuine Australian delicacy — don't knock it until you've tried it.

substitutions.swap: half beef, half pork mince for softer, richer rissoles
chicken stock, or water plus an extra splash of worcestershire
a slice of bread whizzed into crumbs, or a small handful of rolled oats
soy sauce with a tiny pinch of sugar

ingredients.amount: 1 tablespoon, plus a splash for the gravy

steps.title: Mix the rissoles
steps.content: Combine mince, grated onion, garlic, breadcrumbs, egg and worcestershire — mix gently.
steps.detail: Grate one onion on the coarse side of a box grater straight into a large bowl — grating means no crunchy onion bits and keeps the rissoles juicy. Add the mince, crushed garlic, breadcrumbs, egg, worcestershire, salt and a good grind of pepper. Mix with your hands just until it comes together, then stop — overworked mince turns bouncy and tough, and gentle hands are what make tender rissoles.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and anything the raw mince touched before you handle other food.

steps.title: Shape and chill
steps.content: Shape 8 patties about 2 cm thick with a dimple in the middle; chill for 15 minutes.
steps.detail: With wet hands, divide the mixture into 8 and shape each into a patty about 2 cm thick — wet hands stop the mince sticking to you. Press a shallow dimple into the centre of each with your thumb; rissoles puff up as they cook, and the dimple keeps them flat instead of football-shaped. Rest them on a plate in the fridge for 15 minutes so they firm up and hold together in the pan.

steps.title: Fry the rissoles
steps.content: Fry in oil over medium heat, 5 minutes a side, until deeply browned and cooked through.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a large frying pan over medium heat until it shimmers. Add the rissoles with space between them and leave them completely alone for 5 minutes — moving them early tears off the crust that's forming. Flip and give the other side 5 minutes too. They're done when deeply browned outside with no pink in the middle; press one gently and the juices should run clear, not red. Rest them on a plate loosely covered with foil.
tips: If your pan is small, fry in two batches — crowding drops the heat and the rissoles stew instead of browning.

steps.title: Start the onion gravy
steps.content: Soften the sliced onion in the same pan with butter, scraping up the browned bits.
steps.detail: Don't wash the pan — those browned bits stuck to the base are the gravy's flavour. Drop the heat to medium-low, add the butter and the sliced onion, and cook for about 5 minutes, stirring now and then, until soft and golden. As the onion releases its moisture, scrape the pan base with a wooden spoon and everything stuck there will lift into the onions.

steps.title: Finish the gravy
steps.content: Stir in flour, add stock gradually, then simmer until glossy and spoon-coating.
steps.detail: Sprinkle the flour over the onions and stir for a minute so it coats everything and loses its raw, white look. Add the stock a splash at a time, stirring between additions so no lumps form, then add a splash of worcestershire. Let it bubble gently for 3-4 minutes until it's a glossy gravy that coats the back of a spoon. Too thick? Splash of water. Too thin? Keep simmering — it thickens as it reduces.

steps.title: Return and serve
steps.content: Slide the rissoles and their juices back in for 2 minutes, then serve smothered in gravy.
steps.detail: Slide the rissoles back into the gravy along with any juices that have pooled on the resting plate — that's pure flavour going back in. Turn them once to coat and let everything warm through for a couple of minutes. Serve them smothered in onion gravy, with mash, peas or just good bread to mop the plate.

title: Pub Steak Sandwich
description: The front-bar classic: thin steak seared hard, jammy caramelised onions, rocket and two sauces on toasted bread.
equipment: rolling pin or small heavy saucepan
storage: A built sandwich doesn't keep — eat it fresh. Leftover caramelised onions keep a week in an airtight jar in the fridge and are good on nearly everything.
make_ahead: Caramelise the onions up to a week ahead; bring them to room temperature or warm briefly before stacking.

substitutions.swap: minute steak (already thin — skip the flattening), or scotch fillet if you're feeling flush
baby spinach or crisp lettuce
Turkish bread or a crusty roll — anything sturdy enough to hold the juices
aioli, or mayo with a little crushed garlic stirred through

steps.title: Caramelise the onions
steps.content: Cook sliced onions low and slow in butter, about 25 minutes, until deep golden and jammy.
steps.detail: Slice the onions into thin half-moons. Melt the butter with a little of the oil in a pan over low heat, add the onions and a pinch of salt, and cook, stirring every few minutes, for about 25 minutes. Real caramelised onions can't be rushed — high heat gives you burnt edges and raw middles. They're done when they've collapsed to a third of their volume, deep golden and sweet. Stir in the brown sugar and balsamic for the last 2 minutes, then set aside.
tips: Start the onions first and let them do their thing while you prep everything else — they need time, not attention.
If they catch on the pan, a splash of water lifts the sticky bits back into the onions.

steps.title: Flatten the steaks
steps.content: Beat the steaks between baking paper to about 1 cm thick; oil and season well.
steps.detail: Put a steak between two sheets of baking paper and beat it out with a rolling pin or the base of a small saucepan until it's an even 1 cm thick. Thin steak cooks in a flash and stays tender — that's the minute-steak trick every pub uses.
