Rub both steaks with oil and season generously with salt and pepper on both sides, just before they hit the pan.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the rolling pin after handling the raw steak — it comes nowhere near the bread or rocket.

steps.title: Sear hard and fast
steps.content: Sear the steaks in a screaming-hot pan, 60-90 seconds a side.
steps.detail: Get the large pan properly hot over high heat — a drop of water should skitter across it and vanish. Lay the steaks in away from you; they should hiss loudly the moment they land. Leave them for 60-90 seconds until deeply browned, flip once, and give the other side the same. Thin steak overcooks fast, so pull them the moment both sides are browned.
warnings: A very hot pan will smoke a little — turn on the rangehood, and don't add butter at this heat; it will burn.

steps.title: Rest the steaks
steps.content: Rest the steaks on a warm plate for 5 minutes — don't skip this.
steps.detail: Move the steaks to a warm plate and leave them alone for 5 minutes. Resting lets the juices settle back into the meat instead of flooding out the moment you slice — it's the difference between a juicy sandwich and a soggy one. Use the wait to sort the bread.

steps.title: Toast and dress the bread
steps.content: Toast the bread in the steak pan; spread mayo on one slice, tomato sauce on the other.
steps.detail: Wipe most of the fat from the steak pan and toast the bread in it over medium heat, a minute or two a side, until golden — it picks up the steak flavour left behind. A toaster does the job fine too. Spread mayo on two slices and tomato sauce on the other two; the mayo layer also waterproofs the bread against the steak juices.

steps.title: Slice and stack
steps.content: Slice the steak thinly against the grain, then stack with rocket and onions.
steps.detail: Look closely at the steak and find the direction the meat fibres run — that's the grain. Slice thinly across it, not along it; cutting the fibres short is what makes every bite tender. Build each sandwich: mayo slice, a handful of rocket, sliced steak plus any resting juices, a generous pile of caramelised onions, then the sauce slice on top. Press gently, cut in half, and eat while it's warm.

title: Crumbed Lamb Cutlets
description: Golden, parmesan-crumbed lamb cutlets with lemon for squeezing — a retro dinner-party dish that's really just very good finger food.
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Re-crisp in a 180°C fan-forced oven for about 10 minutes — the microwave turns the crumb soggy.
make_ahead: Crumb the cutlets up to a day ahead and keep them on a tray in the fridge — the coating actually sticks better after a rest.
leftovers: Cold crumbed cutlets are classic picnic food, or strip the meat into a wrap with rocket and mayo.

substitutions.swap: pork chops or flattened chicken breast — cook chicken right through to 74°C at the thickest point
finely grated tasty cheese, or leave it out
panko crumbs for an even crunchier shell
a simple rocket and lemon salad instead

steps.title: Set up the crumbing line
steps.content: Three bowls: seasoned flour, beaten egg, crumbs mixed with parmesan. Pat the cutlets dry.
steps.detail: Line up three shallow bowls: flour seasoned with salt and pepper in the first; the eggs beaten well in the second; breadcrumbs mixed with the parmesan in the third. Pat the cutlets dry with paper towel — dry meat is the secret to a crumb that actually sticks. Each layer has a job: flour gives the egg something to grip, egg glues on the crumbs, and the crumbs are what turn golden.

steps.title: Crumb the cutlets
steps.content: Flour, egg, then crumbs — one dry hand, one wet hand — pressing the crumbs on firmly.
steps.detail: Use one hand for the dry bowls and the other for the egg, and you won't end up with crumbed fingers. Dry hand: press a cutlet into the flour, coat both sides, shake off the excess. Wet hand: drag it through the egg and let the extra drip off. Dry hand again: lay it in the crumbs and press them on firmly with your palm — that press is what stops the coating falling off in the pan. Lay the crumbed cutlets on a tray, and if you have 10 minutes, rest them in the fridge so the coating sets.
tips: Only flour as many cutlets as you're about to egg — floured cutlets left sitting turn gluey.
warnings: Wash your hands and wipe the bench after handling the raw lamb, before touching anything else.

steps.title: Heat and test the oil
steps.content: Heat 1 cm of oil over medium-high; a pinch of crumbs should sizzle at once and turn gold in 20 seconds.
steps.detail: Pour the oil into a large frying pan to about 1 cm deep and set it over medium-high heat for a few minutes. Test it with a pinch of breadcrumbs: they should sizzle immediately and turn golden in about 20 seconds. If they sink quietly, wait a little longer; if they brown instantly, lift the pan off the heat for a minute. Oil at the right temperature is everything here — too cool and the crumb soaks it up greasy, too hot and it burns before the lamb cooks.
warnings: Never add water to hot oil, and lower food in away from you so it can't splash back.
If the oil ever starts smoking, take the pan off the heat until it settles.

steps.title: Fry in batches
steps.content: Fry 3-4 cutlets at a time, 2-3 minutes a side, until deep golden; drain and salt while hot.
steps.detail: Lower in 3 or 4 cutlets at a time — no more, or the oil temperature crashes and the crumbs stew instead of crisping. Fry for 2-3 minutes a side, until the coating is a deep, even gold. Lamb is lovely a little pink at the bone, so the colour of the crumb is your cue, not the clock. Drain each batch on the paper-towel plate and sprinkle with salt while hot, then let the oil come back up to heat before the next batch goes in.
tips: Keep finished cutlets warm on a tray in a 120°C fan-forced oven while you fry the rest.

steps.title: Cook the peas
steps.content: Boil the peas for 3 minutes, drain, then toss with butter and a pinch of salt.
steps.detail: Drop the peas into a small saucepan of boiling water and cook for 3 minutes, until bright green and just tender. Drain well, then toss with the butter and a pinch of salt while they're hot so it melts through and glosses every pea.

steps.title: Serve with lemon
steps.content: Pile up the cutlets with lemon wedges — a big squeeze over the top cuts the richness.
steps.detail: Stack the cutlets on a platter with the lemon wedges and the buttered peas alongside. Squeeze lemon generously over the crumb just before eating — that sharpness against the rich, salty crust is the whole point of the dish. Eat them with your fingers, straight off the bone; that's what the frenched handle is for.

title: Tuna Potato Patties
description: Tinned tuna and mash, folded with spring onion and parsley, crumbed and fried until golden. Cheap as chips and just as popular.
storage: Cooked patties keep 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge — good cold, or re-crisped in a dry frying pan. Freeze cooked patties between sheets of baking paper for up to 2 months.
make_ahead: Shape and crumb the patties up to a day ahead and keep them covered in the fridge — they're even sturdier after a long chill.
leftovers: Tuck a cold patty into a bread roll with lettuce and mayo for a lunchbox fish burger.

substitutions.swap: a tin of salmon — pick out the big bones, or leave the soft ones in for calcium
coriander, or a couple of extra spring onion tops
a quarter of a red onion, very finely diced
panko for extra crunch, or crushed cornflakes

ingredients.amount: 1 (zest in the mix, wedges to serve)

steps.title: Boil the potatoes
steps.content: Boil potato chunks in salted water about 15 minutes, until a knife slides through easily.
steps.detail: Peel the potatoes and cut them into even chunks. Start them in a pot of cold salted water, bring to the boil and cook for about 15 minutes, until a knife slides in with no resistance. Drain in a colander and leave them there a couple of minutes to steam-dry — dry potato makes patties that hold together; wet potato makes patties that don't.

steps.title: Make a dry mash
steps.content: Mash the potatoes plain — no milk or butter — then let the mash cool down.
steps.detail: Tip the potatoes back into the warm pot and mash until mostly smooth — a few small lumps are fine and give the patties texture. Skip the milk and butter you'd normally add to mash; a dry, firm mash is the glue that holds these patties together. Spread it out in the pot or over a tray and let it cool for 10 minutes — hot mash would scramble the egg going in next.

steps.title: Mix the patties
steps.content: Fold in tuna, spring onion, parsley, lemon zest, egg and seasoning — gently does it.
steps.detail: Drain the tuna really well, pressing it in the tin — excess liquid is the enemy of a patty that stays whole. Finely slice the spring onions and chop the parsley, then add both to the mash with the tuna, the zest of the lemon, the egg, a spoonful of the breadcrumbs, salt and pepper. Fold it all together gently, keeping some chunks of tuna intact — an overmixed paste makes dense, heavy patties.

steps.title: Shape, crumb and chill
steps.content: Shape 8 patties, coat them in breadcrumbs, then chill for at least 30 minutes.
steps.detail: With damp hands, shape the mixture into 8 patties about 2 cm thick. Tip the remaining breadcrumbs onto a plate and press each patty into them, coating both sides and the edges. Now the step that saves them: line the patties up on a tray and chill for at least 30 minutes. Cold patties are firm patties — this is the single biggest thing that stops them breaking apart in the pan.
tips: In a hurry? 15 minutes in the freezer does the same job as 30 in the fridge.

steps.title: Fry until golden
steps.content: Fry in two batches over medium heat, 3-4 minutes a side, flipping just once.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a large frying pan over medium heat until it shimmers. Add 4 patties — don't crowd them; you need room to get a spatula in cleanly — and leave them completely alone for 3-4 minutes, until the underside is deep golden. Flip each one just once, confidently, and give the other side the same. If one cracks, press it back together with the spatula — the crust will hold it.
tips: Resist the urge to move them around — the crust needs uninterrupted contact with the pan to form.
warnings: Lay the patties in away from you — hot oil can spit when they hit the pan.

steps.title: Serve with lemon
steps.content: Salt while hot; serve with lemon wedges and a dollop of mayo.
steps.detail: Drain the patties briefly on paper towel and sprinkle with a little salt while they're hot. Serve with the lemon wedges for squeezing and mayo for dolloping — they're great with a simple salad, or just as they are, straight off the pan.

title: Sweet and Sour Pork
description: The takeaway classic, made properly at home. Crisp cornflour-coated pork tossed with charred capsicum, onion and pineapple in a glossy sweet-and-sour glaze.
equipment: wok or large deep frying pan
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge, though the coating softens — reheat in a hot oven to bring some crunch back. Cool leftover rice quickly, refrigerate within the hour and eat within 1 day.
make_ahead: Cut the pork and toss it with the soy in the morning, chop the veg and mix the sauce and slurry — but leave the cornflour coating until just before frying, or it turns gummy. Dinner becomes 20 minutes of cooking.
leftovers: Chop any leftovers small and fold them through fried rice with an extra dash of soy.

substitutions.swap: boneless chicken thigh — same coat, same fry, and check it's 74°C inside
a small tin of pineapple pieces, drained really well
apple cider vinegar for a slightly softer tang

ingredients.amount: ½ cup, plus 2 tsp for the slurry

steps.title: Get the rice on
steps.content: Rinse and cook the rice by absorption so it's ready when the pork is.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice in a sieve under the tap until the water runs mostly clear. Tip it into a medium saucepan with 2¼ cups of cold water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, clap on the lid, drop the heat to its lowest and leave for 12 minutes — no peeking. Turn off the heat and let it sit, still covered, while you cook everything else.
tips: The rice holds happily off the heat, lid on, for a good 20 minutes — the trapped steam keeps it warm and finishes the grains.

steps.title: Cut and coat the pork
steps.content: Dice the pork, season with soy, then toss in cornflour until chalky.
steps.detail: Cut the pork shoulder into rough 3 cm chunks, trimming off any big lumps of hard fat. Toss the chunks with 1 tablespoon of the soy sauce so every piece is damp, then scatter over the ½ cup of cornflour and toss until each chunk wears a thin, dry, chalky coat. Damp then dry is the trick — the soy helps the flour cling, and a thin coat fries up light and crisp rather than doughy.
warnings: Wash your hands, board and knife with hot soapy water after handling raw pork, before you touch anything else.

steps.title: Chop veg, mix the sauce
steps.content: Cut capsicum, onion and pineapple into chunks; stir the sauce and the slurry.
steps.detail: Cut both capsicums and the onion into chunky 3 cm pieces to match the pork, and cut the pineapple into similar chunks. Finely chop the garlic. In one bowl, stir together the vinegar, sugar, tomato sauce and the last tablespoon of soy until the sugar dissolves. In a separate small bowl, mix 2 teaspoons of cornflour with 2 tablespoons of cold water — this slurry is what turns the sauce glossy later. Cornflour only mixes smoothly into cold liquid, so never add it straight to a hot pan.

steps.title: Shallow-fry the pork
steps.content: Fry the pork in batches until deep golden and cooked through, 5–6 minutes a batch.
steps.detail: Pour the oil into the wok — about 1 cm deep — and heat over medium-high until a pinch of cornflour sizzles the instant it lands. Fry the pork in two or three batches so the pieces never touch: crowding drops the oil temperature and turns the coating gummy. Give each batch 5–6 minutes, turning halfway, until deep golden and crisp all over. Lift out with a slotted spoon onto a paper-towel-lined plate. Cut the biggest piece open — it should be white to the centre with no pink.
tips: Between batches, give the oil 30 seconds to come back up to heat — the sizzle should be lively the moment the next piece goes in.
warnings: Hot oil and water are enemies — the pork should carry nothing wet beyond its coating, and never add liquid to the frying oil.

steps.title: Stir-fry the vegetables
steps.content: Pour off most of the oil, then stir-fry onion, capsicum, garlic and pineapple hot and fast.
steps.detail: Carefully pour off all but about a tablespoon of the oil into a heatproof jar — never down the sink. Return the pan to high heat, add the onion and capsicum and stir-fry for 2–3 minutes: you want charred edges but plenty of crunch left. Add the garlic and pineapple and toss for another minute, until the garlic smells sweet and the pineapple picks up a little colour.
warnings: Let the poured-off oil cool completely in its jar before you move it or bin it.

steps.title: Thicken the sauce
steps.content: Add the sauce, bring it to a bubble, then stir in the slurry until glossy.
steps.detail: Pour the sauce mixture into the pan and let it come to a proper bubble. Give the slurry a quick re-stir — cornflour settles like sand within a minute — then drizzle in about half of it while stirring constantly. Within 30 seconds the sauce turns from watery to glossy and starts clinging to the vegetables. Add more slurry only if you want it thicker, and remember it has to return to the boil to thicken fully. Gone too far? A splash of water walks it straight back.

steps.title: Toss the pork through
steps.content: Return the pork, toss to coat, and serve straight away over rice.
steps.detail: Tip the fried pork back in and toss for 30 seconds or so — just long enough to gloss every piece, not so long the coating goes soft. Fluff the rice with a fork and spoon the pork, vegetables and sauce over the top. Eat immediately: sweet and sour waits for no one, and the crunch fades as it sits.

title: Mapo-Style Tofu
description: A weeknight pantry take on the Sichuan classic — silky tofu and crispy pork mince in a glossy, chilli-flecked sauce over rice. Fermented black beans and chilli flakes stand in for the traditional doubanjiang.
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge — the tofu keeps drinking up sauce and is arguably better the next day. Cool leftover rice quickly, refrigerate within the hour and eat within 1 day.
leftovers: Spoon leftovers over thick noodles instead of rice, with a splash of water to loosen the sauce as it reheats.

substitutions.swap: chicken or beef mince both work, or leave it out, double the tofu and use veg stock for a vegetarian bowl
a tablespoon of miso plus an extra dash of soy gets you surprisingly close
firm tofu is sturdier and much easier to handle if it's your first time

steps.title: Get the rice on
steps.content: Rinse the rice and cook it by absorption while you make the mapo.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice in a sieve until the water runs mostly clear, then tip it into a saucepan with 2¼ cups of cold water. Bring to the boil, put the lid on, turn the heat to its lowest and leave it for 12 minutes without lifting the lid. Turn off the heat and let it steam, covered, until serving — it will stay hot for ages.

steps.title: Prep tofu and aromatics
steps.content: Cube the tofu; chop the ginger, garlic, spring onion and black beans.
steps.detail: Drain the tofu and cut it into 2 cm cubes, then let them sit on a paper-towel-lined plate to shed some water — drier cubes hold together better in the pan. Finely chop the ginger and garlic. Slice the spring onions, keeping the white and green parts separate. Rinse the fermented black beans under the tap to knock back their saltiness, then roughly chop them — they're doing the job of the traditional doubanjiang here: salty, funky and deeply savoury.
tips: Silken tofu gives the classic wobble but is fragile — cut it while it's still in the packet tray if you can, then slide the cubes out.

steps.title: Crisp the pork mince
steps.content: Fry the mince over high heat until browned and crisping at the edges, about 5 minutes.
steps.detail: Heat the vegetable oil in a large frying pan or wok over high heat until it shimmers. Add the pork mince, break it up with your spoon, then let it fry mostly undisturbed. You're after more than grey-and-cooked — wait until the liquid has sizzled off and the mince catches real golden-brown crispy edges. Those crunchy bits carry the whole dish.
warnings: Wash hands and anything the raw mince touched before moving on.

steps.title: Bloom the seasonings
steps.content: Add black beans, chilli flakes, ginger, garlic and spring onion whites; fry 1 minute.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to medium. Add the chopped black beans, chilli flakes, ginger, garlic and the white parts of the spring onion, and stir constantly for about a minute. The smell will turn from raw and sharp to warm and savoury — that's the seasonings blooming in the hot fat. Keep everything moving so nothing catches.
warnings: Garlic burns in seconds at this heat — if it starts to darken, pour in the stock early rather than let it turn bitter.

steps.title: Simmer the tofu
steps.content: Add stock, soy and sugar, slide in the tofu and simmer gently for 5 minutes.
steps.detail: Pour in the stock, soy sauce and sugar and bring it to a gentle simmer. Slide the tofu cubes in and nudge them under the liquid with the back of your spoon — from here on, push rather than stir, so the cubes stay whole. Let it burble softly for 5 minutes while the tofu heats through and starts soaking up the sauce.

steps.title: Thicken, finish, serve
steps.content: Stir in the cornflour slurry until silky, then finish with sesame oil and spring onion.
steps.detail: Mix the cornflour with 2 tablespoons of cold water, re-stir it just before it goes in, then drizzle it around the pan while gently shaking and nudging with your spoon. Let it bubble for 30–60 seconds — the sauce will turn from thin to silky and cling to the tofu. Off the heat, add the sesame oil and the spring onion greens. Serve over big scoops of rice.
tips: If the sauce over-thickens to gravy, a splash of water brings it straight back to silky.

title: Chicken Chow Mein
description: Wok-tossed egg noodles with velvety chicken and crisp veg in a glossy oyster-soy sauce. On the table faster than the delivery driver.
equipment: wok or largest frying pan you own
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Reheat in a hot pan with a splash of water rather than the microwave — the noodles come back to life.
make_ahead: Slice and velvet the chicken up to a day ahead, covered in the fridge — it only gets more tender.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh — give it an extra minute or two in the wok
thin spaghetti, cooked and rinsed, at a pinch
extra soy with ½ teaspoon of sugar, though you'll lose a little depth

steps.title: Slice and velvet chicken
steps.content: Slice the chicken thinly, then toss with cornflour, soy and water to velvet.
steps.detail: Slice the chicken breast as thinly as you can across the grain — look at the direction of the fibres and cut across them, so every slice is short-fibred and tender. Toss the slices with the cornflour, 1 tablespoon of the soy sauce and 1 tablespoon of water until slick, then set aside while you prep the rest. This quick velvet — the home version of a restaurant trick — coats the chicken so it stays silky in the wok's heat instead of drying out.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash your hands, board and knife with hot soapy water before touching anything else.

steps.title: Prep the veg and sauce
steps.content: Shred cabbage, matchstick the carrot, slice celery and spring onion; stir the sauce.
steps.detail: Shred the cabbage into ribbons about 1 cm wide. Cut the carrot into thin matchsticks and slice the celery thinly on an angle. Slice the spring onions, chop the garlic and grate the ginger. In a small bowl, stir together the oyster sauce, the remaining soy, the sugar, sesame oil and 2 tablespoons of water. Line everything up within arm's reach of the stove — stir-frying is fast, and there's no time to chop once the wok is hot.

steps.title: Ready the noodles
steps.content: Cook the noodles just shy of done, drain, and toss with a little oil.
steps.detail: Cook the noodles 1 minute less than the packet says — they finish cooking in the wok, and overdone noodles snap into short bits when tossed. Drain in a colander, rinse briefly under cold water to stop them cooking, and toss with a teaspoon of the vegetable oil so they don't weld into a block while they wait.

steps.title: Sear the chicken hot
steps.content: Stir-fry the chicken in two batches over the highest heat; set aside.
steps.detail: Get the wok properly hot — a drop of water should skate across and vanish on contact — then add half the remaining oil. Lay in half the chicken in a single layer and leave it alone for a full minute before tossing; move it too soon and the coating tears. Toss for another minute until golden and just cooked, tip onto a plate, and repeat with the second batch. Two batches keeps the wok screaming hot — crowd it and the chicken stews grey in its own juices instead of searing.
tips: The slices are thin and cook fast — cut the thickest one open if unsure; it should be white right through. A thermometer in the fattest slice reads 74°C when done.

steps.title: Stir-fry the vegetables
steps.content: Toss carrot and celery first, then cabbage, then garlic and ginger.
steps.detail: Add the last of the oil to the hot wok. Carrot and celery go in first — toss for about 90 seconds. Then the cabbage: it will squeak, hiss and slump a little, another minute or so. Push everything to one side, drop the garlic and ginger into the gap and stir for 30 seconds until fragrant, then toss the lot together. The veg should still have real bite — it keeps cooking in the next step.

steps.title: Toss it all together
steps.content: Add noodles, sauce and chicken; toss until every strand is glossed and hot.
steps.detail: Add the noodles and pour the sauce over the top. Toss with tongs — lift from underneath and turn, like tossing a salad — for a minute or two, until the noodles are hot and stained brown with sauce. Return the chicken and any juices from its plate, toss once more, and shower over the spring onion. Straight into bowls while it's steaming.

title: Chicken and Corn Soup
description: The Chinese restaurant staple — golden, silky and thick with shredded chicken, sweet corn and soft egg ribbons. One pot, gentle heat, big comfort.
equipment: small jug or bowl for the eggs
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge — reheat gently until piping hot, loosening with a splash of water as it will set thicker. Don't freeze it: the cornflour and egg turn weepy.

substitutions.swap: 2 chicken thighs, poached a few minutes longer
a 420 g tin of creamed corn plus a small tin of kernels — skip the blitzing step

ingredients.amount: a few grinds (white pepper if you have it)

steps.title: Poach the chicken gently
steps.content: Simmer the chicken in the stock with ginger until cooked through, about 15 minutes.
steps.detail: Pour the stock into a large pot, add the ginger slices and the chicken breasts, and bring it up to the barest simmer — small bubbles around the edge, never a rolling boil, which turns chicken stringy. Poach gently for about 15 minutes. The chicken is done when the thickest part reads 74°C, or when it's white right through when you cut into the middle. Lift it onto a plate and keep the stock in the pot.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash your hands and anything it touched before you carry on.

steps.title: Blitz half the corn
steps.content: Blend half the corn with a ladle of stock to make your own creamed corn.
steps.detail: While the chicken poaches, put half the corn in a blender (or a deep jug if you're using a stick blender) with a ladleful of the hot stock. Blitz to a rough, porridgey purée — this is homemade creamed-style corn, and it's what gives the soup its body and sweetness. Leave the other half of the corn whole for texture.
warnings: Hot liquid expands in a blender — fill it no more than halfway, hold the lid down with a folded tea towel and start on low.

steps.title: Shred the chicken
steps.content: Pull the poached chicken into soft shreds with two forks.
steps.detail: Once the chicken is cool enough to handle, shred it with two forks — hold it steady with one and drag with the other, following the grain of the meat. You want soft, rough shreds rather than neat cubes; they float better in the silky soup. If any part of the middle looks pink, slip it back into the hot stock for a few more minutes.

steps.title: Simmer the corn soup
steps.content: Add the blitzed and whole corn to the stock; simmer 5 minutes.
steps.detail: Fish out the ginger slices, then stir the blitzed corn and the whole kernels into the pot. Bring it back to a simmer and let it burble for 5 minutes, until the corn is tender and the soup smells sweet.

steps.title: Thicken with the slurry
steps.content: Stir in the cornflour slurry and simmer until silky.
steps.detail: Mix the cornflour with 3 tablespoons of cold water until smooth — always cold water; cornflour lumps instantly in hot liquid. Re-stir it, then pour into the soup in a thin stream while stirring. Simmer for a minute or two until it thickens to a silky, spoon-coating consistency — think pouring cream, not gravy. It has to reach a simmer to thicken fully, so wait before deciding it needs more.

steps.title: Swirl in egg ribbons
steps.content: Pour beaten egg in a thin stream off a fork, dragging slow circles.
steps.detail: Beat the eggs well in a small jug. Turn the heat to its lowest so the surface of the soup barely moves. Now the restaurant trick: pour the egg slowly over the tines of a fork held just above the pot, moving the fork in wide, slow circles as you go. The thin streams set on contact into soft ribbons. Wait 20 seconds, then give one gentle stir. Hard stirring or a rolling boil shreds the ribbons into cloudy flecks.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.content: Return the chicken, season with soy, sesame oil and pepper, and ladle up.
steps.detail: Stir the shredded chicken back in and give it a minute to warm through. Season with the soy sauce, sesame oil and a good pinch of salt, then taste — it should be gently savoury with real sweetness from the corn. Ladle into bowls and finish with spring onion and a grind of pepper; white pepper, if you have it, is the classic.

title: Ginger Chicken Congee
description: Silky rice porridge simmered in gingery chicken stock until the grains collapse into velvet, with poached chicken folded through. The most soothing bowl in the book.
equipment: large heavy-based pot with a lid
storage: Cool quickly and refrigerate within the hour — cooked rice dishes keep 1 day airtight. Reheat until piping hot with a good splash of water; it sets thick in the fridge.
make_ahead: Rinse the rice, drain it and freeze it overnight in a zip-lock bag. Bring the stock and water to the boil before the frozen grains go in — they split on contact and the congee collapses in nearly half the time.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast — poach the same way but pull it out the moment it hits 74°C so it stays juicy
any long-grain white rice; short-grain gives an even thicker, stickier congee

steps.title: Rinse rice, prep ginger
steps.content: Rinse the rice well; slice half the ginger into coins, the rest into fine matchsticks.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice in a sieve under the tap, rubbing it between your fingers, until the water runs nearly clear — a minute or so. Peel the ginger (the edge of a teaspoon gets into the knobbly bits), slice half of it into coins for the pot, and cut the rest into the finest matchsticks you can manage for serving. Slice the spring onions, keeping the pale parts and the green tops separate.

steps.title: Start the pot
steps.content: Combine rice, stock, ginger coins and the chicken thighs; bring to a gentle simmer.
steps.detail: Put the rice, stock, 2 cups of water, the ginger coins and the whole chicken thighs into a large heavy pot. Bring it to the boil, then drop to the gentlest simmer with the lid ajar. Give it a stir, scraping right across the base so no rice sticks — and do that again every time you walk past the pot.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash your hands and anything it touched before you do anything else.

steps.title: Pull out the chicken
steps.content: After 20 minutes, lift out the chicken and shred it.
steps.detail: By the 20-minute mark the chicken is poached — 74°C at the thickest point, with no pink when you cut into the middle of the biggest thigh. Lift the thighs onto a plate, let them cool enough to handle, and shred them with two forks or your fingers. Set the meat aside — it goes back in at the end so it stays tender instead of stewing to threads.

steps.title: Simmer until it collapses
steps.content: Keep the congee at a low burble for another 30–40 minutes, stirring more as it thickens.
steps.detail: This is the patient part. Keep the pot at a lazy burble, stirring every 10 minutes at first, then more often towards the end — every stir scrapes starch off the grains and makes the congee silkier, the same trick that makes risotto creamy. It's done when the grains have burst open and the whole pot is a soft, flowing porridge, somewhere between soup and warm oats. If it turns gluey-thick early, loosen it with hot water; if it's still watery at 40 minutes, simmer on with the lid off.
tips: Rice sticks silently. If you smell toastiness, don't scrape the base — pour the congee into a fresh pot and leave the caught layer behind.

steps.title: Season, return the chicken
steps.content: Salt to taste, then stir the shredded chicken through to warm.
steps.detail: Fish out any ginger coins you can spot. Season with salt, tasting as you go — congee soaks up more seasoning than you'd expect, and under-salted congee tastes flat. Stir the chicken through and give it a minute or two to warm. The finished congee should slump lazily off the ladle, and it will keep thickening as it stands, so err on the loose side.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.content: Bowl up with ginger matchsticks, spring onion, sesame oil and soy.
steps.detail: Ladle into deep bowls. Scatter over the ginger matchsticks and the spring onion greens, drizzle each bowl with sesame oil and let everyone splash on soy sauce to taste at the table. The raw ginger wakes the whole bowl up — don't skip it.

title: Sesame Peanut Noodles
description: Ten minutes, one pot, and a slurpable peanut-sesame sauce clinging to every strand. Cool cucumber ribbons and crunchy peanuts on top.
storage: Best eaten the moment they're tossed — the noodles drink the sauce as they sit. Leftovers keep 1 day airtight in the fridge and are good cold, loosened with a splash of water.
make_ahead: The sauce (before loosening) keeps 4 days in a jar in the fridge — the raw garlic means it shouldn't sit longer. Make a double batch and dinner really is 10 minutes away.

substitutions.swap: rice noodles or even spaghetti — anything long works
white vinegar with an extra pinch of sugar
any chilli sauce you like, or leave it out for the kids
tahini turns it into straight sesame noodles

steps.title: Whisk the peanut sauce
steps.content: Whisk peanut butter, soy, sesame oil, vinegar, sriracha, sugar and garlic in a big bowl.
steps.detail: In a bowl big enough to toss the noodles in later, whisk together the peanut butter, soy sauce, sesame oil, rice vinegar, sriracha, sugar and the grated garlic. It will seize into a thick, stiff paste and look like a mistake — it isn't. Hot noodle water fixes everything in a couple of steps' time.

steps.title: Ribbon cucumber, crush peanuts
steps.content: Peel the cucumber into long ribbons and roughly crush the peanuts.
steps.detail: Run a vegetable peeler down the length of the cucumber to make long, floppy ribbons, turning it as you go and stopping when you hit the seedy core. Crush the peanuts — easiest in a zip-lock bag with a rolling pin, or rock the flat of your knife over them on the board. Slice the spring onion finely, if you're using it.

steps.title: Boil the noodles
steps.content: Cook the noodles to packet time; scoop out a mug of cooking water before draining.
steps.detail: Bring a large pot of water to the boil and cook the noodles to the packet time — usually 3 to 4 minutes. Taste a strand: it should be tender with a slight spring. Just before you drain them, scoop out about half a mug of the cooking water. That cloudy, starchy water is the secret ingredient — don't tip it down the sink.

steps.title: Loosen the sauce
steps.content: Whisk hot noodle water into the sauce until it's the thickness of pouring cream.
steps.detail: Add the hot noodle water to the seized sauce a tablespoon at a time, whisking between each addition. It will loosen, smooth out and turn glossy — stop when it's the thickness of pouring cream and slides off the whisk in a ribbon. The heat melts the peanut butter and the starch helps the sauce grip the noodles. Gone too thin? Whisk in a little more peanut butter.

steps.title: Toss and pile high
steps.content: Tumble the noodles through the sauce, then top with cucumber, peanuts and spring onion.
steps.detail: Drop the drained noodles into the bowl and toss with tongs until every strand is coated and shiny — lift from the bottom and turn, a good 30 seconds of tossing. Pile into two bowls, top with the cucumber ribbons, crushed peanuts and spring onion, and add an extra squeeze of sriracha if you like heat. Eat straight away, while the sauce is at its silkiest.

title: Oyakodon
description: Japan's chicken-and-egg rice bowl. Thigh and onion simmered in a sweet soy broth, silky egg barely set over the top.
storage: Best eaten straight away — the barely-set egg doesn't reheat gracefully. If you must, fridge leftovers airtight and eat within 1 day (that's the rule for cooked rice), reheated until piping hot.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast sliced thin — watch it closely, it dries out faster than thigh
any white rice you have — a short or medium grain is closest to the Japanese original

steps.title: Get the rice on
steps.content: Rinse the rice, then cook by absorption: 12 min on low, lid on.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice in a sieve or swish it in the saucepan until the water runs mostly clear — this stops it cooking up gluey. Add 1.5 cups of water, bring to the boil, then clamp the lid on and drop the heat to the lowest setting for 12 minutes. When the timer goes, pull it off the heat and leave the lid ON — it finishes steaming while you cook the rest. No peeking; every lift of the lid lets the steam escape.
tips: If the bottom catches slightly, don't scrape it — the loose rice above will still be perfect.

steps.title: Slice chicken and onion
steps.content: Cut chicken into bite-size pieces, slice onion into thin half-moons.
steps.detail: Cut the thighs into pieces about 2–3 cm — big enough to stay juicy, small enough to cook through quickly. Halve and peel the onion, then slice it into thin half-moons about 5 mm wide; they'll soften into the broth and go sweet. Crack the eggs into a small bowl but don't beat them yet — that happens at the last minute.
warnings: Wash your hands, knife and board with hot soapy water after handling raw chicken, before touching anything else.

steps.title: Simmer the broth
steps.content: Combine stock, soy and sugar in the frying pan, add onion, simmer 3 min.
steps.detail: Pour the stock, soy sauce and sugar into a medium frying pan and stir over medium heat until the sugar dissolves. A true oyakodon uses dashi, but chicken stock stands in nicely and keeps it a pantry dish. Scatter in the onion and simmer gently for 3 minutes until the slices start to turn floppy and translucent. It should smell sweet and savoury at once.
tips: Taste the broth now — it should be noticeably sweet-salty, stronger than soup, because plain rice will mellow it.

steps.title: Poach the chicken
steps.content: Add chicken in one layer, simmer gently 7–8 min until cooked through.
steps.detail: Nestle the chicken pieces into the broth in a single layer and keep it at a gentle simmer — small bubbles, not a rolling boil, or the chicken toughens. Turn the pieces once halfway. After 7–8 minutes they should be opaque all the way through: 74°C at the thickest point, or cut the biggest piece open and check there's no pink. The broth will have reduced a little and turned glossy.

steps.title: Pour the egg
steps.content: Pour beaten egg over in two goes; lid on, heat off, until barely set.
steps.detail: Beat the eggs lazily with a fork — you want streaks of white still visible, not a uniform yellow; that's what gives the silky, marbled finish. With the broth simmering, pour about two-thirds of the egg over in a slow circle, lid on for 1 minute. Then pour the rest over, kill the heat entirely, and lid on again for 1–2 minutes. Done is barely set: the egg should still shimmer and wobble. It keeps cooking on the way to the bowl.
tips: If runny egg worries you, leave the lid on an extra minute — but firm scrambled egg means it went too far, so err on the wobble.

steps.title: Slide onto rice
steps.content: Fluff rice into bowls, slide the chicken and egg on top, spoon over broth.
steps.detail: Fluff the rice with a fork and divide it between two deep bowls. Slide half the chicken-and-egg mixture over each — a spatula helps it land in one soft sheet — then spoon the remaining broth over so it seeps down into the rice. Scatter with spring onion and eat straight away, while the egg is still trembling.

title: Japanese Curry Rice
description: Mild, glossy kare raisu from scratch — a browned curry roux, chicken and chunky vegetables ladled next to rice. No packet blocks required.
storage: The curry keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge and gets better as it sits. It freezes for 2 months, though the potato goes a bit grainy — fish it out first if you can. Keep rice separate and use it within 1 day.
make_ahead: Make the curry up to 2 days ahead — the flavour deepens overnight. It thickens in the fridge, so loosen with a splash of water as you reheat.
leftovers: Spoon leftover curry over a crumbed chicken schnitzel and you've made katsu curry — arguably better than the original dinner.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast, or chunks of beef steak simmered 10 minutes longer
sweet potato — it cooks a touch faster, so check it at 12 minutes
a teaspoon of sugar, or a grated apple stirred in with the stock for the classic Japanese trick

steps.title: Prep everything first
steps.content: Chop chicken and vegetables, grate garlic and ginger.
steps.detail: Cut the chicken into 3 cm chunks. Peel the onions and cut into thick wedges — they half-melt into the sauce, which is what you want. Peel the carrots and potatoes and cut both into big 3 cm chunks; Japanese curry is proudly chunky, and small pieces would dissolve. Grate or finely chop the garlic and ginger. Everything ready before the heat goes on means nothing burns while you chop.
warnings: Use a separate board for the raw chicken, or chop the vegetables first and the chicken last — then wash hands, board and knife well.

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.content: Sear chicken in oil over medium-high heat, about 6 min.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in your large pot over medium-high until it shimmers. Add the chicken in a single layer — if it won't fit without crowding, do two batches, because a crowded pot steams instead of browns. Leave the pieces alone for 2 minutes before turning; they'll release from the pot when they're properly golden. You're after colour, not cooked-through — it finishes in the sauce. Lift it out onto a plate.
tips: Those brown bits stuck to the pot are flavour, not mess — the stock will lift them off later.

steps.title: Soften the onion
steps.content: Cook onion 5 min, then garlic and ginger for the last minute.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to medium and add the onion wedges to the same pot. Cook for about 5 minutes, stirring now and then, until the edges turn golden and they smell sweet. Add the garlic and ginger for the final minute only — garlic burns fast and turns bitter. If the pot base is getting too dark, a splash of water rescues it and lifts the good stuff up.

steps.title: Get the rice on
steps.content: Rinse rice, cook by absorption: 12 min on low, lid on, then rest.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice until the water runs mostly clear, then put it in the medium saucepan with 2.25 cups of water. Bring to the boil, lid on, lowest heat for 12 minutes, then off the heat with the lid still on. It will hold happily, warm and fluffy, while the curry finishes — don't lift the lid until serving time.

steps.title: Simmer the vegetables
steps.content: Return chicken, add stock, potato and carrot; simmer 15 min.
steps.detail: Return the chicken and its juices to the pot, pour in the stock and add the potato and carrot. Bring to the boil, then lower to a steady simmer — lazy bubbles across the surface. Skim off any foam that rises in the first few minutes with a spoon. Simmer uncovered for 15 minutes, until a knife slides into the potato with no resistance. While this bubbles away, make the roux in the next step.

steps.title: Brown the curry roux
steps.content: Cook butter and flour to biscuit-brown, then stir in curry powder.
steps.detail: In the small saucepan, melt the butter over medium heat, then whisk in the flour. It will look like wet sand, then loosen into a smooth paste. Keep stirring constantly — this is the step that makes the curry — for 4–5 minutes as it darkens from pale to the colour of a plain sweet biscuit and smells nutty. Stir in the curry powder and cook for 1 minute more until fragrant, then pull it off the heat. If black specks appear, it's burnt; sadly, start again — a burnt roux makes a bitter curry.
tips: The roux keeps darkening off the heat, so pull it a shade lighter than you want.
Make double and fridge the spare in a jar — instant curry blocks for next time.

steps.title: Thicken and season
steps.content: Whisk broth into the roux, stir it back in, simmer 5 min; add honey and soy.
steps.detail: Ladle a cup of the hot broth into the roux and whisk until smooth — doing it this way round stops lumps. Stir the loosened roux back into the pot and simmer for 5 minutes, stirring, until the curry is glossy and coats the back of a spoon. Stir in the honey and soy sauce, taste, and adjust. Too thick? Splash in water until it flows like thick gravy. Too thin? Keep simmering uncovered — it tightens as it reduces, and thickens more as it cools.

steps.title: Serve it Japanese-style
steps.content: Rice on one side of the bowl, curry ladled over the other.
steps.detail: Fluff the rice and press it onto one side of each shallow bowl, then ladle the curry over the other side so it laps at the rice like a shoreline — that's the classic kare raisu look. Check the chicken is cooked through (74°C at the thickest point; it will be after all that simmering). Serve hot.

title: Nasi Goreng
description: Indonesian fried rice with sticky sweet soy, golden chicken and a crispy fried egg on top. Day-old rice makes it.
storage: Fried rice is a same-day dish: if there are leftovers, get them into the fridge within the hour, keep airtight, eat within 1 day and reheat until piping hot. Cooked rice left warm is a real food-safety risk. Fry eggs fresh each time.
make_ahead: Cook the rice the day before: spread it on a tray to cool fast, then fridge it uncovered for an hour and covered overnight. Cold, dry grains fry up separate and toasty — fresh rice turns to mush.

substitutions.swap: prawns (2 minutes a side), or shredded leftover roast chicken tossed in at the end
white sugar or honey — you just need the sweetness to mimic kecap manis
any chilli sauce or sambal, or a fresh red chilli sliced in with the onion

ingredients.amount: 1.5 cups raw, cooked and chilled overnight (about 4 cups cooked)

steps.title: Make the sweet soy
steps.content: Simmer soy sauce and brown sugar 2 min until syrupy.
steps.detail: Real nasi goreng uses kecap manis — a thick, sweet Indonesian soy sauce — and you can get remarkably close with what's in the cupboard. Stir the soy sauce and brown sugar in a small saucepan over low heat for about 2 minutes, until the sugar dissolves and it turns glossy and just slightly syrupy, like thin honey. Take it off the heat; it thickens more as it cools.
warnings: Sugar burns fast and a scorched syrup is both ruined and dangerously hot — keep the heat low and stay with the pan.

steps.title: Prep the rest
steps.content: Dice chicken and onion, chop garlic, slice cucumber and spring onion.
steps.detail: Cut the chicken into small 2 cm pieces so they cook fast and hide happily in the rice. Finely dice the onion, finely chop the garlic, slice the spring onions and slice the cucumber into rounds for serving. Break up the cold rice with damp hands, crumbling any clumps back into separate grains — much easier now than in a hot wok.
warnings: Chop the vegetables before the raw chicken, then wash hands, knife and board in hot soapy water.

steps.title: Fry the chicken
steps.content: Stir-fry chicken in hot oil 5–6 min until golden and cooked through.
steps.detail: Get the wok properly hot over high heat, then add half the oil — it should shimmer and just start to smoke. Add the chicken, spread it out, and let it sit for a minute before tossing so it picks up colour. Stir-fry for 5–6 minutes until golden at the edges and cooked through — cut the biggest piece open and check there's no pink, or probe it at 74°C. Scoop it out onto a plate.

steps.title: Fry the rice
steps.content: Soften onion, add garlic, then rice; toss over high heat until toasty.
steps.detail: Add a splash more oil, then the onion; stir-fry 2 minutes until it softens. Add the garlic and give it just 30 seconds. Tip in the rice and toss everything over high heat for 3–4 minutes, pressing the rice against the hot wok now and then and listening for it to crackle — that's the grains toasting. Done is rice that's hot all the way through, with some golden, chewy edges.
warnings: Garlic burns in under a minute over this heat and turns the whole dish bitter — the rice goes in as soon as it smells fragrant.

steps.title: Sauce the rice
steps.content: Return chicken, add sweet soy and sriracha, toss until every grain is coated.
steps.detail: Return the chicken and pour the sweet soy in around the edge of the wok — hitting the hot metal first makes it sizzle and caramelise instead of just soaking in. Add the sriracha and toss for 2 minutes until every grain is a glossy mahogany brown and the edges catch slightly. Taste: it should be sweet, savoury and gently hot. More sweet soy if it's pale, more sriracha if it's flat.

steps.title: Fry the eggs
steps.content: Fry eggs in hot oil until the edges are crisp and lacy, yolks runny.
steps.detail: Divide the rice between bowls and give the wok a quick wipe. Heat the last of the oil over medium-high until it shimmers, then crack in the eggs — they should bubble and spit at the edges straight away. Fry 2–3 minutes without flipping, until the whites are set with crisp, lacy brown edges and the yolks are still runny. A runny yolk stirred through is the sauce the dish is waiting for.
warnings: Hot oil spits when eggs hit it — crack them in low and close to the pan, and keep water away from the oil.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.content: Egg on each bowl, cucumber on the side, spring onion over the top.
steps.detail: Slide a crispy egg onto each bowl of rice, tuck the cucumber slices down one side — their coolness is the classic foil to the rich rice — and scatter with spring onion. Extra sriracha on the table for the chilli lovers. Eat while the egg still runs.

title: Okonomiyaki
description: Osaka's famous cabbage pancake — crisp outside, tender inside, bacon on top, with tangy sauce and mayo zigzagged all over.
equipment: 26 cm non-stick frying pan with lid
storage: Keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge. Reheat in a dry frying pan over medium heat to bring the crisp back — the microwave turns it sad and floppy. The sauce keeps a week in a jar.
make_ahead: Mix the sauce days ahead. Don't make the batter ahead though — the cabbage weeps water as it sits and the pancake turns soggy. Mix, then cook straight away.

substitutions.swap: thin strips of pork belly, halved prawns, or leave it off for a vegetarian pancake
water with a pinch of salt — the stock just stands in for the traditional dashi

steps.title: Shred the cabbage
steps.content: Shred cabbage as finely as you can; slice the spring onions.
steps.detail: Cut the core out of the cabbage quarter, then shred the leaves as finely as your knife allows — aim for 5 mm ribbons, then run the knife through the pile a few more times. Fine cabbage steams tender inside the pancake; big chunks stay squeaky. Slice the spring onions, keeping a small handful aside for the top. You want a genuinely alarming mountain of cabbage — trust it.

steps.title: Mix the batter
steps.content: Whisk flour, eggs, stock and salt; fold in the cabbage.
steps.detail: Whisk the flour, eggs, stock and a pinch of salt in the large bowl until smooth — it should be a thick pancake batter that falls slowly off the whisk. The stock stands in for the dashi a Japanese cook would use. Now fold in the cabbage and most of the spring onion. It will look like far too much cabbage barely held together by batter — that's exactly right. Okonomiyaki is a cabbage dish wearing a pancake, not the other way round.
tips: If the batter won't coat the cabbage at all, add a splash more stock — but stop while it's still thick and reluctant.

steps.title: Stir the sauce
steps.content: Mix tomato sauce, worcestershire and honey.
steps.detail: Stir the tomato sauce, worcestershire and honey together in the small bowl. Proper okonomi sauce is a bottled thing, but this three-ingredient stand-in lands surprisingly close — sweet, tangy and savoury all at once. Taste it: more honey if it's sharp, more worcestershire if it's flat.

steps.title: Cook the first side
steps.content: Pile batter into the oiled pan, shape a thick round, lay bacon on top; lid on 6 min.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in the pan over medium heat. Pile in all the batter and gently shape it into a round about 2 cm thick, stopping short of the pan edges — don't press it flat and dense. Lay the bacon rashers over the top in a single layer, then put the lid on and cook for 6 minutes. The lid traps steam that cooks the cabbage through. Peek underneath with the spatula: you're after a deep golden brown, and the pancake should slide loosely when you shake the pan.
tips: Medium heat is the whole game — too hot and the outside burns before the middle cooks.

steps.title: Make the flip
steps.content: Slide onto a plate, invert the pan over it, and flip in one confident motion.
steps.detail: Here's the move: slide the pancake out onto a dinner plate, cooked side down. Put the empty pan upside down over the plate like a lid, grip plate and pan handle together (a dry tea towel protects your hands), and flip the whole lot in one smooth, confident motion. Lift the plate away — the bacon side is now down. Commit fully; a hesitant half-flip is the only way this goes wrong. If a bit of edge breaks off, just press it back in. Nobody will know once the sauce goes on.
warnings: The pan is hot on every surface, including the base you're about to grip over — use a dry, folded tea towel, never a damp one, which conducts heat instantly.

steps.title: Cook the second side
steps.content: Slide it back in, lid on, 7 min until the bacon is crisp and the centre is set.
steps.detail: Slide the pancake back into the pan and cook, lid on, over medium heat for another 7 minutes. The bacon underneath crisps against the hot pan and bastes the pancake in its fat — this becomes the presentation side. Done is when a skewer poked into the centre comes out with no wet batter and the pancake feels springy, not squishy, when pressed. If the bacon is browning too fast, nudge the heat down.

steps.title: Dress and serve
steps.content: Sauce over the top, mayo zigzag, nori and spring onion; cut into wedges.
steps.detail: Flip the pancake bacon-side-up onto a board or plate. Spoon the sauce over and spread it to the edges. For the mayo zigzag, spoon the mayo into the corner of a sandwich bag, snip the tip, and pipe lines one way then the other. Scissor the nori into fine ribbons over the top and finish with the reserved spring onion. Cut into wedges and eat hot — the nori will wave in the rising steam, which is exactly how it's meant to look.

title: Vietnamese Chicken Noodle Salad
description: A bright bun-style bowl: cool rice noodles, golden chicken, crunchy veg and a pile of herbs, all pulled together by nuoc cham.
storage: Once dressed, this salad doesn't keep — the noodles drink the dressing and the herbs collapse. Store the parts separately instead: cooked chicken 3 days, prepped veg and noodles 2 days, dressing a week in a jar. Assemble and dress just before eating.
make_ahead: The nuoc cham keeps a week in a jar in the fridge and the chicken can be cooked in the morning — so dinner is just assembly.
leftovers: Leftover undressed chicken, noodles and veg are exactly the filling rice paper rolls want — a bonus lunch.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast (watch the cooking time), or prawns for a couple of minutes a side
roasted cashews, or fried shallots from the Asian aisle for crunch without nuts
the thinnest rice vermicelli you can find is most authentic, but any width works

steps.title: Make the nuoc cham
steps.content: Stir fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, water, garlic and chilli until dissolved.
steps.detail: In the small bowl or jar, stir 3 tablespoons of fish sauce with the juice of both limes, the sugar (keep a teaspoon back for the chicken), 3 tablespoons of water, the garlic finely minced and the chilli finely sliced (seeds out for mild, in for lively). Stir until every grain of sugar dissolves. Now taste it with a bit of cucumber: it should balance right on the edge of salty, sour and sweet, with none of the three winning. Adjust one spoonful at a time — this dressing IS the dish.
tips: It will taste strong on its own — it's meant to. Plain noodles dilute it to perfect.

steps.title: Season the chicken
steps.content: Rub chicken with fish sauce and sugar; rest 10 min while you prep.
steps.detail: Pat the thighs dry with paper towel — dry chicken browns, wet chicken steams. Rub them with the last tablespoon of fish sauce and a teaspoon of the sugar; the sugar helps them caramelise deep golden in the pan. Set aside for 10 minutes while you get on with the noodles and vegetables.
warnings: Wash your hands, board and anything the raw chicken touched before you move on to the salad ingredients.

steps.title: Soak the noodles
steps.content: Cover noodles with boiling water 3–4 min, then drain and rinse cold.
steps.detail: Put the rice noodles in the large heatproof bowl and pour over enough boiling water to cover. Soak for 3–4 minutes (check your packet), prodding with tongs to separate them, until they're white, floppy and tender with no chalky core when you bite one. Drain in the colander and rinse under cold water until fully cool — this stops the cooking and washes off the surface starch so they don't weld into a lump.
tips: Leave them draining in the colander and give them a toss now and then — waterlogged noodles water down the dressing.

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.content: Pan-fry thighs 4–5 min a side until deep golden and 74°C; rest, then slice.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in the frying pan over medium-high until shimmering. Lay the thighs in and leave them alone for 4–5 minutes — moving them early tears the golden crust you're building. Flip and give the other side 4–5 minutes. They're done at 74°C at the thickest point; no thermometer, then cut into the fattest part and check the juices run clear with no pink. Rest on a board for 5 minutes so the juices settle, then slice across into strips.
tips: If the sugar rub darkens too fast, drop the heat a notch — deep amber is flavour, black is bitter.

steps.title: Prep the salad
steps.content: Cut carrot into matchsticks, slice cucumber, pick herbs, crush peanuts.
steps.detail: Peel the carrots, slice them thinly on an angle, then stack the slices and cut into matchsticks. Halve the cucumber lengthways, scrape the seeds out with a teaspoon so it stays crunchy, and slice on an angle. Pick the mint and coriander leaves off their stems and keep them whole — in Vietnamese salads the herbs are salad leaves, not a garnish. Crush the peanuts in a zip-lock bag with a rolling pin or the base of a pan.

steps.title: Build and dress the bowls
steps.content: Noodles, veg and herbs in bowls, chicken on top, nuoc cham over, peanuts last.
steps.detail: Divide the noodles between four bowls and pile the carrot, cucumber and herbs on top in their own little heaps — it should look abundant and unmixed, like a Vietnamese bun bowl. Lay the sliced chicken over, spoon the nuoc cham generously over everything, and finish with the crushed peanuts so they stay crunchy. Everyone tosses their own bowl at the table just before eating.

title: Coconut Chicken Soup
description: A gentle tom kha-style soup: silky coconut broth perfumed with ginger and lime, chicken poached soft, and a hot-sour-salty finish.
storage: Keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge. Reheat gently over low heat without letting it boil, or the coconut milk splits. Freezing isn't recommended for the same reason — coconut milk separates when thawed.
leftovers: Leftover broth makes a lightning lunch — reheat gently and drop in a nest of cooked rice noodles.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh sliced thin — give it 2–3 minutes longer in the broth
any mushroom works — oyster mushrooms torn into strips are closest to the Thai original
vegetable stock — either way, check the label says gluten-free to keep the dish so

steps.title: Prep the pot-ins
steps.content: Slice chicken thinly, slice mushrooms, cut ginger into coins, peel lime zest strips.
steps.detail: Slice the chicken breast across the grain into thin strips about 5 mm thick — thin slices poach quickly and stay silky. Slice the mushrooms. Cut the ginger (no need to peel) into thick coins and give each a whack with the flat of the knife to crack it open. Use the vegetable peeler to take 3 wide strips of zest off one lime, avoiding the white pith. Juice both limes into a cup for later.
tips: Ten minutes in the freezer firms the chicken up and makes thin slicing much easier.
warnings: Wash hands, knife and board with hot soapy water after the raw chicken, before touching anything else.

steps.title: Infuse the stock
steps.content: Simmer stock with ginger coins and lime zest strips, 5 min.
steps.detail: Bring the stock to a simmer in the large saucepan with the ginger coins and lime zest strips. Let it bubble gently for 5 minutes. In Thailand this soup is perfumed with galangal and makrut lime leaves — ginger and fresh lime zest are our honest pantry stand-ins, and they do the same job: filling the broth with fragrance. You should be able to smell it across the kitchen.

steps.title: Add the coconut milk
steps.content: Stir in coconut milk and mushrooms; keep it below a boil, 5 min.
steps.detail: Shake the tin well, then stir in the coconut milk along with the mushrooms. From here on, the one rule: never let it boil. Coconut milk splits at a hard boil — the fat separates into oily beads and the broth loses its silkiness. You want it steaming with the odd lazy bubble breaking the surface, nothing more. Hold it there for 5 minutes while the mushrooms soften. If it does split, a splash of cold stock and a good whisk brings it mostly back.

steps.title: Poach the chicken
steps.content: Slide in the chicken; poach gently 4–5 min until opaque and cooked through.
steps.detail: Slide the chicken slices into the broth, separating any that cling together, and keep the heat gentle — just below a simmer. Thin slices need only 4–5 minutes: they're done when every piece is opaque white all the way through, 74°C if you probe the thickest slice. Gentle heat is what keeps poached chicken tender; a boil would turn the same slices rubbery in the same time.

steps.title: Balance the broth
steps.content: Off the heat: fish sauce, lime juice and sugar. Taste and adjust one at a time.
steps.detail: Take the pan off the heat, then stir in the fish sauce, the lime juice and the sugar. Now taste, and this is the fun part — Thai soups balance salty (fish sauce), sour (lime) and a little sweet (sugar) against the rich coconut. If it tastes flat, it wants fish sauce. If it's heavy, more lime. If it's harsh, a pinch more sugar rounds it off. Adjust one thing at a time, tasting between each, until you can pick out all three in a single spoonful.
tips: Add lime juice off the heat — cooking it dulls the fresh zing you just balanced for.

steps.title: Serve it steaming
steps.content: Ladle into bowls; top with chilli, coriander and spring onion.
steps.detail: Ladle the soup into deep bowls, making sure everyone gets a fair share of chicken and mushrooms. Scatter the sliced chilli over — at the table rather than in the pot, so each bowl can be as fiery as its owner likes — then the coriander and spring onion. Warn everyone off the ginger coins and zest strips, or fish them out as you serve; they've done their job.

title: Chicken Cacciatore
description: Italian hunter's chicken — thighs browned until the skin crackles, then braised low and slow in tomato, capsicum and olives.
equipment: large deep frying pan with lid
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge, or freeze for up to 3 months. Reheat gently on the stove until piping hot right through.
make_ahead: Like most braises it improves overnight — make it a day ahead and reheat gently, adding a splash of water if the sauce has tightened.
leftovers: Pull leftover chicken off the bone and toss it with the sauce through pasta, or spoon it over soft polenta.

substitutions.swap: chicken drumsticks work well — brown them all over and add 10 minutes to the braise
extra chicken stock with a teaspoon of balsamic stirred in
capers give the same salty lift, or leave them out entirely

steps.title: Prep the vegetables
steps.content: Slice onion, capsicum and garlic before any heat goes on.
steps.detail: Halve and peel the onion and slice it into thin half-moons. Deseed the capsicum and cut it into strips about 1 cm wide. Slice the garlic thinly rather than mincing it — bigger pieces won't burn during the long cook. Get all of this done now; once the chicken is browning you won't want to be chopping.

steps.title: Dry and season the chicken
steps.content: Pat the thighs very dry, season the skin generously.
steps.detail: Take the thighs out of their packaging and pat every surface dry with paper towel — wet skin steams instead of browning, and dry skin is the whole secret to a crisp start. Season both sides well with salt and pepper. Salting the skin now also helps it crackle rather than turn rubbery.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and anything raw chicken touched before moving on — and never rinse raw chicken in the sink, it just sprays germs around.

steps.title: Brown the chicken skin
steps.content: Skin-side down over medium heat, 8 minutes — don't move them.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in the pan over medium. Lay the thighs in skin-side down — they should sizzle steadily, not violently. Now the hard part: leave them completely alone for a full 8 minutes. If you try to lift one and it sticks, it isn't ready — properly browned skin releases on its own. When the skin is deep golden and crisp, turn and give the other side 2 minutes, then lift the thighs onto a plate. They'll still be raw inside; that's fine, the braise finishes the job.
tips: Brown in two batches if your pan is crowded — thighs touching each other steam instead of crisping.
warnings: Hot fat can spit as the skin renders — stand back a touch and keep the pan handle turned inwards.

steps.title: Soften the vegetables
steps.content: Cook onion and capsicum in the chicken fat, 6 minutes, garlic last.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to medium-low — there'll be a good slick of chicken fat left in the pan, which is exactly what you want. Add the onion and capsicum with a pinch of salt and cook for about 6 minutes, stirring now and then, until the onion is soft and translucent and the capsicum has lost its rawness. Stir the garlic through for the final minute only — it burns fast, and burnt garlic turns the whole dish bitter.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.content: Deglaze with wine, then add tomatoes, stock, herbs and olives.
steps.detail: Pour in the wine (or a splash of the stock if you're skipping it) and let it bubble for a minute while you scrape the browned bits off the pan base with a wooden spoon — that's concentrated flavour going back into the sauce. Add the tinned tomatoes, crushing them in your hand as they go in, then the stock, oregano, bay leaf and olives. Stir, bring it up to a gentle simmer and taste — olives are salty, so go easy on extra salt for now.

steps.title: Braise the chicken gently
steps.content: Return chicken skin-up, simmer partly covered, 35 minutes.
steps.detail: Nestle the thighs back in skin-side up, keeping the skin above the liquid so it stays crisp, and pour in any juices from the plate. Adjust the heat until the sauce barely burbles — a few lazy bubbles, never a rolling boil, which would toughen the meat. Half-cover with the lid and leave it for 35 minutes. It's done when the sauce has thickened and the chicken reaches 74°C at the thickest point next to the bone, with juices that run clear when pierced.
tips: No thermometer? After 35 minutes of gentle braising, bone-in thighs are reliably done — the meat should be starting to pull away from the bone.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.content: Check seasoning, scatter parsley, serve from the pan.
steps.detail: Fish out the bay leaf. Taste the sauce and adjust with salt and pepper — it should be rich, a little sweet from the capsicum and salty from the olives. Let the pan sit off the heat for 5 minutes so the chicken relaxes, then scatter over the parsley and bring the whole pan to the table. Crusty bread, mash or polenta all love this sauce.

title: Pasta alla Norma
description: Sicily's favourite pasta — eggplant fried until silky and collapsing, folded through a garlicky tomato sauce with basil and ricotta.
storage: The eggplant-tomato sauce keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge — store it separately from the pasta if you can. Reheat gently with a splash of water.
make_ahead: The sauce can be made up to 2 days ahead — it actually deepens in flavour — then reheated while the pasta boils.
leftovers: Leftover sauce is superb spooned onto toast under the grill with extra cheese, or baked with an egg cracked into it.

substitutions.swap: the traditional finish is ricotta salata — if you spot it at a deli, shave it over instead; grated parmesan also works
rigatoni and penne are classic, but spaghetti or any short pasta in the cupboard is fine
zucchini at a pinch — skip the salting and fry until golden

ingredients.amount: 2 tsp for the eggplant, plus more for the pasta water

steps.title: Salt the eggplant
steps.content: Cube the eggplant, salt it and let it sit 20 minutes.
steps.detail: Cut the eggplant into 2–3 cm cubes — no need to peel. Toss the cubes in a colander with the 2 teaspoons of salt and leave them over the sink for 20 minutes. Beads of moisture will form on the surface; that's the salt pulling water out, which is what makes the eggplant fry up creamy instead of soaking oil like a sponge. When time's up, pat the cubes really dry with paper towel — don't rinse them.
tips: Use the wait to slice the garlic, pick the basil leaves and get the pasta water on to boil.

steps.title: Fry the eggplant
steps.content: Fry the cubes in generous oil until deep gold and soft, in batches.
steps.detail: Heat most of the oil in a large frying pan over medium-high — be generous, because eggplant needs oil to turn silky and this is not the dish to skimp on. Fry the cubes in two batches so they sit in a single layer, turning every couple of minutes, until every side is deep golden and the centres are completely soft — 5 to 6 minutes a batch. A cube should squash easily against the side of the pan. Lift them onto a plate lined with paper towel.
tips: Pale, squeaky eggplant means the pan was too crowded or too dry — give the second batch more room and another splash of oil.
warnings: Make sure the eggplant is properly dry before it hits the pan — water in hot oil spits.

steps.title: Make the tomato sauce
steps.content: Sizzle garlic in oil, add tomatoes, simmer 15 minutes.
steps.detail: Wipe the pan out, add a fresh splash of oil and warm the sliced garlic over medium-low with the chilli flakes if using. The moment the garlic turns pale gold and smells sweet — about a minute — tip in the tomatoes, crushing them in your hand as they go. Watch closely: garlic goes from golden to burnt in seconds. Season with salt, then simmer uncovered for 15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until it thickens to a sauce that holds a line when you drag a spoon across the pan.

steps.title: Boil the pasta
steps.content: Cook the pasta in well-salted water, 1 minute short of the packet.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it until it tastes like the sea. Add the pasta, give it a stir so nothing clumps, and cook it 1 minute less than the packet says — it finishes cooking in the sauce. Before you drain, scoop out a mugful of the cloudy cooking water; the starch in it is what turns the sauce glossy.

steps.title: Marry pasta and sauce
steps.content: Fold eggplant and pasta through the sauce with torn basil.
steps.detail: Gently fold the fried eggplant into the tomato sauce, then add the drained pasta and half the basil leaves, torn. Toss over low heat for a minute, adding splashes of the reserved pasta water until the sauce clings to every piece — glossy, not watery or claggy. The eggplant will start breaking down into the sauce as you toss; that's exactly what you want.

steps.title: Serve with ricotta
steps.content: Dollop ricotta over, finish with basil and pepper.
steps.detail: Divide between bowls and dot each with spoonfuls of ricotta — the traditional cheese is ricotta salata, a firm salted version, but fresh ricotta with a small pinch of salt over the top gets you beautifully close. Finish with the rest of the basil and a good crack of pepper. Eat straight away, while the ricotta is melting into the sauce.

title: Stuffed Capsicums
description: Greek-style gemista — sweet red capsicums packed with a herby beef and rice stuffing, baked until the shells slump and caramelise.
equipment: baking dish that fits 4 capsicums snugly
storage: Because of the rice, cool these quickly — into the fridge within the hour — and eat within 1 day, or freeze for up to 2 months. Reheat until piping hot right through.
make_ahead: The stuffing can be made a day ahead and kept in the fridge — fill and bake the capsicums when you're ready.
leftovers: Chop a leftover capsicum, stuffing and all, and pan-fry it into a hash with a fried egg on top.

substitutions.swap: lamb mince is even more traditional and a touch richer
basmati or any long-grain rice works the same way
no mint? just double the parsley
grated cheddar for a milder top, or leave it off

ingredients.amount: 2 tbsp, plus extra for the dish

steps.title: Par-cook the rice
steps.content: Boil the rice for 8 minutes — half-done, still chalky inside.
steps.detail: Bring a saucepan of salted water to the boil and cook the rice for just 8 minutes — it should be bendy on the outside but still firm and chalky in the centre when you bite a grain. Drain it well. It goes in half-done on purpose: the rice finishes cooking inside the capsicums, drinking up the tomato juices as it swells. Fully cooked rice would collapse into mush by the end of the bake.

steps.title: Prep the capsicums
steps.content: Slice off the tops, scoop out seeds, stand them in an oiled dish.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Slice the top 1–2 cm off each capsicum and keep the tops — they're the lids. Pull out the seed cluster and scrape away the white ribs with a spoon. Stand the capsicums snugly in a lightly oiled baking dish; they should lean on each other so nobody tips over. If one won't stand up, shave the thinnest sliver off its base to level it — without cutting a hole through.

steps.title: Brown the mince
steps.content: Soften onion and garlic, then brown the mince — no pink left.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a large frying pan over medium and soften the diced onion for 4–5 minutes until translucent. Stir the garlic through for a minute, then crumble in the mince. Let it sit for a minute or two to catch some colour before you start breaking it up with the spoon. Cook until there's no pink anywhere — about 6 minutes — and any watery liquid has cooked off.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and anything raw mince touched before moving on.

steps.title: Mix the stuffing
steps.content: Stir rice, half the tomatoes, paste and herbs through the mince.
steps.detail: Take the pan off the heat. Stir the drained rice into the mince along with half the tin of tomatoes (crushed in your hand), the tomato paste, oregano, parsley and mint. Season generously with salt and pepper and taste a little — the filling should be punchy now, because the rice will mellow everything as it finishes cooking. It should look moist and saucy, not dry; stir in a splash of water if it isn't.

steps.title: Fill and bake covered
steps.content: Fill loosely, lids on, pour tomato around, bake 40 min under foil.
steps.detail: Spoon the stuffing into the capsicums, stopping about 1 cm from the top — pack it loosely, because the rice still needs room to swell. Pop the lids back on. Pour the rest of the tinned tomatoes and half a cup of water around (not into) the capsicums, cover the whole dish tightly with foil and bake at 180°C fan for 40 minutes. The foil traps steam, and that steam is what actually finishes the rice.

steps.title: Uncover and finish
steps.content: Foil off, feta over, 15 min more until slumped and blistered.
steps.detail: Take the dish out and peel back the foil away from you — mind the escaping steam. Crumble the feta over the tops if using, and return the dish uncovered for another 15 minutes. You're looking for capsicums that have gone soft and slightly slumped with blistered, wrinkled skins, and sauce around them that's thickened. Slumping isn't failure — it's the sign they're properly done.

steps.title: Test the rice
steps.content: Taste a spoonful from the centre — the rice must be fully tender.
steps.detail: Before serving, dig a teaspoon into the middle of one capsicum and taste: the rice should be completely tender with no chalky bite left. If it's still firm, splash a little more water around the dish, cover with the foil again and give it 10 more minutes. Once it's right, rest everything for 10 minutes — the filling settles and the pan sauce thickens — then serve each capsicum with a spoonful of those juices.

title: Potato and Leek Soup
description: The French classic — leeks melted slowly in butter, potatoes simmered soft, everything blended silky with a swirl of cream.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. It freezes well for up to 3 months if you freeze it before the cream goes in — stir the cream through as you reheat.
make_ahead: A genuine make-ahead soup — the flavour is just as good on day three. Reheat gently and loosen with a splash of stock or water if it has thickened.

substitutions.swap: chicken stock gives a rounder flavour if the soup doesn't need to stay vegetarian
milk with an extra knob of butter for a lighter soup, or leave it out entirely
olive oil works, though you lose a little of the classic silkiness

ingredients.amount: 125 ml, plus a swirl to serve

steps.title: Clean the leeks properly
steps.content: Halve lengthways, fan under running water, slice 1 cm thick.
steps.detail: Trim the roots and the tough dark-green tops off the leeks — you want the white and pale-green parts. Halve each one lengthways, then hold it under running water and fan the layers open like a deck of cards; grit hides deep between them, and one sandy layer will ruin a silky soup. Shake them dry and slice into 1 cm half-moons. While you're at the board, peel the potatoes and cut them into rough 3 cm chunks.
tips: Don't bin the dark green tops — they're great flavour for a homemade stock another day.

steps.title: Sweat the leeks
steps.content: Melt in butter over low heat, 12 minutes — soft, never browned.
steps.detail: Melt the butter in the pot over low heat, add the leeks and a good pinch of salt, and stir to coat. Put the lid on and cook gently for 12 minutes, stirring every few minutes. This is sweating, not frying: you should hear a soft murmur, not a sizzle, and the leeks should collapse into a sweet, glossy tangle without taking on any colour.
