steps.detail: Peel the potatoes, then slice them into rounds about 3 mm thick — roughly two stacked coins. Even is everything: same-thickness slices cook at the same rate, while a mix of thick and thin leaves crunchy patches in a soft bake. Take your time and let the knife do the work. Don't rinse or soak the slices — the starch on their surface helps thicken the cream.
tips: Steady the potato by cutting a thin slice off one side first so it sits flat on the board instead of rolling.
warnings: If you use a mandoline, always use the guard or a thick tea towel — it takes a fingertip before you feel it.

steps.title: Warm the garlic cream
steps.content: Warm cream, milk, garlic, thyme, salt and pepper in a saucepan until steaming.
steps.detail: Pour the cream and milk into a small saucepan, grate or crush in the garlic, and add the thyme leaves, salt and a good crack of pepper. Warm it over low heat until wisps of steam rise — don't let it boil. A few minutes of gentle heat pulls the garlic flavour right through the cream. Taste it: it should be confidently seasoned, because the potatoes will drink up a lot of that salt.

steps.title: Layer potatoes and cheese
steps.content: Layer the potato with half the cheddar, then pour the hot cream over.
steps.detail: Arrange a layer of potato slices in the dish, overlapping like fallen dominoes. Scatter over a little cheddar, then repeat — keep half the cheese back for the top later. Press the layers down flat with your hands, then pour the hot garlic cream slowly over the lot. It should come up to just below the top layer, with the odd slice peeking through. Press down again so everything is coated.
tips: Don't worry about perfect layers underneath — only the top one shows. Save your neatest slices for last.

steps.title: Bake covered
steps.content: Cover tightly with foil and bake for 45 min.
steps.detail: Cover the dish tightly with foil and slide it into the oven for 45 minutes. The foil traps steam, which is what actually softens the potato — skip it and you'll get a browned top over crunchy middles. There's nothing to do here but let the kitchen start smelling wonderful.

steps.title: Uncover and brown
steps.content: Remove the foil, scatter the rest of the cheddar, bake 25 min more until golden.
steps.detail: Carefully lift off the foil — mind the puff of steam — and scatter the remaining cheddar over the top. Bake uncovered for another 25 minutes until the top is blistered gold and the cream bubbles at the edges. The real test is a small knife pushed into the centre: it should slide through with no resistance, like soft butter. If it catches on firm potato, give it another 10 minutes.
tips: If the top is browning faster than the middle is softening, sit the foil loosely back on — the colour will hold while the potato finishes.

steps.title: Rest, then serve
steps.content: Rest 10 min before serving.
steps.detail: Straight from the oven the bake is molten and will slump off the spoon. Ten minutes on the bench lets the cream thicken and the layers set, so each scoop holds together. It stays plenty hot — potato bake keeps its heat like nothing else.

title: Crispy Smashed Potatoes
description: Little potatoes boiled, flattened and roasted in hot oil until the edges shatter. Rosemary and parmesan to finish.
equipment: potato masher or a sturdy flat-based glass
storage: Best straight from the tray. Leftovers keep 2 days in the fridge — re-crisp on a tray in a 200°C fan oven for 10 minutes; the microwave turns them soggy.
make_ahead: Boil, dry and smash the potatoes up to a day ahead and keep them covered on the tray in the fridge — then oil and roast just before eating.
leftovers: Chop leftovers into a breakfast hash with bacon and a fried egg.

substitutions.swap: thyme leaves, or a pinch of dried oregano
any hard grating cheese, or skip it to keep things vegan
vegetable oil — it handles the high heat just as well

ingredients.amount: 1 tsp, plus more for the water

steps.title: Boil the potatoes
steps.content: Boil the potatoes whole in well-salted water for 15 min, until a knife slides in easily.
steps.detail: Put the potatoes in a large pot, cover with cold water and salt it generously — it should taste like the sea. Bring to the boil and cook about 15 minutes. Test with a small knife: it should slide into the biggest potato with no resistance. Undercooked potatoes won't smash — they crack into chunks — so if there's any push-back, give them 3 more minutes.

steps.title: Heat the oven hard
steps.content: Heat the oven to 220°C fan with a rack in the top half.
steps.detail: Crisp needs serious heat, so set the oven to 220°C fan now, while the potatoes boil. A rack in the top half of the oven puts the tray where the browning happens. Don't be tempted to roast at a gentler temperature — the potatoes will dry out before they ever crisp.

steps.title: Drain and dry well
steps.content: Drain the potatoes and let them steam-dry in the colander for 5 min.
steps.detail: Drain the potatoes into a colander, give it a good shake, and walk away for 5 minutes. This is the rule that makes the recipe: surface water is the enemy of crisp — wet potatoes steam in the oven instead of frying in the oil. You'll see the skins turn from shiny to dry and matte. That's when they're ready.

steps.title: Smash, oil and space
steps.content: Smash each potato to about 1.5 cm thick on an oiled tray, leaving space between them.
steps.detail: Pour half the oil onto the tray and roll the potatoes around to coat their bases. Press each one down with a masher or the flat base of a glass until it splits and flattens to about 1.5 cm — craggy, ragged edges are exactly what you want, because they crisp the best. Leave a few centimetres between each potato; crowded potatoes steam each other soggy, so use two trays if you need to. Drizzle with the rest of the oil, then scatter over the rosemary and salt.
tips: Press slowly and evenly — a fast whack sends half the potato onto the floor.
warnings: Just-boiled potatoes are full of scalding steam — smash them with a masher or a glass, never the palm of your hand.

steps.title: Roast until crisp
steps.content: Roast for 30 min until deep golden with crackly edges — no flipping needed.
steps.detail: Roast for 30 minutes and resist poking at them early — they release from the tray on their own once the underside is crisp. Done looks like deep gold all over with edges like hot chips, and each one lifts cleanly with a spatula. If they're still pale at 30 minutes, give them another 5 to 10 — colour is flavour here.

steps.title: Finish and serve
steps.content: Grate parmesan over the hot potatoes, add pepper and a last pinch of salt, serve at once.
steps.detail: The second the tray is out, shower the potatoes with finely grated parmesan so it melts on contact, then a crack of pepper and a small pinch of salt. Serve straight away — the crunch is at its peak for about 15 minutes, and no one has ever regretted eating these too soon.

title: Pea and Ham Soup
description: The winter classic without the all-day simmer. Frozen peas, ham and potato in stock, half-blended so it's silky and chunky at once.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge and freezes well for 3 months. The green dulls a little on reheating but the flavour holds — reheat gently until piping hot.
leftovers: It thickens overnight into almost a purée — loosen with a splash of stock or water, or serve it thick over buttered toast.

substitutions.swap: chopped bacon, cooked with the onion until golden
vegetable stock works just as well
parsley, or leave the herbs out altogether

ingredients.amount: 200 g, thick-cut if you can get it

steps.title: Prep the vegetables
steps.content: Dice onion and potato, mince garlic, slice the ham into ribbons.
steps.detail: Dice the onion small and mince the garlic. Peel the potato and cut it into 1.5 cm dice — small pieces are what make this a fast soup, so keep them modest. Slice the ham into short ribbons and set a small handful aside for the top of each bowl. Having it all ready before the heat goes on keeps the next steps relaxed.

steps.title: Soften onion, warm ham
steps.content: Cook the onion in butter for 5 min, then add garlic and most of the ham.
steps.detail: Melt the butter in a large pot over medium heat and cook the onion for about 5 minutes, stirring now and then, until it's soft and translucent — no colour needed. Stir in the garlic for 30 seconds until fragrant, then add all the ham except your reserved handful and let it warm through for a minute. The ham fat melting into the butter is where the soup's savour comes from.
tips: Garlic burns quickly in a hot pot — if it starts to brown, get the stock in early rather than letting it turn bitter.

steps.title: Simmer the potato
steps.content: Add stock and potato, simmer 12 min until the potato is tender.
steps.detail: Pour in the stock, add the potato and bring it to the boil, then drop to a steady simmer for about 12 minutes. The potato is ready when a piece squashes easily against the side of the pot with a spoon. It's doing two jobs: bulking out the soup and, once blended, thickening it — no cream or flour needed.

steps.title: Add the peas
steps.content: Tip in the frozen peas and simmer just 3 min.
steps.detail: Add the peas straight from the freezer — no thawing — and bring the pot back to a simmer for 3 minutes, no longer. Quick cooking is what keeps them sweet and vividly green; boil peas for ten minutes and both the colour and the freshness go khaki. They only need to be heated through and barely tender.
tips: The traditional version simmers split peas with a ham hock for hours — glorious, but this gets you startlingly close on a Tuesday night.

steps.title: Half-blend the soup
steps.content: Blend about half the pot so it's creamy but still chunky, then season.
steps.detail: Take the pot off the heat. Hold a stick blender to one side of the pot and blitz in bursts, blending roughly half — you're after a soup that's silky enough to spoon but still full of whole peas and ham. Too thick? Loosen with a splash of stock or water. Too thin? Blend a little more and the potato will tighten it. Taste before you salt — the ham and stock have already done most of that work — then add pepper.
warnings: If you use a jug blender, blend hot soup in half-full batches with the lid vented and a tea towel over the top — trapped steam can blow the lid off.

steps.title: Serve it up
steps.content: Ladle into bowls, top with the reserved ham and mint, add crusty bread.
steps.detail: Ladle the soup into warm bowls and scatter the reserved ham ribbons over the top, along with a few torn mint leaves and a last crack of pepper. Crusty bread on the side is not optional in spirit, only in the ingredient list. If the soup has sat and thickened while you faffed about, a splash of hot water brings it back.

title: Broccoli Cheddar Soup
description: The mac and cheese of soups. A gentle roux, a whole head of broccoli, and sharp cheddar stirred in off the heat so it stays silky.
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge. Reheat gently and never let it boil — the cheese can split. It freezes, but dairy soups can separate; whisk well as it comes back to heat.
leftovers: Thick leftover soup makes a ridiculous sauce for pasta or a jacket potato.

substitutions.swap: chicken stock, if keeping it vegetarian doesn't matter to you
swap half the milk for thickened cream for a richer soup
leave it out, or grate in a small zucchini instead

ingredients.amount: 200 g, grated — the sharpest you can find

steps.title: Prep everything first
steps.content: Cut broccoli into small florets (chop the stem too), dice onion, grate carrot and cheddar.
steps.detail: Cut the broccoli into small, bite-size florets, then peel the thick stem and chop it finely — it's the sweetest part, don't bin it. Dice the onion, mince the garlic and coarsely grate the carrot. Grate the cheddar too and set it aside; it goes in right at the end. With a roux on the way, you won't have hands free to chop later.
tips: Grate the cheddar from a block — pre-grated cheese is dusted with anti-caking starch and never melts as smoothly.

steps.title: Make the roux
steps.content: Soften the onion in butter, add garlic, then stir the flour through for 2 min.
steps.detail: Melt the butter over medium heat and cook the onion for 4 to 5 minutes until soft and translucent. Stir in the garlic for 30 seconds, then sprinkle the flour over and stir constantly for about 2 minutes. The mixture will look like wet sand and start to smell faintly biscuity — that's the raw flour taste cooking out, and it's what will thicken the whole soup.

steps.title: Whisk in the liquid
steps.content: Whisk in the stock a little at a time, then the milk, until completely smooth.
steps.detail: This is the step that decides between silky and lumpy, and the secret is gradually. Add a splash of stock and whisk until the floury paste is smooth again before adding the next splash. Once about half the stock is in, the mixture loosens and you can pour in the rest, then the milk, whisking as you go. If lumps sneak in anyway, don't panic — whisk hard, or blitz the lot with a stick blender later and no one will ever know.

steps.title: Simmer the broccoli
steps.content: Add broccoli and carrot; simmer gently for 13 min until tender.
steps.detail: Add the broccoli and carrot and bring the pot to a lazy simmer — small bubbles, not a rolling boil. Cook for about 13 minutes, until the thickest floret stem gives easily to a knife. Milky, floury soups love to catch on the bottom, so drag a spoon across the base every few minutes; if you feel resistance, drop the heat a touch.

steps.title: Blend to your liking
steps.content: Blend as much or as little as you like — half-blended is the sweet spot.
steps.detail: Off the heat, run a stick blender briefly through one side of the pot for a creamy soup with plenty of broccoli still in it. Fully smooth or entirely chunky are both legitimate lives to live — this step is yours. If the soup seems thick, loosen it with a splash of milk or stock now.
warnings: If you use a jug blender, blend hot soup in half-full batches with the lid vented and a tea towel over the top — trapped steam can blow the lid off.

steps.title: Melt cheddar off heat
steps.content: Off the heat, stir in the cheddar a handful at a time, then season and serve.
steps.detail: Here's the lesson of the recipe: cheese melted gently stays silky; cheese boiled splits into grease and grit. So take the pot fully off the heat, wait a minute for the bubbling to stop, then stir in the cheddar one handful at a time, letting each melt before the next. Season with salt, pepper and a small pinch of nutmeg if you like. If it ever turns grainy, whisk in a splash of cold milk off the heat and it usually comes back together.
tips: Reheating leftovers? Same rule — low and slow, never boiling, or the cheese splits all over again.

title: Scones with Jam & Cream
description: Devonshire tea at home: tall golden scones, a ten-minute smashed strawberry jam and softly whipped cream.
equipment: baking tray lined with baking paper
6 cm round cutter or a floured glass
storage: Scones are best the day they're baked; keep leftovers airtight for 2 days and refresh in a 160°C fan oven for 5 minutes. The jam keeps 2 weeks in a jar in the fridge; whipped cream is a same-day thing.
make_ahead: The jam can be made days ahead. Baked scones freeze beautifully for 2 months — thaw, then warm through in a low oven.
leftovers: Day-old scones split, toast and butter surprisingly well.

substitutions.swap: frozen berries, straight from frozen — simmer a few minutes longer
buttermilk, for an even more tender crumb
plain flour with 2 teaspoons of baking powder whisked through each cup

ingredients.amount: 3 cups (450 g), plus extra for dusting
1¼ cups (310 ml), plus extra to brush

steps.title: Make the quick jam
steps.content: Simmer strawberries, sugar and lemon juice for 10 min, smashing as they soften.
steps.detail: Put the strawberries, sugar and lemon juice in a small saucepan over medium heat. Once it bubbles, cook for about 10 minutes, squashing the fruit against the side with a fork as it softens. It's done when a spoon dragged across the bottom leaves a trail that holds for a second — it will look loose, but it thickens plenty as it cools. Scrape it into a bowl and set aside; it needs the cooling time anyway.
warnings: Bubbling jam is hotter than boiling water and it clings to skin — never taste it straight from the pan. Cool a spoonful for a minute first.

steps.title: Heat the oven
steps.content: Heat the oven to 200°C fan and line the tray.
steps.detail: Scones want to hit a properly hot oven — the sudden heat is what sends them up tall — so set it to 200°C fan now and let it come fully to temperature while you make the dough. Line the tray with baking paper.

steps.title: Rub in the butter
steps.content: Rub the cold butter into the flour and salt until it looks like coarse breadcrumbs.
steps.detail: Tip the flour and salt into a large bowl and add the cold butter cubes. Using just your fingertips, rub the butter through the flour, lifting your hands high and letting it rain back into the bowl — that keeps everything cool and airy. Stop when it looks like coarse breadcrumbs with a few pea-sized flecks left. Cold and quick is the mantra: warm hands melt the butter, and melted butter means tough scones.
tips: Hot day or warm hands? Chill the cubed butter in the freezer for 10 minutes before you start.

steps.title: Mix a soft dough
steps.content: Add the milk almost all at once and mix with a knife until it barely comes together.
steps.detail: Make a well in the centre and pour in nearly all the milk. Mix with a flat-bladed knife in quick cutting motions — not stirring — until it forms a rough, shaggy dough, adding the last splash only if there's dry flour left in the base of the bowl. Then use your hands just enough to bring it into one soft ball. It should feel pillowy and slightly sticky. Every extra second of working the dough is a tougher scone, so the uglier it looks now, the better it eats.

steps.title: Pat and cut straight
steps.content: Pat the dough 3 cm thick, cut rounds pushing straight down — never twist the cutter.
steps.detail: Tip the dough onto a lightly floured bench and pat it out gently with your hands — no rolling pin — to a generous 3 cm thick. Thick dough is what makes tall scones. Dip the cutter in flour, push straight down and lift straight up. Twisting the cutter seals the cut edge like pinching a straw, and the scone rises lopsided or hardly at all. Press the scraps back together gently, once only, and cut the rest.

steps.title: Bake close together
steps.content: Sit the scones almost touching on the tray, brush with milk, bake 13 min until golden.
steps.detail: Arrange the scones on the tray so they're almost touching — as they rise they lean on each other and climb taller. Brush the tops with a little milk for shine, keeping it off the sides where it can glue the rise. Bake for about 13 minutes until the tops are golden and one lifted from the tray sounds hollow when tapped on its base.
warnings: Don't open the oven for the first 10 minutes — the rush of cold air can stop the rise dead.

steps.title: Whip the cream softly
steps.content: Whip the chilled cream with icing sugar to soft, floppy peaks.
steps.detail: While the scones bake, whip the cold cream with the icing sugar until it holds soft peaks — lift the beaters and the peak should flop over like a beret, not stand stiff. It thickens fast at the end, so slow down and check often. If you go too far and it turns grainy, gently fold through a splash of unwhipped cream to bring back the silk.

steps.title: Build your Devonshire tea
steps.content: Split the warm scones by hand, jam first, cream on top.
steps.detail: Let the scones sit 5 minutes, then split them with your hands or a gentle twist — sawing with a knife squashes that lovely open crumb. Spoon on the strawberry jam, then a generous cloud of cream on top. (Cream first is also correct in some houses; this is not a fight worth having at the table.) Eat while they're warm — that's the whole point of making them yourself.

title: Honey Chicken
description: The takeaway favourite, home-made: crisp cornflour-battered chicken tossed through a glossy honey-soy glaze. Serve it straight away over steamed rice while the batter still crackles.
equipment: wok or large deep frying pan
wire rack or plate lined with paper towel
heatproof bowl or tin for the used oil
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge, though the batter softens — re-crisp in a 200°C oven for 10 minutes rather than the microwave. Store leftover rice separately, cooled fast, and use within 1 day.
leftovers: Chop leftover pieces through a quick fried rice — the glaze seasons the whole pan.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh — juicier and harder to overcook; add a minute to each batch
maple syrup — a touch runnier, so let the glaze bubble an extra minute
basmati or whatever rice is in the cupboard

steps.title: Cut the chicken evenly
steps.content: Cut chicken into 3 cm chunks, season lightly.
steps.detail: Cut the breasts into chunks about 3 cm square — even sizes matter more than exact sizes, because every piece needs to cook in the same time. Pat the pieces dry with paper towel if they look wet; batter grips dry chicken far better. Season with a pinch of salt.
warnings: Raw chicken: use a separate board, and wash hands, knife and board with hot soapy water before touching anything else.

steps.title: Get the rice on
steps.content: Rinse rice, then simmer with the lid on, 12 min.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice in a sieve until the water runs mostly clear — this washes off surface starch so it cooks fluffy, not gluey. Tip into the saucepan with 2¼ cups of cold water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, clap the lid on and drop to the lowest heat for 12 minutes. Then turn the heat off and leave it, lid on, while you cook the chicken — it finishes itself with trapped steam.
tips: No peeking — every lid lift lets the steam out, and steam is what's cooking the rice.

steps.title: Make the batter
steps.content: Whisk eggs, cornflour and water into a thick batter, then coat the chicken.
steps.detail: Whisk the eggs in a big bowl, then whisk in the cornflour and about 2 tablespoons of cold water until smooth — you want the thickness of pouring cream, so it coats a spoon and drips slowly rather than running straight off. Tumble the chicken in and turn until every piece is covered.
tips: Too gluey? A teaspoon of water at a time. Too thin? Another spoonful of cornflour.
warnings: Your hands are in raw chicken here — wash them with hot soapy water before touching taps, handles or anything else.

steps.title: Heat the oil carefully
steps.content: Heat 2 cm of oil until a drip of batter sizzles and floats instantly.
steps.detail: Pour vegetable oil into the wok to about 2 cm deep and set it over medium-high heat for 3–4 minutes. Test with a drip of batter: it should sizzle and float straight away. If it sinks quietly, wait a little longer; if it browns in seconds, back the heat off and give the oil a minute to settle.
warnings: Hot oil and water are enemies — never splash water near the pan, and keep the handle turned in so it can't be knocked.

steps.title: Fry in small batches
steps.content: Fry chicken in 3 batches, 4–5 min each, until deep golden and cooked through.
steps.detail: Lower pieces in one at a time with tongs, working in three batches — crowding drops the oil temperature and you get pale, soggy chicken instead of crisp. Fry each batch 4–5 minutes, turning halfway, until deep golden with a rigid coating, then lift onto the wire rack. Cut the biggest piece open to check: white right through, juices running clear — 74°C at the thickest point if you have a thermometer. Any pink means another minute or two in the oil.
tips: Let the oil come back to temperature for a minute between batches — the sizzle should be lively before the next lot goes in.
warnings: The tongs that handled raw battered chicken shouldn't lift the cooked pieces out — give them a hot soapy wash first, or swap to a clean pair.

steps.title: Make the honey glaze
steps.content: Bubble honey, soy, garlic and a splash of water to a loose syrup, 2 min.
steps.detail: Carefully pour the hot oil into a heatproof bowl or old tin to cool — never down the sink — and wipe the pan out with paper towel, or grab a clean frying pan. Over medium heat, add the honey, soy sauce, grated garlic and 2 tablespoons of water. Let it bubble for 2 minutes until it's a loose syrup that coats the back of a spoon. It thickens more as it cools, so pull it early rather than late.
warnings: Used frying oil stays dangerously hot for a long time — pour it slowly into something heatproof and set it well back from the bench edge.

steps.title: Toss and serve immediately
steps.content: Toss chicken through the glaze, finish with sesame oil and spring onion, pile onto rice.
steps.detail: Turn off the heat, tip all the chicken into the glaze and toss until every piece is shiny. Add a few drops of sesame oil and toss once more. Fluff the rice with a fork, pile the chicken on top and scatter with spring onion. Serve straight away — the batter stays crisp for about 15 minutes and this dish waits for no one.
tips: Glaze at the last second, never ahead — the longer battered chicken sits in sauce, the softer it gets.

title: Salt and Pepper Tofu
description: Crisp cornflour-crusted tofu tossed with garlic, chilli, spring onion and a peppery salt mix — the takeaway classic that happens to be vegan.
equipment: heatproof container for the used oil
storage: Best straight out of the pan — the crust softens as it sits. Leftovers keep 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge; re-crisp in a 200°C oven for 10 minutes.
leftovers: Leftover pieces are great tossed through fried rice right at the end, so they keep some of their crunch.

substitutions.swap: half a teaspoon of chilli flakes, tossed in with the salt mix
any steamed rice, or skip it and serve the tofu as a share plate

ingredients.amount: 1 teaspoon, plus a pinch for dusting
1 teaspoon, coarsely ground (white pepper if you have it)

steps.title: Press the tofu dry
steps.content: Wrap tofu and press under a weight, 15 min.
steps.detail: Crispness lives or dies here. Wrap the block in a clean tea towel or a few layers of paper towel, sit it on a plate, and put something heavy on top — a chopping board with a couple of tins does it. Leave it 15 minutes. Water is the enemy of crisp: the drier the tofu, the better the crust and the less the oil spits later.
tips: Firm or extra-firm tofu only — silken will collapse no matter how long you press it.

steps.title: Prep the aromatics and rice
steps.content: Slice garlic, chilli and spring onion; get the rice on; mix the salt, pepper and sugar.
steps.detail: If you're serving rice, start it now: rinse 1 cup until the water runs mostly clear, simmer with 1½ cups of water, lid on, 12 minutes, then rest off the heat with the lid still on. Finely slice the garlic, chilli and spring onions and set them right by the stove — the final toss takes under a minute, so everything must be ready. Mix the teaspoon of salt with the pepper and sugar in a small bowl; that little pinch of sugar is what makes it taste like the restaurant version.

steps.title: Tear and dust the tofu
steps.content: Tear tofu into 3 cm chunks, toss in cornflour.
steps.detail: Tear the pressed block into rough 3 cm chunks with your hands rather than cutting it — ragged edges fry crunchier than smooth ones. Tip the cornflour and a pinch of salt into a bowl, add the tofu and toss gently until every side wears a thin white jacket. Do this just before frying: left sitting, the coating turns gluey.

steps.title: Fry until deep golden
steps.content: Shallow-fry in 2 batches, 6–8 min each, turning until golden all over.
steps.detail: Heat 1 cm of oil in the wok over medium-high until a pinch of cornflour sizzles on contact. Lower in half the tofu, leaving space between pieces, and — this is the hard part — don't touch it for 2 minutes. It only releases from the pan once the crust has formed. Turn each piece and keep frying until deep golden all over, 6–8 minutes a batch, then drain on paper towel while you fry the rest.
tips: Pale tofu means the oil has cooled — fry fewer pieces at a time and let the oil recover between batches.
warnings: Even pressed tofu can spit when it hits hot oil — lower pieces in away from you and stand back for the first few seconds.

steps.title: Toss with the aromatics
steps.content: Sizzle garlic, chilli and spring onion 30 sec, then toss tofu and seasoning through.
steps.detail: Pour off all but about a tablespoon of oil into a heatproof container — not the sink. Return the pan to high heat, add the garlic, chilli and most of the spring onion, and toss for 30–45 seconds until fragrant and just starting to colour. Tip the tofu back in, scatter over most of the salt-pepper-sugar mix (keep a pinch back for the table), and toss everything hard for another 30 seconds so each piece picks up seasoning and slivers of crisp garlic.
warnings: Garlic burns in seconds at this heat — have the tofu right beside the stove and don't walk away.

steps.title: Serve it straight away
steps.content: Pile onto rice, finish with the last of the spring onion.
steps.detail: Pile the tofu onto bowls of rice or a share plate and scatter with the remaining spring onion. Eat immediately — the crust is at its shattering best for about ten minutes. If it needs anything, it's another small pinch of the seasoning mix, added at the table.

title: Hot and Sour Soup
description: The restaurant staple at home — mushrooms, tofu and silky egg ribbons in a peppery, vinegary broth with real body. Ready in half an hour.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge — it thickens as it sits, so loosen with a splash of water and reheat gently. Not one for the freezer: the cornflour body turns watery once thawed.
make_ahead: The broth (up to the end of the thickening step) can be made a day ahead — add the tofu and egg ribbons fresh when you reheat.

substitutions.swap: white vinegar — sharper, so start with 2 tablespoons and taste
chicken stock is traditional, if it doesn't need to stay vegetarian

ingredients.amount: thumb-sized piece, cut into fine matchsticks
300 g firm, cut into thin batons
1 teaspoon, finely ground (white pepper if you have it)

steps.title: Prep the vegetables and tofu
steps.content: Slice mushrooms, matchstick the carrot and ginger, cut tofu into batons.
steps.detail: Slice the mushrooms thinly, and cut the carrot and peeled ginger into fine matchsticks — this soup is all about slippery strands, so thin and even is the goal. Cut the tofu into batons about the size of your little finger and set them aside gently; they're fragile. Slice the spring onions and keep them for the end.
tips: A speed peeler makes carrot matchsticks easy — peel wide ribbons, stack them, then slice into strips.

steps.title: Simmer the broth
steps.content: Simmer stock with ginger, mushrooms and carrot, 5 min.
steps.detail: Bring the stock to the boil in the big saucepan, then add the ginger, mushrooms and carrot. Drop to a simmer — steady small bubbles, not a rolling boil — for 5 minutes, until the mushrooms are slippery and the carrot has lost its rawness but still has a little bite.

steps.title: Balance hot and sour
steps.content: Add soy, vinegar, pepper and sugar — then taste and adjust.
steps.detail: Stir in the soy sauce, vinegar, pepper and sugar. Now taste from a spoon (blow on it first). The 'hot' in this soup comes from pepper, not chilli, and the 'sour' from vinegar — you want both to make themselves known without shouting. Too flat? More vinegar, half a tablespoon at a time. Not enough warmth? More pepper. The balance is yours; restaurant versions vary just as much.
tips: White pepper is the traditional heat and worth buying, but freshly ground black pepper makes a very good soup too.

steps.title: Thicken with cornflour
steps.content: Stir in the cornflour slurry, simmer 2 min until silky.
steps.detail: Mix the cornflour with 3 tablespoons of cold water in the small bowl until completely smooth — always cold water; straight into hot soup it turns to lumps. Get the soup moving in a gentle whirl and pour the slurry in slowly, stirring the whole time. Simmer 2 minutes: it turns glossy and thickens to the body of pouring cream, just enough to coat the back of the ladle.
tips: Thicker than you like? A splash of water fixes it. Thinner? Mix another teaspoon of cornflour with cold water and repeat.

steps.title: Add tofu and egg ribbons
steps.content: Slide in the tofu, then drizzle beaten egg in a thin stream while swirling gently.
steps.detail: Turn the heat down so the soup is barely simmering and slide in the tofu — stir as little as possible from here so the batons stay whole. Beat the eggs well. Now the fun part: stir the soup slowly in one direction with the ladle, and pour the egg in from about 20 cm up in a thin, steady stream, moving in a circle. It sets on contact into silky ribbons. Wait 30 seconds before stirring again so the ribbons set properly — pour fast and it clumps, stir hard and it turns the soup cloudy.
tips: Pour the egg from a jug with a spout, or over the back of a spoon, for the thinnest, most delicate ribbons.

steps.title: Finish and serve
steps.content: Ladle into bowls, finish with sesame oil and spring onion.
steps.detail: Take the pot off the heat and stir through the sesame oil. Ladle into deep bowls and scatter with spring onion. Put the vinegar bottle and the pepper on the table — the final adjustment belongs to whoever's eating, exactly as it works in the restaurant.

title: Kimchi Fried Rice
description: Korean comfort at its cheapest and best — day-old rice fried in kimchi and its juice, topped with a crisp fried egg and torn nori.
storage: Because it's built on day-old rice, don't stretch it further — leftovers go in the fridge within an hour and get eaten within 1 day, reheated until piping hot. Always fry the egg fresh.
make_ahead: Cook the rice the night before: spread it on a tray so it cools within an hour, then keep it in the fridge. Cold, dry, day-old grains fry up separate and chewy where fresh rice turns to mush.

substitutions.swap: sliced ham, or leave it out and add a little extra sesame oil
sriracha — thinner and sharper, so use 2 teaspoons
any day-old rice — medium grain is actually closer to the Korean original

ingredients.amount: 2 cups cooked, cold and day-old (about ¾ cup raw)
1 cup, chopped, plus 2 tablespoons of the juice
1 tablespoon, plus extra for the eggs

steps.title: Prep everything first
steps.content: Chop kimchi (keep the juice), slice spring onion, chop bacon and garlic, break up the rice.
steps.detail: Fried rice moves fast, so line everything up. Chop the kimchi and keep every drop of juice from the board and the jar — that juice is the seasoning. Slice the spring onions, chop the bacon and garlic, snip the nori into strips. Break the cold rice up with damp hands so there are no fist-sized clumps. If your rice was cooked today, spread it on a tray and give it at least 30 minutes in the fridge — warm fresh rice fries into porridge.
tips: The day-old rice rule: cool cooked rice within an hour, keep it in the fridge, and use it within a day.

steps.title: Crisp the bacon
steps.content: Fry bacon over medium-high until golden, 3 min.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in your biggest frying pan or wok over medium-high. Add the bacon and fry, stirring now and then, until the fat has rendered out and the edges are golden and crisp — about 3 minutes. Skipping the bacon? Just heat the oil and move on; the kimchi carries this dish either way.

steps.title: Fry the kimchi and paste
steps.content: Fry kimchi and garlic 2 min, then the gochujang 1 min more.
steps.detail: Add the kimchi and garlic and fry for 2 minutes, until the kimchi's edges start to catch and caramelise and the kitchen smells sharp and savoury. Push everything aside, dab the gochujang into the clear space and fry it for a minute — frying the paste, rather than just stirring it in, deepens it from sharp to savoury-sweet.
tips: Older, sourer kimchi makes better fried rice — the fresh, crunchy stuff is happier as a side dish.

steps.title: Fry the rice hard
steps.content: Add rice, kimchi juice and soy — press flat, let it crackle, toss, repeat, 4–5 min.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to high and add the rice, kimchi juice and soy sauce. Toss to mix, then press the rice flat against the pan with the spatula and leave it alone for a minute — you're chasing crackle, and it can't crackle while it's moving. Toss, press flat again, and repeat for 4–5 minutes until every grain is stained brick-red and you can hear it popping against the metal. A few chewy, toasted patches on the bottom are a prize, not a mistake.
tips: If the rice sticks stubbornly, run a small splash of oil around the edge of the pan rather than tossing more.

steps.title: Fry the eggs crisp
steps.content: Fry two eggs in a hot oiled pan until the edges frill and the yolks still wobble.
steps.detail: While the rice takes its last press, heat a good film of oil in the small pan over medium-high until it shimmers. Crack in the eggs — they should bubble and frill at the edges straight away. Fry 2–3 minutes without flipping, spooning a little of the hot oil over the whites, until the edges are lacy brown and the yolks still wobble. That runny yolk is the sauce for the whole bowl.
tips: Not confident? Drop the heat and lid the pan for the last 30 seconds — steam sets the top of the white without hardening the yolk.

steps.title: Assemble the bowls
steps.content: Stir sesame oil through, top with the eggs, spring onion and nori.
steps.detail: Off the heat, stir the sesame oil through the rice — adding it at the end keeps its nutty smell, which frying would burn off. Divide between two bowls, slide a fried egg on top of each, and shower with spring onion and nori strips. Break the yolk and stir as you eat.

title: Char Kway Teow
description: The Malaysian hawker classic — wide rice noodles, prawns and egg tossed hard over the fiercest heat your stove can manage. Smoky, sweet-salty and gone in minutes.
equipment: wok or your largest, heaviest frying pan
wok spatula or sturdy flat spatula
small bowls for lining up ingredients
storage: With prawns in the mix, eat it fresh if you can; leftovers keep 1 day in an airtight container in the fridge and want a hard reheat in a smoking-hot pan, not the microwave.

substitutions.swap: thinly sliced chicken thigh — give it 3–4 minutes and check no pink remains before the noodles go in
chorizo gets even closer to lap cheong's sweet, smoky fat
1 teaspoon of sriracha stirred into the sauce — closer to the hawker sambal

ingredients.amount: 400 g fresh wide rice noodles, or 250 g dried
2 tablespoons, plus a splash for the eggs
a good pinch (white pepper if you have it)

steps.title: Line everything up
steps.content: Mix the sauce, prep every ingredient, loosen the noodles — the wok waits for no one.
steps.detail: Once the wok is hot, the whole dish takes about six minutes, so everything must be within arm's reach before you start. Stir the soy sauce, brown sugar and a tablespoon of water together until the sugar dissolves — mixed like this, it stands in for kecap manis, the sweet dark soy the hawkers use. Chop the garlic and chilli, cut the spring onions, slice the bacon, and pat the prawns very dry with paper towel — wet prawns steam instead of sear. Dried noodles get soaked in just-boiled water until pliable but still firm (check the packet), then drained well; fresh ones just need gently separating with your hands.
tips: Fresh wide rice noodles from an Asian grocer are the real thing — if they're fridge-cold, 30 seconds in the microwave softens them enough to separate without tearing.
warnings: Keep raw prawns cold until the moment you cook, and wash hands and board after handling them.

steps.title: Get the wok screaming
steps.content: Heat the empty wok on the biggest burner until a water drop skates, then add oil.
steps.detail: Put the empty wok on your biggest burner, flat out, and give it 2–3 minutes — it's ready when a drop of water flicked in skates across the surface and vanishes. Add the oil and swirl; it should shimmer and show a first wisp of smoke almost immediately. This ferocious heat is the whole dish — it's what hawkers call wok hei, the 'breath of the wok', and a timid pan makes sweet soy noodle soup instead.
warnings: This is hot-pan territory — dry hands, sleeves out of the way, handle turned in, and nothing wet anywhere near the oil.
Don't do this in a nonstick pan — empty nonstick at this heat gives off fumes and wrecks the coating. Carbon steel, cast iron or stainless only.

steps.title: Crisp the bacon
steps.content: Toss bacon strips 1–2 min until the fat renders and the edges catch.
steps.detail: Bacon in first — it stands in for lap cheong, the sweet cured Chinese sausage, and its smoky fat seasons everything that follows. Toss for a minute or two until the strips curl and the edges catch golden-brown. Don't drain the fat; it belongs in the dish.

steps.title: Sear prawns and aromatics
steps.content: Prawns 1–2 min until pink and curled, then garlic and chilli for 30 sec.
steps.detail: Push the bacon aside and lay the prawns flat on the hot metal. Leave them 45 seconds to sear, flip, and cook until pink, opaque and curled into loose Cs — 1–2 minutes all up. Add the garlic and chilli and toss for just 30 seconds until fragrant.
warnings: At this heat garlic goes from golden to burnt in seconds — have the noodles in your other hand, ready to go.

steps.title: Char the noodles
steps.content: Noodles and sauce in — toss, then leave 30–45 sec to char, toss again; 2–3 min total.
steps.detail: Add the noodles and pour the sauce over the top. Toss once so everything is coated and mahogany-brown, then — the hard part — stop. Let the noodles sit against the hot metal for 30–45 seconds, until you smell toasted caramel and hear crackling, then toss and let them sit again. Two or three rounds of this, 2–3 minutes in all. Char only happens to noodles that are holding still; constant stirring just steams them.
tips: Small batches are the real secret — if your pan is under 28 cm, cook one serve at a time with half of everything. Five extra minutes for double the smokiness.
A few torn, catchy noodles are correct — that's char, not failure.

steps.title: Scramble the eggs through
steps.content: Push noodles up the side, crack eggs into the gap, scramble, fold through.
steps.detail: Push the noodles up one side of the wok, add a splash more oil to the cleared space, and crack in the eggs. Scramble them roughly right there for 20–30 seconds until barely set and still glossy, then fold the noodles back over the top so ragged ribbons of egg run all the way through.

steps.title: Finish and serve fast
steps.content: Spring onion and pepper in, one last toss, straight onto plates.
steps.detail: Throw in the spring onion lengths and a good pinch of pepper, toss for a final 30 seconds so the spring onion just wilts, and get it onto plates immediately. Char kway teow is at its peak for about five minutes — the hawkers hand it over the counter still hissing, and that's the standard.

title: Beef Massaman Curry
description: A gentle giant of a curry — brisket and potato braised spoon-soft in coconut milk. No massaman paste needed: red curry paste, peanut butter, cinnamon and star anise get you most of the way there.
storage: Keeps 4 days in an airtight container in the fridge and freezes for 3 months — the potato goes a touch softer but nobody minds. Cool rice quickly and keep it just 1 day.
make_ahead: Like every braise, it's better the next day. Make the whole curry up to 2 days ahead and reheat gently while fresh rice cooks.
leftovers: Spoon leftover curry into a pie dish, top with puff pastry and bake — a ripper massaman pie.

substitutions.swap: chuck or gravy beef braise just as well — anything from the slow-cooking shelf
crunchy or smooth both work; cashew butter if there's a peanut allergy in the house
sweet potato — add it 10 minutes later as it cooks faster

steps.title: Prep the beef and onions
steps.content: Cut brisket into 4 cm cubes, cut onions into thick wedges.
steps.detail: Pat the brisket dry with paper towel, trim off any thick slabs of surface fat (a little marbling through the meat is good — it melts into the sauce), and cut it into big 4 cm cubes. They look huge now but shrink as they braise, and small pieces dry out. Peel the onions, halve them through the root and cut each half into three wedges — chunky is right for a curry this slow.
warnings: Wash your hands, knife and board with hot soapy water after handling raw beef, before touching anything else.

steps.title: Brown the beef in batches
steps.content: Sear beef in two batches until deeply browned, about 5 min a batch.
steps.detail: Heat half the oil in the pot over high heat until it shimmers. Add half the beef with space between the pieces and leave it alone for 2–3 minutes — you want a proper dark crust, and moving it early tears the surface. Turn and brown one more side, then lift into a bowl and repeat with the rest. If you crowd all the beef in at once it steams grey instead of browning, so be patient — that crust is the backbone of the sauce.
tips: The dark stuck-on bits coating the pot base are pure flavour — they lift back into the sauce when the coconut milk goes in.

steps.title: Build the shortcut massaman
steps.content: Fry curry paste, cinnamon and star anise until fragrant, then stir in peanut butter.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to medium, add the remaining oil and the onion wedges, and cook 2 minutes until they start to soften. Add the red curry paste, cinnamon and star anise and fry, stirring, for 1–2 minutes until the paste darkens a shade and smells rich rather than raw. That trio — red paste, warm spices, peanut butter — is our stand-in for real massaman paste, and it gets remarkably close. Stir in the peanut butter with a splash of coconut milk so it loosens instead of catching.
warnings: Curry paste spits when it hits hot oil — stand back a little and keep stirring.

steps.title: Braise low and slow
steps.content: Return beef, add coconut milk and water, simmer very gently 90 min.
steps.detail: Return the beef and every drop of its resting juices, then pour in the rest of the coconut milk and about 250 ml water — the beef should be just barely covered. Scrape the base well to lift the browned bits. Bring it to the laziest possible simmer — one or two bubbles breaking the surface, no more — then set the lid slightly ajar and leave it 90 minutes, stirring every 20 or so. A hard boil can split the coconut milk and seize the beef tough, so if it's bubbling hard, the heat is too high.
tips: Check at the hour mark: a fork should slide in with a little resistance left. That resistance disappears in the final stretch.

steps.title: Add the potatoes
steps.content: Add potato chunks and simmer 30 min more, until beef and potato are spoon-soft.
steps.detail: Peel the potatoes and cut them into chunks about the same size as the beef — do this while the braise ticks along. Nestle them into the sauce, topping up with a splash of water if they're poking out, and simmer gently for 30 minutes. Done means a knife slides through the potato with no push-back and the beef pulls apart under the side of a spoon. If the beef still fights you, keep simmering — it can't overcook at this heat, though you can scoop the potatoes out while it catches up.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse jasmine rice and cook by absorption, 12 min.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice in a sieve under the tap until the water runs mostly clear — this washes off surface starch so it cooks fluffy, not gluey. Tip into the saucepan with 750 ml water, bring to the boil, then clap the lid on and drop to the lowest heat for 12 minutes. Don't lift the lid — the steam is doing the cooking. Turn off the heat and let it sit, still covered, for 5 minutes before fluffing with a fork.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.content: Balance with fish sauce and brown sugar, serve on rice with peanuts.
steps.detail: Fish out the star anise and cinnamon stick. Stir in the fish sauce and brown sugar, then taste — massaman should sit right on the line between salty, sweet and rich. More fish sauce if it's flat, more sugar if it's sharp, and a squeeze of lime lifts the whole pot if it tastes heavy (lime stands in for the tamarind a traditional massaman would use). Serve over rice with roasted peanuts scattered on top.
tips: If a slick of orange oil has risen to the surface, that's normal — stir it back through for richness or skim it off if you prefer it lighter.

title: Teriyaki Chicken Bowl
description: Glossy homemade teriyaki over seared chicken thigh, steamed rice and bright green broccoli. Soy, honey and rice vinegar make the sauce — no bottled teriyaki needed.
storage: Chicken and broccoli keep 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Cool the rice quickly — into the fridge within the hour — and eat it within 1 day, reheated until piping hot.
make_ahead: Mix the sauce up to a week ahead and keep it in a jar in the fridge — then dinner is 25 minutes flat.
leftovers: Sliced teriyaki chicken is excellent rolled into a wrap with mayo and crunchy lettuce for tomorrow's lunch.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast works but dries out faster — sear a minute less a side and still check for 74°C
white vinegar with a small pinch of sugar gets close enough
green beans or snow peas — steam 2 minutes instead of 3
brown sugar, 1 heaped tablespoon, stirred until dissolved

steps.title: Start the rice
steps.content: Rinse rice and cook by absorption, 12 min.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice in a sieve until the water runs mostly clear, then tip it into the medium saucepan with 450 ml water. Bring to the boil, clap the lid on, and turn the heat right down to its lowest for 12 minutes. No peeking — every lid-lift lets the steam escape. When the timer goes, leave it covered off the heat while you finish everything else; it only gets better sitting there.

steps.title: Mix the teriyaki sauce
steps.content: Stir soy, honey, vinegar, ginger and garlic together in a small bowl.
steps.detail: Grate the ginger and garlic finely — a fine grater turns them almost to paste, which is what you want so the sauce stays smooth. Stir them into the soy sauce with the honey and rice vinegar until the honey dissolves. The honey is standing in for mirin, the sweet rice wine a Japanese kitchen would use — honey plus rice vinegar lands you in nearly the same sweet-tangy spot. It'll look thin now; it turns into a glaze in the hot pan later.

steps.title: Prep chicken and broccoli
steps.content: Trim the thighs and pat dry; cut broccoli into small florets.
steps.detail: Pat the chicken thighs dry with paper towel — dry chicken browns, wet chicken steams — and trim off any big flaps of fat with scissors or a knife. Leave the thighs whole; they stay juicier cooked in one piece and sliced after. Cut the broccoli into small, even florets, and don't bin the stem: peel its tough skin and slice the sweet core into coins.
warnings: Use a separate board for raw chicken, and wash hands, board and knife in hot soapy water before touching anything else.

steps.title: Sear the chicken
steps.content: Cook thighs in a hot pan, about 5 min a side, until deep golden.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in the large frying pan over medium-high until it shimmers. Lay the thighs in smooth-side down and leave them — after 5 minutes they should be a deep golden brown and release from the pan without tearing. If they stick, they're not ready; give them another minute. Flip and cook the other side about 5 minutes more. They'll finish cooking in the glaze, so don't stress if they're not quite done yet.
tips: If the pan smokes hard or the chicken darkens too fast, nudge the heat down a notch — golden brown, not mahogany.

steps.title: Glaze the chicken
steps.content: Pour in the sauce and bubble until glossy; check chicken is 74°C.
steps.detail: Pour off all but about a teaspoon of fat, then tip the sauce into the pan — it will bubble up straight away. Turn the thighs through it for 2–3 minutes as it reduces from watery to a glossy glaze that coats the chicken like varnish. It's done when a spoon dragged through the sauce leaves a brief trail. Check the thickest thigh reaches 74°C at its centre — or cut in: the juices should run clear with no pink. Rest the chicken on a board for a couple of minutes, then slice thickly.
tips: Watch the glaze in the last minute — honey burns from glossy to bitter quickly. Off the heat the moment it coats.

steps.title: Steam the broccoli
steps.content: Steam broccoli 3 min while the chicken rests.
steps.detail: Put 2 cm of water in the small saucepan, bring it to the boil, add the broccoli and clap the lid on for 3 minutes. Done is bright green and tender-crisp — a knife slips into a stem with slight resistance. Drain straight away; broccoli left sitting in the hot pot slides from vivid green to sad khaki in a couple of minutes.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Rice, broccoli, sliced chicken, extra glaze, sesame oil and spring onion.
steps.detail: Fluff the rice with a fork and divide it between four bowls. Arrange the broccoli down one side, fan the sliced chicken over the top, and spoon every last bit of glaze from the pan over the chicken and rice. Finish with a few drops of sesame oil — it's strong, so drops, not glugs — and a scatter of spring onion. Eat while the glaze is still sticky-warm.

title: Vegetable Korma
description: Mild, golden and genuinely creamy — cauliflower, carrot and green beans in a cashew, yoghurt and coconut sauce. The gentle curry that wins over curry sceptics.
equipment: large deep frying pan or wide pot
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge — reheat gently and never let it boil, or the yoghurt splits. Cool rice quickly and eat within 1 day.
make_ahead: Cook up to the end of the vegetable simmer a day ahead; reheat gently and stir in the cashew cream and yoghurt just before serving so the sauce stays silky.
leftovers: Leftover korma mashed roughly and wrapped in warm flatbread with a spoon of yoghurt makes a great quick lunch.

substitutions.swap: almonds — soak a little longer, 15 minutes, before blitzing
extra coconut milk to keep it dairy-free and vegan — still add it off the heat
frozen peas, stirred in for the last 2 minutes straight from the freezer

steps.title: Soak the cashews
steps.content: Cover cashews with boiling water and set aside to soften.
steps.detail: Boil the kettle, tip the cashews into a mug or small bowl and cover them with boiling water. They need about 10 minutes to soften — soft cashews blitz into a silky cream, hard ones into gritty bits — and that's exactly how long the prep takes, so they look after themselves while you chop.

steps.title: Prep the vegetables
steps.content: Cut cauliflower into florets, carrots into coins, beans in half; dice onion, grate garlic and ginger.
steps.detail: Break or cut the cauliflower into bite-sized florets — similar sizes mean they cook at the same speed. Peel the carrots and slice into 1 cm coins, top the beans and snap or cut them in half. Dice the onion small (about 5 mm), and grate the garlic and peeled ginger finely. Line everything up near the stove: the cooking moves quickly once it starts.
tips: A teaspoon is the easiest ginger peeler — scrape the skin off with its edge and it steers around the knobbly bits.

steps.title: Start the rice
steps.content: Rinse basmati and cook by absorption, 10 min.
steps.detail: Rinse the basmati until the water runs mostly clear, then put it in the saucepan with 450 ml water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, lid on, lowest heat for 10 minutes, then off the heat — lid still on — to steam while the curry finishes. Resist lifting the lid; trapped steam is what makes each grain separate and fluffy.

steps.title: Soften the aromatics
steps.content: Cook onion in oil until golden, then add garlic and ginger.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in the wide pan over medium heat and cook the onion for about 5 minutes, stirring now and then, until it's soft, translucent and just golden at the edges — that sweetness is the base of the sauce. Add the garlic and ginger and stir for 1 minute more, until the raw smell turns warm and fragrant.

steps.title: Bloom the spices
steps.content: Stir turmeric and curry powder into the hot oil for 30 seconds.
steps.detail: Add the turmeric and curry powder straight into the oily onions and stir constantly for about 30 seconds. This is called blooming: the spices' flavour lives in oil-soluble compounds, so a brief fry in hot fat wakes them up in a way that simmering in liquid never does. You'll smell the difference almost immediately — toasty and rounded instead of dusty. The moment they smell fragrant, move on.
warnings: Ground spices burn in under a minute and turn bitter — keep them moving, and if they blacken, wipe the pan and start the spices again.

steps.title: Simmer the vegetables
steps.content: Add coconut milk, cauliflower and carrot; simmer 10 min, beans in for the last 4.
steps.detail: Pour in the coconut milk, half a tin of water (swirl it in the empty tin) and a good pinch of salt, scraping up anything on the pan base. Add the cauliflower and carrot, bring to a gentle simmer and cook about 10 minutes, adding the beans for the last 4. Done means a knife slides into a carrot coin with barely any push — tender but not collapsing, because they'll sit in the hot sauce a little longer yet.

steps.title: Finish with cashew cream
steps.content: Blitz cashews into cream, stir in, then add yoghurt off the heat.
steps.detail: Blitz the drained cashews with about 100 ml fresh water until completely smooth — like pouring cream, no grit when you rub a drop between your fingers. Stir it into the curry and let it thicken for a minute or two. Now the important bit: take the pan off the heat and stir in the yoghurt one big spoonful at a time. Boiled yoghurt splits into grainy curds and watery whey, and there's no proper way back — off the heat, it melts in silkily. Taste and add salt until the flavours snap into focus.
tips: Extra insurance: stir a spoonful of the hot sauce into the yoghurt first to warm it gently before it goes in.
If it does split, don't tip it out — blitz a ladleful smooth with the stick blender and stir it back through to mostly rescue it.

steps.title: Serve the korma
steps.content: Fluff the rice and ladle the korma over generously.
steps.detail: Fluff the basmati with a fork and pile it into bowls, then ladle the korma over. The sauce should be the colour of pale gold and thick enough to hold a soft mound over the rice — if it's tightened up while sitting, loosen it with a splash of warm water rather than putting it back on a fierce heat.

title: Coconut Fish Curry
description: A gentle golden curry — barramundi poached straight in a turmeric-coconut sauce with tomato and lime. On the table in 40 minutes and it tastes like a holiday.
equipment: large deep frying pan with lid
storage: Seafood is short-lived: leftovers keep 1–2 days in an airtight container in the fridge, reheated gently so the fish doesn't toughen. Cool rice quickly and eat within 1 day.

substitutions.swap: any firm white fish — ling, flathead or snapper all poach beautifully
lemon does the same brightening job
a good pinch of extra salt does the seasoning job — avoid soy sauce here if you're keeping it gluten-free

steps.title: Start the rice
steps.content: Rinse rice and cook by absorption, 12 min.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice until the water runs mostly clear, then into the saucepan with 450 ml water and a pinch of salt. Boil, lid on, lowest heat, 12 minutes, then off the heat with the lid still on. The curry comes together in almost exactly the time the rice takes, so start it first and everything lands together.

steps.title: Prep the flavour base
steps.content: Slice onion, grate ginger and garlic, chop tomatoes, cut fish into chunks.
steps.detail: Finely slice the onion, grate the ginger and garlic, and roughly chop the tomatoes — skins, seeds and all. Cut the barramundi last, on a separate board, into generous 4 cm chunks; big pieces hold together in the sauce where small ones fall apart. Keep the fish in the fridge until the moment it's needed.
warnings: Use a separate board for raw fish and wash hands, board and knife in hot soapy water afterwards.

steps.title: Soften the onion
steps.content: Cook onion in oil over medium heat until soft and golden, 5 min.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in the deep frying pan over medium heat and cook the onion for about 5 minutes, stirring occasionally. You're after soft, translucent and just golden at the edges — if it's browning fast, turn the heat down; a scorched onion makes a bitter curry.

steps.title: Bloom spice, slump tomatoes
steps.content: Stir in ginger, garlic and turmeric, then cook the tomatoes down.
steps.detail: Add the ginger and garlic, stir for 30 seconds until fragrant, then the turmeric for another 30 — frying it briefly in the oil wakes up its earthy warmth and stains everything gold (including wooden spoons, fair warning). Add the tomatoes and cook 3–4 minutes, squashing them against the pan, until they slump into a rough, jammy paste.
warnings: Garlic and ground spices burn fast — keep stirring and have the tomatoes ready to go in; they stop the spices cooking any further.

steps.title: Simmer the sauce
steps.content: Add coconut milk and fish sauce; simmer gently 5 min.
steps.detail: Pour in the coconut milk and fish sauce, stir well and bring to a gentle simmer — small bubbles around the edge, never a rolling boil, which can split coconut milk into oily flecks. Let it simmer 5 minutes to thicken slightly and let the flavours knit. Taste it: it should already be delicious enough to eat with a spoon. Fix it now, because the fish won't wait later.

steps.title: Poach the fish
steps.content: Slide fish into the barely-simmering sauce, lid on, 5–6 min.
steps.detail: Drop the heat so the sauce is barely moving, then slide the fish pieces in and nudge them under with a spoon — don't stir, or they'll break up. Lid on and leave for 5–6 minutes. Done fish is opaque all the way through and flakes into big petals when pressed gently with a fork; if it still looks glassy in the middle or resists flaking, give it another minute. This gentle poach is the whole trick: fish added late to a quiet sauce stays silky where a boil turns it tough.
tips: Thicker pieces go in first if your chunks are uneven — give them a 1-minute head start before the rest.

steps.title: Finish with lime
steps.content: Off the heat, squeeze in lime, then serve over rice with coriander.
steps.detail: Take the pan off the heat and squeeze in half the lime — acid added at the end stays bright instead of cooking away. Taste and add more lime or a splash of fish sauce until it sits nicely between rich, salty and fresh. Ladle over the rice, being gentle with the fish, and finish with coriander leaves and sliced chilli for anyone who wants heat.

title: Garlic Prawn Skewers
description: Prawns charred fast on the barbecue and drenched in garlic-chilli butter with lemon and parsley. Five minutes of cooking, and bread to mop the plate is not optional in spirit.
storage: Best eaten straight off the grill. Cooked prawns keep 1–2 days in an airtight container in the fridge — eat them cold rather than reheating, which turns them rubbery.
make_ahead: Make the garlic butter up to 2 days ahead and keep it in the fridge; thread the prawns a few hours ahead and keep them covered in the fridge until cooking.
leftovers: Cold leftover prawns are terrific tossed through a salad or piled into a buttered roll with a little mayo and lemon.

substitutions.swap: a pinch of chilli flakes, or leave it out entirely for a kid-friendly version
olive oil warmed with the garlic keeps it dairy-free
coriander, for a brighter, more South-East Asian lean

steps.title: Soak the skewers
steps.content: Soak wooden skewers in cold water, 30 min.
steps.detail: If your skewers are wooden or bamboo, lay them in a tray of cold water and weigh them down with a mug so they stay under. Dry skewers scorch and snap over a hot grill; soaked ones survive the trip. Metal skewers skip this step entirely. Use the soaking time to do all the other prep — the timing works out neatly.

steps.title: Make the garlic-chilli butter
steps.content: Melt butter gently with garlic and chilli — soft and fragrant, never browned.
steps.detail: Finely chop the garlic, chilli and parsley, and zest the lemon. Melt the butter in the small saucepan over low heat, add the garlic and chilli, and let them barely sizzle for 1–2 minutes until soft, sweet-smelling and still pale — this is about infusing the butter, not frying. Off the heat, stir in the lemon zest and half the parsley. Set it somewhere warm; you'll pour it over at the end.
warnings: Garlic goes from golden to burnt and bitter in seconds — keep the heat low and pull the pan off early if it's colouring.

steps.title: Thread the prawns
steps.content: Pat prawns dry, thread 3 onto each skewer, oil and salt them.
steps.detail: Pat the prawns really dry with paper towel — dry prawns char, wet prawns steam. Thread each one through twice, once near the tail and once near the head end, so it holds a loose C shape and can't spin when you flip the skewer. Three prawns per skewer, snug but not squashed. Rub them all over with the olive oil and a good pinch of salt.
warnings: Raw seafood rules: wash hands, board and tongs before they touch anything cooked, and keep the prawns in the fridge until the grill is hot.

steps.title: Char the skewers
steps.content: Grill on high, about 2 min a side, until pink, opaque and curled into a loose C.
steps.detail: Get the bbq or chargrill pan properly hot first — a flick of water should dance and vanish on contact. Lay the skewers on and leave them 2 minutes: you'll see the colour turn from grey-blue to coral pink creeping up from the grill. Flip once and give the other side about 2 minutes. Read the curl: a loose C shape with opaque, springy flesh means done; curled into a tight O means overcooked and heading for rubber. Prawns keep cooking from their own heat, so pull them the moment the last translucent patch turns opaque.
tips: Cook in two rounds if your grill is small — crowded skewers drop the temperature and stew instead of charring.

steps.title: Finish with garlic butter
steps.content: Pile skewers on a platter, spoon over the warm butter, add lemon and parsley.
steps.detail: Stack the hot skewers on a platter and spoon the warm garlic-chilli butter over so it runs down into every crevice — the heat of the prawns wakes the garlic right up. Scatter the rest of the parsley, squeeze over half the lemon and cut the rest into wedges for the table. Serve immediately with bread for mopping; the buttery, lemony pool at the bottom of the platter is the best bit.

title: Chicken Enchiladas
description: Tortillas rolled around spiced chicken, smothered in a from-scratch tomato-chilli sauce and a cheddar blanket, baked until bubbling. A proper family tray.
storage: Keeps 3 days covered in the fridge; reheat at 160°C fan until piping hot in the middle. Freezes well for 2 months — thaw overnight in the fridge before reheating.
make_ahead: Assemble the whole tray up to a day ahead, keep it covered in the fridge, and bake straight from cold — add about 10 minutes.
leftovers: A leftover enchilada, chopped and fried up with an egg on top, is a top-tier breakfast.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast works too — watch it closely, it's done the moment it hits 74°C and dries out past that
mozzarella for a stretchier top, or half of each
Greek yoghurt does the same cooling job
wraps are fine — just warm them well so they roll without splitting

steps.title: Prep and preheat
steps.content: Preheat oven to 180°C fan; dice onion, mince garlic, pat chicken dry.
steps.detail: Turn the oven to 180°C fan so it's ready when you are. Dice the onion small (about 5 mm) and mince the garlic. Pat the chicken thighs dry with paper towel and season both sides with salt — dry chicken browns, wet chicken steams. Keep the chicken on its own board, away from everything else.
warnings: Use a separate board for raw chicken, and wash hands, board and knife with hot soapy water before touching anything else.

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.content: Pan-fry the thighs 5–6 min a side until 74°C inside, then rest.
steps.detail: Heat half the oil in a large frying pan over medium-high until it shimmers. Lay the thighs in and leave them alone for 5–6 minutes — moving them early tears the crust that's forming. Flip and give them another 5–6 minutes. They're done at 74°C at the thickest point. Move them to a plate to rest, and keep the pan exactly as it is — those browned bits are flavour.
tips: Don't crowd the pan — cook in two batches if the thighs are touching, or they'll stew instead of brown.
warnings: No thermometer? Cut into the thickest piece: the meat should be opaque all the way through with clear juices, no pink at all.
Wash the tongs (or grab fresh ones) once the raw side is done — don't plate cooked chicken with tools that touched it raw.

steps.title: Make the sauce base
steps.content: Soften onion and garlic, then bloom the spices for 30 seconds.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to medium and add the rest of the oil to the same pan. Cook the onion for 4–5 minutes until soft and translucent, scraping up the chicken's browned bits as it releases moisture. Add the garlic for 1 minute, then the chilli powder, cumin and oregano. Stir for just 30 seconds until the spices smell toasty, then stir in the tomato paste.
warnings: Ground spices burn in seconds and turn bitter — keep them moving, and have the tinned tomatoes open and ready to go.

steps.title: Simmer the sauce
steps.content: Crush in the tomatoes and simmer 10 min until thick and rich.
steps.detail: Crush the tinned tomatoes in with your hand as you pour, then add a good pinch of salt and half a tin of water. Simmer on low for 10 minutes, stirring now and then and squashing any big pieces against the side of the pan. You're after a thick, spoon-coating sauce — it loosens again in the oven. Taste it: gently spicy, a little smoky, seasoned enough to be delicious on its own.
tips: Too spicy? A pinch of sugar or an extra squeeze of tomato paste softens the heat without dulling the flavour.

steps.title: Shred and mix the filling
steps.content: Pull the chicken apart with two forks; mix with some sauce and cheese.
steps.detail: The rested thighs will pull apart easily with two forks — shred them into rough bite-sized pieces and tip any juices from the plate in too. In a large bowl, mix the chicken with about a third of the sauce and a third of the grated cheddar until every strand is coated and glossy. Dry filling makes dry enchiladas, so be generous.

steps.title: Roll the enchiladas
steps.content: Fill each tortilla, roll snugly, and pack seam-down in the dish.
steps.detail: Smear a few spoonfuls of sauce over the base of the baking dish so nothing sticks. Warm the tortillas — 30 seconds in the microwave under a damp paper towel, or wrapped in foil in the oven — so they roll without cracking. Lay one flat, spoon an eighth of the filling in a line just off-centre, roll it up snugly and sit it seam-side down in the dish. Repeat until you have one tight row.
tips: Don't overfill — a rolled enchilada should close with a couple of centimetres of overlap, or it'll burst in the oven.

steps.title: Blanket and bake
steps.content: Pour over the sauce, top with cheddar, bake 20 min until bubbling.
steps.detail: Pour the remaining sauce down the middle of the row, leaving the ends of the tortillas peeking out so they catch and crisp. Scatter the rest of the cheddar over the top. Bake for 20 minutes, until the cheese is melted and golden in patches and the sauce is bubbling up around the edges of the dish.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.content: Rest 5 min, then serve with sour cream and coriander.
steps.detail: Give the tray 5 minutes out of the oven — the filling is lava-hot straight away, and resting lets the enchiladas firm up enough to lift out whole instead of sliding apart. Serve two per person with a spoonful of sour cream on top and a scatter of coriander leaves.

title: Guacamole with Crispy Tortilla Chips
description: Chunky avocado brightened with lime, coriander and a little chilli, with golden oven-baked tortilla chips for scooping. Better than anything from a tub.
storage: Guacamole browns fast — if you must hold it, press cling wrap directly onto the surface and it'll keep in the fridge for a few hours, no more. The chips keep a week in an airtight container.
make_ahead: Bake the chips up to a day ahead and store them airtight once cool; make the guacamole itself just before serving.

substitutions.swap: a pinch of chilli flakes, or leave it out entirely for little kids
two spring onions, finely sliced
lemon in a pinch — different, but still bright
skip it if you're in the tastes-like-soap camp, and add a little extra lime instead

steps.title: Bake the tortilla chips
steps.content: Cut tortillas into wedges, toss with oil and salt, bake at 200°C fan.
steps.detail: Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Stack the tortillas and cut down through the pile into 8 wedges each, like cutting a pizza. Toss the wedges with the oil and a good pinch of salt, then spread over two trays in a single layer — overlapping wedges go soggy. Bake 8–10 minutes until golden with browned edges. They'll still feel slightly soft when hot; they crisp as they cool.
tips: Swap the trays between shelves at halfway so both batches colour evenly.
They go from golden to burnt in under a minute — stay close for the last stretch.

steps.title: Prep the flavour base
steps.content: Finely dice the onion and chilli, chop the coriander, juice the limes.
steps.detail: Dice the red onion as finely as you can — big chunks of raw onion bully everything else in the bowl. Split the chilli lengthwise, scrape out the seeds for mild heat (leave a few in if you like it lively), and dice it fine too. Chop the coriander, stems and all — the stems carry the most flavour. Juice the limes into a cup and set everything aside.
warnings: Wash your hands straight after handling chilli — rubbing your eye later is a mistake you only make once.

steps.title: Check the avocados
steps.content: Test for ripeness, halve, and scoop into a bowl.
steps.detail: A ripe avocado yields to a gentle squeeze at the stem end, like a stick of cold butter. Flick off the little stem button: bright green underneath means ready, dark brown means it's past it. Run a knife around the seed lengthwise, twist the halves apart, then lever the seed out with a spoon. Scoop the flesh into the bowl in big pieces.
warnings: Don't whack a knife into the seed while cupping the avocado in your palm — 'avocado hand' keeps emergency departments busy. Use a spoon.

steps.title: Mash it chunky
steps.content: Barely mash with a fork — chunky, not smooth.
steps.detail: Add half the lime juice and a good pinch of salt, then mash with a fork just until it holds together — 8 or 10 presses, no more. Proper guacamole has real pieces of avocado through it; a smooth puree eats like baby food. The lime is doing two jobs here: brightness, and slowing the browning.

steps.title: Season and taste
steps.content: Fold in onion, chilli and coriander; adjust lime and salt.
steps.detail: Fold the onion, chilli and coriander through with the fork — gently, so you keep the chunks. Now taste properly, with a chip: it should be bright, a little salty and warm at the back of the throat. If it tastes flat, it needs salt; if it tastes heavy, it needs lime. Adjust a little at a time until it pops.

steps.title: Serve straight away
steps.content: Pile onto a plate with the chips — guacamole waits for no one.
steps.detail: Scrape the guacamole into a wide bowl or straight onto a plate, and pile the chips alongside while they're still faintly warm. Avocado starts browning the moment it meets the air, so this is a make-and-eat dish. If it has to sit for a bit, squeeze extra lime over the top and press cling wrap directly onto the surface.

title: Sweet Potato and Black Bean Tacos
description: Caramelised cumin-roast sweet potato, creamy black beans and a punchy avocado-lime smash in warm tortillas. The vegan tacos that convert sceptics.
storage: The roast sweet potato and beans keep 3 days in airtight containers in the fridge — reheat in a hot dry pan so the edges crisp again. Make the avocado smash fresh; it browns overnight.
make_ahead: The sweet potato can be roasted a day ahead — re-crisp it in a hot oven for 10 minutes while you warm the tortillas.
leftovers: Turn leftovers into a burrito bowl: pile the reheated sweet potato and beans over rice with whatever salad is around.

substitutions.swap: pumpkin roasts just the same — same spices, same time
any tinned beans work — kidney or a four-bean mix, rinsed well
wraps, warmed and halved, stand in fine
not a coriander house? A little extra lime and some finely sliced spring onion fill the gap

ingredients.amount: 2 tsp, plus a pinch for the beans

steps.title: Prep and preheat
steps.content: Preheat to 220°C fan; cube sweet potato and toss with oil and spices.
steps.detail: Crank the oven to 220°C fan — high heat is what gets you caramelised edges. Peel the sweet potatoes and cut them into 2 cm cubes, keeping the pieces as even as you can so they all cook at the same rate. Toss them on the tray with the olive oil, cumin, paprika and a good pinch of salt until every piece is stained orange-red.
tips: Round vegetables roll: slice a thin piece off one side first so the sweet potato sits flat and can't slip under the knife.

steps.title: Roast until caramelised
steps.content: Roast 25 min, turning once, until browned and sticky at the edges.
steps.detail: Spread the cubes in a single layer with a little space between each piece — a crowded tray steams instead of roasts, and steam means no caramelised edges. Roast for 25 minutes, turning everything once around the 15-minute mark. Done means deeply browned corners, sticky edges and centres a fork slides into with no resistance.
tips: If the tray looks packed, split across two trays — space matters more than anything else here.

steps.title: Warm the black beans
steps.content: Warm the beans with cumin and a splash of tin liquid; crush a few.
steps.detail: Drain the beans, holding back a couple of tablespoons of the tin liquid. Warm them in a small saucepan over medium heat with that reserved liquid, a pinch of cumin and a pinch of salt for 3–4 minutes. Crush about a third of them against the side of the pan with the back of your spoon — the crushed ones turn creamy and help everything hold together inside the taco.

steps.title: Smash the avocado
steps.content: Mash avocado with lime juice and salt until chunky.
steps.detail: Scoop the avocados into a bowl with the juice of one lime and a good pinch of salt, and mash roughly with a fork — spreadable, but still chunky.
