substitutions.swap: chicken thigh — a little more forgiving and stays juicy
any long pasta such as linguine, or whatever's in the cupboard
any hard grating cheese; a block of tasty at a pinch

steps.title: Prep the lemon and chicken
steps.content: Zest and juice the lemon, slice the chicken, mince the garlic.
steps.detail: Finely grate the zest of the lemon into a small bowl, then juice it into another — keep the two separate. Slice the chicken breast across into 1cm strips so it cooks quickly and evenly. Finely mince the garlic, and pick the basil leaves, tearing the larger ones. Having it all ready means the sauce won't catch while you chop.
tips: Zest only the yellow skin — the white pith underneath is bitter.

steps.title: Boil the spaghetti
steps.content: Cook spaghetti in well-salted water until al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it well — it should taste like the sea. Add the spaghetti, stir so it doesn't clump, and cook 1 minute less than the packet says for a little bite. Before draining, scoop out a mugful of the starchy cooking water — it's the secret to a glossy sauce.
tips: Don't rinse the drained pasta; that starchy coating helps the sauce cling.

steps.title: Pan-fry the chicken
steps.content: Sear chicken strips until golden and cooked through.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high until it shimmers. Season the chicken with salt and pepper, then lay the strips in a single layer — don't crowd them. Leave them undisturbed for 2 minutes to colour, then turn and cook another 2-3 minutes until golden and cooked through with no pink inside. Lift onto a plate.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the knife after the raw chicken, and keep it away from anything you'll eat uncooked.
Chicken is done at 74°C in the thickest part — cut a strip open to check there's no pink.

steps.title: Make the lemon butter
steps.content: Melt butter with garlic, lemon juice and pasta water.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to medium and add the butter to the same pan. Once it foams, stir in the garlic and cook just 30 seconds until fragrant — don't let it brown. Pour in the lemon juice and a good splash of the reserved pasta water and let it bubble into a loose, glossy sauce. Return the chicken and any resting juices to the pan.
warnings: Garlic burns in seconds and turns bitter — keep it moving and pull the pan off the heat if it colours too fast.

steps.title: Toss and finish
steps.content: Toss spaghetti through the sauce with parmesan and basil.
steps.detail: Using tongs, lift the spaghetti straight into the pan with a little water still clinging. Add most of the parmesan and toss hard over low heat for a minute so the cheese and butter emulsify into a silky sauce that coats every strand — splash in more pasta water if it looks tight. Off the heat, fold through the basil and lemon zest. Serve at once with the last of the parmesan and a crack of pepper.
tips: Add the basil right at the end so it stays green and fragrant rather than wilting away.

title: Kimchi Bacon Noodle Bowl
description: Smoky bacon and tangy kimchi tossed through slippery egg noodles, topped with a fried egg and a runny yolk to pull it all together. A fast, punchy bowl for a hungry weeknight.
storage: The noodles keep 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge; reheat in a hot pan with a splash of water. Cook the eggs fresh — they don't reheat well.
leftovers: Cold noodles fry up into a quick bibim-style bowl — hit them hard in a screaming pan and top with a fresh egg.

substitutions.swap: rice noodles, or even spaghetti at a pinch
chopped streaky pork belly for a more classic feel
a drizzle of chilli oil for extra heat

steps.title: Prep the bacon and aromatics
steps.content: Chop bacon, slice spring onions, roughly chop kimchi.
steps.detail: Cut the bacon into short strips. Slice the spring onions, keeping the white parts (for cooking) separate from the green tops (for serving). Roughly chop the kimchi, but hang on to its juice — that brine is pure flavour. Have the soy sauce and sesame oil within reach; once the pan is hot this moves quickly.

steps.title: Boil the noodles
steps.content: Cook egg noodles just short of tender, then drain.
steps.detail: Bring a pot of well-salted water to the boil and cook the egg noodles for a minute less than the packet says — they'll finish in the pan. Drain, rinse briefly under cold water to stop them cooking and clumping, then toss with a few drops of the sesame oil so they stay loose and separate.

steps.title: Crisp the bacon
steps.content: Fry bacon until browned, then the kimchi.
steps.detail: Heat a splash of the olive oil in a wok over medium-high and add the bacon and the white spring onion. Fry for 3-4 minutes until the bacon is golden and its fat has rendered. Tip in the chopped kimchi and cook another 2 minutes until it darkens and smells sweet-sour and caramelised at the edges.
warnings: Kimchi carries moisture that can spit in the hot fat — add it at arm's length and stand back for a second.

steps.title: Toss the noodles
steps.content: Add noodles and soy sauce, toss until glossy.
steps.detail: Add the drained noodles to the pan along with the soy sauce and a splash of the reserved kimchi juice. Toss everything with tongs over high heat for a minute or two until the noodles are hot and streaked orange and glossy. Taste and add a little more soy if it needs it — the kimchi and bacon are already salty, so go steady.

steps.title: Fry the eggs
steps.content: Fry eggs with crisp edges and runny yolks.
steps.detail: Wipe the pan, add the last of the olive oil and fry the eggs over medium-high until the whites are set and lacy-crisp at the edges but the yolks still wobble, about 2-3 minutes. Season with a little pepper. Slide the noodles into bowls, top each with an egg, and scatter over the green spring onion. Break the yolk into the noodles as you eat — it makes an instant sauce.
tips: A drizzle of the remaining sesame oil and an extra spoon of kimchi on top never hurts.
warnings: Hot oil can spit when the egg goes in — crack it close to the pan and tilt the pan away from you.

title: Pumpkin Ginger Coconut Soup
description: Silky pumpkin simmered with fresh ginger and creamy coconut milk, finished with a squeeze of lime and a scatter of coriander. A smooth, warming one-pot bowl.
storage: Keeps 4 days in an airtight container in the fridge and freezes well for up to 3 months. Reheat gently, stirring, until steaming.
make_ahead: Even better the next day once the ginger has mellowed into the pumpkin — make it a day ahead and reheat to serve.
leftovers: Stir leftover soup through cooked rice, or turn it into a fast pumpkin curry with a spoon of red curry paste.

substitutions.swap: sweet potato, or half pumpkin and half carrot
thickened cream if you're not keeping it vegan
chicken stock, though it won't be vegetarian

steps.title: Prep the pumpkin and aromatics
steps.content: Peel and cube pumpkin, dice onion, grate ginger and garlic.
steps.detail: Peel the pumpkin, scrape out any seeds, and cut it into rough 3cm cubes so it cooks evenly. Peel and dice the onion, and finely grate the ginger and garlic. Pick the coriander leaves for serving and cut the lime into wedges. Cutting the pumpkin small is the difference between a 20-minute simmer and a 40-minute one.
tips: Pumpkin skin is tough — steady the piece flat-side down on the board and cut in firm, controlled strokes.

steps.title: Soften the aromatics
steps.content: Sweat onion, ginger and garlic in oil.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a large pot over medium heat and add the onion with a pinch of salt. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring, until soft and translucent but not coloured. Stir in the ginger and garlic and cook another minute until the whole kitchen smells of them — this gentle start builds the soup's backbone.

steps.title: Simmer the pumpkin
steps.content: Add pumpkin and stock, simmer until tender.
steps.detail: Tip in the pumpkin and pour over the vegetable stock — it should just about cover the pumpkin. Bring to a boil, then drop to a simmer and cook for about 18-20 minutes, until a knife slides through a cube with no resistance. If the pumpkin pokes above the liquid, top up with a splash of water.

steps.title: Blend until smooth
steps.content: Blitz the soup smooth, then stir in coconut milk.
steps.detail: Take the pot off the heat and blend to a completely smooth puree with a stick blender. Pour in most of the coconut milk, saving a swirl for serving, and blend again. Return to a low heat just to warm through — don't boil it hard now or the coconut can split.
warnings: Blending hot liquid builds steam pressure — hold the blender fully submerged before you start, and keep your face well back.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.content: Balance with lime, salt and pepper, then add coriander.
steps.detail: Taste and season with salt, pepper and a good squeeze of lime — the acid is what makes the pumpkin taste vivid rather than flat. Ladle into bowls, swirl in the reserved coconut milk, and scatter with coriander. Add more lime at the table.
tips: Too thick? Loosen with a little hot stock or water. Too thin? Simmer a few minutes uncovered.

title: Beetroot Feta Risotto
description: A showstopping magenta risotto — arborio rice stained deep pink by grated beetroot, made creamy with butter and finished with salty feta and a lift of lemon. Earthy, tangy and worth the stirring.
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Risotto firms up cold, so reheat with a good splash of stock or water, stirring until loose and creamy again.
leftovers: Roll cold risotto into balls, crumb and shallow-fry them into pink arancini — leftover risotto's finest hour.

substitutions.swap: any short-grain rice; it just won't be quite as creamy
soft goat's cheese, or ricotta for something milder
chicken stock if you're not keeping it vegetarian

steps.title: Grate beetroot, warm stock
steps.content: Grate beetroot, dice onion, heat stock, crumble feta.
steps.detail: Peel the beetroot and coarsely grate it on a box grater — wear gloves or expect pink fingers for a day. Finely dice the onion, crumble the feta, chop the parsley, and zest and juice the lemon. Pour the stock into a small pot and keep it at a bare simmer beside your risotto pan; ladling in warm stock keeps the rice cooking steadily.
tips: Grate the beetroot over a plate — it flicks juice everywhere and stains chopping boards.

steps.title: Soften the onion
steps.content: Cook onion gently in oil and a little butter.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil and half the butter in a wide pan over medium heat. Add the onion with a pinch of salt and cook for 5 minutes until soft and translucent — you want it sweet and melting, not browned, so keep the heat gentle.

steps.title: Toast the rice
steps.content: Stir in arborio until the grains turn glassy.
steps.detail: Tip in the arborio rice and stir for a minute or two until the grains look glassy at the edges and smell faintly toasty. This step sets up the risotto's bite, so don't skip it — but keep stirring so nothing sticks.

steps.title: Add beetroot, ladle stock
steps.content: Stir in beetroot, then add stock a ladle at a time.
steps.detail: Stir in the grated beetroot, then add a ladle of hot stock and stir until it's almost absorbed before adding the next. Carry on — ladle, stir, wait — for about 18-20 minutes, until the rice is vivid pink and cooked to a creamy, just-firm bite. It should flow like lava, not sit in a stiff mound; add a little hot water if you run out of stock.
tips: You needn't stir non-stop, but frequent stirring is what coaxes the starch out and makes it creamy.

steps.title: Finish with feta and lemon
steps.content: Beat in butter, most of the feta and the lemon.
steps.detail: Off the heat, beat in the rest of the butter, most of the feta, the lemon juice and a good grind of pepper — this final flourish is what makes it glossy and rich. Cover and rest for 2 minutes, then taste for salt, remembering the feta is salty.

steps.title: Garnish and serve
steps.content: Top with feta, parsley and lemon zest.
steps.detail: Spoon into warm bowls and top with the reserved feta, the parsley and a little lemon zest for a fresh, tangy lift against the earthy beetroot. Eat straight away, while it's still loose and creamy.

title: Pork Belly Bok Choy Stir-Fry
description: Strips of pork belly fried until the edges go crisp and sticky, then tossed with bok choy in a quick soy-and-oyster glaze. Fast, glossy and built for a bowl of steaming jasmine rice.
storage: The stir-fry keeps 2 days in the fridge. Cooked rice is best eaten the day it's made — cool it quickly, refrigerate within an hour, and reheat until piping hot before eating.
leftovers: Chop and fold through fried rice the next day with a beaten egg and the last of the greens.

substitutions.swap: pork scotch or thinly sliced pork shoulder
any quick greens — choy sum, broccolini or wedges of cabbage
vegetable or peanut oil, which take the high heat better

steps.title: Rinse and cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse jasmine rice and simmer by absorption.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice in a few changes of cold water until it runs clear, then drain. Put it in a saucepan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt, bring to the boil, then cover and cook on the lowest heat for 12 minutes. Turn off the heat and leave it covered to steam while you cook — don't lift the lid.
tips: Resting the rice off the heat with the lid on for 10 minutes gives you separate, fluffy grains.

steps.title: Slice pork, mix glaze
steps.content: Slice pork belly thin, grate aromatics, mix the glaze.
steps.detail: Slice the pork belly into thin strips about 5mm thick — easier with a very sharp knife, or freeze it for 15 minutes first to firm it up. Grate the ginger and garlic. Trim the bok choy and separate the thick stems from the leaves, since the stems need a head start. In a small bowl, stir together the soy sauce, oyster sauce and honey.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the knife well after the raw pork, and keep it away from the greens you've already cut.

steps.title: Crisp the pork belly
steps.content: Fry pork belly until deep golden and rendered.
steps.detail: Heat a splash of oil in a wok over high heat until it's just smoking. Add the pork belly in one layer and let it fry, stirring only now and then, for 6-8 minutes until the fat has rendered and the edges are deep golden and crisp. Pour off any excess pool of fat, leaving a slick to cook in.
warnings: Fatty pork spits as it renders — use a splatter guard or a deep wok and keep your face back from the pan.

steps.title: Add aromatics and glaze
steps.content: Fry ginger and garlic, then pour in the sauce.
steps.detail: Push the pork to one side, add the ginger and garlic to the space and fry for 30 seconds until fragrant. Pour in the soy-oyster-honey sauce and toss everything together — it will bubble, thicken and coat the pork in a sticky glaze within a minute.
warnings: The garlic and ginger scorch fast on high heat — keep everything moving from the moment they hit the pan.

steps.title: Wilt the bok choy
steps.content: Add bok choy stems then leaves, toss to wilt.
steps.detail: Add the bok choy stems first and stir-fry for a minute, then throw in the leaves with a splash of water. Toss for another minute or two until the leaves are just wilted and the stems still have a little crunch. Grind over some pepper and taste — add a dash more soy if it needs salt.

steps.title: Serve over rice
steps.content: Fluff the rice and pile the stir-fry on top.
steps.detail: Fluff the rested rice with a fork and divide between bowls. Spoon the pork and bok choy over the top and drizzle any glaze left in the pan across the rice.

title: Tomato Olive Couscous Bowl
description: Fluffy couscous forked through with juicy tomato, briny olives and capers, a fistful of parsley and a sharp lemon dressing. Ten minutes, no real cooking, endlessly good cold.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge and travels brilliantly in a lunchbox. Give it a stir and an extra squeeze of lemon before eating.
make_ahead: Made ahead is even better — the couscous soaks up the dressing and the flavours deepen after an hour or two in the fridge.
leftovers: Stuff it into a wrap with a smear of hummus, or pile onto toast with crumbled feta.

substitutions.swap: quinoa for a gluten-free bowl, or pearl couscous for more chew
extra capers, or chopped sun-dried tomato
mint or coriander, or a mix

steps.title: Steam the couscous
steps.content: Cover couscous with boiling water and leave to swell.
steps.detail: Tip the couscous into a heatproof bowl with a pinch of salt and a drizzle of the olive oil. Pour over just enough boiling water to cover it by about a centimetre, cover the bowl tightly with a plate or wrap, and leave for 5 minutes. The grains will drink up the water and swell.

steps.title: Chop the salad
steps.content: Dice tomato, halve olives, chop capers and parsley.
steps.detail: While the couscous steams, dice the tomatoes into small pieces, keeping any juice. Halve or roughly chop the olives, roughly chop the capers, and finely chop the parsley. Zest a little of the lemon, then cut it in half ready to juice.

steps.title: Whisk the lemon dressing
steps.content: Whisk lemon juice, olive oil and pepper.
steps.detail: In a small jar or bowl, whisk the juice of the lemon with the remaining olive oil and a good grind of pepper. Go easy on the salt — the olives and capers bring plenty — then taste and adjust; it should be sharp and savoury.

steps.title: Fork it together
steps.content: Fluff the couscous and fold everything through.
steps.detail: Fluff the steamed couscous with a fork to separate the grains and break up any clumps. Fold through the tomato, olives, capers, parsley and lemon zest, then pour over the dressing and toss until everything is glossy and evenly mixed.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.content: Rest a few minutes so the flavours settle.
steps.detail: Let the bowl sit for 5-10 minutes so the couscous soaks up the dressing and the tomato juices — this short rest is what turns it from plain to properly seasoned. Taste one last time, add more lemon or pepper if it needs waking up, and serve.

title: Charred Corn Tomato Salad
description: Sweet corn charred until smoky and blistered, tossed with juicy tomato, chilli and coriander and a cooling lime-and-sour-cream dressing. A riff on Mexican street corn you eat with a fork.
equipment: chargrill pan or heavy frying pan
storage: Best eaten fresh while the corn is still warm and smoky. To get ahead, char the corn and cut off the kernels, but keep everything separate and dress only just before serving — a dressed salad goes watery within the hour.
leftovers: Fold leftovers through scrambled eggs, or into a quesadilla with extra cheese.

substitutions.swap: Greek yoghurt, or mayonnaise for something richer
frozen or tinned corn, charred hard in a dry hot pan
any crumbly salty cheese — it's standing in for cotija

steps.title: Char the corn
steps.content: Blister corn cobs on high heat until charred.
steps.detail: Rub the corn cobs with a little of the olive oil and lay them on a screaming-hot chargrill or heavy dry pan. Turn every couple of minutes until they're blistered and blackened in patches all over, about 8-10 minutes — those charred spots are the whole point, so don't be timid. Set aside until cool enough to handle.
warnings: The pan needs to be very hot and the cobs can spit — use tongs and keep your hand clear as you turn them.

steps.title: Cut and chop
steps.content: Cut kernels off, chop tomato, chilli and coriander.
steps.detail: Stand a cob upright in a bowl and run a knife down the sides to shear off the kernels, turning as you go. Dice the tomatoes, finely chop the chilli (seeds in for more heat, out for less), and roughly chop the coriander, keeping some leaves for the top. Cut the lime into wedges.
warnings: Wash your hands after chopping the chilli and keep them away from your eyes — the heat lingers on your fingers.

steps.title: Mix the lime dressing
steps.content: Loosen sour cream with lime juice and salt.
steps.detail: In a small bowl, loosen the sour cream with the juice of half the lime and a pinch of salt until it's a pourable, tangy dressing. Taste it — it should be bright and a little sharp to cut through the sweet corn.

steps.title: Toss the salad
steps.content: Combine corn, tomato, chilli and coriander.
steps.detail: In a large bowl, toss the charred corn with the tomato, chilli and chopped coriander. Add most of the dressing and turn everything through gently so the corn stays in clumps rather than turning to mush. Season with pepper and a squeeze more lime.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.content: Crumble over feta and the last of the coriander.
steps.detail: Pile onto a platter, drizzle with the last of the dressing, and crumble the feta over the top — it's standing in for Mexican cotija and brings the salty hit. Finish with the reserved coriander leaves and serve while the corn's still warm.

title: Cheddar Beef Burger Stack
description: Thick, juicy beef patties with a melting cheddar lid, stacked with crisp lettuce, tomato, red onion and a swipe of mayo. A proper homemade burger, no bun required.
storage: Cooked patties keep 3 days in the fridge or freeze for 2 months. Store the components separately and build the burgers fresh, so the bread doesn't turn soggy.
make_ahead: Shape the patties up to a day ahead and keep them covered in the fridge — they hold together better cooked from cold.
leftovers: Crumble a leftover patty into a pasta sauce, or reheat and tuck into a toastie with extra cheese.

substitutions.swap: burger buns or brioche rolls if you have them; toast the cut sides
tasty cheese slices, which melt into a neater lid
aioli, or mayo mixed with a squirt of tomato sauce for a special-sauce feel

steps.title: Shape the patties
steps.content: Divide mince into four, season and shape.
steps.detail: Tip the beef mince into a bowl, season generously with salt and pepper, and mix briefly with your hands — just enough to spread the seasoning, no more, or the patties turn dense. Divide into four and shape into patties a little wider than your bread, then press a shallow dimple into the centre of each so they cook flat instead of bulging.
warnings: Wash your hands and anything the raw mince touched before handling the salad or bread.

steps.title: Prep the stack
steps.content: Slice tomato and onion, wash and tear lettuce.
steps.detail: Slice the tomatoes, and peel and thinly slice the red onion into rings. Wash the lettuce and tear it into cup-shaped leaves. Have the cheddar grated and the mayo ready, so once the patties are cooked you can build fast while everything's hot.

steps.title: Cook the patties
steps.content: Sear, flip once, then melt cheddar on top.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a frying pan over medium-high. Lay the patties in and cook, undisturbed, for about 4 minutes until a deep brown crust forms, then flip once. Pile the grated cheddar on top, add a splash of water to the pan and cover for the last 2 minutes so the steam melts the cheese. Cook until the mince is no longer pink in the middle.
tips: Only flip once; every press or poke squeezes out the juices that keep the patty moist.
warnings: Beef mince must be cooked right through — no pink in the centre and the juices run clear.

steps.title: Toast the bread
steps.content: Toast the bread in the pan juices.
steps.detail: Lift the patties out to rest for a minute. Lay the bread in the same pan and toast for a minute until it soaks up the beefy juices and turns golden and crisp on one side — this is your sturdy, flavour-packed base.

steps.title: Build the stack
steps.content: Layer mayo, lettuce, patty, tomato and onion.
steps.detail: Spread mayo over the toasted bread. Layer on the lettuce first (it stops the base going soggy), then the cheesy patty, then tomato and red onion. Add a little salt on the tomato, top with more mayo or a second slice of bread, and eat straight away while the cheese is molten.

title: Beef Brisket Mash Bowl
description: Beef brisket braised low and slow until it falls into soft shreds in a deep, glossy gravy, spooned over a mound of buttery mash. Sunday-dinner comfort in a bowl.
storage: The braised brisket keeps 3 days in the fridge and freezes for 3 months — it's even better the next day. Mash is best fresh but keeps 2 days; reheat with a splash of milk to bring it back.
make_ahead: Braise the brisket a day ahead and cool it in its gravy — the flavour deepens overnight and the fat sets on top to lift off. Reheat gently while you make the mash.
leftovers: Pile shredded brisket and gravy into soft rolls, or onto toast under grilled cheese for the best next-day lunch.

substitutions.swap: beef chuck or gravy beef, cut into big chunks
thickened cream for a richer mash
chicken stock topped up with a spoon of tomato paste

steps.title: Sear the brisket
steps.content: Brown the brisket all over in a hot pot.
steps.detail: Pat the brisket dry and season it all over with salt and pepper. Heat the olive oil in a heavy casserole over medium-high and brown the brisket hard on every side, about 8-10 minutes total, until deeply crusted — this colour is where half the flavour comes from. Lift it out onto a plate.
warnings: Wash your hands and the board after the raw beef.
The pot and oil are very hot — lower the meat in with tongs rather than dropping it, to avoid a splash.

steps.title: Build the gravy base
steps.content: Soften onion and cook out the tomato paste.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to medium and add the diced onion to the pot, scraping up the browned bits stuck to the base. Cook for 5 minutes until softening, then stir in the tomato paste and cook another 2 minutes until it darkens a shade and smells rich rather than raw.

steps.title: Braise low and slow
steps.content: Add stock and bay, then braise until fork-tender.
steps.detail: Pour in the beef stock, add the bay leaves, and settle the brisket back in with any juices — the liquid should come about two-thirds up the meat. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook in a 150°C fan oven for about 3 hours, turning once halfway, until a fork twists into the meat with no resistance. Top up with a little water if it looks dry.
tips: No oven-safe pot? Keep it at the barest simmer on the stovetop with the lid on, checking now and then.

steps.title: Make the mash
steps.content: Boil potatoes, then mash with butter and milk.
steps.detail: About half an hour before the beef is done, peel and quarter the potatoes and boil them in salted water for 15-20 minutes until a knife slides in easily. Drain well and let the steam die down, then mash with the butter and warmed milk until smooth and creamy. Season with salt and pepper to taste.
tips: Drain the potatoes really well and let them steam-dry for a minute — wet potatoes make gluey mash.

steps.title: Shred and reduce
steps.content: Rest and shred the beef, reduce the gravy.
steps.detail: Lift the brisket out and let it rest while you fish out the bay leaves. If the gravy is thin, simmer it on the stovetop for a few minutes to thicken. Shred or thickly slice the brisket and return it to the gravy to coat, then taste and season.

steps.title: Build the bowl
steps.content: Spoon the brisket and gravy over the mash.
steps.detail: Spoon a generous mound of mash into each bowl and make a well in the middle. Pile in the shredded brisket and spoon plenty of the glossy gravy over the top so it pools into the mash.

title: Lamb Mint Sausage Rolls
description: Golden, flaky sausage rolls with a juicy lamb filling scented with cumin, garlic and fresh mint. Party food that vanishes off the tray before it cools.
storage: Keep cooked rolls 3 days in the fridge and reheat in a hot oven to crisp the pastry back up. They freeze beautifully — freeze them raw and bake straight from frozen, adding 5-10 minutes.
make_ahead: Assemble and chill the rolls a few hours ahead, then egg-wash and bake fresh when guests arrive.
leftovers: Cold sausage rolls are lunchbox gold; pack with a little tomato sauce or chutney on the side.

substitutions.swap: beef or pork mince, or a mix
flat-leaf parsley or coriander
panko, or grated stale bread

steps.title: Soften the aromatics
steps.content: Cook onion, garlic and cumin, then cool.
steps.detail: Finely dice the onion and mince the garlic. Warm the olive oil in a pan over medium heat and cook the onion for 5 minutes until soft, then add the garlic and cumin and cook a minute more until fragrant. Tip it onto a plate to cool completely — hot onion would start cooking the raw lamb before it hits the oven.
tips: Blooming the cumin in the oil like this wakes up its flavour far more than stirring it in raw.

steps.title: Mix the filling
steps.content: Combine lamb, mint, breadcrumbs, egg and onion.
steps.detail: In a bowl, combine the lamb mince, the cooled onion mixture, the breadcrumbs, one of the eggs, the chopped mint, and a good pinch of salt and pepper. Mix with your hands until just combined — the breadcrumbs and egg keep it tender and stop it turning rubbery. Fry a teaspoon of the mix to taste-check the seasoning if you like.
warnings: Wash your hands and the bowl well after handling the raw lamb.

steps.title: Roll and shape
steps.content: Pipe filling along the pastry and roll into logs.
steps.detail: Cut each pastry sheet in half. Spoon a thick log of filling along the long edge of each piece, then roll the pastry over to enclose it, finishing seam-side down. Press the seam to seal, then cut each long roll into smaller pieces — about four per log.
tips: A dab of water along the pastry edge helps the seam stick and stops it bursting open in the oven.

steps.title: Egg-wash and score
steps.content: Brush with beaten egg and slash the tops.
steps.detail: Beat the second egg and brush it over the rolls for a glossy, golden finish. Cut two or three small slashes in the top of each so steam can escape and the pastry stays crisp. Arrange them seam-side down on a lined tray, spaced apart.

steps.title: Bake until golden
steps.content: Bake at 200°C fan until puffed and golden.
steps.detail: Bake in a 200°C fan oven for 25-30 minutes, until the pastry is deep golden and puffed and the filling is cooked right through. Lift one to check the underside is crisp, not pale and doughy. Rest for 5 minutes before serving — the filling is molten hot inside.
warnings: Lamb mince must be cooked through — piping hot in the centre with no pink. Resist opening the oven in the first 20 minutes or the pastry won't rise.

title: Apple Walnut Pancakes
description: Fluffy cinnamon pancakes studded with grated apple and toasted walnuts, cooked in butter until golden. A cosy weekend stack that tastes like apple pie for breakfast.
storage: Best straight from the pan, but leftovers keep 2 days in the fridge. Reheat in a toaster or dry pan to bring back the crisp edges, or freeze with baking paper between them for up to a month.
make_ahead: Mix the dry ingredients the night before, and grate the apple just before cooking so it doesn't brown.
leftovers: Split and toast leftover pancakes, then sandwich with yoghurt and a little honey for a quick snack.

substitutions.swap: buttermilk for extra-fluffy pancakes, or any plant milk
pecans, or leave them out for nut-free
swap the plain flour and baking powder for 1 cup of self-raising flour

steps.title: Grate the apple, chop walnuts
steps.content: Coarsely grate the apple, roughly chop the walnuts.
steps.detail: Peel and coarsely grate the apples on a box grater, stopping at the core. If the gratings look very wet, give them a gentle squeeze so the batter doesn't go sloppy. Roughly chop the walnuts, and toast them in a dry pan for a couple of minutes if you'd like them extra fragrant.

steps.title: Mix the batter
steps.content: Whisk dry, then wet, into a just-combined batter.
steps.detail: In a bowl, whisk together the flour, baking powder and cinnamon. Make a well, crack in the eggs and pour in the milk, then whisk from the centre outwards into a smooth, thick batter — a few small lumps are fine and better than overmixing. Let it rest for 10 minutes so the baking powder gets going and the pancakes rise tall.
tips: Overmixing develops the gluten and makes pancakes tough and flat — stop as soon as the flour disappears.

steps.title: Fold in apple and walnuts
steps.content: Fold grated apple and walnuts through the batter.
steps.detail: Gently fold the grated apple and most of the walnuts through the rested batter with a few strokes — just enough to spread them evenly. Keep a handful of walnuts back for scattering over the finished stack.

steps.title: Cook the pancakes
steps.content: Cook in butter until bubbles form, then flip.
steps.detail: Melt a little butter in a non-stick pan over medium heat. Ladle in rounds of batter and cook until bubbles rise and pop across the surface and the edges look set, about 2 minutes, then flip and cook another 1-2 minutes until golden and springy. Wipe and re-butter the pan between batches, keeping cooked pancakes warm.
tips: Only flip once the bubbles have burst and the edge looks dry, or the pancake will tear and stay gluey.
warnings: Keep the heat at medium — butter and the sugars in the apple catch and burn fast, leaving raw batter inside a too-dark pancake.

steps.title: Stack and serve
steps.content: Stack, scatter walnuts and dust with cinnamon.
steps.detail: Stack the warm pancakes up on plates, scatter over the reserved walnuts and a little extra cinnamon, and add a knob of butter to melt down the sides. A drizzle of honey or maple syrup is never wrong here, if you have it.

title: Pumpkin Sage Brown Butter Pasta
description: Sweet roasted pumpkin, nutty brown butter and sage crisped till it shatters, all tangled through pasta with a shower of parmesan.
storage: Best eaten fresh while the sage is crisp. Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge; reheat gently with a splash of water to loosen.
make_ahead: Roast the pumpkin up to a day ahead; the sauce then comes together in the time the pasta boils.
leftovers: Reheat in a pan with a splash of water, or spread in a dish, top with extra parmesan and grill to a bake.

substitutions.swap: butternut, or sweet potato for a similar sweetness
any short shape — rigatoni, penne or shells all catch the sauce
pecorino, or a block of tasty grated fine
no sage? a little chopped rosemary, crisped the same way

steps.title: Cube and roast the pumpkin
steps.content: Roast pumpkin with oil and salt until caramelised.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Peel the pumpkin and cut into 2-3 cm cubes so they cook evenly. Toss on a tray with the olive oil and a pinch of salt, spread out in a single layer, and roast for about 25 minutes until the edges are deep gold and a fork slides in easily. Those caramelised edges are where the sweetness lives, so don't pull them early.
tips: Line the tray with baking paper so nothing sticks and the wash-up is easy.

steps.title: Boil the pasta
steps.content: Cook pasta in well-salted water until al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it well — it should taste like the sea. Add the pasta, stir so it doesn't clump, and cook one minute less than the packet says for a bit of bite. Just before draining, scoop out a mugful of the starchy cooking water; you'll use it to loosen the sauce.
tips: Don't rinse the drained pasta — that starch helps the brown butter cling.

steps.title: Brown the butter and sage
steps.content: Melt butter with sage until nutty and golden.
steps.detail: Melt the butter in a large frying pan over medium heat. Once it foams, add the whole sage leaves and the sliced garlic. Swirl the pan and keep watching — in 2-3 minutes the foam settles, the milk solids turn golden-brown and it smells nutty and biscuity while the sage goes crisp. Pull it off the heat the moment it's amber; it tips from nutty to burnt fast.
tips: Lift the crisp sage out onto paper to keep it shattery for the top.
warnings: Garlic and butter scorch quickly — the second it smells nutty, take the pan off the heat.

steps.title: Make the pumpkin sauce
steps.content: Mash half the pumpkin into the butter with pasta water.
steps.detail: Tip about half the roasted pumpkin into the brown butter and mash it with a fork or the back of a spoon, adding a good splash of the reserved pasta water. Stir into a loose, glossy sauce that coats the spoon. Keep the rest of the pumpkin in cubes to fold through whole for texture.
tips: Too thick? Add pasta water a splash at a time. Too thin? Let it bubble for a minute.

steps.title: Toss and serve
steps.content: Fold through pasta, parmesan, sage and pepper.
steps.detail: Drag the drained pasta straight into the pan with the whole pumpkin cubes and most of the parmesan. Toss over low heat for a minute, loosening with pasta water until every strand is silky. Finish with a crack of pepper, the crisp sage scattered over and the last of the parmesan. Eat straight away while it's glossy.

title: Haloumi Tomato Pasta Bowl
description: Cherry tomatoes burst into a quick garlicky sauce, tossed through pasta and piled with golden, squeaky haloumi and a hit of lemon.
storage: The tomato pasta keeps 2 days in the fridge. Fry fresh haloumi to serve — it turns rubbery once cold and reheated.
make_ahead: Make the tomato sauce a day ahead; fry the haloumi fresh to serve so it stays golden.
leftovers: Cold, the tomato pasta makes a good next-day lunch — freshen it with a squeeze of lemon.

substitutions.swap: crumbled feta (stir through instead of frying), or firm tofu
a tin of cherry tomatoes when fresh ones are dear
any short shape, or spaghetti
flat-leaf parsley or a handful of rocket

steps.title: Prep the tomatoes and haloumi
steps.content: Halve tomatoes, slice haloumi, slice garlic, zest lemon.
steps.detail: Halve the cherry tomatoes so they collapse quickly. Slice the haloumi into 1 cm planks and pat them very dry with paper towel — dry haloumi is what fries golden instead of steaming. Thinly slice the garlic, zest the lemon into a small pile, and pick the basil leaves. Having it all ready first means the quick cooking won't run away from you.

steps.title: Boil the pasta
steps.content: Cook pasta in salted water until al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it well. Cook the pasta one minute short of the packet time so it keeps some bite. Scoop out a mug of the starchy water before you drain — it's the trick to a silky sauce.
tips: Time the pasta to land as the tomatoes finish so nothing sits and cools.

steps.title: Fry the haloumi golden
steps.content: Pan-fry haloumi until crisp and golden both sides.
steps.detail: Heat half the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high. Lay in the haloumi and leave it undisturbed for 2 minutes until the underside is deep golden, then flip and do the other side. Lift onto a plate — it firms up and stays squeaky. Don't crowd the pan or it'll sweat instead of browning.
tips: Haloumi is very salty, so hold back on adding extra salt to the sauce.

steps.title: Blister the tomatoes
steps.content: Cook garlic and tomatoes into a quick sauce.
steps.detail: In the same pan, add the rest of the oil and the sliced garlic and cook for 30 seconds until fragrant but not coloured. Tip in the cherry tomatoes and a pinch of salt, then cook for about 6 minutes, pressing with a spoon, until they burst and turn jammy and saucy. A splash of pasta water helps them melt down.
warnings: Keep the garlic moving — burnt garlic turns bitter and there's no fixing it.

steps.title: Toss and top
steps.content: Fold pasta through sauce, top with haloumi and lemon.
steps.detail: Drag the drained pasta into the tomato pan with a splash of pasta water and toss over low heat until glossy and coating. Squeeze in half the lemon and stir through most of the basil. Pile into bowls, top with the golden haloumi, the rest of the basil, the lemon zest and a crack of pepper.

title: Leek Potato Thyme Soup
description: Silky, gentle and comforting — sweet softened leeks and potato blitzed smooth, lifted with thyme and a swirl of cream.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Freeze up to 3 months, ideally without the cream — stir it in when you reheat, as cream can split when frozen.
make_ahead: Make the soup base up to two days ahead and stir the cream through when you reheat.
leftovers: Thin leftovers with a little stock, or keep it thick and spoon over baked potatoes.

substitutions.swap: milk for a lighter soup, or coconut milk to keep it dairy-free
swap a quarter for cauliflower for a silkier finish
a couple of brown onions, softened the same slow way
chicken stock for a rounder flavour

steps.title: Slice and wash the leeks
steps.content: Slice leeks, rinse out grit, and dice the potato.
steps.detail: Trim the leeks and use the white and pale-green parts. Halve them lengthwise, slice into half-moons, then rinse in a bowl of water and lift them out — grit hides between the layers and sinks to the bottom. Peel and dice the potato into roughly 2 cm chunks so it softens quickly and blends smooth.
tips: Don't tip the leeks through a sieve or the grit pours back over them — lift them out with your hands.

steps.title: Sweat the leeks
steps.content: Soften leeks in butter and oil without colouring.
steps.detail: Melt the butter with the olive oil in a large pot over low-medium heat. Add the leeks and a pinch of salt, then cook gently for about 10 minutes, stirring now and then, until soft and sweet but still pale. You want them silky, not browned — colour here makes the soup taste harsh instead of sweet. A lid tilted on top helps them steam down.

steps.title: Simmer with potato and thyme
steps.content: Add potato, thyme and stock; simmer until tender.
steps.detail: Add the diced potato, the thyme sprigs and the vegetable stock. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for about 20 minutes, until a knife slides through the potato with no resistance. If the stock doesn't quite cover everything, top up with a splash of water. Fish out the bare thyme stalks before you blend.

steps.title: Blend and finish with cream
steps.content: Blitz smooth, stir in cream, season and serve.
steps.detail: Blend the soup until completely smooth, then return it to low heat and stir through most of the cream. Season with salt and a good grind of pepper, tasting as you go. Ladle into bowls and finish with a swirl of the remaining cream, a drizzle of olive oil and a few reserved thyme leaves.
tips: Too thick? Loosen with a little stock or water. Too thin? Simmer a few minutes uncovered.
warnings: Blend hot soup in small batches with the lid held under a tea towel, or use a stick blender in the pot — hot liquid can erupt.

title: Chorizo Corn Fried Rice
description: Smoky chorizo renders its paprika-red oil, then day-old rice, sweet corn and fluffy egg all crisp up in the pan. A fast, punchy dinner.
storage: Cool quickly and refrigerate within an hour. As it's cooked rice, eat within 1 day and reheat once only, until piping hot right through.
make_ahead: Cook the rice the day before — cold, day-old rice makes the best fried rice.
leftovers: Reheat in a hot pan with a splash of water rather than the microwave, to bring back the fluff.

substitutions.swap: bacon, or sliced snags, browned the same way
tinned corn (drained) or frozen peas
any leftover cooked rice — basmati or long-grain
a splash of oyster sauce added with it for extra depth

steps.title: Cook and cool the rice
steps.content: Cook the rice ahead and cool it completely.
steps.detail: Fried rice needs cold, dry rice — fresh hot rice turns to mush. Rinse the jasmine rice, then cook it in a pan with a lid or a rice cooker until fluffy, about 12 minutes plus resting. Spread it on a tray to steam-dry and cool, then chill it; ideally cook it the day before. Day-old rice from the fridge is perfect.
tips: No time? Spread the cooked rice thin on a tray and freeze it for 20 minutes to cool fast.
warnings: Cool the cooked rice quickly and refrigerate within an hour — rice left sitting warm can grow bacteria that reheating won't destroy.

steps.title: Slice chorizo and spring onion
steps.content: Slice chorizo; keep spring onion whites and greens apart.
steps.detail: Slice the chorizo into thin half-moons. Slice the spring onions, keeping the white parts and green tops in separate piles — the whites go in early for savouriness, the greens on top at the end for freshness. Have your soy sauce and eggs within reach; fried rice moves fast once the pan is hot.

steps.title: Crisp the chorizo
steps.content: Fry chorizo until the edges crisp and oil runs red.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a wok or large frying pan over medium-high, then add the chorizo. Fry for 3-4 minutes until the edges crisp and it releases its smoky, paprika-red oil — that oil is the flavour base for the whole dish, so don't drain it. Add the spring onion whites for the last 30 seconds.

steps.title: Scramble the eggs
steps.content: Push chorizo aside and scramble the eggs.
steps.detail: Push the chorizo to one side of the pan and crack the eggs into the cleared space. Let them sit for a few seconds, then scramble with your spoon until just set but still glossy. Break them into bite-sized curds and fold through the chorizo.

steps.title: Toss the fried rice
steps.content: Add cold rice and corn, toss hot, season with soy.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to high. Add the cold rice and the frozen corn straight from the freezer, breaking up any clumps with your spoon. Toss and press the rice against the hot pan for 3-4 minutes so some grains toast and turn nutty. Pour the soy sauce around the edge of the pan, toss through, then finish with the spring onion greens and a crack of pepper.
tips: Spread the rice out and leave it 30 seconds between tosses to get those toasty golden bits.

title: Prawn Green Bean Stir-Fry
description: Blistered green beans and quick-seared prawns in a glossy garlic-sesame toss, ready in the time it takes the rice to steam.
storage: Best fresh. Cooked prawns keep 1 day in the fridge in an airtight container; store the rice separately. Reheat both until piping hot and don't reheat prawns twice.
leftovers: Toss cold leftovers through a noodle salad, or wrap in lettuce leaves for a light lunch.

substitutions.swap: firm white fish like barramundi in chunks, or calamari rings
snow peas, broccolini or asparagus
basmati, or serve over egg noodles
add a spoon of oyster sauce for a glossier, richer sauce

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Steam the jasmine rice while you prep.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice until the water runs clearer, then cook it in a pot with a lid or a rice cooker — about 12 minutes, then rest 10 with the lid on. Getting it going first means it steams away while you prep and stir-fry everything else.

steps.title: Prep the prawns and beans
steps.content: Pat prawns dry; trim beans; mince garlic and ginger.
steps.detail: Pat the prawns really dry with paper towel so they sear rather than steam. Top and tail the green beans and cut any long ones in half. Finely mince the garlic and ginger and keep them together in a little pile. A stir-fry cooks in minutes, so nothing can be halfway chopped once the pan is hot.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the knife after handling raw prawns, before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Blister the green beans
steps.content: Stir-fry beans until blistered and tender-crisp.
steps.detail: Heat half the olive oil in a wok or large pan over high heat until it shimmers. Add the green beans and stir-fry for 4-5 minutes until they're blistered with brown spots but still have a crisp bite. A splash of water and a quick lid for 30 seconds helps them cook through. Tip them onto a plate.

steps.title: Sear the prawns
steps.content: Sear prawns with garlic and ginger until just pink.
steps.detail: Add the rest of the oil to the hot pan and lay the prawns in a single layer. Sear for about a minute each side until they turn pink and opaque and curl into a loose C — a tight O means overcooked and rubbery. Add the garlic and ginger for the last 30 seconds, tossing so they're fragrant but not burnt.
tips: Prawns are done the moment they're opaque through the middle — pull them early rather than late.
warnings: Add the garlic and ginger only at the end; on high heat they scorch in seconds.

steps.title: Toss and serve
steps.content: Return beans, add soy and sesame, toss, serve on rice.
steps.detail: Return the beans to the pan, pour in the soy sauce and drizzle over the sesame oil. Toss everything together for 30 seconds so it's glossy and coated and the prawns are just cooked through. Taste, add a crack of pepper, and serve straight over the steamed rice.

title: Sesame Salmon Rice Bowl
description: Crisp-skinned salmon lacquered in a sticky soy-sesame glaze over fluffy rice, with cool cucumber and a scatter of spring onion.
storage: Cooked salmon keeps 1-2 days in the fridge; store the rice and cucumber separately. Eat cold, or reheat the salmon gently so it doesn't dry out.
make_ahead: Cook the rice and mix the glaze ahead; the salmon takes only minutes when you're ready.
leftovers: Flake cold salmon through the leftover rice with a little extra glaze, or into a green salad.

substitutions.swap: barramundi, trout or another firm fillet
basmati or brown rice
sliced avocado, or quick-pickled carrot
maple syrup

steps.title: Steam the jasmine rice
steps.content: Cook the rice and let it rest.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice, then cook it in a lidded pot or rice cooker — about 12 minutes plus 10 minutes resting off the heat with the lid on. Fluff it with a fork. Starting the rice first means it's ready and steaming when the salmon comes off the pan.

steps.title: Mix glaze and slice sides
steps.content: Whisk the glaze; slice cucumber and spring onion.
steps.detail: In a small bowl whisk together the soy sauce, honey and sesame oil into a glossy glaze. Slice the cucumber into thin half-moons and finely slice the spring onions, keeping a few green tops for the top. Pat the salmon fillets dry and season the skin with a little salt.

steps.title: Sear the salmon skin
steps.content: Crisp salmon skin-side down in a hot pan.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a frying pan over medium-high. Lay the salmon skin-side down and press gently for a few seconds so it stays flat. Leave it undisturbed for about 4 minutes until the skin is crisp and the flesh has turned pale most of the way up the side. Then flip it.
warnings: Lay the fillets in away from you and pat them dry first — wet fish in hot oil spits.

steps.title: Glaze the salmon
steps.content: Spoon over the glaze and let it turn sticky.
steps.detail: Cook the flesh side for another 2-3 minutes until the salmon flakes easily when nudged but is still juicy in the middle. Pour the glaze into the pan and let it bubble and thicken for a minute, spooning it over the fillets until they're shiny and lacquered. Farmed salmon is lovely a touch pink in the centre; cook a minute longer if you prefer it set right through.
tips: The glaze can catch and burn once it's sticky — keep it moving and pull the pan off the heat if it darkens fast.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Pile rice, salmon, cucumber and spring onion.
steps.detail: Spoon the rice into bowls and rest a salmon fillet on top. Tuck the cucumber alongside, spoon over any glaze left in the pan, and scatter with the spring onion. A final drizzle of sesame oil and a crack of pepper finishes it.

title: Kale Apple Parmesan Salad
description: Massaged kale turned tender and sweet, tossed with crisp apple matchsticks, toasted almonds and salty parmesan in a lemon-honey dressing.
storage: Sturdier than most salads — dressed, it holds a day in the fridge. Keep the almonds separate and scatter on just before serving so they stay crisp.
make_ahead: Massage the kale and make the dressing a day ahead; add apple, parmesan and almonds to serve.
leftovers: Leftovers make a good sandwich filler — pile into a wrap with a slice of cheese.

substitutions.swap: baby spinach or rocket (no massaging needed)
a firm pear, or thinly shaved fennel
walnuts, pecans or pine nuts
shaved pecorino or crumbled feta

steps.title: Strip and massage the kale
steps.content: Tear kale off the stems and massage until tender.
steps.detail: Strip the kale leaves off their tough stems and tear into bite-sized pieces into a big bowl. Add a squeeze of lemon, half the olive oil and a pinch of salt, then scrunch and massage the leaves with your hands for a minute or two. They'll darken, soften and shrink down — this turns raw, chewy kale sweet and tender enough to eat in a salad.
tips: The massaged kale only gets better sitting for 10 minutes while you do the rest.

steps.title: Toast the almonds
steps.content: Toast almonds in a dry pan until golden.
steps.detail: Put the almonds in a dry frying pan over medium heat and toss them for 3-4 minutes until they smell nutty and turn golden. Tip them straight onto a board so they stop cooking, then roughly chop. Toasting wakes up their flavour and crunch.
warnings: Nuts go from golden to burnt in seconds — keep them moving and don't walk away.

steps.title: Whisk the dressing
steps.content: Whisk lemon, honey and oil into a dressing.
steps.detail: In a small bowl whisk the juice of half the lemon with the honey, the rest of the olive oil and a grind of pepper. Taste it on a leaf — it should be sharp, sweet and savoury all at once. Loosen with a teaspoon of water if it's too tight.

steps.title: Slice the apple
steps.content: Cut apples into thin matchsticks.
steps.detail: Quarter and core the apples and slice them into thin matchsticks or wafer-thin slices — thin means they tangle through the leaves rather than sit as chunks. Toss them in a little of the dressing straight away to stop them browning while you finish.

steps.title: Toss and plate
steps.content: Toss kale, apple and dressing; top with parmesan.
steps.detail: Pour the dressing over the massaged kale and toss well to coat. Add the apple and most of the parmesan and almonds and toss again. Pile onto plates and finish with the remaining parmesan, the toasted almonds and a final crack of pepper.

title: Chicken Haloumi Burger Stack
description: A juicy pan-fried chicken fillet stacked with golden haloumi, crunchy lettuce and lemony mayo. A proper pub-style burger at home.
storage: Cook the chicken and haloumi up to 2 days ahead and keep in the fridge, but build the stacks fresh so they don't go soggy. Reheat the chicken until piping hot.
make_ahead: Cook the chicken and mix the mayo ahead; fry the haloumi and toast the bread fresh to serve.
leftovers: Chop leftover chicken and haloumi into a wrap or a green salad the next day.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh fillets for a juicier stack
a slice of melting cheddar instead
burger buns, Turkish bread or a soft roll
baby spinach or rocket

steps.title: Butterfly and season chicken
steps.content: Slice breasts into thin fillets and season.
steps.detail: Slice each chicken breast horizontally through the middle and open it into two thin, even fillets (or lay it between paper and bash it flat). Thin fillets cook fast and evenly right through. Rub with a little olive oil, salt, pepper and a squeeze of lemon.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the knife straight after handling raw chicken, before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Mix the lemon mayo
steps.content: Stir mayo with lemon zest and juice.
steps.detail: Spoon the mayo into a small bowl and stir through the lemon zest, a squeeze of juice and a grind of pepper. This zingy mayo is both the sauce and the glue that holds the stack together. Set it aside.

steps.title: Cook the chicken through
steps.content: Pan-fry the fillets until golden and cooked through.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a frying pan over medium-high. Cook the chicken fillets for about 4-5 minutes each side until deep golden and cooked right through — no pink, and 74°C at the thickest part if you have a thermometer. Rest them on a plate for a few minutes so they stay juicy.
warnings: Chicken must be cooked right through to 74°C — cut into the thickest piece to check the juices run clear.

steps.title: Fry the haloumi
steps.content: Fry haloumi slices until golden both sides.
steps.detail: Slice the haloumi into 1 cm planks and pat dry. In the same pan, fry for about 2 minutes each side until golden and crisp at the edges. It should be soft and squeaky inside — don't overcook it or it turns rubbery as it cools.

steps.title: Toast bread, shred lettuce
steps.content: Toast the bread and shred the lettuce.
steps.detail: Toast or griddle the bread until golden so it holds up under the fillings without going soggy. Shred or tear the lettuce into pieces that sit flat in the stack. Get everything lined up ready to build while the chicken is still warm.

steps.title: Stack the burgers
steps.content: Layer mayo, lettuce, chicken and haloumi.
steps.detail: Spread the lemon mayo generously over the toasted bread. Layer on the lettuce, then the chicken fillet, then the golden haloumi, and finish with more mayo. Press gently so it holds together, and serve straight away while everything's warm and the haloumi's still soft.

title: Chorizo Corn Nachos
description: Home-crisped tortilla chips loaded with smoky chorizo, charred corn and melted cheddar, finished with fresh tomato and cool sour cream.
storage: Best straight away while crisp. Leftover chorizo and corn keep 2 days in the fridge; crisp fresh chips and rebuild rather than reheating a soggy pile.
make_ahead: Crisp the chips and fry the chorizo-corn a few hours ahead; assemble and melt just before serving.
leftovers: The chorizo-and-corn mix is great folded through scrambled eggs or a quesadilla.

substitutions.swap: a bag of ready-made corn chips (skip the crisping step)
bacon or a spicy sausage, browned the same way
mozzarella, or a pre-grated Mexican-style blend
Greek yoghurt

steps.title: Crisp the tortilla chips
steps.content: Cut tortillas into wedges and bake until crisp.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Brush the tortillas lightly with olive oil and cut each into 6 wedges. Spread over two trays in a single layer, sprinkle with a little salt, and bake for about 10 minutes, turning halfway, until crisp and golden. They firm up more as they cool.
tips: Ready-made corn chips work too if you'd rather skip this step.

steps.title: Dice the tomato salsa
steps.content: Dice tomato into a quick fresh salsa.
steps.detail: Finely dice the tomatoes and tip into a bowl with a pinch of salt, a grind of pepper and a small drizzle of olive oil. Give it a stir and set aside — the salt draws the juices out into a fresh, spoonable salsa while everything else cooks.

steps.title: Fry the chorizo and corn
steps.content: Crisp chorizo, then char the corn in its oil.
steps.detail: Slice or crumble the chorizo and fry in a pan over medium-high for 3-4 minutes until crisp and the smoky red oil runs out. Add the frozen corn straight in and cook another 3-4 minutes until it's heated through and catching golden in spots. No need for extra oil — the chorizo gives plenty.

steps.title: Load and melt
steps.content: Pile chips, chorizo, corn and cheddar; bake to melt.
steps.detail: Pile the crisp chips onto one tray, keeping some height. Scatter over the chorizo and corn, then the grated cheddar, letting it fall into all the gaps. Back into the oven for 5-6 minutes until the cheddar is fully melted and bubbling.
warnings: The tray is very hot — use a dry cloth and set it down on a heatproof surface.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.content: Spoon over salsa and sour cream; serve at once.
steps.detail: Spoon the fresh tomato salsa over the hot nachos and add dollops of sour cream so it starts to melt into the cheese. Finish with a crack of pepper and take the whole tray to the table to share while the cheese is still stretchy.

title: Chicken Feta Sausage Rolls
description: Golden puff pastry wrapped around a juicy chicken, feta and spinach filling. The party tray always disappears first.
storage: Cooked rolls keep 3 days in the fridge in an airtight container. Reheat in a 180°C oven for 8-10 minutes to crisp the pastry — the microwave makes it soft.
make_ahead: Assemble the rolls and freeze them uncooked; bake from frozen, adding about 10 minutes.
leftovers: Great cold in a lunchbox, or reheated on a party platter with tomato sauce for dipping.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh for a juicier filling, or use pork mince
ricotta for a milder, creamier filling
frozen spinach, thawed and squeezed dry, or finely chopped kale
panko, or grated day-old bread

steps.title: Heat oven, thaw pastry
steps.content: Preheat the oven and thaw the puff pastry.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan and line a tray with baking paper. Take the puff pastry out of the freezer to thaw until pliable but still cold, about 10 minutes. Cold pastry that's just bendable is easiest to roll without tearing or going greasy.

steps.title: Mix the chicken filling
steps.content: Blitz chicken and combine with feta and spinach.
steps.detail: Blitz the chicken breast in a food processor to a coarse mince (or chop it very finely). Finely chop the spinach and tip into a bowl with the chicken, crumbled feta, breadcrumbs, one egg, a pinch of salt and plenty of pepper. Mix with your hands until it just comes together — the breadcrumbs and egg keep it juicy and hold it as one log.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the processor well after the raw chicken, before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Roll and seal the logs
steps.content: Spoon filling onto pastry and roll into logs.
steps.detail: Cut each pastry sheet in half. Spoon a thick line of filling along one edge of each piece. Beat the second egg and brush the far edge, then roll the pastry over the filling into a log and press the seam to seal. Sit them seam-side down so they don't spring open.

steps.title: Egg-wash and slice
steps.content: Brush with egg and cut into pieces.
steps.detail: Brush the tops all over with the beaten egg for a glossy, golden finish. Cut each log into 3 or 4 pieces and space them out on the lined tray so the sides can crisp. A couple of small slashes in each top lets steam escape.

steps.title: Bake until golden
steps.content: Bake until puffed, deep golden and cooked through.
steps.detail: Bake for 25-30 minutes until the pastry is puffed and deep golden and the filling is cooked right through. Because it's chicken, make sure the centre reaches 74°C or is piping hot with no pink. Rest for 5 minutes — the filling is molten straight out of the oven.
warnings: Chicken must be cooked right through — check the thickest roll reaches 74°C or is steaming hot inside.

title: Creamy Chocolate Ramekins
description: Dark, silky baked chocolate custards with a just-set wobble. Make them the day before, chill, and dessert is sorted.
storage: Cover and keep chilled; best within 3 days. They contain egg and cream, so keep them refrigerated and don't leave them out at room temperature.
make_ahead: Best made a day ahead — bake and chill overnight, ready to serve straight from the fridge.

substitutions.swap: milk chocolate for a sweeter, milder custard (cut the sugar back)
half cream, half milk for a lighter set
a shot of strong coffee for a mocha edge
6 egg yolks instead of 4 whole eggs for an even silkier, richer custard

steps.title: Warm cream, heat oven
steps.content: Heat the oven and warm the cream until steaming.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 150°C fan and sit four ramekins in a deep roasting tray. Put the kettle on for the water bath later. Warm the cream in a saucepan over medium heat until it steams and just trembles at the edges — you want it hot, not boiling.

steps.title: Melt in the chocolate
steps.content: Stir chocolate and vanilla into the hot cream.
steps.detail: Take the cream off the heat and drop in the chopped dark chocolate and the vanilla. Leave it a minute to soften, then stir until smooth and glossy with no lumps. If the odd piece won't melt, a few seconds back over low heat brings it together into a silky ganache.

steps.title: Whisk the eggs and sugar
steps.content: Whisk the eggs and sugar until pale.
steps.detail: In a mixing bowl, whisk the eggs and sugar together for a minute until pale and a little thickened. This is the base that sets the custards, so make sure the sugar is well combined and there are no stringy bits of white left.

steps.title: Temper and strain
steps.content: Pour warm chocolate into the eggs, then strain.
steps.detail: Whisking the eggs constantly, pour in the warm chocolate cream in a slow, thin stream — going gently warms the eggs through without scrambling them. Pour the smooth custard through a sieve into the ramekins, dividing it evenly. The sieve catches any stray cooked bits and gives you that glassy, silky top.
tips: If you rush the pour and see specks of cooked egg, straining still saves it — that's what the sieve is for.

steps.title: Bake in a water bath
steps.content: Bake in a water bath, then cool and chill.
steps.detail: Slide the tray onto the oven rack, then pour hot water from the kettle into the tray until it comes halfway up the ramekins — this gentle steam bath keeps the custards silky instead of cracked. Bake for about 33 minutes until set at the edges with a slight wobble in the middle. Cool, then chill at least 3 hours or overnight; they firm to a dense, truffle-like cream.
tips: They set further as they chill, so pull them while the centres still jiggle like soft jelly.
warnings: Pour the hot water in once the tray is already on the oven rack — carrying a sloshing water bath is how you get scalded.

title: Prawn Chilli Tomato Spaghetti
description: Garlicky prawns and cherry tomatoes that burst into a light sauce, tangled through spaghetti with a hit of chilli and a squeeze of lemon.
storage: Best eaten straight away while the prawns are tender. Leftovers keep 1 day in the fridge; reheat gently just until warm so the prawns don't turn rubbery.
leftovers: Cold, it makes a quick lunch — loosen with a splash of olive oil and an extra squeeze of lemon.

substitutions.swap: firm white fish like barramundi, cut into chunks
any long pasta — linguine or fettuccine both work
a big pinch of dried chilli flakes if you have no fresh

steps.title: Prep the prawns and aromatics
steps.content: Peel prawns, slice chilli and garlic, halve tomatoes, chop parsley.
steps.detail: If your prawns are raw and shell-on, peel them and pull the dark vein from along the back with the tip of a knife, then pat dry. Thinly slice the chilli (scrape the seeds out for less heat) and finely slice the garlic. Halve the cherry tomatoes and roughly chop the parsley. Zest half the lemon and cut the rest into wedges. Keep the garlic and chilli in their own pile — they hit the pan first and burn in seconds.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the knife after handling raw prawns, before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Boil the spaghetti
steps.content: Cook spaghetti in well-salted water until al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it well — it should taste like the sea. Add the spaghetti, stir so it doesn't clump, and cook to one minute short of the packet time. Scoop out a mugful of the starchy cooking water before you drain — you'll need it to bring the sauce together.
tips: Time it so the pasta is ready just as the prawns finish — the sauce waits for no one.

steps.title: Sizzle garlic and chilli
steps.content: Gently fry garlic and chilli in olive oil until fragrant.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium heat. Add the garlic and chilli and cook for about a minute, stirring, until the garlic is pale gold and the kitchen smells sweet and nutty. Don't let the garlic brown or it turns bitter — pull the pan off the heat for a moment if it's racing ahead.
warnings: Garlic scorches fast — the second it smells sharp or acrid it has gone bitter, so keep it moving and the heat moderate.

steps.title: Burst the tomatoes
steps.content: Add cherry tomatoes and cook until slumped and saucy.
steps.detail: Tip in the cherry tomatoes cut-side down with a pinch of salt. Cook for about 5 minutes, pressing them with the back of a spoon as they soften, until they collapse into a loose, jammy sauce and release their juice. A splash of the pasta water helps them along.

steps.title: Cook the prawns
steps.content: Add prawns and cook just until pink and opaque.
steps.detail: Slide the prawns into the tomatoes and cook for 2 to 3 minutes, turning once, until they curl and turn from grey to pink all the way through. They cook fast — the moment the last translucent patch turns opaque, they're done. Any longer and they tighten into rubber.
warnings: Prawns are cooked when evenly pink and firm with no grey, glassy patches — but pull them the instant they get there.

steps.title: Toss it all together
steps.content: Add drained pasta, lemon and parsley; loosen with pasta water.
steps.detail: Drag the drained spaghetti straight into the pan with tongs and toss for a minute so every strand slicks with sauce. Add the lemon zest, a good squeeze of juice, the parsley and a crack of pepper. If it looks tight, splash in pasta water a little at a time until it's glossy and coats the pasta. Serve with lemon wedges.
tips: A final drizzle of your best olive oil off the heat lifts the whole bowl.

title: Lamb Mince Ragu Pasta
description: Lamb mince slow-cooked with rosemary and tomato into a rich, savoury ragu, spooned over pasta with a shower of parmesan.
equipment: large heavy pot or deep frying pan
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge, or freeze the sauce without the pasta for up to 3 months. Reheat until piping hot.
make_ahead: The ragu only improves overnight — make it a day or two ahead and reheat gently while the pasta boils.
leftovers: Spoon leftover ragu over soft polenta, or bake it under mashed potato for a quick shepherd's-style pie.

substitutions.swap: beef mince, or half lamb and half beef for a milder ragu
pappardelle or any short pasta that catches the sauce
a few sprigs of thyme, or a big pinch of dried oregano
any hard grating cheese

steps.title: Chop the aromatics
steps.content: Dice the onion, mince the garlic, strip the rosemary.
steps.detail: Peel and finely dice the brown onion into small, even pieces. Mince or grate the garlic. Run your fingers down each rosemary sprig to strip the needles, then chop them finely so there are no woody bits. Getting all this ready now means you can move without scrambling once the pan is hot.

steps.title: Brown the lamb
steps.content: Sear the lamb mince in olive oil until deeply coloured.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a heavy pot over medium-high until it shimmers. Add the lamb mince in big clumps and leave it undisturbed for 2 minutes to catch and brown before you break it up. Keep cooking and stirring until there's no pink left and the base of the pan is coated in brown flavour. Lamb is fatty — if there's a big pool of grease, spoon off all but a tablespoon.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and utensils after handling raw mince, and don't return cooked meat to the raw plate.

steps.title: Soften onion and rosemary
steps.content: Add onion, garlic and rosemary; cook until soft and sweet.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to medium and tip in the onion, garlic and rosemary. Cook for about 5 minutes, stirring and scraping up the browned bits, until the onion is soft and translucent. Stir in the tomato paste and cook another minute — it should darken slightly and smell rich, which takes away its raw tinny edge.

steps.title: Simmer the ragu
steps.content: Add tomatoes and simmer low until thick and glossy.
steps.detail: Crush the tinned tomatoes in with your hand or spoon, then swirl a splash of water in the empty tin and add that too. Season with salt and pepper. Bring to a gentle simmer, then drop the heat right down and cook uncovered for 30 minutes, stirring now and then, until it's thick, glossy and no longer watery.
tips: If it thickens too far before the pasta is ready, loosen it with a little pasta water or stock.

steps.title: Boil the pasta
steps.content: Cook the pasta in well-salted water until al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it generously. Cook the pasta to one minute short of the packet time so it still has bite. Save a mug of the cooking water, then drain — that starchy water is what makes the sauce cling.

steps.title: Toss and serve
steps.content: Fold pasta through the ragu with parmesan and pasta water.
steps.detail: Tip the drained pasta into the ragu and toss over low heat for a minute, loosening with a splash of pasta water until the sauce coats every piece. Stir through half the parmesan. Serve in warm bowls with the rest of the parmesan grated over and a final crack of pepper.
tips: Grate the parmesan finely so it melts in rather than clumping on top.

title: Tomato Basil White Bean Soup
description: A cosy pantry soup — tinned tomatoes simmered with garlic and basil, white beans for body, finished with a swirl of olive oil and parmesan.
storage: Keeps 4 days in the fridge in an airtight container and freezes well for up to 3 months. Reheat gently, adding a splash of water if it has thickened.
make_ahead: Like most tomato soups it deepens overnight — happily made a day ahead.
leftovers: Reduce leftovers down a little and they become a rustic sauce for pasta or a base for baked eggs.

substitutions.swap: tinned chickpeas or cannellini — any white bean works
a vegetarian-friendly hard cheese, or leave it out to keep the soup vegan
a spoon of pesto stirred in at the end

steps.title: Soften the onion
steps.content: Gently cook onion and garlic in olive oil until soft.
steps.detail: Finely dice the onion and mince the garlic. Warm the olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat, add the onion with a pinch of salt and cook for 5 to 6 minutes until soft and translucent. Stir in the garlic for the last minute — just until fragrant, not browned.

steps.title: Add tomatoes and stock
steps.content: Pour in tinned tomatoes and vegetable stock; simmer.
steps.detail: Tip in the tinned tomatoes, crushing any whole ones against the side of the pan, then pour in the vegetable stock. Drop in a few basil stalks (save the leaves) for a savoury backbone. Bring to a simmer and let it bubble gently for 15 minutes so the flavours meld and the raw tomato edge softens.

steps.title: Drop in the beans
steps.content: Add drained white beans and warm through.
steps.detail: Drain and rinse the beans and stir them in. Simmer for another 5 minutes until heated through and starting to soften — some will break down a little and thicken the soup, which is exactly what you want.

steps.title: Blend to your liking
steps.content: Blitz part of the soup for a silky-but-rustic texture.
steps.detail: For a soup that's smooth but still has body, blitz about half of it with a stick blender and leave the rest chunky — or blend it all for velvety, or none at all for rustic. Taste and adjust with salt and pepper. It should be bright and savoury, not flat.
tips: Keep the blender head fully submerged before you switch it on so hot soup doesn't spit up at you.
warnings: Hot soup can splutter — tilt the pan away from you and blend in short bursts.

steps.title: Finish with basil
steps.content: Tear in basil, top with parmesan and a drizzle of oil.
steps.detail: Turn off the heat and tear in most of the basil leaves so they wilt without cooking to sludge. Ladle into bowls and finish each with grated parmesan, a drizzle of your best olive oil, the last few basil leaves and a crack of pepper.

title: Kimchi Tofu Fried Rice
description: Cold rice fried with tangy kimchi and golden tofu, seasoned with sesame and soy, and topped with a crisp-edged fried egg.
storage: Best fresh, but keeps 1 day in the fridge. Reheat in a hot pan until piping hot; cooked rice shouldn't be kept longer than a day.
make_ahead: Cook the rice the day before — cold, dry rice from the fridge fries into separate grains instead of clumping.

substitutions.swap: leave it out and add extra egg; firm tofu holds together best
any leftover cooked rice — basmati or medium-grain both work
a splash of tamari to keep it gluten-free

steps.title: Prep tofu, kimchi and rice
steps.content: Cube tofu, chop kimchi, slice spring onions, ready the cold rice.
steps.detail: Drain the tofu and press it in a clean tea towel to squeeze out water, then cut into 2 cm cubes. Roughly chop the kimchi, saving any juice in the jar. Slice the spring onions, keeping the white parts and green tops in separate piles. Break up your cold rice with your fingers so there are no clumps.
tips: Squeezing the tofu dry is the difference between golden and soggy — the drier it is, the better it crisps.

steps.title: Crisp the tofu
steps.content: Fry tofu cubes in olive oil until golden on all sides.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a wok over medium-high. Add the tofu and leave it to sit for a minute or two before turning, so each side gets a chance to catch and crisp. Fry for about 6 minutes, turning, until golden and firm, then push it to one side of the pan.

steps.title: Fry the kimchi
steps.content: Cook kimchi with spring onion whites until it smells savoury.
steps.detail: Add the spring onion whites and the chopped kimchi to the pan and stir-fry for 2 minutes, until the kimchi darkens slightly and its sharp smell turns deep and savoury. This little step cooks off the raw edge and builds the base flavour of the whole dish.

steps.title: Fry the rice
steps.content: Add cold rice and toss over high heat until steaming.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to high and add the cold rice, breaking up any last clumps as you toss it with the kimchi and tofu. Fry for 3 to 4 minutes until the grains are hot and starting to catch on the pan. Pour the soy sauce and any saved kimchi juice around the edge, add a drizzle of sesame oil, and toss to coat. Taste before adding salt — the kimchi and soy are already salty.

steps.title: Fry the eggs
steps.content: Fry eggs in a separate pan with crisp, lacy edges.
steps.detail: Heat a little olive oil in a small frying pan over medium-high and crack in the eggs. Fry until the whites are set and the edges are golden and lacy but the yolks are still runny, about 3 minutes. The runny yolk is the sauce that pulls the whole bowl together.
tips: If you prefer the yolk fully set, cook a little longer — go firm if serving anyone who avoids runny egg.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Top rice with a fried egg, spring onion greens and sesame.
steps.detail: Spoon the fried rice into bowls and slide a fried egg on top of each. Scatter over the green spring onion tops, a crack of pepper and a final few drops of sesame oil. Break the yolk and fold it through as you eat.

title: Mushroom Cabbage Sesame Stir-Fry
description: A fast, frugal stir-fry — mushrooms seared until meaty, cabbage wilted just-tender, tossed in soy, ginger and nutty sesame over rice.
storage: Keeps 2 days in the fridge in an airtight container. Reheat in a hot pan rather than the microwave so the cabbage keeps some bite; store the rice separately and use within a day.
leftovers: Fold cold leftovers through a fresh batch of rice with an egg for a quick fried rice.
