substitutions.swap: any mushroom — a mix of button and meatier Swiss brown is lovely
wombok, bok choy or even shredded Brussels sprouts
tamari for a gluten-free version

steps.title: Start the rice
steps.content: Rinse and simmer jasmine rice, then leave it to steam.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then put it in a saucepan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, drop to the lowest heat, cover and cook for 12 minutes. Turn off the heat and leave it, lid on, to steam while you stir-fry — don't peek.
tips: Getting the rice going first means it can rest undisturbed while everything else comes together.

steps.title: Prep and mix the sauce
steps.content: Slice mushrooms and cabbage; grate ginger and garlic.
steps.detail: Slice the mushrooms thickly so they hold up to searing, and shred the cabbage into ribbons about a finger wide. Grate the ginger and garlic. Stir the soy sauce together with a splash of water in a small cup so it's ready to hit the hot pan without stalling the cook.

steps.title: Sear the mushrooms
steps.content: Fry mushrooms in a hot pan until browned, not stewed.
steps.detail: Get the wok properly hot with the olive oil, then add the mushrooms in a single layer and leave them alone for a minute before tossing. Crowding steams them, so give them room and let them brown for about 5 minutes until golden and shrunken — that colour is where the meaty flavour comes from.

steps.title: Add cabbage and aromatics
steps.content: Toss in cabbage, ginger and garlic until just wilted.
steps.detail: Add the cabbage, ginger and garlic and stir-fry over high heat for 2 to 3 minutes. You want the cabbage wilted but still with a bit of crunch, and the garlic and ginger fragrant, not burnt. Keep everything moving so nothing catches.
warnings: Grated garlic and ginger scorch quickly on high heat — keep tossing so they don't turn bitter.

steps.title: Sauce and serve
steps.content: Pour in soy, finish with sesame oil, serve over rice.
steps.detail: Pour the soy mixture around the edge of the pan so it sizzles, and toss for 30 seconds to coat everything in a glossy glaze. Off the heat, add a good drizzle of sesame oil and a crack of pepper. Fluff the rice and pile the stir-fry on top.
tips: Add the sesame oil at the very end — its nutty aroma cooks off if it goes in over high heat.

title: Gochujang Chicken Rice Bowl
description: Sticky gochujang-glazed chicken thigh over rice, with a crunchy lime-dressed cabbage and carrot slaw to cool the heat.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge with the parts stored separately. Reheat the chicken until piping hot (74°C); dress the slaw fresh so it stays crunchy; use the rice within a day.
make_ahead: Mix the gochujang glaze and cut the chicken ahead — it then cooks in one quick session.
leftovers: Shred cold leftover chicken into a wrap with the slaw and a smear of extra gochujang.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast, but watch it closely as it dries out faster
brown sugar or maple syrup for the sticky glaze
wombok or a bag of coleslaw mix

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Simmer jasmine rice and leave it to steam, lid on.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then simmer with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt: boil, drop to low, cover and cook 12 minutes. Take it off the heat and leave it covered to steam while you cook the chicken.

steps.title: Mix glaze and slaw
steps.content: Stir the gochujang glaze; toss cabbage and carrot with lime.
steps.detail: In a small bowl mix the gochujang, honey, grated garlic and a splash of water into a loose, spoonable glaze. Finely shred the cabbage and coarsely grate the carrot into another bowl, then toss with the juice of half the lime and a pinch of salt so it softens into a quick slaw. Cut the chicken thigh into bite-sized pieces.
warnings: Keep the raw chicken and its board well away from the slaw, and wash your hands and the knife before touching anything else.

steps.title: Sear the chicken
steps.content: Brown the chicken pieces in a hot pan until cooked through.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high. Add the chicken in a single layer and cook for about 6 minutes, turning, until golden on the outside and cooked through — no pink in the middle and 74°C at the thickest piece if you have a thermometer.
warnings: Chicken is safe at 74°C in the centre with clear juices — cut a thick piece to check if you're unsure.

steps.title: Glaze until sticky
steps.content: Add the gochujang glaze and toss until it clings and shines.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to medium, pour the gochujang glaze over the chicken and toss for 2 to 3 minutes until it thickens, darkens and coats every piece in a sticky, glossy sheen. Watch closely — the honey means it can catch and burn if the heat is too high, so keep it moving and pull it off the moment it's glazed.
warnings: Sugary glazes scorch fast — lower the heat and keep tossing so it caramelises rather than burns.

steps.title: Assemble the rice bowls
steps.content: Pile rice, sticky chicken and slaw; finish with lime.
steps.detail: Fluff the rice and divide it between bowls. Top with the sticky chicken and a generous heap of the lime slaw alongside. Add a crack of pepper and a wedge of the remaining lime to squeeze over at the table.

title: Pineapple Prawn Noodle Salad
description: A bright, no-fuss noodle salad — cool rice noodles, sweet pineapple and juicy prawns tossed in a lime and fish-sauce dressing with plenty of fresh mint.
storage: A dressed salad won't keep — eat it the day it's made. To get ahead, store the cooked prawns and soaked noodles separately (both up to a day) and dress just before serving.
make_ahead: Cook the prawns and soak the noodles earlier in the day, keep them chilled and separate, then toss with the dressing right before eating.

substitutions.swap: cooked and peeled prawns to skip the pan, or shredded poached chicken
rice vermicelli or thin egg noodles
soy sauce for a lighter, vegetarian-friendly dressing
coriander, or a mix of both

steps.title: Soak the rice noodles
steps.content: Cover rice noodles in just-boiled water until tender, then cool.
steps.detail: Put the rice noodles in a large heatproof bowl and cover with just-boiled water. Leave for 5 to 8 minutes (check the packet) until soft and pliable but not mushy, then drain and rinse under cold water to stop them cooking and wash off the starch. Drain well and leave in the colander.
tips: Rinsing cold keeps the noodles loose and springy instead of gluing into a single clump.

steps.title: Prep pineapple and salad
steps.content: Dice pineapple, ribbon cucumber, slice chilli, pick mint.
steps.detail: Cut the pineapple into bite-sized chunks, saving any juice for the dressing. Run a peeler down the cucumber to make ribbons, or slice it into half-moons. Thinly slice the chilli (seeds out for less heat) and pick the mint leaves off their stalks. Zest and juice the lime.

steps.title: Sear the prawns
steps.content: Cook the prawns in a hot pan until pink, then cool.
steps.detail: If the prawns are raw, peel and devein them. Heat the olive oil in a frying pan over high heat, add the prawns with a pinch of salt and cook for 2 to 3 minutes, turning once, until pink and opaque all the way through. Tip onto a plate to cool for a few minutes so they don't wilt the salad.
warnings: Wash hands, board and knife after handling raw prawns; cook until evenly pink with no grey, glassy patches.

steps.title: Whisk the dressing
steps.content: Mix lime juice, fish sauce and chilli into a punchy dressing.
steps.detail: In the bottom of your big mixing bowl, whisk the lime juice and zest with the fish sauce, a little of the saved pineapple juice and half the sliced chilli. Taste — it should be a lively balance of salty, sour and sweet. Add more pineapple juice if it's too sharp, more lime if it tastes flat.

steps.title: Dress and serve
steps.content: Combine noodles, prawns, pineapple and herbs; scatter mint.
steps.detail: Add the drained noodles, pineapple, cucumber and cooled prawns to the dressing and toss with your hands so everything is glossed and mingled. Pile onto plates, scatter over the mint and the rest of the chilli, and eat straight away while it's cool and fresh.
tips: Toss just before serving — the noodles drink up the dressing if they sit, and the salad loses its zing.

title: Mushroom Feta Burger Stack
description: A meat-free stack that eats like the real thing — golden garlicky mushrooms, salty feta softening in the heat, peppery rocket and sharp red onion on toasted bread with a smear of dijon.
storage: Best assembled and eaten straight away. The cooked mushrooms keep 2 days in the fridge and reheat well; keep the bread, rocket and onion separate so nothing goes soggy.
leftovers: Leftover garlicky mushrooms are great folded through pasta or piled on toast with an egg.

substitutions.swap: soft goat's cheese if you can get it, or a mild soft cheese
burger buns or thick sourdough — anything sturdy enough to hold the stack
baby spinach or crisp lettuce
wholegrain mustard or a swipe of mayo

steps.title: Slice mushrooms and onion
steps.content: Slice mushrooms, ring the onion, crumble feta, mince garlic.
steps.detail: Slice the mushrooms about 5 mm thick. Cut the red onion into thin rings and separate them. Crumble the feta and mince the garlic. Wash and spin the rocket dry. Having it all ready means you can build the stacks the moment the mushrooms are done and everything's still warm.

steps.title: Toast the bread
steps.content: Toast or griddle the bread until crisp and golden.
steps.detail: Toast the bread until crisp and golden — under a grill, in a toaster, or in a dry frying pan. You want it firm enough to hold a juicy pile of mushrooms without collapsing. Smear one side of each piece with dijon while it's still warm.

steps.title: Sear the mushrooms
steps.content: Fry mushrooms in a hot pan until deep golden and shrunken.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high and add the mushrooms with a pinch of salt. They'll release water first — keep cooking and stirring for about 6 minutes until that evaporates and they turn deep golden and glossy. Stir in the garlic for the last minute until fragrant.
tips: Don't crowd the pan or salt too early — give the water a chance to cook off so the mushrooms brown instead of stew.

steps.title: Melt in the feta
steps.content: Scatter feta over the hot mushrooms so it softens.
steps.detail: Turn off the heat, scatter the crumbled feta over the mushrooms and let the residual warmth soften it for a minute. It won't melt like cheddar but it'll turn creamy and cling to the mushrooms, seasoning them as it goes.

steps.title: Build the stack
steps.content: Pile mushrooms, onion and rocket onto the toast.
steps.detail: Spoon the feta mushrooms onto the dijon toast, then top with a few rings of red onion, a handful of rocket and a crack of pepper. Finish with a thread of olive oil and, if you like, lean a second piece of toast on top. Eat while warm.

title: Scallop Lemon Butter Plate
description: Sweet scallops seared until caramelised, bathed in a lemon-garlic butter, with crisp green beans and toasted bread to mop the plate.
storage: Cooked scallops are best eaten straight away and keep only 1 day in the fridge. Reheat very gently, if at all — they toughen the moment they're overcooked.

substitutions.swap: peeled prawns, seared the same way for 2 to 3 minutes
asparagus or broccolini, blanched until just tender
any crusty bread for mopping the butter

steps.title: Dry the scallops
steps.content: Pat scallops bone-dry; mince garlic, chop parsley, cut lemon.
steps.detail: Pat the scallops completely dry on both sides with paper towel — a wet scallop steams instead of searing. If there's a small tough side-muscle, peel it off. Mince the garlic, chop the parsley, and cut the lemon into a half for juicing and wedges for serving. Top and tail the green beans.
tips: Bone-dry scallops and a properly hot pan are the whole secret to a golden crust.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the knife after handling the raw scallops, before touching anything else.

steps.title: Blanch the green beans
steps.content: Boil green beans briefly until bright and just tender.
steps.detail: Bring a small saucepan of salted water to the boil and add the green beans. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes until they turn vivid green and are tender with a little snap. Drain and keep warm — you can toss them in the scallop butter at the end.

steps.title: Toast the bread
steps.content: Toast the bread for mopping up the butter.
steps.detail: Toast the bread until crisp and golden — in a toaster, under the grill, or brushed with a little olive oil and crisped in a dry pan. Set it aside; it's there to soak up every drop of the lemon butter.

steps.title: Sear the scallops
steps.content: Sear scallops in a screaming-hot pan, 1 to 2 min a side.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a heavy pan until it just starts to smoke. Season the scallops with salt and lay them in one at a time, not touching, and don't move them. After about 90 seconds they'll have a deep golden crust — flip and give the other side about a minute. They should feel just firm with a hint of give; overcooked, they go bouncy and dry.
warnings: The pan is very hot and oil can spit — lay the scallops in away from you and stand back.

steps.title: Make the lemon butter
steps.content: Add butter, garlic and lemon; baste the scallops.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to medium and add the butter, garlic and a good squeeze of lemon juice to the pan. As the butter foams, tilt the pan and spoon it over the scallops for 30 seconds until the garlic is fragrant and the butter smells nutty. Toss the parsley through and take it off the heat before the garlic colours.
warnings: Garlic burns in seconds in hot butter — keep the heat moderate and the butter moving.

steps.title: Plate up
steps.content: Plate scallops and beans, spoon over butter, add bread.
steps.detail: Divide the green beans between plates, sit the scallops on top and spoon over the lemon-garlic butter and pan juices. Add a crack of pepper, a lemon wedge and a slice of toasted bread to mop up. Serve straight away while everything's hot.

title: Tomato Goat-ish Feta Tart
description: A savoury tart with a crisp shortcrust base and a soft egg-and-cream custard — feta standing in beautifully for tangy goat's cheese, with roast-sweet tomatoes and basil torn over the top.
equipment: loose-based tart tin (about 22 cm) or pie dish
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge. Eat cold or warm through in a 160°C oven; it's a set custard, so don't freeze it.
make_ahead: Best baked the day you serve it, but you can blind-bake the pastry case a day ahead and keep it in an airtight tin.
leftovers: A cold slice is excellent in a lunchbox with a handful of leaves.

substitutions.swap: soft goat's cheese if you have it, or ricotta for something milder
a blind-baked store-bought tart case to save time
milk for a lighter custard, though it sets a touch softer
chives or a little thyme

steps.title: Blind-bake the base
steps.content: Line the tin with pastry and bake blind until pale gold.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Line the tart tin with the shortcrust, pressing it into the corners, and trim the edge. Prick the base with a fork, lay in baking paper and fill with baking weights or dried beans. Bake for 15 minutes, lift out the paper and weights, and bake another 5 minutes until the base is dry and pale gold.
tips: Blind-baking first stops the dreaded soggy bottom once the wet custard goes in.

steps.title: Salt the tomatoes
steps.content: Slice tomatoes and salt them to draw out water.
steps.detail: While the pastry bakes, slice the tomatoes into rounds about 5 mm thick, lay them on paper towel, sprinkle with a little salt and leave them. This pulls out excess water so it doesn't leak into the custard and make the tart weep. Pat them dry before they go in.

steps.title: Whisk the custard
steps.content: Beat eggs and cream with salt and pepper.
steps.detail: Crack the eggs into a bowl and whisk with the cream, a pinch of salt and a good crack of pepper until smooth and evenly combined. Don't overbeat it into a froth — you want a silky custard, not an omelette. This is what sets around the tomatoes and feta.

steps.title: Fill the tart
steps.content: Scatter feta, arrange tomatoes, pour in the custard.
steps.detail: Turn the oven down to 170°C fan. Crumble the feta over the warm pastry base and arrange the drained tomato slices on top. Set the tart tin on the oven rack pulled halfway out and pour the custard in to just below the rim — filling it at the oven saves you carrying a brimming tart across the kitchen.
tips: Feta is doing the job of goat's cheese here — salty and tangy, it seasons the custard from within, so go easy on extra salt.

steps.title: Bake until just set
steps.content: Bake until the custard is set with a slight wobble in the centre.
steps.detail: Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the custard is puffed at the edges and just set with a slight wobble in the very middle — it firms up as it cools. If the top isn't colouring, a final couple of minutes higher in the oven gives it a golden freckle.
warnings: Resist opening the oven in the first 20 minutes — the draught can make a custard sink or split.

steps.title: Cool and finish with basil
steps.content: Rest the tart, then tear over basil and drizzle oil.
steps.detail: Let the tart settle in the tin for at least 15 minutes — slicing it hot will drag the custard out. Tear the basil over the top and finish with a thread of olive oil and a last crack of pepper. Serve just warm or at room temperature.

title: Vanilla Coffee Ramekins
description: Silky baked coffee custards with a whisper of vanilla — set gently in a water bath and chilled until spoonably smooth, like a coffee pot de crème.
equipment: roasting tin or deep baking dish
storage: Cover and keep in the fridge up to 3 days. Serve them cold, straight from the fridge.
make_ahead: A perfect make-ahead dessert — bake and chill a day before, then they're ready to serve with no fuss.

substitutions.swap: 2 teaspoons of instant coffee dissolved in a little hot water, or chai for a spiced version
the seeds of half a vanilla pod
all cream for an even richer, denser custard

steps.title: Warm the coffee cream
steps.content: Heat cream, milk and coffee until steaming, not boiling.
steps.detail: Put the cream, milk and strong coffee in a small saucepan and warm over medium heat until it steams and just trembles at the edges — don't let it boil. Take it off the heat. Warming the dairy first infuses it with coffee and helps the custard cook evenly.
warnings: Keep it below a boil; boiling dairy can catch and split, and boiling-hot liquid will scramble the eggs in the next step.

steps.title: Whisk eggs and sugar
steps.content: Beat the eggs, sugar and vanilla until smooth.
steps.detail: In a bowl, whisk the eggs with the sugar and vanilla until the sugar dissolves and the mix is pale and smooth. Whisk gently rather than whipping in air — too many bubbles leave a foamy skin on the baked custards.

steps.title: Temper and strain
steps.content: Slowly whisk the warm cream into the eggs, then strain.
steps.detail: Pour the warm coffee cream into the eggs in a slow, steady stream, whisking the whole time so the eggs warm up gradually instead of scrambling. Pour the mixture through a fine sieve into a jug — this catches any stray cooked egg and any coffee grounds, leaving a silky custard.
tips: Sit the bowl on a folded damp tea towel so it can't skid while you pour with one hand and whisk with the other.

steps.title: Bake in a water bath
steps.content: Pour into ramekins and bake in a tray of hot water until set.
steps.detail: Sit the ramekins in a deep roasting tin and divide the custard between them. Slide the tin into a 150°C fan oven, then carefully pour hot water into the tin to come halfway up the ramekins — this gentle water bath stops the custards curdling. Bake for about 35 minutes until set around the edge but still with a slight wobble in the centre.
tips: Pour the hot water in once the tray is already on the oven rack, so you're not carrying a sloshing tin across the kitchen.
warnings: The water bath is scalding — use a dry tea towel and pour carefully to avoid splashing hot water on yourself.

steps.title: Chill until firm
steps.content: Cool, then chill at least 2 hours until spoonably set.
steps.detail: Lift the ramekins out of the water bath and let them cool to room temperature, then cover and chill in the fridge for at least 2 hours, or overnight. They finish setting as they cool — from the oven they seem too soft, but cold they turn dense and creamy.
tips: A small spoon of softly whipped cream on top just before serving makes them feel like restaurant pots de crème.

title: Mushroom Ricotta Pantry Pasta
description: Silky ricotta melted into deep-golden pan-fried mushrooms and wilted spinach, tossed through hot pasta with a splash of its own starchy water. Quiet, creamy comfort from mostly the cupboard.
storage: Best eaten fresh while creamy. Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge; loosen with a splash of water when reheating, as the ricotta tightens once cold.
leftovers: Spread leftovers in a baking dish, scatter with extra parmesan and grill until bubbling for a quick pasta bake.

substitutions.swap: any mushrooms — swiss browns or a mix have more flavour than plain buttons
cottage cheese, or mascarpone for a richer, looser sauce
any short shape — penne, rigatoni or shells all hold the sauce well

steps.title: Boil the pasta
steps.content: Salt the water well and cook pasta to al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it until it tastes like the sea. Add the pasta and cook to just shy of the packet time so it keeps a little bite. Before draining, scoop out a full mug of the starchy cooking water — you'll need it to bring the sauce together. Drain and set aside.
tips: Save more pasta water than you think you need — you can always tip the extra away.

steps.title: Brown the mushrooms
steps.content: Fry mushrooms in oil until deep gold, 6-7 min.
steps.detail: Tear or slice the mushrooms. Heat the olive oil in a wide frying pan over medium-high until it shimmers, then add the mushrooms in one layer. Leave them alone for a couple of minutes to catch colour before stirring — crowding and early stirring makes them steam and go grey. Cook until they're shrunken, glossy and deep golden.
tips: A wide pan with space is the secret — cook in two batches rather than piling them all in.

steps.title: Add garlic and butter
steps.content: Stir in garlic and butter until fragrant.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to medium. Push the mushrooms to one side, drop in the butter and let it foam, then stir the minced garlic through it for about a minute — just until you can smell it, not until it browns. The butter carries the garlic into the mushrooms and gives everything a glossy coat.
warnings: Garlic scorches in seconds and turns bitter — keep it moving and pull the pan off the heat if it colours too fast.

steps.title: Wilt the spinach
steps.content: Add spinach and let it collapse into the pan.
steps.detail: Add the baby spinach a handful at a time, stirring until each addition wilts down into the mushrooms. It looks like far too much at first, then melts to almost nothing in a minute or two. Season with a good pinch of salt and a few grinds of pepper.

steps.title: Melt in the ricotta
steps.content: Loosen ricotta with pasta water into a sauce.
steps.detail: Spoon the ricotta into the pan with a good splash of the reserved pasta water and stir. The starchy water loosens the ricotta into a creamy sauce that clings rather than clumps. Add more water a little at a time until it looks silky, not stiff.

steps.title: Toss and finish
steps.content: Fold pasta through, finish with parmesan.
steps.detail: Tip the drained pasta into the pan and toss over low heat for a minute so every piece takes on the sauce, splashing in more pasta water if it looks tight. Grate in most of the parmesan and stir through, then taste and adjust the salt and pepper. Serve in warm bowls with the last of the parmesan over the top.

title: Anchovy Olive Chilli Spaghetti
description: A big-flavoured pantry plate where the anchovies melt into the oil until they vanish, leaving a savoury backbone for olives, capers and chilli. Done in the time the spaghetti takes to cook.
storage: Best fresh. Keeps a day in the fridge; reheat gently with a splash of water, though the pasta softens.

substitutions.swap: a spoonful of miso or a splash of fish sauce for the same salty depth
linguine or any long pasta
extra capers, or chopped sun-dried tomatoes, if you're out of olives

steps.title: Boil the spaghetti
steps.content: Cook spaghetti in well-salted water to al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it heavily. Add the spaghetti and stir so it doesn't clump, cooking to 1 minute short of the packet time. Scoop out a mug of the starchy pasta water before you drain — it's what pulls the sauce together at the end.

steps.title: Prep the punchy bits
steps.content: Slice garlic and chilli; chop olives, capers, parsley.
steps.detail: Thinly slice the garlic and the chilli, scraping out the chilli seeds first if you want less heat. Roughly chop the olives, drain the capers, and pull the parsley leaves from their stalks and chop them. Keep the parsley aside for the very end; everything else goes into the pan.

steps.title: Melt the anchovies
steps.content: Cook anchovies, garlic and chilli in oil until dissolved.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a wide pan over low-medium heat. Add the anchovy fillets, garlic and chilli and cook gently, stirring, for 2-3 minutes. The anchovies break down and all but disappear into the oil — that deep, savoury, not-fishy base is the whole point. Don't let the garlic brown.
warnings: Keep the heat low — anchovy oil and garlic catch and turn bitter fast.

steps.title: Warm the olives and capers
steps.content: Stir olives and capers through the oil.
steps.detail: Tip in the chopped olives and capers and stir for a minute to warm through and take on the garlicky oil. Add a few grinds of pepper. Go easy on any extra salt — anchovies, olives and capers are all salty already, so taste before you reach for it.

steps.title: Toss with the spaghetti
steps.content: Drag pasta into the pan with a splash of water.
steps.detail: Lift the spaghetti straight from the pot into the pan with tongs, letting some water come across with it. Add a good splash of the reserved pasta water and toss hard for a minute so the starchy water and oil emulsify into a glossy sauce that clings to every strand.

steps.title: Finish with parsley
steps.content: Scatter parsley, drizzle oil and serve.
steps.detail: Take the pan off the heat, throw in the chopped parsley and toss once more. Taste and adjust — maybe more pepper, or a little more pasta water if it looks dry. Serve straight away with a last drizzle of olive oil over each bowl.

title: Mussel Tomato Chilli Soup
description: A pot of plump mussels steamed open in a garlicky tomato and chilli broth, made for slurping with charred bread to mop the bowl. Cheap, fast and a little bit special.
storage: Eat the day it's made — cooked mussels don't keep well. Any leftover broth, with the mussels picked out of their shells, keeps a day in the fridge.

substitutions.swap: clams, or chunks of firm barramundi added for the last 5 minutes
a pinch of chilli flakes if you don't have fresh
any crusty bread or sourdough for mopping

steps.title: Clean the mussels
steps.content: Scrub, debeard and check every mussel.
steps.detail: Tip the mussels into a sink of cold water and scrub the shells, pulling away any wiry 'beard' that hangs from the side. Throw out any that are cracked, and any open ones that don't close when you tap them firmly on the bench — those aren't alive and shouldn't be cooked. Drain and keep cold until you need them.
warnings: Only cook mussels that are shut or that snap closed when tapped; discard any that stay open before cooking, and wash your hands after handling them.

steps.title: Build the tomato base
steps.content: Soften onion, garlic and chilli, then add tomatoes.
steps.detail: Finely dice the onion and slice the garlic and chilli. Warm the olive oil in a large pot over medium heat and cook the onion for 4-5 minutes until soft and translucent, then add the garlic and chilli for another minute until fragrant. Pour in the tinned tomatoes, crushing any whole ones against the side of the pot.

steps.title: Simmer the broth
steps.content: Loosen with water and simmer to meld, 10 min.
steps.detail: Half-fill the empty tomato tin with water and swirl it into the pot so it becomes a loose, soupy broth. Season with a little salt and pepper, bring to a gentle simmer and cook for about 10 minutes so the raw tomato edge softens into something sweet and rounded. Taste and adjust before the mussels go in.

steps.title: Steam the mussels open
steps.content: Add mussels, cover and steam 4-5 min.
steps.detail: Turn the heat up to bring the broth to a lively simmer, then tip in the mussels and clamp on the lid. Steam for 4-5 minutes, giving the pot a shake halfway, until the shells have opened wide and the mussels are plump. The briny liquor they release seasons the whole soup.
warnings: Any mussel that stays firmly shut after cooking should be thrown out, not forced open.

steps.title: Toast the bread
steps.content: Char or toast the bread for mopping.
steps.detail: While the mussels steam, toast or griddle the bread until crisp and golden at the edges. A rub of the cut side against a raw garlic clove is a nice touch if you like. This is your tool for mopping up every last bit of the broth.

steps.title: Scatter and serve
steps.content: Finish with parsley and ladle into bowls.
steps.detail: Chop the parsley and scatter it over the pot, then ladle the mussels and plenty of broth into deep bowls. Serve with the toast on the side and an empty bowl on the table for the shells.

title: Prawn Pineapple Fried Rice
description: Sweet bursts of pineapple and juicy prawns through smoky, soy-slicked rice. The trick is cold rice and a screaming-hot pan — everything else takes minutes.
storage: Cooked rice is best eaten straight away; if you keep leftovers, cool quickly, refrigerate within an hour and eat within a day, reheating until piping hot.
make_ahead: Cook the rice a day ahead and chill it uncovered — cold, dry rice fries far better than fresh, which turns to mush.

substitutions.swap: diced chicken thigh or firm tofu — cook it through before the rice goes in
any leftover long-grain rice; basmati works too
a splash of fish sauce or oyster sauce for a deeper, saltier hit

steps.title: Start with cold rice
steps.content: Use day-old rice, or cook and chill it first.
steps.detail: Fried rice lives or dies on dry, cold rice. If you haven't got leftovers, cook the jasmine rice, spread it on a tray and chill it uncovered for at least an hour (or overnight) so the grains firm up and separate. Fresh, warm rice clumps and steams instead of frying.

steps.title: Prep everything first
steps.content: Dice pineapple, slice spring onion, mince garlic, dry prawns.
steps.detail: Stir-frying is fast, so have it all lined up. Dice the pineapple into bite-sized pieces, mince the garlic, and slice the spring onions — keeping the white parts separate from the green tops. Pat the peeled prawns dry so they'll sear rather than stew.
warnings: Keep raw prawns and their board away from anything you'll eat as-is, and wash hands and knife after handling them.

steps.title: Scramble the eggs
steps.content: Softly scramble the eggs, then set aside.
steps.detail: Heat a teaspoon of the oil in a wok or big pan over high heat. Beat the eggs, pour them in and let them puff at the edges, then fold gently until just set and still glossy. Tip them onto a plate — they'll finish cooking when everything comes back together at the end.

steps.title: Sear the prawns
steps.content: Fry prawns until pink and curled, 2-3 min.
steps.detail: Add another splash of oil and keep the heat high. Lay the prawns in and cook for 2-3 minutes, turning once, until they're pink, opaque and just curled into a loose C. Scoop them out to join the eggs — an overcooked prawn goes rubbery, so pull them early.
warnings: Prawns are cooked when the flesh turns from grey and glassy to white and firm; don't serve any that still look translucent inside.

steps.title: Fry the rice
steps.content: Toss rice with garlic, pineapple and soy on high heat.
steps.detail: Add the last of the oil, then the garlic and spring onion whites, and stir for 30 seconds until fragrant. Tip in the cold rice and press it against the hot pan, tossing, so it catches a little colour and heats through — 3-4 minutes. Add the pineapple and pour the soy sauce around the edge of the pan so it sizzles, then toss to coat every grain.
tips: Break up any clumps of rice with the back of your spatula as it heats so nothing steams in a lump.

steps.title: Bring it together
steps.content: Fold prawns, egg and spring onion greens back in.
steps.detail: Return the prawns and egg to the pan, add the green spring onion tops and a good grind of pepper, and toss everything together for a minute until hot right through. Taste and add a splash more soy or a pinch of salt if it needs it. Serve straight from the pan.

title: Chicken Sriracha Honey Stir-Fry
description: Sticky, sweet-hot chicken thigh caramelised in a sriracha and honey glaze with sweet capsicum and onion, spooned over steamed rice. Loud, fast and very moreish.
equipment: saucepan with lid or rice cooker
storage: The stir-fry keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge; store the cooked rice separately and eat it within a day. Reheat until piping hot.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast (watch it doesn't dry out) or firm tofu for a veg version
any chilli sauce, or gochujang for a thicker, milder heat
maple syrup, or brown sugar dissolved in a splash of water

steps.title: Get the rice going
steps.content: Steam the jasmine rice while you cook.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a saucepan or rice cooker — roughly 1 part rice to 1.5 parts water, lid on, low heat, about 12 minutes, then rest off the heat. Getting it going first means it's fluffy and ready when the stir-fry hits the table.

steps.title: Prep and mix the glaze
steps.content: Slice chicken and veg; stir sriracha, honey and soy.
steps.detail: Cut the chicken thigh into bite-sized strips. Slice the capsicum and onion into chunky pieces and mince the garlic. In a small bowl, stir the sriracha, honey and soy sauce into a glossy glaze — start with less sriracha, as you can always add more heat later.
warnings: Wash the board, knife and your hands after the raw chicken before they touch the vegetables.

steps.title: Sear the chicken
steps.content: Brown chicken in a hot pan until cooked through.
steps.detail: Heat half the oil in a wok over high heat until it just shimmers. Add the chicken in one layer — in two batches if your pan is small — and leave it to colour before tossing. Cook for 5-6 minutes until golden and cooked through to 74°C at the thickest piece, with no pink inside. Lift it out onto a plate.
warnings: Chicken is safe at 74°C in the centre — cut into a thick piece to check it's white all the way through.

steps.title: Stir-fry the vegetables
steps.content: Toss capsicum and onion until blistered but crisp.
steps.detail: Add the rest of the oil and tip in the onion and capsicum. Stir-fry over high heat for 2-3 minutes so they blister and soften at the edges but keep a fresh crunch and bright colour. Add the garlic for the last 30 seconds — you want it fragrant, not browned.

steps.title: Glaze it sticky
steps.content: Return chicken, add glaze, toss until sticky.
steps.detail: Slide the chicken back in, pour over the sriracha-honey glaze and toss everything for 1-2 minutes. The honey bubbles and reduces to a sticky coat that clings to the chicken and veg. If it tightens too fast, a splash of water loosens it. Taste and season with a little salt and pepper.

steps.title: Serve over rice
steps.content: Spoon the sticky chicken over fluffy rice.
steps.detail: Fluff the rice with a fork and divide it between bowls, then spoon the sticky chicken and all its glaze over the top. Make sure to scrape every bit of glaze from the pan — it's the best part.

title: Peach Haloumi Rocket Salad
description: Warm, squeaky haloumi and juicy peach over peppery rocket, with toasted almonds and a bright lemon dressing. Sweet, salty and on the table in fifteen minutes.
storage: Assemble and eat straight away — dressed rocket wilts quickly. Keep the components separate in the fridge for up to a day and bring them together at the last minute.

substitutions.swap: nectarines, or ripe pears in cooler months
crumbled feta (no need to fry) or grilled slices of pumpkin
toasted walnuts or pine nuts

steps.title: Toast the almonds
steps.content: Toast almonds in a dry pan until golden.
steps.detail: Roughly chop the almonds and toast them in a dry pan over medium heat, shaking often, for 2-3 minutes until they smell nutty and turn a shade darker. Tip them straight out of the pan onto a plate so they don't keep cooking and catch. This tiny step is what makes them taste of something.
warnings: Nuts go from golden to burnt in seconds — don't walk away from the pan.

steps.title: Whisk the dressing
steps.content: Mix lemon juice, olive oil and a little honey.
steps.detail: Squeeze the lemon and whisk the juice with the olive oil, a pinch of salt and a few grinds of pepper. Stir in the honey if you'd like a little sweetness to balance the salty haloumi. It should taste sharp and bright — it softens once it meets the peach and cheese.

steps.title: Pan-fry the haloumi
steps.content: Fry haloumi slices golden on both sides.
steps.detail: Slice the haloumi into 1cm slabs and pat them dry. Fry in a dry or lightly oiled hot pan for about 2 minutes each side until deep golden and crisp — it'll squeak and soften but hold its shape. Serve it warm; haloumi turns rubbery as it cools, so this is close to the last thing you do.
tips: Don't salt the haloumi — it's salty enough on its own.

steps.title: Slice the peaches
steps.content: Cut peaches into wedges and pick the mint.
steps.detail: Halve the peaches, twist out the stones and slice into thick wedges. Ripe ones are fragrant and give slightly to a squeeze; if they're firm, slice them a little thinner. Pick the mint leaves from their stems, tearing any large ones.

steps.title: Build the salad
steps.content: Layer rocket, peach, haloumi, almonds and dressing.
steps.detail: Spread the rocket over a platter and scatter over the peach wedges and mint. Lay the warm haloumi on top and shower with the toasted almonds. Drizzle the dressing over just before serving and toss lightly at the table so the leaves stay perky.

title: Chicken Avocado Lime Wraps
description: Spiced pan-fried chicken, creamy avocado and a cool lime-yoghurt drizzle rolled up in a soft wrap. A fast, fresh lunch that beats anything from the servo.
storage: Cook the chicken and mix the yoghurt up to a day ahead and keep chilled; assemble the wraps fresh so they don't go soggy. Built wraps don't keep.
make_ahead: The spiced chicken is great cold — cook a double batch for lunchboxes through the week.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh for juicier meat, or crushed tinned chickpeas with the spices for a veg wrap
sour cream, or mayo loosened with lime
tortillas or flatbread

steps.title: Spice the chicken
steps.content: Butterfly and rub chicken with cumin and paprika.
steps.detail: Slice each chicken breast horizontally through the middle into two thin, even fillets so they cook fast and stay juicy. Pat dry, then rub all over with the olive oil, cumin, smoked paprika, a good pinch of salt and some pepper. Even thickness is the trick to no dry edges.
warnings: Wash hands, board and knife after the raw chicken before you touch the salad or wraps.

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.content: Pan-fry until golden and cooked through.
steps.detail: Heat a pan over medium-high and cook the chicken for about 4 minutes each side, until deep golden and cooked to 74°C at the thickest point with no pink inside. Rest it on a board for 5 minutes to keep the juices in, then slice into strips.
warnings: Check the thickest fillet is white through to the middle before slicing; 74°C is the safe mark.

steps.title: Make the lime yoghurt
steps.content: Stir yoghurt with lime juice and zest.
steps.detail: Zest half the lime into the yoghurt, then squeeze in its juice and add a pinch of salt. Stir into a loose, tangy sauce. Cut the rest of the lime into wedges for squeezing over at the end.

steps.title: Prep the fillings
steps.content: Slice the avocado and shred the lettuce.
steps.detail: Halve the avocados, remove the stones and scoop out the flesh, then slice or roughly mash it — a squeeze of lime over the top keeps it green. Finely shred the lettuce. Having everything laid out makes the rolling quick.

steps.title: Warm and roll
steps.content: Warm wraps, load and roll tight.
steps.detail: Warm each wrap for a few seconds in a dry pan or the microwave so it folds without cracking. Smear with lime yoghurt, then pile chicken, avocado and lettuce down the middle. Fold in the sides and roll up tightly, then cut in half on the diagonal.

title: Prawn Sriracha Toast
description: Juicy pan-fried prawns piled onto crisp toast slicked with sriracha mayo, brightened with lime and spring onion. A five-star snack in about ten minutes.
storage: Best assembled and eaten straight away while the toast is crisp. Cooked prawns keep a day in the fridge; the sriracha mayo keeps 3 days.

substitutions.swap: shredded cooked chicken or flaked tinned tuna in a pinch
Greek yoghurt for a lighter, tangier spread
sourdough or a split baguette

steps.title: Mix the sriracha mayo
steps.content: Stir mayo with sriracha and a squeeze of lime.
steps.detail: Spoon the mayo into a bowl and stir in the sriracha and a squeeze of lime, tasting as you go — start with a little sriracha and build the heat. You want a spreadable, coral-pink sauce that's punchy but still creamy. Cut the rest of the lime into wedges.

steps.title: Prep the prawns and onion
steps.content: Pat prawns dry; slice the spring onions.
steps.detail: Pat the peeled prawns dry with paper towel so they sear instead of steam, and season with a pinch of salt and pepper. Thinly slice the spring onions, keeping some of the green tops aside for scattering over at the end.
warnings: Keep the raw prawns and their board away from the toast and anything ready to eat; wash hands after.

steps.title: Sear the prawns
steps.content: Fry prawns until pink and just curled, 2-3 min.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a pan over high heat and add the prawns in one layer. Cook for 2-3 minutes, turning once, until they're pink, opaque and curled into a loose C. Pull them off the heat the moment they're done, and a squeeze of lime over the hot pan lifts them.
warnings: Prawns are done when the flesh is white and firm, not glassy; don't overcook or they turn rubbery.

steps.title: Toast the bread
steps.content: Toast the bread until crisp and golden.
steps.detail: Toast the bread until deeply golden and crisp enough to hold the topping without going soggy. A sturdy, well-toasted slice is the whole foundation here, so don't be shy with it.

steps.title: Build the toasts
steps.content: Spread mayo, pile prawns, finish with lime and onion.
steps.detail: Spread each slice generously with the sriracha mayo, then pile on the warm prawns. Scatter over the reserved spring onion greens, add a final squeeze of lime and a grind of pepper, and eat straight away while everything is still crisp and warm.

title: Whole Chicken Citrus Roast
description: A Sunday roast chicken perfumed with lemon and rosemary, resting on potatoes and carrots that soak up every drop of golden juice. Crisp skin, tender meat, one tin.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge once cooled. Strip the meat from the bones within a couple of hours of cooking and store it airtight; keep the carcass for stock.
make_ahead: Salt the chicken and leave it uncovered in the fridge a few hours ahead (or overnight) — it seasons the meat and dries the skin for extra crunch.
leftovers: Cold roast chicken makes brilliant sandwiches, or simmer the stripped carcass with an onion and a carrot for a pot of stock.

substitutions.swap: chunks of pumpkin or sweet potato, or a mix of all three
thyme or a few sage leaves
half an orange for a sweeter, gentler citrus note

steps.title: Dry and season the bird
steps.content: Pat the chicken dry and season inside and out.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Pat the whole chicken very dry with paper towel — dry skin is crisp skin. Rub it all over with the olive oil and season generously with salt and pepper, working a little into the cavity too.
warnings: Don't rinse raw chicken — it splashes bacteria around the sink. Wash hands, board and anything it touches.

steps.title: Stuff and tie
steps.content: Fill the cavity with lemon, rosemary and garlic; tie the legs.
steps.detail: Halve the lemon and smash the garlic cloves, then push them into the cavity with the rosemary sprigs — they perfume the bird from the inside as it roasts. Squeeze one lemon half over the skin first if you like. Tie the legs together with kitchen string so it cooks evenly and holds its shape.

steps.title: Prep the vegetables
steps.content: Chop potatoes and carrots into a bed in the tin.
steps.detail: Cut the potatoes into large chunks and the carrots into thick batons. Toss them with a little olive oil, salt and pepper and spread them across the roasting tin. Sit the chicken breast-up on top so the vegetables roast in the dripping fat.

steps.title: Roast until golden
steps.content: Roast about 1 hour 10 until juices run clear.
steps.detail: Roast for about 1 hour 10 minutes, until the skin is deep golden and the juices run clear when you pierce the thigh. The surest test is a thermometer in the thickest part of the thigh, not touching bone: 74°C means it's done. Turn the potatoes once halfway through.
tips: If the skin browns before the inside is done, loosely lay a sheet of foil over the top.
warnings: Chicken must reach 74°C in the thickest part of the thigh — if the juices run at all pink, give it another 10 minutes.

steps.title: Rest, then carve
steps.content: Rest 15 minutes before carving.
steps.detail: Lift the chicken onto a board and rest it, loosely covered with foil, for 15 minutes while the vegetables finish crisping in the oven. Resting lets the juices settle back into the meat instead of running out. Carve the legs and breast and serve with the vegetables and any pan juices spooned over.

title: Pear Walnut Blue-Feta Tart
description: Buttery pastry layered with soft pears, salty feta and blue cheese, crunchy walnuts and thyme, finished with a thread of honey. Sweet-savoury and dinner-party easy.
storage: Best warm from the oven while the pastry is crisp. Keeps a day in the fridge; re-crisp slices in a hot oven rather than the microwave.

substitutions.swap: apples or figs work beautifully the same way
leave it out and double the feta for a milder tart
pecans or hazelnuts

steps.title: Score the pastry
steps.content: Lay pastry on a tray and score a border.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Unroll the shortcrust pastry onto a paper-lined tray. Score a border about 2cm in from the edge with the tip of a knife — don't cut all the way through — to mark a crisp rim around the filling. Prick the inside all over with a fork so the base stays flat instead of doming as it bakes.

steps.title: Slice the pears
steps.content: Core and thinly slice the pears.
steps.detail: Quarter and core the pears, then slice them thinly so they soften through in the oven. Ripe but firm pears hold their shape best; very soft ones will collapse. A light brush of olive oil over the slices helps them colour.

steps.title: Build the tart
steps.content: Layer pears, both cheeses, walnuts and thyme.
steps.detail: Fan the pear slices over the pastry inside the border. Crumble the feta and blue cheese over the top, tuck in the walnuts and strip over the thyme leaves. Add a good grind of pepper — the salty cheeses mean you'll barely need any salt.

steps.title: Bake until golden
steps.content: Bake 25-28 min until the pastry is crisp.
steps.detail: Bake for 25-28 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and cooked through underneath — lift a corner to check it isn't pale and raw — the pears are tender and the cheese has softened and browned in patches. The smell of thyme and caramelising pear tells you it's close.
warnings: Lift the tart with the tray or a board — the tin and the melted cheese are very hot.

steps.title: Drizzle and serve
steps.content: Finish with honey and a few thyme leaves.
steps.detail: Let the tart settle for a few minutes, then thread the honey over the warm top so it melts into the cheese and pears. Scatter with a few fresh thyme leaves, cut into slices and serve warm, on its own or with a simple green salad.

title: Passionfruit Mascarpone Ramekins
description: Silky baked mascarpone custards, rich and just-set, crowned with sharp passionfruit pulp. Make them the day before and pull them from the fridge to impress.
storage: Keep covered in the fridge and eat within 3 days. They contain egg and dairy, so don't leave them out at room temperature for long.
make_ahead: Bake and chill a day ahead — they need at least 4 hours in the fridge to set properly, and overnight is better. Top with passionfruit just before serving.

substitutions.swap: cream cheese, softened and beaten smooth, for a tangier set
a spoon of lemon curd, or fresh berries, if passionfruit is out of season

steps.title: Whisk the eggs and sugar
steps.content: Beat eggs, sugar and vanilla until smooth.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 150°C fan and boil the kettle. In a bowl, whisk the eggs with the sugar and vanilla until smooth and pale. You're dissolving the sugar, not adding air, so there's no need to whisk it frothy.

steps.title: Warm the cream and mascarpone
steps.content: Gently heat the cream and whisk in the mascarpone.
steps.detail: Warm the cream in a saucepan over low heat until it steams but doesn't boil, then take it off the heat and whisk in the mascarpone until it melts into a smooth, pourable cream. Keep the heat gentle and keep stirring — a hard boil would split it.
warnings: Don't let the cream boil — take it off the heat as soon as it steams, or the mascarpone can split into grains.

steps.title: Combine and strain
steps.content: Whisk the warm cream into the eggs, then strain.
steps.detail: Pour the warm mascarpone cream into the egg mixture in a slow stream, whisking the whole time so the eggs warm gently instead of scrambling. Pass the custard through a fine sieve into a jug — this catches any set egg and gives you a glass-smooth pour.

steps.title: Bake in a water bath
steps.content: Fill ramekins, add hot water, bake 30-35 min, then chill.
steps.detail: Sit the ramekins in a deep roasting tin and divide the custard between them. Slide the tin into the oven, then carefully pour hot water from the kettle into the tin until it reaches halfway up the ramekins. Bake for 30-35 minutes until set at the edges but still wobbling like jelly in the very centre. Cool them out of the water bath, then refrigerate for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.
warnings: Pour the boiling water in with the tin already on the oven shelf, so you're not carrying a sloshing bath of hot water.

steps.title: Top with passionfruit
steps.content: Spoon over fresh passionfruit to serve.
steps.detail: Just before serving, halve the passionfruit and spoon the pulp and seeds over each chilled custard. The sharp, perfumed fruit cuts straight through the rich mascarpone — the whole point of the dish. Serve cold.

title: Tuna Caper Lemon Pasta
description: A pantry pasta that tastes like the coast: flaked tinned tuna, sharp capers and briny olives loosened with lemon and grassy olive oil, tangled through hot pasta.
storage: Best eaten straight away while the pasta is silky. Leftovers keep 1 day in an airtight container in the fridge; loosen with a splash of water and a fresh squeeze of lemon when you reheat.
leftovers: Cold, it makes a quick pasta salad the next day; stir through a little extra olive oil and eat at room temperature.

substitutions.swap: tinned salmon works just as well, flaked in the same way
spaghetti or linguine; any long shape suits this loose sauce
chopped cornichons, or just a few extra olives for the salty hit

steps.title: Get the water boiling
steps.content: Boil a big pot of well-salted water and prep the flavourings.
steps.detail: Fill your largest pot, salt it well so it tastes like the sea, and bring to a rolling boil while you prep. Zest the lemon first, taking just the yellow skin and none of the bitter white pith, then halve it ready to juice. Peel and thinly slice the garlic, and roughly chop the parsley and olives.
tips: Zest before you juice; it is near impossible to zest a squeezed lemon half.

steps.title: Boil the pasta al dente
steps.content: Cook the pasta a minute under packet time; save some water.
steps.detail: Slide the pasta into the boiling water, stir so it does not clump, and cook 1 minute less than the packet says so it keeps a little bite. Just before draining, scoop out a mugful of the starchy cooking water; it is the secret to a glossy sauce. Drain, but do not rinse.
tips: Save more pasta water than you think you need; you can always tip the extra away.

steps.title: Sizzle garlic, capers and olives
steps.content: Warm garlic, capers, olives and chilli gently in olive oil.
steps.detail: While the pasta cooks, warm the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-low. Add the sliced garlic, capers, olives and chilli flakes and cook gently for 2 minutes until the garlic is soft and fragrant and pale gold at most. If it starts to brown, pull the pan off the heat straight away; burnt garlic turns bitter and there is no coming back.
tips: Capers can spit as they hit the oil, so stand back for a second.
warnings: Keep the heat gentle; garlic burns in seconds once it colours, and bitter garlic taints the whole dish.

steps.title: Flake in the tuna
steps.content: Fold through drained tuna and lemon off the heat.
steps.detail: Drain the tuna and flake it into the pan in big chunks; do not stir it to a paste, you want texture. Add the lemon zest and a good squeeze of the juice, then take the pan off the heat. Toss gently just to warm the tuna through, as overcooking makes it dry and chalky.
tips: Taste and add more lemon juice if it needs lifting; tinned tuna loves acid.

steps.title: Toss with pasta water
steps.content: Marry pasta and sauce with a splash of the reserved water.
steps.detail: Tip the drained pasta into the pan with the parsley and a good splash of the reserved pasta water. Toss hard for a minute over low heat; the starchy water and oil come together into a light sauce that clings to every strand. Add more water a splash at a time if it looks dry, then season with black pepper.
tips: No cheese needed here; the capers, olives and lemon do all the seasoning.

title: Red Curry Pumpkin Chickpeas
description: A creamy weeknight curry built from the pantry: chunks of pumpkin and chickpeas simmered soft in coconut milk and red curry paste, brightened with lime and fresh coriander.
equipment: large deep frying pan or wok
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge and the flavour deepens overnight. Reheat gently with a splash of water; store the rice separately and use within 1 day.
make_ahead: The curry reheats beautifully, so make it a day ahead and cook fresh rice when you are ready to eat.
leftovers: Thin any leftover curry with a little stock or water for a quick soup, or spoon it over toast for a fast lunch.

substitutions.swap: tinned lentils, or cubes of firm tofu for a heartier bowl
sweet potato or butternut; any firm, sweet vegetable that holds its shape
green curry paste for a fresher, hotter version

steps.title: Start the rice
steps.content: Rinse and steam the jasmine rice, then leave it to rest.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice under cold water until it runs clear, then put it in a saucepan with 2 1/4 cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, drop to the lowest heat, cover, and cook 12 minutes. Take it off the heat and leave it covered to steam while you make the curry; do not lift the lid.
tips: The rice finishes cooking in its own trapped steam, so resist peeking or you let the steam out.

steps.title: Prep the pumpkin
steps.content: Cube the pumpkin, drain the chickpeas, ready the lime and herbs.
steps.detail: Peel the pumpkin and cut it into even 3 cm cubes so they cook at the same rate. Drain and rinse the chickpeas. Cut the lime into wedges and roughly chop the coriander so you can finish fast at the end.
tips: A sturdy vegetable peeler makes short work of the tough skin; a small firm knife also does the job.
warnings: Pumpkin is hard to cut, so steady it on a flat cut side, never a rounded one, so the knife cannot slip.

steps.title: Fry the curry paste
steps.content: Cook the red curry paste in oil until dark and fragrant.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a large deep pan over medium. Add the red curry paste and fry, stirring, for 1 to 2 minutes until it darkens a shade and smells intensely fragrant; this wakes up the spices. Keep it moving so it does not catch and scorch.
tips: If it looks dry or starts to stick, splash in a spoonful of the coconut milk to loosen it.
warnings: Curry paste can spit, so keep the heat at medium and stir constantly.

steps.title: Simmer the pumpkin
steps.content: Add coconut milk and pumpkin; simmer covered until tender.
steps.detail: Pour in the coconut milk, add the pumpkin and stir to coat. Bring to a gentle simmer, then cover and cook 15 minutes, until the pumpkin is tender to the tip of a knife but still holds its shape. Stir now and then so nothing sticks to the base.
tips: Fill the empty coconut tin a third with water, swirl and add it in; nothing wasted, and it stops the curry catching.

steps.title: Add chickpeas and balance
steps.content: Warm chickpeas through; balance with sugar, lime and salt.
steps.detail: Stir in the chickpeas and cook 3 to 4 minutes to heat through. Now balance it: add the sugar, a squeeze of lime and a pinch of salt, then taste. It should be creamy, a little sweet, salty and sour all at once, so add more of whatever it is missing.
tips: Tasting and adjusting at the end is how Thai food gets its balance; trust your tongue over the recipe.

steps.title: Serve over rice
steps.content: Fluff the rice, ladle over the curry, scatter coriander.
steps.detail: Fluff the rested rice with a fork and spoon into bowls. Ladle the curry over the top, scatter with coriander, and add a lime wedge on the side for squeezing at the table.

title: Miso Mushroom Noodle Soup
description: A restorative bowl of earthy mushrooms and greens in a gingery miso broth, poured over slippery noodles. The miso goes in off the heat to keep it alive and savoury.
storage: The broth keeps 2 days in the fridge, but store it separately from the noodles or they swell and go soft. Reheat the broth gently and do not boil it once the miso is in.
leftovers: Leftover broth makes a great base for a quick lunch; add whatever fresh greens and leftover protein you have.

substitutions.swap: rice noodles or udon; even instant noodles work in a pinch, minus the flavour sachet
no true swap, but a spoon of soy loosened with a little tahini gets you close
any mushroom, or a handful of dried shiitake soaked in the broth water first

steps.title: Prep the greens and aromatics
steps.content: Slice mushrooms, ginger, spring onion and bok choy.
steps.detail: Slice the mushrooms thickly, as they shrink a lot as they cook. Peel the ginger by scraping it with a spoon, then cut it into fine matchsticks. Trim and slice the spring onions, keeping the white parts for the broth and the green tops for scattering. Separate the bok choy leaves and wash out any grit.
tips: Keeping the raw green spring onion tops back for the end gives the bowl a fresh, sharp lift against the savoury broth.

steps.title: Brown the mushrooms
steps.content: Sear the mushrooms in sesame oil until golden.
steps.detail: Heat the sesame oil in a large saucepan over medium-high. Add the mushrooms with the white spring onion and ginger, and cook 4 to 5 minutes, stirring now and then, until the mushrooms are golden and any water they released has cooked off. Browning them first builds a deeper, meatier flavour into the broth.
tips: Do not stir too early; let the mushrooms sit and colour before you move them.

steps.title: Simmer the broth
steps.content: Add water and soy; simmer gently for a few minutes.
steps.detail: Pour in 1.5 litres of water and the soy sauce, and bring to a gentle simmer. Let it bubble softly for 5 minutes to pull everything together. Meanwhile, put the miso in a small bowl ready for later, as it must never go in while the pot is boiling hard.

steps.title: Cook noodles and greens
steps.content: Add noodles and bok choy; cook until just tender.
steps.detail: Add the egg noodles and the bok choy to the simmering broth. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes, until the noodles are just tender, the bok choy stems are crisp-tender and the leaves have wilted. Egg noodles cook fast, so taste one; it should be soft with the faintest bite.
tips: If you are not serving it all at once, cook the noodles separately and add to bowls so the leftovers do not turn to mush.

steps.title: Stir in the miso
steps.content: Off the heat, slake the miso and stir it back in.
steps.detail: Take the pot off the heat and let it stop bubbling. Spoon a ladle of the hot broth into the bowl of miso and whisk it smooth, then stir that back into the pot; this stops it going lumpy. Taste, and add a little more miso or soy if it needs it.
tips: Slaking the miso in a little broth first is the trick to a smooth, lump-free bowl.
warnings: Boiling miso dulls its flavour and kills its live cultures, so keep it off the heat from here.

steps.title: Finish with spring onion
steps.content: Ladle into bowls and scatter the green spring onion.
steps.detail: Divide the noodles and greens between bowls, ladle the hot broth over, and scatter with the reserved green spring onion tops. A few extra drops of sesame oil on top is lovely if you like.

title: Bacon Egg Pea Fried Rice
description: The Sunday-night fridge-clearer: cold rice fried hard with smoky bacon, sweet peas and ribbons of egg, seasoned with soy and a drop of sesame. Best with day-old rice.
storage: Cooked rice keeps 1 day only; cool it quickly, refrigerate within an hour and eat the next day. Reheat until piping hot all the way through, and never reheat it twice.
make_ahead: Cook the rice the day before, spread it on a tray to cool, then chill it; cold, dry rice fries far better than fresh.

substitutions.swap: diced ham, chorizo, or leftover roast chicken
frozen mixed veg, or corn kernels
tamari for gluten-free, or a mix of soy and oyster sauce for more depth

ingredients.amount: 4 cups cooked and cold (about 1 1/2 cups raw)

steps.title: Line everything up
steps.content: Beat eggs, dice bacon, slice garlic and spring onion.
steps.detail: Fried rice cooks fast and furious, so have it all ready before the wok gets hot. Beat the eggs in a small bowl. Dice the bacon, finely chop the garlic, and slice the spring onions, keeping the green tops back for the end. Break up any clumps in the cold rice with your fingers so every grain fries separately.
tips: Cold day-old rice is the secret; fresh warm rice turns gluggy and steams instead of frying.

steps.title: Crisp the bacon
steps.content: Fry the bacon in a hot wok until golden and rendered.
steps.detail: Heat the wok over high with half the vegetable oil. Add the bacon and stir-fry 3 to 4 minutes until it is golden and the fat has rendered out. That rendered fat is pure flavour, so leave it in the pan.
warnings: Wash your hands and the board after handling raw bacon before touching anything else.

steps.title: Scramble the eggs
steps.content: Push bacon aside and scramble the egg into soft curds.
steps.detail: Push the bacon to one side, add a touch more oil to the clear space and pour in the beaten egg. Let it sit for a few seconds, then scramble it into soft curds with your spoon. Stir the garlic and white spring onion through for the last 30 seconds until fragrant.
tips: Keep the egg moving once it is nearly set; you want tender curds, not a rubbery omelette.

steps.title: Fry the rice hard
steps.content: Add rice and peas; fry until every grain is hot and separate.
steps.detail: Tip in the cold rice and the frozen peas. Now fry with intent: press the rice against the hot wok, then toss, for 4 to 5 minutes, until every grain is hot, separate and starting to catch a little colour. The peas will thaw and turn bright green in the heat.
tips: Spread the rice out and leave it a moment between tosses; that brief contact with the hot metal gives the toasty edges.

steps.title: Dress with soy and sesame
steps.content: Splash in soy and sesame oil; scatter the spring onion.
steps.detail: Drizzle the soy sauce down the side of the hot wok so it sizzles, add the sesame oil and a good crack of pepper, and toss everything through for a minute. Taste and add a little more soy if it needs it. Kill the heat, scatter with the reserved green spring onion, and serve straight away.
tips: Adding soy down the hot side of the wok, not straight onto the rice, gives it a smoky edge.

title: Calamari Chilli Lime Stir-Fry
description: Fast, fiery and fresh: calamari seared hard for barely a minute, then tossed with chilli, garlic and lime so it stays tender. The trick is high heat and getting it out of the pan quick.
storage: Best eaten immediately, as calamari toughens as it sits. Leftovers keep 1 day in the fridge; slice them cold into a salad rather than reheating, which makes the calamari rubbery.

substitutions.swap: prawns or firm white fish; both love the same chilli-lime treatment
rice noodles, or serve it in lettuce cups for something lighter
a spoon of sriracha or a pinch of chilli flakes if you have no fresh chilli

steps.title: Get the rice going
steps.content: Rinse and steam the jasmine rice first so it is ready.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then simmer in a covered pan with 2 1/4 cups water: boil, drop to low, cover and cook 12 minutes, then rest off the heat with the lid on. Get it going first, as the stir-fry itself takes only minutes.

steps.title: Score and dry the calamari
steps.content: Score the calamari and pat it bone-dry.
steps.detail: If the calamari is in tubes, slit them open and lightly score the inside in a criss-cross with the tip of your knife; this helps it curl prettily and stay tender. Cut into bite-sized pieces, then pat every piece really dry with paper towel. Wet calamari steams and turns grey, while dry calamari sears and stays white and sweet.
tips: The drier the calamari, the better the sear, so give it a proper press with the paper towel.
warnings: Handle the raw calamari on a separate board, and wash hands, knife and board well before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Prep chilli, garlic and lime
steps.content: Slice chilli and garlic, cut the lime, pick the coriander.
steps.detail: Thinly slice the chilli and garlic, and pick the coriander leaves, chopping the tender stems finely as they are full of flavour. Cut the lime in half. Have the soy sauce measured and everything within reach of the stove; once the calamari hits the pan there is no time to chop.
tips: Deseed the chilli if you want the flavour with less heat; most of the fire is in the seeds and white ribs.

steps.title: Sear the calamari
steps.content: Blast the calamari in a screaming-hot wok for 1 to 2 minutes.
steps.detail: Get the wok as hot as you dare with the oil just smoking. Add the calamari in one layer so it hisses loudly, and stir-fry hard for just 1 to 2 minutes until it turns opaque white and the pieces curl. The instant it is opaque, it is done; any longer and it goes rubbery.
tips: Work in two batches if your pan is small; a crowded wok steams the calamari instead of searing it.
warnings: Wet seafood in hot oil can spit, so stand back and add it away from you.
Do not overcook: calamari is either quick-and-hot or long-and-slow, and a couple of minutes here is the sweet spot.

steps.title: Toss with chilli and lime
steps.content: Add garlic, chilli and soy; finish with lime off the heat.
steps.detail: Throw in the garlic and chilli and toss for 30 seconds until fragrant, not browned. Splash in the soy sauce, squeeze over the lime, add a crack of pepper, and toss once more. Pull it off the heat straight away and fold through most of the coriander.
tips: The lime goes in at the very end; its fresh sharpness fades if it cooks.

steps.title: Serve over rice
steps.content: Spoon over the rice with the pan juices and coriander.
steps.detail: Fluff the rice and pile the calamari and all its pan juices on top. Scatter with the last of the coriander and serve with the squeezed lime halves, or a fresh wedge, on the side.

title: Beetroot Feta Walnut Salad
description: Earthy beetroot, creamy feta and toasted walnuts piled on peppery rocket, with a honey-balsamic dressing. Sweet, sharp, crunchy and soft, all on one plate.
storage: Best assembled just before serving so the rocket stays crisp. Keep the components separate in the fridge for up to 2 days and dress at the last minute; a dressed salad wilts within hours.
make_ahead: Toast the walnuts and shake up the dressing ahead; store the walnuts airtight and the dressing in a jar, then it is a two-minute assembly.

substitutions.swap: creamy goat's cheese, or bocconcini for something milder
toasted pecans, hazelnuts or pine nuts
baby spinach or a mix of soft salad leaves

steps.title: Toast the walnuts
steps.content: Dry-toast the walnuts until fragrant, then cool and chop.
steps.detail: Put the walnuts in a dry frying pan over medium heat and toast for 3 to 4 minutes, shaking often, until they smell nutty and turn a shade darker. Tip them straight onto a board to cool, as they keep cooking and can scorch if left in the hot pan. Once cool, roughly chop them.
tips: Toasting is what makes the walnuts sing; raw, they taste flat and a little bitter.
warnings: Nuts go from golden to burnt in seconds, so do not walk away, and pull them the moment they smell toasty.

steps.title: Shake up the dressing
steps.content: Combine balsamic, honey, dijon and oil into a glossy dressing.
steps.detail: In a small jar, combine the balsamic vinegar, honey, Dijon, olive oil and a pinch of salt and pepper. Put the lid on and shake hard until it is thick, glossy and no longer separating; the mustard and honey help it emulsify. Taste it on a rocket leaf and adjust: more honey to sweeten, more vinegar to sharpen.
tips: No jar? Whisk it in a bowl with a fork; just keep going until it thickens and looks creamy.

steps.title: Dress the beetroot
steps.content: Wedge the cooked beetroot and coat it in a little dressing.
steps.detail: Cut the cooked beetroot into bite-sized wedges. If you are using vac-packed or tinned beetroot, drain and pat it dry so it does not bleed pink through the whole salad. Toss the beetroot on its own with a spoon of the dressing first, so it soaks up flavour without staining the leaves.
tips: Wear an apron and use a spare board if you can; beetroot stains hands, boards and clothes.
Cooked beetroot from the fridge section or a tin keeps this a genuine weeknight salad; roast your own if you have the hour.

steps.title: Layer the platter
steps.content: Layer rocket, beetroot, feta and walnuts on a platter.
steps.detail: Spread the rocket over a platter. Scatter the dressed beetroot through it, then crumble the feta over in big rough chunks and shower with the toasted walnuts. Layering rather than tossing keeps the feta from smearing pink and the walnuts on top where they stay crunchy.

steps.title: Drizzle and serve
steps.content: Drizzle over the rest of the dressing just before serving.
steps.detail: Drizzle the remaining dressing over the top just before it goes to the table; dress too early and the rocket collapses. Serve straight away, while the leaves are perky and the walnuts crisp.

title: Prawn Mango Rice Paper Rolls
description: Cool, fresh Vietnamese-style rolls packed with sweet prawns, ripe mango, cucumber and mint, wrapped in soft rice paper. No cooking beyond a quick poach, just happy assembly.
storage: Best eaten within a few hours, as rice paper firms up and goes chewy in the fridge. If you must keep them, wrap each roll in damp paper towel and store airtight for up to a day.
make_ahead: Prep and slice all the fillings and poach the prawns ahead; keep everything covered in the fridge and roll just before serving so the wrappers stay soft.

substitutions.swap: cooked shredded chicken, tofu batons, or crab sticks
just-ripe pear or a little pineapple for the same sweet note
any thin rice vermicelli, or leave them out for a lighter roll

steps.title: Poach the prawns
steps.content: Simmer the prawns until just pink, then cool and halve.
steps.detail: Bring a saucepan of lightly salted water to a gentle simmer. Drop in the prawns and poach for 2 to 3 minutes, just until they turn pink and curl into a loose C; a tight O means they are overdone. Scoop them into cold water to stop the cooking, then drain and slice each prawn in half lengthways so they lie flat in the roll.
tips: Halving the prawns lengthways makes them lie flat and show through the wrapper prettily.
warnings: Keep raw prawns chilled until you cook them, and wash hands and board well afterwards.
An overcooked prawn is tough and rubbery, so pull them the moment they are pink and opaque.

steps.title: Soak the noodles
steps.content: Soften the rice vermicelli in hot water, then cool.
steps.detail: Put the rice vermicelli in a bowl and cover with boiling water. Leave 3 to 4 minutes until soft and floppy, then drain and rinse under cold water to stop them clumping. Snipping through the tangle with scissors makes them easier to portion into the rolls.
tips: Rinsing well with cold water stops the noodles sticking into one solid clump.

steps.title: Slice the fillings
steps.content: Cut mango and cucumber into batons; pick the mint.
steps.detail: Cut the mango cheeks off the stone and slice the flesh into thin batons. Cut the cucumber into similar matchsticks, scraping out the watery seeds first so the rolls do not go soggy. Pick the mint leaves off their stems and lay everything out in a row; this is your rolling station.
tips: Cut everything into similar thin strips; even batons roll up neatly and evenly.

steps.title: Mix the dipping sauce
steps.content: Stir fish sauce, lime and water into a sharp-salty dip.
steps.detail: Squeeze the lime into a small bowl and add the fish sauce and 2 tablespoons of water. Stir and taste; it should be a lively balance of salty and sour. Add a splash more water if it is too intense for you.
tips: A little sweetness rounds the dip if it tastes too sharp; stir in a touch of whatever sweetener you like.

steps.title: Soften a wrapper
steps.content: Dip one rice paper in warm water until just pliable.
steps.detail: Fill a wide shallow bowl with warm water. Dip one sheet of rice paper in for just 10 to 15 seconds, and pull it out while it still feels slightly firm, as it keeps softening on the bench. Lay it on a damp tea towel. Work one wrapper at a time, as they tear and stick if you rush or oversoak them.
tips: The wrapper should still be a little stiff when it comes out; it turns perfectly pliable by the time the fillings are on.
warnings: Use warm, not hot, water; hot water oversoftens the wrapper and scalds your fingertips.

steps.title: Roll them up
steps.content: Layer the fillings, then fold and roll into a tight parcel.
steps.detail: In the lower third of the wrapper, lay a few mint leaves, then a little noodle, mango and cucumber, and finally three prawn halves cut-side up so they show through. Fold the bottom up over the filling, fold in the two sides, then roll up firmly like a little parcel. Keep the finished rolls from touching each other or they stick.
tips: Do not overfill; a slim, snug roll holds together far better than a fat, bursting one.
Roll firmly but not so tight it splits; that is what keeps everything tucked in.

steps.title: Serve fresh
steps.content: Serve whole or halved with the dipping sauce alongside.
steps.detail: Serve the rolls whole, or halve them on the diagonal to show off the fillings, with the dipping sauce alongside. Eat them fresh, as they are at their best within an hour or two of rolling.

title: Avocado Feta Chilli Toast
description: Brunch sorted in ten minutes: creamy smashed avocado on crunchy toast, crowned with salty feta, fresh chilli, coriander and a squeeze of lime.
storage: Assemble and eat straight away, as avocado browns and toast goes soft within the hour. If prepping the avocado ahead, stir the lime through, press cling film onto the surface, and keep it a few hours at most.

substitutions.swap: sourdough is best, but any good crusty bread toasts up well
crumbled goat's cheese, or a dollop of ricotta for something milder
chilli flakes or a drizzle of sriracha

steps.title: Toast the bread
steps.content: Toast the bread until deeply golden and crisp.
steps.detail: Toast the bread until deeply golden and crisp; a sturdy, crunchy base stops it going soggy under the avocado. Sourdough or a seedy loaf is ideal. If you have a chargrill pan, a brush of olive oil and a couple of minutes each side gives lovely smoky bar-marks.
tips: Toast it a shade darker than you think; the crunch is what carries the soft toppings.

steps.title: Smash the avocado
steps.content: Mash the avocado with lime, salt and pepper.
steps.detail: Halve the avocados, twist out the stones and scoop the flesh into a bowl. Add a good squeeze of lime, a pinch of salt and a crack of pepper, then mash with a fork to your liking; chunky is nicer than baby-food smooth. Taste it, as ripe avocado needs enough salt and acid to come alive.
tips: The lime is not just for flavour; its acid keeps the avocado green instead of browning.

steps.title: Prep the toppings
steps.content: Slice the chilli, pick the coriander, crumble the feta.
steps.detail: Thinly slice the chilli into fine rings and pick the coriander leaves. If you like things milder, scrape out the chilli seeds and pale ribs, where most of the heat sits. Crumble the feta into rough rubble ready to scatter.
tips: A little chilli goes a long way; start with less, as you can always add more.

steps.title: Build the toast
steps.content: Spread avocado, then top with feta, chilli and coriander.
steps.detail: Spread the smashed avocado thickly over the hot toast, right to the edges. Scatter over the crumbled feta, then the chilli and coriander. Finish with a drizzle of olive oil, a final squeeze of lime and a crack of pepper, and eat while the toast is still warm and crunchy.
