tips: A last flick of salt over the feta and avocado, right before eating, makes everything pop.

title: Pumpkin Feta Filo Pie
description: Golden, shatteringly crisp filo wrapped around a soft filling of roasted pumpkin, salty feta, spinach and sage. A vegetarian centrepiece that looks far harder than it is.
equipment: round baking dish or tin (about 22 cm)
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. The filo softens once stored, so crisp it back up in a hot oven for 10 minutes rather than the microwave, which makes it soggy.
make_ahead: Roast the pumpkin and make the filling a day ahead; assemble and bake fresh so the filo stays crisp.
leftovers: A cold wedge is excellent in a lunchbox, or reheat it in the oven and serve with a green salad.

substitutions.swap: sweet potato or butternut roasts up just as sweet
ricotta for a milder, creamier filling, or a mix of both
puff pastry for an easier, if less shattery, pie

steps.title: Roast the pumpkin
steps.content: Roast the cubed pumpkin until soft and caramelised.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan-forced. Toss the pumpkin cubes with a little olive oil, salt and pepper on an oven tray and roast for 25 to 30 minutes, until soft and golden at the edges. Roasting rather than boiling drives off water and concentrates the sweetness, so the filling is not wet. Let it cool a little.
tips: Spread the pumpkin out in a single layer; crowded cubes steam and stay pale instead of caramelising.

steps.title: Wilt the spinach and sage
steps.content: Soften garlic and sage, then wilt in the spinach.
steps.detail: Warm a little olive oil in a frying pan over medium heat. Add the finely chopped garlic and the sage leaves and cook 1 minute until fragrant. Add the spinach in handfuls and stir until just wilted, then tip it into a sieve and press out as much liquid as you can; wet spinach is the enemy of crisp pastry.
tips: Squeeze the cooled spinach hard in your hands or against the sieve; you will be surprised how much water comes out.
warnings: Garlic burns fast, so keep it moving and do not let it colour past pale gold or it turns bitter.

steps.title: Mix the filling
steps.content: Fold pumpkin, spinach, feta and egg together.
steps.detail: In a bowl, gently combine the roasted pumpkin, the squeezed spinach, the crumbled feta and the beaten eggs. Season, but remember the feta is already salty, so go easy. Fold rather than mash, as you want to keep some chunks of pumpkin and feta, not a puree.
tips: The egg binds it all so the filling sets and slices cleanly once baked.

steps.title: Layer the filo
steps.content: Brush and layer filo sheets into the dish with an overhang.
steps.detail: Work quickly and keep the filo you are not using under a damp tea towel, or it dries and cracks. Brush one sheet with olive oil, lay it in the dish with the edges overhanging, and repeat with 5 to 6 sheets, fanning them at different angles so the whole dish is covered with plenty of overhang.
tips: Do not fuss over little tears; filo is forgiving, and the layers hide any wrinkles and rips.
warnings: Filo dries out in minutes once unwrapped, so keep the stack covered with a damp towel between sheets.

steps.title: Fill and wrap
steps.content: Spoon in the filling and fold the pastry over the top.
steps.detail: Spoon the filling into the pastry-lined dish and level it off. Fold the overhanging filo up and over the top, scrunching it loosely so it sits in crisp, rustic ruffles. Brush the top with the last of the olive oil for an even, golden finish.

steps.title: Bake until golden
steps.content: Bake until the filo is deep golden and the filling is set.
steps.detail: Bake at 200°C fan-forced for 30 to 35 minutes, until the filo is deep golden brown and crisp all over and a knife slid into the centre comes out clean. If the top browns too fast, lay a sheet of foil loosely over it. Let it settle 10 minutes before cutting so the filling firms up and slices neatly.
tips: The 10-minute rest is worth it; cut too soon and the filling slumps out of the slice.
warnings: Resist opening the oven in the first 20 minutes; the burst of cool air can stop the pastry crisping.

title: Bacon Leek Cheddar Tart
description: A savoury tart on a crisp shortcrust base: sweet, slow-softened leeks, smoky bacon and sharp cheddar set in a silky egg custard. Quiche, but make it dinner.
equipment: tart tin or pie dish (about 24 cm)
baking paper and baking weights or dried rice
storage: Keeps 3 days covered in the fridge. Eat it cold, or reheat slices in a 160°C oven for 10 minutes; the microwave works but softens the pastry.
make_ahead: Blind-bake the shell and cook the bacon and leeks a day ahead, then fill and bake fresh, or bake the whole tart and serve it at room temperature.
leftovers: A cold slice is a brilliant lunchbox or picnic filler with a handful of salad leaves.

substitutions.swap: diced ham or pancetta, or leave it out for a vegetarian leek-and-cheddar tart
a gruyere-style tasty cheese, or a mix with parmesan for more bite
a brown onion, softened the same slow way

steps.title: Blind-bake the shell
steps.content: Line the tin with pastry and bake it blind until pale gold.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 180°C fan-forced. Line the tart tin with the shortcrust, pressing it gently into the corners, and trim the edges. Prick the base with a fork, lay in a sheet of baking paper and fill with baking weights or dried rice. Bake 15 minutes, then lift out the paper and weights; this stops the base puffing up and keeps it crisp under the wet filling.
tips: Blind-baking is the difference between a crisp base and a soggy one, so do not skip the weights.

steps.title: Crisp the bacon
steps.content: Fry the bacon until golden, then set it aside.
steps.detail: While the shell bakes, fry the diced bacon in a dry pan over medium-high for 4 to 5 minutes until golden and crisp at the edges. Scoop it out and set aside, leaving the rendered fat in the pan for the leeks.
warnings: Wash your hands and board after handling the raw bacon before touching anything else.

steps.title: Soften the leeks
steps.content: Sweat the sliced leeks low and slow until sweet.
steps.detail: Slice the leek into half-moons and wash well in a colander, as grit hides between the layers. Add them to the bacon fat with the olive oil over low heat and cook gently for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring, until completely soft and sweet but not browned. Season with a little salt and pepper.
tips: Low and slow is the key; rushed leeks catch and turn bitter, but you want them melting and sweet.

steps.title: Whisk the custard
steps.content: Beat eggs, cream and nutmeg into a smooth custard.
steps.detail: In a bowl, whisk the eggs, cream, a good grating of nutmeg and a little salt and pepper until smooth. The nutmeg is the quiet classic touch that makes a custard taste like a proper quiche. Whisk just until combined, as you do not want it frothy.
tips: Season the custard a little under; the bacon and cheddar are both salty.

steps.title: Fill the tart
steps.content: Layer leeks, bacon and cheddar, then pour in the custard.
steps.detail: Scatter the softened leeks and crisp bacon over the base, then most of the grated cheddar. Sit the tart on the oven shelf, then pour the custard in; filling it at the oven saves carrying a brimming tart across the kitchen. Top with the last of the cheddar.
tips: Pouring the custard once the tin is already on the shelf means no spills on the journey.

steps.title: Bake until just set
steps.content: Bake until golden and the centre barely wobbles.
steps.detail: Bake at 180°C fan-forced for 30 to 35 minutes, until the top is golden and the custard is set with just a faint wobble in the very centre; it firms up as it cools. Let it rest 10 minutes before slicing so it holds together. It is lovely warm or at room temperature.
tips: A set custard should jiggle like a panna cotta, not slosh like liquid; that is your cue it is done.
warnings: Do not bake until the centre is solid; a slight wobble now means silky, not rubbery, custard once it is rested.

title: Pavlova Berry Cloud
description: A cheat's pavlova that comes together in minutes: crisp meringue nests piled with softly whipped vanilla cream, a quick berry crush and fresh strawberries, finished with tart passionfruit.
storage: Assemble just before serving, as meringue turns soft and weeps within an hour of meeting the cream. Keep the parts separate: whipped cream covered in the fridge for a day, meringues airtight at room temperature, fruit chilled.
make_ahead: Whip the cream and make the berry crush a few hours ahead and chill them; keep the meringues in their airtight box and assemble at the last minute.

substitutions.swap: any frozen berries, or fresh raspberries lightly crushed
any soft summer fruit; sliced peaches, mango or kiwi
fold a little mascarpone through the cream for a richer, more stable topping

steps.title: Make the berry crush
steps.content: Thaw the frozen berries and lightly crush half into a sauce.
steps.detail: Tip the frozen berries into a bowl with a spoon of the icing sugar and let them thaw, about 15 minutes at room temperature. As they soften, crush about half with a fork to make a loose, glossy sauce, leaving the rest whole. Taste and add a little more icing sugar if they are sharp.
tips: The syrup that pools as the berries thaw is liquid gold, so spoon every drop over the pavlova.

steps.title: Whip the cream
steps.content: Whip cream with vanilla and icing sugar to soft peaks.
steps.detail: Pour the cold cream into a large bowl with the vanilla and the remaining icing sugar. Whip, by machine or with a balloon whisk and some elbow grease, until it holds soft, floppy peaks that just fold over on themselves. Stop there, as overwhipped cream turns grainy and stiff and there is no going back.
tips: Cold cream in a cold bowl whips faster and holds better, so chill both if you have time.
Soft peaks, not stiff; the cream should still look silky and slump gently, not stand up rigid.

steps.title: Prep the fruit
steps.content: Hull and slice the strawberries; scoop the passionfruit.
steps.detail: Hull the strawberries and slice them, saving a few small whole ones for the top. Halve the passionfruit and scoop out the tangy pulp; its sharpness cuts through all that sweet cream and meringue. Have everything ready so you can build fast.
tips: A ripe passionfruit feels heavy and has a wrinkled skin; smooth and light means it is not ready.

steps.title: Pile it up
steps.content: Top the meringue with cream, berries and fruit.
steps.detail: Set the meringue nests on plates and spoon the whipped cream generously into the hollows. Spoon over the berry crush and its syrup, pile on the sliced strawberries, and finish with the passionfruit pulp and a dusting of icing sugar. Serve straight away, while the meringue is still crisp against the soft cream.
tips: Assemble at the very last moment; meringue starts to soften the instant the cream and fruit go on.

title: Bacon Broccoli Cream Pasta
description: Salty, crisp bacon and tender broccoli caught in a silky parmesan cream that clings to every twist of pasta. A twenty-minute bowl that eats like a treat.
storage: Best eaten fresh, as the cream tightens and the broccoli softens on standing. Leftovers keep 2 days in the fridge; loosen with a splash of milk when reheating gently.
leftovers: Fold cold leftovers through a beaten egg and fry into a crisp, golden pasta cake.

substitutions.swap: pancetta, or diced ham for a lighter, less smoky result
evaporated milk, or milk thickened with a teaspoon of cornflour
any short shape — penne, spirals or shells all hold the sauce
any hard grating cheese; a block of tasty cheddar does the job

steps.title: Prep broccoli and bacon
steps.content: Cut broccoli into small florets, dice bacon, slice garlic.
steps.detail: Cut the broccoli into small, even florets so they cook quickly and sit neatly on a fork; peel and slice the stalk too — it's sweet and a shame to bin. Slice the bacon into short strips and finely slice the garlic. Keep the garlic on its own — it goes in late and burns in seconds.

steps.title: Boil pasta and broccoli
steps.content: Cook pasta, adding the broccoli for the last 3 minutes.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of well-salted water to a rolling boil and add the pasta. Set a timer for 3 minutes less than the packet says, then tip the broccoli florets straight in to finish cooking together. Before draining, scoop out a mugful of the starchy cooking water — it's the secret to a glossy sauce. Drain, but don't rinse.
tips: Salt the water until it tastes like the sea — it's your only chance to season the pasta itself.

steps.title: Crisp the bacon
steps.content: Fry bacon in a little oil until golden and rendered.
steps.detail: While the pasta boils, heat the oil in a large frying pan over medium-high and add the bacon. Fry, stirring now and then, for about 5 minutes until the edges are golden and crisp and the fat has rendered out. Don't rush it — that rendered bacon fat is the backbone of the sauce.
tips: If a lot of fat pools, spoon off all but a tablespoon so the sauce doesn't turn greasy.

steps.title: Make the cream sauce
steps.content: Soften garlic, add cream and parmesan, simmer to thicken.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to medium, add the sliced garlic and stir for 30 seconds until fragrant — don't let it colour or it turns bitter. Pour in the cream, add a good crack of pepper and bring to a gentle simmer. Let it bubble softly for 2 to 3 minutes until slightly thickened, then stir through most of the parmesan.
warnings: Garlic scorches fast in hot bacon fat — keep it moving and drop the heat if it starts to brown.

steps.title: Toss it all together
steps.content: Combine pasta, broccoli and sauce, loosening with pasta water.
steps.detail: Tip the drained pasta and broccoli into the pan and toss to coat everything in the sauce. Splash in the reserved pasta water a little at a time until the sauce is silky and clings rather than sitting in a puddle. Taste and adjust the salt and pepper. Serve straight away with the last of the parmesan grated over.

title: Green Curry Barramundi Rice Bowl
description: Flaky barramundi gently poached in a fragrant coconut green curry, spooned over jasmine rice with just-wilted bok choy. Ready in the time it takes the rice to steam.
equipment: large deep frying pan or wok
storage: The curry keeps 2 days in the fridge; store the rice separately. Reheat gently so the fish doesn't break up, and as with all seafood, don't leave it out at room temperature.
leftovers: Flake any leftover fish and sauce through rice the next day, or spoon it over rice noodles.

substitutions.swap: any firm white fish — snapper, ling or basa; or swap for prawns
red curry paste for a milder, sweeter curry
coconut cream for a richer sauce, thinned with a splash of water
any quick greens — baby spinach, snow peas or green beans

steps.title: Steam the jasmine rice
steps.content: Rinse and simmer the rice until fluffy, then rest.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice in a couple of changes of cold water until it runs clearer, then drain. Put it in a saucepan with 3 cups of water, bring to the boil, then drop to the lowest heat with the lid on for 12 minutes. Take it off the heat and leave it covered to steam while you cook the curry — no peeking, as the trapped steam finishes the job.
tips: A tight lid is everything; if yours is loose, lay a tea towel under it to trap the steam.

steps.title: Prep fish and greens
steps.content: Cut barramundi into chunks, halve bok choy, wedge the lime.
steps.detail: Pat the barramundi dry and cut into big bite-sized pieces, feeling for any stray bones. Wash the bok choy and cut in half lengthways, or into quarters if large. Cut the lime into wedges. Keep the fish on its own plate, away from everything else.
warnings: Wash your hands, board and knife after handling raw fish before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Fry the curry paste
steps.content: Sizzle green curry paste in oil, then add coconut milk.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a wide pan over medium. Add the green curry paste and fry, stirring, for about a minute until it darkens slightly and smells intensely fragrant — this wakes up the spices. Pour in the coconut milk, stir to dissolve the paste and bring to a gentle simmer.
tips: Scoop the thick cream from the top of the coconut milk tin and fry that with the paste first for extra richness.

steps.title: Poach the barramundi
steps.content: Simmer fish and bok choy gently, 4–5 min, then season.
steps.detail: Slide the barramundi into the simmering sauce and tuck the bok choy in around it. Simmer gently — don't let it boil hard or the fish falls apart — for 4 to 5 minutes, until the fish is opaque and flakes at a nudge and the greens are just wilted. Season with the fish sauce and a good squeeze of lime, then taste: it should be salty, sour and a little sweet.

steps.title: Spoon over rice
steps.content: Fluff the rice into bowls and ladle over the curry.
steps.detail: Fluff the rested rice with a fork and divide between four bowls. Ladle the barramundi, greens and plenty of that green coconut sauce over the top. Finish each with a lime wedge to squeeze at the table.

title: Chicken Lemon Rice Soup
description: A brothy, restorative bowl of poached chicken, soft rice and sweet vegetables, brightened with a big squeeze of lemon right at the end. The one you want when you're run down.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge. The rice drinks up the broth as it sits, so add a splash of water or stock when reheating. Freezes well for up to 2 months.
make_ahead: Poach the chicken and make the broth a day ahead; add the rice only when you reheat so it doesn't go mushy.
leftovers: Thickened overnight it's almost a risotto — loosen with stock and stir through a handful of baby spinach.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast for a leaner soup; poach it gently so it stays tender
any long-grain rice, or orzo/risoni pasta
a handful of chopped fennel or leek
vegetable stock, plus a parmesan rind for extra depth if you have one

steps.title: Chop the soup vegetables
steps.content: Dice onion, carrot and celery; cube the chicken.
steps.detail: Finely dice the onion, and cut the carrot and celery into small, even pieces so they soften at the same rate. Trim the chicken thighs of any big lumps of fat and cut into bite-sized chunks. Halve the lemon and set it aside for the end.
warnings: Keep the raw chicken and its board separate from the vegetables, and wash your hands after.

steps.title: Soften the base
steps.content: Sweat onion, carrot and celery in oil until soft.
steps.detail: Warm the oil in a large pot over medium and add the onion, carrot and celery with a pinch of salt. Cook gently for 6 to 8 minutes, stirring now and then, until the onion is soft and translucent and the kitchen smells sweet. You're building the backbone of the broth, so don't rush it or let it brown.

steps.title: Poach the chicken
steps.content: Add stock and chicken, simmer about 12 min.
steps.detail: Pour in the chicken stock, scraping the base of the pot, and add the chicken. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for about 12 minutes, skimming off any foam that rises, until the chicken is tender and cooked through — 74°C, with no pink at the centre of a piece.
warnings: Check the biggest piece is opaque all the way to the middle before moving on.

steps.title: Add the rice
steps.content: Stir in rice and simmer until tender.
steps.detail: Stir the rinsed rice into the simmering broth and cook for another 12 to 15 minutes, until the grains are soft and swollen. Keep an eye on the liquid — rice is thirsty, so top up with a cup of water or stock if it looks too thick for a soup.
tips: Give the bottom a stir every few minutes so the rice doesn't catch and stick.

steps.title: Finish with lemon
steps.content: Squeeze in lemon, season well and serve.
steps.detail: Take the pot off the heat and squeeze in the lemon — start with half and add more to taste; it should be bright and lively, not sour. Season well with salt and plenty of black pepper. Ladle into bowls and serve piping hot.

title: Chicken Ginger Fried Rice
description: Wok-hot fried rice, loud with fresh ginger and garlic, studded with chicken, sweet carrot and soft ribbons of egg. The best possible home for yesterday's rice.
storage: Keeps 1 day only in the fridge, well covered — cooked rice is best eaten promptly and reheated just once, until piping hot all the way through.
make_ahead: Cook the rice a day ahead and chill it — cold, dry rice fries far better than fresh, which turns to mush.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh for juicier meat, or leftover roast chicken
any cold cooked rice — basmati or long-grain both work
tamari for gluten-free, or a mix of soy and oyster sauce
half a brown onion, finely diced and cooked a little longer

steps.title: Prep everything first
steps.content: Dice chicken, grate ginger and garlic, slice veg, beat eggs.
steps.detail: Fried rice cooks in minutes and won't wait, so get everything ready first. Cut the chicken into small cubes. Peel and finely grate the ginger and garlic. Dice the carrot small and slice the spring onions, keeping the white parts and green tops separate. Beat the eggs in a small bowl.
warnings: Cut the raw chicken on its own board and wash your hands, knife and board before touching anything else.

steps.title: Scramble the eggs
steps.content: Cook the beaten egg until just set, then set aside.
steps.detail: Heat a tablespoon of the oil in the wok over high heat. Pour in the beaten eggs, let them set for a few seconds, then push and fold until just cooked but still soft. Tip them back into the bowl — they'll finish later and you don't want them rubbery.

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.content: Stir-fry chicken until golden and cooked through.
steps.detail: Add the last of the oil and the chicken to the hot wok, spread out in one layer. Leave it a minute to catch some colour, then stir-fry for 4 to 5 minutes until golden and cooked through — no pink remains and it reaches 74°C. Push it to one side of the wok.
warnings: Check a thick piece is white all the way through before you move on.

steps.title: Fire the aromatics and carrot
steps.content: Fry ginger, garlic, spring onion whites and carrot.
steps.detail: Add the grated ginger and garlic with the spring onion whites and diced carrot to the centre of the wok. Stir-fry for about a minute, until wildly fragrant and the carrot is just tender. Keep it all moving so the garlic and ginger don't scorch.
warnings: Ginger and garlic burn in seconds on high heat; if the wok smokes too hard, lift it off the flame for a moment.

steps.title: Fry the rice
steps.content: Add cold rice and toss hard over high heat.
steps.detail: Tip in the cold rice, breaking up any clumps with your spatula. Toss and press it against the hot wok for 3 to 4 minutes so it heats through and picks up a few toasty, chewy bits. The higher the heat, the more of that smoky wok character you get.
tips: Cold day-old rice is drier and fries into separate grains; fresh warm rice clumps and steams.

steps.title: Season and finish
steps.content: Return egg and chicken, splash soy, top with greens.
steps.detail: Return the chicken and egg to the wok, drizzle the soy sauce around the edge so it sizzles, and toss everything together. Season with a pinch of salt and pepper, taste, and add a little more soy if it needs it. Scatter over the green spring onion tops and serve straight from the wok.

title: Eggplant Miso Stir-Fry
description: Silky pan-fried eggplant glazed in a sweet-salty miso and soy, finished with fresh ginger and spring onion. Japanese comfort food over a bowl of steamed rice.
storage: Keeps 2 days in the fridge and reheats well. Store the rice separately and reheat it until piping hot.

substitutions.swap: white or red miso both work; red is deeper and saltier, so use a little less
tamari for a gluten-free version
zucchini or mushrooms, though they cook faster
honey or brown sugar for the glaze

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Simmer jasmine rice while you cook the eggplant.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice, then simmer it in 3 cups of water: bring to the boil, drop to the lowest heat with the lid on for 12 minutes, then rest off the heat, covered, for 5. It'll be ready just as the eggplant is done.

steps.title: Mix the miso glaze
steps.content: Stir miso, soy, sugar and water into a smooth sauce.
steps.detail: In a small bowl, stir the miso, soy sauce and sugar with 2 tablespoons of water until smooth. Having the glaze mixed and ready means you can pour it straight in without the eggplant overcooking. Grate the ginger and slice the spring onions now too.

steps.title: Fry the eggplant
steps.content: Cube and fry the eggplant until golden and soft.
steps.detail: Cut the eggplant into big bite-sized chunks. Heat the oil in a wide pan over medium-high and add the eggplant in a single layer. Fry, turning, for 8 to 10 minutes until deeply golden and completely soft inside — eggplant is only good properly cooked, silky rather than squeaky. Add a splash more oil if the pan dries out; it drinks it up.
tips: Don't crowd the pan or it steams instead of browning — cook in two batches if you need to.

steps.title: Glaze and toss
steps.content: Add ginger, pour in the glaze and toss to coat.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to medium, add the grated ginger and stir for a few seconds until fragrant, then pour in the miso glaze. It bubbles and thickens quickly — toss the eggplant to coat every piece in the glossy, sticky sauce, about a minute, then take it off the heat.
warnings: Miso and sugar scorch fast once the water cooks off — keep it moving and pull it off the heat as soon as it's glossy.

steps.title: Serve over rice
steps.content: Spoon over rice and scatter with spring onion.
steps.detail: Fluff the rested rice and divide between bowls. Spoon the glossy miso eggplant over the top and scatter with the sliced spring onions for a fresh, sharp bite against the rich glaze.

title: Chicken Mango Cucumber Salad
description: Juicy pan-fried chicken, sweet mango and crunchy cucumber tossed in a zingy lime dressing. A bright, fresh plate built for a hot day.
equipment: small jar or bowl for dressing
storage: Best assembled and eaten fresh while the cucumber is crisp. Keep the cooked chicken and cut fruit separately in the fridge for up to 2 days and toss together just before serving.

substitutions.swap: leftover roast or barbecue chicken, shredded — skip the cooking entirely
a just-ripe peach, or fresh pineapple
shredded cabbage or baby spinach
a pinch of chilli flakes, or leave it out for the kids

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.content: Pan-fry seasoned chicken breast until golden, then rest.
steps.detail: Pat the chicken dry and rub with a little oil, salt and pepper. Heat a frying pan over medium-high and cook the breasts for 4 to 5 minutes each side, until golden and cooked through to 74°C with no pink in the middle. Set aside on a board to rest while you build the salad — resting keeps it juicy.
warnings: Wash your hands, board and knife after handling raw chicken.
Don't slice it until it's rested and you've checked it's white all the way through.

steps.title: Make the lime dressing
steps.content: Whisk lime juice, olive oil and chilli into a dressing.
steps.detail: Squeeze the lime into a small jar or bowl and whisk in a couple of tablespoons of olive oil and a pinch of salt. Finely chop the chilli — scrape out the seeds for less heat — and stir it through. Taste: it should be sharp and bright; add a squeeze more lime if the mango is very sweet.

steps.title: Prep the salad
steps.content: Cut mango and cucumber, tear the lettuce, pick coriander.
steps.detail: Slice the cheeks off the mango and cut the flesh into strips or cubes. Halve the cucumber lengthways and cut into chunky half-moons. Tear or shred the lettuce into a large bowl and pick the coriander leaves off their stalks.

steps.title: Slice and toss
steps.content: Slice chicken, combine everything and dress to serve.
steps.detail: Slice the rested chicken across the grain into thick strips. Add it to the bowl with the mango, cucumber and most of the coriander, pour over the dressing and toss gently so everything is just coated. Pile onto plates, scatter with the last of the coriander and eat straight away while the cucumber is still crunchy.

title: Black Bean Corn Tacos
description: Smoky, spiced black beans and charred sweet corn piled into warm tortillas with fresh lime and coriander. A ten-minute vegetarian dinner that doesn't skimp on flavour.
storage: The bean and corn filling keeps 3 days in the fridge and reheats well. Warm the tortillas fresh — assembled tacos go soggy, so build them at the table.
leftovers: The spiced beans make a great topping for nachos or a jacket potato.

substitutions.swap: tinned kidney beans or brown lentils
tinned corn, drained, or kernels cut from a fresh cob
any capsicum, or diced zucchini
small flour or corn tortillas; soft or crisped hard-shell

steps.title: Char the corn and capsicum
steps.content: Fry corn and diced capsicum until blistered and golden.
steps.detail: Dice the capsicum small. Heat the oil in a frying pan over high heat and add the corn (straight from frozen is fine) and capsicum. Fry, stirring occasionally, for 4 to 5 minutes until the corn takes on golden char and the capsicum softens. Those toasty spots are where the flavour lives, so let it colour.
warnings: Frozen corn can spit as it hits the hot oil — stand back and use a lid as a shield if you need to.

steps.title: Spice the beans
steps.content: Add drained beans with cumin and smoked paprika.
steps.detail: Drain and rinse the black beans. Add them to the pan with the cumin, smoked paprika, a good pinch of salt and pepper. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes, mashing about a quarter of the beans against the pan so the filling holds together. Add a splash of water if it looks dry.

steps.title: Warm the tortillas
steps.content: Heat tortillas until soft, flexible and lightly toasted.
steps.detail: Warm the tortillas one or two at a time in a dry pan for about 20 seconds a side, until soft, flexible and toasted in spots. Stack them in a clean tea towel to keep warm and steam-soft. Cold tortillas crack when you fold them; warm ones fold like a dream.

steps.title: Dress the filling
steps.content: Squeeze in lime and stir through the coriander.
steps.detail: Take the pan off the heat and squeeze over half the lime, stirring it through the beans and corn. Roughly chop most of the coriander and fold it in. Taste and adjust — it should be smoky, a little sharp from the lime, and well seasoned.

steps.title: Build the tacos
steps.content: Fill the tortillas, top with coriander and lime wedges.
steps.detail: Spoon the warm bean and corn filling down the middle of each tortilla. Scatter with the remaining coriander and serve with the rest of the lime cut into wedges for squeezing. Let everyone fold their own at the table.

title: Ricotta Fig Honey Toast
description: Thick-cut toast under a cloud of lemony ricotta, torn fresh figs, toasted walnuts and a lazy drizzle of honey. Brunch that looks fancy and takes five minutes.
storage: Best assembled and eaten immediately, while the toast is crisp. The lemony ricotta can be mixed a day ahead and kept covered in the fridge.

substitutions.swap: smooth cottage cheese or mascarpone, or soft goat's cheese for tang
sliced pear, fresh peach or halved strawberries
toasted almonds, pecans or hazelnuts
maple syrup

steps.title: Toast the walnuts
steps.content: Toast walnuts in a dry pan until fragrant.
steps.detail: Roughly chop the walnuts and toast them in a dry frying pan over medium heat for 2 to 3 minutes, tossing, until they smell nutty and darken a shade. Tip them straight out of the pan so they don't keep cooking and catch. This tiny step is what makes them taste of something.
tips: Nuts go from golden to burnt in seconds — don't wander off.

steps.title: Whip the lemon ricotta
steps.content: Beat ricotta with lemon zest and a pinch of salt.
steps.detail: Spoon the ricotta into a bowl. Finely grate in the zest of the lemon, add a small pinch of salt and beat with a fork for a minute until it loosens and turns creamy and spreadable. A squeeze of the lemon juice sharpens it — add to taste.

steps.title: Toast the bread
steps.content: Toast the bread until deep golden and crisp.
steps.detail: Toast the bread until deeply golden and crisp — it needs to be sturdy enough to hold the toppings without going floppy. Thick-cut sourdough or a good seeded loaf is ideal. If you like, drizzle the hot toast with a little olive oil.

steps.title: Build the toast
steps.content: Spread ricotta, tear over figs, add walnuts and honey.
steps.detail: Spread the lemon ricotta thickly over each slice. Tear or slice the figs and arrange them on top, cut side up so they show off. Scatter with the toasted walnuts and drizzle generously with honey. Finish with a tiny crack of pepper if you like the sweet-savoury edge, and eat straight away.

title: Chicken Leek Puff Pie
description: Tender chicken and sweet, slow-softened leeks in a creamy thyme sauce under a lid of golden, shattering puff pastry. Proper cold-weather comfort.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge. Reheat in a hot oven, loosely covered with foil, so the pastry crisps up again rather than going soft in the microwave.
make_ahead: Make the filling a day ahead and chill it — cold filling is easier to top with pastry and helps it stay put. Assemble and bake when you're ready to eat.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast, or leftover roast chicken (skip the browning)
a couple of brown onions, sliced
milk, or a spoon of crème fraîche for a slight tang
shortcrust for a sturdier pie, or mashed potato for a cottage-pie lid

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.content: Sear diced chicken thigh until golden, then set aside.
steps.detail: Cut the chicken thighs into bite-sized pieces. Heat the oil in a large pan over medium-high and brown the chicken in batches, 3 to 4 minutes, until golden on the outside — it needn't be cooked through yet, as it finishes in the sauce. Lift out onto a plate.
warnings: Wash your hands, board and knife after the raw chicken, and don't crowd the pan or it steams instead of browning.

steps.title: Soften the leeks
steps.content: Sweat sliced leeks in butter with thyme until silky.
steps.detail: Slice the leeks into rounds and wash well — grit loves to hide between the layers. Melt the butter in the same pan over medium, add the leeks and thyme leaves with a pinch of salt, and cook gently for 6 to 8 minutes until soft, sweet and silky. Don't rush them to colour; you want them meltingly tender.

steps.title: Make the creamy sauce
steps.content: Stir in flour, then stock and cream, and return chicken.
steps.detail: Scatter the flour over the leeks and stir for a minute to cook out the raw taste. Slowly pour in the stock, stirring, then the cream, and let it bubble into a thick, glossy sauce that coats the back of a spoon. Return the chicken and its juices, simmer 5 minutes until cooked through, then season well with salt and pepper and let it cool a little.

steps.title: Top with pastry
steps.content: Fill the dish and lay over the puff pastry, sealed.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan-forced. Spoon the filling into a pie dish. Lay the puff pastry over the top, trim the overhang and press the edges onto the rim to seal. Cut two small slits in the centre for steam to escape, then brush all over with a little cream for a glossy, golden finish.

steps.title: Bake until golden
steps.content: Bake 25–30 min until puffed and deep gold, then rest.
steps.detail: Bake for 25 to 30 minutes, until the pastry is puffed, crisp and a deep golden brown and you can see the filling bubbling at the slits. If the top browns too fast, lay a sheet of foil loosely over it. Rest 5 minutes before serving so the filling settles.
warnings: Resist opening the oven in the first 20 minutes — a blast of cool air can stop the puff pastry rising.

title: Apple Cinnamon Loaf
description: A moist, tender tea loaf packed with soft cinnamon apple and fanned with fruit across the top. The kind of thing you slice thick, butter warm and eat far too much of.
storage: Keeps 4 days in an airtight container at room temperature, or up to a week in the fridge. It freezes well — slice first, then thaw a piece at a time.
make_ahead: Bakes beautifully a day ahead; the flavour deepens and the crumb firms up overnight.
leftovers: Day-old slices are gorgeous fried in butter like French toast, or toasted and buttered.

substitutions.swap: self-raising flour; if so, leave out the baking powder
brown sugar for a deeper, caramel note
125 ml vegetable oil for a lighter crumb
firm pear, or a mix of apple and pear

steps.title: Prep tin and oven
steps.content: Heat oven to 160°C fan-forced and grease and line a loaf tin.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 160°C fan-forced. Grease a loaf tin with a little butter and line it with baking paper, leaving some overhanging the long sides as handles to lift the loaf out later. A moderate oven is right here — too hot and the outside sets before the middle cooks.

steps.title: Prep the apples
steps.content: Dice one apple, slice the other for the top.
steps.detail: Peel and core the apples. Cut one into small dice to fold through the batter, and slice the other into thin half-moons to fan across the top. Toss the slices with a little of the cinnamon so they perfume the crust as it bakes.

steps.title: Whisk the wet mix
steps.content: Whisk melted butter, sugar, eggs and vanilla until smooth.
steps.detail: In a large bowl, whisk the melted butter and sugar together, then beat in the eggs one at a time and the vanilla. Whisk for a minute until smooth and slightly paler — this dissolves the sugar and gives the loaf a fine, even crumb.

steps.title: Fold in the dry mix
steps.content: Fold through flour, baking powder, cinnamon and apple.
steps.detail: Add the flour, baking powder, the rest of the cinnamon and a pinch of salt. Fold gently with a spoon just until no dry streaks remain — overmixing makes it tough and rubbery. Fold the diced apple through last.
tips: A few small lumps in the batter are fine; a smooth, over-beaten batter bakes dense.

steps.title: Bake and cool
steps.content: Top with apple slices, bake 50–55 min, then cool.
steps.detail: Scrape the batter into the tin, level it and fan the reserved apple slices over the top. Bake for 50 to 55 minutes, until risen, golden and a skewer pushed into the centre comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then lift out by the paper and cool at least another 20 before slicing — warm loaf crumbles, cooled loaf slices clean.
warnings: Keep the oven door shut for the first 40 minutes; opening it early can make the loaf sink in the middle.

title: Mango Passionfruit Pavlova
description: A shortcut pavlova — crisp meringue nests piled with softly whipped vanilla cream and a tumble of mango, kiwi and passionfruit. Summer on a plate, no oven required.
storage: Assemble just before serving — meringue turns soft and weeps within an hour of meeting the cream. The whipped cream can be made a couple of hours ahead and kept in the fridge; cut the fruit fresh.

substitutions.swap: whipped coconut cream for a dairy-free version
sliced strawberries or banana
tinned mango cheeks, drained, when out of season
a tub of passionfruit pulp from the fridge aisle

steps.title: Whip the vanilla cream
steps.content: Beat cream, icing sugar and vanilla to soft peaks.
steps.detail: Pour the cold cream into a large bowl with the icing sugar and vanilla. Beat until it holds soft, floppy peaks — thick enough to hold its shape but still silky, not stiff. Stop the moment it thickens; over-whipped cream turns grainy and buttery.
tips: Cold cream in a cold bowl whips faster and holds better — chill the bowl if it's a hot day.

steps.title: Prep the fruit
steps.content: Dice mango, slice kiwi, scoop out passionfruit.
steps.detail: Slice the cheeks from the mango and cut the flesh into cubes. Peel the kiwi and slice into rounds. Halve the passionfruit and scoop the pulp and seeds into a small bowl — that tart, fragrant pulp is what lifts the whole thing.

steps.title: Fill the meringue nests
steps.content: Spoon the whipped cream into the nests.
steps.detail: Set the meringue nests on plates or a serving platter. Spoon the whipped cream generously into the hollow of each one, mounding it up. Do this at the last minute so the meringue stays crisp and crunchy underneath.

steps.title: Crown with fruit
steps.content: Pile on mango and kiwi, spoon over passionfruit.
steps.detail: Tumble the mango and kiwi over the cream, then spoon the passionfruit pulp across the top so the seeds and juice run down the sides. Serve straight away, while the meringue still shatters under the spoon.

title: Eggplant Tomato Mozzarella Pasta
description: Golden pan-fried eggplant folded through a quick garlic-tomato sauce, then finished with melting mozzarella and torn basil. A pasta alla Norma at heart.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Reheat gently with a splash of water; the mozzarella firms up cold but softens again with heat.
make_ahead: The tomato and eggplant sauce keeps 2 days — reheat it while the pasta boils and fold the mozzarella through fresh.
leftovers: Pile cold leftovers into a baking dish, top with extra cheese and grill into a bubbling pasta bake.

substitutions.swap: zucchini, cut a little thicker so it doesn't collapse
a handful of grated cheddar, or torn bocconcini
spaghetti, penne or rigatoni — anything that catches sauce

steps.title: Salt the eggplant
steps.content: Cube the eggplant, salt it and rest 10 minutes.
steps.detail: Cut the eggplant into 2cm cubes and toss with a good pinch of salt in the colander. Leave it 10 minutes to sweat out bitter moisture — you'll see beads of water on the surface. Pat the cubes dry with a tea towel; drier eggplant drinks less oil and fries far more golden.

steps.title: Fry the eggplant
steps.content: Fry eggplant in olive oil until deep golden, 8 minutes.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a deep frying pan over medium-high until it shimmers. Add the eggplant in a single layer and fry, turning, for about 8 minutes until deep golden and soft to the middle. It drinks the oil at first, then releases it again as it browns, so resist adding more. Scoop out onto a plate.
warnings: Hot oil spits — lower the eggplant in gently and stand back a little.

steps.title: Start the sauce
steps.content: Soften the garlic, then add the tomatoes.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to medium and, if the pan looks dry, add a little more oil. Slice the garlic and cook it just 30 seconds until fragrant — don't let it colour or it turns bitter. Tip in the tinned tomatoes, crushing them against the pan with your spoon, and season with salt and pepper.
warnings: Garlic scorches in seconds — keep it moving and drop the heat the moment it smells nutty.

steps.title: Simmer the tomato
steps.content: Simmer 12 minutes, then return the eggplant.
steps.detail: Let the sauce bubble gently for about 12 minutes, stirring now and then, until it thickens and turns jammy and glossy rather than watery. Fold the fried eggplant back in for the last couple of minutes to warm through and soak up the sauce.
tips: A splash of water swirled around the empty tin and tipped in rinses out every last bit of tomato.

steps.title: Boil the pasta
steps.content: Cook the pasta in well-salted water until al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it heavily — it should taste like the sea. Add the pasta and cook 1 minute under the packet time so it stays al dente. Scoop out a mug of the starchy water before draining; don't rinse the pasta.

steps.title: Toss and melt
steps.content: Fold pasta through the sauce with mozzarella and basil.
steps.detail: Tip the drained pasta into the sauce with a splash of the reserved water and toss over low heat. Scatter over the grated mozzarella and most of the torn basil, tossing until the cheese pulls into soft strands through the pasta. Loosen with more pasta water if it looks tight, then finish with the last of the basil and serve straight away.
tips: Serve it the moment the cheese melts — mozzarella turns rubbery if it sits and cools.

title: Paneer Tomato Cashew Curry
description: Soft paneer simmered in a silky tomato and cashew sauce, warm with spice and scattered with coriander. Rich and creamy without a drop of cream.
equipment: large frying pan or wide pot
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge and the flavour deepens overnight. Reheat gently with a splash of water. Store cooked rice separately and use it within a day.
make_ahead: The sauce is better made a day ahead — hold the paneer and coriander until you reheat and add them fresh.
leftovers: Spoon cold curry with rice into a wrap and toast it for a next-day lunch.

substitutions.swap: firm tofu or haloumi, pan-fried first for golden edges
a good splash of thickened cream or coconut milk for the same silkiness
jasmine rice, or warm naan to scoop

steps.title: Cook the basmati
steps.content: Rinse and steam the rice, then leave it covered.
steps.detail: Rinse the basmati under cold water until it runs almost clear, then tip into a saucepan with 2 1/4 cups water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, clamp on the lid and drop to the lowest heat for 12 minutes. Take it off the heat and let it sit, still covered, for 5 minutes before fluffing with a fork.
tips: Don't lift the lid while it steams — you'll let out the trapped steam that finishes cooking the grains.

steps.title: Blend a cashew cream
steps.content: Soak the cashews, then blend to a smooth paste.
steps.detail: Cover the cashews with just-boiled water and soak 10 minutes to soften. Drain, then blend with a splash of fresh water into a completely smooth, pourable paste. This is what gives the curry its creamy body without any cream.

steps.title: Fry the aromatics
steps.content: Cook garlic and ginger, then toast the curry powder.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a wide pan over medium. Add the grated garlic and ginger and fry for a minute until fragrant, then stir in the curry powder and cook 30 seconds until it smells warm and nutty. Add a splash of water if it starts to catch on the base.
warnings: Garlic, ginger and dry spices burn fast — keep them moving and lower the heat if they darken.

steps.title: Build the tomato sauce
steps.content: Simmer the tomatoes, then stir in the cashew cream.
steps.detail: Tip in the tinned tomatoes, crushing them with the spoon, and season with salt. Simmer 10 minutes until they darken and the oil starts to split from the sauce. Stir through the cashew cream to turn it velvety and pale, loosening with a little water to a spoon-coating sauce.

steps.title: Simmer the paneer
steps.content: Add the paneer cubes and warm through, 5 minutes.
steps.detail: Cut the paneer into 2cm cubes and slide them into the sauce. Simmer gently for 5 minutes so they soften and soak up flavour — keep it to a lazy bubble, as a hard boil can make paneer squeaky and tough. Taste and adjust the salt.
tips: For extra texture, pan-fry the paneer cubes in a little oil until golden before adding them.

steps.title: Scatter coriander and serve
steps.content: Stir through coriander and spoon over the basmati.
steps.detail: Stir half the chopped coriander through the curry, then spoon it over the fluffed basmati. Scatter the rest of the coriander on top. A squeeze of lemon or a spoon of yoghurt over each bowl lifts it beautifully.

title: Sweet Potato Peanut Soup
description: A West African-inspired bowl of sweet potato simmered soft and blended smooth with peanut butter and coconut milk, brightened with lime and coriander.
storage: Keeps 4 days in the fridge or freezes for 3 months. Reheat gently, thinning with a splash of water or stock as it thickens on standing.
make_ahead: Even better the next day once the flavours settle, so it's a fine one to make ahead.
leftovers: Loosen with a little stock and toss it through noodles, or use it as a satay-style sauce over grilled chicken.

substitutions.swap: pumpkin or butternut, cut to the same size
any nut butter, or tahini for a nut-free version
evaporated milk or thickened cream if you're not keeping it vegan

steps.title: Chop the sweet potato
steps.content: Peel and cube the sweet potato; dice onion and garlic.
steps.detail: Peel the sweet potato and cut it into even 3cm cubes so it cooks at the same rate. Dice the onion and mince the garlic, keeping the garlic to one side — it goes in later and burns faster than the onion.

steps.title: Soften the onion
steps.content: Fry onion until soft, then add the garlic.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a large pot over medium and cook the onion for about 5 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the garlic for the last minute, just until fragrant.
warnings: Don't let the garlic brown — scorched garlic will turn the whole soup bitter.

steps.title: Simmer the sweet potato
steps.content: Add sweet potato and stock, simmer 15 minutes.
steps.detail: Tip in the sweet potato and vegetable stock and season with salt and pepper. Bring to the boil, then simmer for 15 minutes until the sweet potato is completely fork-tender — a piece should fall apart when pressed against the side of the pot.

steps.title: Blend in peanut and coconut
steps.content: Blend smooth with peanut butter and coconut milk.
steps.detail: Take the pot off the heat, add the peanut butter and most of the coconut milk, and blend until completely smooth and velvety — a stick blender in the pot is easiest. Return it to low heat to warm through.
warnings: Blitzing hot soup can spit and scald — if you use a jug blender, fill it only halfway and hold the lid down with a folded cloth.

steps.title: Brighten and serve
steps.content: Finish with lime, coriander and a swirl of coconut.
steps.detail: Squeeze in the lime juice to cut the richness and taste for salt. Ladle into bowls, swirl over the last of the coconut milk and scatter with coriander. A pinch of chilli or a dash of hot sauce adds a gentle heat if you like it.

title: Mushroom Miso Fried Rice
description: Cold rice fried hard in a hot wok with meaty golden mushrooms, a savoury hit of miso and softly scrambled egg. The best possible use for last night's rice.
storage: Best eaten fresh. Keeps 1 day only in the fridge — cooked rice doesn't keep longer — and should be reheated once, until piping hot right through.
make_ahead: Cook the rice the day before and chill it; cold, dry rice fries into separate grains far better than fresh, sticky rice.

substitutions.swap: any mushroom — button, Swiss brown or shiitake for extra savour
an extra splash of soy sauce if you have no miso
any cold leftover rice; basmati or brown both work

ingredients.amount: 3 cups cooked and cold (about 1 1/2 cups raw)

steps.title: Ready the rice and mushrooms
steps.content: Break up the cold rice and slice everything.
steps.detail: Break up any clumps of cold rice with wet fingers so the grains separate. Slice the mushrooms thickly — they shrink a lot — and slice the spring onions, keeping the white and green parts apart. Mince the garlic, and loosen the miso in a tablespoon of water so it spreads evenly. Fried rice moves fast, so have it all lined up by the wok.

steps.title: Scramble the egg
steps.content: Softly scramble the eggs, then set aside.
steps.detail: Heat a teaspoon of the oil in the wok over high heat. Pour in the beaten eggs, let them set for 10 seconds, then fold into soft, glossy curds and pull them out while still a touch underdone — they'll finish cooking when they go back in.

steps.title: Brown the mushrooms
steps.content: Fry mushrooms hard until deep golden, 5 minutes.
steps.detail: Add the rest of the oil and tip in the mushrooms. Spread them out and leave them to colour before stirring — cook about 5 minutes until deep golden and their water has cooked off, which is where the savoury flavour lives. Add the garlic and spring onion whites for the last 30 seconds.

steps.title: Fry the rice
steps.content: Add the rice and press it against the hot wok.
steps.detail: Tip in the cold rice, spread it out and press it against the hot wok, letting it sit before each toss so some grains toast and turn slightly chewy. Keep the heat high and toss for 3 to 4 minutes.

steps.title: Season and finish
steps.content: Stir through miso, soy, egg and sesame oil.
steps.detail: Pour the loosened miso and the soy sauce down the side of the wok and toss to coat every grain. Return the egg, add the spring onion greens, and finish with a drizzle of sesame oil off the heat. Taste and add pepper.
tips: Taste before adding any salt — the miso and soy are already doing that job.

title: Duck Orange Stir-Fry
description: Crisp-skinned duck, seared and sliced, tossed with bok choy in a glossy orange-soy glaze over steamed rice. A takeaway favourite made properly at home.
storage: Keeps 2 days in the fridge. Reheat the duck briefly and gently, as hard reheating turns it chewy. Cooked rice keeps 1 day only.
make_ahead: Steam the rice ahead; you can sear and slice the duck up to a day before and warm it through in the finished glaze to serve.
leftovers: Shred any cold duck into a wrap, or scatter it over a noodle salad.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh — cook it right through to 74C rather than leaving it pink
broccolini, snow peas or any quick-cooking green
a spoon of brown sugar or maple syrup

steps.title: Steam the rice
steps.content: Rinse and steam the jasmine rice, then rest it.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice until the water runs fairly clear, then add to a saucepan with 2 1/4 cups water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, lid on, lowest heat for 12 minutes, then rest off the heat for 5 minutes and fluff with a fork.

steps.title: Score and sear the duck
steps.content: Render the skin in a cold pan until crisp, 8 minutes.
steps.detail: Pat the duck dry and score the skin in a criss-cross, cutting into the fat but not the meat; season with salt and pepper. Lay the breasts skin-side down in a cold, dry pan, then turn the heat to medium — starting cold slowly renders the fat so the skin crisps and turns deep golden, about 8 minutes. Flip and cook 2 to 3 minutes more.
tips: Spoon off the rendered fat as it pools and save it — it makes the crispest roast potatoes.
warnings: Wash your hands, board and knife after handling the raw duck, and keep it away from the rice and greens.

steps.title: Rest and slice
steps.content: Rest the duck 5 minutes, then slice thin.
steps.detail: Move the duck to a board and rest it for 5 minutes — it should feel springy, and the centre stays rosy, which is exactly how duck breast is meant to be. Slice it across into thin strips.

steps.title: Make the orange glaze
steps.content: Reduce orange juice, soy and honey to a glaze.
steps.detail: Pour off all but a teaspoon of the duck fat and set the pan over medium. Fry the ginger and garlic for 30 seconds, then add the juice of both oranges, the soy sauce, honey and a little grated orange zest. Bubble for 3 to 4 minutes until syrupy and glossy enough to coat a spoon.
warnings: Ginger and garlic scorch in hot duck fat — keep them moving from the moment they hit the pan.

steps.title: Wilt the bok choy
steps.content: Add bok choy and toss until just wilted.
steps.detail: Cut the bok choy into wedges and add to the glaze with a small splash of water. Toss for 1 to 2 minutes until the stems are crisp-tender and the leaves have just wilted and turned glossy.

steps.title: Glaze the duck and serve
steps.content: Return the duck, toss in the glaze, serve over rice.
steps.detail: Slide the sliced duck and any juices from the board back into the pan and toss quickly just to coat in the sticky glaze — don't cook it further or it will toughen. Pile it over the steamed rice with the bok choy.

title: Prawn Avocado Lime Salad
description: Quick pan-seared prawns over crisp lettuce and cucumber with buttery avocado, all lifted by a sharp lime and chilli dressing. Fresh, fast and summery.
storage: Best assembled and eaten fresh, as a dressed salad wilts within the hour. Keep cooked prawns and cut veg separate in the fridge for up to a day. Cooked prawns keep 1 to 2 days.
make_ahead: Cook the prawns and mix the dressing a few hours ahead; keep the avocado whole and slice it only at the last minute.
leftovers: Any spare cooked prawns are lovely folded into a wrap with the leftover salad the next day.

substitutions.swap: pre-cooked prawns — just warm them through, or serve them cold
rocket or baby spinach
a pinch of chilli flakes, or leave it out for the kids

steps.title: Make the lime dressing
steps.content: Whisk lime juice, olive oil, chilli and garlic.
steps.detail: In a small bowl, whisk the juice of one lime with the olive oil, finely chopped chilli (seeds out for less heat), a small grated garlic clove, and salt and pepper. Taste it — it should be sharp, savoury and a little fiery. Set aside so the flavours mingle.

steps.title: Prep the salad
steps.content: Tear the lettuce; slice cucumber and avocado.
steps.detail: Wash and tear the lettuce into a large bowl, slice the cucumber into half-moons, and halve, stone and slice the avocado just before serving so it stays green. Pile it all loosely so the dressing can find its way through.
tips: A squeeze of the second lime over the cut avocado keeps it from browning.

steps.title: Sear the prawns
steps.content: Sear prawns in a hot pan until pink, 2 to 3 minutes.
steps.detail: Pat the prawns dry and get a pan hot with a little oil. Lay them in a single layer and cook 1 to 2 minutes a side until they turn pink and opaque and curl into a loose C — a tight O means they've gone too far. They cook fast, so don't wander off.
warnings: Wash your hands and board after handling raw prawns, and never return the cooked prawns to the raw plate.

steps.title: Dress and serve
steps.content: Top the salad with prawns, dressing and coriander.
steps.detail: Scatter the warm prawns over the salad and spoon the lime dressing over everything. Finish with torn coriander and the remaining lime cut into wedges. Serve straight away, while the warm prawns meet the cool, crisp salad.

title: Beef Mince Cheese Tacos
description: Smoky cumin-and-paprika beef mince piled into warm tortillas with melty cheddar, crunchy lettuce and fresh tomato. Friday night sorted.
storage: The mince keeps 3 days in the fridge or freezes for 3 months. Store the components separately and warm the tortillas fresh each time.
make_ahead: Make the mince ahead — it reheats brilliantly — and prep the toppings to keep chilled and ready.
leftovers: Spare mince makes a fast nacho topping, or a burrito bowl spooned over rice.

substitutions.swap: pork, chicken or lamb mince — or tinned beans for a meat-free version
any melting cheese, or crumbled feta for a tangy hit
hard taco shells, or soft wraps

steps.title: Chop the toppings
steps.content: Shred lettuce; dice tomato and onion.
steps.detail: Finely shred the lettuce and dice the tomato, seeds and all. Finely dice the onion. Keep the lettuce and tomato aside for topping — the onion goes into the pan with the mince.

steps.title: Brown the mince
steps.content: Fry the beef mince until deep brown, 8 minutes.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a pan over medium-high and add the mince in big chunks. Leave it to sear untouched before breaking it up, and cook about 8 minutes until no pink remains and the base of the pan is browned and sticky — that's pure flavour. Tip off any big pool of fat.
warnings: Wash your hands and anything the raw mince touched, and cook it right through with no pink left.

steps.title: Soften the onion
steps.content: Add the onion and cook until soft.
steps.detail: Stir the diced onion into the mince and cook 4 to 5 minutes until soft and golden, scraping the sticky browned bits off the base of the pan as it releases moisture.

steps.title: Spice the mince
steps.content: Stir in cumin and paprika with a splash of water.
steps.detail: Stir in the cumin and smoked paprika and cook for a minute until fragrant. Add a splash of water and let it bubble down into a glossy, clingy sauce. Season well with salt and pepper.
tips: A squeeze of tomato or a dash of hot sauce loosens the mince and lifts the flavour.

steps.title: Warm the tortillas
steps.content: Warm tortillas in a dry pan until soft.
steps.detail: Heat each tortilla in a dry pan (or straight over a gas flame) for 15 to 20 seconds a side until soft, pliable and lightly charred in spots. Stack them in a clean tea towel to keep warm and steam-soft.

steps.title: Build the tacos
steps.content: Fill with mince, cheese and salad.
steps.detail: Spoon the mince down the middle of each warm tortilla and scatter the cheddar over while it's hot so it starts to melt. Pile on the lettuce and tomato, fold, and eat over a plate.

title: Rosemary Chicken Potato Tray
description: One tray, minimal fuss — chicken thighs and potatoes roasted golden with rosemary, garlic and lemon, with green beans tumbled in at the end.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge. Reheat in a hot oven to re-crisp rather than the microwave. Cooked chicken keeps 3 days.
make_ahead: Chop the potatoes and season the chicken a few hours ahead, kept covered in the fridge, ready to hit the tray.
leftovers: Strip leftover chicken and potato into a frittata, or over a salad with the soft roasted garlic mashed into the dressing.

substitutions.swap: drumsticks, or chicken breast — breast needs a little less time
sweet potato or pumpkin, cut a touch larger
broccoli, asparagus or halved Brussels sprouts

steps.title: Heat oven and chop
steps.content: Heat oven to 200C fan; cut potatoes into chunks.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200C fan (220C conventional). Cut the potatoes into 3cm chunks so they roast quickly, strip and roughly chop the rosemary, smash the garlic cloves with their skins on, and cut the lemon into wedges.

steps.title: Start the potatoes
steps.content: Roast the potatoes on their own for 15 minutes.
steps.detail: Toss the potatoes on a large tray with half the oil, salt and pepper, and spread them out in a single layer — crowding steams them instead of crisping. Roast for 15 minutes to give the slower spuds a head start.

steps.title: Season the chicken
steps.content: Coat chicken in oil, rosemary and lemon.
steps.detail: While the potatoes start, toss the chicken thighs with the rest of the oil, the chopped rosemary, a squeeze of lemon, and salt and pepper. If your thighs have skin, pat it dry so it crisps in the oven.
warnings: Wash your hands and board after handling the raw chicken, and keep it clear of anything you'll eat raw.

steps.title: Add chicken and roast
steps.content: Nestle chicken among the potatoes, roast 25 minutes.
steps.detail: Pull out the tray and turn the potatoes. Nestle the chicken skin-side up among them, tucking in the smashed garlic and lemon wedges, and roast for 25 minutes.

steps.title: Add the beans
steps.content: Scatter in green beans, roast 8 minutes more.
steps.detail: Trim the green beans and tumble them onto the tray. Roast a final 8 minutes until the beans are tender-crisp and the chicken is cooked through — the juices run clear and it reaches 74C at the thickest point.
warnings: Check the chicken is 74C at the thickest part before serving; cut into a piece if you're unsure.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.content: Rest 5 minutes, then squeeze over the roast lemon.
steps.detail: Rest the tray for 5 minutes so the juices settle back into the meat. Squeeze the softened roasted lemon over everything and spoon up the garlicky pan juices — the roasted garlic squeezes out of its skin like a sweet paste.

title: Beef Mushroom Shortcrust Pie
description: A proper pub pie — chunks of beef and mushroom braised in a rich, thyme-scented gravy under a golden, crisp shortcrust lid.
equipment: large heavy pot or casserole with lid
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge. Reheat in the oven at 180C to keep the pastry crisp. The filling alone freezes for 3 months.
make_ahead: The filling is better made a day ahead — chill it, then top and bake fresh. A cold filling also tops far more neatly.
leftovers: Leftover filling is a ready-made base for a shepherd's-style bake under a layer of mash.

substitutions.swap: beef chuck, gravy beef or brisket — anything that loves a slow braise
puff pastry for a flakier lid
leave out, or add carrot and peas for a classic mix

steps.title: Brown the beef
steps.content: Toss beef in flour and sear in batches.
steps.detail: Cut the beef into 3cm chunks, pat dry, and toss with the flour, salt and pepper. Sear in hot oil in batches until deep brown on all sides, then set aside — the flour clinging to the meat will thicken the gravy later.
tips: Browning in batches is worth it — a crowded pot steams the meat grey instead of building a crust.
warnings: Wash your hands and board after handling the raw beef.

steps.title: Fry onion and mushroom
steps.content: Brown the onion and mushrooms, 6 minutes.
steps.detail: In the same pot, cook the diced onion and sliced mushrooms for about 6 minutes until softened and golden and the mushroom water has cooked away. Scrape up the browned bits from the base as you go.

steps.title: Build the gravy
steps.content: Stir in tomato paste, stock and thyme.
steps.detail: Stir the tomato paste through for a minute until it darkens and smells rich. Return the beef, pour in the beef stock and add the thyme, scraping the base clean, then bring to a gentle simmer.

steps.title: Braise until tender
steps.content: Simmer low for 75 minutes until the beef is soft.
steps.detail: Cover and simmer very gently for about 75 minutes, until a chunk of beef crushes easily against the side of the pot. Uncover and let it reduce until the gravy is thick and glossy enough to coat the meat — a runny filling makes for a soggy pie. Cool it before topping.

steps.title: Top with pastry
steps.content: Cover the cooled filling with shortcrust and vent it.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200C fan (220C conventional). Spoon the cooled filling into the pie dish and lay the shortcrust sheet over the top. Trim the overhang, press the edges to the rim with a fork to seal, and cut two small slits in the lid for steam to escape. Brush with a little oil for shine.
tips: The filling must be cool, or it melts the pastry into a greasy layer before it can crisp up. A beaten egg brushed on gives an even glossier top.

steps.title: Bake the pie
steps.content: Bake 30 minutes until deep golden.
steps.detail: Bake for 30 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and crisp and the gravy bubbles up through the vents. Rest it 5 minutes so the filling settles before you cut in.
warnings: The filling is molten straight from the oven — let it rest before serving so no one gets a scald.

title: Banana Chocolate Chip Loaf
description: A soft, moist banana loaf shot through with melting chocolate chips. The easiest, most forgiving way to use up spotty bananas.
storage: Keeps 4 days in an airtight container, or freeze the slices well-wrapped for up to 3 months. It actually moistens and improves on day two.
make_ahead: Bakes and keeps beautifully — a day-ahead loaf is even more moist and tender.
leftovers: Thick slices are gorgeous toasted and spread with butter, or warmed with a scoop of ice cream.

substitutions.swap: the same weight of olive or vegetable oil for a dairy-light loaf
chopped walnuts, pecans or dark chocolate
brown sugar for a deeper, caramel note
self-raising flour — just leave out the baking powder

steps.title: Heat oven and line the tin
steps.content: Heat oven to 160C fan; grease and line a loaf tin.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 160C fan (180C conventional). Lightly grease the loaf tin with a little butter and line it with baking paper, leaving an overhang on the long sides so you can lift the loaf out later.

steps.title: Mash the bananas
steps.content: Mash the ripe bananas until fairly smooth.
steps.detail: Peel the bananas and mash them in a large bowl with a fork until fairly smooth — a few lumps are welcome and keep the loaf moist. The riper and browner the bananas, the sweeter and more fragrant the loaf.

steps.title: Mix the batter
steps.content: Beat in melted butter, sugar and eggs.
steps.detail: Stir the melted butter and sugar into the mashed banana, then beat in the eggs one at a time until the mixture is glossy and well combined.

steps.title: Fold in flour and chips
steps.content: Fold through flour, baking powder, salt and chocolate.
steps.detail: Add the flour, baking powder and a pinch of salt, and fold gently with a spoon just until no dry streaks remain — overmixing develops the gluten and makes the loaf tough. Fold most of the chocolate chips through, saving a few for the top.
tips: Toss the chips in a little of the flour first so they don't all sink to the bottom.

steps.title: Bake and cool
steps.content: Bake 50 to 55 minutes, then cool before slicing.
steps.detail: Scrape the batter into the tin, scatter the reserved chips on top, and bake 50 to 55 minutes until risen, golden, and a skewer poked into the middle comes out clean (or with a little melted chocolate, but no wet batter). Cool in the tin 10 minutes, then lift out by the paper and cool until at least warm before slicing.
warnings: Don't open the oven in the first 40 minutes, or the loaf can sink in the middle.

title: Poached Pear Saffron Fruit
description: Whole pears poached until glassy and tender in a golden saffron and vanilla syrup, served with soft mascarpone and toasted almonds. A quietly elegant dessert.
storage: The pears keep 3 to 4 days submerged in their syrup in an airtight container in the fridge, and only get better. Keep the mascarpone chilled and add it just before serving.
make_ahead: An ideal make-ahead dessert — poach the pears a day or two ahead and chill them in the syrup, then toast the almonds and assemble to serve.
leftovers: Slice cold poached pears over porridge or yoghurt, or fold them through the mascarpone for a quick trifle.

substitutions.swap: firm apples or quinces, though quinces need longer to soften
thick Greek yoghurt, softly whipped cream or vanilla ice cream
toasted pistachios, pine nuts or hazelnuts

steps.title: Make the saffron syrup
steps.content: Warm water, sugar, saffron, vanilla and lemon.
steps.detail: In a saucepan just big enough to hold the pears standing up, combine about 750ml water with the sugar, saffron, vanilla, a couple of strips of lemon peel and a squeeze of the juice. Warm over medium, stirring until the sugar dissolves, and let the saffron bloom into a golden syrup.
tips: For a grown-up version, swap half the water for white wine.

steps.title: Peel the pears
steps.content: Peel the pears, keeping the stalks on.
steps.detail: Peel the pears smoothly from top to bottom, leaving the stalks on for looks, and slice a thin sliver off each base so they stand upright. Rub them all over with the cut lemon to stop them browning while you work.
tips: Choose firm, slightly under-ripe pears — soft, ripe ones turn to mush in the syrup.

steps.title: Poach until tender
steps.content: Simmer the pears 20 to 25 minutes until glassy.
steps.detail: Lower the pears into the syrup and cover them with a round of baking paper to keep them submerged. Poach on the barest simmer for 20 to 25 minutes, turning now and then, until a skewer slides in with no resistance and the flesh looks translucent. Ripeness changes the timing, so go by feel.

steps.title: Reduce the syrup
steps.content: Lift out the pears, boil the syrup to a glaze.
steps.detail: Lift the pears out gently with a slotted spoon. Turn up the heat and boil the syrup for 5 to 8 minutes until it reduces to a light, glossy glaze that coats a spoon — remember it thickens further as it cools.
warnings: Sugar syrup is far hotter than boiling water and sticks to skin — never taste it straight from the pan.

steps.title: Toast the almonds
steps.content: Toast the flaked almonds until golden.
steps.detail: In a dry pan over medium heat, toss the flaked almonds for 2 to 3 minutes until golden and smelling nutty. Tip them straight out of the hot pan the moment they colour.
warnings: Nuts go from golden to burnt in seconds — keep them moving and don't walk away.

steps.title: Serve with mascarpone
steps.content: Plate pears with mascarpone, syrup and almonds.
steps.detail: Sit each pear on a generous spoonful of mascarpone, drizzle the saffron glaze over and around, and scatter with the toasted almonds. Lovely served warm or well chilled.
tips: Loosen the mascarpone with a little of the cooled syrup and a drop of vanilla for a softer, sweeter cream.

title: Chorizo Capsicum Spaghetti
description: Smoky chorizo fried until its orange oil slicks the pan, sweet red capsicum and a quick tinned-tomato sauce clinging to every strand.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Reheat gently with a splash of water; the sauce alone freezes well (without the pasta) for up to 3 months.
make_ahead: The sauce can be made a day ahead and reheated while the spaghetti boils.
leftovers: Fold cold leftovers into beaten egg and fry as a crisp pasta frittata for lunch.

substitutions.swap: a good pork snag squeezed out of its skin, or spicy salami
any long pasta — linguine or pappardelle work a treat

steps.title: Slice the chorizo and veg
steps.content: Slice chorizo into coins, dice capsicum, mince garlic, chop parsley.
steps.detail: Cut the chorizo into 5mm coins so they can crisp. Halve, deseed and dice the capsicum into 1cm pieces, mince the garlic, and roughly chop the parsley — keep the parsley aside for the very end so it stays fresh and green. Chorizo and garlic both catch fast, so having everything ready means you're not scrambling once the pan is hot.

steps.title: Crisp the chorizo
steps.content: Fry chorizo coins until crisp and the pan floods with orange oil.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a large pan over medium and add the chorizo coins. Let them sizzle for 3-4 min, turning once, until the edges crisp and the pan floods with smoky orange oil — that oil is the flavour base for the whole dish. Keep the heat moderate; too high and the outsides scorch before the fat renders.
tips: Chorizo is oily, so go easy on the added oil — you can always add a little more.

steps.title: Soften the capsicum
steps.content: Soften the capsicum, then add garlic and chilli flakes.
steps.detail: Tip the diced capsicum into the orange oil and cook for 5 min until it softens and picks up colour at the edges. Add the garlic and a pinch of chilli flakes for the last minute, just until the garlic smells nutty. Stir so nothing sticks to the hot pan base.
warnings: Garlic burns in seconds and turns bitter — add it late and keep it moving.

steps.title: Simmer the tomato sauce
steps.content: Crush in the tomatoes and simmer to a glossy sauce.
steps.detail: Crush the tinned tomatoes in with your hand or the back of a spoon, season with salt and pepper, and drop the heat to low. Simmer for 12-15 min, stirring now and then, until it thickens to a deep brick red that coats the spoon. Splash in a little water from the tin if it gets too tight.
tips: Swirl a little water in the empty tin and tip it in — no tomato left behind.

steps.title: Boil the spaghetti
steps.content: Boil spaghetti in well-salted water until al dente.
steps.detail: While the sauce simmers, bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it well — it should taste like the sea. Add the spaghetti and cook 1 min under the packet time for al dente. Scoop out a mugful of the starchy cooking water before you drain — it is the secret to a sauce that clings.

steps.title: Toss and finish
steps.content: Toss pasta through the sauce, loosen with pasta water, add parsley.
steps.detail: Use tongs to drag the spaghetti straight into the sauce with a good splash of pasta water. Toss over low heat for a minute until every strand is glossy and coated — add more water if it looks dry. Off the heat, fold through the chopped parsley and serve straight away.

title: Chicken Coconut Lentil Curry
description: A gentle, creamy weeknight curry — coconut milk and red lentils cook down around tender chicken thigh, spooned over fluffy basmati.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge, or freeze for up to 3 months. Store the rice separately and eat cooked rice within a day. Reheat until piping hot.
make_ahead: Even better the next day — make it ahead and the flavours deepen overnight.
leftovers: Thin leftovers with a little stock into a soup, or wrap in a warm tortilla with the rice.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast (add it later so it doesn't dry out), or leave it out for a vegetarian dhal
a couple of spoons of red curry paste for a punchier, different flavour
jasmine rice, or warm naan and flatbread to scoop

steps.title: Chop the aromatics and chicken
steps.content: Dice onion, grate ginger and garlic, cut chicken into chunks.
steps.detail: Dice the brown onion, grate or finely mince the garlic and ginger, and cut the chicken thigh into 3cm chunks. Keep the raw chicken on its own board. Thighs stay juicy in a slow curry, so don't trim them too lean.
warnings: Keep raw chicken and its board away from anything you'll eat raw; wash hands, board and knife straight after.

steps.title: Bloom the curry powder
steps.content: Soften onion, then toast garlic, ginger and curry powder.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large pot over medium and soften the onion for 5 min until translucent. Stir in the garlic, ginger and curry powder and cook 1 min — the spices will smell warm and toasty and coat the onion. This quick toasting wakes them up, so keep it moving and don't let it catch.
tips: Curry powders vary in heat — start with 1 1/2 tablespoons and add more later once it simmers.

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.content: Add chicken and colour it in the spiced onion.
steps.detail: Turn the heat up a touch and add the chicken chunks, stirring to coat them in the spiced onion. Cook 3-4 min until the outsides turn from pink to pale gold — they don't need to cook through here, they will finish in the sauce. Season with salt and pepper.
warnings: The chicken isn't cooked through yet — it finishes in the simmer, so don't taste it now.

steps.title: Simmer with lentils and coconut
steps.content: Add lentils and coconut milk; simmer until thick and creamy.
steps.detail: Rinse the red lentils, then add them with the coconut milk and a coconut-tin's worth of water. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook uncovered for 20-25 min, stirring often, until the lentils collapse into a thick, creamy sauce and the chicken is cooked through — 74°C at the thickest point, with no pink. Add a splash more water if it gets too stodgy.
tips: Red lentils love to catch on the base — scrape the bottom every few minutes with your spoon.

steps.title: Cook the basmati
steps.content: Cook the basmati until fluffy.
steps.detail: Rinse the basmati until the water runs clear, then cook it covered — 1 1/2 cups rice to 2 1/4 cups water: boil, drop to low for 12 min, then rest 5 min off the heat and fluff with a fork. Starting it when the curry goes to simmer means both are ready together.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.content: Taste, adjust and spoon over the rice.
