steps.detail: Taste the curry and adjust — more salt, a little more curry powder for punch, or a splash of water if it's too thick. Spoon it generously over the fluffy basmati. A few extra minutes on the heat only makes it better, so don't panic if the lentils need longer.

title: Corn Zucchini Chowder
description: Sweet corn and summer zucchini in a creamy potato broth. A big pot of comfort that happens to be meat-free.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge. Reheat gently without boiling so the cream doesn't split. Freezing isn't ideal — the potato can turn grainy and the cream can separate.
make_ahead: Make the whole pot ahead and stir the cream through only when you reheat to serve.
leftovers: Blitz leftovers completely smooth and thin with stock for a silky soup starter.

substitutions.swap: milk for a lighter chowder, or coconut milk to keep it dairy-free
a tin of drained cannellini beans, blitzed, thickens it just as well

steps.title: Dice the vegetables
steps.content: Dice potato, zucchini, onion and garlic; slice spring onions.
steps.detail: Peel and dice the potatoes into 1.5cm cubes so they cook evenly, dice the zucchini a little larger since it softens faster, and finely dice the onion and garlic. Slice the spring onions and keep them aside for garnish. Even-sized potato is the difference between a smooth chowder and one with hard lumps.

steps.title: Sweat the base
steps.content: Soften onion and garlic in oil without browning.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a large pot over medium and cook the onion for 5 min until soft and translucent — you want it sweet, not browned. Add the garlic for the last minute until fragrant. This gentle start builds the savoury backbone of the chowder.

steps.title: Simmer the potato
steps.content: Add potato and stock; simmer until tender.
steps.detail: Add the diced potato and pour in the vegetable stock — it should just cover them. Bring to a simmer and cook 12-15 min until the potato is fork-tender. Season with salt and pepper as it goes.

steps.title: Add corn and zucchini
steps.content: Stir in corn and zucchini; cook until just tender.
steps.detail: Tip in the frozen corn and diced zucchini and simmer another 5-6 min, until the zucchini is tender but still holds its shape and the corn is sweet and hot through. Don't overcook the zucchini or it turns to mush.

steps.title: Thicken and cream
steps.content: Blitz a little, then stir through the cream.
steps.detail: For a chowder that's creamy but still chunky, blitz just a ladleful or two against the side of the pot with a stick blender (or mash a little of the potato with your spoon), then stir through the cream and warm gently — don't boil it hard or it can split. Taste and adjust the salt.
tips: No stick blender? Scoop a cup of the soup into a mug, mash with a fork, and stir it back in.

steps.title: Garnish and serve
steps.content: Ladle up and scatter with spring onion.
steps.detail: Ladle the chowder into bowls and scatter over the sliced spring onions with a good grind of pepper. A drizzle of olive oil on top looks the part and adds richness. Lovely with crusty bread to mop it up.

title: Black Bean Beef Rice Bowl
description: Cumin-seared steak, smoky black beans and sweet capsicum piled on jasmine rice, brightened with lime and coriander.
storage: Store the components separately in the fridge — the beef and beans keep 3 days, but cooked rice is best eaten within a day. Reheat until piping hot.
make_ahead: Cook the rice and warm the beans ahead; sear the beef fresh so it stays juicy.
leftovers: Wrap the lot in a tortilla with cheese and griddle it into a crunchy burrito.

substitutions.swap: beef mince, or leftover roast chicken shredded through
brown rice or basmati; warmed tortillas turn it into burritos

steps.title: Cook the jasmine rice
steps.content: Rinse and simmer the jasmine rice until fluffy.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice until the water runs clear, then cook 1 1/2 cups rice to 2 1/4 cups water — bring to a boil, drop to low with the lid on for 12 min, then rest 5 min off the heat and fluff. Getting this going first means it's ready and rested by the time the beef is done.

steps.title: Slice and season the beef
steps.content: Slice steak thin and toss with cumin and garlic.
steps.detail: Pat the steak dry and slice it thinly across the grain — that's across the lines running through the meat, which keeps it tender. Toss the strips with the cumin, minced garlic, salt and pepper. Wash your hands and board after the raw beef.
warnings: Wash hands, knife and board after handling the raw beef before touching anything else.

steps.title: Sear the beef
steps.content: Sear the beef hot and fast until browned.
steps.detail: Get a large pan very hot with the olive oil, then add the beef in a single layer — work in two batches if it looks crowded. Sear 2-3 min, turning once, until browned outside but still juicy. Crowding the pan steams the meat grey, so give it room.
tips: A screaming-hot pan is the whole game — wait for the oil to shimmer before the beef goes in.

steps.title: Warm capsicum and beans
steps.content: Soften capsicum, then warm the black beans.
steps.detail: In the same pan, add the sliced capsicum and cook 3-4 min until it softens and picks up the beefy browned bits. Drain and rinse the black beans, add them, and warm through for 2 min. Squeeze in half the lime and scrape the tasty bits off the pan base.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Pile rice, beef and beans; finish with lime and coriander.
steps.detail: Spoon the fluffy rice into bowls and top with the beef, capsicum and beans. Scatter over torn coriander and serve with the rest of the lime cut into wedges for squeezing. A dollop of yoghurt or a splash of hot sauce is lovely here if you like.

title: Pork Mince Green Bean Stir-Fry
description: Blistered green beans and crisp golden pork mince tossed with garlic, ginger and chilli — a fast, punchy weeknight stir-fry over rice.
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge; eat the rice within a day. Reheat in a hot pan until steaming.
make_ahead: Chop everything and cook the rice ahead so the stir-fry itself takes just a few minutes.
leftovers: Spoon cold leftovers into lettuce cups, or stir through a quick noodle soup.

substitutions.swap: chicken or turkey mince, or crumbled firm tofu for meat-free
snake beans, or broccolini cut into batons

steps.title: Get the rice going
steps.content: Get the jasmine rice simmering.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice until the water runs clear, then cook 1 1/2 cups rice to 2 1/4 cups water — boil, then low with the lid on for 12 min, and rest 5 min. Starting the rice first means it's ready when the fast stir-fry is done.

steps.title: Prep beans and aromatics
steps.content: Trim beans; mince garlic, ginger and chilli.
steps.detail: Trim the green beans and cut them into 4cm lengths. Finely mince the garlic and ginger, and slice the chilli — leave the seeds in for more heat or scrape them out for less. Have everything by the stove: a stir-fry moves fast and there's no time to chop once the wok is hot.

steps.title: Blister the beans
steps.content: Stir-fry the beans until wrinkled and charred in spots.
steps.detail: Heat the vegetable oil in a wok over high until it shimmers, then add the beans and stir-fry 4-5 min until they wrinkle and char in spots but still have a bite. Tip them onto a plate. This blistering is what gives the classic dry-fried bean its flavour.
warnings: Pat the beans dry first — any water on them will spit in the hot oil.

steps.title: Brown the pork
steps.content: Fry the pork mince hard until crisp and golden.
steps.detail: In the same hot wok, add the pork mince and press it flat. Let it sit 2 min to catch and brown before you stir — you want crisp golden bits, not grey steamed mince. Cook 5 min until no pink remains.
warnings: Wash your hands and any utensils that touched the raw pork before they touch cooked food.

steps.title: Add aromatics and sauce
steps.content: Add garlic, ginger and chilli, then the soy sauce.
steps.detail: Push the pork to one side, add the garlic, ginger and chilli to the space and stir 30 seconds until fragrant, then mix it all through. Return the beans, pour in the soy sauce, and toss everything over high heat for a minute so the sauce coats and sizzles. Add a pinch of salt and pepper to taste.

steps.title: Finish with sesame oil
steps.content: Off the heat, drizzle sesame oil and serve on rice.
steps.detail: Take the wok off the heat and drizzle over the sesame oil — adding it at the end keeps its nutty aroma, which high heat would burn off. Toss once and serve over the fluffy rice. Taste and add another splash of soy if it needs it.

title: Tuna White Bean Tomato Salad
description: The Italian pantry classic — flaked tuna, soft white beans and juicy tomato in a lemony dressing. No cooking, all flavour.
storage: Best eaten fresh. Undressed, the components keep a day in the fridge — dress just before serving so the beans and tomato don't go soggy.
make_ahead: Soak the onion and chop the tomatoes ahead; toss everything together just before serving.
leftovers: Pile onto toasted sourdough and grill with cheese for a quick melt.

substitutions.swap: tinned salmon, or a tin of chickpeas for meat-free
cannellini or butter beans are best; chickpeas work too

steps.title: Soften the onion
steps.content: Thinly slice red onion and steep in lemon juice.
steps.detail: Thinly slice the red onion and toss it in a small bowl with the juice of half the lemon and a pinch of salt. Letting it sit while you build the rest takes the raw bite out and turns it sweet and pink. Ten minutes is plenty.
tips: The lemony onion juices become part of the dressing — don't tip them out.

steps.title: Drain the beans and tuna
steps.content: Rinse the beans, drain the tuna, chop tomato and parsley.
steps.detail: Tip the beans into a sieve and rinse under cold water until the foam stops — this freshens their flavour. Drain the tuna well and flake it into big chunks with a fork. Chop the tomatoes into bite-sized wedges and roughly chop the parsley.

steps.title: Make the dressing
steps.content: Whisk lemon, olive oil, capers and pepper into a dressing.
steps.detail: In the onion bowl, whisk the rest of the lemon juice with a good glug of olive oil, the capers and a grind of pepper until it looks a little cloudy and combined. Taste it — it should be bright and savoury. Add a pinch more salt if it needs lifting.

steps.title: Toss and serve
steps.content: Fold everything together gently and serve fresh.
steps.detail: Add the beans, tomatoes, tuna, parsley and the soaked onion to a big bowl, pour over the dressing, and fold gently so the tuna stays in chunks rather than breaking to paste. Taste one last time for salt and lemon. Best eaten fresh while the tomatoes are juicy.

title: Fish Finger-ish Barramundi Tacos
description: Crunchy crumbed barramundi fingers tucked into warm tortillas with crisp lime slaw and a quick lime mayo.
storage: Best assembled and eaten straight away while crisp. Cooked fish keeps 1 day in the fridge; the slaw and mayo keep 2 days separately. Reheat fish in a hot oven, not the microwave, to keep it crunchy.
make_ahead: Make the slaw and lime mayo a few hours ahead; crumb and fry the fish just before eating.
leftovers: Leftover crumbed fish is great flaked through a salad or in a sandwich with the slaw.

substitutions.swap: any firm white fish — flathead, ling or basa
Greek yoghurt whisked with lime for a lighter sauce
soft flour wraps, or crisp taco shells

steps.title: Make slaw and lime mayo
steps.content: Shred cabbage for slaw and mix lime into the mayo.
steps.detail: Finely shred the cabbage and toss it with a squeeze of lime, a pinch of salt and a little pepper — scrunch it with your hands to soften. In a small bowl, stir the mayo with a squeeze of lime and a pinch of salt to make the sauce. Set both aside while you crumb the fish.

steps.title: Set up the crumbing station
steps.content: Set up three bowls: flour, egg and breadcrumbs.
steps.detail: Line up three shallow bowls: seasoned flour in the first, beaten eggs in the second, breadcrumbs in the third. Cut the barramundi into finger-width strips. This little assembly line gives an even, crunchy coating.
warnings: Keep the raw fish separate and wash your hands after cutting it, before you touch the tortillas or slaw.

steps.title: Crumb the fish
steps.content: Coat each fish strip in flour, egg, then crumbs.
steps.detail: Roll each barramundi strip in flour and shake off the excess, dip it into the egg, then press it into the breadcrumbs until fully coated. Lay them on a plate as you go. Pressing the crumbs on firmly stops them falling off in the pan.

steps.title: Fry until golden
steps.content: Shallow-fry the strips until golden and cooked through.
steps.detail: Heat a generous layer of olive oil in a pan over medium-high until a crumb dropped in sizzles straight away. Fry the strips 2-3 min each side until deep golden and crisp and the fish flakes easily inside. Drain on paper towel and season with a little salt.
warnings: The oil is hot enough to spit — lay the strips in gently, away from you, and don't crowd the pan.

steps.title: Warm the tortillas
steps.content: Warm the tortillas until soft and pliable.
steps.detail: Wipe the pan and warm each tortilla for 20-30 seconds a side, or straight over a low gas flame for a little char, until soft and foldable. Wrap them in a clean tea towel to keep warm and steamy while you finish.

steps.title: Build the tacos
steps.content: Fill with slaw, fish and lime mayo.
steps.detail: Lay a little slaw down each warm tortilla, top with a couple of crunchy fish strips, and drizzle with the lime mayo. Finish with a squeeze of fresh lime and eat straight away while the fish is still crisp. Serve any extra lime in wedges on the side.

title: Barramundi Lemon Potato Plate
description: Crisp-skinned barramundi over golden roast potatoes and green beans, brought together with a lemon-garlic butter.
storage: Best eaten fresh. Leftover fish keeps 1 day in the fridge; potatoes and beans keep 3 days. Reheat the potatoes in a hot oven to re-crisp.
make_ahead: Parboil and part-roast the potatoes ahead, then reheat to crisp while you fry the fish.
leftovers: Flake leftover fish and potato into a quick fishcake with an egg to bind.

substitutions.swap: any firm white fillet — snapper or ling — or salmon
asparagus or broccolini, roasted alongside the potatoes

steps.title: Roast the potatoes
steps.content: Cut potatoes into chunks and roast until crisp.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Cut the potatoes into 3cm chunks, toss on a tray with the olive oil, salt and pepper, and roast 30-35 min, turning once, until deep golden and crisp at the edges. Give them room on the tray — crowded potatoes steam instead of crisping.

steps.title: Blanch the green beans
steps.content: Boil the green beans briefly, then drain.
steps.detail: When the potatoes have about 8 minutes left, trim the green beans and boil them in salted water for 3 min until bright green and just tender. Drain and set aside — they'll get tossed in the lemon butter at the end. Don't overcook them or they lose their snap.

steps.title: Pan-fry the barramundi
steps.content: Pan-fry the barramundi skin-side down until crisp.
steps.detail: Pat the barramundi fillets very dry and season with salt and pepper. Heat a little olive oil in a pan over medium-high, lay the fillets in skin-side down and press flat for a few seconds. Fry 3-4 min until the skin is crisp and the flesh has turned opaque three-quarters up the side, then flip and cook 1-2 min more until it flakes. Drying the fish is what gets you crisp skin instead of steamed.
tips: Press each fillet down for the first few seconds so the skin stays flat against the pan and crisps evenly.
warnings: Wash your hands and board after handling the raw fish, before touching the cooked plate.

steps.title: Make the lemon-garlic butter
steps.content: Melt butter with garlic and lemon; toss the beans through.
steps.detail: Lift the fish out to rest. Drop the heat to low, add the butter and garlic to the pan and swirl for a minute until the garlic smells sweet and the butter foams — don't let it brown. Squeeze in the lemon juice and stir up the tasty browned bits, then toss the drained beans through this pan sauce.
warnings: Garlic scorches fast in hot butter — keep the heat low and keep it moving.

steps.title: Plate up
steps.content: Plate the fish, potatoes and beans; spoon over lemon butter.
steps.detail: Divide the crispy potatoes and lemon-buttered beans between plates and set a barramundi fillet on top. Spoon over any lemon-garlic butter left in the pan and scatter with chopped parsley and an extra wedge of lemon. Serve straight away while the skin is still crisp.

title: Tuna Corn Puff Pies
description: Golden puff parcels stuffed with tuna, sweet corn and melty cheddar — the pantry raid that tastes like a treat.
storage: Keep 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Re-crisp in a 180°C oven for 10 minutes rather than the microwave, which goes soggy. Baked pies also freeze for up to 2 months.
make_ahead: Assemble the pies and chill them raw for a few hours, then bake fresh — or bake and reheat.
leftovers: A cold pie is a cracking lunchbox filler — no reheating needed.

substitutions.swap: tinned salmon, or shredded leftover roast chicken
tasty or mozzarella; whatever melts is in the fridge

steps.title: Thaw pastry, heat oven
steps.content: Thaw the pastry and heat the oven.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan and line a tray with baking paper. Take the puff pastry sheets out to thaw until floppy but still cold — too warm and they tear, too frozen and they crack. Thaw the corn under warm water and drain it well; wet corn makes a soggy pie.

steps.title: Mix the filling
steps.content: Combine tuna, corn, cheddar, mayo and spring onion.
steps.detail: Drain the tuna and flake it into a bowl. Add the drained corn, grated cheddar, mayo and sliced spring onions, then season with salt and pepper and mix. The mayo binds it into a filling that holds together rather than spilling out — it should be moist but not wet.

steps.title: Cut and fill the pastry
steps.content: Cut pastry squares and spoon filling onto half.
steps.detail: Cut each pastry sheet into 4 squares to give 8 in total. Spoon filling onto the centre of 4 of the squares, leaving a 1.5cm border clear. Brush the borders with beaten egg — this is the glue that seals the pies.

steps.title: Seal the pies
steps.content: Lay tops on, press and crimp the edges.
steps.detail: Lay a second pastry square over each mound of filling and press the edges together, then crimp firmly all around with a fork to seal. Cut a small steam vent in each top. A good seal stops the filling bubbling out in the oven.

steps.title: Glaze and bake
steps.content: Brush with egg and bake until puffed and golden.
steps.detail: Brush the tops with the rest of the beaten egg for a glossy finish, and bake 20-25 min until the pastry is puffed, deep golden and cooked through underneath. Resist opening the oven early — the pastry needs steady heat to rise. Cool 5 min before eating.
warnings: The filling is very hot out of the oven — let the pies sit 5 minutes so no one burns their mouth.

title: Date Walnut Loaf
description: A moist, old-fashioned date loaf studded with walnuts. Lovely sliced thick and spread with butter.
storage: Keeps 4 days wrapped in an airtight container at room temperature, or up to a week in the fridge. Slices freeze well for up to 3 months.
make_ahead: Bakes a day ahead happily — the flavour and moistness only improve overnight.
leftovers: Toast thick slices and spread with butter, or turn a stale slice into a quick bread-and-butter pudding.

substitutions.swap: pecans, or leave the nuts out and stir through extra dates
brown sugar for a deeper, caramel note
60 ml light olive oil or vegetable oil for a dairy-free loaf

steps.title: Soak the dates
steps.content: Pour boiling water over chopped dates and butter.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 160°C fan and line a loaf tin with baking paper. Roughly chop the dates into a heatproof bowl, add the butter, and pour over 3/4 cup boiling water. Leave it 10 minutes — the dates soften to a jammy pulp and the butter melts through. This soaking is what makes the loaf so moist without much added sugar.
warnings: Take care pouring the boiling water — do it over the sink and keep it away from little hands.

steps.title: Chop the walnuts
steps.content: Roughly chop the walnuts.
steps.detail: While the dates soak, roughly chop the walnuts — some fine, some in bigger pieces for texture. Save a small handful to press on top of the loaf for a pretty finish. If you like a deeper flavour, toast them in a dry pan for a couple of minutes until fragrant.

steps.title: Mix the batter
steps.content: Stir sugar and eggs into the dates, then fold in flour.
steps.detail: Stir the sugar into the warm date mixture, then beat in the eggs one at a time — let it cool a minute first if it's still steaming so they don't scramble. Sift in the flour and baking powder and fold gently until just combined, then stir through most of the walnuts. Don't overmix or the loaf turns tough; a few streaks of flour are fine.
tips: Fold, don't beat, once the flour is in — overworking develops gluten and makes the crumb heavy.

steps.title: Bake the loaf
steps.content: Pour into the tin, top with walnuts and bake.
steps.detail: Scrape the batter into the lined tin, level the top and scatter over the reserved walnuts. Bake 45-55 min until risen, deep golden, and a skewer pushed into the centre comes out clean with no wet batter. If the top browns too fast, lay a piece of foil loosely over it.
warnings: Don't open the oven in the first 30 minutes — the rush of cool air can make the loaf sink in the middle.

steps.title: Cool before slicing
steps.content: Rest in the tin, then cool on a rack before slicing.
steps.detail: Let the loaf sit in the tin for 10 minutes, then lift it out by the paper onto a wire rack to cool. It slices best once fully cool — warm date loaf crumbles. Lovely as is, or spread with butter.

title: Maple Peach Poached Fruit
description: Peaches poached soft in a maple-vanilla syrup, spooned over cool yoghurt with toasted pecans.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge in its syrup, in an airtight container. Lovely cold — but add the yoghurt and pecans fresh so they don't go soft.
make_ahead: Poach the peaches and reduce the syrup a day ahead; serve chilled or gently rewarmed.
leftovers: Chop leftover peaches through morning porridge, or over pancakes with the syrup.

substitutions.swap: nectarines, plums or firm pears (pears need a few minutes longer)
honey, or brown sugar dissolved in the water
vanilla ice cream, or thick cream

steps.title: Make the poaching syrup
steps.content: Warm water, maple, vanilla and cinnamon into a syrup.
steps.detail: In a medium saucepan, bring 2 cups of water, the maple syrup, vanilla and the cinnamon stick (or a good pinch of ground cinnamon) to a gentle simmer. Stir until it smells fragrant and the maple has dissolved through. This lightly spiced syrup is what flavours the peaches as they poach.

steps.title: Halve the peaches
steps.content: Halve the peaches and remove the stones.
steps.detail: Run a knife around each peach through to the stone, twist the halves apart, and lift the stone out. If they're clingstone and stubborn, cut the flesh away in wedges instead. No need to peel — the skins slip off easily after poaching.

steps.title: Poach the peaches
steps.content: Poach the peaches gently until tender.
steps.detail: Slip the peach halves cut-side down into the simmering syrup and poach gently for 8-12 min, turning once, until a knife slides in easily but they still hold their shape. Riper peaches take less time. Keep it at a lazy simmer, not a boil, so they cook evenly without falling apart.
tips: If the peaches bob up, lay a round of baking paper on the surface to keep them under the syrup.

steps.title: Toast the pecans
steps.content: Toast the pecans until fragrant.
steps.detail: While the peaches poach, toast the pecans in a dry pan over medium heat for 2-3 min, shaking often, until they smell nutty and warm. Tip them out straight away so they don't scorch, then roughly chop. Toasting wakes up their flavour and adds crunch against the soft fruit.
warnings: Nuts go from golden to burnt in seconds — keep them moving and pull them off the heat the moment they smell toasty.

steps.title: Reduce the syrup
steps.content: Lift out the peaches and boil the syrup to a glaze.
steps.detail: Lift the peaches out with a slotted spoon. Turn up the heat and boil the poaching liquid for 3-5 min until it reduces to a glossy syrup that coats the back of a spoon. Slip the skins off the peaches if you like — they'll pull away easily now.

steps.title: Serve with yoghurt
steps.content: Spoon over yoghurt, drizzle syrup, scatter pecans.
steps.detail: Spoon a dollop of Greek yoghurt into each bowl, nestle the warm peaches on top, and drizzle over the reduced maple syrup. Scatter with the toasted pecans and a final dusting of cinnamon. Lovely warm, and just as good chilled the next day.

title: Salmon Spinach Lemon Pasta
description: Pan-seared salmon flaked through a silky lemon-garlic cream with baby spinach wilted in at the end. A special-feeling pasta that lands on the table in half an hour.
storage: Best eaten fresh. Leftovers keep 1 day in an airtight container in the fridge — reheat gently with a splash of milk or water so the salmon doesn't dry out. Cream sauces shouldn't sit out at room temperature.
leftovers: Flake any cold leftovers through a green salad, or fold into a quick omelette.

substitutions.swap: barramundi or another firm fish; or hot-smoked salmon flaked through at the very end
any long pasta — linguine, fettuccine or spaghetti
milk loosened with a spoonful of cream cheese for a lighter sauce
any hard grating cheese, such as a block of tasty

steps.title: Zest the lemon and prep
steps.content: Zest and juice the lemon, mince garlic, grate parmesan.
steps.detail: Finely grate the yellow zest from the lemon, avoiding the bitter white pith, then halve it and squeeze the juice into a small bowl. Peel and finely mince the garlic and grate the parmesan. Getting this done before the pan is hot — mise en place — keeps the cook calm.

steps.title: Sear the salmon
steps.content: Sear salmon skin-side down until crisp, then flake.
steps.detail: Pat the salmon fillets very dry and season with salt and pepper. Heat 1 tbsp oil in the pan over medium-high and lay the fillets skin-side down. Leave them alone for 4 minutes until the skin is crisp and the flesh has turned pale most of the way up, then flip for 1–2 minutes. It's done when it flakes easily and is just opaque in the middle. Lift out, peel off the skin if you like, and break into big chunks.
tips: Salmon keeps cooking off the heat, so pull it a touch early — a moist centre is the goal, not a dry one.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the knife after handling raw salmon before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Boil the pasta
steps.content: Cook pasta in well-salted water until al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it well — it should taste like the sea. Add the pasta and cook to the packet time for al dente, stirring now and then so it doesn't clump. Just before draining, scoop out a mugful of the starchy cooking water and keep it — it's the secret to a glossy sauce.

steps.title: Make the lemon cream
steps.content: Soften garlic, add cream and lemon, wilt the spinach.
steps.detail: Wipe the salmon pan, add a splash of oil and the garlic over low heat for 30 seconds until it smells sweet — don't let it colour or it turns bitter. Pour in the cream and lemon juice and bring to a gentle simmer for 3 minutes until slightly thickened. Stir through most of the parmesan and the lemon zest, then add the spinach in handfuls, folding until just wilted.
warnings: Garlic burns in seconds on high heat and turns acrid — keep it low and moving.

steps.title: Toss it together
steps.content: Fold pasta and salmon through the sauce, then serve.
steps.detail: Drag the drained pasta into the sauce and toss to coat, adding a splash of the reserved pasta water if it looks tight. Fold the salmon chunks through gently so they don't break up completely. Taste, add a little salt, pepper and a squeeze of lemon, and finish with the last of the parmesan. Eat straight away while it's silky.

title: Prawn Sweet Potato Red Curry
description: Sweet potato simmered soft in red curry coconut, with prawns slipped in at the end so they stay plump and sweet. Finished with lime and coriander over fluffy jasmine rice.
equipment: large deep frying pan or wok
storage: The curry keeps 1–2 days in the fridge; cool it quickly and reheat until piping hot. Cooked rice is safest eaten within a day.
make_ahead: The sweet potato base can be simmered a day ahead — reheat it, then poach the prawns fresh so they don't overcook.
leftovers: Any spare curry is great spooned over rice for a next-day lunch.

substitutions.swap: firm white fish like barramundi in chunks, or chicken thigh simmered a little longer
green curry paste for a fresher, hotter version
pumpkin or butternut, cut the same size
basmati rice

steps.title: Cook the jasmine rice
steps.content: Rinse and simmer jasmine rice by the absorption method.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice in a sieve until the water runs clear, then tip into a saucepan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, drop to the lowest heat, cover, and cook 12 minutes. Take it off the heat and leave it covered to steam for another 10 minutes — no peeking — then fluff with a fork.
tips: Resting the rice off the heat with the lid on is what makes it fluffy rather than gluey.

steps.title: Cube the sweet potato
steps.content: Peel and cube the sweet potato; pat the prawns dry.
steps.detail: Peel the sweet potato and cut it into even 2–3 cm cubes so they cook at the same rate. If the prawns still have shells, peel them, then pat them all dry with paper towel — dry prawns cook far better. Keep the prawns on a separate plate in the fridge until you need them.
warnings: Keep raw prawns away from the cooked rice and anything eaten raw, and wash your hands and board after handling them.

steps.title: Fry the curry paste
steps.content: Bloom the red curry paste in oil until fragrant.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a wide pan over medium and add the red curry paste. Fry it for about a minute, stirring constantly, until it darkens a shade and smells deeply fragrant — this wakes up the spices. Spoon in a few tablespoons of the thick cream from the top of the coconut milk tin and let it sizzle and split slightly.

steps.title: Simmer the sweet potato
steps.content: Add coconut milk and sweet potato, simmer until tender.
steps.detail: Pour in the rest of the coconut milk plus half a tin of water and stir to a smooth sauce. Add the sweet potato, bring to a gentle simmer, and cook uncovered for about 15 minutes until the cubes are tender to the tip of a knife but still holding their shape. Stir now and then so nothing catches on the base.

steps.title: Poach the prawns
steps.content: Slip in the prawns and cook just until pink.
steps.detail: Stir the fish sauce and a squeeze of lime into the sauce, then slide in the prawns. Simmer gently for 2–3 minutes, just until they turn pink and curl into a loose C-shape — a tight O means they're overcooked and rubbery. Taste and balance with more fish sauce, lime or a pinch of salt.
tips: Prawns keep cooking in the hot sauce, so take the pan off the heat the moment they turn opaque.

steps.title: Serve over rice
steps.content: Spoon over rice, finish with coriander and lime.
steps.detail: Spoon the fluffy rice into bowls and ladle the curry over the top. Scatter with torn coriander and serve with the remaining lime cut into wedges for squeezing. A little extra chilli or fish sauce at the table lets everyone tune it to taste.

title: Beef Barley-Style Quinoa Soup
description: A hearty winter warmer that swaps the traditional barley for quinoa — seared beef and sweet root vegetables in a rich, peppery broth that only gets better the next day.
equipment: large heavy-based pot or dutch oven
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge and freezes well for up to 3 months. The quinoa keeps drinking up liquid, so loosen with a splash of stock or water when you reheat.
make_ahead: Like most soups, it's even better the next day once the flavours have settled.
leftovers: A thick bowlful the next day is almost a stew — pile it onto hot buttered toast.

substitutions.swap: beef brisket or chuck for a richer, slow-cooked version — simmer an extra hour
pearl barley for the classic (no longer gluten-free), or rice
chicken or vegetable stock
a chopped leek

steps.title: Dice the veg and beef
steps.content: Dice onion, carrot, celery; cube the beef; rinse quinoa.
steps.detail: Dice the onion, carrot and celery into rough 1 cm pieces and mince the garlic. Cut the beef into 2 cm cubes and pat them dry so they'll brown rather than steam. Rinse the quinoa in a fine sieve until the water runs clear — this washes off its naturally bitter coating.

steps.title: Brown the beef
steps.content: Sear the beef cubes in batches until deeply coloured.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in the pot over medium-high until shimmering. Brown the beef in two batches so the pan stays hot — crowd it and it stews grey instead of searing. Give each batch a few minutes without stirring to build a dark crust, then lift out and set aside.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the knife after handling the raw beef.
Pat the beef dry and stand back as it hits the oil — any water will spit.

steps.title: Soften the vegetables
steps.content: Cook onion, carrot, celery and garlic in the beefy fat.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to medium and tip the onion, carrot and celery into the same pot. Cook for 6–8 minutes, scraping up the browned bits from the base, until the onion is soft and translucent. Stir in the garlic for the last minute until fragrant.

steps.title: Simmer the broth
steps.content: Return beef with stock, water and bay; simmer until tender.
steps.detail: Return the beef and any juices, pour in the stock and about 3 cups of water, and drop in the bay leaf. Season with salt and pepper, bring to a gentle simmer, then partly cover and cook for 30 minutes, until the beef is turning tender.

steps.title: Add the quinoa
steps.content: Stir in quinoa and simmer until the grains uncurl.
steps.detail: Stir in the rinsed quinoa and simmer for another 15 minutes, until the little grains turn translucent and pop open with a visible spiral tail. The soup thickens noticeably as they swell — add more water if you like it brothier. Fish out the bay leaf before serving.
tips: Taste and add plenty of salt and pepper at the end — a big pot of soup needs more seasoning than you'd expect.

steps.title: Ladle and serve
steps.content: Check the seasoning and ladle into warm bowls.
steps.detail: Give it a final taste, adjusting the salt and pepper until the broth tastes lively rather than flat. Ladle into deep bowls. A drizzle of olive oil over the top brings a little richness.

title: Chickpea Couscous Herb Bowl
description: Fluffy lemon couscous tossed with chickpeas and a big lift of fresh parsley and mint, with crunchy cucumber and tomato through it. Bright, filling and easy to pack.
storage: Undressed, the components keep 2 days in the fridge. Once dressed, eat within a day — the tomato and cucumber start to weep and soften.
make_ahead: Steam the couscous and chop everything ahead; toss with the dressing just before serving so it stays fresh.
leftovers: Spoon into a wrap with a dollop of yoghurt for a next-day lunch.

substitutions.swap: quinoa for a gluten-free bowl, or bulgur
tinned white beans such as cannellini or butter beans
coriander, or a mix of the two
a splash of red wine vinegar

steps.title: Steam the couscous
steps.content: Pour boiling water over the couscous and cover.
steps.detail: Tip the couscous into a large bowl with a pinch of salt and a drizzle of oil. Pour over just enough boiling water to cover it by a centimetre, cover the bowl tightly with a plate, and leave for 5 minutes. Fluff it up with a fork — the grains should be tender and separate, not gluggy.
tips: Stirring a little oil through before the water helps keep the grains loose and separate.

steps.title: Chop the salad
steps.content: Dice cucumber and tomato, chop the herbs.
steps.detail: Dice the cucumber and tomato into small, even pieces so every forkful has some. Finely chop the parsley and mint, using the tender parsley stalks too. Keep the herbs to one side to stir in at the end so they stay vivid green.

steps.title: Season the chickpeas
steps.content: Drain chickpeas and toss with cumin, oil and lemon.
steps.detail: Drain and rinse the chickpeas well, then tip them into a bowl. Toss with the ground cumin, a squeeze of lemon, a good glug of olive oil and a pinch of salt — the cumin gives them a warm, earthy note without any cooking. Tinned chickpeas are already cooked, so they just need seasoning.

steps.title: Shake the dressing
steps.content: Shake lemon juice, olive oil, salt and pepper together.
steps.detail: Squeeze the rest of the lemon into a jar with about three times as much olive oil, a pinch of salt and a good grind of pepper. Put the lid on and shake hard until it looks thick and cloudy. Taste it on a piece of cucumber and adjust — it wants to be sharp and bright.

steps.title: Toss and serve
steps.content: Fold everything together and check the seasoning.
steps.detail: Fork the couscous to loosen it, then fold through the chickpeas, cucumber, tomato and most of the herbs. Pour over the dressing and toss gently. Taste for salt, lemon and pepper, scatter the last of the herbs on top, and serve at room temperature.

title: Paneer Capsicum Stir-Fry
description: Golden cubes of paneer tossed with sweet red and green capsicum and warm curry spices, cooled with a swirl of yoghurt and piled onto fluffy basmati. A quick meat-free midweek dinner.
storage: Keeps 2 days in the fridge. The paneer firms up cold, so reheat gently with a splash of water to loosen. Cooked rice is best eaten within a day.
make_ahead: Cube the paneer and slice the vegetables ahead so the actual cook is a fast 15 minutes.
leftovers: Wrap the leftovers in a warm tortilla with the yoghurt for an easy lunch.

substitutions.swap: haloumi (firmer and saltier), or firm tofu for a vegan version
a splash of coconut milk or cream
jasmine rice
your own mix of cumin, turmeric and paprika

steps.title: Cook the basmati rice
steps.content: Rinse and simmer basmati by the absorption method.
steps.detail: Rinse the basmati until the water runs clear, then simmer in a covered pan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt: boil, drop to low, and cook 10–12 minutes. Rest it off the heat with the lid on for 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork.

steps.title: Cube and slice
steps.content: Cube the paneer; slice capsicum, onion and garlic.
steps.detail: Cut the paneer into 2 cm cubes and pat them dry. Slice the red and green capsicum into strips, cut the onion into wedges, and mince the garlic. Keeping the garlic separate matters — it burns much faster than the chunky vegetables.

steps.title: Fry the paneer golden
steps.content: Brown the paneer cubes on all sides, then set aside.
steps.detail: Heat 1 tbsp oil in the wok over medium-high. Add the paneer in a single layer and fry, turning, until golden and crusted on several sides, about 5 minutes. Lift the cubes out onto a plate — they'll finish by warming back through at the end.
warnings: Pat the paneer dry and stand back — trapped moisture makes hot oil spit as the cubes hit the pan.

steps.title: Stir-fry the capsicum
steps.content: Toss onion and capsicum, then bloom the curry powder.
steps.detail: Add the last of the oil, then the onion and capsicum. Stir-fry over high heat for 4–5 minutes until they soften but still have some bite and a few charred edges. Push them to one side, add the garlic and curry powder to the space and stir for 30 seconds until fragrant, then toss everything together.
tips: A splash of water in the pan turns the toasted spices into a light sauce that coats the vegetables.

steps.title: Fold through the yoghurt
steps.content: Return paneer, take off the heat, swirl in yoghurt.
steps.detail: Return the paneer and toss to warm through and coat in the spices. Take the pan off the heat before you stir in the yoghurt — adding it off the heat stops it splitting into grains. Season with salt and loosen with a little water if it looks tight.

steps.title: Serve over rice
steps.content: Spoon over the fluffy basmati.
steps.detail: Fluff the rested rice and spoon it into bowls. Pile the paneer and capsicum on top, spooning over any spiced yoghurt sauce from the pan. Serve straight away, while the paneer is hot and the rice is fluffy.

title: Roast Pumpkin Quinoa Salad
description: Sweet caramelised pumpkin and nutty quinoa tumbled with peppery rocket, salty feta and toasted pine nuts, brought together with a sharp lemon dressing. A meal-prep hero.
storage: Undressed, the roast pumpkin and quinoa keep 3 days in the fridge — a great base to build lunches on. Once the rocket and dressing are through, eat within a few hours before the leaves wilt.
make_ahead: Roast the pumpkin and cook the quinoa up to 3 days ahead; add the rocket and dressing just before serving.
leftovers: Warm the leftovers and top with a fried egg, or fold into a wrap.

substitutions.swap: sweet potato, cut the same size
creamy goat's cheese, or pan-fried haloumi
almonds or walnuts, roughly chopped
couscous

steps.title: Roast the pumpkin
steps.content: Toss pumpkin in oil and roast until caramelised.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Cut the pumpkin into 3 cm wedges or chunks (no need to peel if the skin's thin), toss on a lined tray with oil, salt and pepper, and spread out in a single layer. Roast for about 28 minutes, turning once, until deeply golden and tender at the edges.
tips: Give the pieces room — crowded pumpkin steams and goes soggy instead of caramelising.

steps.title: Cook the quinoa
steps.content: Simmer rinsed quinoa, then rest and fluff.
steps.detail: Rinse the quinoa in a fine sieve, then simmer in a covered pan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt for 12–15 minutes, until the water is absorbed and the grains have uncurled. Rest off the heat with the lid on for 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork and spread out to cool a little.

steps.title: Toast the pine nuts
steps.content: Toast pine nuts in a dry pan until golden.
steps.detail: Scatter the pine nuts into a dry frying pan over medium heat. Toss constantly for 2–3 minutes until patchy gold and smelling toasty, then tip them straight out of the pan — they keep cooking in the hot pan and burn in seconds.
warnings: Don't walk away — pine nuts go from golden to bitter and burnt almost instantly.

steps.title: Whisk the lemon dressing
steps.content: Whisk lemon juice with olive oil, salt and pepper.
steps.detail: Squeeze the lemon into a small bowl and whisk in about three times as much olive oil with a pinch of salt and a grind of pepper until it looks glossy and slightly thick. Taste and balance — it wants to be zingy to cut the sweet pumpkin.

steps.title: Build the salad
steps.content: Layer quinoa, rocket, pumpkin, feta and pine nuts.
steps.detail: Toss the cooled quinoa and rocket with most of the dressing on a big platter. Scatter over the roast pumpkin, crumble the feta on top, and finish with the toasted pine nuts and the last of the dressing. Serve while the pumpkin is still a little warm against the cool leaves.

title: Haloumi Pumpkin Wraps
description: Warm wraps stuffed with cumin-roasted pumpkin, squeaky golden haloumi and peppery rocket, with a cool garlicky lemon yoghurt smeared through. A properly hearty vegetarian lunch.
storage: Best assembled fresh. Roast the pumpkin and mix the yoghurt up to 2 days ahead, but fry the haloumi to order — it turns squeaky and rubbery once it goes cold.
make_ahead: Have the roast pumpkin and lemon yoghurt ready in the fridge; then it's just frying the haloumi and rolling.
leftovers: The roast pumpkin and haloumi are great cold, tossed through a rocket salad.

substitutions.swap: grilled chicken, or crumbled feta if you'd rather not fry
sweet potato
tortillas, or warmed pita and flatbread
hummus or a drizzle of tahini sauce

steps.title: Roast the cumin pumpkin
steps.content: Toss pumpkin with cumin and oil, roast until tender.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Cut the pumpkin into thin 2 cm slices, toss on a lined tray with the cumin, oil, salt and pepper, and spread out. Roast for about 28 minutes, turning once, until soft and caramelised at the edges. Thin slices sit flat in a wrap better than chunks.

steps.title: Mix the lemon yoghurt
steps.content: Stir yoghurt with garlic, lemon and salt.
steps.detail: Grate or crush the garlic into the yoghurt, add a good squeeze of lemon and a pinch of salt, and stir. Taste — it should be tangy and garlicky enough to stand up to the salty haloumi. Loosen with a splash of water if you want it drizzly.

steps.title: Fry the haloumi
steps.content: Fry haloumi slices until deep golden both sides.
steps.detail: Slice the haloumi into 1 cm slabs and pat dry. Fry in a dry or lightly oiled pan over medium-high for 1–2 minutes each side until deep golden with crisp edges. Eat it while it's hot and squeaky — haloumi turns rubbery as it cools.
tips: Haloumi is already very salty, so there's no need to season it before frying.

steps.title: Warm the wraps
steps.content: Warm the wraps so they roll without cracking.
steps.detail: Warm each wrap for a few seconds in the empty haloumi pan until soft and pliable. A cold wrap cracks and splits when you roll it; a warm one folds neatly around the filling. Stack them under a tea towel to stay soft.

steps.title: Build and roll
steps.content: Spread yoghurt, layer the filling and roll tight.
steps.detail: Smear a good spoon of the lemon yoghurt down the centre of each wrap. Layer on rocket, roast pumpkin and haloumi, keeping the filling to the lower third. Fold in the sides, then roll away from you firmly into a tight cylinder. Cut in half on the diagonal to serve.

title: Steak Mushroom Pepper Plate
description: Pan-seared steak rested and sliced over buttery mash, smothered in a creamy black-pepper mushroom sauce, with peppery rocket alongside. A steakhouse dinner made at home.
storage: Best eaten fresh. Leftover steak and sauce keep 2 days in the fridge; slice cold steak thinly for sandwiches rather than reheating, which overcooks it. Mash keeps 2 days.
make_ahead: The mash can be made ahead and reheated with a splash of milk or cream while you cook the steak.
leftovers: Cold sliced steak makes a great sandwich with the leftover mushroom sauce and rocket.

substitutions.swap: a whole piece of rump or scotch fillet, sliced after resting
a mix of button and Swiss brown for deeper flavour
milk sharpened with a spoon of dijon for a lighter sauce
sweet potato, for orange mash

steps.title: Boil the potatoes
steps.content: Boil peeled potato chunks until fork-tender.
steps.detail: Peel the potatoes and cut into even chunks. Put them in a pot of cold salted water, bring to the boil, and simmer for about 15 minutes until a knife slides in with no resistance. Drain well and let them steam-dry in the colander for a minute — dry potato makes fluffier mash.

steps.title: Season and rest the steak
steps.content: Bring steak to room temp, season, slice mushrooms.
steps.detail: Take the steak out of the fridge 20 minutes ahead so it cooks evenly, then pat it very dry and season generously with salt and lots of the coarse pepper. Slice the mushrooms and mince the garlic while it sits. A cold, wet steak steams instead of searing.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the knife after handling the raw steak.

steps.title: Sear the steak
steps.content: Sear steak hard, then rest it under foil.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in the heavy pan until almost smoking. Lay the steak down away from you and sear 3–4 minutes each side for medium, pressing for good contact — it should be deep brown, not grey. Move it to a plate, cover loosely with foil, and rest at least 5 minutes so the juices settle back in.
tips: Press the steak with a finger to judge doneness: soft is rare, springy is medium, firm is well done.
warnings: A hot pan and a just-dried steak will spit — lay the meat down gently and away from you.

steps.title: Make the pepper sauce
steps.content: Fry mushrooms and garlic, add pepper and cream.
steps.detail: In the same pan with its browned juices, add a knob of the butter and the mushrooms over medium-high. Fry for 5 minutes until golden and any liquid has cooked off, then stir in the garlic and a heaped teaspoon of coarse pepper for 30 seconds. Pour in the cream, scrape up the sticky bits, and simmer 2–3 minutes until it coats the back of a spoon.

steps.title: Mash the potatoes
steps.content: Mash with butter and a splash of cream, season.
steps.detail: Mash the dried potatoes with the rest of the butter and a splash of the cream until smooth. Beat in salt to taste — potato is bland and needs more than you'd think. Keep it warm with the lid on until you plate up.

steps.title: Slice and plate
steps.content: Slice the steak, spoon on sauce, add rocket.
steps.detail: Slice the rested steak against the grain into thick strips. Spoon a mound of mash onto each plate, lean the steak against it, and ladle over the mushroom pepper sauce. Finish with a handful of rocket dressed in a little olive oil and salt.

title: Spinach Ricotta Filo Coil
description: A coiled Turkish-style borek — creamy spinach, ricotta and feta wrapped in shatteringly crisp filo and baked golden in a spiral. Just as good warm or cold, cut into wedges to share.
equipment: round cake tin or oven tray
storage: Keeps 2 days in the fridge. Re-crisp in a hot oven for 10 minutes rather than the microwave, which turns the filo soggy.
make_ahead: Assemble the coil, cover and refrigerate for up to a day, then bake it fresh when you need it.
leftovers: A wedge is excellent cold in a lunchbox or packed for a picnic.

substitutions.swap: puff pastry for an easier, richer (though less shattery) version
well-drained cottage cheese
grated haloumi, or more ricotta with extra salt
silverbeet or kale, wilted and squeezed dry

steps.title: Wilt and squeeze the spinach
steps.content: Wilt the spinach, then squeeze out every drop.
steps.detail: Wilt the spinach in a dry or barely oiled pan over medium heat until collapsed, about 2 minutes, then tip into a sieve to cool. Once it's cool enough to handle, squeeze it hard in your hands to wring out the water — this is the most important step, as wet spinach makes soggy filo. Roughly chop the squeezed ball.

steps.title: Mix the cheese filling
steps.content: Beat ricotta, feta, egg, lemon and nutmeg together.
steps.detail: In a bowl, mash the ricotta with the crumbled feta, one of the eggs, the lemon zest, a grating of nutmeg and plenty of pepper. Stir through the chopped spinach. The feta is salty, so taste before adding any salt. It should be thick and scoopable, not runny.

steps.title: Fill a filo rope
steps.content: Brush filo, spoon filling along the edge, roll.
steps.detail: Lay one filo sheet on the bench with a long edge towards you and brush lightly with oil; lay a second on top and brush again. Keep the rest under a damp tea towel so it doesn't dry out. Spoon a line of filling along the long edge nearest you, leaving a border, then roll it up into a long, loose rope.
tips: Work with two sheets at a time and keep the pile covered — filo dries to brittle shards within minutes of being exposed to the air.

steps.title: Coil into the tin
steps.content: Curl the ropes into a spiral in the tin.
steps.detail: Lightly oil the tin. Curl the first rope into a loose spiral in the centre, then join each new rope onto the end of the last, coiling outwards until the tin is filled like a snail shell. Don't wind it too tight or it'll burst as it bakes. Brush the top all over with oil and the remaining beaten egg for shine.

steps.title: Bake until crisp
steps.content: Bake at 180°C until deep golden and crisp.
steps.detail: Bake at 180°C fan for about 35 minutes, until the whole coil is deep golden brown and crisp right through — a pale borek will be doughy inside. If the top browns too fast, lay a loose sheet of foil over it. Let it settle for 10 minutes before cutting so it holds together.
warnings: The tin and filling are very hot out of the oven — let it rest before you cut into it.

title: Chocolate Banana Muffins
description: Deeply chocolatey, properly moist muffins that use up spotty bananas, studded with melting chocolate chips. One bowl for the wet, one for the dry, and no mixer needed.
storage: Keep 3 days in an airtight container, or freeze for up to 2 months — a frozen muffin thaws in a lunchbox by mid-morning.
make_ahead: These freeze beautifully; bake a batch and pull them out one at a time.
leftovers: Split a day-old muffin, warm it through, and spread with a little butter.

substitutions.swap: roughly chopped dark chocolate
the riper and spottier the better; or 2/3 cup unsweetened apple puree
self-raising flour — then leave out the baking powder
the same amount of melted butter, cooled

steps.title: Heat oven and mash bananas
steps.content: Heat oven to 180°C, line the tin, mash the bananas.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 180°C fan and line 6–8 holes of a muffin tin with paper cases. Peel the bananas into a large bowl and mash with a fork until fairly smooth — the riper and browner they are, the sweeter and more banana-y the muffins. A few small lumps are fine.

steps.title: Whisk the wet mix
steps.content: Whisk eggs, sugar, oil and milk into the banana.
steps.detail: Whisk the eggs, sugar, oil and milk into the mashed banana until well combined and a little frothy. Whisking properly here dissolves the sugar and gives an even crumb. It'll look thin — that's exactly right before the dry mix goes in.

steps.title: Fold in cocoa and chips
steps.content: Fold flour, cocoa, baking powder and chips through.
steps.detail: Sift the flour, cocoa and baking powder over the wet mix to catch any lumps, then add most of the chocolate chips. Fold gently with a spatula just until no dry streaks remain — a few folds too many and the muffins turn tough and tunnelled. The batter should be thick and droppable.
tips: Stop mixing the moment the flour disappears; a few lumps are better than an overworked batter.

steps.title: Fill and bake
steps.content: Divide, top with chips, bake ~20 min, then cool.
steps.detail: Spoon the batter into the cases until three-quarters full and scatter the reserved chocolate chips on top. Bake for about 20 minutes, until the tops are domed and springy and a skewer comes out with just a few moist crumbs — a smear of melted chip doesn't count. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes, then lift onto a rack.
warnings: Don't open the oven in the first 15 minutes — the rush of cold air can make the muffins sink in the middle.

title: Fig Honey Poached Fruit
description: Fresh figs gently poached in a honey and cinnamon syrup until soft and jammy, spooned over whipped mascarpone with toasted walnuts. An elegant dessert in under twenty minutes.
equipment: wide saucepan or deep frying pan
storage: The poached figs and syrup keep 3 days in the fridge and are lovely cold. Whip the mascarpone fresh and keep it separate until serving.
make_ahead: Poach the figs and reduce the syrup a day ahead; they only get better sitting in the honey.
leftovers: Spoon leftover figs and syrup over morning porridge, yoghurt or a scoop of ice cream.

substitutions.swap: fresh pears or peaches, cut into wedges and poached a little longer
thick Greek yoghurt, or a scoop of vanilla ice cream
pecans or hazelnuts
maple syrup

steps.title: Make the honey syrup
steps.content: Simmer honey, water, lemon, cinnamon and vanilla.
steps.detail: In a wide pan, warm the honey with a cup of water, a couple of strips of lemon peel, a squeeze of lemon juice, the cinnamon and the vanilla. Stir over medium heat until the honey dissolves and it comes to a gentle simmer. Taste — it should be lightly sweet and fragrant, not cloying.

steps.title: Poach the figs
steps.content: Halve the figs and poach gently until softened.
steps.detail: Halve the figs from stem to base and lay them cut-side up in the simmering syrup. Poach very gently for about 5 minutes, spooning syrup over the tops, until they're softened and glistening but still holding their shape. Fresh figs are delicate — a hard boil collapses them into mush.
warnings: Simmering syrup clings to skin and scalds worse than water — keep the heat gentle and use a long spoon.

steps.title: Reduce the syrup
steps.content: Lift out the figs, reduce the syrup to a glaze.
steps.detail: Lift the figs out with a slotted spoon onto a plate. Turn the heat up and let the syrup bubble for about 5 minutes until reduced by half and lightly thickened into a glossy glaze that coats a spoon. Remember it thickens further as it cools.

steps.title: Toast the walnuts
steps.content: Toast walnuts in a dry pan until fragrant.
steps.detail: Toast the walnuts in a dry pan over medium heat for 2–3 minutes, tossing, until they smell nutty and colour slightly. Tip them out straight away and roughly chop — toasting wakes up their flavour and adds crunch against the soft figs.
warnings: Nuts burn fast and turn bitter — keep them moving and pull them off the heat the moment they smell toasty.

steps.title: Whip the mascarpone
steps.content: Loosen the mascarpone with a little honey.
steps.detail: Beat the mascarpone in a bowl with a small drizzle of honey until smooth and soft enough to spoon — just a few strokes, as it can turn grainy if overbeaten. A little squeeze of lemon keeps it fresh against the sweet figs.

steps.title: Spoon and serve
steps.content: Dollop mascarpone, top with figs, syrup and walnuts.
steps.detail: Spoon a generous dollop of mascarpone into each bowl and nestle the warm figs on top. Drizzle over the honey glaze and scatter with the toasted walnuts. Lovely while the figs are still warm and the mascarpone cool.

title: Zucchini Feta Mint Pasta
description: Summery and quick — pasta tangled with pan-golden zucchini, salty feta melting into a light sauce, and a bright lift of mint and lemon.
storage: Best eaten fresh, but leftovers keep 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Loosen with a splash of water and a drizzle of oil when reheating, as the pasta drinks up the sauce.

substitutions.swap: any short pasta like penne or fusilli holds the sauce just as well
a milder crumble of ricotta, or goat's cheese for extra tang
basil, or a mix of both

steps.title: Prep zucchini and mint
steps.content: Slice zucchini, mince garlic, pick mint, zest lemon.
steps.detail: Trim the zucchini and slice into thin coins, or coarsely grate if you'd rather it melt into the sauce. Finely mince the garlic and pick the mint leaves, tearing any big ones. Zest the lemon into a small bowl, then cut it in half ready to juice. Keep the garlic separate from the zucchini — it goes in later and burns in seconds.
tips: Salting the zucchini coins and leaving them 5 minutes draws out water so they brown instead of stewing.

steps.title: Boil the pasta
steps.content: Cook pasta in well-salted water until al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it well — it should taste like the sea. Add the pasta, stir so it doesn't clump, and cook 1 minute short of the packet time. Just before draining, scoop out a mugful of the starchy cooking water — it's what pulls the sauce together later.

steps.title: Fry the zucchini golden
steps.content: Fry zucchini in olive oil until soft and golden.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high until it shimmers. Add the zucchini in a single layer and leave it to colour before stirring — you want golden, caramelised edges, not pale and watery. This takes about 8 minutes; it'll soften and start to collapse. Season with salt and pepper.
tips: Resist stirring too often — contact with the hot pan is what gives you colour and sweetness.

steps.title: Add garlic and lemon
steps.content: Stir in garlic and lemon zest for 30 seconds.
steps.detail: Turn the heat down to medium and stir the garlic and lemon zest through the zucchini. Cook just until it smells fragrant, about 30 seconds — don't let the garlic brown or it turns bitter. Squeeze in the juice of half the lemon and let it sizzle for a moment.
warnings: Garlic scorches fast on a hot pan; if it starts to colour too quickly, pull the pan off the heat straight away.

steps.title: Toss with feta and mint
steps.content: Toss pasta, feta and mint with a splash of pasta water.
steps.detail: Drag the drained pasta straight into the pan with a splash of the reserved pasta water. Crumble in most of the feta and toss over low heat for a minute — the cheese half-melts into a light, creamy sauce that clings to the pasta. Add more pasta water if it looks tight. Off the heat, fold through the mint, then scatter the last of the feta, a crack of pepper and a squeeze of lemon on top.
tips: Save the smaller whole mint leaves to scatter on top so they stay bright and fresh-looking.

title: Tofu Green Bean Green Curry
description: A fragrant weeknight green curry — crisp-fried tofu and just-tender green beans in silky coconut, spooned over fluffy jasmine rice with torn basil.
storage: The curry keeps 3 days in the fridge; store the rice separately and eat it within a day. Reheat the curry gently so the coconut milk doesn't split.
make_ahead: The curry reheats beautifully — make it a day ahead and cook fresh rice to serve.

substitutions.swap: a drained tin of chickpeas for a no-press option
snow peas or broccoli florets
coriander, or a mix of both
red curry paste for a milder, sweeter curry

steps.title: Press and cube the tofu
steps.content: Press tofu dry and cube it; trim the beans.
steps.detail: Drain the tofu and press it: wrap in a clean tea towel, sit a plate on top and leave 10 minutes to squeeze out water so it fries instead of stewing. Cut into 3cm cubes. Trim the green beans and cut into finger-lengths, and pick the basil leaves off their stalks.
tips: The drier the tofu, the crispier it fries and the better it soaks up the curry sauce.

steps.title: Cook the jasmine rice
steps.content: Simmer rinsed rice under a lid until fluffy.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice under cold water until it runs clear, then tip into a saucepan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, drop to the lowest heat, cover, and cook 12 minutes. Leave it covered off the heat for another 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork — don't lift the lid while it steams.

steps.title: Fry the tofu golden
steps.content: Pan-fry the tofu cubes until crisp and golden.
steps.detail: Heat the vegetable oil in a large frying pan or wok over medium-high. Add the tofu in one layer and fry, turning every couple of minutes, until several sides are golden and crisp — about 8 minutes. Lift out and set aside; it will finish softening in the sauce later.
warnings: Lower the tofu into the pan gently — any water clinging to it can make the hot oil spit and splatter.

steps.title: Fry the curry paste
steps.content: Sizzle the green curry paste, then pour in coconut milk.
steps.detail: In the same pan, spoon in the green curry paste and fry for a minute until it smells fragrant and toasty. Pour in the coconut milk, scraping up any tasty bits stuck to the pan, and bring to a gentle simmer — it will turn a pale jade green and loosen into a sauce.
tips: Not all green curry paste is vegan — check the label for shrimp paste or fish sauce if that matters to you.

steps.title: Simmer beans and tofu
steps.content: Add the beans and tofu; simmer until just tender.
steps.detail: Slide the green beans and fried tofu into the sauce and simmer for 8 minutes, until the beans are just tender with a little bite and the tofu has drunk up the flavour. Stir now and then. The sauce should coat the back of a spoon; if it's too thick, loosen with a splash of water.

steps.title: Season and finish
steps.content: Balance with soy and lime; scatter over the basil.
steps.detail: Take the pan off the heat and stir in the soy sauce and a good squeeze of lime — taste and add more of either until it's savoury, rich and a touch zingy. Tear in most of the basil. Spoon over the fluffy jasmine rice with the last of the basil and a lime wedge on the side.

title: Cauliflower Turmeric Soup
description: Silky, golden and warming — cauliflower simmered soft with turmeric and garlic, blitzed with cream and finished with toasted almonds.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge or freezes for 3 months (freeze before adding cream for the best texture). Reheat gently and don't boil, or the cream can split.

substitutions.swap: coconut milk for a dairy-free soup that suits the turmeric beautifully
toasted pine nuts, or croutons for extra crunch
chicken stock if that's what you have on hand

steps.title: Prep cauliflower and onion
steps.content: Break cauliflower into florets; dice onion and garlic.
steps.detail: Cut the cauliflower into small, even florets so they cook quickly — the smaller they are, the faster the soup is ready. Dice the onion and finely chop the garlic. Set aside a few tiny florets if you'd like to fry them for a garnish later.

steps.title: Soften onion and bloom turmeric
steps.content: Sweat onion and garlic, then toast the turmeric.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the onion with a pinch of salt and cook gently for 6 minutes until soft and translucent but not browned. Stir in the garlic and turmeric and cook 1 minute more — blooming the turmeric in the oil wakes up its earthy flavour and deep golden colour.
tips: Turmeric stains everything yellow — wipe the board and your hands promptly, and mind pale clothing.

steps.title: Simmer the cauliflower
steps.content: Add cauliflower and stock; simmer until tender.
steps.detail: Tip in the cauliflower and pour over the vegetable stock — it should just cover the florets, so top up with a little water if needed. Bring to the boil, then simmer for about 18 minutes until a knife slides through the cauliflower with no resistance. It needs to be properly soft to blend silky-smooth.

steps.title: Blend until silky
steps.content: Blitz smooth, then stir through the cream.
steps.detail: Take the pan off the heat and blitz with a stick blender until completely smooth and glossy — a good couple of minutes, chasing down any lumps. Stir in most of the cream and warm through gently without boiling. Taste and season well with salt and pepper; a soup this simple needs enough salt to sing.
warnings: Hot soup can erupt when blended — keep the blender head fully submerged and start it slowly.

steps.title: Toast almonds and serve
steps.content: Toast the flaked almonds and scatter over the soup.
steps.detail: In a small dry frying pan over medium heat, toast the flaked almonds for a couple of minutes, shaking often, until golden and nutty — watch closely as they catch fast. Ladle the soup into bowls, swirl in the last of the cream, and finish with the almonds, a crack of pepper and a drizzle of olive oil.

title: Haloumi Quinoa Roast Veg Bowl
description: A hearty bowl of fluffy quinoa, sticky roast zucchini and capsicum, and golden pan-fried haloumi, pulled together with a garlicky lemon yoghurt.
storage: The quinoa and roast veg keep 3 days in the fridge; fry the haloumi and dress fresh, as it doesn't reheat well. Store the components separately.
make_ahead: Roast the veg and cook the quinoa ahead — then it's just a quick fry of haloumi and a stir of yoghurt at dinnertime.

substitutions.swap: couscous or brown rice
crumbled feta, or firm tofu to keep it vegan
plant-based yoghurt for dairy-free
eggplant or pumpkin, cut a little smaller

steps.title: Heat oven and chop veg
steps.content: Oven to 220°C; chop zucchini, capsicum and haloumi.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Cut the zucchini into thick half-moons and the capsicum into strips, discarding the seeds and white pith. Slice the haloumi into 1cm slabs and pat dry. Grate or crush the garlic ready for the yoghurt, and cut the lemon into wedges.

steps.title: Roast the vegetables
steps.content: Toss veg in oil and roast until caramelised.
steps.detail: Spread the zucchini and capsicum on a large tray, toss with the olive oil, salt and pepper, and roast for about 22 minutes until soft and caramelised at the edges. Give them a shake halfway. Don't crowd the tray — cram them together and they'll steam pale instead of roasting.

steps.title: Cook the quinoa
steps.content: Simmer rinsed quinoa until the grains uncurl.
steps.detail: Rinse the quinoa in a sieve — this washes off its natural bitter coating. Tip into a saucepan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt, bring to the boil, then cover and simmer 15 minutes until the little grains have uncurled their white tails and the water is absorbed. Rest 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork.

steps.title: Pan-fry the haloumi
steps.content: Fry the haloumi slabs golden on both sides.
steps.detail: Heat a dry frying pan over medium-high. Lay in the haloumi and fry for 2 to 3 minutes each side until a deep golden crust forms and the cheese softens inside. Haloumi needs no oil — it releases its own. Eat it while it's hot and squeaky, as it turns rubbery once it cools.
tips: A squeeze of lemon over the haloumi the moment it leaves the pan cuts its saltiness.

steps.title: Mix the lemon yoghurt
steps.content: Stir yoghurt with garlic, lemon and salt.
steps.detail: In a small bowl, stir the yoghurt with the grated garlic, a good squeeze of lemon juice and a pinch of salt. Loosen with a teaspoon of water to a drizzling consistency. Taste — it should be tangy, garlicky and bright against the rich haloumi.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Pile quinoa, veg and haloumi; drizzle the yoghurt.
steps.detail: Spoon the fluffy quinoa into bowls and pile on the roast vegetables and golden haloumi. Drizzle over the lemon yoghurt and finish with a crack of pepper and a lemon wedge. Serve straight away while the haloumi is still warm and soft.

title: Lentil Tomato Couscous Skillet
description: A cosy one-pan dinner — red lentils simmered into spiced tomato, then couscous steamed right in the pan until fluffy. Proper budget comfort.
equipment: large deep frying pan with lid
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. The couscous drinks up moisture, so loosen with a splash of water or stock when you reheat.
leftovers: Lovely cold the next day as a lunch salad — add a squeeze of lemon and a handful of crumbled feta.

substitutions.swap: a drained tin of lentils or chickpeas — stir in near the end, as they're already cooked
quinoa or rice, cooked separately
coriander or mint
chicken stock, or water with a good pinch of salt

steps.title: Dice onion and garlic
steps.content: Dice onion, mince garlic, chop parsley, rinse lentils.
steps.detail: Dice the onion into small, even pieces and finely mince the garlic. Roughly chop the parsley — stalks and all for the cooking, leaves saved for the top. Rinse the red lentils in a sieve until the water runs clear.

steps.title: Toast cumin and soften onion
steps.content: Soften the onion, then bloom the cumin and garlic.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a large deep frying pan over medium heat. Add the onion with a pinch of salt and cook 6 minutes until soft. Stir in the garlic and cumin and cook for a minute until fragrant — toasting the cumin in the oil deepens its warm, smoky flavour.
warnings: Ground cumin scorches quickly; keep it moving and add the wet ingredients promptly so it doesn't turn bitter.

steps.title: Simmer the lentils
steps.content: Add lentils, tomato and stock; simmer until thick.
steps.detail: Tip in the rinsed lentils, the tinned tomatoes and 2 cups of the stock, crushing the tomatoes against the pan. Bring to a simmer and cook about 18 minutes, stirring now and then, until the lentils collapse into a thick, saucy stew. Add a splash more stock if it dries out before the lentils are soft.
tips: Red lentils catch on the base as they thicken — scrape the bottom of the pan each time you stir.

steps.title: Steam in the couscous
steps.content: Stir in couscous with hot stock; cover off the heat.
steps.detail: Stir the couscous through the lentils with the last of the hot stock, making sure the grains are just submerged — add a splash of boiling water if not. Clamp on a lid, take the pan off the heat and leave it 5 minutes so the couscous swells and steams to fluffy. Don't stir while it steams.

steps.title: Fluff and finish
steps.content: Fork it through, season, and scatter over parsley.
steps.detail: Fluff everything together with a fork, breaking up any clumps of couscous. Taste and season well with salt and pepper — lentils are hungry for salt. Drizzle with olive oil and scatter the parsley leaves over the top.

title: Apple Cheddar Cabbage Slaw
description: Crunchy and sweet-sharp — shredded cabbage, matchsticks of apple and tasty cheddar in a tangy mustard-mayo. The slaw that outshines the mains.
storage: Best within a few hours of dressing, as the cabbage softens and weeps. Undressed, the shredded veg keeps 2 days in the fridge.
make_ahead: Shred the veg and whisk the dressing separately up to a day ahead, then toss together with fresh apple just before serving.

substitutions.swap: half mayo, half Greek yoghurt for a lighter, tangier dressing
any tasty cheese, or a sharp vintage for more punch
a firm pear, or leave out and add extra carrot

steps.title: Shred cabbage and carrot
steps.content: Finely shred the cabbage and coarsely grate the carrot.
steps.detail: Cut the core out of the cabbage wedge and shred it as finely as you can with a sharp knife — thin ribbons soak up the dressing best. Coarsely grate the carrots or cut them into matchsticks. Pile both into a large bowl.
tips: A pinch of salt tossed through the shredded cabbage now, left 10 minutes, softens it and stops the slaw watering down later.

steps.title: Cut apple and cheddar
steps.content: Matchstick the apple and cheddar; toss apple in lemon.
steps.detail: Core the apples and cut into matchsticks — skin on for colour and crunch. Toss them straight away with a squeeze of lemon so they don't brown while you work. Cut the cheddar into small matchsticks, or coarsely grate it if you'd rather.

steps.title: Whisk the dressing
steps.content: Whisk mayo, dijon, lemon and oil into a dressing.
steps.detail: In a small bowl, whisk the mayo, dijon, the rest of the lemon juice and the olive oil with a crack of pepper until smooth and pourable. Taste it — it should be creamy with a mustardy tang and a squeak of acidity. Loosen with a teaspoon of water if it's too thick to coat.

steps.title: Toss and rest
steps.content: Fold everything through the dressing, then rest.
steps.detail: Add the apple and cheddar to the cabbage and carrot, pour over the dressing and toss until every strand is glossy. Let it sit 10 minutes for the flavours to mingle and the cabbage to relax, then taste for salt and pepper. Give it a final toss just before serving.

title: Chicken Caesar-ish Toasties
description: Everything you love about a chicken caesar, pressed hot between toast — juicy pan-fried chicken, crisp bacon, garlicky parmesan mayo and cool cos.
equipment: sandwich press or heavy frying pan
storage: Best eaten hot and fresh. The cooked chicken keeps 2 to 3 days in the fridge on its own — build and toast to order, as an assembled toastie goes soggy.

substitutions.swap: leftover roast chook, or chicken thigh for juicier meat
leave it out for a lighter toastie
any hard grating cheese, or cheddar for extra melt
sourdough or a good sturdy sandwich loaf

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.content: Season and pan-fry chicken to 74°C, then slice.
steps.detail: Slice thick chicken breasts through the middle to make thinner, even cutlets so they cook fast without drying out. Rub with a little olive oil, salt and pepper. Fry in a hot pan for 4 to 5 minutes each side until golden and cooked through — 74°C at the thickest point, with no pink and clear juices. Rest 5 minutes, then slice.
warnings: Wash hands, board and knife after handling raw chicken, and keep it away from the bread and lettuce.
Chicken must reach 74°C in the centre — check the thickest slice before serving.

steps.title: Crisp the bacon
steps.content: Fry the bacon until crisp, then drain.
steps.detail: In the same pan, fry the bacon over medium heat until deeply golden and crisp, about 5 minutes, turning once. Lift onto paper towel to drain. Leave a little of the tasty bacon fat in the pan for toasting the sandwiches later.

steps.title: Mix the caesar mayo
steps.content: Stir mayo with parmesan, garlic and worcestershire.
steps.detail: In a bowl, stir the mayo with most of the grated parmesan, the crushed garlic, the worcestershire and a good crack of pepper. This is your quick caesar-ish dressing — savoury, sharp and garlicky. Taste and add more worcestershire if you want it punchier.

steps.title: Build the toasties
steps.content: Spread the mayo and layer chicken, bacon and cos.
steps.detail: Spread the caesar mayo over all the bread. On four slices, layer the sliced chicken, crisp bacon and shredded lettuce, then top with the remaining parmesan and the other slices. Brush the outsides lightly with olive oil so they toast up golden.

steps.title: Toast until golden
steps.content: Press and toast until crisp and the cheese melts.
steps.detail: Toast in a sandwich press, or in the pan under a weight such as a second pan pressed on top, for about 4 minutes — turning if using a pan — until both sides are deep golden and crisp and the parmesan has melted at the edges. The lettuce stays cool and crunchy inside. Cut on the diagonal and eat hot.

title: Lamb Cutlet Couscous Plate
description: Quick-seared lamb cutlets, crusted with cumin, on fluffy couscous with a cooling cucumber-mint yoghurt and a squeeze of lemon.
equipment: large frying pan or grill pan
storage: Store the components separately in the fridge: cooked lamb and couscous keep 2 to 3 days, the yoghurt sauce 2 days. The lamb is best reheated briefly, or eaten cold and sliced over the couscous.
make_ahead: The couscous and minty yoghurt can be made a few hours ahead — sear the cutlets fresh just before serving.

substitutions.swap: lamb loin chops or backstrap, adjusting the time for thickness
quinoa, or a warm pita on the side
plant-based yoghurt for dairy-free
parsley, or a mix of both

steps.title: Season the cutlets
steps.content: Rub cutlets with oil, cumin, salt and pepper.
steps.detail: Pat the lamb cutlets dry with paper towel — dry meat sears and browns far better. Rub them all over with a little olive oil, the cumin, salt and pepper. Leave them out of the fridge for 15 minutes to take the chill off, so they cook evenly.
warnings: Wash your hands and the board after handling the raw lamb, before you touch anything else.

steps.title: Steam the couscous
steps.content: Pour boiling water over couscous and cover.
