steps.detail: Put the couscous in a heatproof bowl with a pinch of salt and a drizzle of olive oil. Pour over 1 1/2 cups of boiling water — just enough to cover by a fingernail — then cover tightly with a plate and leave 5 minutes. Fluff with a fork; the grains should be tender and separate.

steps.title: Make the minty yoghurt
steps.content: Stir yoghurt with cucumber, mint and garlic.
steps.detail: Coarsely grate the cucumber, then squeeze out the excess water in your hands or a tea towel so the sauce doesn't go watery. Stir it through the yoghurt with the crushed garlic, most of the chopped mint, a squeeze of lemon and a pinch of salt. Taste — it should be cool, tangy and fresh.

steps.title: Sear the cutlets
steps.content: Sear cutlets 2–3 min a side, then rest.
steps.detail: Heat a frying or grill pan until very hot. Sear the cutlets for 2 to 3 minutes each side for pink and juicy, standing them on their fatty edge for a moment to render and crisp. They should have a deep brown crust and feel springy, not soft. Rest on a warm plate for 5 minutes so the juices settle back in.
tips: Cook them in batches if your pan is crowded — cutlets jammed together steam and go grey instead of searing.

steps.title: Plate it up
steps.content: Pile couscous, cutlets, yoghurt and lemon.
steps.detail: Fork the couscous onto plates and lean the lamb cutlets on top. Add a generous dollop of the minty cucumber yoghurt, scatter over the last of the mint, and tuck in a lemon wedge to squeeze over. Spoon any resting juices from the plate back over the lamb.

title: Lamb Mince Filo Coil
description: Spiced lamb mince curled inside crisp filo into a golden spiral, baked until it shatters, and served with cool minty yoghurt for dipping.
equipment: round baking tin or oven tray
storage: Keeps 2 days in the fridge. Re-crisp in a hot oven rather than the microwave, which turns the filo soft and soggy.
make_ahead: Make and cool the lamb filling up to 2 days ahead; roll, coil and bake fresh so the filo stays crisp.

substitutions.swap: beef mince, or half lamb and half beef
not a true swap, but puff pastry makes an easier, richer coil
plant-based yoghurt for dairy-free
parsley or dill, or a mix

steps.title: Brown the spiced lamb
steps.content: Cook onion, lamb, garlic and cumin until dry.
steps.detail: Finely dice the onion and cook in a splash of olive oil over medium heat until soft, about 5 minutes. Turn up the heat, add the lamb mince and break it up, browning until no pink remains and any liquid has cooked off. Stir in the garlic, cumin, salt, pepper and the pine nuts if using for a final minute. The filling must be fairly dry, or it will make the filo soggy.
tips: Spread the cooked filling on a plate to cool for 10 minutes — hot filling steams and tears the delicate filo.
warnings: Wash hands, board and utensils after handling the raw lamb mince, and cook it right through until no pink remains.

steps.title: Roll the filo logs
steps.content: Brush filo, add filling and roll into long logs.
steps.detail: Once the filling is cool, stir through half the chopped mint. Lay one filo sheet out, brush lightly with olive oil and lay a second sheet on top. Spoon a line of filling along one long edge, leaving a border, then roll up into a long, snug log. Repeat to make four logs.
warnings: Filo dries out and shatters within minutes — keep the sheets you're not using covered with a damp tea towel.

steps.title: Coil into the tin
steps.content: Curl the logs into a spiral in an oiled tin.
steps.detail: Lightly oil a round baking tin or oven tray. Take one log and curl it into a tight spiral in the centre, then join the next log to its end and keep coiling outward, like a snail's shell, until you've used them all. Brush the whole coil generously with olive oil so it bakes up golden and crisp.

steps.title: Bake until crisp
steps.content: Bake at 190°C until deep golden and shattering.
steps.detail: Bake at 190°C fan for about 32 minutes, until the coil is deep golden brown and crisp all over — if the top browns too fast, drop the temperature slightly. It should sound hollow and crackly when tapped. Resist opening the oven in the first 20 minutes so it crisps evenly.

steps.title: Stir the minty yoghurt
steps.content: Stir yoghurt with the remaining mint and salt.
steps.detail: While the coil bakes, stir the yoghurt with the rest of the chopped mint and a pinch of salt until smooth and flecked green. It's the cool, tangy foil to the rich, spiced lamb. Keep it in the fridge until the coil comes out.

steps.title: Rest, slice and serve
steps.content: Rest the coil, then cut into wedges.
steps.detail: Let the coil settle for 5 minutes out of the oven — it firms up and slices far more neatly warm than piping hot. Cut into wedges like a cake and serve with the minty yoghurt for dipping. It's best fresh, while the filo still shatters.

title: Cheddar Corn Muffins
description: Fluffy savoury muffins loaded with sweet corn and sharp cheddar, with a gritty golden cornbread bite — best warm from the tin.
storage: Keep 2 days in an airtight container, or freeze for up to 2 months. Warm through, or split and toast, to bring them back to life.
leftovers: Split and toast with butter, or serve alongside a bowl of soup or chilli.

substitutions.swap: 1 cup milk with 1 tbsp lemon juice, left 5 minutes to curdle
any tasty melting cheese, or a little parmesan for sharpness
a drained tin of corn kernels
just use extra flour if you have none, for a softer muffin

steps.title: Heat oven and prep tin
steps.content: Oven to 190°C; grease a 12-hole muffin tin.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 190°C fan and grease a 12-hole muffin tin or line it with cases. Tip the frozen corn into a sieve and rinse under warm water to thaw, then shake it dry — wet corn will make the batter soggy.

steps.title: Mix the dry ingredients
steps.content: Whisk flour, polenta, baking powder, salt and pepper.
steps.detail: In a large bowl, whisk together the flour, polenta, baking powder, salt and pepper. The polenta gives the muffins that gritty, golden cornbread character. Make a well in the centre ready for the wet mix.

steps.title: Whisk the wet ingredients
steps.content: Beat eggs, buttermilk and olive oil together.
steps.detail: In a jug, whisk the eggs, buttermilk and olive oil until smooth and pale. The buttermilk reacts with the baking powder for lift, and keeps the crumb tender and gently tangy.

steps.title: Fold in corn and cheddar
steps.content: Fold wet into dry, then the corn and most of the cheese.
steps.detail: Pour the wet into the dry and fold with a few gentle strokes, then add the thawed corn and most of the cheddar and fold until only just combined — lumpy batter is exactly right. Overmixing gives tough, tunnelled muffins, so stop while you can still see a streak or two of flour.
tips: Toss the corn and cheese in a little of the flour first so they stay suspended rather than sinking to the bottom.

steps.title: Fill, top and bake
steps.content: Fill the tin, top with cheese and bake 20 min.
steps.detail: Divide the batter between the muffin holes, filling each about three-quarters full, and scatter the reserved cheddar over the tops for a crisp cheesy crust. Bake for about 20 minutes until golden, domed and springy, and a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tin 5 minutes before lifting out — they're best warm.
warnings: Don't open the oven in the first 15 minutes, or the rising muffins can sink in the middle.

title: Chocolate Walnut Cookies
description: Thick, chewy cookies studded with pools of dark chocolate and crunchy walnuts. A little salt makes the chocolate sing.
storage: Keep in an airtight container for up to 5 days. The raw dough also freezes well rolled into balls — bake from frozen, adding a couple of minutes.
make_ahead: Roll the dough into balls and freeze; bake straight from the freezer for warm cookies any night.

substitutions.swap: pecans or hazelnuts, or leave out for nut-free
a good handful of chocolate chips
all caster sugar for a crisper, less chewy cookie

steps.title: Heat oven and prep
steps.content: Oven to 170°C; line trays; chop choc and walnuts.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 170°C fan and line two baking trays with paper. Roughly chop the dark chocolate into rubbly chunks — some big, some small, for pools of melted chocolate — and coarsely chop the walnuts. Make sure the butter is properly soft, so it creams easily.

steps.title: Cream butter and sugars
steps.content: Beat butter with both sugars until pale and fluffy.
steps.detail: Beat the softened butter with the caster sugar and brown sugar for 2 to 3 minutes until noticeably paler and fluffy — this whips in air for a lighter cookie. The brown sugar brings a toffee note and a chewy centre. Scrape down the bowl as you go.

steps.title: Beat in egg and vanilla
steps.content: Beat in the egg and vanilla until smooth.
steps.detail: Add the egg and vanilla and beat until just combined and smooth. It may look a little curdled — that's fine, the flour will bring it back together. Don't overbeat once the egg is in.

steps.title: Fold in flour and chocolate
steps.content: Fold through flour, salt, chocolate and walnuts.
steps.detail: Add the flour and salt and fold through with a spatula until nearly combined, then add the chopped chocolate and walnuts and fold until just mixed — don't overwork it or the cookies turn tough. The dough should be soft but scoopable; chill it 15 minutes if it feels too soft to handle.

steps.title: Scoop and bake
steps.content: Roll tablespoons, space apart, and bake 12 min.
steps.detail: Roll heaped tablespoons of dough into balls and space them well apart on the trays — at least 5cm, as they spread a lot. Bake for about 12 minutes until the edges are set and golden but the centres still look soft and underdone. They firm up as they cool, so pull them early for a chewy middle.
tips: For picture-perfect cookies, press a few extra chocolate chunks and walnut pieces onto the tops as they come out of the oven.
warnings: The trays and cookies are very hot and fragile straight from the oven — let them set on the tray for 5 minutes before moving.

title: Chicken Pesto-Style Rocket Pasta
description: A quick blender pesto of peppery rocket, basil and toasted pine nuts, tossed through pasta with golden pan-fried chicken thigh and a bright squeeze of lemon.
storage: Best eaten fresh. Leftovers keep 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge, though the pesto dulls in colour; the chicken is safe to reheat once, until piping hot.
make_ahead: Make the pesto up to 3 days ahead — press cling film onto the surface or float a little oil on top to stop it browning.
leftovers: Cold pesto pasta makes a great lunchbox salad — loosen with a splash of oil and a squeeze of lemon.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast, cooked a little less so it stays juicy
toasted almonds or walnuts, roughly chopped
any hard grating cheese — a block of tasty does the job

steps.title: Toast the pine nuts
steps.content: Dry-toast the pine nuts until golden and nutty.
steps.detail: Warm a dry frying pan over medium heat and tip in the pine nuts. Shake the pan often — in 2 to 3 minutes they turn from pale to gold and smell toasty. Tip them straight onto a plate the moment they colour; they keep cooking in a hot pan and go bitter if they scorch.
warnings: Pine nuts go from golden to burnt in seconds — don't walk away from the pan.

steps.title: Pan-fry the chicken
steps.content: Season and brown chicken thigh until cooked through, 74°C.
steps.detail: Pat the chicken thigh dry and season both sides with salt and pepper. Heat a tablespoon of the olive oil in the pan over medium-high until it shimmers, then lay the thighs in without crowding. Leave them undisturbed to colour, then flip — cook 5 to 6 minutes a side until deep golden and 74°C at the thickest part, with no pink left.
tips: Thigh is forgiving — a couple of extra minutes won't dry it out the way it would a breast.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the tongs after raw chicken, and keep it away from anything you'll eat uncooked.

steps.title: Boil the pasta
steps.content: Cook pasta in well-salted water until al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it well — it should taste like the sea. Add the pasta, stir, and cook to 1 minute under the packet time so it keeps some bite. Scoop out a mugful of the starchy cooking water before you drain — you'll use it to loosen the pesto.

steps.title: Blitz the rocket pesto
steps.content: Blend rocket, basil, garlic, pine nuts, parmesan and lemon.
steps.detail: Put the rocket, basil, garlic, most of the toasted pine nuts and the parmesan in a processor with the zest and juice of the lemon. Pulse to a rough paste, then pour in the olive oil with the motor running until it's a loose, spoonable green sauce. Taste and adjust with salt — rocket is peppery, so a little more lemon helps balance it.
tips: No processor? Chop everything finely by hand and stir in the oil — a rustic pesto is just as good.

steps.title: Rest and slice the chicken
steps.content: Rest the chicken, then slice into thick strips.
steps.detail: Move the chicken to a board and let it rest for 5 minutes so the juices settle back through the meat. Slice into thick strips across the grain — they stay juicier than thin slices and won't get lost in the pasta.

steps.title: Toss it all together
steps.content: Coat pasta in pesto, fold through chicken, finish with parmesan.
steps.detail: Return the drained pasta to the warm pot off the heat and spoon in the pesto, tossing to coat every strand. Splash in cooking water a little at a time until the sauce turns glossy and clings. Fold through the sliced chicken, then serve topped with the reserved pine nuts, extra parmesan and a good grind of pepper.
tips: Keep the pot off the heat — pesto stays fresh and green warmed through, not cooked.

title: Lamb Mince Chickpea Curry Bowl
description: Spiced lamb mince simmered with chickpeas and tomato until thick and glossy, spooned over fluffy basmati and finished with fresh coriander. A fast, freezer-friendly curry with real depth.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge, or freeze the curry (without rice) for up to 3 months. Cool cooked rice quickly and eat it within a day.
make_ahead: The curry is even better the next day — make it up to 2 days ahead and reheat gently while the rice cooks.
leftovers: Spoon leftovers into wraps with a little rice, or pile over a jacket potato.

substitutions.swap: beef or pork mince
tinned brown lentils or beans
a mix of ground cumin, turmeric and a pinch of chilli powder

steps.title: Chop the onion and garlic
steps.content: Finely dice the onion and mince the garlic.
steps.detail: Halve and peel the brown onion, then dice it small — about 5mm — so it melts into the sauce. Smash the garlic cloves under the flat of your knife to slip off the skins, then mince or grate them. Keep the garlic in its own pile; it burns faster than onion and goes in later.

steps.title: Cook the basmati
steps.content: Simmer rinsed basmati by absorption, 12 min.
steps.detail: Rinse the basmati in a sieve until the water runs clear, then tip into a saucepan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, drop to the lowest heat, cover and cook 12 minutes without lifting the lid. Pull it off the heat and let it steam, covered, for 5 more minutes, then fluff with a fork.
tips: Rinsing washes off surface starch so the grains stay separate and fluffy, not gluey.

steps.title: Brown the lamb mince
steps.content: Fry lamb mince until well browned; drain the fat.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a wide pan over medium-high. Add the lamb mince in chunks and leave it to sit for a couple of minutes before breaking it up — you want deep brown crust, not grey stew. Season with salt and pepper as it cooks. Lamb is fatty, so tip off most of the fat once browned, leaving a little for flavour.
warnings: Wash your hands and any utensils that touched the raw mince before they go near other food.

steps.title: Toast the curry powder
steps.content: Cook onion, garlic and curry powder until fragrant.
steps.detail: Add the onion to the browned lamb and cook 4 minutes until soft, then stir in the garlic and curry powder. Keep it moving for a minute — the spices should smell warm and toasty as they cook in the oil, which wakes up their flavour. If it looks dry or starts to catch, add a splash of water.
tips: Blooming curry powder in the hot fat for a minute makes a far deeper curry than just simmering it in the sauce.

steps.title: Simmer with tomato and chickpeas
steps.content: Add tomato and drained chickpeas; simmer 15 min.
steps.detail: Pour in the tinned tomatoes and half a tin of water, then add the drained, rinsed chickpeas. Season with salt, bring to a simmer and cook uncovered for 15 minutes, stirring now and then, until thick and glossy. Squash a few chickpeas against the side of the pan to thicken the sauce naturally.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Spoon curry over basmati; top with coriander.
steps.detail: Spoon the fluffy basmati into bowls and ladle the lamb curry over the top. Scatter with roughly chopped coriander and add a good grind of pepper. Serve while it's hot and steaming.

title: Prawn Laksa-ish Noodle Soup
description: A quick cheat's laksa — red curry paste fried into coconut and stock, plump prawns poached till just pink, and slippery rice noodles, brightened with lime and coriander.
storage: Best fresh. Store leftover broth and prawns (without the noodles) up to 1 day in the fridge — they're seafood — and reheat gently, cooking fresh noodles to serve.
make_ahead: Make the coconut broth ahead and reheat it, poaching the prawns and softening the noodles fresh just before serving.
leftovers: Any leftover broth is lovely the next day, poured hot over a fresh nest of noodles.

substitutions.swap: firm white fish or cubes of tofu
green curry paste for a fresher heat
vermicelli or egg noodles
a splash of soy sauce

steps.title: Peel and devein the prawns
steps.content: Peel prawns, remove the dark vein, pat dry.
steps.detail: Peel the prawns, leaving the tails on if you like. Run a small knife down the back of each and lift out the dark vein. Pat them dry on paper towel and keep in the fridge until the broth's ready — cold prawns hold their shape better when they hit the hot soup.
warnings: Keep raw prawns cold and separate, and wash your hands and board well before touching anything else.

steps.title: Soak the rice noodles
steps.content: Soften rice noodles in just-boiled water.
steps.detail: Put the rice noodles in a heatproof bowl and cover with boiling water. Leave them about 5 minutes until pliable but still with a little bite — they finish cooking in the hot broth. Drain, rinse under cold water to stop them sticking, and set aside.
tips: Toss the drained noodles with a drop of oil if they're sitting a while, so they don't clump into a block.

steps.title: Fry the curry paste
steps.content: Sizzle red curry paste in oil until fragrant.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large pot over medium. Add the red curry paste and fry for 1 to 2 minutes, stirring constantly, until it darkens a shade and smells fragrant. This quick fry blooms the spices and takes away any raw, harsh edge.
warnings: The paste can spit as it hits the hot oil — stand back and keep the heat at medium.

steps.title: Build the coconut broth
steps.content: Add coconut milk and stock; simmer and season.
steps.detail: Pour in the coconut milk and chicken stock, stirring the fried paste through until smooth. Bring to a gentle simmer and season with the fish sauce, which gives the salty, savoury backbone. Let it bubble softly for 5 minutes to come together, then taste and add more fish sauce if it needs it.

steps.title: Poach the prawns
steps.content: Slip prawns into the broth for 2–3 min.
steps.detail: Slide the prawns into the simmering broth and cook just 2 to 3 minutes, until they curl and turn opaque pink all the way through. Pull the pot off the heat straight away — prawns overcook fast and go rubbery if left to boil.

steps.title: Serve with lime and coriander
steps.content: Divide noodles into bowls and ladle over the soup.
steps.detail: Nest the drained noodles into deep bowls and ladle the prawns and hot broth over the top. Finish with a shower of coriander and a good squeeze of lime, which cuts through the rich coconut. Serve with extra lime wedges on the side.

title: Salmon Avocado Rice Bowl
description: Warm rice seasoned sushi-style, crisp-skinned salmon, cool avocado and cucumber, torn nori and a sesame-soy drizzle. A rice bowl that eats like a deconstructed sushi roll.
storage: Cooked rice and salmon keep 1 day in the fridge, stored separately. Slice the avocado fresh as it browns, and eat the salmon cold or gently reheated.
make_ahead: Cook the rice a day ahead and keep it chilled; warm it through and season with the vinegar just before building the bowls.
leftovers: Flake leftover salmon and rice into a quick fried rice with a splash of soy and any veg you have.

substitutions.swap: barramundi, or tinned tuna for a no-cook bowl
basmati or brown rice
tamari to keep it gluten-free
any mild vinegar with a pinch of sugar stirred in

steps.title: Cook the jasmine rice
steps.content: Steam rinsed jasmine rice by absorption.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice until the water runs clear, then cook with 3 cups of water: bring to the boil, cover, drop to the lowest heat and leave 12 minutes. Rest it off the heat, still covered, for 5 minutes so the grains firm up, then fluff with a fork.

steps.title: Season the rice sushi-style
steps.content: Fold rice vinegar through the warm rice.
steps.detail: While the rice is still warm, drizzle over the rice vinegar and a small pinch of salt, then fold it through gently with the fork so the grains stay whole. This gives it the lightly tangy, sushi-rice flavour that makes the bowl. Let it cool to just-warm while you cook the salmon.
tips: A teaspoon of sugar dissolved into the vinegar makes it taste even more like proper sushi rice.

steps.title: Sear the salmon
steps.content: Pan-sear salmon skin-side down until it flakes.
steps.detail: Pat the salmon dry and season with salt and pepper. Heat the olive oil in a non-stick pan over medium-high and lay the fillets in skin-side down, pressing gently for a few seconds so the skin stays flat. Cook 4 minutes until the skin is crisp, then flip and give it 2 to 3 minutes more, until it flakes easily and is just cooked through.
warnings: Wash your hands and board after handling the raw salmon before touching the rice or vegetables.

steps.title: Prep the avocado and cucumber
steps.content: Slice avocado, ribbon the cucumber, snip the nori.
steps.detail: Halve, stone and slice the avocado. Cut the cucumber into thin half-moons, or peel it into ribbons for a prettier bowl. Snip or tear the nori into thin strips with scissors. Do this last so the avocado doesn't brown.

steps.title: Whisk the drizzle and build
steps.content: Mix soy and sesame; layer everything over the rice.
steps.detail: Stir the soy sauce and sesame oil together in a small bowl. Spoon the seasoned rice into bowls and arrange the flaked salmon, avocado and cucumber on top. Scatter over the nori strips and spoon the sesame-soy dressing across the lot.

title: Saffron Prawn Rice Pan
description: A one-pan crowd-pleaser: saffron-stained rice cooked in stock with sweet capsicum and peas, prawns nestled in on top to steam. Not a strict paella, but the same golden, get-a-spoon-in feeling.
storage: Keeps 1 day in the fridge — with both cooked rice and prawns, eat it within a day and reheat until piping hot right through.
make_ahead: Bloom the saffron and chop the onion, garlic and capsicum ahead so the pan comes together fast when you're ready to cook.
leftovers: Leftovers make a great next-day lunch — a squeeze of lemon wakes the rice back up.

substitutions.swap: arborio or paella rice for a more traditional bite
chicken thigh pieces, or a mix of both
vegetable or fish stock
green beans, cut short

steps.title: Bloom the saffron
steps.content: Steep saffron threads in a little warm stock.
steps.detail: Warm a small splash of the chicken stock and crumble in the saffron threads. Leave it to steep while you cook — the liquid turns a deep gold and carries that honeyed saffron aroma right through the rice.
tips: A pinch of saffron is plenty; too much turns the dish bitter and medicinal.

steps.title: Peel the prawns
steps.content: Peel and devein prawns; keep them cold.
steps.detail: Peel the prawns, pull out the dark vein along the back, and pat them dry. Keep them in the fridge until the very end — they only need a few minutes on top of the rice, so there's no rush to bring them out early.
warnings: Keep raw prawns cold and wash your hands and board before handling anything else.

steps.title: Soften onion and capsicum
steps.content: Sauté onion and capsicum, then the garlic.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a wide pan over medium and cook the diced onion and capsicum for 6 to 7 minutes until soft and sweet. Stir in the garlic and cook another minute until fragrant. Season with salt and pepper — this soffritto is the flavour base the whole pan builds on.

steps.title: Toast the rice
steps.content: Stir rice through the base to coat in oil.
steps.detail: Tip in the jasmine rice and stir for a minute so every grain glistens with the oil and takes on the colour of the vegetables. Toasting the rice briefly helps it stay separate and soak up the stock evenly rather than turning to mush.

steps.title: Simmer in saffron stock
steps.content: Add saffron stock; simmer without stirring, 18 min.
steps.detail: Pour in the saffron liquid and the rest of the stock, spread the rice level and bring to a simmer. Now leave it alone — no stirring — for about 18 minutes on low, so the rice cooks evenly and a little golden crust forms on the base. It's ready when the liquid is absorbed and the top grains are tender.
tips: Resist stirring — that crust on the bottom, the socarrat, is the best bit of a rice pan.

steps.title: Nestle in prawns and peas
steps.content: Press prawns and peas in; cook till prawns are pink.
steps.detail: Scatter the peas over the rice and press the prawns in on top. Cover with a lid or foil and cook 5 to 6 minutes, until the prawns curl and turn opaque pink and the peas are hot through. Rest the pan off the heat for a few minutes, then serve straight from the pan.

title: Broccoli Almond Yoghurt Salad
description: Blistered broccoli tossed in a lemony garlic-yoghurt dressing with toasted almonds and parsley. A green side that holds its own next to anything off the barbecue.
storage: Best eaten the day it's made. Undressed, the components keep separately in the fridge for 2 days; a dressed salad goes watery, so toss it together at the last minute.
make_ahead: Get ahead by keeping the blanched broccoli, the dressing and the toasted almonds separate in the fridge for up to 2 days, then toss together just before serving.
leftovers: Leftover salad is good chopped through cooked grains or piled into a wrap the next day.

substitutions.swap: cashews, or toasted flaked almonds
sour cream loosened with a little lemon
cauliflower, or a mix of both

steps.title: Cut the broccoli
steps.content: Cut broccoli into small florets; slice the stalk.
steps.detail: Cut the broccoli into small, bite-sized florets so they cook quickly and catch the dressing. Don't waste the stalk — peel off the tough outside and slice the tender core into coins. Even sizes mean everything cooks at the same rate.

steps.title: Toast the almonds
steps.content: Dry-toast almonds until golden; roughly chop.
steps.detail: Toast the almonds in a dry frying pan over medium heat, shaking often, for 3 to 4 minutes until golden and fragrant. Tip onto a board to cool, then roughly chop for crunch. Toasting deepens their flavour and makes them snap.
warnings: Nuts scorch quickly once they colour — keep them moving and pull them off the heat while golden.

steps.title: Blanch and char the broccoli
steps.content: Boil broccoli 2 min, then blister in a hot pan.
steps.detail: Drop the florets into boiling salted water for 2 minutes, until just tender but still bright green, then drain well. Heat the olive oil in a hot pan and toss the broccoli for a couple of minutes to catch some golden, blistered edges. Those charred bits add a nutty flavour the yoghurt loves.

steps.title: Whisk the yoghurt dressing
steps.content: Mix yoghurt, lemon, garlic and oil.
steps.detail: Stir the yoghurt with the juice of half the lemon, the grated garlic, a little olive oil, salt and pepper until smooth and pourable. Taste it — it should be bright and garlicky, sharp enough to cut the richness. Loosen with a splash of water if it's too thick.
tips: Use just one small clove of raw garlic — it's punchy uncooked, and more can overwhelm the dish.

steps.title: Toss and scatter
steps.content: Dress the broccoli; top with almonds and parsley.
steps.detail: Spread most of the yoghurt dressing over a platter, pile the warm broccoli on top and drizzle with the rest. Scatter over the toasted almonds and torn parsley, and finish with a squeeze of the remaining lemon. Serve warm or at room temperature.

title: Tuna Melt Tomato Toastie
description: The lunchbox hero, hot from the pan — creamy tuna and spring onion, juicy tomato and a molten cheddar layer, all pressed between crisp golden bread.
storage: Best hot and crisp from the pan. The tuna mix keeps 1 day in the fridge; assemble and toast fresh, as a made-up toastie turns soggy.
make_ahead: Mix the tuna filling a day ahead and keep it covered in the fridge — then it's just build, toast and eat.
leftovers: Any extra tuna mix is great spooned onto crackers or stirred through hot pasta.

substitutions.swap: tasty cheese or mozzarella
a spoon of sour cream or Greek yoghurt
tinned salmon
sourdough, or a wrap for a quick toasted version

steps.title: Mix the tuna filling
steps.content: Fold drained tuna with mayo and spring onion.
steps.detail: Drain the tuna well and flake it into a bowl. Fold through the mayo and finely sliced spring onions, then season with salt and pepper. You're after a moist but not sloppy mix that holds together when you spread it — too wet and the toastie goes soggy.

steps.title: Slice and drain the tomatoes
steps.content: Thinly slice tomatoes; salt to draw out water.
steps.detail: Slice the tomatoes thinly, lay them on paper towel and give them a light sprinkle of salt. Leave a few minutes, then pat the tops dry — this pulls out excess juice so it doesn't leak into the bread and make it damp.
tips: A wet tomato is the number one cause of a soggy toastie, so don't skip the pat-dry.

steps.title: Build the toasties
steps.content: Layer tuna, tomato and cheddar between bread.
steps.detail: Lay out four slices of bread and spread the tuna mix over each. Top with tomato slices and a generous handful of grated cheddar, then close with the remaining bread. Putting cheese right against both slices helps it melt and glue the toastie shut.

steps.title: Toast until golden and melting
steps.content: Toast both sides until crisp and the cheese runs.
steps.detail: Brush the outsides lightly with olive oil and cook in a pan over medium heat — not high — for about 3 minutes a side, pressing gently, until deep golden and crisp. The lower heat gives the cheddar time to melt right through before the bread burns. Cut in half and eat while it's molten.
tips: A lid over the pan traps heat and melts the cheese faster, or use a sandwich press if you have one.

title: Pork Chop Apple Sage Plate
description: Golden pan-fried pork chops with a buttery apple and sage pan sauce, creamy mash and crisp green beans. A Sunday-dinner feeling on a weeknight timeline.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Reheat the pork gently so it doesn't dry out, and refresh the mash with a splash of milk.
make_ahead: Peel and chunk the potatoes ahead, keeping them in a bowl of cold water so they don't brown before boiling.
leftovers: Slice leftover pork and pile it into a roll with the apples and a smear of mustard.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh or a thick pork steak
a firm pear
a splash of white wine or a squeeze of lemon
broccolini or peas

steps.title: Boil the potatoes
steps.content: Simmer peeled potato chunks until tender.
steps.detail: Peel the potatoes and cut into even chunks, then put them in a pot of cold salted water. Bring to the boil and simmer about 15 minutes, until a knife slides in with no resistance. Drain well and let them steam-dry in the colander for a minute — dry potato makes fluffier mash.

steps.title: Season the pork chops
steps.content: Pat chops dry and season both sides.
steps.detail: Take the pork chops out of the fridge, pat them really dry and season both sides with salt and pepper. Dry meat is the secret to a proper golden crust — any surface moisture just steams. Snip through the fat rim in a couple of places so the chops don't buckle in the pan.
warnings: Wash your hands and board after handling the raw pork before touching the cooked sides.

steps.title: Sear the pork chops
steps.content: Sear chops until golden and 70°C, then rest.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a pan over medium-high until shimmering. Lay the chops in and sear 4 to 5 minutes a side, holding them fat-edge down with tongs for a minute to render it golden. They're done at 70°C in the middle, with just a blush of juice. Rest them on a warm plate while you make the sauce.
tips: Resting for 5 minutes lets the juices settle back through the meat so they don't run out when you cut.

steps.title: Make the apple and sage butter
steps.content: Cook apple and sage in butter with a splash of vinegar.
steps.detail: Melt most of the butter in the same pan over medium heat and add the sage leaves — they crisp and perfume the butter in under a minute. Add the apple wedges and cook 3 to 4 minutes until golden and just soft, then splash in the cider vinegar and scrape up the sticky pork bits from the base. Let it bubble into a glossy pan sauce.
warnings: Butter browns fast — keep the heat moderate so it turns nutty, not black and bitter.

steps.title: Cook the green beans
steps.content: Boil green beans until just tender and bright.
steps.detail: Trim the beans and drop them into boiling salted water for 3 to 4 minutes, until tender but still with a snap and vivid green. Drain and season. They should have a little bite left — overcooked beans go khaki and limp.

steps.title: Mash and plate up
steps.content: Mash the potato; plate with pork, sauce and beans.
steps.detail: Mash the drained potato with the milk and the last of the butter until smooth, seasoning to taste. Spoon the mash onto plates, sit a rested chop alongside and spoon the apple-sage butter over the pork. Add the green beans and serve straight away, while everything's hot.

title: Tomato Olive Mozzarella Pizza
description: A crisp homemade base under a garlicky crushed-tomato sauce, melting mozzarella, briny olives and fresh basil torn over the top. Proper pizza-night stuff, no takeaway required.
storage: Best straight from the oven. Leftover slices keep 2 days in the fridge and crisp up in a hot, dry pan — skip the microwave, which makes the base floppy.
make_ahead: Make the dough a day ahead and prove it slowly in the fridge overnight — bring it back to room temperature before shaping.
leftovers: Cold pizza is a fine breakfast, but leftover slices crisp up beautifully in a hot, dry pan.

substitutions.swap: torn bocconcini, or a mix with cheddar
capers or anchovies for extra punch
bread flour for a chewier base

steps.title: Make the dough
steps.content: Mix flour, yeast, salt, oil and warm water into a dough.
steps.detail: Stir the flour, yeast and a good pinch of salt together in a big bowl. Make a well, pour in a tablespoon of the olive oil and about 3/4 cup of warm (not hot) water, and mix to a shaggy dough. Turn out and knead for 8 minutes until smooth and springy — it should bounce back slowly when pressed.
tips: Warm water wakes the yeast; hot water kills it, so aim for blood temperature — comfortable on your wrist.

steps.title: Prove until doubled
steps.content: Rest the dough somewhere warm until doubled.
steps.detail: Shape the dough into a ball, sit it in an oiled bowl and cover with a tea towel. Leave it somewhere warm for about an hour, until doubled in size and pillowy. It's ready when a finger pressed in leaves a dent that fills back only slowly.

steps.title: Mix the tomato sauce
steps.content: Crush tomatoes with garlic, oil and seasoning.
steps.detail: Tip the tinned tomatoes into a bowl and crush them smooth with your hands or a fork. Stir in the grated garlic, a tablespoon of olive oil, salt and pepper. Keep it raw and simple — it cooks and concentrates on the pizza, so there's no need to simmer it first.

steps.title: Shape and top the base
steps.content: Stretch the dough, then sauce, cheese and olives.
steps.detail: Heat your oven as high as it goes, around 240°C fan, with a tray inside. Stretch the proved dough on baking paper into a rough circle, keeping a thicker rim. Spread a thin layer of sauce to the edges, scatter over the mozzarella and dot with olives — go easy on the sauce so the base crisps rather than steams.

steps.title: Bake until blistered
steps.content: Bake on the hot tray until golden and bubbling.
steps.detail: Slide the pizza, paper and all, onto the hot tray and bake 12 to 15 minutes, until the crust is puffed and golden and the cheese is bubbling with a few brown spots. A hot tray gives the base an instant blast of heat so the bottom crisps up like a pizzeria oven.
warnings: The tray and oven are very hot — use a dry cloth and pull the whole thing out on the paper to avoid a burn.

steps.title: Finish with basil
steps.content: Tear over fresh basil and a drizzle of oil.
steps.detail: Let the pizza sit for a minute, then tear the fresh basil over the top and add a thin drizzle of olive oil. Adding the basil after baking keeps it bright green and fragrant instead of blackened. Slice and eat hot.

title: Berry Yoghurt Oats Jar
description: Overnight oats done right — rolled oats soaked in vanilla-scented milk and yoghurt, layered with a quick berry mash and a scatter of toasted almonds. Grab-and-go breakfast for the week.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge with the lids on. Add the almonds just before eating so they stay crunchy.
make_ahead: Built to be made ahead: assemble the jars up to 3 days before and breakfast sorts itself for the week.
leftovers: Blend a leftover jar with a splash of milk for an instant breakfast smoothie.

substitutions.swap: any milk — dairy or plant
maple syrup
fresh berries or chopped banana
walnuts, pecans or a spoon of muesli

steps.title: Mix the oat base
steps.content: Stir oats with milk, yoghurt, vanilla and honey.
steps.detail: In a bowl, stir together the rolled oats, milk, yoghurt, vanilla and half the honey until everything's coated. The oats need this liquid to soften overnight into something creamy and spoonable, so make sure they're all submerged.

steps.title: Make the berry layer
steps.content: Mash frozen berries with the rest of the honey.
steps.detail: Tip the frozen berries into a bowl with the remaining honey and roughly mash half of them with a fork, leaving some whole. As they sit and thaw they release a jammy, ruby syrup — no cooking needed. They'll keep thawing in the jars overnight.

steps.title: Toast the almonds
steps.content: Toast almonds until golden, then chop.
steps.detail: Toast the almonds in a dry pan over medium heat for 3 to 4 minutes, shaking often, until golden and smelling nutty. Cool and roughly chop. Keeping them separate until serving means they stay crunchy against the soft oats.
warnings: Almonds catch and burn quickly once they colour — keep them moving and take them off while golden.

steps.title: Layer and chill overnight
steps.content: Layer oats and berries in jars; refrigerate.
steps.detail: Spoon a layer of the oat mix into each jar, then a layer of the berry mash, and repeat. Seal with the lids and chill for at least 6 hours, or overnight. By morning the oats are soft and creamy and the berry syrup has bled down through the layers.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.content: Scatter almonds and drizzle honey to serve.
steps.detail: When you're ready to eat, scatter the toasted almonds over the top and add a final drizzle of honey if you like it sweeter. Eat straight from the jar — a splash of extra milk loosens it if it's set too thick overnight.

title: Oat Raisin Cookies
description: Chewy-in-the-middle, golden-at-the-edges oat cookies studded with plump raisins and warm cinnamon. One bowl, a wooden spoon, and about twenty minutes to a full tray.
storage: Keeps 5 days in an airtight tin at room temperature. The raw dough also freezes well, rolled into balls.
make_ahead: The rolled dough balls freeze well — bake them straight from frozen, adding a couple of extra minutes.
leftovers: Crumble a spare cookie over vanilla ice cream, or sandwich two around a smear of peanut butter.

substitutions.swap: brown sugar for a chewier, more caramel cookie
chocolate chips, chopped dates or dried cranberries
self-raising flour — then skip the baking powder

steps.title: Cream the butter and sugar
steps.content: Beat soft butter and sugar until pale; heat the oven.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 170°C fan and line two trays with baking paper. Beat the softened butter and sugar together with a wooden spoon for a couple of minutes until pale and creamy. This beats in tiny air bubbles that help the cookies lift and go tender.
tips: If the butter's fridge-hard, cut it into cubes and leave 10 minutes, or grate it — soft butter creams in seconds.

steps.title: Beat in the egg
steps.content: Mix in the egg until smooth.
steps.detail: Crack in the egg and beat until it's fully combined and the mixture looks smooth and glossy. Don't panic if it looks slightly split or curdled — it comes back together the moment you add the dry ingredients.

steps.title: Fold in the oats and raisins
steps.content: Stir through oats, flour, baking powder, cinnamon and raisins.
steps.detail: Add the flour, baking powder, cinnamon and a pinch of salt, then the oats and raisins. Fold everything together until it forms a thick, scoopable dough with no floury patches. Don't overmix once the flour's in — just bring it together, or the cookies turn tough.

steps.title: Roll and space the dough
steps.content: Roll tablespoons of dough; space well apart.
steps.detail: Roll heaped tablespoons of dough into balls and set them well apart on the trays — they spread as they bake, so give them a good 5cm each. Flatten each one slightly with damp fingers and press a couple of raisins on top so they peek out golden. Even sizes bake at the same rate.

steps.title: Bake until golden
steps.content: Bake 12 min; cool on the tray to firm up.
steps.detail: Bake for about 12 minutes, until the edges are set and golden but the centres still look a touch soft. Leave them on the hot tray for 5 minutes to firm up before moving them to a rack — this is when they finish setting into that chewy-middle, crisp-edge texture.
tips: They'll seem underbaked in the middle when you pull them out — that's right; a fully firm cookie in the oven bakes to a hard biscuit once cooled.

title: Pork Mince Tomato Shell Pasta
description: A quick weeknight ragu: pork mince browned till sweet, garlic and a spoon of tomato paste cooked down with tinned tomatoes, all caught in the ridges of shell pasta and finished with parmesan.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge; freeze the sauce on its own (without pasta) for up to 3 months. Reheat until piping hot.
make_ahead: The sauce is even better the next day. Make it up to 2 days ahead and reheat gently while the pasta boils.
leftovers: Spare sauce makes a quick jaffle filling or spooned over a baked potato with grated cheese.

substitutions.swap: beef mince, or half pork and half beef for a richer sauce
any short pasta — penne, spirals or macaroni all trap the sauce well
any hard grating cheese; a block of tasty at a pinch

steps.title: Dice onion and garlic
steps.content: Finely dice the onion and mince the garlic before the pan goes on.
steps.detail: Halve and peel the onion, keeping the root end on so it holds together, then dice it small — about 5 mm. Smash the garlic cloves under the flat of your knife to loosen the skins, then mince finely. Keep the garlic in its own little pile: it burns far faster than onion, so it goes in later. Having it all ready now means a calm cook.

steps.title: Brown the pork mince
steps.content: Sear the pork in hot oil until deeply golden, about 6 minutes.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a heavy pan over medium-high until it shimmers. Crumble in the pork mince in big chunks and leave it alone for a couple of minutes to catch colour before you start breaking it up. Pork mince throws off a fair bit of liquid — keep cooking until that water boils away and the meat sizzles and browns rather than stews. Those golden bits are pure flavour.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and anything the raw pork touched before it goes near food you will eat uncooked.

steps.title: Sweat the onion
steps.content: Drop the heat and soften the onion and garlic in the pork fat.
steps.detail: Turn the heat down to medium and tip in the onion. Stir it through the mince and cook for about 4 minutes until it turns translucent and soft. Add the garlic and a pinch of salt and cook one minute more, just until it smells fragrant — don't let it colour or it turns bitter. Scrape any browned bits off the base of the pan as the onion releases its moisture.

steps.title: Caramelise the tomato paste
steps.content: Fry the tomato paste for a minute to deepen and sweeten it.
steps.detail: Push the mince to one side, add the tomato paste to the bare pan and let it sizzle and fry for about a minute, stirring, until it darkens from bright red to a deeper brick colour. Cooking the paste like this takes away its raw tinny edge and builds a savoury backbone for the sauce. Stir it through the meat once it has darkened.

steps.title: Simmer the tomato sauce
steps.content: Add the tinned tomatoes and simmer low until rich, 20 minutes.
steps.detail: Tip in the tinned tomatoes, crushing any whole ones against the pan with your spoon. Swill a splash of water around the empty tin and add that too. Season with salt and a good crack of pepper, then drop to a gentle simmer — lazy bubbles, not a rolling boil. Let it reduce for 20 minutes, stirring now and then, until it is thick, glossy and coats the spoon.
tips: If it thickens too fast or starts to catch, splash in a little water and lower the heat.

steps.title: Boil the shells
steps.content: Cook the pasta shells in well-salted water until just al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it well — it should taste like the sea. Add the shells, give them a stir so they don't clump, and cook for 1 minute less than the packet says so they keep a little bite. Just before draining, scoop out a mugful of the starchy pasta water and keep it — it is the secret to a glossy sauce.

steps.title: Toss through the sauce
steps.content: Fold the shells through the sauce with a splash of pasta water, top with parmesan.
steps.detail: Tip the drained shells straight into the sauce and fold them through over low heat for a minute so every ridge fills up. If it looks tight, loosen with a splash of the reserved pasta water until it clings and glistens. Off the heat, stir through half the parmesan, then serve with the rest grated over and a final crack of pepper.

title: Eggplant Tomato Coconut Curry
description: Golden pan-fried eggplant simmered soft in a spiced tomato and coconut sauce, spooned over fluffy basmati and showered with fresh coriander. Mild, creamy and properly satisfying.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge and the flavour deepens overnight. Reheat gently with a splash of water; cooked rice is best eaten within a day.
make_ahead: Make the curry a day ahead — it thickens and mellows beautifully. Reheat with a splash of water and cook the rice fresh.
leftovers: Spoon leftovers into a wrap with a handful of rocket for a quick lunch.

substitutions.swap: zucchini or cauliflower florets, added straight to the sauce
2 tbsp red curry paste for a Thai-leaning version
jasmine rice, or any long-grain white rice

steps.title: Chop eggplant and aromatics
steps.content: Cube the eggplant and dice the onion and garlic.
steps.detail: Cut the eggplant into 3 cm chunks — bigger than you think, as they shrink right down. Dice the onion and mince the garlic, keeping them separate. There is no need to salt or peel the eggplant here; the frying takes care of any bitterness. Have everything within reach before the pan heats up.

steps.title: Brown the eggplant
steps.content: Fry the eggplant in oil until golden and collapsing, about 8 minutes.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large pan over medium-high. Add the eggplant and let it fry, turning now and then, until deeply golden on most sides and softening in the middle — around 8 minutes. Eggplant drinks oil greedily at first, then releases it as it cooks, so resist adding loads more; a dry patch just means it needs a minute longer before it lets go. Scoop it out onto a plate.
tips: A crowded pan steams rather than browns. If your pan is small, fry the eggplant in two batches.

steps.title: Bloom the curry powder
steps.content: Soften the onion, then toast the garlic and curry powder till fragrant.
steps.detail: In the same pan, cook the onion over medium heat for 4 to 5 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the garlic and curry powder and stir for about a minute — you want the spices to sizzle and smell toasty and warm, which wakes them up. Keep it moving so the garlic and powder don't catch on the base and turn bitter.
tips: A thumb of grated ginger stirred in with the garlic lifts the whole curry if you have some.

steps.title: Simmer with tomato and coconut
steps.content: Add tomatoes, coconut milk and the eggplant, then simmer 20 minutes.
steps.detail: Tip in the tinned tomatoes and coconut milk, then slide the eggplant back in. Season with salt and pepper and stir to bring it together. Simmer gently, uncovered, for about 20 minutes until the sauce turns silky and a shade of burnt-orange and the eggplant is meltingly soft. Taste and add more salt if it needs lifting.
tips: For a richer curry, hold back a couple of spoonfuls of the thick coconut cream and swirl it in at the end.

steps.title: Cook the basmati
steps.content: Rinse and simmer the rice by absorption until tender and fluffy.
steps.detail: Rinse the basmati under cold water until it runs clear, then tip it into a saucepan with 2 1/4 cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, drop to the lowest heat, put the lid on and cook for 12 minutes. Don't lift the lid — the trapped steam is doing the work. Take it off the heat and let it sit, still covered, for 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork.

steps.title: Finish with coriander
steps.content: Scatter coriander over the curry and spoon it over the rice.
steps.detail: Roughly chop the coriander, stalks and all, and scatter most of it through the curry. Spoon the curry over bowls of the fluffy rice and finish with the last of the coriander for a fresh, green lift against the creamy sauce.

title: Tomato Chickpea Harissa-Style Soup
description: A punchy tinned-pantry soup: smoky paprika, cumin and chilli build a harissa-style warmth around tomatoes and chickpeas, cooled with a swirl of yoghurt.
storage: Keeps 4 days in the fridge or freezes for 3 months. Reheat until steaming; stir the yoghurt in only when serving, never before storing.
make_ahead: The soup base holds happily for a few days — make it ahead, then just reheat and swirl in the yoghurt to serve.
leftovers: Reduce leftover soup down thick and toss it through pasta as a smoky chickpea sauce.

substitutions.swap: tinned white beans, drained and rinsed the same way
coconut yoghurt, or a drizzle of coconut milk, to keep it vegan
a spoonful of sriracha, or a fresh chopped red chilli

steps.title: Fry the garlic and spices
steps.content: Warm oil, then sizzle the garlic, paprika, cumin and chilli into a rough harissa.
steps.detail: There is no real harissa here, so we build its smoky-hot flavour from the spice rack. Warm the olive oil in a saucepan over medium heat, add the minced garlic and stir for 30 seconds until it smells sweet, then add the smoked paprika, cumin and chilli flakes. Stir for another 30 seconds until the spices turn glossy in the oil and smell toasty — this is the base of the whole soup.
warnings: Garlic and spices scorch in seconds and turn bitter. Keep the heat moderate and the spoon moving; pull the pan off if it races ahead.

steps.title: Add tomato and chickpeas
steps.content: Stir in the tinned tomatoes and drained chickpeas to coat in spice.
steps.detail: Tip in the tinned tomatoes, crushing any whole pieces against the pan, then add the drained and rinsed chickpeas. Stir for a minute so everything takes on the red, spiced oil. Let it bubble for a couple of minutes to cook off the raw tomato tang before you add the liquid.

steps.title: Simmer the soup
steps.content: Pour in the stock, season and simmer for 20 minutes.
steps.detail: Pour in the vegetable stock and season with salt and pepper. Bring to a simmer and let it tick away gently for 20 minutes so the flavours marry and the chickpeas turn soft and creamy. Taste as it goes — the smoked paprika deepens and the chilli builds, so adjust the heat to suit you.

steps.title: Crush for body
steps.content: Blitz or mash part of the soup so it is thick but still chunky.
steps.detail: For a soup with body, blitz about a third of it with a stick blender, or scoop a couple of ladles into a bowl, mash with a fork and stir back in. You want it thick and a little rustic, with plenty of whole chickpeas left for texture. If it has reduced too far, loosen with a splash of hot water.

steps.title: Swirl in yoghurt
steps.content: Ladle into bowls, swirl through yoghurt and drizzle with oil.
steps.detail: Ladle the hot soup into bowls and swirl a spoon of cool yoghurt through each one — it softens the chilli and looks lovely against the red. Finish with a thread of olive oil and an extra pinch of chilli flakes if you like it fierier. Great with warm flatbread for dunking.

title: Tuna Sweetcorn Rice Bowl
description: A cool, quick rice bowl — creamy tuna mayo, charred sweetcorn, crunchy cucumber and spring onion piled over warm jasmine rice. Weeknight dinner, mostly from the pantry.
storage: Best eaten fresh. The tuna mayo keeps 1 day covered in the fridge; store the rice separately in its own container and eat within a day.
leftovers: Roll cold leftovers up in a sheet of nori as a scrappy hand roll for lunch.

substitutions.swap: tinned salmon, or shredded leftover roast chicken
Greek yoghurt, or half mayo and half yoghurt for a lighter dressing
a pinch of chilli flakes, or a dab of sweet chilli sauce

steps.title: Rinse and cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the jasmine rice and cook by absorption until fluffy.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice under cold water until it runs clear to wash off the loose starch. Tip it into a saucepan with 2 1/4 cups of water and a pinch of salt, bring to the boil, then drop to the lowest heat and clamp the lid on for 12 minutes. Rest it off the heat, still covered, for 5 minutes before fluffing with a fork. Warm rice is best for this bowl.

steps.title: Char the corn
steps.content: Dry-toast the frozen corn in a hot pan until golden and lightly charred.
steps.detail: Pat the frozen corn dry with a tea towel, then tip it into a dry non-stick pan over high heat. Leave it fairly still, tossing occasionally, for about 5 minutes until the kernels dry out, turn golden and pick up a few dark char marks. That toasting turns bland frozen corn sweet and nutty — worth the few minutes over just boiling it.
tips: The corn may pop and spit as the ice turns to steam. Stand back a little and keep it moving.

steps.title: Mix the tuna mayo
steps.content: Flake the drained tuna and fold through mayo, soy and a little sriracha.
steps.detail: Drain the tuna well, pressing out the liquid, and flake it into a bowl. Fold through the mayo, the soy sauce and a squeeze of sriracha if you want some heat. You are after a creamy, spoonable mix that still has some texture — not a paste. Taste and add a touch more soy or sriracha to balance it.

steps.title: Prep cucumber and onion
steps.content: Dice the cucumber and finely slice the spring onions.
steps.detail: Cut the cucumber into small dice for crunch — no need to peel it. Finely slice the spring onions on an angle, using both the white and green parts. Keeping these raw and fresh is what balances the rich tuna mayo, so cut them just before serving so they stay crisp.

steps.title: Assemble the bowls
steps.content: Pile rice, tuna mayo, corn, cucumber and spring onion into bowls.
steps.detail: Spoon the warm rice into four bowls and lay the tuna mayo over one side. Arrange the charred corn, diced cucumber and sliced spring onion around it in their own little piles so each bowl looks generous. Finish with a crack of pepper and an extra drizzle of sriracha for the chilli lovers.

title: Chicken Chorizo Rice Pan
description: Smoky chorizo and browned chicken thigh cooked into paprika-stained rice with sweet red capsicum — a loose, one-pan take on paella for a busy night.
equipment: large deep frying pan or paella pan
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Cool it quickly, and reheat until piping hot right through — cooked rice should only be reheated once.
leftovers: Squash cold leftovers into patties and pan-fry until crisp for golden rice cakes.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast, added a little later so it doesn't dry out
a spicy pork sausage, or chopped bacon for a milder smoky hit
any long-grain white rice, or arborio for a stickier finish

steps.title: Brown the chorizo
steps.content: Slice and fry the chorizo until its orange oil runs into the pan.
steps.detail: Slice the chorizo into coins. Add the olive oil to a cold pan with the chorizo and bring it up over medium heat so the sausage renders slowly, releasing its smoky, paprika-red oil — about 5 minutes until the coins are crisp at the edges. Scoop the chorizo out with a slotted spoon and leave that flavoured oil behind; it is going to cook everything else.

steps.title: Sear the chicken
steps.content: Brown the seasoned chicken thigh chunks in the chorizo oil.
steps.detail: Cut the chicken thigh into big bite-sized pieces and season with salt and pepper. Sear them in the hot chorizo oil over medium-high for about 6 minutes, turning, until golden on the outside — they don't need to cook through yet, as they finish in the rice. Lift them out to join the chorizo.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the knife after handling raw chicken, and keep it away from anything served raw.

steps.title: Soften capsicum and onion
steps.content: Cook the onion, capsicum and garlic with the paprika into a soft base.
steps.detail: Dice the onion and capsicum and add them to the pan with a little more oil if it looks dry. Cook for 5 minutes until soft and glossy, then stir in the garlic and smoked paprika and cook one minute more until fragrant. This soft, sweet, spiced base is what carries the rice.

steps.title: Toast the rice
steps.content: Stir the jasmine rice through the base to coat every grain.
steps.detail: Tip in the dry jasmine rice and stir it through the vegetables for a minute or two, letting the grains toast in the oil and turn slightly translucent at the edges. Coating the rice in the flavoured base now means it soaks up far more taste as it cooks.

steps.title: Simmer in stock
steps.content: Pour in the stock, nestle back the meat and simmer without stirring, 18 minutes.
steps.detail: Pour in the chicken stock and return the chorizo and chicken, tucking them into the rice. Bring to a simmer, spread everything into an even layer, then leave it to cook gently for about 18 minutes without stirring — stirring makes it gluey and stops the prized crust forming on the base. It is done when the liquid has been absorbed and the rice is tender.
tips: Resist lifting the pan too often. If the top looks dry but the rice is still firm, add a small splash of hot stock or water and re-cover.

steps.title: Rest and fluff
steps.content: Rest covered for 5 minutes, check the chicken, then fluff and serve.
steps.detail: Cover the pan with a lid or foil and let it rest off the heat for 5 minutes so the grains settle. Check the biggest piece of chicken is cooked through to 74°C with no pink at the centre. Fluff gently with a fork and serve straight from the pan.
warnings: Chicken must reach 74°C at its thickest point. If any piece is still pink inside, return the pan to low heat for a few minutes.

title: Tomato Peach Basil Salad
description: Ripe summer tomatoes and juicy peaches with torn mozzarella, basil and olives, dressed in balsamic and the tomatoes' own sweet juices. Ten minutes, no cooking.
storage: Best eaten straight away, while the fruit is at its ripest. Undressed, the cut components keep a few hours in the fridge; once dressed the salad quickly goes watery.

substitutions.swap: torn buffalo mozzarella or bocconcini, or crumbled feta for a saltier edge
nectarines, or ripe apricots, cut a little smaller
a squeeze of lemon for a fresher, sharper dressing

steps.title: Slice tomato and peach
steps.content: Cut the tomatoes and peaches into thick, rustic wedges.
steps.detail: Cut the tomatoes into thick wedges or chunky slices. Halve the peaches, twist out the stones and cut them into wedges about the same size as the tomato — matching them up makes every forkful a mix of the two. Ripeness is everything here, so use the softest, sweetest fruit you can find.

steps.title: Salt the tomatoes
steps.content: Salt the tomato wedges and let them sit to draw out their juices.
steps.detail: Scatter the tomato wedges with a good pinch of salt and leave them for 5 minutes. The salt pulls out a pool of sweet-savoury tomato juice, which seasons the fruit from within and gives you a head start on the dressing. Tip that juice into your dressing bowl rather than throwing it away.

steps.title: Whisk the dressing
steps.content: Whisk olive oil and balsamic into the reserved tomato juices with pepper.
steps.detail: In a small bowl, whisk the olive oil and balsamic into the tomato juices you saved, along with a crack of pepper, until it comes together into a glossy dressing. Taste it on a piece of tomato — it should be tangy and just sweet enough. Add a touch more balsamic if the peaches are very sweet.

steps.title: Layer the plate
steps.content: Arrange tomato, peach, torn mozzarella and olives on a platter.
steps.detail: Spread the tomato and peach over a large platter, tucking them together so the colours mingle. Tear the mozzarella into rough pieces with your hands — torn edges catch the dressing far better than neat slices — and dot it over along with the olives. Scatter, don't stack, so every part gets dressed.

steps.title: Dress and scatter basil
steps.content: Spoon over the dressing, tear basil on top and finish with salt.
steps.detail: Spoon the dressing all over the platter, then tear the basil leaves and scatter them on top just before serving so they stay bright and don't bruise. Finish with a final small pinch of salt over the peaches and mozzarella. Serve at room temperature — fridge-cold mutes all the flavour.

title: Mushroom Mozzarella Toastie
description: Buttery pan-fried mushrooms with garlic and thyme, melted into stringy mozzarella between crisp golden bread. The vegetarian toastie worth making properly.
storage: Best hot and fresh off the pan. The cooked mushroom mix keeps 2 days in the fridge — build and toast fresh from cold.
leftovers: The garlicky mushroom mix is great stirred through pasta or piled onto toast the next day.

substitutions.swap: any good melter — cheddar, tasty, or a mix of both
sourdough or thick white sandwich bread
a pinch of dried oregano, or a little chopped parsley stirred in at the end

steps.title: Slice the mushrooms
steps.content: Slice the mushrooms, mince the garlic and strip the thyme.
steps.detail: Wipe the mushrooms clean with a damp cloth rather than washing them, then slice them about 5 mm thick. Mince the garlic and pull the tiny thyme leaves off their woody stems by running your fingers down each sprig. Keep the garlic aside — it goes in near the end so it doesn't scorch.

steps.title: Fry the mushrooms
steps.content: Cook the mushrooms in oil and butter until golden and dry, 8 minutes.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil with half the butter in a large pan over medium-high. Add the mushrooms and let them cook, stirring now and then, for about 8 minutes. They will release a lot of water and look soggy first — keep going until that liquid boils away and they turn deep golden and glossy. Stir in the garlic, thyme, salt and pepper for the final minute.
warnings: Add the garlic only in the last minute — on high heat it burns fast and turns the whole pan bitter.

steps.title: Butter the bread
steps.content: Spread the remaining butter on the outer face of each slice.
steps.detail: Soften the rest of the butter and spread it right to the edges on one side of each slice of bread. That buttered face goes on the outside against the hot pan and is what fries up crisp and golden. A thin, even layer browns better than thick patches.

steps.title: Layer the toasties
steps.content: Build each toastie with mozzarella above and below the mushrooms.
steps.detail: Lay four slices buttered-side down. Scatter a little mozzarella straight onto the bread, pile on the mushrooms, then top with more mozzarella and the second slice, buttered-side up. Putting cheese on both layers glues the toastie together and stops the filling tumbling out when you bite in.

steps.title: Toast until golden
steps.content: Fry each toastie, pressing, until crisp and the cheese has melted.
steps.detail: Set the toasties in a dry pan over medium heat and press down with a spatula. Cook for about 3 minutes a side until deep golden and crisp, and the mozzarella is stringy and molten in the middle. Keep the heat medium, not high — too hot and the bread burns before the cheese melts. Cut in half so the cheese pulls, and eat straight away.

title: Salmon Beetroot Yoghurt Plate
description: Roast salmon with sweet roasted beetroot and crisp potatoes, a lemony yoghurt to swoosh underneath and a handful of peppery rocket. A colourful plate that looks harder than it is.
storage: Eat the salmon within 1 to 2 days, kept cold in the fridge. The roast veg and lemon yoghurt keep 2 to 3 days; dress the rocket only when serving.
make_ahead: Roast the beetroot and potatoes and mix the lemon yoghurt earlier in the day, then roast the salmon fresh just before serving.
leftovers: Flake leftover salmon through pasta, or over a grain salad with the roast beetroot the next day.

substitutions.swap: barramundi or any firm fish fillet; adjust the roast time to thickness
sour cream, thinned with a little lemon juice
carrots or pumpkin wedges, roasted the same way

steps.title: Heat the oven and prep the veg
steps.content: Heat the oven to 200°C fan and cut the beetroot and potatoes.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Peel the beetroot and cut it into 2 cm wedges — small enough to roast in the same time as the spuds. Cut the potatoes into similar chunks, leaving the skin on. Toss both with olive oil and a good pinch of salt. Keep the beetroot slightly apart on the tray, or it will stain everything else magenta.
tips: Beetroot stains hands and boards. A quick rinse straight after cutting, or thin gloves, saves pink fingers.

steps.title: Roast the beetroot and potatoes
steps.content: Roast the veg for about 35 minutes until tender and crisp-edged.
steps.detail: Spread the beetroot and potatoes in a single layer on the tray, giving them room so they roast rather than steam. Roast for about 35 minutes, turning once halfway, until the potatoes are golden and crisp at the edges and a knife slides easily through the beetroot. If they need longer, give them another 5 to 10 minutes.

steps.title: Mix the lemon yoghurt
steps.content: Stir lemon zest and juice into the yoghurt with a pinch of salt.
steps.detail: Finely zest one lemon into the yoghurt, then squeeze in half its juice. Add a small pinch of salt and stir until smooth and pourable. Taste and add more lemon if you want it sharper — it should be bright and tangy to cut the richness of the salmon. Pop it in the fridge until serving.

steps.title: Roast the salmon
steps.content: Season the salmon and roast until it just flakes, about 12 minutes.
steps.detail: Pat the salmon dry, rub with a little oil and season with salt and pepper. When the veg has had its 35 minutes, make space on the tray and lay the fillets skin-side down. Roast for 10 to 12 minutes, until the flesh turns pale pink and flakes when nudged with a fork but is still juicy in the middle. Thicker fillets need the extra couple of minutes.
warnings: Wash your hands and anything the raw salmon touched, and keep it away from the cooked veg until the fish is roasted.

steps.title: Dress the rocket
steps.content: Toss the rocket with a squeeze of lemon and a drizzle of oil.
steps.detail: Just before plating, toss the rocket in a bowl with a squeeze from the second lemon, a drizzle of olive oil and a tiny pinch of salt. Dress it at the last second so the leaves stay perky rather than wilting into a sad clump.

steps.title: Plate it up
steps.content: Swoosh yoghurt on each plate and pile on veg, salmon and rocket.
steps.detail: Spoon the lemon yoghurt onto each plate and drag the back of the spoon through it to make a swoosh. Pile on the roast beetroot and potatoes, set a salmon fillet alongside and finish with the dressed rocket and a lemon wedge. Serve while the fish and veg are still warm.

title: Chorizo Capsicum Pizza
description: Homemade base, a quick oregano tomato sauce, molten mozzarella and slices of spicy chorizo with sweet red capsicum. Better than delivery and made from scratch.
storage: Best fresh from the oven. Leftover slices keep 2 days in the fridge and crisp up again in a hot oven or a dry pan — skip the microwave or the base goes soft.
make_ahead: Make the dough in the morning and leave it to prove slowly in the fridge all day; pull it out an hour before baking to come back to room temperature.
leftovers: Cold chorizo pizza is a genuinely good next-day breakfast, or reheat a slice in a dry pan to re-crisp the base.

substitutions.swap: bread flour for a chewier, stretchier base
salami, pepperoni or a crumbled spicy sausage
sliced mushrooms or red onion

steps.title: Bloom the yeast
steps.content: Stir yeast and sugar into warm water and wait until frothy.
steps.detail: Measure out 1 cup of warm water — comfortably warm to a finger, not hot, or it kills the yeast. Stir in the sugar and yeast and leave it for 5 to 10 minutes until a foamy, beige raft forms on top. That froth tells you the yeast is alive and the dough will rise. If nothing happens, your yeast is dead and it is worth starting again.

steps.title: Knead the dough
steps.content: Mix into the flour with oil and salt, then knead until smooth.
steps.detail: Put the flour and a good pinch of salt in a bowl, make a well and pour in the frothy yeast water and the olive oil. Mix to a shaggy dough, then tip onto the bench and knead for about 8 minutes until it turns smooth, springy and no longer sticky. If it clings to your hands, add flour a little at a time; if it is dry and crumbly, wet your hands.

steps.title: Prove the dough
steps.content: Cover and rest in a warm spot until doubled in size.
steps.detail: Shape the dough into a ball, sit it in an oiled bowl and cover with a tea towel or cling film. Leave it somewhere warm for about an hour until doubled in size — it is ready when you press a finger in and the dent springs back slowly. Use this time to make the sauce and prep the toppings.

steps.title: Make the tomato sauce
steps.content: Blitz or crush the tomatoes with oregano and salt into a quick sauce.
steps.detail: Tip the tinned tomatoes into a bowl and crush them smooth with a fork or a quick blitz. Stir in the dried oregano and a pinch of salt. There is no need to cook it — a raw, well-seasoned sauce roasts down beautifully on the hot base and keeps that fresh tomato tang. Keep it fairly thick so the pizza doesn't go soggy.

steps.title: Slice the toppings
steps.content: Thinly slice the chorizo and the red capsicum.
steps.detail: Slice the chorizo into thin coins so it crisps and renders its spicy oil in the oven. Halve the capsicum, cut out the seeds and white pith, and slice it into thin strips. Thin slices cook through and char nicely in the short bake; thick chunks stay raw and firm.

steps.title: Shape and top
steps.content: Heat the oven hot, stretch the dough and load on sauce and toppings.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to its hottest, around 240°C fan, with the trays inside. Knock the air out of the dough, split it in two and roll each piece out thin on a floured sheet of baking paper. Spread with the tomato sauce, leaving a border, then scatter over the mozzarella, chorizo and capsicum and a crack of pepper. A thin, even layer of toppings bakes far better than a heavy pile.

steps.title: Bake until blistered
steps.content: Bake on the hot trays until the crust is golden and cheese bubbles.
steps.detail: Slide the pizzas, paper and all, onto the preheated trays and bake for 10 to 12 minutes until the crust is puffed and golden, the mozzarella is bubbling and blistered, and the chorizo edges have crisped. Let each pizza sit for a minute before slicing so the cheese sets enough to cut cleanly.
warnings: The trays and oven are extremely hot. Use dry oven mitts and take care sliding the pizzas on and off.

title: Banana Peanut Oats Jar
description: Creamy overnight oats mashed with banana, peanut butter and a little honey, spiced with cinnamon. Stir it up tonight, grab a jar tomorrow.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge with the lids on — it is designed to be made ahead. Slice the fresh banana on top only when you are ready to eat.
make_ahead: This is a make-ahead breakfast — stir it together the night before and grab a jar on the way out the door.

substitutions.swap: any milk — dairy, oat, soy or almond all work
almond butter, or tahini for a nut-free jar
maple syrup to keep it vegan, or a few chopped dates

steps.title: Mash the banana
steps.content: Mash two ripe bananas smooth, saving one for the topping.
steps.detail: Peel two of the bananas and mash them in a big bowl with a fork until fairly smooth — a few small lumps are fine and add texture. The riper and spottier the bananas, the sweeter and more fragrant your oats will be. Keep the third banana whole for topping in the morning.

steps.title: Whisk the wet mix
steps.content: Stir milk, peanut butter, honey, vanilla and cinnamon into the banana.
steps.detail: Add the milk, peanut butter, honey, cinnamon and vanilla to the mashed banana and whisk until it comes together into a smooth, creamy liquid. Peanut butter can be stubborn — keep stirring and it loosens as the milk warms it slightly. Taste and add a touch more honey if you like it sweeter.

steps.title: Stir in the oats
steps.content: Fold the oats through and divide between the jars.
steps.detail: Tip in the rolled oats and stir until every flake is coated in the banana-peanut milk. It will look quite loose and sloppy at this stage — that is exactly right, as the oats drink up the liquid overnight and thicken to a soft, creamy porridge. Spoon it evenly between four jars.

steps.title: Chill overnight
steps.content: Seal the jars and refrigerate overnight, or at least 6 hours.
steps.detail: Screw the lids on and put the jars in the fridge overnight, or for at least 6 hours. The oats soften and swell as they sit, so there is no cooking needed. If they look too thick in the morning, just loosen with a splash more milk and stir through.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.content: Slice the last banana over each jar with peanut butter and cinnamon.
steps.detail: In the morning, give each jar a stir and slice the reserved banana over the top. Add a small drizzle of peanut butter and a last dusting of cinnamon for a breakfast that looks as good as it tastes. Eat cold straight from the jar, or warm it through if the weather is cold.

title: Creamy Leek Mushroom Spaghetti
description: Silky sweet leeks and golden mushrooms folded through spaghetti in a garlicky parmesan cream. Proper comfort food, on the table in half an hour.
storage: Best eaten fresh. Leftovers keep 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge; reheat gently with a splash of milk or water to loosen the sauce.
leftovers: Fold cold leftovers through a beaten egg and pan-fry into a quick spaghetti frittata.

substitutions.swap: milk warmed with a teaspoon of cornflour, for a lighter sauce
any mushrooms — swiss browns bring the most flavour
any hard grating cheese, such as a block of tasty

steps.title: Clean and slice the leek
steps.content: Halve the leeks, wash out the grit, then slice into half-moons.
steps.detail: Leeks trap sandy grit between their layers, so trim the root and dark tops, halve them lengthwise and fan the layers open under cold running water. Slice into thin half-moons. Slice the mushrooms thickly and finely chop the garlic, keeping the garlic in its own little pile — it burns far faster than everything else.

steps.title: Soften the leek
steps.content: Sweat the leek in oil until silky, about 8 minutes.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-low heat and tip in the leek with a pinch of salt. Cook gently, stirring now and then, until collapsed and sweet with no raw bite, about 8 minutes. Don't rush it on high heat or the leek colours and turns bitter; you want soft and pale.

steps.title: Brown the mushrooms
steps.content: Push the heat up, colour the mushrooms, then add garlic.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to medium-high and add the mushrooms. Leave them to sit for a minute before stirring so they catch and turn golden rather than stewing in their own water. Once browned and shrunk, stir in the garlic and cook for 30 seconds until fragrant — no longer, or it scorches.
tips: If the pan floods with mushroom liquid, keep the heat high and let it boil away before you expect any browning.

steps.title: Boil the spaghetti
steps.content: Cook the spaghetti in well-salted water until al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it well — it should taste like the sea. Add the spaghetti, stir to stop it clumping, and cook to just al dente, usually a minute less than the packet says. Scoop out a mugful of the starchy cooking water before you drain.
tips: That cup of pasta water is liquid gold — it loosens and glosses the sauce right at the end.

steps.title: Make the cream sauce
steps.content: Pour in the cream and parmesan, simmer to a glossy coat.
