steps.detail: Pour the cream into the mushroom pan and bring to a gentle simmer, scraping up any tasty browned bits. Let it bubble softly for a few minutes to thicken slightly, then stir through most of the parmesan until it melts into a glossy sauce. Season with pepper and taste for salt.

steps.title: Toss through the spaghetti
steps.content: Fold the pasta into the sauce, loosening with pasta water.
steps.detail: Drag the drained spaghetti straight into the sauce and toss over low heat until every strand is coated. If it looks tight or claggy, splash in some of the reserved pasta water a little at a time until it turns silky. Serve at once with the last of the parmesan and an extra grind of pepper.
tips: Serve immediately — cream sauces thicken and dull the moment they sit.

title: Beef Tomato Coconut Curry
description: Seared strips of beef in a mellow curry sauce where tinned tomato meets coconut milk. Spooned over fluffy basmati for a soft weeknight warmer.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge, or freeze the curry (without rice) for up to 2 months. Reheat until piping hot.
make_ahead: The curry deepens overnight — make it a day ahead and reheat gently while fresh rice cooks.
leftovers: Spoon cold leftovers into a warmed wrap with a dollop of yoghurt for a quick roti-style roll.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh, cooked through to 74°C
2 tbsp red curry paste for a Thai-leaning version
jasmine rice, or ready microwave rice in a hurry

steps.title: Prep the beef and aromatics
steps.content: Slice the beef into thin strips; dice onion, grate garlic and ginger.
steps.detail: Pat the beef steak dry and slice it across the grain into thin strips — thin cuts stay tender in a quick curry. Dice the onion, then finely grate the garlic and ginger together into a rough paste. Keep the beef on its own board, away from anything you'll eat raw.
warnings: Wash hands, board and knife well after handling raw beef, and keep it away from the rice and anything served uncooked.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse and simmer the basmati by absorption, then steam.
steps.detail: Rinse the basmati until the water runs clear, then add it to a saucepan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, drop to the lowest heat, cover and cook for 12 minutes. Take it off the heat and leave it covered to steam while you make the curry, then fluff with a fork before serving.

steps.title: Sear the beef
steps.content: Brown the beef strips hard and fast, then set them aside.
steps.detail: Heat half the oil in a large deep pan over high heat until shimmering. Add the beef in a single layer, in two batches if you need to, and sear for about a minute each side until well browned but not cooked through. Lift it out onto a plate; it finishes later so it stays juicy.
tips: Don't crowd the pan or the beef steams grey instead of browning.

steps.title: Bloom the curry base
steps.content: Soften the onion, then toast garlic, ginger and curry powder.
steps.detail: Lower the heat to medium, add the rest of the oil and the onion, and cook until soft and golden, about 5 minutes. Stir in the garlic-ginger paste and the curry powder and cook for a minute until fragrant and the spices smell toasty. This blooming step wakes the spices up; if it looks dry, add a splash of water.

steps.title: Simmer the sauce
steps.content: Add tomato and coconut milk, simmer until thick and glossy.
steps.detail: Tip in the tinned tomatoes, crushing them against the pan with your spoon, then pour in the coconut milk. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook uncovered for about 15 minutes, stirring now and then, until the sauce thickens and turns a rich rusty orange. Season with salt and pepper to taste.
tips: If the coconut milk starts to split into oily flecks, drop the heat to a bare simmer to bring it back together.

steps.title: Return the beef
steps.content: Slide the beef back in to warm through for 5 minutes.
steps.detail: Return the seared beef and any resting juices to the pan and simmer gently for about 5 minutes, just until heated through and tender. Avoid a hard boil now, or the quick-cooking steak can toughen. Spoon it over the fluffy basmati.

title: Bacon Cabbage Potato Soup
description: A rib-sticking bowl built on smoky bacon, sweet cabbage and potatoes crushed right into the broth to thicken it. Cheap, hearty and ready in under an hour.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. It thickens as it sits, so loosen with a little water or stock when reheating until piping hot.
make_ahead: Happily made a day ahead — the smoky flavour settles and deepens overnight.
leftovers: Blitz leftovers smooth with a stick blender for a completely different, velvety soup.

substitutions.swap: chorizo for a spicier, paprika-rich pot, or sliced ham
vegetable stock to keep it lighter
kale or savoy cabbage, torn from the stems

steps.title: Dice potato and shred cabbage
steps.content: Dice the potato and bacon, shred the cabbage, dice the onion.
steps.detail: Peel and dice the potatoes into 2 cm chunks so they cook evenly. Chop the bacon into short strips, dice the onion, mince the garlic, and shred the cabbage into ribbons, discarding the tough core. Keep the cabbage in its own pile — it goes in last so it keeps some bite.

steps.title: Render the bacon
steps.content: Fry the bacon in a little oil until golden and crisp.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a large pot over medium heat and add the bacon. Fry until it releases its fat and turns golden and crisp at the edges, about 6 minutes. Scoop out a spoonful of the crispiest bits to scatter on top later, leaving the rest and all that smoky fat in the pot.

steps.title: Sweat onion and garlic
steps.content: Soften the onion and garlic in the smoky bacon fat.
steps.detail: Add the onion to the bacon fat and cook until soft and translucent, about 5 minutes, scraping up any browned bits stuck to the base. Stir in the garlic for the last 30 seconds until fragrant. This smoky base is where the soup gets its backbone.

steps.title: Simmer the potatoes
steps.content: Add the potato and stock, simmer until fork-tender.
steps.detail: Tip in the potatoes and pour over the chicken stock — it should just cover them, so top up with a little water if needed. Bring to a simmer and cook until the potatoes are fork-tender, about 15 minutes. Season carefully with salt, remembering the bacon and stock are already salty.

steps.title: Wilt in the cabbage
steps.content: Stir the cabbage through and cook until just tender.
steps.detail: Stir the shredded cabbage into the pot and cook until wilted and tender but still a touch green, about 8 minutes. It will look like a mountain at first, then collapse down into the broth. Add a splash more water if the soup gets thicker than you like.

steps.title: Crush, season and serve
steps.content: Mash a few potatoes to thicken, then finish with parsley.
steps.detail: Press a few of the potato chunks against the side of the pot with your spoon and stir them back in — this thickens the broth without needing any cream. Taste and adjust the salt and pepper. Ladle into bowls and top with chopped parsley and the reserved crispy bacon.

title: Pumpkin Feta Couscous Bowl
description: Cumin-roasted pumpkin and fluffy couscous tossed with peppery rocket and salty feta, brightened with lemon and toasted pine nuts. A lunch bowl that travels well.
storage: The dressed couscous and pumpkin keep 3 days in the fridge. Keep the rocket separate and add it fresh, as it wilts once dressed.
make_ahead: Roast the pumpkin and steam the couscous ahead, then assemble with the rocket and feta just before eating.
leftovers: Pack cold into a container for a lunchbox that holds up all morning.

substitutions.swap: sweet potato, roasted the same way
haloumi, pan-fried in slices
flaked almonds or roughly chopped walnuts
quinoa, cooked and cooled

steps.title: Roast the pumpkin
steps.content: Toss pumpkin with oil and cumin, roast until caramelised.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan-forced. Peel and cut the pumpkin into 3 cm chunks, toss on a tray with a tablespoon of the olive oil, the cumin and a pinch of salt, and spread out in a single layer. Roast for 25 to 30 minutes, turning once, until soft and caramelised at the edges.

steps.title: Toast the pine nuts
steps.content: Toast the pine nuts in a dry pan until golden.
steps.detail: While the pumpkin roasts, warm a small dry frying pan over medium heat and add the pine nuts. Shake the pan often and watch them closely — they go from pale to golden to burnt in seconds. Tip them onto a plate the moment they smell nutty and look golden.
warnings: Pine nuts scorch in the blink of an eye; don't walk away from the pan.

steps.title: Soak the couscous
steps.content: Cover the couscous with boiling water, steam, then fluff.
steps.detail: Put the couscous in a heatproof bowl with a pinch of salt and a drizzle of the olive oil. Pour over enough boiling water to sit about a centimetre above the grains, cover with a plate and leave for 5 minutes. Fluff it up with a fork to separate every grain into light, steamy clumps.

steps.title: Make the lemon dressing
steps.content: Whisk lemon juice with olive oil, salt and pepper.
steps.detail: Zest the lemon over the couscous, then squeeze its juice into a small bowl. Whisk in the remaining olive oil with a pinch of salt and pepper until it comes together into a loose dressing. Taste and add more lemon if you like it sharp.

steps.title: Build the bowl
steps.content: Fold it all together, then crumble over feta and pine nuts.
steps.detail: Fold the roasted pumpkin and half the rocket through the fluffed couscous, then pour over most of the dressing and toss gently. Pile into bowls with the rest of the rocket, crumble the feta over the top and scatter with the toasted pine nuts. Finish with the last of the dressing.

title: Mussel Tomato Rice Pan
description: Saffron-stained rice simmered with tomato in one wide pan, then crowned with mussels steamed open in the trapped steam. A weeknight nod to paella.
equipment: large wide frying pan with lid
storage: Seafood is best eaten the day it's cooked. Refrigerate any leftovers within an hour and eat within 1 day, reheating once until piping hot. Discard any unopened mussels.

substitutions.swap: prawns, added for the last few minutes until pink
fish or vegetable stock
1/2 tsp turmeric for colour, though the flavour differs
arborio or another short-to-medium grain

steps.title: Clean the mussels
steps.content: Scrub and debeard the mussels, and check every one is alive.
steps.detail: Scrub the mussels under cold water and pull away the wiry beard from each shell. Discard any with cracked shells, and any that gape open and won't close when you tap them on the bench — those are no longer alive. Give them a final rinse and keep them chilled until you need them.
warnings: Only cook mussels that are tightly shut or that close when tapped; throw away any that stay open beforehand.

steps.title: Bloom saffron and soften onion
steps.content: Steep saffron in warm stock; soften the onion and garlic.
steps.detail: Warm the chicken stock in a small pan and crumble in the saffron to steep, turning it a deep gold. Meanwhile, heat the olive oil in a large wide pan and cook the onion over medium heat until soft, about 6 minutes, then add the garlic for a final minute. This gentle base, or sofrito, is the flavour foundation of the whole pan.

steps.title: Toast the rice
steps.content: Stir the rice into the base, then add the tomato.
steps.detail: Tip the jasmine rice into the pan and stir for a minute so every grain glistens with oil and toasts slightly. Add the tinned tomatoes, crushing them with your spoon, and cook for another minute until jammy. Season with a good pinch of salt and pepper.

steps.title: Simmer the rice
steps.content: Pour in the saffron stock and simmer, barely stirring.
steps.detail: Pour in the warm saffron stock, spread the rice into an even layer and bring to a gentle simmer. Cook uncovered for about 15 minutes, resisting the urge to stir — this lets the rice cook evenly and form a little golden crust on the base. It's ready when most of the liquid is absorbed and the rice is nearly tender.
tips: If the pan dries before the rice is cooked, add a splash more hot stock or water around the edges.

steps.title: Steam the mussels
steps.content: Nestle the mussels into the rice, cover, and steam open.
steps.detail: Nestle the mussels hinge-down into the rice, cover the pan with a lid or foil, and steam for about 5 minutes until the shells swing open and the rice finishes cooking in the trapped steam. The rice underneath should be tender with a golden layer beneath. Discard any mussels that stay firmly shut.
warnings: Any mussel still closed after steaming hasn't cooked through — throw it out, don't force it open.

steps.title: Rest and scatter parsley
steps.content: Rest off the heat, then shower with fresh parsley.
steps.detail: Take the pan off the heat and let it sit, still covered, for 5 minutes so the rice settles and firms up. Shower generously with chopped parsley and bring the whole pan to the table, spooning up the golden crust from the base as you serve.

title: Smoked Salmon Beetroot Salad
description: Sweet cooked beetroot and peppery rocket piled over a lemony whipped cream cheese, draped with ribbons of smoked salmon and a scatter of capers. A brunch plate that never sees the stove.
storage: Best assembled and eaten fresh. The whipped cream cheese keeps 2 days covered in the fridge; store the smoked salmon by its use-by date and keep the rocket dry and separate until serving.
make_ahead: Whip the cream cheese and dress the beetroot up to a day ahead, then plate it up in minutes when you're ready.

substitutions.swap: hot-smoked salmon, flaked, or drained tinned salmon
feta whipped with a little yoghurt for a tangier smear
baby spinach or watercress
chopped green olives or finely diced pickle

steps.title: Whip the lemon cream cheese
steps.content: Beat the cream cheese with lemon zest and juice until smooth.
steps.detail: Zest the lemon, then beat the cream cheese in a bowl with half the zest, a squeeze of the juice and a good grind of pepper. Loosen it with a teaspoon of the olive oil until it's soft and spreadable, like a thick dip. Taste and add a small pinch of salt if it needs lifting.

steps.title: Wedge the beetroot
steps.content: Cut the cooked beetroot into wedges and dress lightly.
steps.detail: Cut the cooked beetroot into bite-sized wedges and put them in a bowl with a splash of the olive oil and a little more lemon juice. Toss to coat and set aside — dressing them now lets the flavour soak in. Wear an apron, as beetroot stains everything it touches.
tips: No pre-cooked beetroot? Wrap raw beetroot in foil and roast at 200°C for about an hour until a knife slides in easily.

steps.title: Dress the rocket
steps.content: Toss the rocket with oil, lemon and a pinch of salt.
steps.detail: Just before serving, toss the rocket in a bowl with the last of the olive oil, a squeeze of lemon and a small pinch of salt. Dress it lightly — the leaves should glisten, not drown. Dressing at the last minute keeps them perky rather than wilted.

steps.title: Build the plate
steps.content: Smear the cream cheese, pile on rocket, beetroot and salmon.
steps.detail: Smear a generous swoosh of the whipped cream cheese across each plate. Pile on the dressed rocket and beetroot wedges, then drape the smoked salmon over and around in loose ribbons. Keep it relaxed and tumbling rather than neatly arranged.

steps.title: Scatter capers and finish
steps.content: Top with capers, pepper and the last of the lemon zest.
steps.detail: Scatter the capers over the top for little salty pops, then finish with a grind of pepper and the reserved lemon zest. A last thread of olive oil brings it all together, and it's ready to serve straight away.

title: Kimchi Cheese Toastie
description: Tangy kimchi and molten cheddar pressed between mayo-crisped bread until deep golden. The fridge-raid toastie with a Korean kick.
storage: Toasties are at their best hot from the pan and don't keep well. If you must, refrigerate up to a day and re-crisp in a dry pan over low heat rather than the microwave, which turns them soft.

substitutions.swap: mozzarella for extra stretch, or tasty cheese
thick-cut sourdough or white sandwich bread
sauerkraut for a milder tang

steps.title: Drain and chop the kimchi
steps.content: Squeeze the kimchi dry and chop; slice the spring onions.
steps.detail: Squeeze the kimchi firmly over the sink to wring out as much liquid as you can — wet kimchi makes a soggy toastie. Roughly chop it and thinly slice the spring onions. The drier the filling, the crispier the finished toastie.

steps.title: Butter and mayo the bread
steps.content: Butter the outsides, spread mayo on the insides.
steps.detail: Butter one side of each slice of bread — this is the side that meets the pan and goes golden. Flip them over and spread the dry insides with a thin layer of mayonnaise, which adds tang and helps the cheese grip. It feels back-to-front, but the butter on the outside is the secret to a crisp crust.

steps.title: Build the toasties
steps.content: Layer cheese, kimchi and spring onion, cheese on both sides.
steps.detail: On the mayo side of two slices, scatter half the cheddar, then the chopped kimchi and spring onion, a grind of pepper, and the rest of the cheddar on top. Cheese against both slices melts and glues the toastie shut. Close each with another slice, buttered side facing out.

steps.title: Grill the toasties
steps.content: Cook low and slow, pressing, until golden on both sides.
steps.detail: Set a frying pan over medium-low heat and lay the toasties in, buttered side down. Cook gently for about 3 minutes, pressing down with a spatula, until deep golden, then flip and repeat on the other side. Low and slow is key — too hot and the bread burns before the cheese melts.

steps.title: Rest and slice
steps.content: Rest a minute so the cheese sets, then cut across.
steps.detail: Lift the toasties onto a board and let them sit for a minute — this lets the molten cheese firm up just enough to slice cleanly. Cut across on the diagonal and eat while the middle is still stretchy. The filling holds serious heat, so that first bite can wait a moment.
warnings: Molten cheese and kimchi hold heat like lava — let the toastie rest before biting in.

title: Duck Orange Cabbage Plate
description: Crisp-skinned duck breast rested to a rosy pink and glossed with a honey-orange pan sauce, served with buttery braised cabbage and crunchy roast potatoes. A special-occasion plate worth the effort.
storage: The duck keeps 2 days in the fridge. Store the components separately and reheat the potatoes and cabbage in a hot oven; the duck is lovely gently warmed or sliced cold.
make_ahead: Parboil the potatoes and prep the cabbage ahead; cook the duck fresh, as it's best straight from the pan.
leftovers: Slice cold duck over a leafy salad, or through egg noodles with a splash of soy.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh fillets, skin-on, cooked through to 74°C
maple syrup for the glaze
two mandarins, or a blood orange in season
sweet potato wedges

steps.title: Roast the potatoes
steps.content: Parboil the potatoes, then roast in oil until crisp.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan-forced. Peel and cut the potatoes into large chunks, parboil in salted water for 8 minutes, then drain and let them steam-dry. Tip onto a tray with the olive oil and a pinch of salt, and roast for 35 to 40 minutes, turning once, until deep golden and crisp.
tips: Rough up the parboiled edges by shaking them in the colander — those craggy bits crisp up beautifully.

steps.title: Score and season the duck
steps.content: Crosshatch the skin and season the duck all over.
steps.detail: Pat the duck breasts dry and score the skin in a crosshatch with a sharp knife, cutting through the fat but not into the meat. Season both sides generously with salt and pepper. The scoring helps the fat render out so the skin crisps rather than turning rubbery.
warnings: Wash your hands, knife and board after handling the raw duck, before you touch anything else.

steps.title: Render the duck skin
steps.content: Start skin-down in a cold pan; render until crisp, then flip.
steps.detail: Lay the duck skin-side down in a cold, dry frying pan and only then turn the heat to medium. As the pan warms, the fat renders out and the skin turns deep golden and crisp, around 7 minutes. Pour off the excess fat, flip, and cook the flesh side for 3 to 4 minutes for rosy-pink medium, then rest it on a board.
tips: Spoon off the rendered duck fat as it pools and save it — it makes the best roast potatoes next time.
warnings: The rendered fat gets very hot and spits; tip it into a heatproof container, never down the sink.

steps.title: Braise the cabbage
steps.content: Wilt cabbage wedges in duck fat with a splash of orange.
steps.detail: Cut the cabbage into thick wedges and cook in a spoonful of the reserved duck fat over medium heat until they colour. Squeeze in the juice of half an orange, add the thyme, cover and braise for about 10 minutes until tender but still holding together. Season with salt and pepper.

steps.title: Make the orange glaze
steps.content: Deglaze the duck pan with orange juice and honey.
steps.detail: Pour off all but a little fat from the duck pan and set it over medium heat. Add the juice of the remaining oranges, the honey and a few thyme leaves, scraping up the sticky brown bits. Let it bubble down to a glossy syrup that coats the back of a spoon, about 4 minutes.

steps.title: Slice and plate
steps.content: Slice the rested duck and spoon over the warm glaze.
steps.detail: By now the duck has rested for at least 5 minutes, so the juices settle back into the meat. Slice each breast across into thick pink slices and fan them onto plates with the braised cabbage and crisp potatoes. Spoon the warm orange glaze over the duck and finish with a little orange zest.

title: Pumpkin Sage Ricotta Pizza
description: A homemade base spread with sweet roasted pumpkin, dolloped with ricotta and scattered with sage that crisps in the oven. No tomato in sight.
storage: Best eaten fresh from the oven. Leftover slices keep 2 days in the fridge; re-crisp in a hot oven or dry pan rather than the microwave, which leaves them limp.
make_ahead: Make the dough a day ahead and prove it slowly in the fridge overnight, and roast the pumpkin in advance.

substitutions.swap: torn fresh mozzarella or bocconcini
crumbled feta or soft goat's cheese
sweet potato, roasted the same way
2 cups self-raising flour mixed with yoghurt for a quick no-yeast base

steps.title: Mix and knead the dough
steps.content: Combine flour, yeast, salt, water and oil; knead until smooth.
steps.detail: In a large bowl, mix the flour with the yeast and a good pinch of salt. Stir in about 2/3 cup of warm water and a tablespoon of the olive oil to bring it into a shaggy dough, then knead on the bench for 8 minutes until smooth and springy. Add a little more flour if it's sticky, a splash more water if it's dry.

steps.title: Prove until doubled
steps.content: Cover and leave in a warm spot until doubled in size.
steps.detail: Shape the dough into a ball, drop it into an oiled bowl and cover with a tea towel. Leave it somewhere warm until it has doubled in size, around an hour. It's ready when a fingertip pressed gently into the dough leaves a dent that springs back slowly.

steps.title: Roast the pumpkin
steps.content: Roast pumpkin until soft, then mash half into a base.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 220°C fan-forced. Cut the pumpkin into small cubes, toss with a little of the olive oil and a pinch of salt, and roast for about 20 minutes until soft. Mash roughly half of it into a spreadable purée with a fork and keep the rest as whole pieces for texture.

steps.title: Stretch and top the base
steps.content: Stretch the dough and layer on pumpkin, ricotta and cheese.
steps.detail: Stretch the risen dough onto an oiled tray into a rough circle, pushing from the centre outward and leaving a thicker rim. Spread the mashed pumpkin to the edges, dot over spoonfuls of ricotta, then scatter with the mozzarella, the pumpkin pieces and a few sage leaves. Finish with pepper and a drizzle of oil.

steps.title: Bake the pizza
steps.content: Bake in a very hot oven until the crust is golden.
steps.detail: Turn the oven up to 240°C fan-forced and bake the pizza for 12 to 15 minutes, until the crust is puffed and golden and the cheese is bubbling and blistered. The base should sound hollow when tapped and lift crisp from the tray. Every oven differs, so trust the colour over the clock.
warnings: The tray and oven are fiercely hot — use a dry, folded cloth, as a damp one carries the heat straight through.

steps.title: Crisp the sage and serve
steps.content: Fry the last sage leaves in oil to scatter on top.
steps.detail: While the pizza cools for a minute, fry the remaining sage leaves in a little hot oil for a few seconds until they turn crisp and translucent, then drain on paper towel. Scatter them over the pizza for a savoury, crackly finish. Slice and serve straight away.

title: Mango Coconut Oats Jar
description: Oats soaked overnight in coconut milk and yoghurt, topped with ripe mango, toasted coconut and a squeeze of lime. Breakfast that's ready before you are.
storage: The oats keep sealed in the fridge for up to 3 days. Add the mango and toasted coconut fresh each morning so they stay bright and crunchy rather than soggy.
make_ahead: This is built to be made ahead — stir up a few jars on Sunday for grab-and-go breakfasts through the week.
leftovers: Blend a leftover jar with a handful of ice and extra coconut milk into a mango smoothie.

substitutions.swap: tinned mango cheeks, drained, or frozen mango thawed
dairy-free coconut yoghurt to keep it plant-based
maple syrup, which also makes it vegan
regular milk or any plant milk for a lighter jar

steps.title: Stir up the oats
steps.content: Mix oats with coconut milk, yoghurt, honey and lime zest.
steps.detail: In a bowl or straight in your jars, stir together the rolled oats, coconut milk, half the yoghurt and the honey. Grate in the zest of the lime for a fragrant lift. Stir well so no dry oats are left hiding at the bottom — they need to be fully submerged to soften.

steps.title: Chill overnight
steps.content: Seal and refrigerate at least 6 hours or overnight.
steps.detail: Divide between jars if you haven't already, seal with lids and refrigerate for at least 6 hours, ideally overnight. The oats drink up the coconut milk and swell into a thick, spoonable, creamy porridge. They'll keep happily for up to 3 days, so this is perfect for getting ahead.

steps.title: Toast the coconut
steps.content: Toast the desiccated coconut in a dry pan until golden.
steps.detail: In the morning, warm a small dry pan over medium heat and add the desiccated coconut. Stir constantly until it turns pale gold and smells toasty — this only takes a couple of minutes and it burns fast. Tip it straight onto a plate to stop it cooking further.
warnings: Desiccated coconut goes from golden to burnt in seconds; keep it moving and don't step away.

steps.title: Dice the mango
steps.content: Cut the mango cheeks and dice the flesh into cubes.
steps.detail: Slice the fat cheeks off either side of each mango stone. Score the flesh of each cheek into a criss-cross without cutting through the skin, then push the skin inside-out and slice the cubes free. Squeeze a little lime juice over to keep them bright.

steps.title: Layer and serve
steps.content: Loosen the oats, then top with mango, coconut and honey.
steps.detail: Give the chilled oats a stir and loosen with a splash more coconut milk if they've set too firm. Spoon the remaining yoghurt over the top, pile on the mango, and shower with the toasted coconut. Finish with a drizzle of honey and a last grating of lime zest.

title: Barramundi Lemon Herb Spaghetti
description: Delicate pan-fried barramundi flaked through spaghetti in a lemon, garlic and browned-butter sauce with a shower of fresh parsley. A bright seafood dinner on the table in half an hour.
storage: Best eaten fresh. Leftovers keep 1 day in an airtight container in the fridge; warm gently so the fish doesn't dry out.
leftovers: Fold cold leftovers through a green salad with an extra squeeze of lemon for lunch.

substitutions.swap: any firm white fish like snapper or ling, or a couple of salmon fillets
linguine or any long pasta you have in the cupboard
an extra splash of olive oil to keep it dairy-light

steps.title: Prep lemon, garlic and herbs
steps.content: Zest and juice the lemon, slice garlic, chop parsley.
steps.detail: Finely grate the zest from the lemon, then halve and juice it into a small bowl. Peel and thinly slice the garlic so it cooks evenly and doesn't catch. Roughly chop the parsley leaves. Getting all this ready before the pan is hot keeps you calm, because the sauce comes together fast at the end.

steps.title: Boil the spaghetti
steps.content: Cook spaghetti in well-salted water until al dente.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil and salt it well, so it tastes like the sea. Add the spaghetti, stir so it doesn't clump, and cook until al dente, one minute under the packet time. Just before draining, scoop out a mugful of the starchy cooking water, which is the secret to a glossy sauce.
tips: Set a timer for one minute under the packet time, then taste a strand; it should have a slim pale core.

steps.title: Pan-fry the barramundi
steps.content: Fry the fillets until golden and just cooked through.
steps.detail: Pat the barramundi fillets very dry and season with salt and pepper. Heat the olive oil in a frying pan over medium-high until it shimmers, then lay the fillets in skin-side down, pressing gently for a few seconds so they don't curl. Cook without moving for about 4 minutes until the skin is crisp, then flip and give them 2 minutes more; the flesh should be opaque and flake at a nudge. Lift onto a plate.
warnings: Wash hands, board and knife after handling the raw fish before they touch anything else.
Lay the fillets in away from you; hot oil spits when the wet fish hits the pan.

steps.title: Build the lemon butter
steps.content: Melt butter with garlic, then lemon and chilli.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to medium and drop the butter into the same pan. As it melts and foams, add the sliced garlic and a pinch of chilli flakes and swirl for under a minute until fragrant and just turning pale gold; don't let the garlic brown or it turns bitter. Pour in the lemon juice and a splash of the pasta water, and let it bubble into a light, silky sauce.
warnings: Garlic burns in seconds in hot butter; keep it moving and pull the pan off the heat if it colours too fast.

steps.title: Toss and flake through
steps.content: Toss pasta in the sauce and flake the fish back in.
steps.detail: Lift the drained spaghetti straight into the pan and toss with tongs, loosening with a little pasta water until the sauce clings and shines. Break the barramundi into big flakes and fold it through gently so it doesn't turn to mush. Finish with the parsley, a scatter of lemon zest, a crack of pepper and a pinch more salt if it needs it, and serve at once.

title: Chickpea Spinach Coconut Curry
description: A fast, creamy coconut curry: chickpeas simmered soft in spiced coconut milk with baby spinach wilted through, brightened by a squeeze of lemon and spooned over fluffy basmati.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge, or freeze the curry (without rice) for up to 2 months. Cooked rice keeps 1 day only, so cool it fast and reheat until piping hot.
make_ahead: The curry tastes even better the next day; make it ahead and cook fresh rice to serve.
leftovers: Leftover curry is great folded into a wrap or spooned over a jacket potato.

substitutions.swap: a tin of lentils or white beans, drained and rinsed
a few handfuls of kale or silverbeet, sliced thin
2 tbsp of your favourite curry paste, added with the garlic

steps.title: Rinse rice, chop aromatics
steps.content: Rinse the basmati and dice the onion and garlic.
steps.detail: Rinse the basmati in a sieve under cold water until the water runs clear, which stops it turning gluey. Dice the brown onion into small even pieces and finely chop or grate the garlic. Drain and rinse the chickpeas so they're ready to go in.

steps.title: Cook the basmati
steps.content: Simmer rice with a lid until tender, then rest.
steps.detail: Put the rinsed rice in a saucepan with about one and a half times its volume of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, then drop to the lowest heat, cover, and cook for 12 minutes. Take it off the heat and leave it covered for 10 minutes to steam, then fluff with a fork.
tips: Don't lift the lid while it cooks; the trapped steam is what cooks the rice through.

steps.title: Fry onion and spice
steps.content: Soften the onion, then toast the curry powder.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large pan over medium and cook the onion for about 5 minutes until soft and translucent. Stir in the garlic and curry powder and cook for another minute until the kitchen smells warm and toasty; this wakes the spices up. Keep it moving so the garlic and spice don't scorch.
warnings: Ground spices and garlic catch and turn bitter fast; stir constantly and lower the heat if the pan looks dry.

steps.title: Simmer in coconut milk
steps.content: Add chickpeas and coconut milk; simmer to thicken.
steps.detail: Tip in the drained chickpeas and pour over the coconut milk, scraping the spiced bits off the bottom of the pan. Bring to a gentle simmer and let it bubble away for about 10 minutes, stirring now and then, until the sauce thickens slightly and turns a pale gold. Season with salt to taste.

steps.title: Wilt the spinach
steps.content: Stir spinach through until just wilted; add lemon.
steps.detail: Add the baby spinach a handful at a time, stirring until it collapses into the sauce; it only takes a minute or two. Squeeze in the lemon juice and taste, adding more salt or a crack of pepper if it needs lifting. It should taste rich, gently spiced and fresh.

steps.title: Serve over rice
steps.content: Spoon the curry over the fluffed basmati.
steps.detail: Fluff the rested rice with a fork and divide it between bowls. Spoon the chickpea curry over the top and add an extra wedge of lemon on the side. Scatter over the fresh coriander if you have it, for a bright, fragrant finish.

title: Green Pea Mint Soup
description: Sweet frozen peas blitzed with soft leek and a handful of fresh mint into a vivid green soup, finished with a swirl of yoghurt and a squeeze of lemon. Twenty minutes, start to bowl.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge, or freeze for up to 3 months (add the yoghurt only when serving). Reheat gently and don't boil, or the fresh colour dulls.
make_ahead: Make the soup base a day ahead; reheat gently and swirl in the yoghurt just before serving.
leftovers: Leftovers are lovely served cold as a summer soup; just check the seasoning first.

substitutions.swap: frozen broad beans, or a mix of peas and frozen spinach
a splash of cream, or coconut milk to keep it vegan
1 brown onion, softened the same gentle way
half mint and half basil for a different, peppery note

steps.title: Slice and wash the leek
steps.content: Trim, slice and rinse the leek free of grit.
steps.detail: Trim the root and the tough dark green top from the leek, then slice the white and pale-green part into thin rounds. Leeks hide grit between their layers, so tip the slices into a bowl of cold water, swish them about, and lift them out leaving the dirt behind. Shake off the excess water.

steps.title: Sweat the leek
steps.content: Soften the leek gently in oil without colouring.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a saucepan over medium-low and add the leek with a pinch of salt. Cook gently for about 7 minutes, stirring often, until soft and sweet but not browned; you want it melting, not golden. If it starts to catch, add a splash of water and turn the heat down.

steps.title: Add the stock
steps.content: Pour in the stock and bring to a simmer.
steps.detail: Pour in the vegetable stock, turn the heat up and bring it to a gentle simmer. Let it tick over for about 5 minutes to bring the flavours together before the peas go in.

steps.title: Add peas and mint
steps.content: Simmer the peas briefly to keep them bright.
steps.detail: Tip in the frozen peas and cook for just 3 minutes, no longer, or they lose their sweet colour and turn a dull khaki. Take the pan off the heat and stir in most of the mint leaves, saving a few for serving. The residual heat is enough to release their scent.

steps.title: Blend until smooth
steps.content: Blitz to a smooth soup and season with lemon.
steps.detail: Blend the soup until completely smooth, either with a stick blender in the pan or in batches in an upright blender. Stir in a squeeze of lemon and season with salt and pepper to taste. If it's thicker than you like, loosen with a splash of hot water or stock.
warnings: Hot soup can erupt when blended; vent the lid under a folded tea towel, or keep a stick blender fully submerged before switching it on.

steps.title: Swirl yoghurt and serve
steps.content: Ladle up, swirl yoghurt and scatter mint.
steps.detail: Ladle the soup into warm bowls and swirl a spoon of yoghurt through each. Scatter over the reserved mint, add a drizzle of olive oil and a final crack of pepper. Serve with crusty bread for dunking.

title: Chicken Tahini Quinoa Bowl
description: Cumin-rubbed chicken thighs seared golden and sliced over fluffy quinoa with cucumber and tomato, all pulled together with a creamy lemon-tahini drizzle. A fresh bowl that travels well for lunch.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge; store the tahini sauce separately and dress just before eating. Cool the cooked chicken quickly and keep it cold.
make_ahead: Cook the quinoa and whisk the tahini sauce a day ahead; sear the chicken and assemble fresh.
leftovers: Wrap cold leftovers in a flatbread with extra tahini for an easy next-day lunch.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast (take care not to overcook it), or drained chickpeas for a veg bowl
couscous or brown rice
smooth peanut butter loosened with lemon and water

steps.title: Rinse and cook the quinoa
steps.content: Simmer rinsed quinoa until tender, then fluff.
steps.detail: Rinse the quinoa well in a sieve, which washes off the natural coating that can taste soapy or bitter. Tip it into a saucepan with two parts water to one of quinoa and a pinch of salt, bring to the boil, then cover and simmer low for about 13 minutes until the little grains uncurl and the water is gone. Rest for 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork.

steps.title: Season the chicken
steps.content: Rub the thighs with oil, cumin, salt and pepper.
steps.detail: Pat the chicken thighs dry and toss them in a bowl with the olive oil, ground cumin, salt and pepper until evenly coated. Dry thighs and a little oil are what give you a proper golden crust rather than a grey steam.
warnings: Wash your hands, the bowl and the board well after handling raw chicken, and keep it away from the salad ingredients.

steps.title: Pan-fry the thighs
steps.content: Fry until golden and cooked to 74°C, then rest.
steps.detail: Heat a frying pan over medium-high and lay the thighs in smooth-side down. Cook for about 6 minutes until deep golden, then flip and cook another 4 to 5 minutes until cooked right through. They're done at 74°C at the thickest point, or when the juices run clear with no pink. Rest for a few minutes, then slice.
warnings: Chicken must reach 74°C in the middle; cut into the thickest thigh to check there's no pink before serving.

steps.title: Whisk the tahini sauce
steps.content: Loosen tahini with lemon, garlic and water.
steps.detail: In a small bowl, whisk the tahini with the lemon juice and grated garlic. It will seize into a thick paste at first, so don't panic. Add cold water a tablespoon at a time, whisking, and it loosens into a smooth, pourable cream. Season with a pinch of salt.
tips: If it's still tight, keep adding water bit by bit; it always looks like it's splitting just before it goes silky.

steps.title: Chop the salad
steps.content: Dice the cucumber and tomato.
steps.detail: Dice the cucumber and tomato into bite-sized pieces and season with a small pinch of salt. Keeping them chunky gives the bowl a fresh, crunchy contrast to the soft quinoa and chicken.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Layer quinoa, chicken and salad; drizzle tahini.
steps.detail: Spoon the quinoa into bowls as a base and pile the sliced chicken, cucumber and tomato on top. Drizzle generously with the lemon-tahini sauce and finish with a crack of pepper. Add a wedge of lemon on the side for squeezing.

title: Black Bean Corn Rice Skillet
description: A one-pan supper of jasmine rice toasted with cumin and sweet red capsicum, folded with black beans and corn and blanketed in melted cheddar, finished with a good squeeze of lime.
equipment: large deep frying pan with lid
storage: Cooked rice keeps 1 day only, so cool it quickly, refrigerate, and reheat until piping hot right through. It also freezes for up to 1 month.
leftovers: Roll cold leftovers into a wrap with a spoon of salsa, or crisp them in a hot pan for a golden base.

substitutions.swap: tinned kidney or pinto beans, drained and rinsed
basmati or any long-grain rice
mozzarella or a Mexican-style cheese mix
a drained tin of corn kernels

steps.title: Chop onion and capsicum
steps.content: Dice the brown onion and red capsicum.
steps.detail: Dice the brown onion and the red capsicum into small, even pieces so they soften at the same rate. Drain and rinse the black beans, and measure out the frozen corn so everything is ready to go into the one pan.

steps.title: Soften and toast the cumin
steps.content: Cook onion and capsicum, then toast the cumin.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large deep frying pan over medium and cook the onion and capsicum for about 5 minutes until softened. Stir in the ground cumin and let it toast for a minute until fragrant; this is what gives the whole dish its warm, smoky backbone.

steps.title: Toast the rice
steps.content: Stir the jasmine rice to coat and lightly toast.
steps.detail: Add the jasmine rice to the pan and stir for a minute or two so every grain is coated in the oil and spice and just starts to turn translucent at the edges. Toasting the rice first keeps the grains separate and adds a subtle nuttiness.

steps.title: Simmer covered
steps.content: Add water, cover and simmer until the rice is tender.
steps.detail: Pour in about two and a quarter cups of water with a good pinch of salt, stir once, and bring to the boil. Drop to low, cover, and cook for 12 minutes until the liquid is absorbed and the rice is tender. Turn off the heat and leave it covered.
tips: Resist lifting the lid while it cooks; the trapped steam is doing the work.
Use vegetable stock in place of the water for a deeper flavour if you have it.

steps.title: Fold in beans and corn
steps.content: Stir through beans and corn to warm.
steps.detail: Gently fold the drained black beans and frozen corn through the hot rice and put the lid back on for 3 to 4 minutes to heat them through. Season with salt and pepper and taste; it should be warm-spiced and a little sweet from the corn.

steps.title: Melt cheese and lime
steps.content: Scatter cheddar to melt, then squeeze over lime.
steps.detail: Scatter the grated cheddar over the top, cover, and leave for 2 minutes off the heat until it melts into the rice. Squeeze over the lime just before serving; the sharpness cuts through the richness and ties it all together.

title: Chickpea Cucumber Herb Salad
description: Nutty chickpeas tossed with crisp cucumber, ripe tomato and a big handful of parsley and mint in a sharp lemon dressing. A no-cook salad that only gets better as it sits.
storage: Best eaten the day it's made. Undressed, it keeps 2 days in the fridge; once dressed, the cucumber and herbs soften after a few hours.
make_ahead: Chop everything and make the dressing ahead; toss together up to an hour before serving so the herbs stay bright.
leftovers: Stir leftovers through cooked couscous, or pile into a wrap with a smear of hummus.

substitutions.swap: tinned white beans or lentils, drained and rinsed
2 sliced spring onions for a milder bite
all parsley, or add a little dill

steps.title: Drain and dry chickpeas
steps.content: Drain, rinse and pat the chickpeas dry.
steps.detail: Drain the chickpeas and rinse them under cold water to wash off the tinny liquid, then tip them onto a clean tea towel and pat dry. Dry chickpeas soak up the dressing instead of sliding off it, so this small step is worth doing.

steps.title: Chop cucumber, tomato, onion
steps.content: Dice cucumber and tomato; finely slice red onion.
steps.detail: Dice the cucumber and tomato into rough, bite-sized chunks and finely slice the red onion. If raw onion is too sharp for you, soak the slices in cold water for 5 minutes and drain; it softens the bite while keeping the crunch.

steps.title: Chop the herbs
steps.content: Roughly chop the parsley and mint.
steps.detail: Roughly chop the parsley and pick and tear the mint leaves. Use a generous amount, because the herbs aren't a garnish here; they're a main part of the salad and give it its fresh, green lift.

steps.title: Whisk the lemon dressing
steps.content: Whisk lemon juice with olive oil, salt and pepper.
steps.detail: In a small jar or bowl, whisk the lemon juice with the olive oil, a good pinch of salt and a crack of pepper until it looks thickened and combined. Taste it on a chickpea; it should be zippy and well-seasoned, since it has to flavour the whole bowl.

steps.title: Toss and rest
steps.content: Toss everything together and let it sit briefly.
steps.detail: Tip the chickpeas, cucumber, tomato, onion and herbs into a big bowl, pour over the dressing and toss well. Let it sit for 5 minutes so the flavours mingle, then taste and adjust the salt and lemon. Serve at room temperature.

title: Bacon Egg Bagel Stack
description: Toasted bagels stacked with cream cheese, peppery rocket, tomato, crisp bacon and a fried egg with a golden, runny yolk. The kind of brunch that eats like a hug.
storage: Best assembled and eaten straight away. Cooked bacon keeps 2 days in the fridge; the eggs and toasted bagels don't keep, so cook those to order.
make_ahead: Fry the bacon ahead and crisp it back up in a hot pan for a minute; toast the bagels and fry the eggs fresh.
leftovers: Any spare crisp bacon is brilliant crumbled over a salad or through a bowl of pasta.

substitutions.swap: sliced ham, or halloumi fried till golden for a vegetarian stack
smashed avocado
baby spinach
English muffins or thick sourdough

steps.title: Fry the bacon crisp
steps.content: Cook the bacon in a dry pan until crisp.
steps.detail: Lay the bacon in a cold frying pan and set it over medium heat; starting cold renders the fat and crisps it evenly. Cook for about 7 minutes, turning once, until deep golden and crisp at the edges, then lift onto paper towel to drain. Leave the tasty bacon fat in the pan for the eggs.
warnings: Wash your hands after handling the raw bacon.
The rendered fat spits; keep it at a steady medium and stand back a little as you turn the rashers.

steps.title: Fry the eggs
steps.content: Fry eggs in the bacon fat, yolks still runny.
steps.detail: Crack the eggs into the hot bacon fat, adding a splash of olive oil if the pan looks dry, and fry for 2 to 3 minutes, spooning a little fat over the tops, until the whites are set but the yolks still wobble. Season with salt and pepper. For firmer yolks, cover the pan for the last minute.

steps.title: Toast bagels, slice tomato
steps.content: Halve and toast the bagels; slice the tomato.
steps.detail: Halve the bagels and toast the cut sides until golden and crisp, in the toaster or under a hot grill. While they toast, slice the tomatoes into thick rounds. A well-toasted base holds up to the fillings instead of going soggy.

steps.title: Spread the cream cheese
steps.content: Spread cream cheese thickly on the warm bagels.
steps.detail: Spread a thick layer of cream cheese over the toasted cut sides of each bagel while they're still warm so it softens and spreads easily. This is the glue that holds the stack together and adds a cool, tangy richness.

steps.title: Stack and top
steps.content: Layer rocket, tomato, bacon and egg; add the lid.
steps.detail: On each bagel base pile a handful of rocket, then the tomato, the crisp bacon and finally the fried egg. Add a crack of pepper, set the bagel lid on top and press down gently. Eat straight away, while the yolk is still warm and the bacon crisp.

title: Pork Ribs Honey Paprika Plate
description: Sticky honey and smoked-paprika pork ribs, slow-roasted until the meat slips off the bone, plated up with golden roast potatoes and a sharp, creamy cabbage slaw.
storage: Cooked ribs and potatoes keep 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge; reheat, covered, until piping hot. Dress the slaw fresh, as it weeps and softens overnight.
make_ahead: Slow-roast the wrapped ribs a day ahead; glaze and caramelise them just before serving so they come to the table hot and sticky.
leftovers: Strip leftover rib meat off the bone and pile it into a toasted roll with slaw for a next-day sandwich.

substitutions.swap: pork belly strips or chicken wings (they'll need less time in the oven)
maple syrup or brown sugar melted with a splash of water
Greek yoghurt for a lighter slaw
white wine vinegar or a squeeze of lemon

steps.title: Rub and wrap the ribs
steps.content: Coat the ribs in the spice rub and wrap in foil.
steps.detail: Pat the ribs dry and rub them all over with the olive oil, most of the smoked paprika, the crushed garlic, salt and pepper, saving a little paprika for the glaze. Sit them on a large sheet of foil on a roasting tray and wrap them into a sealed parcel; this traps the steam that makes them tender.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and anything the raw pork touched before moving on.

steps.title: Slow-roast until tender
steps.content: Roast the wrapped ribs low and slow until tender.
steps.detail: Roast in a 160°C fan oven for about 1 hour 30 minutes, until the meat is tender and has pulled back from the ends of the bones. Don't unwrap them to check too early; they need the full time in their steamy parcel to go meltingly soft.
warnings: Open the foil parcel away from your face; a burst of scalding steam escapes when you unwrap it.

steps.title: Roast the potatoes
steps.content: Roast oiled potato chunks until crisp and golden.
steps.detail: Cut the potatoes into chunks, toss them with olive oil and salt on a second tray, and turn the oven up to 200°C fan. Roast for about 40 minutes, turning once halfway, until crisp and deep golden. The wrapped ribs are happy to keep roasting alongside them at the higher heat.

steps.title: Make the slaw
steps.content: Toss shredded cabbage with mayo and cider vinegar.
steps.detail: Finely shred the cabbage and toss it in a bowl with the mayonnaise, apple cider vinegar, a pinch of salt and a crack of pepper. Let it sit for 10 minutes to soften slightly and take on the tang, then taste and add a little more vinegar if you like it sharper.

steps.title: Glaze and caramelise
steps.content: Brush with honey glaze and blast until sticky.
steps.detail: Unwrap the ribs and brush them all over with the honey mixed with the reserved smoked paprika. Turn the oven up to 220°C fan and roast for 8 to 10 minutes, until the glaze is bubbling, sticky and lacquered. Watch them closely at this stage.
warnings: Honey glaze burns fast under high heat; check every couple of minutes and pull the tray the moment it turns deep and sticky.

steps.title: Rest, slice and plate
steps.content: Rest the ribs, slice between the bones and plate up.
steps.detail: Rest the ribs for 5 minutes, then slice down between the bones into individual ribs. Pile them onto plates with the crisp roast potatoes and a generous heap of the slaw alongside.

title: Prawn Chilli Lemon Pizza
description: A blistered white pizza with no tomato, just a garlicky olive-oil base, molten mozzarella, sweet prawns and fresh chilli, finished with a squeeze of lemon and torn parsley.
equipment: large baking tray or pizza tray
storage: Best straight from the oven. Leftover slices keep 1 day in the fridge; reheat in a hot oven or pan until piping hot, never lukewarm.
make_ahead: Make the dough the night before and prove it slowly in the fridge; bring it back to room temperature before stretching.
leftovers: Reheat leftover slices in a dry hot pan to bring the crisp base right back.

substitutions.swap: sliced calamari or firm white fish, or halloumi for no seafood
a pinch of chilli flakes
half mozzarella and half torn bocconcini
a bought pizza base to skip the dough entirely

steps.title: Mix and knead the dough
steps.content: Bring the dough together and knead until springy.
steps.detail: In a big bowl, stir the flour, yeast and a good pinch of salt together, then mix in about two-thirds of a cup of warm water and a tablespoon of the olive oil to make a shaggy dough. Tip it onto the bench and knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth, soft and springy, so it bounces back when you press it. Add a little more flour if it's sticky, a little water if it's dry.

steps.title: Prove until doubled
steps.content: Rest the dough somewhere warm until doubled.
steps.detail: Shape the dough into a ball, return it to the oiled bowl and cover with a tea towel. Leave it somewhere warm for about an hour, until it has doubled in size and feels puffy and airy. This is where the base gets its light, chewy crust.

steps.title: Prep prawns, chilli, garlic
steps.content: Mix garlic oil and toss the prawns with chilli.
steps.detail: Peel and devein the prawns if they aren't already and pat them dry. Finely slice the chilli and crush the garlic, then stir most of the garlic into the remaining olive oil to make a garlicky base oil. Toss the prawns with the sliced chilli, the rest of the garlic and a little lemon zest so they're full of flavour before they hit the heat.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and knife after handling the raw prawns before touching the dough or anything else.

steps.title: Stretch and top the base
steps.content: Stretch the dough, brush with garlic oil, add toppings.
steps.detail: Stretch the proved dough out on an oiled tray into a large round, leaving a slightly thicker rim. Brush the base all over with the garlic oil, scatter over the grated mozzarella, then dot the prawns and chilli evenly across the top. Don't overload the middle or the base won't crisp.

steps.title: Bake until blistered
steps.content: Bake in a hot oven until the crust and prawns are done.
steps.detail: Bake in a 240°C fan oven for 10 to 12 minutes, until the crust is puffed and blistered, the cheese is bubbling and the prawns have turned pink and opaque. Prawns cook fast, so this hot-and-fast blast keeps them sweet and juicy rather than rubbery.
warnings: Don't overcook the prawns; they're ready as soon as they curl and turn pink, and any longer toughens them.

steps.title: Finish with lemon and parsley
steps.content: Squeeze over lemon and scatter parsley to serve.
steps.detail: Straight out of the oven, squeeze over the lemon juice and scatter with torn parsley and a final crack of pepper. Slice and eat while it's hot and the base is crisp. The lemon over the top is what makes the whole thing sing.

title: Chocolate Cherry Oats Jar
description: Creamy overnight oats gone chocolatey with cocoa and vanilla, studded with sweet cherries. Stir it together tonight and grab a jar on your way out in the morning.
storage: Keeps 3 days in sealed jars in the fridge, which is the whole point. Give them a stir and loosen with a splash of milk before eating.
make_ahead: Made for it: mix all four jars up to 3 days ahead for grab-and-go breakfasts through the week.

substitutions.swap: any plant milk such as oat, almond or soy for dairy-free
maple syrup to keep it vegan
frozen berries or chopped strawberries
cocoa plus a handful of chocolate chips for extra richness

steps.title: Pit and halve the cherries
steps.content: Pit the cherries and halve most of them.
steps.detail: Pit the cherries and cut most of them in half, keeping a few whole to top the jars. If you're using frozen cherries, let them thaw first and hold onto any ruby juices to stir through, as they colour the oats beautifully.
tips: No cherry pitter? Push each cherry down onto the neck of a bottle with a chopstick and the stone pops straight through.

steps.title: Mix the chocolate oats
steps.content: Stir oats with cocoa, milk, honey and vanilla.
steps.detail: In a bowl, stir the rolled oats, cocoa powder, milk, honey and vanilla together until there's no dry cocoa left streaking through. It'll look loose, which is right; the oats drink up the milk as they sit and thicken overnight into something spoonable.
tips: Whisk the cocoa into the honey first to knock out any lumps before you add the milk.

steps.title: Fold in the cherries
steps.content: Stir the halved cherries through the oats.
steps.detail: Fold the halved cherries and any thawed juices through the chocolate oats so they're spread all the way through. Hold back a small handful of the prettiest ones for topping.

steps.title: Jar and chill overnight
steps.content: Divide into jars, top with cherries and chill.
steps.detail: Spoon the mixture into four jars or containers, top with the reserved cherries, and seal with a lid. Chill in the fridge for at least 4 hours, but overnight is best; they'll be thick, cold and ready to eat straight from the jar.

steps.title: Loosen and serve
steps.content: Stir, loosen with milk and drizzle with honey.
steps.detail: In the morning, give the oats a stir. If they've set thicker than you like, loosen with a splash more milk. Drizzle with a little extra honey and scatter over the last of the cherries before digging in.

title: Gochujang Pork Noodle Toss
description: Sticky, spicy pork in a glossy gochujang sauce, tangled through egg noodles with sweet cabbage and a hit of sesame. Big Korean flavour in about twenty minutes.
equipment: large wok or deep frying pan
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Reheat in a hot pan with a splash of water to loosen the noodles.
make_ahead: Stir the gochujang and soy into a quick sauce and shred the cabbage a few hours ahead, so it's a fast fry at dinnertime.
leftovers: Cold, the noodles make a good next-day lunch — refresh with a drizzle of sesame oil and extra spring onion.

substitutions.swap: beef mince works, or crumbled firm tofu for a meat-free toss
rice noodles, or even spaghetti at a pinch
sriracha with a small spoon of miso, though you lose some of the deep chilli sweetness

steps.title: Slice the aromatics and cabbage
steps.content: Shred cabbage, slice spring onion, grate ginger and garlic, mix the sauce.
steps.detail: Finely shred the cabbage into 1cm ribbons. Slice the spring onions on an angle, keeping the white and green parts in separate piles — the whites go in early, the greens are for the finish. Grate the ginger and garlic. In a small bowl, stir the gochujang and soy together with a splash of water so it's pourable.

steps.title: Boil the noodles
steps.content: Cook egg noodles to the packet time, then drain and oil.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to the boil and cook the egg noodles to the packet time — usually around 4 minutes — until just tender with a little bite. Drain in a colander and toss with a few drops of the sesame oil so they don't clump while you cook the pork.

steps.title: Brown the pork
steps.content: Fry pork mince hard until deep golden with crisp edges.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in the wok over high until it shimmers. Add the pork mince and press it flat, letting it sit for 2 minutes to catch and brown before you break it up. Keep frying, breaking it into crumbs, until cooked through with crisp, golden edges and no pink remains, about 6 minutes.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and anything raw pork touched before it goes near food you'll eat as is.

steps.title: Add aromatics and cabbage
steps.content: Stir in garlic, ginger and onion whites, then the cabbage.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to medium-high. Add the garlic, ginger and spring onion whites and stir for 30 seconds until fragrant. Tip in the shredded cabbage and toss for 2 to 3 minutes until it wilts down but keeps a little crunch.
warnings: Garlic burns fast on this heat — keep it moving and add the cabbage before it colours past pale gold.

steps.title: Toss it together
steps.content: Add the sauce and noodles, toss until glossy.
steps.detail: Pour in the gochujang-soy sauce and let it bubble for a few seconds, then add the drained noodles. Toss everything with tongs for 1 to 2 minutes until the noodles are glossy and evenly coated and the sauce clings. Taste and adjust with a pinch of salt and pepper.

steps.title: Finish with sesame and greens
steps.content: Drizzle sesame oil and scatter the spring onion greens.
steps.detail: Off the heat, drizzle over the last of the sesame oil for that toasty aroma and scatter with the reserved spring onion greens. Serve straight away in bowls while it's steaming and glossy.

title: Chicken Peanut Lime Curry
description: A mellow, family-friendly Thai-style curry: red curry paste and coconut milk rounded out with peanut butter and lifted with fresh lime. Mild enough for the kids.
equipment: large deep frying pan or wok
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge and the flavour deepens overnight. Reheat gently, loosening with a splash of water. Store the rice separately and eat within a day.
make_ahead: The curry sauce reheats beautifully — make it a day ahead and cook fresh rice to serve.
leftovers: Spoon leftovers into a warm wrap with fresh coriander for a quick lunch.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh for a juicier result, or chickpeas to make it vegetarian
any smooth nut butter, or a spoon of tahini
green curry paste for a fresher, hotter version

steps.title: Cook the jasmine rice
steps.content: Rinse and simmer the rice, then let it steam.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice under cold water until it runs clear, then put it in a saucepan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, drop to the lowest heat, put the lid on and cook for 12 minutes. Take it off the heat and leave it to steam, lid on, for another 10 minutes while you make the curry — don't lift the lid.
tips: The lowest heat you can manage stops the base catching and gives separate, fluffy grains.

steps.title: Sear the chicken
steps.content: Brown the diced chicken, then set it aside.
steps.detail: Cut the chicken breast into 2cm pieces. Heat the olive oil in a wide pan over medium-high and sear the chicken until golden on the outside, about 4 minutes — it won't be cooked through yet. Lift it out onto a plate and leave the pan on the heat.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash your hands, the board and the knife straight after, and don't let it touch anything served raw.

steps.title: Fry the curry paste
steps.content: Sizzle the red curry paste until fragrant.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to medium. Add the red curry paste to the same pan and fry for about 1 minute, stirring, until it smells fragrant and the oil takes on its colour. This wakes up the spices; if it looks close to catching, move straight to the next step.

steps.title: Make the coconut sauce
steps.content: Stir in coconut milk, peanut butter and fish sauce.
steps.detail: Pour in the coconut milk and stir in the peanut butter until it melts into a smooth, glossy sauce. Add the fish sauce for a savoury backbone. Bring to a gentle simmer — small bubbles around the edge, not a hard boil, or the coconut milk can split.

steps.title: Simmer the chicken through
steps.content: Return the chicken and simmer until cooked to 74°C.
steps.detail: Slide the chicken and any resting juices back in. Simmer gently for 8 to 10 minutes until the sauce thickens enough to coat a spoon and the chicken is cooked through — 74°C at the thickest point, with no pink inside.

steps.title: Finish with lime and coriander
steps.content: Squeeze in lime, scatter coriander, serve over rice.
steps.detail: Take the pan off the heat and squeeze in the juice of half the lime. Taste, then add more if it needs lifting — it should be creamy, savoury and gently sour. Spoon over the fluffed rice and scatter with torn coriander, with lime wedges on the side.

title: Chicken Ginger Bok Choy Broth
description: A clean, restorative bowl of poached chicken thigh in a gingery stock, with bok choy wilted in at the last minute. Light, warming and on the table in half an hour.
storage: Keeps 2 days in the fridge in an airtight container; add fresh bok choy when you reheat so it stays green. Bring to a full simmer before serving.
make_ahead: The gingery broth base can be simmered a day ahead; poach the chicken and add the bok choy just before serving.
leftovers: Turn leftovers into a noodle soup — add cooked egg noodles and a soft-boiled egg.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast, though thigh stays more tender in a broth
any Asian green — pak choy, choy sum, or even baby spinach
a spoon of miso for a deeper, cloudier broth

steps.title: Slice the ginger and greens
steps.content: Slice ginger, smash garlic, wash and separate the bok choy.
steps.detail: Slice the ginger into thin coins — no need to peel if it's fresh. Smash the garlic cloves with the flat of a knife. Trim the bok choy and separate the leaves, washing well between them where grit hides. Slice the spring onions, keeping the whites and greens in separate piles.

steps.title: Bloom the aromatics
steps.content: Sizzle ginger, garlic and onion whites in oil.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a large pot over medium heat. Add the ginger coins, smashed garlic and spring onion whites and stir for 1 to 2 minutes until fragrant and just starting to colour — this is the backbone of the broth.
warnings: Garlic scorches quickly and turns bitter — keep the heat moderate and stir it constantly.

steps.title: Poach the chicken
steps.content: Add stock and water, simmer the thighs gently.
steps.detail: Pour in the chicken stock and 2 cups of water, then slide in the whole chicken thighs. Bring to a bare simmer — barely trembling, not boiling — and cook for 15 minutes until tender. Skim off any foam that rises for a clear broth.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash your hands and anything it touched before handling other food.

steps.title: Shred and season
steps.content: Lift out the chicken, shred it, season the broth with soy.
steps.detail: Lift the thighs out and shred or slice them — they should pull apart easily and show no pink, 74°C at the thickest point. Return the meat to the pot. Season the broth with the soy sauce, then taste and adjust with salt and a little pepper.

steps.title: Wilt the bok choy
steps.content: Drop in the bok choy for just 2 minutes.
steps.detail: Add the bok choy to the simmering broth and cook for just 2 minutes — the stems should keep a slight crunch and the leaves turn silky and bright green. Any longer and they go grey and floppy.

steps.title: Ladle and drizzle
steps.content: Ladle into bowls, finish with sesame oil and greens.
steps.detail: Ladle the broth, chicken and greens into deep bowls. Drizzle each with a few drops of sesame oil for aroma and scatter over the reserved spring onion greens. Serve piping hot, on its own or over steamed rice.

title: Tofu Peanut Rice Bowl
description: Crisp pan-fried tofu over fluffy jasmine rice with raw cabbage and carrot, pulled together by a quick peanut-lime sauce. A fresh, filling vegan bowl.
storage: Best fresh while the tofu is crisp. The components keep separately for 2 days in the fridge; the tofu softens once dressed, so keep the sauce apart until serving.
make_ahead: Press the tofu and whisk the peanut sauce ahead; shred the veg and store it in the fridge, then fry and assemble at dinnertime.
leftovers: Pack cold into a lunchbox with the sauce on the side to keep the veg crunchy.

substitutions.swap: tempeh, or crumble and pan-fry it for a mince-like texture
smooth cashew or almond butter
tamari to keep it gluten-free

steps.title: Press and cube the tofu
steps.content: Drain, press and cube the tofu for maximum crisp.
steps.detail: Drain the tofu and press it: wrap it in a clean tea towel, sit a plate with a couple of tins on top, and leave for 10 minutes to squeeze out the water — the drier it is, the crispier it fries. Cut into 2cm cubes.
tips: Use firm or extra-firm tofu; silken will collapse in the pan.

steps.title: Cook the jasmine rice
steps.content: Rinse and steam the rice until fluffy.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then simmer in a lidded pan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt: bring to the boil, drop to low, lid on for 12 minutes, then rest off the heat for 10 minutes. Fluff with a fork.

steps.title: Shred the vegetables
steps.content: Finely shred the cabbage and grate the carrots.
steps.detail: Shred the cabbage into thin ribbons and grate or julienne the carrots into matchsticks. Keep them raw and crisp — they're the fresh crunch against the soft rice and tofu.

steps.title: Whisk the peanut sauce
steps.content: Whisk peanut butter, soy, lime and warm water smooth.
steps.detail: In a bowl, whisk the peanut butter with the soy sauce, the juice of half the lime and 3 to 4 tablespoons of warm water until smooth and pourable, like thin cream. Taste: it should be salty, nutty and tangy. Loosen with more water if it's stiff.
tips: Warm water loosens stubborn peanut butter far better than cold.

steps.title: Crisp the tofu
steps.content: Toss tofu in cornflour and fry until golden.
steps.detail: Toss the tofu cubes in the cornflour with a pinch of salt and pepper to coat lightly. Heat the olive oil in a non-stick pan over medium-high and fry, turning every couple of minutes, until golden and crisp on all sides, about 8 minutes. Don't crowd the pan or the tofu steams instead of crisping.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Layer rice, veg and tofu, then spoon over the sauce.
steps.detail: Spoon the fluffed rice into bowls and top with the cabbage, carrot and crisp tofu. Spoon the peanut sauce generously over the top and finish with a wedge of lime to squeeze at the table.

title: Sweet Potato Lentil Pan
description: A cosy one-pan of soft sweet potato and red lentils simmered in curried stock until thick, finished with wilted spinach and a cooling spoon of yoghurt.
equipment: large deep frying pan with lid
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge and thickens as it sits — loosen with a splash of water or stock when reheating. Freezes well for up to 2 months; add the yoghurt fresh.
make_ahead: Even better the next day — make it ahead and reheat, stirring the spinach through as it warms.
leftovers: Thin leftovers with stock for a quick lentil soup, or fold into a wrap.

substitutions.swap: tinned lentils — rinse and add for the last 10 minutes
a good spoon of curry paste, mild or hot
coconut yoghurt to keep it dairy-free and vegan
pumpkin or butternut, cut the same size

steps.title: Dice sweet potato and onion
steps.content: Cube the sweet potato, dice the onion, mince garlic, rinse lentils.
steps.detail: Peel the sweet potato and cut it into 2cm chunks so it cooks evenly. Dice the onion and mince the garlic, keeping the garlic aside — it burns faster than onion. Rinse the red lentils in a sieve until the water runs clear.

steps.title: Soften the onion
steps.content: Cook the onion until soft, then add the garlic.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large deep pan over medium heat. Add the onion with a pinch of salt and cook for 5 minutes until soft and translucent. Stir in the garlic and cook 1 minute more until fragrant.
warnings: Garlic burns fast — stir it through and don't let it colour past pale gold or it turns bitter.

steps.title: Toast the curry powder
steps.content: Fry the curry powder to bloom the spice.
steps.detail: Add the curry powder and stir for about 1 minute until it smells toasty and fragrant. Cooking it in the oil now draws out far more flavour than stirring it into liquid later.

steps.title: Simmer sweet potato and lentils
steps.content: Add sweet potato, lentils and stock, then simmer covered.
steps.detail: Tip in the sweet potato and drained lentils, then pour over the vegetable stock. Bring to a simmer, cover and cook for 20 minutes, stirring now and then, until the lentils are soft and collapsing and the sweet potato is tender to a fork. Add a splash of water if it gets too thick.
tips: Give the base a stir every few minutes so the lentils don't catch and stick.

steps.title: Wilt the spinach
steps.content: Stir the spinach through until just wilted.
steps.detail: Stir the baby spinach through in handfuls, letting each wilt down before adding more — it takes only a minute or two. Taste and adjust with salt and pepper; lentils love salt, so it may need more than you expect.

steps.title: Spoon over yoghurt
steps.content: Serve hot with a cool spoon of yoghurt.
steps.detail: Serve the pan hot, straight to the table, with a spoon of cold yoghurt swirled over each bowl to offset the warm spice. It's good on its own or with rice or warm flatbread.

title: Pear Blue Cheese-ish Feta Salad
description: Sweet ripe pear, salty feta standing in for blue cheese, peppery rocket and toasted walnuts, dressed with honey and balsamic. A ten-minute salad that eats like a starter.
storage: Best assembled and eaten fresh — once dressed, the rocket wilts within the hour. Keep the components separate and undressed in the fridge for up to a day.
make_ahead: Toast the walnuts and whisk the dressing ahead; slice the pear and toss it just before serving so it doesn't brown.

substitutions.swap: actual blue cheese if you want the real thing, or a mild goat's cheese
pecans or toasted flaked almonds
crisp apple, or fresh figs when they're in season
baby spinach or a bag of mixed leaves

steps.title: Toast the walnuts
steps.content: Toast the walnuts in a dry pan until fragrant.
steps.detail: Toast the walnuts in a dry frying pan over medium heat, shaking often, for 3 to 4 minutes until they smell nutty and turn a shade darker. Tip them onto a board straight away so they don't scorch in the hot pan, then roughly chop.
tips: Nuts go from toasted to burnt in seconds — pull them the moment they smell nutty.

steps.title: Whisk the dressing
steps.content: Whisk honey, balsamic, oil and seasoning together.
steps.detail: In a small jar or bowl, whisk the honey and balsamic vinegar with the olive oil and a pinch of salt and pepper until it comes together into a glossy dressing. Taste it on a leaf — it should be sweet, sharp and balanced.
tips: Shake it in a lidded jar if whisking feels fiddly.

steps.title: Slice the pears
steps.content: Quarter, core and slice the pears into thin wedges.
steps.detail: Quarter and core the pears, then slice into thin wedges. Leave the skin on for colour and bite. Slice them just before serving, as pear browns quickly once cut — a little of the dressing over them slows it.

steps.title: Layer the salad
steps.content: Spread rocket, arrange pear, crumble feta, scatter walnuts.
steps.detail: Spread the rocket over a platter. Arrange the pear through it, then crumble the feta over in big rough chunks and scatter with the toasted walnuts. Building it in layers means every serve gets a bit of everything.

steps.title: Dress and serve
steps.content: Drizzle the dressing over just before eating.
steps.detail: Drizzle the honey-balsamic dressing over the top just before serving — don't toss it like a leafy salad, or the pear breaks up and the feta smears. A final crack of pepper and it's done.

title: Chicken Schnitzel Baguette
description: A crisp, golden chicken schnitzel still warm from the pan, packed into a baguette with crunchy lettuce, tomato and plenty of mayo. Better than any takeaway.
storage: Eat the schnitzel hot and crisp. Cooked schnitzels keep 2 days in the fridge; reheat in a hot oven, not the microwave, to bring back the crunch. Build the baguette fresh.
make_ahead: Crumb the schnitzels a few hours ahead and keep them on a tray in the fridge, then fry them to order.
leftovers: A cold schnitzel sliced over a salad, or back in a baguette the next day, is no hardship.

substitutions.swap: pork loin steaks for a pork schnitty, or crumbed eggplant slices for a veggie version
a soft white roll or Turkish bread
aioli, or mustard stirred through the mayo
panko for extra crunch

steps.title: Flatten the chicken
steps.content: Halve and pound the chicken to an even thickness.
steps.detail: Slice each chicken breast through the middle into two thin fillets. Lay them between two sheets of baking paper and pound with a mallet or rolling pin to an even 1cm — even thickness means even cooking and no raw middle.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash your hands, the board, the knife and the mallet well afterwards, and keep it away from the salad.

steps.title: Set up the crumbing station
steps.content: Line up three bowls: seasoned flour, egg, crumbs.
steps.detail: Line up three shallow bowls: plain flour seasoned with salt and pepper in the first, the eggs beaten in the second, and the breadcrumbs in the third. Keep one hand for wet and one for dry so you don't end up with battered fingers.

steps.title: Crumb the schnitzels
steps.content: Coat each fillet in flour, then egg, then crumbs.
steps.detail: Dip each fillet in the flour and shake off the excess, then into the egg to coat fully, then press firmly into the breadcrumbs so they stick all over. Set the crumbed schnitzels on a tray. Press well — a solid crust won't fall off in the pan.

steps.title: Fry until golden
steps.content: Shallow-fry 3-4 min each side until cooked to 74°C.
steps.detail: Heat a 5mm depth of olive oil in the frying pan over medium-high until a crumb dropped in sizzles briskly. Fry the schnitzels for 3 to 4 minutes each side until deep golden and cooked through — 74°C at the thickest point. Drain on paper towel.
warnings: Hot oil: lower the schnitzels in away from you and don't overcrowd the pan, or the oil can spit and drop in temperature.

steps.title: Slice the salad and bread
steps.content: Shred lettuce, slice tomato, split and spread the baguette.
steps.detail: While the schnitzels drain, shred the lettuce and slice the tomatoes. Split the baguette lengthways, not quite all the way through, and spread both cut sides generously with mayo.

steps.title: Fill the baguette
steps.content: Layer schnitzel and salad, press, cut and eat.
steps.detail: Lay a hot schnitzel into each baguette, breaking it to fit, then pile on the lettuce and tomato. Press the top down, cut in half and eat while the schnitzel's still crisp.

title: Chicken Wing Sriracha Pile
description: Oven-crisp wings tossed in a sticky sriracha-honey glaze, piled high with lime and spring onion and a cool sriracha mayo for dunking. Properly sticky-fingered.
