storage: Best hot and crisp from the oven. Keep 2 days in the fridge and re-crisp in a hot oven rather than the microwave. The sriracha mayo keeps 3 days covered.
make_ahead: Pat the wings dry and toss them with the baking powder and salt hours ahead — sitting uncovered in the fridge dries the skin for even more crunch.
leftovers: Strip cold leftover wings and toss the meat through a rice bowl or noodle salad.

substitutions.swap: chicken drumettes or nibbles
gochujang or any chilli-garlic sauce
maple syrup for the glaze
Greek yoghurt for a lighter dip

steps.title: Dry and season the wings
steps.content: Pat wings dry, toss with baking powder, salt and oil.
steps.detail: Pat the wings really dry with paper towel — dry skin is crisp skin. Toss them in a bowl with the baking powder, a good pinch of salt, a crack of pepper and the olive oil until evenly coated. The baking powder is the trick that crisps the skin like deep-frying.
tips: It's baking powder, not baking soda — don't swap them, or the wings taste soapy.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash your hands and the bowl well after handling, before you touch anything else.

steps.title: Roast until crisp
steps.content: Bake on a rack at 220°C for 40 minutes, turning once.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 220°C fan-forced. Sit the wings in a single layer on a wire rack over a tray so the fat drips away and air circles underneath. Roast for 40 minutes, turning once, until deep golden and crisp and cooked through — 74°C at the thickest point.
warnings: Don't open the oven in the first 20 minutes — you let out the heat that's crisping the skin.

steps.title: Make the sticky glaze
steps.content: Melt butter with sriracha, honey and lime.
steps.detail: While the wings roast, gently melt the butter in a small pan and stir in the sriracha, honey and a squeeze of lime. Warm it until smooth and glossy — don't let it boil hard, or the honey turns bitter.

steps.title: Mix the sriracha mayo
steps.content: Stir mayo with sriracha and a squeeze of lime.
steps.detail: Stir the mayo with a squirt of sriracha and a small squeeze of lime for a quick cooling dip. Taste and add more sriracha if you like it hotter.

steps.title: Toss and pile
steps.content: Coat the hot wings in glaze and pile them up.
steps.detail: Tip the hot wings into a big bowl, pour over the warm glaze and toss until every wing is sticky and glistening. Pile them onto a platter and scatter with sliced spring onion, with the sriracha mayo and lime wedges alongside.

title: Chicken Broccoli Pasta Bake
description: Tender chicken and green broccoli folded through a creamy cheese sauce and pasta, topped with buttery crumbs and baked until golden and bubbling. Family comfort food.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Reheat, covered, in a moderate oven until piping hot in the middle. Unbaked, it can be assembled and chilled for a day before baking.
make_ahead: Assemble the whole bake up to a day ahead, cover and refrigerate, then bake fresh — add 10 minutes if it's going in cold.
leftovers: A slice of cold pasta bake reheats brilliantly in a lunchbox, or fry squares of it for a crisp-edged breakfast.

substitutions.swap: leftover roast chicken, or diced ham, stirred through at the sauce stage
penne, macaroni or spirals — anything that holds sauce
tasty, mozzarella, or a mix of both
a simple white sauce of milk, butter and flour if you'd rather

steps.title: Boil pasta and broccoli
steps.content: Cook pasta, adding broccoli florets for the last 3 minutes.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan-forced. Bring a big pot of salted water to the boil and cook the pasta 2 minutes short of the packet time — it finishes in the oven. Cut the broccoli into small florets and drop them in for the final 3 minutes, then drain it all together.
tips: Slightly undercooked pasta is the secret to a bake that isn't mushy — it soaks up sauce as it bakes.

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.content: Sear the diced chicken, then add garlic.
steps.detail: Cut the chicken breast into 2cm pieces. Heat the olive oil in a large pan over medium-high and cook the chicken for 5 to 6 minutes until golden and just cooked through — 74°C, no pink. Stir in the garlic for the last minute.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash your hands, the board and the knife before touching anything else.

steps.title: Make the creamy sauce
steps.content: Pour in cream and melt in half the cheddar.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to low and pour the cream into the pan with the chicken. Stir in half the grated cheddar and let it melt into a smooth, glossy sauce, about 2 minutes. Season well with salt and pepper — the pasta and broccoli need it.

steps.title: Combine and fill the dish
steps.content: Fold pasta and broccoli through the sauce, then dish up.
steps.detail: Tip the drained pasta and broccoli into the pan and fold everything together until well coated. Spoon it into a baking dish and spread it level. If it looks a touch dry, a splash of milk or pasta water loosens it.

steps.title: Top with buttery crumbs
steps.content: Scatter crumbs and the rest of the cheese over the top.
steps.detail: Mix the breadcrumbs with the melted butter so they crisp rather than dry out, then scatter over the top with the last of the cheddar. This is what turns golden and crunchy in the oven.

steps.title: Bake until golden
steps.content: Bake 20 minutes until bubbling and crisp, then rest.
steps.detail: Bake for 20 minutes until the top is golden and crisp and the sauce bubbles at the edges. Let it sit for 5 minutes before serving so it holds together on the spoon.

title: Frozen Berry Smoothie Bowl
description: A thick, spoonable berry-and-banana smoothie blended almost like soft-serve, loaded with crunchy muesli, almonds and a drizzle of honey. Breakfast that feels like a treat.
storage: Best eaten straight away while thick and cold — it melts fast. If you must, freeze the blended base for up to a month and re-blend, though the texture is never quite the same.
make_ahead: Portion the frozen fruit into bags so a bowl is a two-minute job on a busy morning.

substitutions.swap: coconut yoghurt for a dairy-free, vegan bowl (skip the honey too)
any milk — dairy, oat, almond or coconut
granola or toasted oats
any nut or seed, or a spoon of peanut butter

steps.title: Soften fruit and chill bowls
steps.content: Rest the frozen fruit 5 minutes; chill the serving bowls.
steps.detail: Take the frozen berries and banana out and let them sit for 5 minutes to just soften at the edges — it saves your blender and gives a creamier blend. Meanwhile, pop the serving bowls in the freezer so they're icy cold when you plate up.

steps.title: Ready the toppings
steps.content: Roughly chop the almonds, have muesli and honey to hand.
steps.detail: Roughly chop the almonds so they scatter easily, and set out the muesli and honey. Once the smoothie is blended you'll want to plate fast, before it softens, so having everything ready pays off.

steps.title: Blend to soft-serve
steps.content: Blend fruit, yoghurt and milk thick and smooth.
steps.detail: Tip the fruit into the blender with the yoghurt and half the milk. Blend on high, stopping to scrape down and push the fruit onto the blades, until thick and smooth like soft-serve. Add the rest of the milk only a splash at a time — you want it spoonable, not pourable.
tips: A splash more milk if the blender won't move; a few more frozen berries if it's too loose.

steps.title: Bowl and top
steps.content: Spoon into cold bowls, then add the toppings.
steps.detail: Spoon the thick smoothie into the chilled bowls and smooth the tops. Scatter over the muesli and almonds, keeping them to one side so they stay crisp, and finish with a drizzle of honey. Eat straight away with a cold spoon.

title: Miso Butter Mushroom Noodles
description: Buttery seared mushrooms in a salty-sweet miso glaze, tossed through egg noodles with wilted spinach. A fast, deeply savoury bowl for a Tuesday.
storage: Best eaten fresh while the noodles are glossy. Leftovers keep 1 day in the fridge; the noodles drink up the sauce, so loosen with a splash of water in a hot pan to reheat.
leftovers: Any leftovers make a good cold noodle salad — loosen with a little extra soy and a squeeze of lime.

substitutions.swap: udon, thick wheat noodles, or even spaghetti at a pinch
any mushroom — button, Swiss brown, or a mix; oyster mushrooms are lovely here
for a dairy-free version, use extra olive oil or a plant butter

steps.title: Slice mushrooms and aromatics
steps.content: Slice mushrooms thick; separate spring onion whites and greens.
steps.detail: Wipe the mushrooms clean and slice them thickly — thick slices hold their bite and colour better than thin ones. Slice the spring onions, keeping the white parts for cooking apart from the green tops for scattering at the end. Mince the garlic. Loosen the miso in a small bowl with 2 tablespoons of warm water so it melts into the pan cleanly instead of clumping.
tips: Don't rinse mushrooms under the tap — they soak up water and steam instead of browning. A quick wipe with a damp cloth is all they need.

steps.title: Boil the egg noodles
steps.content: Cook noodles to just tender, then drain and toss with a little oil.
steps.detail: Bring a pot of water to the boil and salt it. Add the egg noodles and cook to the packet time — usually 3 to 4 minutes, until just tender with no raw centre. Drain in a colander, then toss with a few drops of oil so they don't clump while you finish the mushrooms. Save a mug of the cooking water.

steps.title: Sear the mushrooms golden
steps.content: Fry the mushrooms hard in a hot pan until deep golden.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a wide pan over medium-high until it shimmers. Tip in the mushrooms and spread them out — a crowded pan steams them grey, so use your widest pan or do two batches. Leave them alone for a couple of minutes to catch colour, then toss and repeat until they're deep golden and shrunk down, about 6 to 8 minutes.
tips: The mushrooms squeak and release liquid first; keep the heat up and wait — the browning only starts once that moisture cooks off.

steps.title: Wilt garlic and spinach
steps.content: Add garlic and spring onion whites, then the spinach.
steps.detail: Turn the heat down to medium. Add the garlic and spring onion whites and stir for about a minute, just until fragrant — don't let the garlic brown or it turns bitter. Pile in the baby spinach and toss; it collapses to almost nothing in under a minute.
warnings: Garlic scorches in seconds on a hot pan and turns acrid — keep it moving and pull the heat back if it colours too fast.

steps.title: Melt in the miso butter
steps.content: Stir through butter, loosened miso and soy until glossy.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to low. Add the butter, the loosened miso and the soy sauce, and stir so the butter melts into a glossy, savoury glaze that coats everything. Splash in a little of the reserved noodle water if it looks thick or greasy — it pulls the sauce together. Taste before adding any salt; miso and soy are already salty.

steps.title: Toss through the noodles
steps.content: Fold in the noodles and finish with spring onion greens.
steps.detail: Add the drained noodles and toss with tongs until every strand is coated and glossy, loosening with more noodle water if needed. Scatter over the reserved spring onion greens and a crack of pepper, and serve straight away while hot.

title: Cauliflower Cashew Korma Bowl
description: A mild, creamy korma built on coconut milk and toasted cashews, with cauliflower simmered until tender and spooned over fluffy basmati. Gentle enough for the whole table.
equipment: large deep frying pan or pot
storage: The curry keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge and reheats well with a splash of water. Cooked rice is best eaten the day it's made — cool it quickly, refrigerate within an hour, keep no longer than a day, and reheat until piping hot.
make_ahead: The korma sauce deepens overnight — make it a day ahead and reheat gently while fresh rice steams.
leftovers: Spoon leftover korma into a wrap with a little yoghurt for lunch, or thin it with stock into a quick soup.

substitutions.swap: swap in potato, pumpkin or a tin of chickpeas — anything that takes a simmer
almonds or blanched peanuts toast up just as well
thickened cream works, or evaporated milk for something lighter
a couple of spoons of red or green curry paste if that's what you have

steps.title: Rinse and cook the basmati
steps.content: Rinse the rice, then simmer covered until fluffy.
steps.detail: Rinse the basmati in a sieve until the water runs almost clear — this washes off surface starch and keeps the grains separate. Tip into a saucepan with 2 1/4 cups of water and a pinch of salt, bring to the boil, then drop to the lowest heat, cover, and cook 12 minutes. Take it off the heat and leave the lid on for another 10 minutes to steam, then fluff.
tips: Resist lifting the lid while it cooks — the trapped steam is what cooks the grains through evenly.

steps.title: Cut cauliflower and aromatics
steps.content: Cut cauliflower into florets; dice onion, grate garlic and ginger.
steps.detail: Break or cut the cauliflower into bite-sized florets. Dice the onion small, and grate the garlic and ginger so they melt into the sauce. Roughly chop the cashews. If you have a minute, toast the cashews in a dry pan over medium heat until they smell nutty and turn pale gold, then set them aside for the topping.

steps.title: Fry onion and spices
steps.content: Soften the onion, then toast the spices and aromatics.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a deep pan over medium and cook the onion for about 5 minutes until soft and translucent. Stir in the grated garlic and ginger for a minute until fragrant, then add the curry powder and stir for 30 seconds — toasting it in the oil wakes up the spice and takes away any raw, dusty taste. It should smell warm and fragrant.
warnings: Ground spices catch and burn fast once they hit hot oil — keep them moving and add the coconut milk promptly if the pan looks dry.

steps.title: Simmer the cauliflower
steps.content: Add cauliflower and coconut milk; simmer until fork-tender.
steps.detail: Tip in the cauliflower and pour over the coconut milk, adding a splash of water if it doesn't quite cover. Bring to a gentle simmer, then cover and cook about 15 minutes, stirring now and then, until the cauliflower is fork-tender and the sauce has thickened to a creamy gravy. Stir a small handful of the cashews through now if you'd like a thicker, nuttier sauce.

steps.title: Season and build the bowls
steps.content: Salt to taste, spoon over rice, top with cashews and coriander.
steps.detail: Taste and add salt until the flavours lift — coconut curries need a bit more than you'd think. Spoon the fluffed basmati into bowls, ladle the korma over, and finish with the toasted cashews and a scatter of torn coriander.

title: Roasted Capsicum Tomato Soup
description: Red capsicums roasted until sweet and smoky, blended with tinned tomato and a swirl of cream. Silky, warming, and even better with cheese toast on the side.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge or freezes for 3 months — ideally freeze the base before you add the cream. Reheat gently and don't let it boil hard once the cream is in, or it can split.
make_ahead: The blended base, before the cream and basil, freezes beautifully — thaw, warm through, then stir in cream and herbs to finish.
leftovers: A mugful makes a great pasta sauce — reduce it down a little and toss through cooked penne.

substitutions.swap: coconut milk for a dairy-free version, or whole milk for something lighter
chicken stock if you're not keeping it vegetarian
a spoon of pesto stirred in at the end if you've no fresh basil

steps.title: Roast the capsicums
steps.content: Roast capsicum until the skins blister and blacken.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Halve the capsicums, pull out the seeds and white ribs, and lay them skin-side up on an oven tray with a drizzle of oil. Roast about 25 minutes, until the skins are blistered and charred in patches and the flesh has collapsed. Tip them into a bowl and cover with a plate for 10 minutes — the trapped steam loosens the skins.
tips: The blacker the skin, the sweeter and smokier the soup — don't be shy about letting them char.

steps.title: Soften onion and garlic
steps.content: Cook onion until soft, then add garlic.
steps.detail: While the capsicums roast, heat a little oil in a pot over medium. Add the diced onion and cook 5 to 6 minutes until soft and translucent, then stir in the garlic for another minute until fragrant. You want them sweet and soft, not browned.

steps.title: Peel and add the capsicum
steps.content: Slip off the charred skins and add the flesh to the pot.
steps.detail: The cooled skins should peel away easily with your fingers — don't worry about a few stubborn flecks, they add character. Tear the soft flesh into the pot and stir it through the onion and garlic for a minute.

steps.title: Simmer with tomato and stock
steps.content: Add tinned tomato and stock; simmer to meld.
steps.detail: Pour in the tinned tomatoes, crushing them with your spoon, and the vegetable stock. Bring to a simmer and cook 15 minutes with the lid off, letting the flavours meld and the liquid reduce a little. Season with salt and pepper.

steps.title: Blend, then swirl in cream
steps.content: Blend smooth, then stir through cream and basil off the boil.
steps.detail: Take the pot off the heat and blend until completely silky — a stick blender in the pot is easiest, or work in batches in a jug blender. Return to low heat, stir in the cream and most of the torn basil, and warm through without boiling. Taste and adjust the salt and pepper, and serve with the last of the basil torn over.
warnings: Hot soup can erupt when blended — if using a jug blender, fill it half full at most, hold the lid down with a folded cloth, and start on low.

title: Lamb Sumac Couscous Bowl
description: Spiced lamb mince crisped in the pan, piled on lemony sumac couscous with a fresh tomato and cucumber salad and a cool dollop of yoghurt. A fast Middle Eastern dinner bowl.
storage: Keep the components separately in the fridge for up to 3 days — the couscous and lamb reheat well, and the salad and yoghurt stay fresh. Assemble just before eating.
make_ahead: Brown the lamb and steam the couscous ahead; cut the salad and warm the lamb through when you're ready to serve.
leftovers: Stuff leftover lamb and salad into a warmed wrap with the yoghurt for a next-day lunch.

substitutions.swap: beef mince works, or a mix of the two
quinoa, or fluffy rice if you'd rather
no sumac? use the zest of a lemon and an extra squeeze of juice for the same tang

steps.title: Steam the couscous
steps.content: Pour boiling water over the couscous and leave it to swell.
steps.detail: Put the couscous in a heatproof bowl with a pinch of salt and a teaspoon of oil. Pour over the same volume of boiling water, about 1 1/2 cups, cover tightly with a plate or wrap, and leave for 5 minutes until the water is absorbed. Rake through with a fork to fluff and separate the grains, then stir in half the sumac.
tips: A fork, not a spoon — raking rather than stirring keeps the couscous light and fluffy instead of pasty.

steps.title: Chop the salad
steps.content: Dice cucumber and tomato and dress with sumac.
steps.detail: Dice the cucumber and tomatoes into small, even pieces and toss them in a bowl with a drizzle of oil, a pinch of salt and a good dusting of some of the remaining sumac. The sumac brings a lemony tang that stands in for a squeeze of lemon.

steps.title: Brown the lamb
steps.content: Fry the lamb mince hard until deeply browned and crisp.
steps.detail: Heat a splash of oil in a frying pan over medium-high. Add the lamb mince and press it flat, then leave it to sit for 2 minutes to catch colour before breaking it up. Keep cooking 6 to 8 minutes until no pink remains and the edges are crisp and browned — those crunchy bits are the best part. Tip off any excess fat if there's a lot.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and anything the raw lamb touched before it goes near the salad or serving bowls.

steps.title: Spice the lamb
steps.content: Stir in garlic, cumin and sumac until fragrant.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to medium. Stir in the garlic, cumin and a good pinch of the remaining sumac, and cook for a minute or two until fragrant and the spices coat the meat. Season with salt and pepper and taste — it should be savoury, warm and just a little tangy.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Layer couscous, lamb and salad, and finish with yoghurt.
steps.detail: Spoon the sumac couscous into bowls and top with the spiced lamb and the tomato and cucumber salad. Add a generous dollop of yoghurt and a final pinch of sumac over the top for colour and tang.

title: Tomato Egg Shakshuka Rice
description: Eggs gently poached in a smoky, cumin-spiced tomato and capsicum sauce, all in one pan. Scoop it up with charred bread for a big, lazy brunch.
equipment: large deep frying pan with a lid
storage: The tomato base keeps 3 days in the fridge, but shakshuka is best cooked fresh — the eggs don't reheat well. If you have sauce left, refrigerate it and poach fresh eggs when you want more.
make_ahead: Make the spiced tomato and capsicum base up to 2 days ahead; reheat it in the pan and poach the eggs fresh just before serving.

substitutions.swap: red or green, whatever you have; a jar of roasted capsicum works in a pinch
a spoon of curry powder, or a pinch of chilli powder alongside it for more warmth
any crusty bread, or warm flatbread to scoop

steps.title: Dice onion and capsicum
steps.content: Dice the onion and capsicum; mince the garlic.
steps.detail: Dice the onion and the capsicum into small, even pieces so they soften at the same rate. Mince the garlic and keep it separate — it goes in later so it doesn't burn. Crack the four eggs into cups or ramekins, ready to slide in gently later.

steps.title: Soften onion and capsicum
steps.content: Cook onion and capsicum until soft and sweet.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a wide, deep pan over medium. Add the onion and capsicum with a pinch of salt and cook, stirring now and then, for about 8 minutes until soft and sweet and just starting to colour at the edges.

steps.title: Toast the spices
steps.content: Add garlic, cumin, paprika and chilli until fragrant.
steps.detail: Push the vegetables to one side and add the garlic, cumin, smoked paprika and a pinch of chilli flakes if you like heat. Stir for about a minute until everything smells fragrant and toasty — this blooms the spices and takes away any raw edge.
warnings: Garlic and ground spices scorch quickly — keep them moving and add the tomatoes before they can catch.

steps.title: Simmer the tomato sauce
steps.content: Add tomatoes and simmer to a thick, jammy sauce.
steps.detail: Pour in the tinned tomatoes, crushing any whole ones against the pan, and add a splash of water. Season with salt and pepper and simmer, uncovered, for 10 to 12 minutes until thick and jammy — a spoon dragged across the base should leave a trail that fills in slowly.

steps.title: Poach the eggs
steps.content: Make wells, slide in the eggs, cover and cook until set.
steps.detail: Make four shallow wells in the sauce with the back of a spoon and slide an egg into each. Cover the pan and cook gently for 6 to 8 minutes, until the whites are just set but the yolks still wobble — they keep cooking off the heat.
tips: For runny yolks, pull the pan off the heat the moment the whites turn opaque; the residual heat sets them the rest of the way.

steps.title: Char the bread and serve
steps.content: Toast the bread and bring the pan to the table.
steps.detail: Toast or grill the bread until crisp and golden, and serve it alongside the pan for scooping up sauce and yolk. A crack of pepper over the eggs finishes it. It's just as good spooned over steamed rice if you'd rather something more filling.

title: Calamari Lime Cabbage Salad
description: Flash-fried calamari, tender and just curled, piled on a crunchy lime and chilli cabbage slaw with a creamy lime mayo. Fresh, fast and a little bit fancy.
storage: Best eaten straight away — dressed slaw wilts and cooked calamari toughens as it sits. Keep any undressed slaw and the mayo separately in the fridge for a day; don't dress until serving.
make_ahead: Shred the slaw and mix the lime mayo a few hours ahead and keep them chilled and separate — fry the calamari at the last minute.

substitutions.swap: prawns or firm white fish cook the same quick way
Greek yoghurt for a lighter, tangier dressing
wombok or a bag of coleslaw mix saves the shredding
lemon works just as well

steps.title: Shred the slaw
steps.content: Finely shred cabbage; slice chilli, spring onion and coriander.
steps.detail: Shred the cabbage as finely as you can — thin ribbons soak up dressing and stay crunchy. Thinly slice the spring onions and the chilli, scraping out the chilli seeds first for less heat, and roughly chop the coriander, stalks and all. Toss it all together in a big bowl.
tips: Sprinkle the shredded cabbage with a small pinch of salt and scrunch it with your hands — it softens the raw edge and makes it more tender.

steps.title: Mix the lime mayo
steps.content: Stir mayo with lime zest and juice into a loose dressing.
steps.detail: Zest the lime into the mayo, then squeeze in half its juice and a pinch of salt. Stir to a smooth, pourable dressing — loosen with a teaspoon of water if it's too thick. Taste and add more lime if you want it sharper. Save the other half of the lime for wedges.

steps.title: Dust the calamari
steps.content: Pat the calamari dry and toss in seasoned cornflour.
steps.detail: Pat the calamari really dry with paper towel — any water makes hot oil spit. If using tubes, slice them into rings or open them out and lightly score a criss-cross so they curl. Toss the pieces in the cornflour seasoned with salt and pepper, shaking off the excess, for a thin, crisp coat.
warnings: Raw seafood: wash your hands, the board and the knife well before they touch the slaw, and dry the calamari thoroughly so the oil doesn't spit.

steps.title: Sear it hot and fast
steps.content: Fry the calamari 1 to 2 minutes in a screaming-hot pan.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in a pan until it's very hot and shimmering. Add the calamari in a single layer — don't crowd it — and cook just 1 to 2 minutes, turning once, until opaque and lightly golden with curled edges. The second it turns from glassy to white, it's done.
warnings: Calamari toughens to rubber in moments if overcooked — pull it out early rather than late; it keeps cooking on the plate.

steps.title: Dress and plate
steps.content: Toss slaw with dressing, top with calamari, add lime wedges.
steps.detail: Toss the slaw with about half the lime mayo — just enough to coat, not drown. Pile it onto plates, top with the hot calamari, and drizzle over the rest of the dressing. Scatter with a little extra coriander and serve with the lime wedges to squeeze over.

title: Pork Belly Pickle-Style Baguette
description: Crisp, sticky pork belly and a tangle of quick-pickled carrot packed into a crusty roll with cucumber, coriander and a swipe of mayo. A banh-mi-inspired lunch worth the mess.
storage: The pickled carrot keeps a week in the fridge and only gets better. Cooked pork keeps 3 days. Build the baguettes to order, though — assembled, they go soft within the hour.
make_ahead: Make the pickled carrot up to a week ahead. Crisp the pork just before building so it stays crunchy.
leftovers: Leftover pork and pickle are brilliant over rice or tossed through noodles the next day.

substitutions.swap: pork shoulder, or chicken thigh sliced thin
any crusty roll or a length of soft baguette
white or cider vinegar works for the pickle

steps.title: Quick-pickle the carrot
steps.content: Toss shredded carrot with vinegar, sugar and salt to pickle.
steps.detail: Peel and coarsely grate or julienne the carrots into a bowl. Add the rice vinegar, sugar and a good pinch of salt, and scrunch it together with your hands. Leave it to sit while you cook — 15 minutes is enough to turn it tangy and slightly softened, and it only improves the longer it sits.
tips: A splash of just-boiled water over the carrot speeds the pickling and takes the raw bite off.

steps.title: Prep cucumber and herbs
steps.content: Cut cucumber into batons and pick the coriander.
steps.detail: Slice the cucumber into long batons or thin ribbons. Pick the coriander into sprigs, stalks and all — the stalks carry loads of flavour. Set both aside ready for building.

steps.title: Crisp the pork belly
steps.content: Fry thin slices of pork belly until golden and crisp.
steps.detail: Slice the pork belly into thin strips, about 5mm, and season with salt and pepper. Lay them in a cold pan with just a touch of oil, then bring up to a medium-high heat — starting cold helps the fat render out. Fry 8 to 10 minutes, turning, until deeply golden and crisp at the edges and cooked all the way through.
warnings: Pork must be cooked through with no pink; wash hands and board after handling it raw.
Rendering pork fat spits — keep the heat steady and stand back as you turn the slices.

steps.title: Glaze with soy
steps.content: Add soy and a little sugar; toss until sticky.
steps.detail: Tip out most of the rendered fat, then add the soy sauce and a pinch of sugar to the pan. Toss the pork for a minute or two until the liquid bubbles down to a sticky, glossy glaze that clings to each piece. Take it off the heat before the sugar can catch and burn.

steps.title: Warm and spread the rolls
steps.content: Split and warm the rolls, then spread with mayo.
steps.detail: Split the rolls lengthways without cutting all the way through, and warm them in the oven or a dry pan until crisp outside and soft within. Spread the cut sides generously with mayo.

steps.title: Build and press
steps.content: Layer pork, pickled carrot, cucumber and coriander; press shut.
steps.detail: Lay the sticky pork along each roll, pile on the drained pickled carrot, and tuck in the cucumber batons and a good handful of coriander. Press the lid down firmly so it holds together, and eat while the pork is still hot and crisp.

title: Lemon Pepper Fish and Chips
description: Barramundi in a crunchy lemon-pepper crumb with proper oven chips and a zesty lemon mayo. All the pub favourite, baked not battered, on one weeknight.
storage: Best eaten hot and crisp. Fish keeps 1 to 2 days in the fridge; reheat the fish and chips in a hot oven, not the microwave, or they'll go soft.
make_ahead: Crumb the fish and cut the chips a few hours ahead, keeping both covered in the fridge, so it's just a matter of baking at dinnertime.
leftovers: Flake cold leftover fish into a lettuce wrap or a soft roll with the lemon mayo for lunch.

substitutions.swap: any firm white fish — flathead, snapper or basa; salmon also crumbs well
sweet potato for sweeter chips, though they'll be softer
panko for extra crunch
shop tartare sauce, or stir capers through the lemon mayo

steps.title: Cut and parboil the chips
steps.content: Cut potatoes into chips and parboil to fluff the edges.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Cut the potatoes, peeled or not, into chips about 1.5cm thick. Drop them into a pot of salted water, bring to the boil and cook 5 to 6 minutes until the outsides just soften — this is the secret to chips that go crisp outside and fluffy within. Drain well and let the steam die down.
tips: Give the drained chips a gentle shake in the colander to rough up the edges — more surface means more crunch.

steps.title: Roast the chips
steps.content: Toss chips in oil and roast until golden, turning once.
steps.detail: Tip the chips onto an oven tray, drizzle with the oil and a good pinch of salt, and toss to coat. Spread them out in a single layer so they roast rather than steam, and bake about 30 minutes, turning once halfway, until deep golden and crisp.

steps.title: Set up the crumb
steps.content: Line up seasoned flour, beaten egg and lemon-pepper crumb.
steps.detail: Set out three shallow bowls: plain flour with a pinch of salt in the first, the eggs beaten in the second, and the breadcrumbs mixed with the zest of the lemon, plenty of black pepper and a pinch of salt in the third. That lemon-pepper crumb is the whole point — be generous with the pepper.

steps.title: Crumb the fish
steps.content: Coat each fillet in flour, then egg, then crumb.
steps.detail: Pat the barramundi fillets dry. Dip each one first in the flour and shake off the excess, then through the egg, then press firmly into the crumb so it sticks all over. Set the crumbed fillets on a lined tray.
warnings: Raw fish: wash your hands, the board and the bowls after coating, and keep the fish away from the chips and salad until it's cooked.

steps.title: Bake the fish
steps.content: Drizzle with oil and bake until golden and flaking.
steps.detail: When the chips have had their first turn, slide the fish tray into the oven too. Drizzle the fillets with a little oil and bake 12 to 15 minutes, until the crumb is golden and the fish flakes easily when pressed with a fork. Thin fillets cook faster, so check early.

steps.title: Mix lemon mayo and serve
steps.content: Stir lemon into mayo and serve with the chips.
steps.detail: Stir a squeeze of lemon juice into the mayo for a quick zesty sauce. Serve the crumbed fish and hot chips together with the lemon mayo and the remaining lemon cut into wedges for squeezing over.

title: Tuna Tomato Pasta Bake
description: Store-cupboard hero: pasta in a garlicky tomato and tuna sauce under a molten cheddar crust with a crunchy breadcrumb top. The kind of dinner you can build from tins.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge and reheats well, covered, until piping hot in the middle. It also freezes for up to 3 months — thaw overnight before reheating.
make_ahead: Assemble the whole bake, cover and refrigerate a day ahead; add 10 minutes to the baking time if it's going in fridge-cold.
leftovers: A slab of cold pasta bake is a solid lunchbox filler, or reheat a portion for a fast next-day dinner.

substitutions.swap: tinned salmon, or leave it out for a plain tomato and cheese bake
mozzarella for stretch, or tasty for a sharper hit
any short shape — penne, spirals or macaroni
panko or crushed crackers for the crunchy top

steps.title: Boil the pasta al dente
steps.content: Cook pasta 2 minutes short of the packet time and drain.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Bring a big pot of salted water to the boil and cook the pasta 2 minutes less than the packet says — it needs to stay firm because it keeps cooking in the oven. Drain it, saving a mug of the cooking water.

steps.title: Soften onion and garlic
steps.content: Cook onion until soft, then stir in garlic.
steps.detail: While the pasta cooks, heat the oil in a pan over medium and cook the diced onion for about 5 minutes until soft. Stir in the garlic for another minute until fragrant, without letting it brown.

steps.title: Simmer tomato and tuna
steps.content: Add tomatoes, simmer, then flake through the tuna.
steps.detail: Pour in the tinned tomatoes, crushing them against the pan, season with salt and pepper, and simmer 8 to 10 minutes until slightly thickened. Drain the tuna and flake it through, along with most of the chopped parsley. Taste and adjust the seasoning.

steps.title: Combine and fill the dish
steps.content: Toss pasta through the sauce and tip into a baking dish.
steps.detail: Tip the drained pasta into the sauce and stir until every piece is coated, loosening with a splash of the reserved pasta water if it looks dry — it should be a touch saucier than you want, as the bake dries it out. Spread it into a baking dish.

steps.title: Top with cheese and crumb
steps.content: Scatter cheddar and breadcrumbs over the top.
steps.detail: Scatter the grated cheddar evenly over the pasta, then the breadcrumbs, and drizzle the crumbs with a little oil so they crisp and gold in the oven. Finish with a crack of pepper over the top.

steps.title: Bake until golden
steps.content: Bake until bubbling at the edges and golden on top.
steps.detail: Bake 20 to 25 minutes, until the sauce bubbles up at the edges and the top is crisp and golden. Let it settle for 5 minutes before serving so it holds together on the spoon, and scatter with the last of the parsley.
warnings: The dish and sauce will be very hot straight from the oven — use a dry cloth or oven gloves and let it rest before digging in.

title: Mango Lime Smoothie Bowl
description: Frozen banana and ripe mango blended thick as soft-serve, sharpened with lime and finished with crunchy muesli and coconut. A tropical breakfast you eat with a spoon.
storage: Smoothie bowls are best the moment they're blended — they melt and separate as they sit. If you have extra, freeze it in a tub and let it soften for a few minutes before scooping.
make_ahead: Cube and freeze extra mango when it's ripe and cheap, so you always have a bag ready for the blender.

substitutions.swap: frozen mango straight from the bag, or ripe peach
frozen mixed berries for a pink bowl; you may need a touch more liquid
coconut yoghurt and a plant milk for a dairy-free bowl
granola, or a handful of toasted oats and nuts

steps.title: Cube mango, juice lime
steps.content: Cube the mango, zest and juice the lime.
steps.detail: Cut the cheeks from the mango, score the flesh into a grid and scoop it out, setting aside a few cubes for the top. Zest a little of the lime and squeeze out its juice. Have the frozen banana measured and ready — working quickly keeps everything cold and thick.

steps.title: Blend it thick
steps.content: Blend banana, mango, yoghurt and lime to soft-serve thickness.
steps.detail: Put the frozen banana, most of the mango, the yoghurt and the lime juice in the blender with just a splash of the milk. Blend in bursts, stopping to scrape down the sides, until thick and smooth like soft-serve. Add milk only a spoonful at a time if the blades stall — too much and it turns to a drink.
tips: If your blender struggles, let the frozen banana sit out for 2 to 3 minutes first, or push the mixture onto the blades with a spatula between bursts, with the blender off.

steps.title: Dial in the texture
steps.content: Aim for a thick scoop that holds a spoon-trail.
steps.detail: It should be thick enough to hold the mark of a spoon dragged across the top — that's what lets the toppings sit rather than sink. Too runny, add a few more frozen banana chunks and blend again; too stiff to move, add another small splash of milk. Taste and add a squeeze more lime if you want it sharper.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.content: Scoop into bowls and add muesli, coconut and mango.
steps.detail: Scoop into cold bowls and smooth the top. Arrange the muesli, a scatter of desiccated coconut, the reserved mango cubes and a little lime zest in neat lines or clusters. Eat straight away, before it starts to melt.

title: Peanut Lime Chicken Noodles
description: Silky rice noodles tangled through seared chicken thigh and a quick peanut-lime sauce, cooled down with crunchy cucumber and a fistful of coriander.
storage: Best eaten fresh while the noodles are still slippery. Leftovers keep 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge; the noodles firm up cold, so loosen with a splash of water and a squeeze of lime before eating.
make_ahead: Whisk the peanut-lime sauce up to 2 days ahead and keep it in a jar in the fridge — bring it back to a pourable consistency with a little warm water.
leftovers: Great cold the next day as a noodle salad — toss through extra cucumber and herbs straight from the fridge.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast, sliced thin — cook it a minute less so it stays juicy
egg noodles, or even spaghetti at a pinch; cook to the packet and drain
a splash of sesame oil stirred in at the end gives a nuttier, more authentic finish

steps.title: Prep chicken and garnishes
steps.content: Slice the chicken, ribbon the cucumber, chop coriander and juice the lime.
steps.detail: Slice the chicken thigh into thin, bite-sized strips and pat them dry so they sear rather than steam. Halve the cucumber lengthwise, scrape out the watery seeds with a spoon, then slice into thin half-moons. Roughly chop the coriander stalks and all, grate or finely mince the garlic, and roll the lime firmly on the bench before halving and juicing it. Keep the raw chicken well away from the cut cucumber and herbs.
warnings: Keep the raw chicken and its board away from the cucumber and coriander, and wash your hands and knife before touching anything you'll eat raw.

steps.title: Soak the rice noodles
steps.content: Cover the rice noodles in just-boiled water until soft and pliable.
steps.detail: Sit the rice noodles in a large heatproof bowl and pour over enough just-boiled water to cover them completely. Leave them for 6-8 minutes, teasing them apart with tongs halfway, until they're floppy and tender with no chalky core. Drain in a colander and rinse briefly under cold water so they stop cooking and don't clump. A drizzle of the olive oil through the drained noodles keeps them loose while you cook the chicken.
tips: Rice noodles vary — check a strand at 5 minutes; it should bend easily with no hard white centre.

steps.title: Whisk the peanut-lime sauce
steps.content: Stir peanut butter, soy, honey and lime into a pourable sauce.
steps.detail: In a small bowl, whisk the peanut butter with the soy sauce, honey and most of the lime juice. It'll seize up and look claggy at first — add warm water a tablespoon at a time, whisking, until it loosens into a smooth sauce about the thickness of pouring cream. Taste it: it should be nutty, salty, sweet and sharp all at once, so adjust with more lime or honey to balance.
tips: Hold back a little lime juice to squeeze over at the very end — a fresh hit right before serving wakes the whole bowl up.

steps.title: Sear the chicken
steps.content: Fry the chicken in a hot pan until golden, adding garlic at the end.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large frying pan or wok over medium-high until it shimmers. Spread the chicken out in a single layer, season with salt and pepper, and leave it undisturbed for 2 minutes to catch some colour before tossing. Cook for 5-6 minutes in total until golden at the edges and cooked through with no pink in the middle, then add the garlic for the last 30 seconds — just until fragrant, not browned.
tips: Don't crowd the pan; if it looks packed, sear the chicken in two batches so it browns instead of stewing.
warnings: Cook the chicken right through — cut a thick strip to check it's white all the way, not pink.

steps.title: Toss it all together
steps.content: Add the noodles and sauce to the pan and toss over low heat.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to low. Tip the drained noodles into the pan with the chicken, pour over the peanut-lime sauce, and toss everything with tongs until the noodles are glossy and coated and warmed through, about a minute. If it looks tight or sticky, loosen with a splash of warm water — the sauce should cling to the noodles, not clump in the base.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.content: Pile into bowls and finish with cucumber, coriander and lime.
steps.detail: Twirl the noodles into bowls with tongs, lifting them high so they fall in loose tangles. Scatter over the cool cucumber half-moons and plenty of chopped coriander, then squeeze the reserved lime across the top. Serve straight away, while the noodles are still slippery and warm.

title: Pork Shoulder Coconut Curry
description: A gentle Thai-style red curry — pork shoulder braised low and slow in coconut milk until it falls apart, warmed with ginger and brightened with a squeeze of lime over jasmine rice.
equipment: large heavy pot or casserole with lid
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge and freezes well for up to 3 months — the flavour deepens overnight. Cool cooked rice quickly and eat within a day. Reheat the curry until piping hot.
make_ahead: This curry is even better the next day, so it's a good one to make ahead — cool, chill, and reheat gently, loosening with a splash of water if it's thickened.
leftovers: Shred any leftover pork and pile it into wraps or toasted rolls with a little of the sauce and some fresh coriander.

substitutions.swap: boneless chicken thigh — braise for just 35-40 minutes instead of the full time
green curry paste for a fresher, hotter version
basmati rice, or warm flatbread to mop up the sauce

steps.title: Cube the pork
steps.content: Trim the pork shoulder into large chunks and grate the ginger.
steps.detail: Cut the pork shoulder into large 3-4 cm chunks, trimming off any thick pads of fat but leaving some — the fat and connective tissue are what melt into silkiness over a long braise. Pat the pieces dry with paper towel and season with a pinch of salt so they'll brown. Peel and finely grate the ginger. Wash your hands and board well after handling the raw pork.
warnings: Wash your hands, knife and board thoroughly after cutting the raw pork, before touching anything else.

steps.title: Brown the pork
steps.content: Sear the pork in batches until deeply coloured, then set aside.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large heavy pot over medium-high until shimmering. Brown the pork in two or three batches — don't crowd it — leaving each piece to colour for a couple of minutes before turning, until deep golden-brown all over. Lift the browned pieces onto a plate as you go. That colour is the backbone of the curry's flavour, so take your time and don't rush it out pale.
tips: A crowded pot steams the meat grey instead of browning it — give the pieces room and do it in batches.
warnings: The oil is hot enough to spit as the pork goes in — lay the pieces down away from you.

steps.title: Fry the curry paste
steps.content: Cook the red curry paste and ginger until fragrant.
steps.detail: Turn the heat to medium. Spoon the red curry paste and the grated ginger into the oily pot and fry, stirring constantly, for about a minute until it darkens a shade and smells intensely fragrant. Scrape up any browned bits stuck to the base — that's pure flavour. If it looks like catching, pull the pot off the heat for a moment; the paste burns quickly.
tips: Frying the paste in oil before adding liquid blooms the spices and makes the whole curry taste rounder and deeper.

steps.title: Braise in coconut
steps.content: Return the pork, pour in coconut milk, and simmer low and slow.
steps.detail: Return the pork and any resting juices to the pot, pour in the coconut milk, and add a splash of water so the meat is just about covered. Bring to a gentle simmer, then drop the heat right down, part-cover, and leave it to blip away softly for about 90 minutes, stirring now and then. It's done when the pork is completely tender and pulls apart at the nudge of a spoon, and the sauce has thickened to a glossy gravy.
tips: If the sauce reduces too far before the pork is tender, top up with a little water; if it's still thin at the end, simmer uncovered for the last 10 minutes.

steps.title: Cook the jasmine rice
steps.content: Steam the rice by absorption while the curry finishes.
steps.detail: About 20 minutes before the curry's ready, rinse the jasmine rice in a few changes of cold water until it runs clear, then tip it into a saucepan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, stir once, then clamp on the lid and turn the heat to its lowest setting for 12 minutes. Take it off the heat and leave it, lid on, for another 10 minutes, then fluff with a fork.
tips: Don't lift the lid while it steams — the trapped steam is what cooks the rice evenly.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.content: Balance with fish sauce, sugar and lime, then spoon over rice.
steps.detail: Off the heat, stir the fish sauce and brown sugar through the curry — the fish sauce brings a savoury depth in place of plain salt, and the sugar stands in for the palm sugar of a proper Thai curry, rounding off the heat. Squeeze in half the lime and taste: it should be rich, a little sweet, salty and just tangy. Spoon over bowls of jasmine rice, with the remaining lime cut into wedges.

title: Lentil Pumpkin Coconut Soup
description: A velvety, gently spiced soup — sweet pumpkin and red lentils simmered soft and blended smooth, mellowed with coconut milk and scattered with fresh coriander.
storage: Keeps 3-4 days in an airtight container in the fridge and freezes beautifully for up to 3 months. Reheat gently, adding a splash of water if it's thickened on standing.
make_ahead: Made a day ahead it only gets better — the spices settle and deepen. Reheat gently and add the coconut swirl and coriander fresh.
leftovers: Thin any leftovers with a little stock for a lighter soup, or stir through cooked rice to turn it into a thick dhal.

substitutions.swap: sweet potato works just as well — same weight, peeled and cubed
2 tablespoons of red curry paste for a Thai lean
parsley, if coriander isn't your thing

steps.title: Prep pumpkin and aromatics
steps.content: Peel and cube the pumpkin; dice the onion and mince the garlic.
steps.detail: Peel the pumpkin and cut it into rough 2-3 cm cubes — the smaller they are, the faster they'll cook. Dice the onion and finely mince or grate the garlic, keeping the garlic separate since it burns faster than the onion. Rinse the red lentils in a sieve under cold water until the water runs clear, which stops the soup turning gluey.
tips: Pumpkin skin is tough — steady it on a flat cut side before slicing so the knife doesn't slip.

steps.title: Soften the onion
steps.content: Cook the onion in oil, then add garlic and curry powder.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a large pot over medium heat and cook the diced onion for 5-6 minutes, stirring now and then, until soft and translucent but not browned. Stir in the garlic and the curry powder and cook for another minute until fragrant — you'll smell the spices bloom. If the pot looks dry or the spices threaten to catch, add a splash of the stock.
warnings: Ground spices scorch and turn bitter in seconds — keep them moving and pull the pot off the heat if they start to smoke.

steps.title: Simmer until soft
steps.content: Add pumpkin, lentils and stock; simmer until everything is tender.
steps.detail: Tip in the pumpkin, the rinsed lentils and the vegetable stock, and season with a good pinch of salt and pepper. Bring to the boil, then lower to a steady simmer and cook, part-covered, for about 20 minutes, until the pumpkin is falling-apart soft and the lentils have completely collapsed. Stir occasionally and scrape the base so nothing sticks.
tips: Check a cube of pumpkin against the side of the pot — it should crush with no resistance before you blend.

steps.title: Blend until smooth
steps.content: Blend the soup to a velvety, lump-free puree.
steps.detail: Take the pot off the heat and blend the soup smooth with a stick blender, working it around until there's not a lump left, or blend in batches in an upright blender. It should turn thick, glossy and a deep gold. If it's thicker than you like, loosen with a little more stock or water.
warnings: Hot soup spits and can scald — keep the stick blender fully submerged, and if using an upright blender fill it half full and hold the lid down with a folded tea towel.

steps.title: Stir in the coconut
steps.content: Warm through most of the coconut milk and check the seasoning.
steps.detail: Return the pot to low heat and stir in most of the coconut milk, saving a couple of tablespoons for the top. Warm it through gently without boiling, then taste and adjust — it may want another pinch of salt, a grind of pepper, or a little more curry powder. The coconut turns it silky and rounds off the earthiness of the lentils.

steps.title: Ladle and swirl
steps.content: Serve with a swirl of coconut and a scatter of coriander.
steps.detail: Ladle the soup into warm bowls, swirl the reserved coconut milk across the top with a spoon, and scatter over roughly chopped coriander. A final grind of pepper and, if you like, a drizzle of olive oil finishes it. Lovely with crusty bread for dunking.

title: Mango Prawn Quinoa Bowl
description: A bright summer bowl — fluffy quinoa, quick-seared prawns and sweet ripe mango, tossed with cool cucumber, mint and a sharp lime dressing.
storage: Cooked prawns keep 1-2 days in the fridge in a sealed container; cooked quinoa keeps 3 days. Store the components separately and dress just before eating, as the mango and cucumber weep once salted.
make_ahead: Cook the quinoa a day ahead and keep it chilled — it's an easy head start, and cold quinoa tosses through beautifully.
leftovers: Fold leftovers together with the last of the dressing for a cold lunchbox salad the next day.

substitutions.swap: sliced chicken breast or firm white fish like barramundi, cooked through
couscous or rice if that's what you have
mango out of season? ripe peach or pineapple gives the same sweet contrast

steps.title: Rinse and cook quinoa
steps.content: Simmer rinsed quinoa until fluffy with little tails.
steps.detail: Rinse the quinoa in a sieve under cold running water for 30 seconds — this washes off its natural bitter coating. Tip it into a saucepan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt, bring to the boil, then cover and simmer on low for 12-15 minutes until the water's absorbed and each grain has uncurled a little white tail. Take it off the heat, leave the lid on for 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork and spread out to cool slightly.
tips: If there's still water in the base when the grains look done, tip it off and dry the quinoa over low heat for a minute.

steps.title: Dice mango and cucumber
steps.content: Cube the mango, dice cucumber, chop mint and juice the lime.
steps.detail: Slice the cheeks off the mango either side of the flat stone, score the flesh into cubes and scoop them out with a spoon. Dice the cucumber into similar-sized pieces for a fresh crunch. Pick and roughly chop the mint leaves, and halve and juice the lime. Keep the mango and cucumber together in a bowl and the mint aside so it stays perky.

steps.title: Peel and season the prawns
steps.content: Peel the prawns, pat dry and toss with garlic.
steps.detail: If your prawns still have shells, peel them, leaving the tails on or off as you like, and run a knife down the back to lift out the dark vein. Pat them thoroughly dry with paper towel — wet prawns steam instead of searing — then toss with the minced garlic, a pinch of salt and pepper. Wash your hands and board well after handling the raw prawns.
warnings: Wash your hands, knife and board after handling the raw prawns, and keep them away from the cooked quinoa and cut fruit.

steps.title: Sear the prawns
steps.content: Cook the prawns in a hot pan just until pink and opaque.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a frying pan over high until it's properly hot. Lay the prawns in one layer and cook for about 1-2 minutes each side, until they turn from grey to pink and curl into a loose C-shape, the flesh just opaque all through. Pull them off the heat the moment they're done — a tight, tough curl means they've gone too far. The garlic should smell fragrant, not scorched.
tips: A prawn cooked just right curls into a gentle C; a tight O means it's overcooked and rubbery.
warnings: Prawns cook in a couple of minutes — don't wander off, as they turn rubbery fast on high heat.

steps.title: Dress and build the bowls
steps.content: Toss the lime dressing through and layer everything into bowls.
steps.detail: Whisk the lime juice with the remaining olive oil, a pinch of salt and a grind of pepper into a quick dressing. Spoon the fluffy quinoa into bowls as a base, top with the mango and cucumber, then pile on the warm prawns. Drizzle the lime dressing over everything and finish with the chopped mint. Serve straight away, while the prawns are still warm against the cool fruit.

title: Green Shakshuka Spinach Feta
description: Eggs poached in a soft tangle of sweet leek and wilted spinach, studded with salty feta and scooped up with charred bread — the green cousin of the brunch classic.
equipment: large deep frying pan with lid
toaster or grill for the bread
storage: Best eaten straight from the pan while the yolks are runny. The green base (without eggs) keeps 2 days in the fridge — reheat it, then poach fresh eggs to serve.
make_ahead: Make the leek and spinach base a day ahead and keep it chilled; on the day, reheat until bubbling and crack the eggs in fresh.
leftovers: Any leftover greens are lovely stirred through pasta or piled onto toast with an extra crumble of feta.

substitutions.swap: ricotta, or a mild goat's cheese, for something creamier
kale or silverbeet, sliced thin — give it a couple of extra minutes to wilt
a brown onion or a bunch of spring onions if you've no leek

steps.title: Slice the leek and greens
steps.content: Slice the leek, chop the spinach, mince garlic and crumble the feta.
steps.detail: Trim the leek, halve it lengthwise and slice into thin half-moons, then swish the slices in a bowl of cold water to wash out any grit hiding between the layers, and drain well. Roughly chop the spinach if the leaves are large, finely mince the garlic, and crumble the feta into rough nuggets. Having it all ready means the eggs won't overcook while you scramble to prep.
tips: Leeks trap soil between their layers — washing the sliced pieces, not the whole leek, gets the grit out.

steps.title: Soften the leek
steps.content: Cook the leek gently in oil until sweet and silky.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a large deep frying pan over medium-low heat and add the leek with a pinch of salt. Cook gently for 7-8 minutes, stirring often, until it's completely soft, sweet and just starting to turn golden at the edges — you're coaxing out sweetness, not colour, so keep the heat moderate. Stir in the garlic for the last minute until fragrant.

steps.title: Wilt the spinach
steps.content: Pile in the spinach and cook it down to a soft green base.
steps.detail: Add the spinach to the pan in big handfuls, stirring each lot until it collapses down before adding more — it looks like far too much but wilts to almost nothing. Once it's all silky and dark green, season with a grind of pepper and a little more salt if it needs it. If the pan looks watery, let it bubble for a minute so the base isn't sloppy when the eggs go in.

steps.title: Nestle in the eggs
steps.content: Make wells in the greens and crack an egg into each.
steps.detail: Use the back of a spoon to make four shallow wells in the green base, spacing them evenly. Crack an egg into each well — cracking it into a cup first and tipping it in gives you more control. Scatter the crumbled feta around and between the eggs, so it softens into the greens as they cook.

steps.title: Cover and poach
steps.content: Lid on, cook gently until the whites set and yolks stay runny.
steps.detail: Put the lid on the pan, turn the heat to low, and let the eggs poach in the steam for 6-9 minutes. Start checking at 6: the whites should be just set and no longer clear, while the yolks still wobble when you shake the pan. Cook a minute or two longer if you prefer firmer yolks — but remember they keep cooking in the hot pan once it's off the heat.
tips: A jiggle test beats the clock — give the pan a gentle shake; runny yolks wobble, set whites don't.

steps.title: Char the bread and serve
steps.content: Toast the bread and bring the pan straight to the table.
steps.detail: While the eggs finish, toast or grill the bread until crisp and golden, and drizzle it with a little olive oil. Grind a little more pepper over the shakshuka and bring the pan straight to the table. Scoop the eggs and greens up with the charred bread, mopping through the soft yolk and melting feta.

title: Lentil Carrot Parsley Salad
description: A bright, lemony salad of tender lentils and crisp grated carrot tossed in warm cumin oil, piled onto a garlicky yoghurt smear and showered with parsley.
storage: The dressed salad keeps 2 days in the fridge, though it's freshest on the day. Keep the yoghurt smear separate and spread it just before serving.
make_ahead: Cook and dress the lentils and carrot a few hours ahead — they only get tastier as they sit. Add the parsley and yoghurt just before serving.
leftovers: Spoon leftovers into a wrap with the yoghurt for a quick lunch, or stir through a tin of drained chickpeas to stretch it into a bigger meal.

substitutions.swap: tinned brown lentils or chickpeas hold their shape far better in a salad — just drain, rinse and skip the cooking
coarsely grated raw beetroot for an earthy, magenta version
mint or coriander, or a mix of all three

steps.title: Simmer the lentils
steps.content: Cook the red lentils just until tender but still holding.
steps.detail: Rinse the red lentils well, then simmer them in plenty of lightly salted water for 8-10 minutes. Red lentils soften fast and will turn to mush if you look away, so start testing at 8 minutes — you want them tender but only just holding their shape, not collapsed. Drain them straight away in a sieve, rinse briefly under cold water to stop the cooking, and leave to drain completely.
tips: Red lentils are naturally soft — if you want lentils that stay proudly separate, the tinned brown lentils in the swaps are the surer bet.

steps.title: Grate carrot and prep
steps.content: Coarsely grate the carrot; chop parsley and juice the lemon.
steps.detail: Peel the carrots and coarsely grate them on the big holes of a box grater into a large bowl. Roughly chop the parsley, leaves and tender stalks together. Finely grate the zest from the lemon, then halve it and squeeze out the juice, keeping the seeds back. Grate the garlic clove to a paste, ready for the dressing.

steps.title: Bloom the cumin
steps.content: Toast cumin in olive oil to make a warm, nutty dressing.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in a small pan over medium heat and stir in the ground cumin. Toast it for just 30-60 seconds, until it smells warm and nutty and fizzes softly in the oil, then take it straight off the heat. Stir in half the lemon juice and a pinch of salt — it'll splutter — to make a warm, fragrant dressing.
tips: Cumin turns bitter the second it scorches, so pull the pan off the heat as soon as you smell it toasting.
warnings: The oil is hot and the cumin browns in seconds — have the lemon juice ready so you can stop it cooking the moment it's fragrant.

steps.title: Whisk the yoghurt smear
steps.content: Stir garlic, lemon and salt through the yoghurt.
steps.detail: In a small bowl, stir the grated garlic and the remaining lemon juice through the yoghurt with a pinch of salt and a grind of pepper. Taste it — it should be tangy and just garlicky. Loosen with a teaspoon of water if you'd like it more spreadable.

steps.title: Toss the salad
steps.content: Combine lentils, carrot, parsley and the warm cumin dressing.
steps.detail: Tip the drained lentils and grated carrot into the big bowl with most of the parsley. Pour over the warm cumin dressing and the lemon zest, and toss gently with two spoons so the lentils don't crush. Taste and adjust with more salt, pepper or lemon until it's bright and well seasoned.

steps.title: Plate with yoghurt
steps.content: Spread the yoghurt and pile the salad on top.
steps.detail: Spread the garlicky yoghurt across a serving plate in a swoosh with the back of a spoon. Pile the dressed lentil-and-carrot salad on top, leaving a rim of yoghurt showing. Scatter over the last of the parsley and a final drizzle of olive oil, and serve while the lentils are still faintly warm.

title: Steak Rocket Baguette
description: The proper steak sanga — seared steak rested and sliced, piled into a crusty baguette with jammy balsamic onions, sharp Dijon and a handful of peppery rocket.
storage: Best built and eaten fresh while the steak is warm and the bread crisp. Cooked steak keeps 2 days in the fridge; store it separately from the bread and salad so nothing goes soggy, and slice it cold or gently rewarmed.
make_ahead: The balsamic onions can be made a day ahead and gently reheated — they're even jammier the next day.
leftovers: Any spare sliced steak is brilliant cold in a wrap the next day, or tossed through a rocket salad with the leftover onions.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast, flattened and pan-fried, for a lighter roll
ciabatta, Turkish bread or a soft milk bun instead of baguette
whole-egg mayonnaise, or mix the two into a quick mustard mayo

steps.title: Season and slice
steps.content: Salt the steak and let it warm; slice the red onion.
steps.detail: Take the steak out of the fridge, pat it dry, and season both sides generously with salt and pepper — do this 20-30 minutes ahead if you can, so it comes towards room temperature and cooks evenly. Peel the red onion, halve it, and slice into thin half-moons. Wash your hands and board after handling the raw steak.
warnings: Wash your hands, knife and board after handling the raw steak, before touching the bread or rocket.

steps.title: Caramelise the onions
steps.content: Cook the red onion slowly in butter with balsamic until jammy.
steps.detail: Melt the butter in a pan over medium-low heat, add the sliced onion and a pinch of salt, and cook gently for 12-15 minutes, stirring often, until soft, sweet and deep purple-brown. Pour in the balsamic vinegar and let it bubble away until sticky and jammy. Scrape the onions into a bowl and wipe the pan out for the steak.
tips: Low and slow is the trick — rush the onions on high heat and they'll scorch and turn bitter instead of sweet.

steps.title: Sear the steak
steps.content: Cook the steak hard and fast to a good crust.
steps.detail: Get the frying pan smoking hot, add the olive oil, and lay the steak down away from you. Don't move it — let it sear for 2-3 minutes until a deep brown crust forms, then flip and do the other side. For a steak about 2 cm thick, that's roughly 2-3 minutes a side for medium-rare; press it and it should feel springy with a little give. Adjust up or down for how you like it.
tips: No thermometer? Medium-rare feels like the fleshy pad at the base of your thumb; firmer means more well done.
warnings: The pan is very hot and the oil may spit as the steak goes in — lay it down away from you and stand back.

steps.title: Rest and slice
steps.content: Rest the steak, then slice thinly against the grain.
steps.detail: Lift the steak onto a board and let it rest for at least 5 minutes — this is not optional, it lets the juices settle back into the meat instead of running out onto the board. Look for the direction the muscle fibres run, then slice thinly across them, against the grain, so every bite is tender rather than stringy.
tips: Slicing against the grain shortens the muscle fibres and makes even a firmer steak eat tender.

steps.title: Toast and spread
steps.content: Split and lightly toast the rolls, then spread with Dijon.
steps.detail: Split the rolls and warm them cut-side down in the steak pan for a moment to toast and soak up the beefy juices, or crisp them under the grill. Spread the bases with Dijon mustard — as much or as little bite as you fancy.

steps.title: Build the baguette
steps.content: Layer steak, jammy onions and rocket into the rolls.
steps.detail: Pile the sliced steak onto the mustardy bases, spoon over the sticky balsamic onions, and top with a generous handful of peppery rocket. Press the lids on, cut in half if they're big, and eat straight away while the steak's still warm and the bread crisp.

title: Chicken Schnitzel Rocket Plate
description: Golden, shatteringly crisp chicken schnitzels crumbed with parmesan, served with a sharp lemony rocket salad and plenty of lemon to squeeze over.
equipment: wire rack or tray lined with paper towel
storage: Cooked schnitzels keep 2 days in the fridge. Reheat in a hot oven rather than the microwave so the crumb crisps up again. Dress the rocket salad only just before serving.
make_ahead: Crumb the schnitzels a few hours ahead and keep them uncooked on a tray in the fridge — chilling actually helps the crumb stay put when they hit the pan.
leftovers: A cold schnitzel sliced into a roll with the rocket and a smear of mayo makes a cracking lunch the next day.

substitutions.swap: boneless chicken thigh, or pork loin steaks for a pork schnitzel
panko crumbs give an even lighter, crunchier coat
pecorino, or a finely grated tasty cheese

steps.title: Flatten the chicken
steps.content: Halve and pound the breasts to an even thinness.
steps.detail: Slice each chicken breast in half horizontally through the middle to make two thin fillets. Lay them between two sheets of plastic or baking paper and pound with a meat mallet or rolling pin, working from the centre out, until an even 1 cm thick all over. Even thickness means they cook through at the same rate and stay tender. Wash your hands, board and mallet after the raw chicken.
warnings: Handle the raw chicken carefully — wash your hands, board, mallet and knife before they touch the salad or crumbs.

steps.title: Set up the crumbing station
steps.content: Three bowls: seasoned flour, beaten egg, parmesan crumbs.
steps.detail: Line up three shallow bowls. In the first, season the flour well with salt and pepper. In the second, beat the eggs with a splash of water. In the third, mix the breadcrumbs with the grated parmesan. Set a wire rack or a lined tray at the end of the line to lay the crumbed schnitzels on.

steps.title: Crumb the schnitzels
steps.content: Coat each fillet in flour, then egg, then crumbs.
steps.detail: Working one at a time, dredge a fillet in the seasoned flour and shake off the excess, dip it fully through the beaten egg letting the drips fall away, then press it firmly into the parmesan crumbs on both sides so they really stick. Lay each crumbed schnitzel on the rack. Keep one hand for the wet egg and one for the dry crumbs so your fingers don't turn into batter clubs.
tips: Resting the crumbed schnitzels in the fridge for 15 minutes helps the coating grip and stops it sliding off in the pan.
warnings: You're still handling raw chicken — wash your hands well once the crumbing's done.

steps.title: Fry until golden
steps.content: Shallow-fry the schnitzels crisp and golden, cooked through.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high — it's ready when a crumb dropped in sizzles briskly. Fry the schnitzels one or two at a time for 3-4 minutes each side until deep golden and crisp, and cooked through to 74°C at the thickest point with no pink. Drain on paper towel and season with a little salt while hot, then keep them warm while you cook the rest.
warnings: Hot oil spits — lower the schnitzels in gently away from you, and never leave the pan unattended.
Make sure the chicken is cooked through to 74°C at the thickest point before serving.

steps.title: Dress the rocket
steps.content: Toss rocket with lemon, oil and shaved parmesan.
steps.detail: Just before serving, pile the rocket into a bowl, squeeze over half the lemon, drizzle with a little olive oil and season lightly. Toss with your hands so every leaf is lightly coated — dress it too early and it wilts into a sad heap. Shave or grate a little extra parmesan over if you like.

steps.title: Plate with lemon
steps.content: Serve the schnitzels with the rocket and lemon wedges.
steps.detail: Sit a hot, crisp schnitzel on each plate with a tangle of the dressed rocket alongside. Cut the remaining lemon into wedges for squeezing over — that sharp squeeze cuts straight through the richness of the golden crumb. Serve while they're at their crispest.

title: Eggplant Tomato Feta Bake
description: Silky slices of fried eggplant layered with a garlicky tomato sauce and salty feta, under a crunchy herbed breadcrumb lid, baked until bubbling and golden.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge and reheats well in a moderate oven. It also freezes for up to 2 months — cool completely first, then thaw overnight before reheating.
make_ahead: Assemble the whole bake (without the crumb topping) a day ahead and keep it chilled; scatter the crumbs on and bake when you're ready to eat.
leftovers: Cold leftovers are excellent stuffed into a crusty roll the next day, pressed into a warm eggplant sandwich.

substitutions.swap: zucchini, sliced lengthwise, if eggplant's not your thing
torn mozzarella for a meltier, milder bake, or ricotta dolloped between the layers
oregano or parsley, or a spoon of dried oregano stirred into the sauce

steps.title: Salt the eggplant
steps.content: Slice and salt the eggplant to draw out moisture.
steps.detail: Slice the eggplant into rounds about 1 cm thick. Lay them out in a colander or on a tray, sprinkle both sides with salt, and leave for 15-20 minutes — beads of moisture pull to the surface, taking the raw bitterness with them and helping the slices fry up silky rather than spongy. Pat them thoroughly dry with paper towel before cooking.
tips: Pressing the salted slices between paper towel squeezes out even more moisture, so they soak up less oil in the pan.

steps.title: Make the tomato sauce
steps.content: Simmer onion, garlic and tinned tomato into a rich sauce.
steps.detail: While the eggplant sits, warm a little olive oil in a frying pan and soften the diced onion over medium heat for 5-6 minutes until translucent. Add the minced garlic for a minute until fragrant, then tip in the tinned tomatoes, crushing any whole ones with your spoon. Season with salt and pepper and simmer for 12-15 minutes until thick and glossy, then stir through half the torn basil.
warnings: Garlic burns and turns acrid fast — add it after the onion has softened and keep it moving.

steps.title: Fry the eggplant
steps.content: Brown the eggplant slices golden on both sides.
steps.detail: Heat a good film of olive oil in the frying pan over medium-high and fry the eggplant slices in batches, 2-3 minutes a side, until deep golden and completely soft in the middle. Add a little more oil between batches as the eggplant drinks it up. Drain the cooked slices on paper towel.
tips: Don't rush this on low heat or the slices stew and go greasy — a properly hot pan gives colour fast.
warnings: The oil is hot and eggplant can spit as it goes in — lay the slices down gently and away from you.

steps.title: Layer the bake
steps.content: Layer eggplant, tomato sauce and feta in the dish.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan-forced. Spread a little tomato sauce over the base of a baking dish, then layer in half the fried eggplant, half the remaining sauce and half the crumbled feta. Repeat with another layer of eggplant, sauce and feta, finishing with a layer of sauce on top so the crumbs have something to cling to.

steps.title: Top and bake
steps.content: Scatter herbed breadcrumbs over and bake until golden.
steps.detail: Toss the breadcrumbs with a drizzle of olive oil and a little salt and pepper, then scatter them evenly over the top. Bake for 25-30 minutes until the crumb topping is deep golden and crunchy and the sauce is bubbling up around the edges. If the top browns before the middle's hot, lay a sheet of foil loosely over it.
warnings: Try not to open the oven in the first 20 minutes — the blast of cold air slows the crumb from crisping.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.content: Rest the bake, then finish with fresh basil.
steps.detail: Let the bake sit for 10 minutes out of the oven — it's molten straight away and needs a moment to settle so it holds together when you serve it. Scatter over the rest of the torn basil and cut into squares. Lovely with a green salad and bread to mop the dish.

title: Peanut Banana Smoothie Bowl
description: Thick, soft-serve-style frozen banana blended with peanut butter and a hint of cocoa, spooned into bowls and loaded with crunchy muesli and a lazy drizzle of peanut butter.
storage: Smoothie bowls are best eaten the moment they're blended, as they melt quickly. If the base softens while you add toppings, firm it back up in the freezer for 10 minutes.
make_ahead: Peel and freeze ripe, spotty bananas in chunks ahead of time so you've always got the base ready to blend.

substitutions.swap: any plant milk — oat or almond — to keep it dairy-free
almond or cashew butter works just as well
maple syrup, especially if you're keeping it vegan

steps.title: Blend the base
steps.content: Blitz frozen banana, peanut butter, cocoa and honey with a little milk.
steps.detail: Tip the frozen banana into a blender or food processor with the peanut butter, cocoa and honey, and pour in just half the milk to start. Blitz on high, stopping to scrape down the sides and push the chunks back onto the blades. It'll be stubborn at first — be patient and resist flooding it with milk, or it'll turn into a thin drink instead of a thick, spoonable bowl.
tips: A tamper or the pulse button helps break up frozen banana without overheating the motor; a short rest lets the chunks settle back down.

steps.title: Adjust the texture
steps.content: Add milk a splash at a time until thick and spoonable.
steps.detail: Add the rest of the milk only a splash at a time, blending between each, until it comes together into a smooth, thick swirl like soft-serve ice cream — thick enough that a spoon stands up in it for a second. Taste and blend in a little more honey if the cocoa's made it too bitter for you.

steps.title: Spoon into bowls
steps.content: Divide the thick smoothie between chilled bowls.
steps.detail: Spoon the thick smoothie into two bowls and smooth the tops flat with the back of the spoon to make a neat canvas for the toppings. Work quickly — it starts to soften the moment it leaves the blender. Cold bowls straight from the freezer buy you a little extra time.

steps.title: Load the toppings
steps.content: Pile on muesli and finish with a peanut butter drizzle.
steps.detail: Scatter the muesli over the top for crunch, then warm a little extra peanut butter for a few seconds until runny and drizzle it back and forth across the bowl. A final dusting of cocoa and a thread of honey finishes it. Eat straight away with a spoon, while the base is still thick and frosty.

title: Tofu Sesame Bok Choy Noodles
description: Silky rice noodles tossed with golden seared tofu, just-wilted bok choy and a glossy sesame-soy dressing. On the table in twenty minutes flat.
storage: Best eaten fresh while the noodles are silky. Leftovers keep 1 day in an airtight container in the fridge; loosen with a splash of water and reheat quickly in a hot pan.
make_ahead: Press and cube the tofu and soak the noodles ahead — the stir-fry itself is a five-minute job once the wok is hot.
leftovers: Cold noodles make a good lunchbox — toss with an extra splash of soy and a few drops of sesame oil.

substitutions.swap: firm or extra-firm tofu holds best; tempeh, or thickly sliced mushrooms, also work
egg noodles or thin wheat noodles — cook them to the packet instead of soaking
any quick greens: pak choy, choy sum, or a couple of handfuls of baby spinach stirred in at the end

steps.title: Press and cube the tofu
steps.content: Drain, press and cut tofu into 2 cm cubes.
steps.detail: Wrap the tofu block in a clean tea towel and press gently under your hands or a plate for 5 minutes to squeeze out water — drier tofu browns instead of steaming grey.
