Cut into 2 cm cubes and pat dry again. While you're at it, peel and finely grate the garlic and ginger and keep them together in a little bowl.
tips: The more water you press out now, the crispier the edges later — don't skip it.

steps.title: Soak the noodles
steps.content: Soften rice noodles in just-boiled water.
steps.detail: Sit the rice noodles in a large bowl and cover with boiling water. Leave to soak until just pliable but still with a little bite, usually 5 to 8 minutes depending on thickness — check the packet, and remember they finish cooking in the wok. Drain, rinse briefly under cold water so they stop sticking, and set aside.

steps.title: Sear the tofu
steps.content: Fry tofu in olive oil until golden and crisp.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in the wok over medium-high until it shimmers. Add the tofu in a single layer and leave it alone for 2 to 3 minutes so a golden crust forms before you turn it — fidgeting tears it. Colour two or three sides, about 8 minutes total, until firm and deep gold, then lift out onto a plate.
tips: Don't crowd the pan — cook in two batches if your wok is small, or the tofu steams instead of browning.
warnings: Lay the tofu in gently and stand back — hot oil spits when the damp cubes hit it.

steps.title: Wilt the bok choy
steps.content: Stir-fry garlic and ginger, then the bok choy.
steps.detail: Trim the bok choy and separate the thick stems from the leaves. In the same hot wok add the grated garlic and ginger and stir for 20 to 30 seconds until fragrant. Add the stems first for a minute, then the leaves, tossing until bright green and just wilted but still crisp.
warnings: Garlic and ginger burn fast and turn bitter — keep them moving and drop the heat if they colour too quickly.

steps.title: Toss it all together
steps.content: Return tofu and noodles, splash in soy, finish with sesame.
steps.detail: Return the tofu and drained noodles to the wok, pour over the soy sauce, and toss everything with tongs for a minute so the noodles heat through and pick up the sauce. Take off the heat, drizzle with the sesame oil, and season with a little salt and pepper — go easy on the salt, the soy is already salty.
tips: If the noodles clump, a small splash of water helps them loosen and slide apart.

title: Mushroom Miso Coconut Curry
description: Meaty mushrooms browned hard, then simmered in coconut milk loosened with savoury miso and spooned over jasmine rice. A gentle, brothy curry from one pan.
equipment: large deep frying pan or wok
storage: The curry keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge; the rice is best eaten within a day. Reheat the curry gently so the coconut doesn't split.
make_ahead: The curry only deepens overnight — make it a day ahead and cook fresh rice to serve.
leftovers: Thin any leftovers with a splash of stock or water and add noodles for a quick soup.

substitutions.swap: any mushrooms — button, Swiss brown, or a mix; oyster mushrooms are lovely torn in
white miso is the mildest; a spoon of soy sauce will stand in if you've none
light coconut milk for a thinner broth
basmati or any long-grain rice, or serve over noodles

steps.title: Cook the jasmine rice
steps.content: Simmer rice with a lid until fluffy.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice until the water runs clear, then tip it into a saucepan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, drop to the lowest heat, cover and cook 12 minutes. Take off the heat and leave it covered another 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork.
tips: Don't lift the lid while it cooks — the trapped steam is what cooks the grains evenly.

steps.title: Prep the aromatics and mushrooms
steps.content: Tear mushrooms; grate garlic and ginger; slice spring onion.
steps.detail: Wipe the mushrooms clean and tear or slice them into thick pieces — chunky holds up far better than thin. Peel and finely grate the garlic and ginger. Slice the spring onions, keeping the white parts for cooking and the green tops for scattering at the end.

steps.title: Brown the mushrooms
steps.content: Fry mushrooms hard until golden and nutty.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large pan over medium-high. Add the mushrooms and leave them to sit before stirring — they'll release water first, then, once it cooks off, start to brown and smell nutty, about 8 minutes. Add the spring onion whites, garlic and ginger for the last minute until fragrant.
tips: Resist stirring too early; that deep browning is where the savoury depth comes from.
warnings: Once the garlic and ginger go in, keep everything moving so they don't catch and turn bitter.

steps.title: Loosen the miso and simmer
steps.content: Whisk miso into coconut milk and simmer gently.
steps.detail: Spoon the miso into a small bowl and loosen it with a couple of tablespoons of the coconut milk until smooth — this stops it clumping in the pan. Pour the rest of the coconut milk in, stir through the loosened miso, and bring to a gentle simmer. Let it bubble softly for 5 minutes into a glossy sauce; don't let it boil hard or the coconut can split.

steps.title: Wilt the spinach and serve
steps.content: Stir through spinach, season, spoon over the rice.
steps.detail: Add the baby spinach a handful at a time, stirring until just wilted and glossy, about a minute. Taste and adjust — a little salt and pepper, and if it wants a deeper savoury lift, another small dab of miso. Spoon over the jasmine rice and scatter with the reserved spring onion greens.

title: Prawn Lemon Pea Risotto
description: Sweet prawns and bright peas stirred through creamy arborio rice, finished with a big hit of lemon and parmesan. Restaurant-good, made in the one pan.
equipment: large heavy-based frying pan or shallow pot
storage: Risotto is best eaten straight away while it's loose and creamy. Leftovers keep 1 day in the fridge — the prawns especially don't keep longer; reheat gently with a splash of stock.
make_ahead: For a dinner party, cook the rice to three-quarters done, spread it on a tray to cool, then finish with hot stock and the prawns just before serving.
leftovers: Roll cold risotto into balls, crumb them and shallow-fry for arancini.

substitutions.swap: scallops or firm white fish; or leave out and add extra peas, using vegetable stock, for a meat-free version
any risotto rice — arborio or carnaroli; short-grain rice in a pinch
fish or vegetable stock works just as well
any hard grating cheese — grana or pecorino

steps.title: Warm stock, prep aromatics
steps.content: Heat the stock; dice onion, zest lemon, ready the prawns.
steps.detail: Pour the stock into a small saucepan and keep it at a bare simmer on a back burner — adding hot stock is what keeps a risotto creamy. Finely dice the onion. Zest the lemon into a small bowl, then cut it into wedges for juicing later. If your prawns still have shells, peel them and pat dry.
warnings: Peel the prawns on a separate board and wash your hands and the board before touching anything else — raw seafood.

steps.title: Soften the onion
steps.content: Sweat onion in oil and a little butter.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil with a small knob of the butter in a wide heavy pan over medium. Add the onion with a pinch of salt and cook gently for 5 minutes, stirring, until soft and translucent but not coloured — you're after sweetness, not browning.

steps.title: Toast the rice
steps.content: Stir arborio until the grain edges turn glassy.
steps.detail: Tip in the arborio rice and stir for a minute or two so every grain is coated in the fat and the edges turn translucent while the centres stay chalky white. This toasting helps the rice keep its bite, and you'll hear it lightly click against the pan.

steps.title: Add stock ladle by ladle
steps.content: Stir in hot stock a ladleful at a time.
steps.detail: Add a ladle of hot stock and stir until it's almost all absorbed before adding the next, keeping it at a lazy simmer. Carry on, stirring often, for about 18 minutes until the rice is creamy and just tender with a slight bite in the centre. You may not need every drop, or you might want a splash of hot water.
tips: Stirring coaxes the starch out of the rice — that's what makes it creamy without any cream.

steps.title: Cook the prawns and peas
steps.content: Stir in prawns and peas for the last few minutes.
steps.detail: When the rice is nearly there, stir in the prawns and frozen peas. Cook 3 to 4 minutes until the prawns turn pink and opaque and the peas are vivid green and warmed through.
tips: Prawns are done the moment they curl into a loose C and turn pink — a tight O means they've gone too far and turned rubbery.

steps.title: Beat in cheese and lemon
steps.content: Off the heat, stir through butter, parmesan and lemon.
steps.detail: Take the pan off the heat. Beat in the remaining butter and the grated parmesan until glossy — this final stir is what makes risotto luxurious. Stir through the lemon zest and a good squeeze of juice, then season with salt and pepper. It should be loose enough to spread when you tap the pan; loosen with a splash of stock if not, and serve at once.
tips: Add the lemon juice a little at a time and taste as you go — you want bright, not sour.

title: Crispy Tofu Broccoli Stir-Fry
description: Golden cubes of tofu with a shatter-crisp cornflour coating, tossed with tender-crisp broccoli in a glossy soy, garlic and ginger sauce over jasmine rice.
storage: The tofu is crispiest straight from the pan. Leftovers keep 1 day in the fridge; the coating softens but a quick blast in a hot pan revives it. Keep rice no longer than a day.
make_ahead: Press and cube the tofu and mix the sauce ahead; the stir-fry itself is a five-minute job once you start.
leftovers: Stuff cold leftovers into a wrap with some fresh salad for an easy lunch.

substitutions.swap: firm or extra-firm tofu; tempeh also crisps well
broccolini, or cauliflower cut into small florets
tamari for gluten-free; a spoon of oyster sauce adds body if you don't need it vegan
basmati, or serve over noodles

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Simmer jasmine rice with a lid, then rest.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice until the water runs clear, tip into a saucepan with 3 cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, drop to the lowest heat, cover and cook 12 minutes. Rest off the heat, still covered, for 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork.
tips: Getting the rice on first means it's resting happily while you stir-fry.

steps.title: Press and coat the tofu
steps.content: Press tofu, cube, toss in cornflour.
steps.detail: Wrap the tofu in a clean tea towel and press under a plate for 5 to 10 minutes to squeeze out water. Cut into 2 cm cubes, pat dry, then toss in a bowl with the cornflour and a pinch of salt and pepper until lightly dusted all over — this thin coat is what crisps up.
tips: Dry tofu is the whole secret — wet tofu steams and stays pale and soft.

steps.title: Prep broccoli and sauce
steps.content: Cut broccoli small; mix soy with garlic and ginger.
steps.detail: Cut the broccoli into small, even florets so they cook fast; peel the stem and slice that too, it's sweet. Grate the garlic and ginger. In a small bowl stir the soy sauce with the garlic, ginger and a splash of water to make your stir-fry sauce.

steps.title: Fry the tofu crisp
steps.content: Sear tofu in hot oil until golden all over.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in the wok over medium-high until shimmering. Add the tofu in one layer and leave it to form a golden crust before turning, 2 to 3 minutes a side, until crisp and deep gold all over, about 10 minutes. Lift out onto a plate.
tips: Don't crowd the pan; cook in two batches if you need to, or it steams instead of crisping.
warnings: Lay the tofu in gently — hot oil spits when the damp, floury cubes hit it.

steps.title: Stir-fry the broccoli
steps.content: Toss broccoli in the hot wok until bright and just tender.
steps.detail: Add the broccoli to the same wok with a splash of water and toss over high heat for 3 to 4 minutes. It's ready when it's vivid green and tender-crisp, still with a bite rather than soft. A lid on for a minute steams it through if the pieces are big.

steps.title: Bring it together
steps.content: Return tofu, pour in sauce, toss to glaze.
steps.detail: Return the crispy tofu to the wok and pour in the soy-garlic sauce. Toss for a minute — the cornflour on the tofu thickens the sauce into a glossy glaze that clings to everything. Taste, adjust with a little more soy if it needs it, and pile over the rice.
warnings: The garlic in the sauce can catch once it hits the hot pan — keep everything moving and serve straight up.

title: Chickpea Tomato Shakshuka
description: Eggs poached in a cumin-spiced tomato and chickpea sauce, cooked and served in the one pan. Scoop it up with charred bread — a brunch that feeds a table.
equipment: large deep frying pan with a lid
storage: The tomato base keeps 3 days in the fridge and freezes well; add fresh eggs when you reheat. Once the eggs are in, eat the same day — poached eggs don't keep.
make_ahead: Make the chickpea-tomato sauce ahead, then reheat it and poach the eggs fresh when you're ready to eat.
leftovers: Spare sauce is great tossed through pasta or spooned over rice the next day.

substitutions.swap: tinned white beans or lentils work the same way
any crusty bread, pita, or toasted sourdough
flat-leaf parsley if coriander isn't your thing
a spoon of smoked paprika or curry powder for a different warmth

steps.title: Soften onion and garlic
steps.content: Cook diced onion, then garlic and cumin.
steps.detail: Dice the onion. Warm the olive oil in a deep frying pan over medium and cook the onion with a pinch of salt for 5 to 6 minutes until soft and golden at the edges. Add the sliced garlic and the cumin and stir for a minute until fragrant and the kitchen smells warm and toasty.
warnings: Cumin and garlic scorch quickly — a minute is plenty, and lower the heat if they colour fast.

steps.title: Simmer the tomato base
steps.content: Add tomatoes and chickpeas, simmer to thicken.
steps.detail: Tip in the tinned tomatoes, crushing any whole ones against the pan with your spoon, along with the drained chickpeas. Add a splash of water, season with salt and pepper, and simmer for 10 minutes, stirring now and then, until it thickens to a rich sauce that no longer runs when you drag a spoon through.
tips: If the sauce tastes sharp, let it simmer a couple of minutes longer to mellow and sweeten.

steps.title: Make wells for the eggs
steps.content: Hollow out dips in the sauce and crack in the eggs.
steps.detail: Use the back of your spoon to make four wells in the sauce, spacing them out. Crack an egg into each well and season the eggs with a little salt and pepper.
tips: Crack each egg into a cup first, then slide it into its well — much easier to catch any stray shell.

steps.title: Poach the eggs
steps.content: Cover and cook until the whites just set.
steps.detail: Cover the pan with a lid and cook over low-medium heat for 6 to 8 minutes, until the whites are set but the yolks still wobble when you nudge the pan — they carry on cooking off the heat. Watch closely near the end; a soft yolk to break into the sauce is the goal.
tips: No lid? A tray or a sheet of foil over the top traps the steam that sets the tops of the whites.

steps.title: Char the bread and serve
steps.content: Toast bread, scatter coriander, serve from the pan.
steps.detail: Toast or grill the bread until charred at the edges. Scatter the shakshuka with torn coriander and bring the pan straight to the table with the bread alongside for scooping and dipping into the yolks.

title: Watermelon-Style Mango Mint Salad
description: Juicy mango stands in for watermelon in this no-cook salad — cool cucumber, salty feta, fresh mint and a chilli-lime dressing. Ten minutes, no stove.
storage: Best assembled and eaten fresh — salt draws water from the fruit as it sits. If you must get ahead, keep the cut fruit and the dressing separate in the fridge and toss just before serving.
make_ahead: Cube the mango and cucumber and whisk the dressing a few hours ahead; add the feta and mint and dress only when you're ready to serve.

substitutions.swap: goat's cheese, or leave it out for a dairy-free salad
ripe peach or nectarine — or actual watermelon when it's in season
basil, or a mix of mint and coriander
a pinch of chilli flakes, or leave it out for no heat

steps.title: Cube the mango
steps.content: Slice and cube the ripe mango.
steps.detail: Stand the mango up and cut down either side of the flat stone. Score the flesh of each cheek into a criss-cross without cutting through the skin, then push it inside-out and slice the cubes off. Trim any fruit clinging to the stone too. Aim for bite-sized pieces that stand in for watermelon.
tips: A firm-ripe mango holds its shape; an overripe one collapses to mush the moment you toss it.

steps.title: Chop cucumber and mint
steps.content: Cube cucumber, pick and tear the mint.
steps.detail: Cut the cucumber into chunks a similar size to the mango — leave the skin on for crunch and colour. Pick the mint leaves from their stems and tear the larger ones so they release their scent.

steps.title: Make the chilli-lime dressing
steps.content: Whisk lime juice, olive oil, chilli and salt.
steps.detail: Finely slice the red chilli, flicking out the seeds if you'd rather less heat. In a small bowl whisk the juice of the lime with the olive oil, most of the chilli and a pinch of salt until it looks cloudy and combined. Taste — it should be zingy and just a touch salty.
warnings: Wash your hands well after slicing chilli and keep them away from your eyes.

steps.title: Assemble and serve
steps.content: Layer fruit, crumble feta, dress and scatter mint.
steps.detail: Scatter the mango and cucumber over a platter and crumble the feta over in rough chunks. Spoon the dressing across the top, then scatter with the torn mint and the last of the sliced chilli. Serve immediately, while everything is cold and crisp.
tips: Dress it at the last minute — sitting in the dressing softens the fruit and draws out watery juices.

title: Chickpea Fritter Lettuce Cups
description: Cumin-spiced chickpea fritters, crisp and golden, tucked into cool lettuce cups with cucumber and a lemony yoghurt. Light, fresh and fun to build at the table.
storage: The cooked fritters keep 2 days in the fridge and reheat crisp in a hot pan or oven. Keep the lettuce, cucumber and yoghurt separate and build the cups fresh — assembled, they turn soggy.
make_ahead: Shape the fritter mixture and make the yoghurt sauce a day ahead; fry the fritters just before serving.
leftovers: Squash leftover fritters into a wrap with the yoghurt and some salad for lunch.

substitutions.swap: tinned white beans, or half a tin of each
dairy-free yoghurt, or a mix of tahini and lemon for the sauce
baby cos or iceberg cups; or serve the lot in wraps
for egg-free, add an extra spoon of flour and a splash of water to bind

steps.title: Mix the yoghurt sauce
steps.content: Stir yoghurt with lemon, salt and a little cumin.
steps.detail: Spoon the yoghurt into a small bowl. Grate in a little lemon zest, squeeze in half the juice, and stir with a pinch of salt and a small pinch of the cumin. Taste — it should be tangy and cool to offset the spiced fritters. Pop it in the fridge while you cook.

steps.title: Mash the chickpea mixture
steps.content: Mash chickpeas with egg, flour and cumin.
steps.detail: Drain and rinse the chickpeas, tip them into a bowl and mash with a fork or masher until mostly broken down but still a bit rough. Crack in the egg, add the flour, the rest of the cumin and a good pinch of salt and pepper. Mix to a thick batter that just holds together — add a little more flour if it's loose.
tips: Leaving some chickpeas whole gives the fritters a nicer, nubbly bite.

steps.title: Prep the cups and cucumber
steps.content: Separate lettuce leaves; slice the cucumber.
steps.detail: Separate the lettuce into whole cup-shaped leaves, then wash and pat them dry so the filling doesn't slide. Cut the cucumber into small batons or half-moons for crunch.

steps.title: Fry the fritters
steps.content: Cook spoonfuls until golden and crisp both sides.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a frying pan over medium. Drop in heaped spoonfuls of the chickpea mixture and flatten each slightly with the back of the spoon. Fry 3 to 4 minutes a side until deep golden and crisp, pressing gently so they cook through, then drain on paper.
tips: Let each side set and colour before you flip — turning too early breaks the fritters apart.

steps.title: Build the cups
steps.content: Fill lettuce with fritters, cucumber and yoghurt.
steps.detail: Set out the lettuce cups and tuck a fritter into each. Add some cucumber, a spoon of the lemony yoghurt and a last squeeze of lemon. Serve straight away and eat with your hands.

title: Eggplant Parmesan Crumbed Plate
description: Crumbed eggplant baked until golden, layered with a quick basil-tomato sauce and melting mozzarella. All the comfort of parmigiana, on a plate.
storage: Keeps 3 days covered in the fridge and reheats well in a hot oven. The crumbed eggplant is crispest fresh; leftovers soften but stay tasty.
make_ahead: Crumb the eggplant and make the tomato sauce ahead; assemble and bake just before serving.
leftovers: Layer cold leftovers into a crusty roll with extra sauce for a cracking eggplant sub.

substitutions.swap: large zucchini, sliced lengthways and crumbed the same way
any melting cheese — bocconcini torn over, or extra cheddar
panko for extra crunch, or crush up stale bread
pecorino, or a hard tasty cheese

steps.title: Heat oven, slice the eggplant
steps.content: Preheat to 200°C; slice eggplant into rounds.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan-forced and drizzle a baking tray with some of the olive oil. Slice the eggplant into 1 cm thick rounds. If you have time, lay them out, sprinkle with salt and leave 10 minutes, then pat dry — it draws out moisture for a crisper result.

steps.title: Set up the crumbing station
steps.content: Three bowls: flour, then egg, then cheesy crumbs.
steps.detail: Line up three shallow bowls: plain flour with salt and pepper in the first; the eggs beaten in the second; and the breadcrumbs mixed with half the parmesan in the third. This order — flour to dry, egg to glue, crumb to coat — is what makes a crust that stays put.

steps.title: Crumb and bake the eggplant
steps.content: Coat each round and bake until golden.
steps.detail: Dip each eggplant round in the flour, then the egg, then press it into the crumbs on both sides. Lay them on the oiled tray, drizzle with a little more oil, and bake for 25 minutes, flipping halfway, until deep golden and crisp at the edges and soft inside.
tips: A drizzle of oil over the crumbed tops helps them colour like they've been fried.

steps.title: Simmer the basil-tomato sauce
steps.content: Cook tinned tomato with basil into a quick sauce.
steps.detail: While the eggplant bakes, warm a splash of olive oil in a pan, tip in the tinned tomatoes and crush them with a spoon. Season with salt and pepper and simmer for 10 minutes until thick and glossy. Tear in most of the basil right at the end.
tips: Season the sauce well — it's the flavour that carries the mild, silky eggplant.

steps.title: Layer, melt and serve
steps.content: Top eggplant with sauce and mozzarella, melt in the oven.
steps.detail: Spoon the tomato sauce over the baked eggplant rounds, scatter the mozzarella and the remaining parmesan on top, and return to the hot oven for 5 to 8 minutes until the cheese is melted and bubbling. Scatter with the last of the basil and serve.
warnings: The dish and melted cheese are searingly hot — use a dry cloth and let it settle a minute before serving.

title: Chicken Pumpkin Rice Bake
description: Everything cooks together in the one dish — juicy chicken thighs, sweet pumpkin and jasmine rice that drinks up the stock, finished with crisp sage and parmesan.
equipment: large deep baking dish or roasting tin
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge — the rice is best within a day. Reheat covered with a splash of stock until piping hot, at least 75°C right through.
make_ahead: Dice the pumpkin and onion and trim the chicken ahead; assemble and bake when you're ready to eat.
leftovers: Shred any leftover chicken and rice into a quick soup with extra stock.

substitutions.swap: bone-in thighs or drumsticks (add 10 to 15 minutes); or breast, though thigh stays juicier
basmati or long-grain white rice; avoid brown rice here, it needs much longer
sweet potato, or a mix of pumpkin and carrot
thyme or rosemary, stripped from the stalk

steps.title: Heat oven and prep
steps.content: Preheat to 190°C; cube pumpkin, dice onion, season chicken.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 190°C fan-forced. Peel and cut the pumpkin into 3 cm chunks so it roasts in step with the rice. Dice the onion and crush the garlic. Pat the chicken thighs dry and season all over with salt and pepper.
warnings: Wash your hands, board and knife after handling the raw chicken, before you touch anything else.

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.content: Sear thighs until golden, then set aside.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in the baking dish on the stovetop, or in a frying pan, over medium-high. Lay the thighs skin-side down (or their smoother side) and sear for 4 to 5 minutes until golden — you're after colour and rendered fat, not cooked through yet. Lift out and set aside.
warnings: The chicken is only browned, not cooked — rest it on a clean plate, away from anything ready to eat.

steps.title: Toast onion, garlic and rice
steps.content: Soften onion and garlic, then stir in the rice.
steps.detail: In the same dish, cook the onion in the chicken fat over medium for 4 minutes until soft, then add the garlic for a minute. Stir in the jasmine rice so the grains glisten and pick up all that flavour from the base.
warnings: Garlic burns fast in the hot fat — a minute, stirring constantly, then move straight on.

steps.title: Assemble and bake covered
steps.content: Add stock and pumpkin, nestle chicken, bake covered.
steps.detail: Pour in the hot chicken stock and stir, scraping the base. Scatter the pumpkin over, nestle the chicken thighs back on top, cover tightly with foil and bake for 30 minutes. The rice cooks in the stock below while the chicken finishes above.
tips: Press the foil right down over the edges — trapping the steam is what cooks the rice evenly.

steps.title: Uncover, crisp and check
steps.content: Bake uncovered to colour; check chicken hits 74°C.
steps.detail: Take off the foil, scatter over the parmesan, and bake another 15 minutes uncovered until the rice is tender, the liquid absorbed and the chicken golden. Check the thickest thigh reads 74°C, or that the juices run clear with no pink.
warnings: Chicken must reach 74°C at the thickest point — if it's not there, cover and give it a few more minutes.

steps.title: Crisp the sage and serve
steps.content: Fry sage leaves crisp, scatter over the bake.
steps.detail: While it rests, heat a little olive oil in a small pan and fry the sage leaves for about 30 seconds until they turn crisp and dark green and smell nutty. Scatter over the bake with a grind of pepper and serve from the dish.
warnings: The oil is hot and the leaves spit as their moisture escapes — add them carefully and step back.

title: Pineapple Mint Smoothie Bowl
description: A thick, tropical breakfast bowl — frozen banana and pineapple blitzed with yoghurt and fresh mint, spooned up cold and showered with toasted coconut.
storage: Smoothie bowls are best blended and eaten straight away while thick and icy. If you blend ahead, keep it in the freezer and stir before serving; add the toppings only at the last minute.
make_ahead: Freeze the peeled banana and pineapple in chunks ahead of time — frozen fruit is what makes the bowl thick and cold enough to spoon.
leftovers: Pour any extra into ice-block moulds for a frozen treat later.

substitutions.swap: coconut or any dairy-free yoghurt for a vegan bowl
maple syrup, or a couple of pitted dates blended in
coconut milk for extra tropical richness, or any plant milk
frozen mango or extra pineapple — it just needs to be frozen for the thick texture

steps.title: Toast the coconut
steps.content: Toast desiccated coconut until golden and fragrant.
steps.detail: Warm a dry frying pan over medium and add the desiccated coconut. Stir it constantly for 2 to 3 minutes until it turns golden and smells nutty, then tip it straight out of the pan onto a plate — it colours in seconds and burns just as fast. Set aside for topping.
tips: Coconut goes from golden to burnt in a blink — don't walk away, and get it out of the hot pan the moment it colours.

steps.title: Prep the fruit and mint
steps.content: Cut pineapple, ready the banana, pick the mint.
steps.detail: Cut the skin off the pineapple, slice out the tough core and chop the flesh into chunks — keep a few pieces back for topping. Have your frozen banana ready from the freezer. Pick the mint leaves, saving a small sprig to garnish.

steps.title: Blend it thick
steps.content: Blitz fruit, yoghurt, milk, honey and mint.
steps.detail: Put the frozen banana, most of the pineapple, the yoghurt, milk, honey and mint into the blender. Blitz until thick and smooth — you want it spoonable, like soft-serve, not pourable. Add milk a splash at a time only if it won't turn over, stopping to scrape down the sides as needed.
tips: Too runny? Add a few more frozen banana chunks. Too thick to blend? The smallest splash more milk.

steps.title: Bowl and top
steps.content: Spoon into bowls and pile on the toppings.
steps.detail: Spoon the thick smoothie into cold bowls and smooth the tops. Scatter over the toasted coconut, the reserved pineapple chunks and a sprig of mint. Eat straight away with a spoon, before it softens.

title: Prawn Coconut Rice Noodles
description: Silky rice noodles pulled through a fragrant coconut sauce with sweet prawns, fresh chilli, ginger and a big squeeze of lime.
storage: Best eaten fresh while the noodles are silky. Leftovers keep 1 day covered in the fridge; reheat gently with a splash of water, as the prawns toughen if cooked hard again.
make_ahead: Peel the prawns and chop the aromatics earlier in the day, then the actual cook is under fifteen minutes.
leftovers: Cold, the noodles make a quick lunch tossed with extra lime and a handful of fresh coriander.

substitutions.swap: firm white fish such as barramundi, or thinly sliced chicken thigh
light coconut milk for a thinner, less rich sauce
a pinch of chilli flakes if you have no fresh chilli
flat rice noodles or thin egg noodles

steps.title: Soak the rice noodles
steps.content: Cover the noodles in just-boiled water until bendy.
steps.detail: Put the rice noodles in a big heatproof bowl and cover with just-boiled water from the kettle. Leave them to soak until floppy and bendy but with a little bite left, usually 5 to 8 minutes for thin noodles. Drain in a colander and rinse under cold water so they stop cooking and don't clump into a lump.
tips: Check the packet — thick noodles take longer, while fine vermicelli may only need a couple of minutes.

steps.title: Prep the prawns and aromatics
steps.content: Peel prawns, grate ginger, slice chilli, chop coriander.
steps.detail: Peel the prawns if they still have shells and pull out the dark vein along the back. Grate the ginger, mince the garlic, and finely slice the chilli, scraping out the seeds for less heat. Chop the coriander, keeping the tender stalks for the sauce and the leaves for the top, and cut the lime into wedges.
tips: Pat the prawns dry with paper towel so they sear rather than stew in the sauce.
warnings: Handle raw prawns on a separate board, and wash your hands, the board and the knife before they touch anything you'll eat raw.

steps.title: Sizzle the aromatics
steps.content: Fry ginger, garlic and chilli until fragrant.
steps.detail: Warm the vegetable oil in a large frying pan or wok over medium heat. Add the ginger, garlic and most of the sliced chilli and stir for about 30 seconds, until they smell fragrant and just start to colour. Keep them moving the whole time.
warnings: Don't let the garlic brown past pale gold or the whole sauce turns bitter.

steps.title: Simmer the coconut sauce
steps.content: Pour in coconut milk and fish sauce; simmer to thicken.
steps.detail: Pour in the coconut milk and add the fish sauce and the reserved coriander stalks. Bring to a gentle simmer and let it bubble softly for 3 to 4 minutes to thicken a little and take on the aromatics. Taste — it should be savoury and rounded; add a pinch of salt or a splash more fish sauce if it needs lifting.

steps.title: Poach the prawns
steps.content: Add prawns and cook until pink and curled.
steps.detail: Slide the prawns into the simmering sauce and cook for 2 to 3 minutes, turning once, until they turn pink all over and curl into a loose C shape. They're done the moment the flesh is opaque right through — any longer and they go rubbery.
warnings: Prawns cook in minutes; pull them off the heat as soon as they're opaque all the way through.

steps.title: Pull the noodles through
steps.content: Toss the drained noodles in the sauce to coat.
steps.detail: Add the drained noodles to the pan and toss with tongs so every strand gets coated in the coconut sauce. If it looks tight, loosen with a splash of water; if it's loose, let it bubble another minute. Squeeze in the juice of half the lime and taste again.

steps.title: Finish with lime and coriander
steps.content: Scatter coriander and serve with lime wedges.
steps.detail: Pile the noodles and prawns into bowls. Scatter over the coriander leaves and the last of the fresh chilli, and serve with lime wedges to squeeze at the table. A final squeeze of lime just before eating keeps everything bright.

title: Red Lentil Tomato Curry Soup
description: A cosy, spiced red lentil soup that cooks down thick and soft. Curry powder toasted in oil, sweet tinned tomato, and a cooling swirl of yoghurt to finish.
storage: Keeps 3 to 4 days in an airtight container in the fridge and freezes well for up to 3 months. Add the yoghurt and coriander per bowl rather than to the whole pot.
make_ahead: Like most lentil soups it's even better the next day, once the spices have had time to settle.
leftovers: It thickens to almost a dahl overnight — loosen with a splash of water and pile onto hot toast, or spoon over rice.

substitutions.swap: coconut milk for a dairy-free, vegan bowl
a mix of ground cumin, turmeric and smoked paprika
chicken stock if you're not keeping it vegetarian
any quick-cooking split lentil

steps.title: Dice the onion and garlic
steps.content: Finely dice the onion; mince the garlic.
steps.detail: Peel and finely dice the brown onion — the smaller the dice, the more it melts into the finished soup. Mince or grate the garlic. Keeping the onion fine means it softens quickly and you won't get big pieces floating in a smooth soup.

steps.title: Rinse the red lentils
steps.content: Rinse the lentils until the water runs clear.
steps.detail: Tip the red lentils into a sieve and rinse under cold water, swishing with your hand, until the cloudy water runs mostly clear. This washes off the surface starch so the soup doesn't turn gluey. There's no need to soak red lentils — they cook fast.

steps.title: Toast the curry powder
steps.content: Soften the onion, then bloom the curry powder in oil.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in the pot over medium heat and cook the onion for 4 to 5 minutes until soft and translucent. Stir in the garlic and curry powder and cook for another minute — you want the spices to sizzle and smell toasty, which wakes up their flavour. If it looks dry or starts to catch, add a splash of the stock.
tips: Blooming the curry powder in oil instead of straight water makes the whole soup taste deeper.
warnings: Keep the spices moving; they scorch and turn bitter in seconds over a dry, hot base.

steps.title: Simmer the lentils
steps.content: Add lentils, tomatoes and stock; simmer until soft.
steps.detail: Add the rinsed lentils, the tin of tomatoes and the vegetable stock, crushing the tomatoes against the side of the pot with your spoon. Bring to a boil, then drop to a gentle simmer and cook for 20 to 25 minutes, stirring now and then, until the lentils have collapsed and the soup is thick. Scrape the base occasionally so nothing sticks.
tips: If it gets too thick, loosen with a little extra stock or water — lentils drink up a surprising amount.

steps.title: Blend to your liking
steps.content: Blitz smooth or leave it rustic, then season well.
steps.detail: For a smooth soup, blitz it with a stick blender right in the pot; for a rustic one, leave it as is. Season well with salt and pepper — lentils are bland without enough salt, so taste and keep adding until it tastes lively rather than flat.
tips: A squeeze of lemon or a splash of vinegar at the end brightens the whole bowl if it tastes dull.

steps.title: Swirl in yoghurt and coriander
steps.content: Top each bowl with yoghurt and coriander.
steps.detail: Ladle into bowls and swirl a spoon of Greek yoghurt through each one — it melts into cool ribbons against the warm spice. Scatter over the coriander and serve. Adding the yoghurt to the bowls rather than the pot means any leftovers reheat cleanly.

title: Chicken Leek Risotto
description: Creamy, slow-stirred arborio with sweet softened leek and golden chicken thigh, finished the proper way with cold butter and a shower of parmesan.
storage: Best eaten straight away while it's loose and creamy. Keeps 1 day in an airtight container in the fridge; reheat gently with a splash of stock or water, stirring, until piping hot.
leftovers: Cold risotto is the secret to arancini — roll it into balls, crumb them and shallow-fry until golden and molten inside.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast — just take care not to overcook it and dry it out
another risotto rice such as carnaroli
a finely diced brown onion
a squeeze of lemon with an extra splash of stock

steps.title: Slice the leek and chicken
steps.content: Wash and slice the leek; dice the chicken thigh.
steps.detail: Trim the leek, halve it lengthwise and fan the layers under running water to wash out any grit hiding inside, then slice into thin half-moons. Cut the chicken thigh into 2 cm pieces and pat them dry. Grate the parmesan now so it's ready for the finish.
warnings: Keep the raw chicken, its board and its knife away from the leek; wash your hands and the board before reusing them.

steps.title: Keep the stock simmering
steps.content: Heat the stock in a separate pot and keep it hot.
steps.detail: Pour the chicken stock into a small saucepan, bring to a bare simmer, then keep it there over low heat. Adding hot stock to the rice is what keeps a risotto cooking evenly and turning creamy — cold stock stalls it. If you run low, top up with hot water from the kettle.

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.content: Sear the chicken in oil and butter until golden.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil and a knob of the butter in your wide pan over medium-high. Add the chicken in a single layer and let it sear undisturbed for a couple of minutes before turning, until golden on most sides and nearly cooked through. Scoop it onto a plate — it'll finish in the rice and reach 74°C at the centre.
warnings: Wash anything the raw chicken touched before it meets the cooked rice.

steps.title: Soften the leek
steps.content: Cook the leek in the same pan until silky.
steps.detail: Drop the heat to medium and add the sliced leek to the same pan, with all its chicken-y bits. Cook gently for 4 to 5 minutes, stirring, until soft and sweet but not browned. A pinch of salt helps it collapse.

steps.title: Toast the rice
steps.content: Stir in the arborio, then splash in the wine.
steps.detail: Add the arborio rice and stir for a minute or two until the grains look glassy at the edges and smell toasty — this keeps them from going mushy later. Pour in the white wine, if using, and stir until it has almost all bubbled away.
tips: No wine? A ladle of stock with a small squeeze of lemon does the same brightening job.

steps.title: Add stock ladle by ladle
steps.content: Stir in hot stock a ladle at a time, about 18 min.
steps.detail: Add a ladle of hot stock and stir until it's almost absorbed before adding the next — never drown the rice. Keep going, stirring often, for about 18 minutes. It's done when creamy and just tender with the faintest bite in the centre. Stir the chicken back in for the last few minutes to warm through to 74°C.
tips: You may not need every drop of stock, or you may need a splash of water — go by the texture, not the clock.

steps.title: Beat in butter and parmesan
steps.content: Stir through cold butter and parmesan, then rest.
steps.detail: Off the heat, beat in the rest of the cold butter and the grated parmesan hard for a few seconds — this final step, the mantecatura, is what makes it glossy and rich. Season with pepper and a little salt if it needs it. Rest for 2 minutes, then serve loose enough to spread slightly on the plate.

title: Beef Snow Pea Stir-Fry
description: Fast, glossy and green — strips of beef seared hard, snappy snow peas and a quick oyster-garlic sauce, all spooned over fluffy jasmine rice.
equipment: saucepan with lid for the rice
storage: Keeps 2 days in the fridge, though the snow peas soften. Store the rice separately in a covered container and use it within a day.
make_ahead: Slice and marinate the beef and prep the veg earlier in the day; the stir-fry itself takes only minutes.
leftovers: Turn day-old rice and any leftover beef into a quick fried rice with a beaten egg stirred through.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh strips or firm tofu
sugar snap peas, broccolini or green beans
hoisin, or extra soy sauce with a pinch of sugar
basmati, or serve it on egg noodles

steps.title: Cook the jasmine rice
steps.content: Simmer rinsed rice by absorption, then let it rest.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice until the water runs clear, then put it in a saucepan with 2 and a quarter cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to a boil, cover, drop to the lowest heat and cook for 12 minutes. Take it off the heat and leave it, lid on, for 10 minutes to steam — don't peek — then fluff with a fork.
tips: Getting the rice on first means it rests while you stir-fry, which actually improves the texture.

steps.title: Slice and marinate the beef
steps.content: Slice beef thinly; toss with soy and cornflour.
steps.detail: Slice the steak as thinly as you can across the grain — shorter fibres mean tender bites. Toss the strips in a bowl with the soy sauce and cornflour until each piece is lightly coated. The cornflour velvets the beef so it stays silky and helps the sauce cling later. Leave it to sit while you prep the rest.
warnings: Use a separate board for the raw beef and wash your hands, the knife and the board before touching the vegetables.

steps.title: Prep ginger, garlic and snow peas
steps.content: Grate ginger, mince garlic, trim the snow peas.
steps.detail: Grate the ginger and mince the garlic. Pull the stringy top off each snow pea by snapping the stalk end and drawing it down the seam. Have everything within arm's reach of the stove — a stir-fry moves too fast to chop as you go.

steps.title: Sear the beef in batches
steps.content: Fry the beef hard in a hot wok, in two batches.
steps.detail: Get the wok smoking-hot with half the oil. Add half the beef in a single layer and leave it for a minute to sear before tossing — crowding the pan steams the meat grey instead of browning it. Cook each batch for about 2 minutes until browned but only just cooked, then tip onto a plate.
warnings: Add the beef away from you and stand back — a screaming-hot wok can spit oil.

steps.title: Stir-fry the snow peas
steps.content: Fry snow peas with the ginger and garlic.
steps.detail: Add the last of the oil, then the ginger and garlic, and stir for 20 seconds until fragrant. Throw in the snow peas and stir-fry for 1 to 2 minutes — you want them bright green and still snappy, not floppy. A splash of water helps them steam-cook without losing their crunch.
warnings: Ginger and garlic burn fast on wok heat; keep everything moving.

steps.title: Glaze and serve
steps.content: Return beef, add oyster sauce, toss and plate on rice.
steps.detail: Return the beef and any resting juices to the wok, spoon in the oyster sauce and a good splash of water, and toss everything for a minute until glossy and coated. Season with pepper. Serve straight over the rested jasmine rice.

title: Polenta Mushroom Ragout Bowl
description: Soft, parmesan-rich polenta under a deep, garlicky mushroom ragout with thyme. Proper cold-weather comfort in a bowl.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge, stored separately. Cold polenta sets firm — reheat it with a good splash of water or milk, whisking until creamy again; the ragout reheats as it is.
make_ahead: The ragout is better a day on — make it ahead and cook the polenta fresh just before serving.
leftovers: Spread leftover polenta in a tray to set, cut into wedges and pan-fry until crisp, then top with the warm ragout.

substitutions.swap: a mix of whatever mushrooms you can get for more flavour
pecorino, or a good sharp tasty cheese
beef or chicken stock for a meatier depth
instant polenta cooks in 5 minutes; traditional takes about 30

steps.title: Slice mushrooms and aromatics
steps.content: Slice mushrooms; dice onion; mince garlic; strip thyme.
steps.detail: Slice the mushrooms thickly — they shrink a lot, so keep them chunky. Finely dice the onion, mince the garlic and strip the little thyme leaves from their stalks. Grate the parmesan and set it aside for both the polenta and the top.

steps.title: Brown the mushrooms
steps.content: Fry the mushrooms hard until deep golden.
steps.detail: Heat the olive oil in a large frying pan over high heat and add the mushrooms. Leave them alone at first — they'll release water and go pale, but keep cooking and that liquid boils off and they start to brown and shrink. You want them deep golden and meaty, about 6 to 8 minutes. A wide pan stops them steaming.
tips: Resist stirring too early; colour equals flavour, and it only comes once the water has cooked away.

steps.title: Simmer the ragout
steps.content: Add onion, garlic, thyme and stock; simmer saucy.
steps.detail: Add the onion and a knob of the butter to the mushrooms and cook for 3 minutes until soft, then stir in the garlic and thyme for a minute. Pour in about a cup of the stock and let it bubble down for 5 minutes into a loose, glossy ragout that just coats the mushrooms. Season with salt and pepper.

steps.title: Cook the polenta
steps.content: Whisk polenta into simmering stock until thick.
steps.detail: Bring the rest of the stock, topped up with water to make roughly 4 cups of liquid, to a simmer in a heavy saucepan. Rain in the polenta in a steady stream, whisking constantly so it doesn't clump. Swap to a wooden spoon and stir until thick, smooth and no longer gritty on the tongue — about 5 minutes for instant polenta, up to 30 for traditional.
warnings: Polenta bubbles like lava and can spit; keep the heat low and your hand out of the way.

steps.title: Finish the polenta
steps.content: Beat in parmesan and butter until creamy.
steps.detail: Off the heat, beat most of the grated parmesan and the last of the butter through the polenta until it's soft, creamy and loose enough to spread. If it stiffens, whisk in a splash of hot water. Taste and add salt; polenta needs a fair bit to sing.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Spoon the ragout over the polenta; top with parmesan.
steps.detail: Spoon the soft polenta into warm bowls and make a well in the middle. Pile the mushroom ragout on top so the juices run into the polenta, then finish with the last of the parmesan, a grind of pepper and a few thyme leaves.

title: Zucchini Ricotta Lemon Salad
description: Raw zucchini ribbons tossed with peppery rocket and basil, piled on lemony whipped ricotta and scattered with toasted pine nuts.
storage: A dressed salad won't keep — the zucchini weeps and softens within the hour. The whipped ricotta keeps 2 days covered in the fridge, so store it separately and assemble just before serving.
make_ahead: Whip the ricotta and toast the pine nuts ahead; shave and dress the zucchini only when you're ready to eat.

substitutions.swap: soft feta mashed with a little oil, or a goat's-style cheese
flaked almonds or roughly chopped walnuts
baby spinach for a milder leaf
mint, or a mix of both

steps.title: Toast the pine nuts
steps.content: Toast the pine nuts in a dry pan until golden.
steps.detail: Put the pine nuts in a small dry pan over medium heat and shake them constantly. In 3 to 4 minutes they'll turn golden and smell nutty. Tip them straight onto a plate the moment they colour — left in the hot pan they carry on cooking. This is the one step you can't walk away from.
warnings: Pine nuts go from golden to burnt in seconds; don't leave the pan or answer the phone.

steps.title: Shave the zucchini
steps.content: Peel zucchini into ribbons; salt to soften.
steps.detail: Run a vegetable peeler down each zucchini to make long, thin ribbons, turning as you go and stopping at the seedy core. Lay them in a bowl, sprinkle with a small pinch of salt and toss — this seasons them and draws out a little water so they turn silky rather than squeaky. Leave for 10 minutes, then tip off any liquid.

steps.title: Whip the ricotta
steps.content: Beat ricotta with lemon zest and oil until smooth.
steps.detail: Zest the lemon and beat the zest into the ricotta with a spoonful of the olive oil and a pinch of salt until smooth and spreadable. Loosen with a teaspoon of water if it's stiff. Keep the zested lemon back for juicing.

steps.title: Dress the leaves
steps.content: Toss ribbons, rocket and basil with lemon and oil.
steps.detail: Squeeze the juice of the lemon into a bowl with the rest of the olive oil and a grind of pepper, and whisk with a fork. Add the drained zucchini ribbons, the rocket and torn basil, and toss lightly with your hands so everything is just glossed, not drowned.

steps.title: Plate the salad
steps.content: Spread ricotta, pile the leaves, scatter pine nuts.
steps.detail: Spread the whipped ricotta across a platter with the back of a spoon. Pile the dressed ribbons and leaves on top so it looks loose and tumbling, then scatter over the toasted pine nuts and a few extra basil leaves. Finish with a last drizzle of oil and a crack of pepper, and serve right away.

title: Pork Mince Lettuce Cups
description: San choy bau, the crowd-pleaser — savoury ginger-and-garlic pork spooned hot into cold, crisp lettuce cups with cool cucumber and spring onion.
storage: Keep the cooked pork filling and the lettuce separate: the filling lasts 3 days in the fridge, while the lettuce needs to stay dry and crisp. Assemble cups only when you're ready to eat.
make_ahead: Cook the pork filling ahead and simply reheat it, then fill fresh cups to order.
leftovers: The pork filling is brilliant the next day tossed through noodles or fried rice.

substitutions.swap: chicken or turkey mince
iceberg for the crunchiest cups, or baby cos
hoisin, or extra soy sauce with a pinch of sugar
grated carrot or water chestnut for crunch

steps.title: Prep the cups and crunch
steps.content: Separate lettuce cups; dice cucumber; slice spring onion.
steps.detail: Peel the lettuce apart into whole cup-shaped leaves and drop them into a bowl of iced water to go glass-crisp while you cook. Dice the cucumber small, and slice the spring onions, keeping the pale whites for the pan and the green tops for scattering over at the end.
tips: A few minutes in iced water is the trick to lettuce cups that actually crunch.

steps.title: Grate the ginger and garlic
steps.content: Grate the ginger; mince the garlic.
steps.detail: Grate the ginger and mince the garlic, and have your soy and oyster sauces measured and ready by the stove. The pork cooks fast, so you don't want to be reaching for jars mid-fry.

steps.title: Brown the pork mince
steps.content: Fry the pork mince until golden and cooked through.
steps.detail: Heat the vegetable oil in a wok over high heat and add the pork mince. Let it sit for a minute to catch some colour, then break it up with your spoon and fry for 5 to 6 minutes until no pink remains and some bits are properly golden and crisp. Tip off any big pool of fat if there's a lot.
warnings: Cook the pork right through until there's no pink left, and wash your hands and utensils after handling raw mince.

steps.title: Add the aromatics
steps.content: Fry the ginger, garlic and spring onion whites.
steps.detail: Push the pork to one side and add the ginger, garlic and spring onion whites to the clear space. Stir them for about 30 seconds until fragrant, then mix through the pork. Left still over the heat, they'll catch and burn.
warnings: Garlic and ginger scorch in seconds on high heat — keep everything moving.

steps.title: Glaze the filling
steps.content: Toss through soy and oyster sauce until sticky.
steps.detail: Pour in the soy and oyster sauces with a small splash of water and toss for a minute until the pork is glossy and sticky and the liquid has cooked down. Season with a little pepper and taste — the sauces are salty, so it likely needs nothing more.

steps.title: Fill the lettuce cups
steps.content: Spoon pork into cups; top with cucumber and spring onion.
steps.detail: Shake the lettuce cups dry and lay them out. Spoon the hot pork into each one, then top with the diced cucumber and the reserved spring onion greens for freshness and crunch. Eat with your hands, and expect a little happy mess.

title: Corn Zucchini Fritter Stack
description: Golden, crisp-edged fritters packed with sweet corn, zucchini and salty feta, stacked up and topped with a cool dollop of yoghurt.
storage: Fritters keep 2 to 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Re-crisp them in a hot dry pan or the oven rather than the microwave, which turns them soft.
make_ahead: The cooked fritters reheat well, so make a batch ahead; the batter itself is best cooked fresh once the baking powder is in.
leftovers: Cold fritters are a great lunchbox filler, tucked into a wrap with the yoghurt and some salad.

substitutions.swap: grated cheddar, or ricotta for something milder
tinned or fresh corn kernels
self-raising flour, and skip the baking powder
sour cream

steps.title: Grate and squeeze the zucchini
steps.content: Grate zucchini, salt it, then wring out the water.
steps.detail: Grate the zucchini on the coarse side of a box grater, toss it with a good pinch of salt and leave it for 5 minutes. Then pile it into the middle of a clean tea towel, gather the corners and wring hard over the sink — you'll be surprised how much water comes out. This is the difference between crisp fritters and soggy ones.
tips: Squeeze it drier than you think you need to; wet batter spreads and steams instead of frying up crisp.

steps.title: Mix the batter
steps.content: Stir together veg, eggs, flour, feta and spring onion.
steps.detail: Tip the squeezed zucchini into a bowl with the corn, eggs, flour, baking powder, crumbled feta and sliced spring onion. Season with pepper and stir just until it comes together into a thick, spoonable batter — a few lumps are fine. If it looks wet and loose, add a spoon more flour.

steps.title: Fry the fritters
steps.content: Cook spoonfuls in oil, about 3 minutes each side.
steps.detail: Heat a thin layer of olive oil in a frying pan over medium heat. Drop in heaped spoonfuls and flatten each one slightly with the back of the spoon. Fry for about 3 minutes until the underside is deep golden and the edges look set, then flip and cook the other side. Work in batches so you don't crowd the pan, adding a little more oil as needed.
warnings: Hot oil spits when the wet batter goes in — lower each spoonful in gently, away from you.

steps.title: Season the yoghurt
steps.content: Stir yoghurt with salt and pepper for the topping.
steps.detail: While the fritters drain on paper towel, stir the yoghurt with a pinch of salt and pepper in a small bowl. A squeeze of lemon or a grating of garlic lifts it if you have them, but plain and cold against the hot fritters is lovely on its own.

steps.title: Build the stack
steps.content: Stack the fritters and top with the yoghurt.
steps.detail: Stack three or four fritters per plate, dollop the seasoned yoghurt on top and crumble over any leftover feta with a few spring onion greens. Serve while they're still hot and crisp at the edges.

title: Mushroom Leek Potato Gratin
description: Thin potato layered with buttery leek and browned mushrooms, soaked in garlicky thyme cream and baked until bubbling and golden on top.
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge. Reheat portions covered in a moderate oven until piping hot in the centre; it firms up cold, which actually makes it easier to slice.
make_ahead: Assemble the whole gratin a day ahead and keep it covered in the fridge, then bake when you need it — add 10 minutes as it starts cold.
leftovers: Cut cold leftovers into slabs and fry until crisp on both sides for a next-day side to eggs or a roast.

substitutions.swap: half cream and half milk for a lighter gratin
a gruyère-style or good sharp tasty cheese
leave them out for a classic potato-and-leek dauphinoise
a little chopped rosemary, used sparingly

steps.title: Heat the oven and slice potatoes
steps.content: Oven to 180°C fan; slice the potatoes thinly.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Rub the baking dish with a cut clove of garlic and a little of the butter. Slice the potatoes into thin rounds, about 3 mm — a mandoline makes quick, even work of it, but a steady hand and a sharp knife are fine. Even slices cook evenly, so aim for consistent thickness.
warnings: If you use a mandoline, use the guard — the blade is unforgiving of fingertips.

steps.title: Brown the mushrooms and leek
steps.content: Fry mushrooms golden, then soften the leek.
steps.detail: Wash and slice the leek into half-moons. Melt the rest of the butter with the olive oil in a frying pan over medium-high and brown the sliced mushrooms until golden and their water has cooked off, about 6 minutes. Add the leek and cook for another 3 to 4 minutes until soft. Season with salt and pepper.

steps.title: Infuse the cream
steps.content: Warm the cream with garlic and thyme.
steps.detail: Pour the cream into a small saucepan with the minced garlic, the thyme leaves and a pinch of salt. Warm it gently until it just steams — don't let it boil over — then take it off the heat. Warming the cream with the aromatics carries their flavour right through the dish.

steps.title: Layer the gratin
steps.content: Layer potato, mushroom-leek, cream and parmesan.
steps.detail: Lay a third of the potato slices over the base, overlapping them like roof tiles. Scatter over some of the mushroom and leek, a little parmesan and a slick of the warm cream. Repeat for two more layers, finishing with potato, the last of the cream and a good handful of parmesan. Press it all down so the potato sits in the cream.

steps.title: Bake until golden
steps.content: Bake covered, then uncovered, until tender.
steps.detail: Cover the dish with foil and bake for 30 minutes, then remove the foil and bake for another 25 to 30 minutes until the top is golden and bubbling and a knife slides through the potato with no resistance. If the top browns too fast, lay the foil back loosely over it.
warnings: Resist opening the oven in the first 20 minutes — the temperature drop slows the bake.

steps.title: Rest before serving
steps.content: Rest 10 minutes so it sets and slices cleanly.
steps.detail: Let the gratin rest for 10 minutes out of the oven before serving. Straight out it's molten and slides apart; a short rest lets the cream thicken around the potato so you get clean, tidy squares.

title: Lemon Ricotta Pancakes
description: Soft, fluffy pancakes lifted with ricotta and bright with lemon, finished simply with a squeeze of juice and a sprinkle of sugar.
storage: Best eaten fresh and warm. Leftovers keep 1 day in the fridge — refresh them in a toaster or dry pan. Cooked pancakes also freeze well, stacked between squares of baking paper.
make_ahead: The batter can sit for up to 30 minutes before cooking; any longer and you're better off cooking the pancakes and reheating them.
leftovers: Toast leftover pancakes and spread with butter and a drizzle of honey for a quick treat.

substitutions.swap: well-drained cottage cheese, or mascarpone for something richer
buttermilk for extra tang and lift
self-raising flour, and leave out the baking powder
a neutral oil if you'd rather not fry in butter

steps.title: Zest and juice the lemon
steps.content: Zest the lemon, then juice it.
steps.detail: Finely grate the zest from the lemon, taking just the yellow and none of the bitter white pith, then cut it in half and squeeze out the juice. Keep the zest for the batter and the juice for finishing at the end.

steps.title: Separate the eggs
steps.content: Split the eggs; whisk the whites to soft peaks.
steps.detail: Separate the eggs, putting the yolks in a large bowl and the whites in a clean, dry one. Whisk the whites until they hold soft, floppy peaks — this is what makes these pancakes so light. If you'd rather keep it simple, skip this and whisk the eggs in whole; they'll still be good, just a touch denser.
tips: Not a speck of yolk or grease can be in the whites or they won't whip — use a spotless bowl.

steps.title: Mix the batter
steps.content: Combine ricotta, yolks, milk, lemon zest and flour.
steps.detail: In the large bowl, whisk the ricotta, egg yolks, milk, lemon zest and sugar until fairly smooth — a little ricotta texture is nice. Sift in the flour and baking powder and fold gently until just combined. Then fold the whipped whites through in two goes, keeping as much air as you can; stop while it still looks a bit streaky.
tips: Overmixing knocks the air out and makes them rubbery — a few streaks of white left in is exactly right.

steps.title: Rest the batter
steps.content: Let the batter sit for 10 minutes.
steps.detail: Leave the batter to rest for about 10 minutes. This lets the flour relax and the baking powder start to work, so the pancakes cook up taller and more tender. It should look puffy and bubbled on top when it's ready.

steps.title: Cook the pancakes
steps.content: Fry in butter over medium-low until bubbles set.
steps.detail: Melt a little butter in a non-stick pan over medium-low heat — low and slow, because the ricotta and sugar catch and brown fast. Spoon in rounds of batter and cook until bubbles rise and pop across the surface and the edges look dry, about 2 to 3 minutes, then flip and cook the other side until golden and springy. Wipe the pan and add fresh butter between batches.
warnings: Keep the heat gentle; too hot and they'll be dark outside and raw and gluey in the middle.

steps.title: Serve with lemon and sugar
steps.content: Stack, squeeze over lemon and sprinkle sugar.
steps.detail: Stack the warm pancakes on plates, squeeze over the reserved lemon juice and sprinkle with a little extra sugar so it melts into a quick lemony glaze. Serve straight away while they're at their fluffiest.

title: Beef Black Bean Noodle Bowl
description: Strips of seared steak in a punchy fermented black bean and soy sauce, tangled through slippery egg noodles with sweet green capsicum.
storage: Best eaten fresh while the noodles are slippery. Leftovers keep 1 day in an airtight container in the fridge; loosen with a splash of water and reheat hard in a hot pan.
make_ahead: Slice the beef and mix the black bean sauce a few hours ahead, keep both covered in the fridge, and the stir-fry comes together in minutes.
leftovers: Cold noodles pack a good lunchbox — toss with an extra splash of soy before you box them up.

substitutions.swap: thinly sliced chicken thigh, or firm tofu for a meat-free bowl
1 tbsp ready-made black bean sauce from a jar, stirred in with the soy
thick hokkien or rice noodles, or even spaghetti at a pinch

steps.title: Slice beef and prep aromatics
steps.content: Slice steak thin across the grain; chop garlic, ginger, capsicum and spring onion.
steps.detail: Firm the steak in the freezer for 15 min, then slice it into thin strips across the grain so it stays tender. Season with a little pepper. Finely chop the garlic and ginger and keep them together in a heap. Deseed the capsicum and cut into strips, and cut the spring onions into 3 cm lengths, keeping the white and green parts separate.
tips: Slicing across the grain — cutting through the lines in the meat, not along them — keeps quick-cooked beef from going chewy.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and the knife after handling raw beef before they touch anything you'll eat raw.

steps.title: Rinse and mash beans
steps.content: Rinse the salty black beans, mash, and mix with soy.
steps.detail: Fermented black beans are intensely salty and pungent, so tip them into a sieve and rinse briefly under the tap, then shake dry. Roughly mash them with the back of a spoon in a small bowl. Stir through the soy sauce and a good splash of water to make a loose, savoury sauce ready to hit the wok.

steps.title: Boil the egg noodles
steps.content: Cook egg noodles until just tender, drain and oil.
steps.detail: Bring the pot of water to a rolling boil and cook the egg noodles for the packet time, usually about 4 min, until just tender with no chalky centre. Drain in a colander, then toss with a few drops of the oil so they don't clump while you get everything else moving.

steps.title: Sear the beef
steps.content: Sear beef in a rip-hot wok, in two batches.
steps.detail: Get the wok smoking-hot with the rest of the oil. Lay the beef out in a single layer and leave it for a minute to brown before tossing for another minute, until just seared with a little pink gone. Do it in two batches so the meat sears instead of stewing, and tip each batch onto a plate as it's done.
warnings: A screaming-hot wok can spit — add the beef away from you and stand back for a second.

steps.title: Fry capsicum and sauce
steps.content: Stir-fry capsicum and aromatics, then pour in the sauce.
steps.detail: Into the same wok add the capsicum, garlic, ginger and spring onion whites, tossing for 1–2 min until fragrant and the capsicum is just tender but still bright. Pour in the black bean and soy mixture and let it bubble for 30 seconds to come together into a glossy sauce.
warnings: Garlic and ginger scorch in seconds on high heat — keep everything moving and drop the heat if it starts to catch.

steps.title: Toss it all together
steps.content: Return beef and noodles, toss to coat, scatter greens.
steps.detail: Tip the beef and any resting juices back in, then add the drained noodles. Toss everything with tongs for a minute so the sauce clings to every strand, splashing in a little water if it looks tight. Scatter over the spring onion greens and serve straight away in deep bowls.

title: Chicken Corn Coconut Soup
description: A gentle, silky soup of poached chicken and sweet corn in a coconut and ginger broth, finished with spring onion and fresh coriander.
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Reheat gently without a hard boil so the coconut milk stays smooth rather than splitting.
make_ahead: Poach and shred the chicken a day ahead — the broth comes back together in minutes when you're ready to eat.
leftovers: It thickens as it sits, so loosen leftovers with a splash of stock or water and it's a fresh bowl again.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh for a richer, more forgiving poach
light coconut milk for a leaner soup
kernels cut from 2 fresh cobs when corn is in season

steps.title: Grate and chop aromatics
steps.content: Grate ginger, mince garlic, slice spring onion, chop coriander.
steps.detail: Peel and finely grate the ginger, and mince the garlic. Slice the spring onions, keeping the white parts for the soup and the green tops for garnish. Chop the coriander, saving the tender stems to go in early for flavour and the leaves to scatter at the end.

steps.title: Sweat ginger and garlic
steps.content: Gently cook the aromatics in oil until fragrant.
steps.detail: Warm the oil in the pot over medium heat, then add the ginger, garlic, spring onion whites and coriander stems. Cook gently for about 2 min, stirring, until the kitchen smells fragrant and everything has softened. Don't let it colour — you want it sweet, not browned.
warnings: Ginger and garlic catch and turn bitter fast, so keep the heat moderate and stir the whole time.

steps.title: Poach the chicken
steps.content: Simmer chicken breasts in stock until cooked through, then shred.
steps.detail: Pour in the chicken stock and bring it to a bare simmer with just a few bubbles breaking the surface. Slip in the whole chicken breasts and poach gently for 12–15 min until firm and cooked through to 74°C at the thickest point. Lift them out, rest a moment, then shred into rough pieces with two forks.
tips: A hard boil turns chicken breast rubbery — keep it to a lazy simmer.
warnings: Check the thickest breast reaches 74°C, and wash anything the raw chicken touched.

steps.title: Add coconut and corn
steps.content: Return chicken, add coconut milk and corn, simmer gently.
steps.detail: Slide the shredded chicken back in, pour in the coconut milk and add the corn. Bring it to the gentlest simmer and cook for about 5 min until the corn is sweet and hot through. Keep it just trembling, not boiling, so the coconut stays creamy and silky.

steps.title: Season and garnish
steps.content: Season to taste and top with spring onion and coriander.
steps.detail: Taste the broth and season with salt and a little pepper until it's savoury and rounded. Ladle into bowls and finish with the reserved spring onion greens and a shower of coriander leaves. A wedge of lime on the side is lovely if you have one.

title: Tomato Basil Risotto
description: Creamy arborio rice simmered with tinned tomato and torn basil, finished with butter and parmesan for that classic slow-stirred gloss.
storage: Risotto is best straight away. Leftovers keep 2 days in the fridge; reheat with a splash of stock or water, stirring, to bring back the creaminess.
leftovers: Roll cold risotto into balls, crumb them and shallow-fry for arancini.

substitutions.swap: chicken stock for a deeper base if it needn't be vegetarian
a spoonful of pesto stirred through at the end
carnaroli, or any short-grain risotto rice

steps.title: Warm stock, dice onion
steps.content: Heat the stock to a bare simmer; finely dice onion, mince garlic, tear basil.
steps.detail: Pour the stock into a saucepan and keep it at a bare simmer beside your risotto pan — cold stock stalls the cook. Finely dice the onion and mince the garlic. Tear the basil leaves and set them aside; adding them warm keeps the perfume fresh.

steps.title: Soften the soffritto
steps.content: Cook onion gently in oil, then add garlic.
steps.detail: Warm the olive oil in the wide pan over medium heat and cook the onion gently for about 5 min, stirring now and then, until soft and translucent with no colour. Stir in the garlic for the last 30 seconds, just until it smells sweet. This soft base is the backbone of the risotto.
warnings: Don't let the garlic brown — bitter garlic will haunt the whole pan.

steps.title: Toast the rice
steps.content: Stir in the arborio and toast until the edges turn glassy.
steps.detail: Tip in the arborio and stir for 1–2 min so every grain gets coated in the oil and warms through. The edges will turn translucent while the centre stays chalky white, and it will smell faintly nutty. This toasting step helps the rice hold its bite later.

steps.title: Stir in the tomato
steps.content: Add the tinned tomato and let it reduce.
steps.detail: Pour in the tinned tomatoes, crushing any whole ones against the side of the pan with your spoon. Let it bubble and reduce for about 3 min until the raw-tomato smell mellows and the mixture looks jammy and thick around the rice.

steps.title: Ladle and stir
steps.content: Add hot stock a ladle at a time, stirring, until creamy and al dente.
steps.detail: Add a ladle of hot stock and stir often, letting each addition almost fully absorb before adding the next. Keep it at a lively simmer and carry on for 18–20 min, seasoning with salt and pepper as you go, until the rice is creamy but still has a slight bite in the centre. You may not need every drop of stock.
tips: Taste a grain near the end — it should be tender with the faintest firm core, never mushy.

steps.title: Finish with butter and parmesan
steps.content: Beat in butter, parmesan and basil off the heat, then rest.
steps.detail: Pull the pan off the heat and beat in the cold butter, the grated parmesan and most of the torn basil — this final stir, the mantecatura, is what makes it glossy and creamy. Let it rest for 2 min, loosen with a last splash of stock so it spreads like lava, then serve topped with the rest of the basil.

title: Chicken Cashew Capsicum Stir-Fry
description: Golden wok-fried chicken with toasted cashews and sweet red capsicum in a glossy soy and oyster sauce, spooned over fluffy jasmine rice.
storage: Store the stir-fry and rice in separate airtight containers in the fridge. Cooked rice is safest eaten within a day, so cool it quickly and reheat once, until piping hot.
leftovers: Wrap the cold stir-fry and rice in a warmed tortilla for a quick lunch.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh, which stays juicier under high heat
roasted peanuts or almonds
hoisin, or extra soy with a pinch of sugar

steps.title: Cook the jasmine rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice and steam by absorption while you cook.
steps.detail: Rinse the jasmine rice in a few changes of water until it runs clearer, then drain. Put it in the saucepan with one and a half times its volume of water, bring to the boil, then clamp the lid on and drop to the lowest heat for 12 min. Rest it off the heat for 5 min, still covered, then fluff with a fork.
tips: Resist lifting the lid while it steams — the trapped steam is what cooks the grains evenly.

steps.title: Slice chicken and veg
steps.content: Cut chicken into bite-size pieces; prep capsicum, garlic and ginger.
steps.detail: Cut the chicken into even 2 cm pieces so they cook at the same rate, and season with a little pepper. Deseed the capsicum and cut into bite-size squares. Finely mince the garlic and ginger and keep them together, ready to hit the wok fast.
warnings: Keep raw chicken away from the rice and anything else, and wash your hands and the board straight after.

steps.title: Toast the cashews
steps.content: Dry-toast cashews until golden, then set aside.
steps.detail: Set the dry wok over medium heat and toss the cashews for a couple of minutes until they turn golden and smell nutty. Tip them straight onto a plate — they carry on cooking in a hot pan and go from golden to burnt in seconds.
warnings: Nuts scorch quickly, so keep them moving and pull them off the heat the moment they colour.

steps.title: Sear the chicken
steps.content: Fry chicken in hot oil until golden and cooked through.
steps.detail: Add the oil to the wok and get it hot, then spread the chicken out in a single layer. Leave it to gain colour before tossing, and stir-fry for about 5 min until golden all over and cooked through to 74°C with no pink inside. Work in two batches if your wok is small.
warnings: Hot oil can spit — lower the chicken in gently, away from you.
Make sure the chicken is white right through, with no pink, before the next step.

steps.title: Fire the aromatics and capsicum
steps.content: Add garlic, ginger and capsicum; toss until fragrant.
steps.detail: Push the chicken to the side and add the garlic, ginger and capsicum. Toss hard for about 2 min until fragrant and the capsicum is crisp-tender and glossy but still has some snap. Keep everything moving so nothing catches on the hot metal.
warnings: Garlic and ginger burn in seconds on high heat — stir constantly and pull back the heat if needed.

steps.title: Sauce and finish
steps.content: Pour in soy and oyster sauce, toss cashews back, serve on rice.
steps.detail: Pour in the soy and oyster sauce and let it bubble for 30 seconds, tossing so it coats everything in a glossy glaze. Fold the toasted cashews back through at the very end so they keep their crunch. Spoon straight over the fluffy jasmine rice.

title: Beef Brisket Polenta Bowl
description: Beef brisket braised low and slow in beef stock and tomato until it falls apart, spooned over soft, cheesy parmesan polenta.
equipment: heavy casserole or dutch oven with lid
storage: The braise keeps 3 days in the fridge and freezes for 3 months. Cook the polenta fresh, as it sets firm once cold. Reheat the beef until piping hot.
make_ahead: The brisket is even better a day on — braise it ahead, chill, lift off any set fat, then reheat while the polenta cooks.
leftovers: Pile leftover brisket and its gravy into a toasted roll, or toss it through pasta.

substitutions.swap: chuck or gravy beef, cut into large chunks
half stock, half red wine for a richer braise
soft mashed potato if you're out of polenta

steps.title: Sear the brisket
steps.content: Season and brown the brisket all over in hot oil.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 150°C fan. Pat the brisket dry and season it well with salt and pepper. Heat the oil in the casserole until it shimmers, then sear the beef for a few minutes on each side until deeply browned — that dark crust is where the flavour comes from. Lift it out onto a plate.
warnings: The pan and oil are very hot, so lower the beef in with tongs and keep your face back from any spitting.
Wash your hands and anything the raw beef touched before it meets cooked food.

steps.title: Soften onion, toast the paste
steps.content: Cook onion, add garlic and rosemary, then darken the tomato paste.
steps.detail: Dice the onion and drop it into the same pot, cooking for about 5 min in the beefy fat until soft. Stir in the minced garlic and rosemary sprigs, then the tomato paste, and cook for a minute until the paste darkens to a deep brick red and smells rich rather than raw.
warnings: Garlic scorches fast in a hot pan — keep it moving once it goes in.

steps.title: Braise low and slow
steps.content: Return the beef, add stock, cover and braise until fork-tender.
steps.detail: Settle the brisket back in and pour in the beef stock so it comes most of the way up the meat. Bring it to a gentle simmer, put the lid on, and slide it into the oven for about 3 hours. It's ready when a fork slides in with no resistance and the meat pulls apart in shreds.
tips: Keep the braise at the barest bubble — a hard boil makes brisket stringy and tight instead of meltingly soft.

steps.title: Reduce the gravy
steps.content: Rest the beef and simmer the liquid into a glossy gravy.
steps.detail: Lift the brisket out and let it rest while you set the pot on the stove. Simmer the braising liquid for a few minutes until it thickens into a glossy gravy that coats a spoon. Shred or chunk the beef and fold it back through the sauce.

steps.title: Cook the parmesan polenta
steps.content: Rain polenta into simmering liquid, stir until creamy, beat in parmesan.
